text: Of course, we have to improve the system, but we also have to get a grip on the numbers. This is an important issue. People need to know what's in our manifestos. I've set out my policies. But people need to know that the Liberal Democrats propose an amnesty for illegal immigrants. That could mean that some 600,000 people who are here illegally would actually be allowed to stay here and be given full citizenship, access to welfare and council housing, and could also bring a relative each into our country. I think that just doesn't make sense. That, I think, is a complete mistake that would make a bad situation we've had after 13 years of Labour even worse. I say, let's grip this problem. Let's talk about it sensitively and sensibly. Let's bring immigration down to more manageable levels. And I think the public will respond by making this not an issue at future elections.

I think this is partly what's been going wrong for so long. We've had both major parties running governments over the last 20 years talking tough about immigration and delivering complete chaos in the way in which it's run. I'm like anybody else. I just want a fair, workable immigration system that counts people in, counts people out, only make sure immigrants come here if there's jobs for them to do in parts of the country where they don't place unreasonable strain on housing, on public services and so on. I think the regional approach that we're putting forward, which would be a major innovation, they do it in Canada, they do it in Australia, but it would be a major innovation here, which I think would restore public confidence in an immigration system.

When we talked about it last week, Nick didn't tell us that he wants an amnesty for illegal immigrants. I think that would be a wrong policy for this reason. It would encourage people to come to this country thinking that at some point we would legalise their presence. I think that would be not a deterrent on people coming to our country illegally, it would mean that more and more people would come into our country. Now our policy is to control and manage immigration. We've got a point system to do so. No unskilled worker from outside Europe can now come into our country. If you've got a skill, then we will get a British person to do that job advertised in a job centre if the skill is available in Britain. We're gradually reducing the number of skills we need to come from abroad. So cooks and care assistants in future, they will not come from abroad, they are trained up in Britain. And gradually we'll go through the skills list and make sure that those people who get jobs in Britain are skilled in Britain to do the jobs here. Thank you.

Well, net inward migration is falling as a result of the measures we're taking, and the points system is the key to this. It's done in Australia and in other countries. Oh, the only real fear, I suppose, I have is for my family. I've got a boy who is just turning 16, he's 15 and three quarters. I've got a girl who's 13. These are really difficult ages for children. If they're under five, it would probably be easier. If they were 20 or over, it'd be easier. And my only fear, really, is the impact it's going to have on them. That's why we never name them in public, never have a photo shoot with them. I want them to be able to walk to school and have their own lives, unaffected as much as I can by what I'm doing. And I don't fear the big decisions. In fact, I relish the chance to change our country. And on the first day, I want to start on our first steps. I want you to see the change that we can bring about. I know you're going to hear a lot about the past. in this election, but this election is about the future. Well, leadership means being straight with people about what you plan to do. I'm not on You're on the record for not saying you want to.

Steve, you're right to highlight the issue. It's completely unfair what's happening. When people come here illegally, jump the queue, it not only puts pressure on public services and not only threatens our security, it's an affront to that sense of fairness, which is fundamental to our national character. And that's why I've made it a priority to stop the boats. We have made progress. The numbers have come down over the past 12 months compared to the 12 months before. But, in order to fully solve this problem, you need a deterrent. We need to make it crystal clear to people that if they come to our country illegally, they won't be able to stay. And that's why, if I'm your Prime Minister on July 5th, the flights will go to Rwanda, we will build that deterrent that multiple other European countries now agree is the right way to deal with this problem, and that's how we solve this problem. If Keir Starmer is your Prime Minister, all those illegal migrants will be out on our streets. And that is the choice for you. Do not surrender our borders to the Labour Party.

I think one of the things about this election is it feels incredibly polarised and like everybody's just at each other's throats, but there's actually cause for optimism. So Adam and Sian and myself, our parties are all working together in this election on the Unite to Remain Alliance. We've been working together on stopping Brexit and although Nicola and I have our robust differences on Scotland's rule in the UK. We've also worked together on stopping Brexit. I've worked with Rishi's colleague Andrea Ledsom, who I disagree with on most things, but I've worked with her on how we can try and stamp out harassment and bullying for staff working in Parliament. And Richard's colleague Stella Creasy have joined on many a feminist campaign and trying to protect women in terms of rights over their own bodies. So I think there's a lot that we can be positive about where there's actually a lot of cooperation in politics, which can help us to work together on those big issues, including climate change and social care.

Well, Joan, I told you at the start they were all the same, that they all agreed, and they do. They all agree they should be part of the European Union. So the answer to your question is, as members of the EU, what can we do to control immigration? Let me tell you, nothing. Nothing. The Prime Minister can talk about benefits. Ed Miliband can talk about benefits. This isn't about benefits. This is about numbers. and we have a total open door to 10 former communist countries and to the Eurozone, where people are suffering very, very badly. I don't blame a single migrant that comes to Britain from Eastern Europe wanting to better their lot. It has depressed wages. It has led to such a crisis in housing. You know, we have to build a new house. one house every seven minutes just to cope with current levels of immigration. We need to change our relationship with Europe to one of trade and friendship, take back control of our borders and put in place an Australian-style points system. And 77% of British people want something done.
OK. I'll try and deal with some of those questions, if I may. You've got about a minute left, I'm afraid, Mr Corbyn.

pre-school children in particular. Of course, we've got to give our young people the best start in life. What we need is to have a wrap around care. Gordon Brown. Well, you know, imagine a European Council meeting if David Cameron was in charge two months from now. And Linda's absolutely right. And I was coming back to that point because that eroded people's trust. At a time when people wanted the government to do the right thing, they didn't. OK, so the answer to this question starts with me saying something nice about Nicola Sturgeon and the SNP. Wait for it. Well, actually, people seem to think I have a very kind of healthy lifestyle, and I go to the gym, and there's this obsession about my fasting. Let me make one important point which is having learned the lesson of having to bail out the banks, I think the next lesson we must learn is we must not put ourselves in the position of having to bail out other European economies. People need to know that the Liberal Democrats in their manifesto are still in favour of joining the Euro. As late as last year, Nick Clegg was saying the Euro would be an anchor for our economy. If we were in the Euro now, your taxes, your National Insurance would not be going on hospitals and schools and police officers, it would be going to Greece and possibly other countries as well. That's why I say one of the lessons to learn is let's stay out of the Euro, let's keep our own currency and let's recognise what a massive strategic error the Liberal Democrats would have made.

Our view is straightforward. Three years ago, the British people voted to leave the EU. It's time we respected that and actually left. Whether you voted leave or remain, it's time for us as a country to move forward, not remain stuck in neutral. So rather than spending more years going round in circles with more referendums and the division and delay that that would bring, Jeremy Corbyn propped up by the SNP, let's just get Brexit done. Thank you. Nicola Sturgeon.
Well I, I - I've just explained to you why it's impossible to do that
We don't in principle and in practice.

Well, what I think is that we need to first of all celebrate the free movement of people in the EU. As Nicola said, that's a huge potential opportunity for people of Britain to move around the EU, just as we benefit from people from the EU coming here. In terms of the rest of the world, what we need for the non-EU immigrants is a fair, humane and controlled system. What we have at the moment is controlled, but it's neither fair nor humane. And if we look at the way we're treating refugees in this country, a quarter of the appeals against asylum refusal are successful. These are people who are refugees we're not treating fairly. We need a different kind of system, a fairer, humane system that acknowledges that lots of British people can't live in their own country with a non-EU spouse or partner. because of unfair and unreasonable immigration rules. We need to be fair and reasonable. And I just want to pick up on the NHS point. Nigel Farage says he's worried about the effect of immigrants on the NHS. One in four doctors is foreign born. 40% of NHS staff are foreign born.

It only happens when all four countries agree, so that you don't just have the biggest nation pulling everyone else out.

Thank you. Joan, we do need strong and effective controls on immigration, we need to make sure people don't get away with abusing the systems that the rest of us pay for. We also need to recognise that in parts of the UK there is a strain on housing and public services. I take the view, though, that the answer to that is investing more in homes and in our public services and in enforcing a decent minimum wage, not in scapegoating immigrants. I think the views of the Westminster parties on this issue have been driven by the fear of UKIP rather than by rational debate. And I think we need to have a balanced debate. So, here's some facts that we need to bear in mind. EU immigrants are net contributors to UK public finances. The majority of migrants work A majority of those who don't work are students. So let's have a debate, let's not duck the issues, but let's make sure it's a decent and a civilised debate, not one that's driven by the intolerance of Nigel Farage.

Stephen, immigration is too high, and we will need to take bold action to bring it down, whether that's legal migration, where we're going to introduce a new legal cap to guarantee that it will come down every year, or the boats, which you mentioned, where we got the numbers down last year by a third, and now we have a deterrent ready to go. Migrants have been detained, the flights will go in July, but only if I'm your Prime Minister. If Keir Starmer's elected, All those people will be released, the flights will be cancelled and we will have no deterrent to stop the unfairness of people crossing in boats. Gerard, you talked about a fair, workable immigration system. That's exactly what I want. What's happened over the last several years is almost precisely the reverse. You've had lots and lots of tough talking about immigration from both Conservative and Labour governments and complete chaos in the actual administration of the system. There was a Conservative government that removed the exit control so we knew who was leaving as well as who was coming in. That's what the Labour government then followed up on as well. So what I think we need to do is firstly make sure that we restore those exit controls. We have borders so we know exactly who's coming in, but also when they're supposed to leave. And the second thing that I would do is this. At the moment under the immigration system, if you want to come and work in this country, you have to show two things. Firstly, that you've got a sponsor who's sponsoring your arrival in this country, and secondly, that there's a job for you to do. I want to add a third element, that you also only go to a place, to a region, where you are needed, so that we only send immigrants to those places where they can be coped.
Thank you. One honest member of the panel. Well done.
You should bring your mum. You come to university in Britain, you can't bring your mum, can you?
Where are they? The answer is incredibly simple.

You can see one of the problems with hung parliament and coalition forming is there's quite a lot of bickering going on already. And there is a difference here. There really is a difference about what we believe, particularly on the jobs tax. I think trying to stop this is vital. What Nick and the Liberal Democrats are saying is, first of all, when we announced let's stop the jobs tax, they said it was nauseating. And in their manifesto, they've now said that actually it's their aspiration. It's the first time in politics I can remember someone coming up with a nauseating aspiration that they want to fulfil. But it is a difference between us. We want to stop this jobs tax to keep the economy going. The other two parties are not going to do it.

What happened, as you may remember, was the previous government, the Labour government, introduced fees and then increased them. When we came into government, there was no money left. David Cameron's just been waving around the letter which was provided to the Liberal Democrat Chief Secretary of the Treasury at the time, saying there was no money left. And both the larger parties, as they, I think, would admit if you'd asked them about it, wanted fees to go up very, very considerably. In fact, the report commissioned at the time into all of this said there should be no upper limit at all. In a sense what we did was get the fairest deal we could in those circumstances and thankfully now got more young people at university than ever before. But look, as I said earlier, my experience is some people absolutely will say, you know, we can't forget that. I hope there are plenty of other fair-minded folk who will accept that nonetheless there are many, many, many other good policies that I did put into practice.
In America?

I'll come to it. Just let me, let me tell you how, how we get to where we want to be. At the moment, we have a system that is desperately unfair and inhumane. The people who benefit from it are not genuine refugees, they are the people who pay the people smugglers to come to this country. Genuine
We've had... Oh, enough. That's fine.

Look, all I can say... I went to run the Crown Prosecution Service. You referenced that. I was the chief prosecutor for five years, effectively bringing in every criminal prosecution in England and Wales, including here in Grimsby, in our courts, to make sure that where people broke the law, they were prosecuted and you were kept safe as a result of that. Working, of course, with my staff. I worked before that in Northern Ireland, so I've had this constant theme in my life of trying to serve the public. That's why I worked in Northern Ireland. That's why I worked in the Crown Prosecution Service. That's why I took the decision to go into politics. I came into politics quite late in life. I'd done other things beforehand. But I took the decision that I couldn't take the issues, make the changes that I thought were necessary, unless I went into politics. I was frustrated. Do you think you've changed? Well, I've been much clearer in my own mind that the country must come first and the party and party politics second. I'm really, you know, I don't apologise for that. I think that's really important. I think it probably is a result of coming into politics late because I'm not tribally political. I actually do believe there are good people who vote other than Labour who want their family, their community, and their country to go forward. And that's why I've said, if we are to come into power, I want a decade of national renewal. And I want everybody who wants the country to improve to feel that there's a place for them, whether or not they traditionally voted Labour, because I actually I genuinely think that the vast majority of people in this country are reasonable people. They're tolerant people who sort of live and let live. And if given the chance, would pull together to advance their community, to advance their family, and to advance their country. And I don't think you need to be tribally Labour for that. I think you can actually pull the country together. And that would be a real moment for us.

But let's at least not pretend that you can put forward these ideas, which have got no substance, haven't been thought through, at least the kind of ideas I've been putting through, are trying to deal with a really, really difficult issue, which has been brushed under the carpet for too long.

Look, I haven't changed my view on whether I think we are better off in the European Union and watching what I have seen in the last three and a half years watching in Parliament MPs who voted to leave, who want Brexit, unable to agree amongst themselves about what Brexit looks like. So Theresa May proposes a Brexit that Boris Johnson doesn't think is Brexity enough. Boris Johnson proposes a Brexit that first of all Nigel Farage says he can't handle, but then who knows what discussions they've had and what's been offered in order to get him on board. If even those MPs who have been putting forward Brexit can't agree, I tell you, I genuinely don't have confidence that there is a majority in this country for any specific type of Brexit. And when the government analysis says that Brexit will make our country poorer and it will mean people lose their jobs and there is less money for our NHS, If we really don't think that there is a majority for any specific type of Brexit, I think it would be irresponsible to go ahead with that without at least checking. So we've argued for a people's vote. And in this election, we're saying if you vote Liberal Democrat and get a Lib Dem majority government, we will revoke Article 50. And if you don't, we will argue for a people's vote. So what do you say to your former Lib Dem voter here?
You have failed the British people and people can see that.
It absolutely was not doing so and the Green Party is really clear that we want to be about building communities, building cross-community understanding in this country and beyond.
Well, I'm making it clear that we're standing on the manifesto that we launched.
Michelle, that's what I said. I said to Rob, I recognise his frustrations.
We do.

No, I've been in politics quite a long time and I've always said at every election the only poll that matters is the one that takes place on polling day. And I think the British people, when they're voting, everybody's casting their vote, people here and people watching and others, have a very simple choice and it is about Who do you trust to have that leadership to take us to get the best deal for Brexit, a Brexit deal in Europe? And who's got the will and the vision, not just to take us through Brexit, not just to get on with the job of delivering Brexit, but to take us beyond and build a better future for this country?
David...
Well it wasn't a question of falling for it the first time. The fact is

Richard Tice. Yeah, you're quite right. I mean, it's complete and utter chaos. 15 years ago, people were assessed within a fortnight, had a right to appeal within about a week or two, and then a final decision was made, either to be accepted or to be rejected. And then the number being accepted was about 20%. The number now is about 75%. And going back to the previous point, you know, congratulations, sir. But actually, of course, we want a fair, compassionate system which works promptly and efficiently. We've got an incompetent Home Office, which means that the backlog has increased, and therefore the cost to all of us as taxpayers has soared through the roof. The right way to have a fair system is to assess people promptly, rapidly, so that everybody gets a fair deal and knows where they stand. And that just seems to be completely beyond the wit of the Home Office, despite minister after minister after minister. It's just unacceptable.

How do you pretend to cut taxes and pay for more, Mr Johnson? Because Labour didn't produce the funding for anything. I'm afraid it's the hard work of the British people and it's British companies, it's British businesses, large and small, that produce the funding that pays for our National Health Service and everything else. And we're a government that understands that if you're going to have a dynamic market economy, you cannot clobber them. You cannot clobber companies with the highest taxes in Europe, which is what Mr Corbyn and the Labour Party would do. It would be entirely wrong for this country to go down the route that they are proposing. I quoted the IFS ad, Mr Corbyn.
I think the Conservatives should support us as well.

Well, I've got to say, Beth, it hasn't been an easy 18 months in general, so what I've done, though, is just keep going to try and do my best for people, and that's what I'm doing in this campaign. I'm excited to have the opportunity to talk to everyone, not just tonight, but across the campaign, out and about in the country, about our plans for the future, because I know we've been through a tough time, of course we have, and I'm sure we'll talk about it. It's been tough for all of you here tonight, everyone watching, but I do believe we've turned a corner, We've got a clear plan for the future to make a difference to people, to cut their taxes, to bring down immigration, to protect pensions. Those are all things that I think matter. I think those are things that people want. And so I'm going to keep fighting hard till the last day of this election.

Well if, if you want me to apologise for the war in Iraq, I'm afraid I can't say that's sorry we removed Saddam, no, if (fluffs), if you're asking me other things that I've got wrong over the past eight years, I've already said there are certain things that any government gets wrong; I suppose I've had my share of those.

Firstly, you need to tighten the borders at Bethlehem so that we know who is coming in and who is going out. Previous Labour and Conservative governments removed the exit controls so we didn't know who had to leave. I would want to see those reinstalled immediately. I want to see a border police force. We have every right to police our borders. Secondly, I would make sure that we direct people who come to live and work in this country to those areas in the country where of course they have a job and they've got someone who can vouch for them, but also where there is space for them to live where there isn't unreasonable strain placed on public services. And thirdly, we've got to do something about the legacy of the problem created by the chaos which Labour and Conservative governments created in our immigration system. And that was lots of people coming here illegally, who are now still living, years and years and years, in the shadows of our economy. And I'd rather get them out of the hands of criminal gangs, so we can go after those criminal gangs, into the hands of the tax man, if they want to play by the rules, pay their taxes, speak English. That is a smart, fair, effective way of dealing with immigration.
Yeah¿

Our priority is obviously to get it sorted. We will, within three months, negotiate a credible leave option with the European Union and within six months put that to a referendum of the British people to decide between that option of leaving whilst protecting jobs and trade and the Good Friday Agreement with Europe or remaining as full members of the European Union. That will be the choice put before the British people. The idea that the Prime Minister Boris Johnson's deal can be dealt with and finished by the end of January is such nonsense. What he's proposing is a trade deal with the United States that would take at least seven years to negotiate, whilst at the same time saying he would negotiate a special trade deal with the European Union. The two things are actually incompatible. Thank you, Mr Corbyn.
Well, our plans don't require us to do that. But I can't sit here today

I've got four children. My youngest starts secondary school in a few months' time. And my main motivation, when they're my age, I want to look them in the eye and say I did absolutely everything I could to protect our country's future, to protect their future from the calamitous, extreme Brexit that Theresa May has chosen. She is not enacting the will of the people. She has chosen to interpret it in an extreme way. And that has been backed by Jeremy Corbyn and Nigel Farage as they trooped through the lobbies to back that extreme Brexit will damage future generations. So the deal is this. The Brexit negotiations between Brussels and London over these next few months will lead to outcomes that none of us can predict for the time being. And that means, at the end, you should have the final say on the Brexit deal. Either the politicians will sign it off, or the people. I trust the people. And if you want to reject that deal, you must have the right to remain in the European Union.
What was your name, I'm sorry?

Well, regime change on its own isn't enough. You have to have other things as well. At the moment, they're not being processed. So they're sitting in hotels, which you are all paying for. They're not processed. I've read the credit agency reports and actually what they said was the biggest overhang on the UK economy was the uncertainty and paralysis caused by Brexit. Caused by your government's mishandling of Brexit? Over the last 12 months, the number of crossings have down a third because the plans we've put in place are starting to make a difference. But the choice of this election is about the future. We will continue to lead with Labour. Don't let people create scare stories to frighten you into thinking that we can't change Europe. Of course we can change Europe. I, unlike David Cameron and Gordon Brown, have been in there, have saw changes. We can do it if we leave.

Well, actually, the corporation tax level that I'm proposing would actually be less than there is anywhere in the major industrial countries of Western Europe anyway. The crucial thing, and you're right to raise it, has to be gaining tariff-free trade access to the European market as the basis on which they can continue. Because look at it this way. If Airbus are in this country, as they are, they manufacture aircraft parts, they manufacture satellite parts, and the final assembly for the aircraft takes place in Toulouse. some of the parts are made here, some of the engines are made in other places, some parts are made in Spain, that final assembly takes place. If we don't have that tariff-free access to the European market, are they going to stay? Would they want to stay? Ask yourself that question. You could say the same for many other big manufacturing enterprises. That is why the crucial thing has to be maintaining that economic relationship with Europe outside membership of the European Union. That is the best way of guaranteeing jobs and a growing manufacturing economy in Britain. But it will also have a government that is investing alongside them to improve skill levels and improve communications within this country.
All right, Mr Clegg now.

Well, look, the system is being sped up. The productivity has gone up about three times. About 112,000 asylum decisions were made last year. The so-called legacy backlog, those are the older cases before certain legal changes, is down by 95%. It's gone from 92,000 down to 5,000. Now, there are some changes we're making through a thing called the Illegal Migration Act, which essentially returns to a system more like the one that Richard was describing. where decisions are made quickly and you can't make these sort of repeated claims. Those have arisen over time as essentially case law has expanded the grounds on which people can claim asylum again and again. The Illegal Migration Act, which I think Labour voted against, is designed to make that process a lot more streamlined. So it's speeding up already and the commencement of that act will make the process a lot faster and a lot more final.

Well, I hope the union is not going to be broken up or sacrificed in this way, but the agreements that the Prime Minister negotiated or he's put, not negotiated, he's put towards the Parliament was about creating a border between the Republic of Ireland and Northern Ireland, creating a different customs arrangement for Northern Ireland with the rest of Ireland, having promised to the Democratic Unionist Party he would never do that. Clearly, there is an issue there. Clearly, there are issues all around about this deal that he's proposed. And I do think that this deal is damaging to this country, damaging to the rights we have at work and consumer protection and environmental protection in this country, and very damaging to manufacturing industry jobs all over Britain, some of which have already gone because of uncertainty over Brexit.
We can do that. The Belgian authorities have been doing it off their own shores. They've been taking people back.

I mean all I'd say is that the more they argue amongst each other, the more it's absolutely clear to me the only way you'll get real change, real action, sensible action on this issue is from a Conservative government. It is a sensitive issue and it needs to be dealt with sensitively, reasonably. It's very important that we talk about this properly but I think the country wants and deserves firm immigration control instead of the sort of nonsense we're hearing over here. And that would make a difference. And what it would really achieve is getting back to where we used to be, where we didn't have immigration questions at public meetings, we weren't asked about it on the street, when you knocked on a door, nobody raised it. Why? Because the British public was happy with the level of immigration. We knew that was a level we could integrate and provide good health and housing and schools for people. It's been out of control these last 13 years, that's Labour's fault, and from all I can hear, the Liberal Democrats would make it much, much worse.

No, I think that... You haven't changed your mind. Look, the referendum drew a line under those debates. What the referendum did was took the view of the British people. Now, I think it's right that we do that now, and I think, I genuinely think, that if we get Brexit right, and this is really important for us, if we get Brexit right, we can make a real success of the opportunities that open up for us. But we can only do that if we grasp those opportunities. But you still think it's a bad idea. No, I believe we're doing the right thing in making a success of Brexit.

I don't know whether we'd have a pint. It's hard. Look, it's hard when we share a bacon sandwich or something. Joe Swinson. I'm going to stick to my principles of wanting to remain in the EU, whether it's popular or whether it's not. Humza Yousaf.

No, I don't think it will, in all honesty. Nobody's talking about a return to the days in the 1970s in this country when the top income earners were on 90 plus, 95% plus levels of tax. That was crazy. That caused a brain drain, a loss of talent and money to the country. And do remember that the people you're talking about here, those top rate taxpayers, 1% of people, They've got children that they want to put through university. They've got elderly parents that they're caring about. They benefit from the wider policies that we're putting forward. So I think it's a fairer system. There must be something wrong, surely, that after eight years of a Labour government, and let's face it, They've had a pretty benign economic backdrop over those eight years. The poorest people in our society, with direct and indirect tax, now pay, still pay, a higher proportion of their household income in tax than do the very top earners. Now, I don't think that's a fair society. OK.
Repeatedly. Repeatedly for us to stand down across the entire country.
They will tell you that this has been...

Look, I think that politicians, I believe that politicians should be serious about voters' concerns and show how they're going to address this. I've tried to do an hour interview. Is that true or not? No, it's not true. What we're saying is that if you get your degree, you graduate, you go into the labour market, once you're above a certain level of income, you have a £2,000 contribution to make. over phased installments. That money sets up an endowment fund, it doesn't go to the universities, and it goes to widen the net of poorer students who can come in as the next generation of students. Now I think that's good. And can I come back to what the lady who asked me the question was pointing out about this? What has been the implication? The implication in Scotland, the subsequent events have shown, that the rate of increase of application for Scots wanting to go to Scottish universities is now significantly higher than it is in England and Wales for English and Welsh students wanting to go to English and Welsh universities. And you can't tell me that isn't because people look at the appalling prospect of £12,000, £20,000 worth of debt and say, I can't afford it, my family can't afford it. That's terrible.
If I just look at...

Well, the truth is, I mean, there is a big difference between the Conservative party that is gripping this problem and the other two parties that I think are just dancing around it. I mean, the government's had 13 years to control immigration, and yet we've had some of the highest levels of immigration in the last 50 years. And frankly, I think Nick's ideas would actually make the situation worse. There are two bad ideas. The idea of the amnesty, it's been shown all over Europe, that what that leads to is a big increase in false asylum claims and more immigration. That's a bad idea. And I think this idea of somehow having regional immigration saying it's okay to come and work in Bristol but you can't live in Birmingham or you can live in Bradford but you can't live in... I mean, it sounds like they're going to put up border controls along the M5. I mean, it really doesn't make sense. It hasn't been thought through. It would make a bad situation worse. And we need real change, genuine change, that only the Conservatives are offering.
I do. I know what kind of terrorism powers are there.
No. We've got to make priorities of what we have from the, the wealth of accumulated decisions of the party, then put those priorities to the public.

By four billion pounds in our first budget. I wish that we could undo all the damage which Labour have done in the last eight years at one fell swoop; we can't do that. No deal is better than a bad deal. And let me just tell you... That wasn't exactly my question. My question was, are you prepared to walk away... Where is it? We had legislated... It doesn't exist. No, I'm sorry. It existed because we said we were going not to introduce... When did it exist? Now, I happen to think our system is fairer. We already, incidentally, in this country... You shake your head, but it's true.

What we've done now is introduce a completely different system of student finance. It's not top-up fees as with the old system where you paid the fees as you went through university. It's important people understand what this proposal is. From now on, people will pay no fees as they go through university. They will then, and there will be maintenance grants, reintroduce £3,000 a year for the poorest students. Then when students graduate, they will pay back into the pot a fair repayment system based on their ability to pay. So, for example, If you stop work, or you're a woman and you start to raise a family, or, for example, for any reason, you become ill or unemployed, you don't pay. And the system is one in which we're able to get more money into the universities, more people able to go to university, without anyone having to pay fees as they go through the system. You didn't say that in the manifesto, did you? No, I didn't say that about maintenance grants being reintroduced for poor students.

we go any further, can I just remind all of us that diversity is one of our great strengths and I can see that as a look out to this audience. In respect of Parliament, the Attorney General's advice is never published to Parliament. It's been published, I think, three times over the last hundred years. And yet it's now. Exactly. Published to everybody. Exactly, because it's been leaked. And therefore, you might as well have the full opinion. The Attorney General went through his reasoning and explained to Cabinet why, in his view, there was a reasonable case that could be made that the action was lawful. Now, that is what has happened. And look, as I say, in the end, you can frame this any way you want. But the question was, when we were faced with the situation, I think it's important people understand this. Back in March 2003, we were trying to get a second United Nations resolution. We couldn't get one. because France said they would not accept any resolution with an ultimatum to Saddam. In the end, therefore, I had to decide. We had 250,000 UK and US troops down there. It was a tough decision, it was a difficult one. I don't expect people, everyone, to agree with it. I don't disrespect people who take a completely different point of view. But I'm afraid this was an occasion, there was no fence to sit on, I had to take the decision and I took it.

Now, Jonathan, before I was a politician, I was the chief prosecutor for England and Wales for five years, prosecuting all of the cases in our criminal courts in England and Wales. but I had to deal with things like terrorism, where there were very serious gangs running terrorism, sometimes in this country, which we had to smash, but also involving countries in Europe. We put together law enforcement, police, security and intelligence teams, and we took those gangs down. These were very sophisticated terrorist gangs. There are six other party leaders on this platform tonight. They may all look different, but actually on some of the big issues that affect this country, they're very much the same. All six of them support Britain's membership of the European Union and most of our laws being made somewhere else. And as a consequence of that, all of them support open door immigration. Is it any wonder that trust in politics has broken down to the extent that it has? Well, I represent UKIP and we believe in Britain and we believe this country should be a self-governing nation. We believe we're good enough to do that. And we also believe that open-door immigration has depressed the wages for ordinary people, made buying houses for youngsters very difficult, made it tough to get a GP appointment and not been good for this country. We have a positive alternative. Let's have a trade deal with Europe. Let's cooperate with them as friends, but make our own laws. And let's take back control of our borders and put in place an Australian-style points system so we can choose the quantity and quality of who comes to Britain. By doing that, we'll give ordinary working people an even break.
Thank you. Thank you very much.
Richard Sarnes.

Yes, that's exactly what I'm saying. On what grounds? On the grounds that I've just given, that Saddam Hussein was a threat to the peace of the region, was a threat to wider peace. He had tried to get weapons of mass destruction in the past and used them. He would have tried again, and he was in breach of several United Nations Security Council resolutions. That you'll have to ask the Attorney General. No, but you think. You're a lawyer, you've been talking about this all day. Yes, and the Attorney General recognises that there's... No WMD and you can still go to war against Saddam. Yes, I think so. The Attorney General recognises that there's more than one view and that's the view I take. Isn't that a regime change war rather than anything to do with the United Nations? Well, it's regime change plus because I also... It's a great question, Shirley, and let me answer it directly. Yes, there's this rule that Shirley's referring to called the non-DOM rule. Now some of you may know about it, some of you may not, but basically it means you can live here, work here, be permanently settled here, but not pay taxes here. It's been in place, believe it or not, for 200 years. That's 40 Prime Ministers. I'm going to get rid of it. I'm going to get rid of it because I believe in a country where there's one rule for all, not one rule for the rich and powerful and another rule for everybody else. Now David Cameron wants to defend that rule. Now I think he's wrong about that. I think we've got to tackle tax avoidance wherever we find it and make people at the top live up to their responsibilities.

So David Cameron and I, together actually in coalition government, legislated for the circumstances in which a referendum will take place. So you're happy with that? Well, I'll come to, I am happy with that. He appears to have changed his mind. I think it is perfectly sensible to say to you, in law, that in future if your powers, if the The sovereignty of our nation is in any way shared or pooled with the European Union. At that point it should be your choice whether we carry on in the European Union or not. It shouldn't be the choice of the Parliament or the government of the day. We shouldn't be able to instead give your powers away behind your back. That is what we legislated for in 2011 and there are stirring speeches by William Hague and David Cameron in the House of Commons saying this is the right approach. Within months The ink was barely dry on the legislation. The Conservatives have now changed their mind again, and again, and again, and again. But I remain of the view, as I always have done, is that we should have a referendum on whether we should stay in or leave the European Union when new powers are given up to the European Union. And I will, by the way, again, shock horror, always argue that we remain part of the European Union, flawed though it is, because I think if we quit, we become poorer, unemployment goes up, and investment in our country goes down.
And I recognise the job that you do.

They didn't freak out. No, but they didn't launch a national election campaign 36 hours later either. They had a more decent period of paternity leave than was available to me, unfortunately. However, what we're basically saying in this campaign as Liberal Democrats, we want a fair tax system and we want the proceeds of that tax applied fairly in the country. We're saying that if you want to get rid of student top-up fees and tuition fees, If you want older people to enjoy free long-term care if they are suffering from Alzheimer's or Parkinson's with washing, feeding or dressing, and if you want to put money in to get rid of the council tax and replace it with a fair system of local tax based on people's ability to pay, a 1% number of people, the 1% top income taxpayers, to achieve all those things, they would see their top rate of tax go up to 50p for every pound above £100,000 income. That's still less than the same people were paying. For most of the period, Mrs Thatcher was Prime Minister and the top rate of taxpayers felt they had quite a good deal with her. James Gray, does that explain it?
This is the backlog of claims that you're dealing with. And these are the people who are being removed from the country.
To tell you.

This is really getting desperate. No, I'm not advocating entry into the Euro, I'd only advocate it, by the way, if ever, if the economic conditions were right, if it was good for your jobs, good for pensions, good for savings, and, of course, it always has to be only decided, if we were to do that as a country, on a referendum where you can vote on it. Can I just go back to the issue which was at hand which was the banks. Gordon Brown talks about waiting for the rest of the world to catch up and impose a levy. I think we need to impose a 10%, a 10% levy on the profits of the banks now. And I'll tell you why. Because under our tax system in this country, the banks can offset the tax they're supposed to pay against the losses they've already made. That means that, for some banks, they're basically not paying any tax at all, even though we bailed them out, and the only tax they would pay would be this 10% levy we say should be on their profits. That's the way forward. If we wait for the rest world to catch up, we will never, ever get round to doing this at all.
Shameful.

I have to say, David Cameron's the most creative justification I've ever heard for giving tax breaks to double millionaires. But, anyway, there you go. Look, I think Adina's point is that taxes are unfair on millions of people on ordinary incomes, not the double millionaires that David Cameron wants to help. Millions of ordinary people are simply struggling to pay the fuel bills, to pay the petrol prices, to pay the weekly shopping bills. What I'm... I'm totally with you on this, Adina. I think it's just wrong, let's say you are a teaching assistant on £10,000 a year. At the moment, you will pay, maybe you work three days a week. You will pay about £1,000 of that in tax and national insurance. Under our plan, by lifting the income tax threshold to £10,000, you won't pay any income tax on that first £10,000. I believe that if people work hard, particularly if they want to get off benefits and start working, even if it's just part-time, we should help them keep more of their money. It is as simple as that. That is the fair thing to do.

I'm happy. We've had figures out today that show that visa applications have fallen by 30% and the OBR's own forecasts say that we will halve immigration by next summer. That's the trajectory we are on. And we will give Parliament the chance to set an annual cap on family and work visas. That will be enshrined, a legal cap, And that is what we are offering. That's why you can have confidence that those numbers have come down. They're projected to come down. And the only alternative you have in this election is that or what the Labour Party have on offer. Nigel is a Labour enabler. Nigel, do you want to come back? And he is a enabler.

Well, Gary, of course they will. And I come from a family of immigrants. My parents emigrated here. I'm living, breathing proof that this is a welcoming, meritocratic, and tolerant society. But it's absolutely vital that we have control over our immigration system. And after we leave the EU, we can replace the free movement of people with an Australian-style points-based system, which allows us to decide who's coming here based on what they have to offer, not just where they happen to live as currently. Will immigration be higher or lower? Immigration will be lower, but the alternative to all of this, the alternative to control, leaving the EU and having control over immigration, is unlimited, uncontrolled immigration offered by the Labour Party.

Right. One, we will negotiate a credible leave deal with the European Union. Let me finish, please. I'm trying to answer the gentleman's question. Secondly, we will put that alongside remain in a referendum. My role and the role of our government will be to ensure that that referendum is held in a fair atmosphere, and we will abide by the result of it. and I will adopt, as Prime Minister if I am at the time, a neutral stance so that I can credibly carry out the results of that to bring our communities and country together rather than continuing an endless debate about the EU and Brexit. This will be a trade deal with Europe or remaining in the EU. That will be the choice that we put before the British people within six months. Any other option will require years of negotiations either with the EU or the USA and put our public services at risk.

Well, first I'd say, sir, with due respect, the election isn't over. I'm on here to try to persuade people in this audience and across the country viewing it to vote Liberal Democrat. Because I think we've got great ideas. And I think we've been getting great responses. Are you trying to persuade people that you can be Prime Minister? Listen, I'm realistic about our chances, right? We've had three very bad elections. Everyone knows that. And when I became leader, I said my top priority was to defeat as many Conservative MPs as possible. So do you think you can or cannot be Prime Minister? We might as well hear it from you. We know what the polls say. It will be challenging given the polls. OK. But I don't want to put a ceiling on our ambition. And, you know, this is a bit of time to go.
Sure.

What we can do - well, let me deal with that. I'm, I can tell you, as I just have done, with certainty what we're going to do in our first budget, and we're going to cut taxes by four billion pounds in our first budget. Labour, by contrast, all the independent commentators say, will have to increase taxes by ten or eleven billion pounds. But if you're asking me what I'm - I'll be able to do three or four years out, then in truth, although we've spelled out our plans, although our plans certainly don't need us to increase taxes at all, I can't foresee exactly what the position is going to be in three or four years time. I, I - there may be unforeseen events.

Well, first of all, in terms of European immigration, we celebrate the free movement of people in the EU. Well, not to give advice to Gareth Southgate, who, when I met him, we did discuss who had the worst job in Britain in terms of other people giving you their opinions. But, Isa, you need to have a clear plan and you need to take bold action, because you don't do anything without those things.

I honestly didn't think that we had a chance of winning that election. So you said it anyway. I wanted to campaign for our colleagues. And I then, once we got the result that we did, which were the results that I expected that we might get, as I say, I decided to step up to lead the party, not something I had planned to do when I came into Parliament, and to change it and to put it back in the service of working people. The reason I'm... And we will move on. It's just it's a trust issue, right? Because... Well, look, let me deal with this issue. I worked... I went to Northern Ireland, worked with the police service over there. I said I would work with the team to change it, and we did. I then ran the Crown Prosecution Service. I was the chief prosecutor for five years. I said we would change the Crown Prosecution Service, make it better, particularly on violence against women and girls. I changed it, we did it. I said I would change the Labour Party four and a half years ago. Most people said to me, you can't do it. I did. So I stand on my record.

Rishi Singh. Well, our view is very straightforward. Three years ago, the British people voted to leave the EU. It's time we respected that and actually left. And with a Conservative government, we can do that within weeks. With our new deal that we've already negotiated, we can leave by the end of January. But whether you voted leave or remain, it's time for us as a country to move forward. not remain stuck in neutral. So rather than spending years more going round in circles with more referendums, Jeremy Corbyn propped up by the SNP, let's just get Brexit done.

Uncertain times call for a clear plan and bold action. So I'll keep this simple. In five weeks, either Keir Starmer or I will be Prime Minister. Beyond raising your taxes and raiding your pensions, no-one knows what Labour would actually do. But you know what I would do? I'll cut your taxes, protect your pension, and reduce immigration. I have a clear plan for a more secure future for you and your family.
How are you feeling? Got a lot of work to do. I know we've got to earn every vote and I won't stop working until 10 o'clock on Thursday week.
Thank you, Nigel Farage.

I want to govern and change the country. And I think, I've just got to say to you, the stakes are incredibly high. You get this choice once every five years. because we had the Attorney General there at Cabinet, and he was able to tell people his opinion, explain his reasoning, answer any questions that they had. And in the end, remember, for the past few days, it's been said that the Attorney General advised that it was illegal to go to war. He didn't. He advised it was lawful. Now, I totally understand why there will be people in this audience tonight who very, very strongly disagree with my decision to take this country to war in Iran. OK. All I can say to you is this. It's not a matter of the Attorney-General's advice, because it's been shown that he advised it was lawful. Neither is the matter of misusing the intelligence. There have been four inquiries into that. It is, however, a question of a difficult decision I had to take. Was it better to leave Saddam in power or put him in prison? I think it was better to put him in prison.

First of all, we're not doing any deals with any other parties. I'm not trying to form a coalition government. I'm fighting this election to win it for Labour, to win it for the people of this country, to end universal credit, deal with the issues of inequality, injustice and poverty, and deliver an economy that absolutely works for all. And our taxation proposals will affect the richest 5% of the people in this country. We will end the marriage tax allowance because I think it's discriminatory against non-married couples and it ought to be treated equally across the society and individual taxation is a fairer way of doing things. Our manifesto, fully prepared, fully costed, I think this is an opportunity for our country to come together, deal with the inequality, injustice and poverty and actually go forward to the future with some real degree of hope. Thank you very much.

Well, we're saying 25% of people, and this is the Institute of Fiscal Studies that's looked at this. 25% of people are going to contribute more than they do under the Council Tax. 50% of households, will be better off, 25% will be unaffected. Now that's being very direct because the power to tax, and the power to make everybody better off, is not a power known to politicians in all of history, and we're not trying to hoodwink people on that.

Well, this has gone on for too long. We've been stuck in this gridlock and this paralysis for years. We want to move forward. And our deal does that. If we get a majority Conservative government, we will bring that deal back to Parliament, we will vote on it before Christmas, and we will leave before the end of January. And then, as a country, we can move forward. and get on with all the other things that we want to do. Investing in the NHS, police and sport. We can't do that. Can I just finish, Nick? A vote for anyone else on this stage is just going to prolong this gridlock, this delay, this division in our society. We need to move the country forward.

Well let me answer that very directly. I made the promise and outside the EU we did get immigration down. I now need to make these benefit changes, these welfare changes, the things I've just described, and I need to negotiate those in Europe. Now I would argue I've got a track record of that. You'll get that in-out referendum, you the British people deciding, before the end of 2017.

Well, Emma, the situation in Gaza is intolerable. It's catastrophic. tens of thousands of people have been killed, a disproportionate number of women and children. So, Omar, what we need now is a ceasefire, a cessation of the hostilities straight away. And for these purposes, which is really important because it's a very grave situation, firstly, we have to get the hostages out. They've been held for a very, very long time, and I dread to think that the state they're in. Secondly, we desperately need humanitarian aid into Gaza. Desperately, it's catastrophic. Thirdly, and this is where the leadership piece comes in, we have to find a path to a lasting resolution. That has to be a two-state solution, and we need to show leadership on that. And I know that if we're elected into office, if we're humble enough to serve, that will be my solemn duty. Thank you. Rishi Sunak.

I think I want one. I guess you want Bethlehem what everyone wants. This is just an immigration system which works. We've had an immigration system which has been absolutely shambolic. No idea who's coming in, who's going out. No idea, you know, who's been living here for many, many years. Now, you know, Gordon Brown talks about amnesty. The only person actually in British politics who's advocating a blanket amnesty. I'm not. It's Boris Johnson, the Conservative Mayor for London. What I'm saying is you can't live in denial of a problem which has been created by the chaos in the immigration system in the past. You can wish away, if either of you wish, that there are people who've been living here for a long time in the shadows of our economy. I want to go after the criminal gangs who are exploiting those people, and if there are those people who've lived here for 10 years, speak English, want to play by the rules, want to pay their taxes, why don't we say to them, okay, you come out of the shadows, pay your taxes, do some community service to make up for the fact that you came here illegally, and then we can free up resources to go after the criminal gangs. I think that is the right balance between fairness but also making sure that we have a system which works.
As a proportion of income? Yes. Of national income?

Let me be really clear about tax, because this has been a big issue in the last three weeks. So let me be absolutely clear. All of our plans are fully costed, fully funded. We will not raise tax on working people. That means no tax rises for income tax, for national insurance, for VAT. Our manifesto comes out tomorrow. It's a very forward-looking manifesto, but there's nothing in our manifesto that requires us to raise tax. Swerve the top 5% of earners there. No, no, no. Nothing requires us to raise tax. And I tell you... OK. No, no, I do want to... You'll challenge me and I want to deal with this. I want to do things differently. I want to grow our economy. OK. I accept that previous Labour leaders have sort of pulled the tax lever every single time and driven up spending. I want to grow our economy. The manifesto tomorrow will be a manifesto, a plan for wealth creation. Now, you may not hear a Labour leader say that very often, but for me, that is the most important thing. Therefore, I'm not going to do what other Labour leaders have done and be tempted to simply say every time we're going to pull the tax lever. We're not going to do that.
I, I believe that we do need to bring powers back from ¿

Yes, and the numbers are too high. I'm not making any bones about that. The numbers are too high, Beth. But what I can tell you is, what I've done in the time I've had this job is already start to bring them down because we've taken action to change things. So last year, the numbers were down by 10% already. And this year, the number of visas that we've issued is down by around a quarter. And the forecast show that we're on track to halve net migration in just over a year's time. So that shows and hopefully gives people some confidence we're now heading in the right direction. To give you an example of some of the things we've done, we've clamped down on abuse of the social care visa, tightened up the number of dependents that students can bring, and also raised the salary so that you have to earn £38,500 to come here on a work visa. Now, these measures are now starting to have an impact. You've seen that this year, as I said, numbers down by a quarter, forecast to fall by half overall for net migration.
I didn't hear it.

Yes, they were down last year by a third. They're down over the last 12 months by a little less than that. This year, there's what we've seen is one particular country, Vietnam, accounting for the majority of the increase. But actually, the plans that we've put in place last year show that it is possible to make a difference to this problem, which, by the way, before I got this job, everybody said was impossible. Everybody said it was impossible to get the numbers down at all. But we showed last year that that was possible. And now we have a clear plan to put a deterrent in place.

Joan, thank you for your question. I've changed Labour's approach on immigration because I don't think it's prejudice to worry about immigration. I think people's concerns are real and they've got to be dealt with, and I want to explain how we'll deal with them. If I'm elected as Prime Minister, We'll put in place new rules which say that if you come to this country, you won't get benefits for at least the first two years. And we'll also do something else, which I think is of a concern to many people in our country. We'll stop the undercutting of wages and conditions that happen so often. Employers exploiting migrant labour, not paying the minimum wage, recruiting only from abroad to undercut wages. There have been just two prosecutions for failure to pay the minimum wage in the last five years. We've got to deal with it, and if I'm Prime Minister, I will. There's one other thing I'll say to you, though. If you want a party that will cut Britain off from the rest of the world, that's not me. I believe we've got to play our role in the world. But if you want a party with controls on immigration, that's what I offer.
Yes, sorry.

If, in the EU referendum, the final say referendum that we have, the people vote to remain, then free movement in the EU will continue. If people vote to take the credible leave option, then a new migration policy would need to be negotiated. That would be fair and reasonably managed and put the needs of our economy on our society first but also would show that we value migrants, we don't scapegoat them, we don't demonise them and we don't criticise them in order to try and get votes.
The police have done a brilliant job. I'm glad you are.

Angela Rayner, whilst I pick my cards up. Thank you. Well, actually, in our first steps, we talk about a border force command so that we can deal with the crossings and people that are putting their lives at risk. Because the Rwanda scheme will never work. It's always been a gimmick. We would put that money into tackling the gangs. And Keir Starmer said today, he's worked on counterterrorism with overseas before. We can do it again. These gangs that are putting people's lives at risk should not be carrying on in the way they are.

Well, I disagree with that. You disagree with the IFS? Well, I'd like to see their workings. I can show them ours. We showed that last year, if we'd had this share buyback scheme, it would have raised 2.3 billion, actually. And we've allowed for behavioural change. And in fact, what we're looking at is what President Joe Biden did in the United States. They raised a huge amount of money for this approach. So we can raise money. We've got other sources of raising money. We've got a proposal, for example, to make sure that there's a mental health expert in every primary and secondary school to deal with the problems of mental health for our children and young people. And we've said that the digital service tax on the social media giants and Amazon and Google, that should go up to raise the money. But Nushka, this is really important.
Well, you'll be really shocked by this.

Well, I completely agree with Gordon Brown about the bravery of our forces. I mean, I've been to Afghanistan four times. Every time, you are just blown away by the professionalism of these people. I actually, to prepare for this debate, I went for a run this morning with someone who just got back from Afghanistan. I have to say, I couldn't really keep up, but he was someone who'd served for six months and had done an incredible job there. But just going back to this point about how we get these things right, one of the things that strikes you when you go to Afghanistan is that actually we're not getting the political situation right. The south of the country is where the Taliban grew, where they came from. That is the Pashtun part of the country. And yet when you see the Afghan National Army, it is dominated by people from other parts of the country. And there is a really big political issue there. We've got to make sure the whole country in Afghanistan feels it's part of the Afghan government. And that's absolutely key for making sure we can bring our soldiers back home. Not just training the Afghan army and police, as Gordon has said, vital though that is. We need a political settlement as well to help make sure we can come home.
We've got a huge opportunity.
Yeah, but let me, let me explain something to you.

Keir Starmer, briefly... Game plan, good squad, and he's got both of those. I really think England are going to do really well this year. He's got a fantastic team and I'm really... Are you radical or do you play it safe? Sorry? You need a strategy for winning, so it depends who you're playing, what the issue is. That's what Gareth does. He's very, very good at this. And he's built a squad. OK. And just like I've got a brilliant Cabinet.
They're both as bad as each other. They're both socialism, high taxes, big state, mass immigration. We're all going to get poorer.
And I also said that the sky wouldn't fall in if we left the European Union. So you have changed your mind, have you? We gave people the choice.

But you can hear there is a difference, there's a real difference between us. And the reason about this European party is I just think it's the hypocrisy that people are fed up with of British politicians standing here in Bristol saying I'm going to stand up for us in Europe and we shouldn't give away all these powers and we should fight for British interests and then over they go to Brussels and they do exactly the opposite. I want to make sure that we say the same thing when we're in Bristol. about wanting to be in Europe but not run by Europe, wanting to have a single market that's good for our country, wanting to trade with Europe, but not go over to Brussels and say something different. It's that that saves our politics.
I love this issue.
No, you can't have in the same way, Fiona.
I can't give you a precise figure. Less than a hundred and fifty thousand. We will consult

Those. Well and actually I didn't, if, if you remember we had precisely the same discussion before, and not just in relation to National Insurance, but other taxes too. And what I said to you then, and I say it to you now, is the spending proposals that we have are adequately catered for, by the tax plans that we've got.
I just don't think people can trust you if you're continually changing your mind.
No.
As I say, no deal would be better than a bad deal.

Yeah, well, this is a poster, you know, it's about immigration. It's a real picture. It was in The Guardian in October 2015, I believe. People have real concerns about the level of immigration that we've had over the last 20 years. If I can answer, I will finish if you'd let me, you did ask me a question. We need to address the rapid mass immigration that we've had over the years of Blair, Brown, Cameron and May, which is far, far higher than it was before. People are concerned about the effects on integration in our country. People are also concerned about the effects it's having on housing and on our public services, on our hospitals being overstretched, schools being But there's a big political question here. But people do want it to be brought back into balance.

No, because I don't think that if you are in the serious business of politics, and whether you agree with me or not in this room or watching this programme, What you do want from your politicians, I think, is a consistency of principle and values. And you can't, by definition in my view, be consistent and principled about your values and your approach if you're willing to shuffle around with them. What do you do in Scotland? In Scotland, the difference is you have a voting system which recognises that in Scotland... Why not jump out of the voting system in Westminster and start doing exactly what you do in Scotland, negotiating, make conditions, do deals, why not?
It's OK, we're out of time. Because otherwise, you simply will be in the same position.
Well, I'll tell you.
Thank you.
Definitely not, under any circumstance. Richard Tice.
Yes.

This is real... I'm sorry to say, and with great respect, this is complete Bermuda Triangle stuff, there is no... As is the suggestion... If that's the case. As is the suggestion that the referendum, when 17.4 million people voted to leave, was somehow false, vitiated, not fair, wrong, and should now be cancelled. I don't think that is true. My strong view is that the people of this country voted to leave. they've waited three and a half years to get that done. I think at least the Liberal Democrats are saying scrub the referendum. That's my impression. Aren't they saying revoke Article 50?
Well you can't obviously be certain about the development of the security situation in Iraq, but you can be certain that there's a UN mandate that runs out and...
Oh, I very much hope so yes, that's my intention.
But we would put it in to practise.
Are you disagreeing with the IFS, Angela?
...that he wasn't originally in favour, er, he's developed his view and that's something that we have to take account of but we...
And it wasn't tripled, and we didn't triple tuition fees, there were 1,000 at Labour.

Ed Miliband. You've heard tonight from five different parties, but there's one fundamental choice at this election. Do we carry on with a conservative plan that says we should put the richest and most powerful first in our country? or do we choose a different idea? I believe it's time to put working families first again in our country, putting your living standards first, having more doctors and nurses in our National Health Service, having a future for our young people, all underpinned by cutting the deficit every year and balancing the books. Now, David Cameron refused to come and debate tonight, but I've got a message for him. David, if you think this election is about leadership, then debate me one on one. I believe my ideas, my vision for the country is better for the working families of Britain. If you disagree, then prove it. Debate me and let the people decide.

Well, the first thing I'd say is the Tories are in no position to lecture anyone about tax rises. We've got the highest tax burden. For 70 years. And I think that working people shouldn't pay more tax and we will not raise their tax. OK. What we need to do is get the economy going. There's a reason that taxes have gone up so much in recent years, and that's because the economy is flatlining. We have to address that problem. And tomorrow, our manifesto is a plan for growth, a detailed plan of the first steps we will do in government to get our economy working so that living standards go up and we have the money we need for our public services.

Well, in relation to taxation, I do need to be really clear. We are not going to increase income tax, national insurance or VAT. And the reason for that is because I think with the tax burden being as high as it is and a cost of living crisis, that is the wrong thing to do, particularly for working people. So we're not going to do that. We will put some immediate money into our public services. I ran a public service for five years. I believe in our public services and I know they need to be properly funded so we will put in money to the NHS straight away and the purpose of that will be to allow us to do 40,000 extra appointments and operations every week That's 2 million a year extra appointments to bring the waiting list down, because it's nearly 8 million now and we've got to get it down. And what about defence? And that will allow the NHS to be properly, you know, to get back on its feet. We'll have to then make it fit for the future. And what about defence? That's also in the question. Where are you going to find the money for that if you don't increase streams of taxation? Defence has to be the first priority of any government defending your country, and therefore we will always make the money available for defence. And you can see at the moment we live in a particularly volatile world. So we will make the money available for defence, we will put that extra money into the NHS, and I want to see our public services running properly. And my wife works in the NHS, my mum worked in the NHS, my sister worked in the NHS, and to see it in the state that it's in now, where I say it's on its knees, my wife says no it's not, it's flat on its face. is a complete disgrace. After 14 years of this government, we will have to pick it up, put it back on its feet, but also do something else, reform, to make sure that it's fit for the future. That will require a different model, a preventative model. I'll just give this one last example. Very brief, if you want, because there are a lot of hands up. I went to Alder Hey Children's Hospital. Some people will know it. It's a brilliant children's hospital. in Liverpool, where I saw heart surgery, or the wards where they do it, on children aged 0-2. It was very humbling and uplifting, but I also discovered that the single most common reason that 6-10 year olds are admitted to the children's hospital there, same across the country, is to have their teeth taken out because of teeth decay. That is a complete and utter disgrace. So we need to move the model and change the model for the NHS as well. Thank you.

I don't say there is no difference between Ed Miliband and David Cameron, I say there is not a big enough difference between Ed Miliband and David Cameron. Charlotte's question was rightly about the next generation. We have experts saying that if we continue with austerity cuts, by 2020 there are going to be one million more children across the UK living in poverty. That would be a betrayal of the next generation. What I am proposing is responsible, modest spending increases that still gets the deficit down, because yes, that is important, but doesn't do it by heaping more and more pain onto the backs of the people who can least afford it. And I want to say to Ed, We share a desire to see the back of the Tories, but surely we don't want to replace the Tories with Tory light.

Thank you. Sian Berry. I understand completely the motivation behind the question, because we're all really just hoping that the country will stop being so divided. And I have to say, I really, truly believe that the best way to finish off Brexit, to finish off this process, is with more democracy, with a people's vote. And I think, I mean, looking around this panel, we've got a majority of parties here who are committed to putting back the Brexit question in a people's vote. I do believe that's the best way to finish it off. And I do believe that Brexit would be a disaster. The reason I want to get a people's vote done is so we can get back to fixing the country, to dealing with the problems that austerity caused. And I don't think we can trust the Conservatives with another vote in this election, another government, because they've had three years, they've made a hash of Brexit, and they cannot be trusted again. Thank you. Nigel Farage.

Well, we are quite far down the road towards the Misogyny Act, actually informed by the work of Helena Kennedy, who's a Labour peer in the House of Lords. And it's a huge issue, and it reflects wider society, the misogyny that we see there. And some of the stuff that's happened in the Met, which people will be familiar with, is not unknown in Scotland as well. And it doesn't just affect women and girls or members of the public, it affects women and girls, well, women in particular, in the police force. So you have to have that transparency that's been talked about. You have to have proper vetting. The vetting has not been good, and even finding out a police officer that served with another police force, what their background is, you have to know that, and you have to have the accountability, which means that when somebody is accused of something, there's a proper process that people can have faith in. So there is a huge job to do in terms of rebuilding trust across the country. The same will be true in Scotland as well. But the root of it is the misogyny, which is evident in wider society, and that's why I'm very proud that Scotland's taking the lead in terms of misogyny.

Mr Brown? Well, we've got to be clear about this. I, too, want freedom for schools, and that's why, where a school is failing, it will be taken over by a federation or by an academy so that it can work, usually a local federation, a local academy. I want the best discipline in our schools as well, and I'm tough on what headteachers have got to do to ensure that there is discipline not just in the school itself, but around the school as well. Mr Clegg? We've got to face up to this fact about spending.

We are going to find £1 out of every 100 that the government spends and save that in each of the next two years. We've done it for five years, and if we do it for two more, combined with the extra taxes in terms of tax avoidance and tax evasion, we will eliminate the deficit. But we'll do it without reaching into working people's pockets and taking their money, which is what Ed Miliband is recommending.
Yes, you can. I don't think there's a thing as a hard Brexit or a soft Brexit. I think it's a good Brexit. And what Theresa May needs is a mandate.

The Canny Scots have paid more tax per head of population to the Treasury in every single year for the last 34 years, that is the reality. The question for Ed Miliband, Ed talks the language of anti-austerity but it is only a few weeks since Ed Miliband tripped through the lobbies of the House of Commons with Nick Clegg and David Cameron to vote for £30 billion of cuts over the next two years. I take a different view to that. I don't believe you can simply cut your way out of deficit. I think David Cameron has proved that. He's missed all of his borrowing targets. We need to invest and grow our way out of the deficit. We've got experts saying that austerity has held back economic growth.
