So let's have spending increases, modest spending increases, that allow us to invest in the things that matter.

I don't accept that. Nigel is basically saying, give up before you've begun. In fact, if you look at my track record on Europe, I said... I have. ..we cut the European budget. People said it was impossible. We cut that European budget. I said, let's get out of these bailout funds where British taxpayers' money was being put into countries like Greece. People said, you'll never do that. We got out of those Greek bailout funds. So, instead of giving up, let's get stuck in and negotiate. And the problem with Nigel, in the end, is, ultimately, Nigel, you're just the back door to a Labour government, which will give us open-door immigration, which is not what this country wants.

And the NHS was in no way a part of that co-operation agreement because we do not agree with Labour's approach on the NHS in Wales. My job as an opposition member is to persuade them to make some bold decisions to take us on the right direction. But you know what? You need more than anything, Aishani, is assurance that sustainability will be built in for the workforce within the NHS. Workforce planning and giving you the support through your pay packet, one, but all those other means that show that you are valued as a health worker. OK. Nigel Farage.

We don't want Scotland to be left behind in these things, but we want to invest more in public transport. In answer to your question earlier on, do people have to fly less? Yes, I think we do, where we can. Something like half of all international flights are taken by 10% of the population. So we have to invest more. We are investing half a billion pounds to improve bus infrastructure. We need to get the rail services. better. Where flights are necessary, in a country like Scotland, sometimes they are, we have set a target to decarbonise all flights within Scotland by 2040. We have already one of the most comprehensive electric vehicle charging networks anywhere in Europe with a target of phasing out... Are you beseeching people to fly less or are you going to tax them to make sure they fly less? The UK already has one of the highest levels of air passenger duty. My party had a long-standing commitment to cut that, and we decided we couldn't do that, in all conscience, because of the climate emergency. So, you know, I think we need to make sure the alternatives are there and encourage people. But on all of this discussion about individual behaviour, important though it is, somebody made a point about the small number of companies responsible for the majority of emissions. We've got to make sure that industry and government are leading by example.

Tonight's debate is about you: about your job, the taxes you pay, your family, about the prosperity of our economy. We need to do things differently to to build a new, stronger and fairer economy. The way they got us into this mess is not the way out. We need to be frank about the cuts that will be needed, so we can protect things like schools and hospitals. We'll need to break up our banking system so that irresponsible bankers can never again put your savings and your businesses at risk. We have to rediscover our passion for innovation, for building things, not just placing bets on the money markets, and we need fair taxes, so that you don't pay any income tax on your first £10,000. Of course, they'll tell you tonight that these things can't be done. I think we've got to do things differently, to deliver the fairness, the prosperity, and the jobs that you and your families deserve.

I'm disappointed that Adam is playing the Tory game by trying to blame Labour for the Tory-created Brexit crisis. This isn't about not taking a position. This is about taking the adult position. In our country, there are families with different views on the question of Brexit. There's people at work, work colleagues with different positions on this. If we want to bring people together, I think we need less of the finger-pointing, ranting and electioneering. And we need to say, let the people have the final say, and a Prime Minister, Jeremy Corbyn, will implement whatever the people say. That's democratic. That's going to bring people together. I don't understand why people have such a problem with it.

All I would appeal for is just a bit of honesty in this debate. I mean, people know that money is tight. People know that you can't promise something for nothing. You can't say you're going to fill the deficit tomorrow and you're going to give lots and lots of tax breaks to people, inheritance tax breaks for double millionaires, tax breaks for, what is it, one in three hand-picked married couples, and also extra, extra, extra money to the NHS without explaining how you're going to do it.

Well, the only way to end austerity is by getting a Labour government. And we need to end austerity in order to properly fund our public services. OK, we'll leave that question mark hanging in the air.

I think we should always listen very carefully to what the police and security services say. But the police and security services already have fairly wide-ranging powers. I listened to some of your conversations with Tim Farron.

Well... LAUGHTER This is why the Prime Minister made the very clear pledges that he did, and you can measure the progress being made against them. We are making progress in this country. We have been through some very hard times, but the recovery is there. What we must ensure is that that is not choked off. And we must ensure that your taxes are being lowered. That is the pledge that we make to you. And that is what we have been doing with national insurance cuts that we have seen this year. That is what you need. It's not just your taxes that I'm worried about. I'm worried about my constituents being able to afford a Labour government. Angela mentioned GB Energy. Do you know what the GB stands for? It stands for Giant Bills. And more bills are coming with the net zero plans that Labour have. That is the most important thing because everything depends on you having that disposable income. And that is why tax is at the heart of this. The pledges that we are making to you on cutting your taxes and what Labour will not tell you about the taxes they are going to be putting up.

Well, we said that the most important task for the government was to get the economy growing, was to get jobs growing, and was to get the deficit down. And I would say on any analysis, and we've had to take, we had an appalling inheritance. We inherited a situation where Britain's budget deficit was forecast to be bigger than Greece's. Now, we had to take difficult decisions. I will defend all of those decisions. Difficult ones, ones that were hard to take, but the right thing for the country. And the result, there's a connection between the difficult decisions we had to take and the fact that we now have the fastest growing economy of any major Western economy. We've created 2 million private sector jobs. We've got 750,000 more businesses. The British economy is working. Now we need to make sure that people can... What I want is an economy that doesn't just look good on the page. I want people to feel that they can get a job, they can have a livelihood, they can buy a home, they can get a good school place for their children, they can enjoy a retirement. Those are the things. But that's what this plan, Jeremy, is all about.

question to Rishi. Say the Tories get their way, I hope they don't, the UK is out, nobody but nobody thinks a trade deal can be negotiated and agreed by the end of next year. That's nonsense, we do. We don't trust you, sorry. I don't trust you. I don't trust you because you don't trust democracy. You don't trust 17.4 million people. 17.4 million people. The Brexit party have got a big responsibility for the mess the UK is in right now. And we're very proud of what we've achieved. He's proud of the mess he's achieved in the UK. But can I finish my question? We haven't achieved the mess.

Good leaders don't run away from a debate. Theresa May undoubtedly should be here. Whatever we have discussed this evening, her absence is undoubtedly the shadow that hangs over this election. How dare you call a general election and then run away from the debate? We are all products of our upbringing. So I was brought up in Preston during the 1980s. And I saw people around me, what happens when a society, a community, is taken for granted, in that case, by a conservative government with a colossal majority. Half my mates' parents out at work at any given time. And I am determined to build a country for our kids, mine included, where people are decent to one another, where people have enough. We have a national health service that is properly funded. And that means being honest with the British people, saying we will put a penny on income tax to have the best health service in the world. It means stopping Theresa May's plans to do to your kids' schools what she's currently doing to our hospitals. And it also means if you want to lead the people, you have to kind of like them as well and spend time amongst them. And if you trust the people, then you do not impose that Brexit deal on the British people without them having the final say. Either the politicians will sign it off or the people will. Well, I trust the people.

OK, don't give up on me quite yet, Angus. It's only halfway through the debate. Theresa may not be here, but I hope to make a good fist of setting out the Conservative Party policy. We've made a clear decision to make sure that we protect the poorest in our society. Which means that the pensioners will be protected, that winter fuel payment won't be paid to millionaires. No, it'll be focused. So where will the levels be put then? Where will the levels be put? Under a Labour government, the pensioners saw a rise in their pensions of 85p in one year. Under this government, we've seen pensioners' pay rise by over £1,000.

What we want to do in UKIP is we want to put more money in people's pockets. We know that there's been a stagnation of living standards in recent years and how we will put money in people's pockets is firstly we would scrap VAT on domestic fuel bills. We would also scrap the green levies, which will save the average household £170 every single year. We'd increase the personal allowance, so you would have to be earning over £13,000 before you paid any tax. That would put more money in your pocket. UKIP will also oppose any rise in national insurance, because we believe as a party that people know how to spend their own money better than any government does on their behalf. We're a party of a small state. We believe that people know what best to do with their own money.

in any meaningful way? Obviously, it's my responsibility, because I'm the one in charge, and I've been very clear. We haven't made as much progress on waiting lists as I would have liked. I mean, you talked about the pandemic. It's, I think, important for everyone to just understand the scale of what happened. During the Covid years, six million referrals that normally would have happened didn't happen. Just think about that, six million. Now, the NHS today, because of the money we've put in, the doctors and nurses we've hired, is doing more than it's ever done. It's working flat out, the amount of treatments, checks, test stands, operations, all of that is at record levels. But obviously, it is just a challenge to work through the fact that we've got this six million missed referrals that obviously now are coming into the system and we're catching up on. and it's going to take time to work through that. I'm not going to pretend it can be fixed overnight, but I can assure you that the NHS budget will continue to increase ahead of inflation in the next Parliament. It's already at record levels. We've got more doctors and nurses, and we're hiring more, and we're using new technology and new innovations to get you the health care that you need. So we're doing all the right things, but when you've had six million referrals that were missed and are going to take time to come back, it's just going to take a bit of time.

With greatest respect, that's not what we're doing. We're investing record sums into it. We're training more doctors and nurses than we ever have, and there are more doctors and nurses in the NHS today than it's also had. But we also need to do things differently. And it's whether it's things... And you'll know this from... And what's your particular specialty? I'm a physiologist in cardiology. And you're in cardiology, right. So you'll know that we're doing things like elective surgical hubs, virtual wards, community diagnostic centres, of which there's one right here in Grimsby. And those are all new innovations that you and your colleagues have pioneered, that we have supported, that will actually mean that people can get the care they need quicker and secure the NHS for the long term.

And you're going to get an answer. Look, I think the Conservative government's been a total disaster and a calamity. So it can't be out of office quick enough, in my view. I think it's an absolute racing certainty that the Labour Party will win the election in England, and I hope people in Scotland will recognise the importance of having strong SNP voices in the House of Commons who will argue for decisions to be made in Scotland for Scotland, and that will put the interests of people in Scotland first.

It's a question of wanting to get things in Europe for your country and standing up for your country. And one of the things the Liberal Democrats would want to do is actually take away Britain's seat on the United Nations Security Council and replace it with a European one. And that's one of the things that actually gives us the ability to punch above our weight in the world. It gives us influence in the world.

I would disagree mainly with my friend on the far right there. We already spend 6% of our budget on defence. I think that that proportion can be spent more wisely. I don't see the sense in spending £100 billion on replacing the Trident nuclear missile system, but I do think that there is a strong argument for making sure that the troops that we do have serving abroad are properly equipped and that they are looked after when they come home, because far too many of them end up on the streets as homeless or in prison as a result of not having proper aftercare. And Plaid Cymru has worked very hard in ensuring a strong deal for veterans through the work of my colleague Elvin Lloyd in Westminster.

It's a terrible choice facing the two main Westminster parties, a no-deal policy on the one hand and with the Labour Party, no policy at all. Here's a question, Richard, that possibly you can answer. You probably do admire Tony Benn. He used to talk about, didn't he, weathervane politicians that just go with the winds, et cetera, or the signpost politicians. He was actually clear. He was against the European Union. But what we've got in the Labour Party now are weathervane politicians. You won't even say, going into this general election, where you stand. You say you'll consult your members. You're not even able to be honest with the people. Where's the leadership in that when we're crying out for it in this country?

You supported the Health and Social Care Act, which is a recipe for privatisation in our NHS, but there's a bigger issue for people at home. And that's future plans for the NHS. I've set out where the money's coming from. David Cameron is planning to double the spending cuts next year and bigger cuts in the next three years. Think what that would mean, not just for the NHS, but social care. If you cut social care dramatically, as has happened in this Parliament, you undermine the NHS fundamentally, because elderly people don't get the help in their own home, they end up in hospital, and the NHS is creaking at the seams. That is what is happening now.

Well, what my manifesto... Well, right, I used that word trust, and I used it because that is actually what politicians, when we go out, when we ask people to vote for us, we are asking people to trust us in the role that we want to be voted into. If I look at our manifesto, what our manifesto has done is to be open with people about the great challenges that we believe that this country faces and that need to be addressed by whoever is in government. We've been open about that. I've also been open that there will be some hard choices to be made in addressing those various challenges. You talk about figures in our manifesto. Of course, we already have budgets that have been set out in the autumn statement as government and in the spring budget. And we've added some figures in various areas like our extra funding on the NHS and schools in the manifesto. But I think it's important that the next government sets out for people the really big issues that are going to have to be addressed by whoever is in government. And those are in our manifesto.

Unemployment, I mentioned wage levels, and I mentioned inflation. On unemployment, I was quoting the Office of Budget Responsibility forecast. Now, as it turned out, that forecast turned out to be wrong. But look, let's go to the big picture here. The big picture is of this election. Do we think the economy is fixed? David Cameron was on earlier. He says things are good, right? And things are fine. I don't think things are fine for the reasons that I set out in the town meeting earlier on. Because I think we are too much based on low wages. I think work is too insecure. I think our young people are burdened down by a lack of opportunity. And that is what's got to change about the country. What would you cut? Well, let me explain. We've said outside areas like education and health, key protected areas, there are going to be reductions in spending. And we've set out some of those cuts, like the winter fuel allowance for pensioners with incomes over £42,000 a year. We've talked about the ways in which we will cut money through efficiencies in the police, in local government. We've said, for example, that child benefit will be restricted to 1%. We said we'll reverse the... What does that come to? Well, the figure is at least hundreds of millions, more than a billion pounds. Hundreds of millions. But look, that's not the point. Because let me explain. We're going to have to make these decisions when we're in government. But I've got to set out an overall approach. And I have set out an overall approach. I've set out an overall approach which says, as I said earlier, it's about fair taxes. It's about, I'm a Labour leader going into the election saying we're going to reduce spending outside a small minority of areas. Tony Blair never went into an election saying that. What Tony Blair did is Tony Blair's affair, isn't it? No, it's a point about my approach, Jeremy. I'm not denying the need. I'm not denying the need. Would overall spending be greater? No, it would fall. You would spend less than the Tories. Would you borrow more than the Tories? There are going to be reductions outside the protected departments.

Angela, please take it. You want to put off the climate test if we're investing. And that's not going to work if you want to tackle climate change. Because we need people to be investing and businesses to be innovating to meet our obligations.

I'm absolutely dismayed by this. I mean, this is something I actually put forward in the House of Commons. We already could have had that law. People already could have had the right to sack corrupt MPs. Labour MPs voted against it. Conservative MPs didn't. It's great we're saying the same thing. You've also got to do the right thing to clean up politics.

Well Terry, like you, I care about the NHS because I've had so many scrapes in my life, I've needed it far more than most people and I think that when it comes to emergency care, it probably is the best in the world. That doesn't mean there aren't some things that occasionally go wrong. This whole question of how we fund the health service with a population that's rapidly rising, with a population that is ageing, is a huge question. We've had a big internal debate in our party about it and we've decided the best way to do it is to run it efficiently, to run it as a public service, free at the point of access. But to recognise that there has been a 48% growth in middle management of the NHS since 1997. The Labour Party attempted to privatise large chunks of the health service. That I also don't believe has worked. I'd put an extra £3 billion in from savings to our EU contributions and I'd stop the tax on illness by ending hospital parking charges.

David's got it wrong. We're making £15 billion of efficiency savings now. He wants these savings on top of that without putting the money back into the economy. You go to America, look at France, look at Germany, look at the other countries. They're saying, as all the international institutions are saying, don't withdraw the support from the recovery until the recovery is assured. What David would do in an emergency budget in a few weeks' time is, for ideological reasons, take £6 billion out of the economy and put our recovery at risk. The time to do the deficit reduction is when the recovery is assured, and David, you've just got it wrong economically. It's the same mistake the Conservatives made, the same old Conservative Party of the 1930s, the 1980s, and the 1990s.

Well, we have met many of our manifesto commitments from the last election, including recruiting public sector workers. And that's why you can see we're making improvements in many public services. As we spoke about earlier, we have a particular issue with the NHS, but we are increasing healthcare professionals. Many more doctors, many more nurses. But integrity is vital. It is not right to say that you are not going to increase taxes and are. It's not right to say that you are going to support the nuclear deterrent when you voted against it. Thank you, I'm going to... It's not right to say... Thank you, Penny Morden.

Actually, having spoken to lots of people from the armed forces, they're very supportive of the plan, and they think it will do brilliance for them over time to attract people into the armed forces. And actually, the money that we spend on levelling up is partly spent. spreading skills and opportunities for young people, and I think this is the best way to do it. But again, you've had 14 years, 14 years with nothing to do but think about the future, but you have nothing to say about it, and all you can do is talk about the past. And it's clear, there are no big ideas here. If we want to transform our country for the better and deliver a secure future, you've got to have leaders who are prepared to do big, bold things, and I am, and you just don't have anything to offer.

Grace, the earnings link needs to be restored. It needs to be restored immediately. I think there's just no doubt about that. It shouldn't have been broken in the first place. You've lost out for many years. It needs to be restored. But something which I'm sure you're familiar with, which is that there are so many other costs which the elderly have to confront, which really do make it extremely difficult to make ends meet. Fuel costs. We just had a very cold winter, for example, is a very good example. I got a letter a few months ago from an elderly couple who said to me they now found it so difficult to heat their homes on cold winter days, that on those cold winter days they get into a bus in their town and travel around the bus just to stay warm. That is wrong. It is wrong that energy companies charge you more for the first bits of energy that you use in your home than the later bits. That means that someone who's heating a great big five-storey mansion is paying cheaper, paying less for their energy than someone who's just heating a one-bedroom flat. That needs to change too so we can give you the dignity that you deserve.
Nuclear weapons are an abomination.

Of course we're not privatizing the NHS, nor do we have any. And of course, we are investing 30. Nor will the NHS, just let me repeat this point, nor will the NHS be ever up for sale in any negotiation. Let me repeat that point and lay this more to rest. Ladies and gentlemen, we will continue to fund the NHS massively as we are. We can only do so because we have a strong and dynamic economy. And by the way, don't forget that under Labour, they ruined the public finances. They did. Liam Byrne said himself there was no money left. The economy has grown every year in the last nine years. And what could be more ruinous for the NHS than a crackpot plan for a four-day week, which would add massively to the burdens not just of the NHS, but of every public service in the country.

We're the least tribal party. Where there's common ground, we're always going to work with other parties to try and find a way forward. We believe we need a better form of politics and of course we don't agree with the Lib Dems on loads of things. We stood up proudly against austerity in 2015 when even Labour compromised. We've unequivocally stood up for migrants and refugees when the other parties have compromised. And that horrible rhetoric that came out in the referendum, that scaremongering. And Nigel, you standing in front of that breaking point poster was the lowest point in my life in politics. The truth can be horrible, can't it?

Where do you stand on the Trident nuclear deterrent? So I think we should keep the nuclear deterrent in this unsafe world, but I don't think we need to keep it on the same basis on which it was originally designed to flatten Moscow at the press of a button. and this comes down to basically whether the Trident nuclear system has four nuclear submarines or three nuclear submarines and whether you need a nuclear submarine absolutely 24 hours a day, 365 days of the whole year going around the world. I personally think we can step down the nuclear ladder while keeping ourselves safe. And that, I think, is a sensible, balanced way to keep ourselves safe, but not spend huge amounts of money on a Cold War replacement to the nuclear trident system, which just doesn't fit the post-Cold War world that we live in. The kind of threats we face, by the way, aren't like the Cold War. They are now stateless groups, extremist groups, terrorist groups. The civil war that we're seeing raging in Syria and elsewhere, those are the big threats. They're not solved by having four nuclear submarines rather than three. You sit up there at the back.

Nicola Sturgeon. He said this morning in the interview on the BBC that Parliament had blocked the proposals in his Queen's speech that would have led to tougher sentences. Parliament actually supported his Queen's speech. There is an obligation here on a Prime Minister, I agree with Adam, to try to bring people together in the aftermath of an attack like this. not to seek to politicise it for party political gain. But on these issues, of course, we have to reflect on what happens after an atrocity like this, but we also need to do more to prevent these things. And that's where good community policing is so important. The 20,000 cuts to police in England and Wales has been a disgrace. We've increased police numbers in Scotland. Let's get more police into our communities, helping to prevent these attacks, not just have us all standing here wondering how we react.

Look, we started this interview with that, right? I spent a summer, a couple of summers ago, saying that what Liz Truss was proposing was wrong. And I was behind and everyone said to me, why are you bothering? Why are you still talking about this? No-one wants to hear what you have to say. I kept talking about it every day of that campaign... But why didn't people believe...? ..because I believed in what I was saying, right? And I said very clearly I didn't think that was right for our country. And I was very clear on the first day I got this job that mistakes had been made. I was right then when I warned about Liz Truss. And that's why all of you can trust me now when I also warn about the damage that Keir Starmer would do to our economy. In that election, everyone said I was going to lose. I was saying something that no one wanted to hear. But I was saying it because I believed it. And I'm telling you again now, all seems a bit familiar, if Keir Starmer is your Prime Minister, the economy is going to suffer and all of you are going to suffer. I don't want to see that happen. That's why I'm carrying on going every day in this election. I believe in what I'm saying and I want to stop those tax rises happening for all of you. Do you ever wish you were...

Nigel, of course we have to look at those issues and deal with those issues, but I really don't believe that that is at the root of all of the problems of the National Health Service. And I want to pick up on something David Cameron said, because he said he'd protected the NHS. I asked you at home to decide whether this is what protection looks like. A million people waited last year in A&E for more than four hours. We've got ambulances queuing outside our accident and emergency units. We're missing the targets for cancer treatment for the first time ever. And to add to that, we had a tent erected in a hospital car park to treat people in 2015 in our United Kingdom. I do not believe that is protecting the NHS and his spending plans for the next Parliament are even more dangerous for our National Health Service.
The Chancellor says the money's been spent. Stamp duty and first-time donor relief for you, help to buy, help you get on the housing ladder, that's a choice for you.

Richard Tice, the very essence of Peter's question is that people no longer trust and respect what politicians say. Manifestos, frankly, has become synonymous with fibs and nonsense. I mean, let's remember the Labour Party manifesto in 2017 when they promised that they would respect the referendum and get Brexit done. This is a question about the spending and the economy, Richard Tice. It's about trust. It's about trust in politicians. That's the point. And that's why... That may be about trust, but this question is not. This question is about spending, Richard Tice, and how you can guarantee your spending. That's why we called it a contract, which is a business-like word, because I come from the world of business. I've been three decades in business. In order to get the economy moving, we need to invest over £100 billion building road and rail projects all across the regions that have been so left behind. And the truth is that with a proper Brexit, we can cut prices, we can raise wages with a sensible immigration policy, and that will grow the economy faster.

The question is about how to combat hostility. How about these guys stop creating and stoking that hostility in the first place? We need to have an honest debate. Of course, we need to control borders. Every country controls its borders. But to blame migrants for housing, for problems in the NHS, to blame what for public spending cuts that have been... It's called pressure on public services.

Gordon Brown. Well, I think Grace's issue is that every woman should have a full state pension, and I think it's right that we say that that is what we're going to do as a result of our pension reforms. But it's also the case that everyone who works for an employer will now also have an occupational pension, and that is another change that we're making that is coming in in the Parliament to come. Now, when we look at the needs of pensioners, it's absolutely true to say we need help with urgent needs, social care in the home, And that's what we're introducing over the next few months, so that people don't have to go into opiate homes. They can get the home helps and the health visitors that are necessary for them, so that they can have comfort in their own homes. But I've got one or two problems with the two manifestos of the other parties. David doesn't seem to have mentioned free prescriptions for the elderly or free eye tests. And to be honest, Nick has got a problem in his manifesto because he seems to be cutting the budget of the winter fuel allowance this year. And I would like them to explain to the pensioners of this country what, in fact, they propose to do.

Well, actually, I have a question for David Cameron, because he's just said to all of us that he wants to stay the course. But of course, that's not what the Conservative Party want to do at all. Remarkably, the Conservative Party have said they're not going to ask the richest in society to make a single extra penny of contribution to balancing the books through the tax system. They want to impose ideologically driven cuts on schools. When I hear the Conservatives talk about a choice between competence and chaos, just imagine, David Cameron, the chaos in people's lives, the people who in the NHS don't know whether you're going to find the money, the people who don't know whether their nursery or their college or their schools are going to close. That's why Johnny's right. You need to take a balanced approach. You do need to reduce spending, but you also need to ask the richest to make a contribution. It's the only fair way of finishing the job.

I think a good example of saying, look, you know, benefits, in-work benefits, things like unemployment benefits should be frozen for two years. That will save £2 billion. That isn't an easy decision to make, but I think it's important people can see it's better off, you know, that people are better off in work and we try and keep on creating those jobs as we've done. And we find savings, including from efficiency. This Parliament, we've saved something like £20 billion by just being a bit smarter. We still don't have one company that owns all the government's buildings and makes sure we use them efficiently. So let's do things like that. Businesses have to do this every year. They don't get more efficient and then say, that's it, I've finished. They think every year, how can we save money? How can we provide a better service without spending so much money? OK. We've had to do that in... One example, just quickly, policing.

Well, I hope you've enjoyed tonight's talent show. It's been a competition to see who can be the most politically correct, so therefore I must have failed here, but maybe not with you back at home. You know, I set this party up earlier this year. If I hadn't, I think Mrs May would still be Prime Minister. I want us to become an independent country, to leave the European Union. There's a bigger, more exciting world out there, our friends in the Commonwealth, America. There's so much we can do, but we have to get Brexit done properly and then reform our political institutions. And what I've seen tonight, there's no desire to change anything. So we need to get some fresh, strong, non-PC voices into the House of Commons, and let's fight not just for Brexit, but let's try and change politics for good.

This is the most important general election in generations. The last chance to remain in the EU and transform Britain. The very last chance to prevent runaway climate chaos. A half of all voters say that climate breakdown will influence their choice on December the 12th. Voting with them is the only way to get more Green MPs and to keep up the pressure. So if you've ever thought about voting Green, do it. If not now, when?

That is totally and utterly ridiculous. We are deeply committed to an NHS free at the point of use as it always has been. The real risk to the NHS, Rebecca, why don't you tell us, how much is it going to cost to implement the four-day week in the NHS? LONG-BAILEY: OK, let's clarify that. LONG-BAILEY: We already said that there's going to be a four-day week in the NHS, so don't try and throw around figures like that that you know aren't true. LONG-BAILEY: I'm a bigger man than that, Rishi.

Well, first of all, can I thank you for your incredible service to the NHS. I think the NHS is a wonderful, wonderful thing. What it did for my family and for my son, I will never forget. I went from hospital to hospital, A&Es in the middle of the night, sleeping in different wards in different places and the dedication and the vocation and the love that you get from people who work in the NHS just I think makes me incredibly proud of this country and so thank you for all that you've done. I think it is special the NHS and we made a special exception of the NHS and said yes there are going to have to be difficult financial decisions elsewhere but we think that the NHS budget should grow in real terms, i.e. more than inflation, every year under a Conservative government. My vision is that we improve it, we expand it, we develop it, we make sure that it's got more choice and more control for the patient. But we need to do some short-term things too, like make cancer drugs available to people who need them. There are some tragic cases now of people just not getting what they need.
We've got a workforce plan for the NHS. We've got a workforce plan for social care.

I hear what people say about it. No, we have ample time to do a fantastic free trade deal with our friends and partners in the EU, because we're already in a state of perfect alignment, both for tariffs and for quotas. And we still have not heard, Julie. You will notice. I've answered your questions. But there is a glaring lacuna still in this debate. We still don't know. I'm sorry to say, we still don't know. There is a vast emanation. OK. Allow Mr. Corbyn to respond to the question from Mr. Johnson. Is Jeremy Corbyn going to campaign for the deal he proposes to do? Or is he going to invite his Labour colleagues to destroy the contraption that he's created?

95% of all private sector involvement in the NHS came under a Labour government. But let's point this out, the last thing that Britain needs, more importantly, the last thing that patients and doctors and nurses need is another restructuring. Perhaps the most radical thing any health secretary could do in this country is to let our hospitals and our health care services get on with the job and stop restructuring and instead invest the money. And that is why there is no point politicking and disagreeing on how you might do it. You've got to be honest with the British people. That is why we will say to you it will cost us all one penny in the pound, the equivalent of a cup of coffee every week for most people, to make sure we put £30 billion extra into the National Health and Social Care Service. It's time we were honest with the British people and rescued health and social care.

Well, we're not going to coalition. I made that very, very clear. I mean, first of all, whether one likes it or not, Theresa May is heading for a landslide on Thursday. I wish she wasn't, but she called the election for that very purpose. You just told us you didn't believe the polls, that you shouldn't trust the polls. How on earth do you know she's running for a landslide? There you go. You got me. You got me. So, Nick, you got me. But I think that one assumes nobody calls an election at the point that she did. If she did not assume, maybe she's taking us all for granted that that will be the outcome. Either way, though, we've been very clear we will not be going into coalition with any party after this election. You can still make a massive difference in opposition. Here in Scotland, quite a good example is the SNP government just short of a majority here. The Liberal Democrats are not in coalition or any partnership or pact with the SNP government. We stay in opposition and we make a difference from the opposite side. If you want, for example, to stop the dementia tax, that's in education and in hospitals, then being in opposition gives you the chance to prevent all of those things.

What is your answer to that, Boris Johnson? I have to say, I'm afraid that shows a slight ignorance of the reality, which is that as we leave the EU, we are coming out with our deal, with a state of perfect equivalence, zero tariff, zero quota. We'll be able to begin not only putting in our Australian-style points-based system for immigration, taking back control of our laws, taking back control of our money. We'll also be able to... He asked you about trade, though, Mr Johnson. Trade talks, not just with countries around the world, But we will do a fantastic deal with the EU and with the US.

We'll be giving half the cost of an NHS operation if someone wants to go private. And these are all people who've paid their taxes. They've paid for the cost of their NHS operation. They're lessening the NHS waiting lists by going into the private sector. I think it's only fair that they should get a proportion of what it would have cost the NHS after all. It's a good deal for the NHS, because instead of having to pay for the whole of the cost of the operation, they're only paying a contribution, a half of it, and that person goes off the waiting list. Would you do the same for schools? People wanting to go private? No. What we would do for schools... Same principle, isn't it? It's not quite, because we're very practical about this, we're very pragmatic. There's a difference between the two. The problem in health is overwhelmingly one of capacity, so we want to encourage more capacity. The problem in education is a bit different. You don't have children on waiting lists in general. I know there are some in London at the moment, and that's a disgrace. But in general, children are not on waiting lists to go to school. It's a question of choice and quality and standards, and so we would encourage... What we say is, if private sector can provide education at the same cost as the state, then children and parents should be able to choose that private school. But only if it's the same cost as the state.

Because we will make sure that our jobs and our manufacturing industries are able to continue trading. Over half of our trade is with Europe at the present time. Walk away from that. put up a barrier on that, then you've got real problems for manufacturing industry jobs all across the country. Many jobs have already been lost because of the uncertainty surrounding all this. We have to deal with this within six months. The Prime Minister's suggestions of a trade deal with the USA, which will take at least, in their estimation, seven years to negotiate, means seven years of complete uncertainty and continued job losses in manufacturing industry.

Well, clearly, the principle of the reason why we went into Afghanistan, why I supported our mission in Afghanistan, unlike the illegal invasion in Iraq, is to keep us safe. It's not to sort of parachute democracy into Afghanistan. It's because we believe that if you allow Afghanistan to be a haven of extremism and terrorism, there will be more terrorist attacks here in Britain. So from that principle, if we need to do that again, we should. The problem, of course, is that We've done it in a manner where I don't think we've pursued the right strategy, we haven't given the right equipment to our troops, we haven't had proper international coordination on the ground in Afghanistan, we haven't worked properly with other countries in the region to do so. I think if you put soldiers into harm's way, you either do the job properly or you don't do it at all. ever take that decision again. Let's make sure we've got the right equipment, the right strategy, so that they can come back, having done the job we've asked them to do, with their heads held high, knowing they've done the job well.

Well, first of all, let's just take the one example of cancer. What we're seeing with cancer under this government is 460,000 more people a year getting seen and getting examined for cancer than used to happen. Our survival rates for cancer that used to be some of the worst in Europe are now actually some of the best in Europe. We are changing the NHS and we're improving it. But a strong NHS needs a strong economy. If we go back to Labour's plans for taxes and debt and spending and welfare, the economy will be wrecked, and that will wreck the NHS. And this point about the commitments we made is important, because when I said we'd fund the NHS more every year, the Labour view was that was irresponsible. The Liberal Democrat view was that wasn't the right approach. And Labour cut the NHS in Wales, where the outcomes are worse.

David Cameron! Thank you. Well, let me take on very directly this question of money and public spending, because I think it's going to be a common feature right through these debates about how do we fund the public services that we need. And I think it's really important we start focusing on what we get out of the money that we put in. Because if we think that the future is just spending more and more money, we're profoundly wrong. Mr. Brown. Hold on.

It's a risk too far if you cut teachers and teaching assistants. What Gordon Brown isn't telling you is that he's putting up national insurance contributions on every single job in 2011. The biggest cost schools have is teachers. So he's going to be taking money out of every single school in the country. Primary school, secondary school, FE college. We say stop the waste in government now so we can stop the lion's share of that national insurance increase, that jobs tax next year. That's the best way to make sure we keep the money going into the schools.

No, it's a political decision. I didn't want Jo Swinson to win lots of seats in the south and the southwest of England, which, indeed, she would have done. No question about that. So we got the Conservative Party dragged, kicking and screaming, into talking about something that nearly resembles Brexit. Were you offered a peerage in exchange for that? Good God, no. I am not for sale, unlike most people in politics. But I'm pleased you've asked me that, because that links in. Hang on a minute.

We have set out in our manifesto, you can look at it in the back pages, we've set out numbers right there, specifying the savings that we do think should be made. Set out in much greater detail than any other party. £15 billion worth of savings which are a kind of upfront down payment to deal with this huge black hole we have in our public finances. What are we talking about? Things like scrapping the new generation of biometric passports, public sector pay restraint, saying the top 20% of recipients of tax credits shouldn't receive those tax credits so they can be targeted elsewhere. I'm saying no to things like the multi-billion pound Eurofighter Typhoon project - a defence project. Those are the kind of big decisions you need to take. What you can't do - and this is where I really disagree with David Cameron and Gordon Brown - is try and fool you into thinking that just efficiency savings are enough. You can't fill the black hole by just a few savings on pot plants and paper clips in Whitehall.

Whether it's Labour-run Wales or SNP-run Scotland or Conservative-run England, we all know that whilst you can get great care, the NHS model isn't working. You can't get GP appointments, things that we've all grown up taking for granted. And they can argue about, it's all money. I'm going to spend more money. You're going to spend more money. It doesn't work. Just a few years ago, we were spending 7, 7.5% of the national cake on the NHS. We're now spending over 11% of the national cake on the NHS.

Leaving the European Union means we withdraw from the Treaty of Rome, we withdraw from the 1972 decision that was made by the British government at that time to join with the European Union. It means that there is no longer a legislative authority over UK law. within the EU or a parliamentary consent for it. It means we have to have an independent and separate relationship with the European Union and I think we've got two years to negotiate it and I can't wait to get started to make sure that we do retain manufacturing industry and service industry jobs in Britain that are so essential to our economy and our economic growth. Threatening will not work. Is your aim to remain Is your aim to remain in the single market? Do you think that's possible? Our aim is to have a tariff-free trade access to Europe. I think we should put it in those terms rather than anything else at this stage.
I think the person I admire most in the world at the present time is the General Secretary of the United Nations, Guterres, trying to bring the world together.

I agree with the young woman at the front who says that we're better to stick together through thick and thin. That's what I think that we should do. But to specifically answer the question from the young man behind, look, I'd much have preferred us to be having a people's vote on the specific Brexit deal than a general election. Liberal Democrats tabled amendments to try and secure a people's vote 17 times and the Labour Party, instead of backing those amendments, 19 Labour MPs voted for Boris Johnson's Brexit deal. That's why we're having an election, because if we hadn't done that, he was going to get his Brexit deal passed on the back of Labour votes.

I have every sympathy with what you say, because education is important, as well as getting good grades, that actually we're opening young people's minds to all the best things that have been written and all the best things that have been said, and to really excite people about education. And I think there is a danger that our education system has become terribly bureaucratic. We send 4,000 pages of information to schools every year. We spend £300 million on educational quangos. We're not getting enough to the front line, actually following the child into the school. And as someone who's got two children, one of whom started a state school in London and hopefully another one, another child to come. I'm passionate about getting as much money into the school as possible rather than wasting it in Whitehall. In terms of exams, we've got to have good external marking, done properly and high standards. I think that's absolutely key. I wouldn't want to see that change. But let's set the schools free so we trust in the vocation of the teachers who do what they want. They're there because they have a vocation they care about. Nick Clegg.

To answer the question directly about how we're handling public finances in this electoral period, I would just point out that I have shelved a plan to cut corporation tax, saving... It's already the lowest in Europe. He would whack it up to the highest in Europe. But we've shelved that cut. That gives us the ability to put another £6 billion into the NHS and other priorities. And we will do that. We can do that because we have a strong economy. And what worries me about this country at the moment is that we are going to continue with this pointless paralysis. I have no hesitation in repeating this point. If we go on with more deadlock and division next year, if we fail to get Brexit done, then the economy will suffer. And that is the reality.

Let's bring in Leanne Wood and then we'll go to Paul Nuttall. We are going to leave the European Union. The question for us now is how we leave the European Union. And Plaid Cymru has said right from the very beginning, the question of jobs and the economy has to be at the centre of all the decision making. We have 200,000 jobs reliant on tariff-free access with a single market in Wales. Those jobs are at risk. if Theresa May walks away without a deal. There are £680 million that come from the EU every year because Wales is one of the poorest countries in the European Union. We've had no guarantees that that money will be available for us beyond 2020. That would mean the end of the Welsh farming industry and the end of many people's jobs. But it's not just about defending and protecting what we've got. We also have to have a post-Brexit plan which creates the conditions for us to be able to develop our economy and create even more better paid jobs.

Yeah. Let me say two things, though, about this, which are important. OK, you said... Let me just make a point about immigration, because you asked me a question. Let me answer it. Look, I think that we benefit from our diversity. Immigrants over the years have made a big contribution to our country, but we do need proper controls. Let me just say this, Jeremy. A Labour government would say that if people come here, they can't get benefits for the first two years, and we will do something else.

Our country is crying out for change, and it's not hard to see why. Under this Conservative government, everything feels broken. Nothing works. Under Ed Davies' leadership, the Liberal Democrats will fix our NHS and social care, tackle the cost-of-living crisis, and put an end to the scandal of filthy raw sewage being dumped in our rivers and streams. Every vote for the Liberal Democrats is a vote for a fair deal and a vote to help deliver the change that we all so desperately want and need.

And I said there wouldn't be a general election. And after I became Prime Minister, I felt that the most important thing for the country was the stability and getting on with triggering the Article 50 so that we made sure that we were delivering on that vote on Brexit. What became clear when we were going through that process is that other parties want to frustrate that process of those Brexit negotiations. We have the Liberal Democrats wanting a second referendum. That's all happened since March, has it? We have the... Well, actually, it was March. It was in March that I triggered Article 50 by writing the letter to the European Council.

No, but what we've done... Do you accept that? No, but that's not what we've done. What we have done is say that if people are going to bring family members here, they just need to be able to support them. I think that's reasonable, because any person that comes to our country obviously adds to the pressure on public services, and it's a very reasonable proposition to say, if people are bringing their dependents, family dependents, then they need to be able to demonstrate that they're going to be able to support them. That is, I think, a very common-sense measure, and that's what we are doing.

What I would say is, for the past seven years of austerity, that austerity has hit public sector workers with a 1% cap. It has hit all our schools and public services. It has caused a housing crisis. And do you know what? The very richest in our society have got richer. There's been more and more tax giveaways at the top end and more and more charges at the other end. It's time to rebalance it. Our manifesto is a serious, well thought out document that I believe is getting a lot of support and people are very excited by the idea of how we could do things differently.

Thank you. It's clear from last week's debate that the country wants change. But the question is, what sort of change and whose best place to lead that change? If you vote Conservative, you will get a new team running the country from May the 7th. And you won't be stuck with what you've got now. But real change comes from your values. And there, there are big differences between us. I believe that we need to do more to help families. They are the absolute bedrock of a strong society. I want government to be accountable. I want less waste, less bureaucracy, stopping the jobs tax, but making sure we have good public services that you get good value for money for. As we're going to discuss tonight, I want us to keep our defences strong, I want to keep our borders secure and our country safe. but real change. Real change comes not just from politicians, but from when we all recognise that we have responsibilities. We're all in this together, and that's how we'll build the big and strong society I believe we need in our country.

Thank you very much. Penny Mordaunt. Thank you. Well, thank you for what you do. And you'll know from the people that you work with that when you're facing financial hardship, how frightening that is. and how exhausting it is because you have to be so organised. I think this is the issue of the election and it is not true to say that we've put the highest burden on the poorest. The tax burden on those on low and middle incomes is the lowest that it has been since 1975. We've got four million people into work. We have a million fewer working households. But what we need to do now is cut tax. We have to enable people to keep more of the money they earn. That is what we will do. Labour will do the opposite. They will put up taxes, they will put up bills, and the people you work with will suffer.

That's a totally fair challenge and that's why I've been much clearer and I hope, maybe you didn't hear it, about the red lines without which the Liberal Democrats simply won't go into any coalition government. So, for instance, education spending. You just heard Ed Miliband say that the Labour Party wants to increase education spending to keep up with prices. What he didn't tell you is that there are going to be 460,000 new youngsters going into education system. So you need to increase spending to help them as well. The Conservatives want to do the reverse, they want to keep up with increased pupil numbers but not with prices. Both amount to a cut, a multi-billion pound cut to the money that goes into our nurseries, our schools and our colleges. I cannot be more clear with you, the Liberal Democrats will not go into any coalition, any government, we won't sign a coalition agreement unless, if either of those parties insist on those cuts to our education system because I think that really is short-changing the little children of today who should be given just

What I want to do in relation to the details of the policy, how it actually works, including the level of the cap, is to consult. Consult with people, consult with voters, also consult with organisations, charities and others, working with older people, so that we ensure that we get that right. And I think that's a fair way to do it, rather than just producing a figure now. I think it's important to have that consultation.

In 2010, in addition to there being no money left, IFOI, the Department of Education, about the Building Schools for the Future programme. They weren't even looking at secondary schools in that programme. And what Angela and the Labour Party are going to do is, unbelievably, they are going to tax education. The result of that is, yes, some families will not be able to send their kids to the schools they're currently going to, but also, they've admitted this week, class sizes will go up as a consequence. It's crazy. It doesn't serve anyone. And it's the kind of dogma that we need to keep out

Well, I just think it's another attempt to try and frighten people after 13 years of failure. 13 years in which we now have a budget deficit the same size as Greece. And don't take it from me, take it from the 1,100 business leaders. You know, people who run Sainsbury's, Mothercare, Next, Marks & Spencers, these great British businesses are going to help us out of recession saying cut the waste this year, stop the jobs tax next year. But to go back to Frank's question, I do have one specific proposal where I really think we should come together much more effectively. And that is, I want to see the National Security Council sitting as a war cabinet from day one if I become Prime Minister. Because day one, Afghanistan is the top priority, getting it right. And I would invite, as Prime Minister, the leaders of the other two main parties to come to that meeting several times a year to make sure that they were really seeing the advice we were getting from the security services, from the generals, from the chiefs of staff. I think in matters of peace and war, you've got to bring people together better than we do now.

Plaid Cymru wants to end austerity. We want to rebalance power and wealth throughout these islands. We want to invest in job creation. We want to end Trident renewal, introduce a living wage, end zero-hours contracts, but most importantly, we want to bring an end to this Tory Government with its ideological-driven cuts, and we can work together as progressives here on this stage can work together in order to achieve that. We can have strong public services, we can have a strong economy with a future for our young people and we can also get a strong deal for Wales. There is no longer any need for us to continue with a third-rate devolution settlement. Like Nicola, I will not prop up a Conservative government, but I will also not prop up a Labour government hell-bent on implementing Tory policies. We can have an alternative.

I actually happen to agree, strongly agree, in something I've been calling for for years, that we should have a complete review about whether our military equipment is right for the jobs we're asking our brave soldiers and brave service men and women to do. Because of course the world is changing and the threats to this country are changing with it. What I simply don't understand is that if we hold that review, as I think is going to be likely after the general election, whoever wins that election, Both David Cameron and Gordon Brown want to rule out one of the biggest items of defence expenditure of all, which is the Trident nuclear missile system. This was a system that was designed at the height of the Cold War to flatten St Petersburg and Moscow at the crest of battle.

Well, I do think it's welcome that the Pope is coming to Britain, and I would, if I was your Prime Minister, I would want to support that visit and make sure I could do everything in my power to make it a success. There are millions of people in our country who will welcome that, who share the Pope's Catholic faith, and I think we should try and make a success of it. But do I agree with everything the Pope says? No, I don't agree with him about contraception, I don't agree with him about homosexuality, and I think the Catholic Church has got some very, very serious work to do to unearth and come to terms with some of the appalling things that have happened, and they need to do that. But I do think we should respect people of faith. I think faith is important in our country. I think faith-based organizations, whether they are Christian or Jewish or Muslim or Hindu, do amazing things in our country, whether it's working in our prisons or providing good schools or actually helping some of the most vulnerable people in our country. So a country where faith is welcome, yes. A good visit from the Pope, yes. But does that mean we have to agree with everything he says? No. Nick Clegg.

All I can say is what I think and what I believe. And when my disabled son was desperately ill, I went to the NHS night after night. I went to different hospitals. I was in different places. But I got the most fantastic care, support, and I'd say love from those people in the NHS for my family and for my son. It was always there for me and I will always make sure it is there for other families in our country. Now, I showed you that note about we had no money when we became the government. When we got that note, we still made a very big decision, which is we said, yes, we're going to have to make cuts in some areas of government spending, but the NHS is going to get more money every year I'm Prime Minister. And it has had more money, and that's why there are, compared with when I became Prime Minister, 9,000 more doctors, there are 7,000 more nurses, we're carrying out millions more operations every year. Do I think it's perfect? No I don't, and I've got a big ambition. for the next five years, which is to deliver a seven-day-a-week NHS, including seven-day opening, eight in the morning, eight in the evening, for GP surgeries. I know we can do that, because we're already delivering it to about eight million people in our country. If you elect me as Prime Minister, I'll finish the job and make sure we have seven-day opening for GP surgeries right across our country. That will really help working people to access the NHS that we all want.

Anaya, national security is of paramount importance. It's one of the most important responsibilities of any politician and any government. But I think we need to make sure that the country is defended appropriately. We've seen savage cuts to conventional defence forces in recent years, a 30% drop in MOD employment, at the same time as our forces have been fighting on the front line in Iraq and Afghanistan. Britain is an island nation, and yet doesn't have a single maritime patrol aircraft. And at the same time, Westminster parties plan to spend £100 billion on new nuclear weapons. I think those priorities are plain wrong. To those who say we need Trident to make us safe, there are 190 or so out of the 200 countries in the world that don't have nuclear weapons. So I think it's time to invest in strong conventional forces, not in nuclear weapons. And if it comes to a choice of how to spend £100 billion, I choose childcare, health and education over new Trident weapons any day of the week.

Inheritance tax means we have tax credits and six million people benefit from that. As far as the teaching assistant who's earning a very low income is concerned, there's the working tax credit that is available to them. But I come back to the central question about fairness that has been raised by our questioner. How can David possibly justify an inheritance tax cut for millionaires at a time when he wants to cut Child Tax Credits? Let's be honest. The inheritance tax threshold for couples is £650,000, if your house is worth less than that you pay no inheritance tax. What David is doing is giving 3,000 people, the richest people in the country, he's going to give them £200,000 each a year. That is simply unfair, when he also wants, like Nick, to cut the Child Tax Credits from ordinary families in this country. I've got to speak out about this because it's simply unfair and immoral for the Conservatives to put this as their election manifesto.

Well what we've had is a government for the past seven years that has borrowed more, run up a greater deficit and cut public services at the same time and at the lowest growth rate of any industrial country. The system they have is not working. What we're proposing is to raise corporation tax, yes, in order to make the funding commitments that I've already outlined, but it's also to establish a serious national investment bank to invest in infrastructure all around this country so we do get a sustainable growing economy. We cannot go on giving money away to the very rich. This government's proposing another 60 billion in tax giveaways in the next five years. Instead, I say turn it round and invest in the future of all of our people.

With Europe, I'm worried about some of the tendencies across Europe because I'm obviously leading a progressive party and I think that the answers to the problems we face globally, whether that's climate, whether that's conflict, whether that's extreme poverty, getting our economy going, are progressive answers to that. Now, I will have to work, if I'm elected into government, with all leaders across Europe, but I will always make that progressive argument that, in the end, you have to stabilise your economy, you have to get investment in to make sure that we get the jobs of the future, we have to take our climate obligations seriously and not see them as just a sort of challenge that we don't want, but as a brilliant opportunity for the next generation of jobs and play our full part in descaling conflict. If we're able to get over the line in two weeks' time, we have a platform for that, which is really important. If you look across Europe, I am genuinely worried by some of the political trends and moves that we see. Thank you.

So the first point I'd like to say in response to your question is that I accept there are some very significant challenges in the National Health Service in Scotland today. And some of that's a product of the impact of COVID and the necessity of trying to catch up on cases that were delayed. and we're making headway on the long waits that Fiona just put to me in the additional part of the question. So I accept, first of all, that there's a real challenge in the National Health Service, and we need to put more funding in to do something about that. There's two elements to the funding we have available to us. There's the block grant that comes from Westminster, which is affected by the same austerity and spending constraints that affect budgets in England and Wales. but there's also the money we can raise in Scotland. So we've taken decisions over the last three or four years to increase tax on higher earners so we could raise more money to invest in public services. And we now have one and a half billion pounds available to us that we invest in public services that wouldn't be there if we hadn't taken those decisions. That's an example of how we're using the powers that we have today to try to address the very issue that you fairly put to me about the challenges in the National Health Service. But the deeper problem we have, and this is a problem I think that we share with the rest of the United Kingdom, is a long-term effect of 14 years of austerity. and I'm not hearing coming out of the UK election debate between the Labour Party and the Conservative Party the type of financial solution to the challenges in the National Health Service that we need to hear because there's just not enough money proposed to be invested in improving the public services of the United Kingdom and that would have an effect on Scotland as well.

And we are, and that's the point, we are doing both. We're cutting taxes for young people buying their first home, creating new apprenticeships, and the Triple Lock Plus to protect pensioners who have worked hard all their lives and are grandparents to those young people. We are doing those things, and as I said when I was sitting there, we can cut taxes for people at every stage of their life. That's the type of country I want to build.

It is what the Committee on Climate Change said is at the outer limits right now. That is what we should be aiming for at the very latest. What Scotland has done is pile on the pressure to meet higher targets earlier. We are going beyond what the Committee on Climate Change has said for 2030, a 75% reduction in all emissions by 2030, carbon neutrality by 2040. Now, the IPCC, the international expert, says that the world needs to reach carbon neutrality by 2050 and half emissions, carbon emissions, by 2030. So we are going beyond that. And unlike the other leaders here, I'm actually in government, leading a government that is leading by example. Our targets are the toughest anywhere in the world. They include aviation, shipping and they don't rely on international credits and that's the kind of ambition we need to see all countries take.

Let's be clear. From 2011, there will be cuts in spending in departments other than NHS, other than schools, and other than policing. But we will make the commitment to maintain these front line services and build on the improvements that we've made. Once you've built a school and a hospital as we've done in the last few years, you don't need to build it again, so we can have cuts in capital investment. Public sector pay will not rise as it did in previous years and public sector pensions are being reformed. But I have to say one thing that is absolutely crucial to the time we're in at the moment in this uncertain and dangerous world: David is proposing that there be cuts in public spending now - £6 billion - and that will shrink the economy at a time when we need to support the economy. We cannot afford to lose jobs and businesses and lose growth now. We must maintain the recovery and support it, and please let us not make the mistake of the 1930s and the 1980s and the 1990s, and let us support the economy until the recovery is assured. DAVID DIMBLEBY Your response, David Cameron?

When we came into office, James, Labour had left this country on the brink of bankruptcy. We took many decisions which mean now the economy is stronger, millions more are in work, wages are rising, and we can now bring the change that you and many more want to see. Investment in our public services like the NHS, making sure that this is the best place to raise a family, to start a business. and to keep the cost of living and your taxes low. The alternative to all of this, all of this hard work, is Labour's reckless spending plans. And we know who will pay for those. It's very clear. Ordinary working families up and down this country will hit their wallets with more taxes because of Labour's spending plans. And that's a risk we must avoid.

Rebecca, do you know even what has been going on in Wales? We've had the scandals, haven't we, over the maternity services and the deaths of babies in England. We've had exactly the same at Cwm Taf here in Wales, you know, an official report pointing out to the serious failings of your government. If you criticise the Conservative Party rightly for the unnecessary failings there, surely admit and be honest about the fact that your government here has also failed those mothers and fathers and people in the NHS in Wales as well. LONG-BAILEY: There should be a full legal process. LONG-BAILEY: I don't know about the case that's been referred to but knowing from my legal experience there should be a full legal investigation and a public inquiry into this incident which sounds horrific but when I talk about public funding for our NHS we know that even in Wales it can be increased and they are linked to LONG-BAILEY: to the British government in terms of the funding that they can receive on many areas. LONG-BAILEY: And we are not receiving funding for our NHS and that's why Labour has promised to give the biggest increase we have ever seen to our NHS.

Well, thanks, Hamza, first of all, for what you do and all your colleagues do in our incredible NHS, and I'm very sorry to hear about your experience. I think the first thing to say to you is I come from an NHS family. So, like you, like my parents, they worked in the NHS, and my dad was a GP, my mum was a pharmacist, and they dedicated their lives. to helping people and I was surrounded by that growing up and actually what they instilled in me was an importance of how we can continue to help people and I worked for my mum doing exactly that. So that's the first thing you need to know about me when it comes to the NHS and that's why we've put record funding into it but your point is the right one. What we have not done for not just years but for decades is train enough staff and then be reliant on having to bring people here from abroad. And in the NHS's 75-year history, it has never ever had a long-term workforce plan. But for the first time, it does, because I thought that was the right thing to prioritise. And we announced it together. It was the NHS's plan that the government has fully backed. And I said to them, look, this is the right thing to do. because we need to train more doctors, nurses, dentists, philotherapists, everything today so that in years to come the NHS will have the staff that it needs to ease some of the pressures, reduce reliance on staff from overseas and make sure it's a fantastic career for everybody. And look, candidly, We're the best one in the world, you know, I won't be here in the 14 years that it takes to train a consultant that we're paying for today, but I thought it was the right thing to do to strengthen our NHS for the long term because people like you are brilliant, we want to make sure we train as many of you as possible so we have lots of colleagues, ease the burden and get everyone the care that they need.

No, of course I'm not. I want you to own your own home, because it's an incredibly special thing. I remember what it felt like. I want everyone to feel like that, because owning your own home is part of what allows you to build a life, build a family, and gives you a stake in your community. It's something I passionately believe, and I want to make it easier for you. I'll tell you two very specific things that I will do for you and everyone else if I'm your prime minister on July 5th. One is to reintroduce a new form of help to buy. which means you won't have to save up as much for the deposit. It will save you tens of thousands of pounds. So that's a big issue, waiting till you're 40 to save up for the deposit. It will help solve that problem. And the second thing we're going to do is abolish stamp duty for first-time buyers, effectively. There'll be a cap, but most people will be covered by it. And then again, that's going to save you thousands of pounds, and it's going to get you on the housing ladder far quicker. Because the challenge that I see when I talk to everyone of your age is, actually, they could probably afford the mortgage. The challenge, I can see you nodding, the challenge is how long will it take me to save up the deposit? And the combination of a new help to buy and actually abolishing stamp duty means that you'll be able to get on the housing ladder far quicker. So it's two very specific things so you can own your own home. And again, I'll point this out, because all elections are a choice, the Labour Party have said no to both of those things. So you're only going to get those things if I'm your Prime Minister.

Is it fired? I think fine. Well, thank you very much for a very important question. And look, on the issue of trust, I think that trust in politics and in Parliament has been corroded. And that is the most fundamental reason for that is because Parliament asked the people to vote on membership of the EU, and then Parliament has repeatedly refused to honour that promise, to respect the views of the people. I'm afraid this, Jeremy Corbyn, Jeremy Corbyn and the Labour Party are now blocking Brexit. The way I think, which is absolutely true, the way to restore trust in politics is to get Brexit done and move forward. We have a great deal that enables us to do it. I'm unclear as to why it's necessary to spend another year in a debate about Brexit, which is, as Farhan says, corrosive advice.

So you pay VAT on other services, but you don't pay it for private schools. Now, I understand why that's been in place, but it's a tax break that we are removing. It's not an introduction of a new tax. We're simply saying we need to find the money to properly fund our state secondary schools so that, in maths, to take an example, it's not the only example, I want every child to have a maths teacher teaching them maths, because it's... The Prime Minister says he wants maths to the age of 18. Let's first get the right teachers for every single child up to 16, because at the moment they don't have it. And I genuinely worry, if you don't get maths, at the right level in your GCSEs, it's really difficult to go on to A-levels, it's really difficult to go on to apprentices, and that child will be held back. Now, this is difficult stuff, but I'm not going to tolerate the fact that we haven't got the right teachers in our state secondary schools. That's the position we're in now. It's a disgrace in that position.
