Well, we've had austerity for nine years in this country. We've had a growth of billionaires in this country. We've had a growth of extreme poverty in this country. We've had a growth of personal debt from people trying to look after children, look after older people. I think the first thing has to be an increase in wages by having a £10 an hour living wage for all people, an end to zero hours contracts, and an investment strategy across the country that does give us an increase in manufacturing jobs all across Britain, which we're not having at the present time. I think this election is a turning point in the way in which we're going to manage our economy in the future.

Let me put the point on the size of the deficit to Leanne Wood. The deficit can be cut, but setting arbitrary deadlines is irresponsible. Labour have voted for more austerity. They voted for the austerity charter, which means another £30 billion worth of cuts. And in the valleys where I live, we've yet to recover from the recession before last, let alone this one. And your party, Ed, represents many areas of Wales at all different levels. Do you accept that you've failed people in Wales because we represent some of the poorest communities in the whole of the EU and your party is presiding over those communities?

I want more people to have full-time work and that is what is happening in our economy. It has been difficult. We have had a very difficult few years following the longest and deepest recession virtually in our country's history. We are coming out of that We created 1.89 million new jobs. That's 1,000 a day. The majority of those have been full-time jobs, not part-time jobs. Most of them have been in relatively well-paid occupations. Only one in 50 jobs is a zero-hours contract, and some people choose those contracts. But if you ask me, do I want more people to have the dignity and security of work and a regular wage? Yes. Do I want a higher minimum wage? Yes. And we've seen the minimum wage increase now above inflation for the first time in many years. And I want to see that minimum wage go through £7 on to £8. And crucially, Jeremy, in terms of what the government can do, we have cut taxes. So we've taken the 3 million lowest paid people in our country out of income tax altogether. so you can earn now £10,600 before you start paying income tax at all. I think that is vitally important, and that's been the priority for the government.

Can I just go back to Mary's question, which is about people feeling switched off from politics? Something which neither of the two old parties want to address is this. We have a culture of jobs for life in politics. You've got hundreds and hundreds of MPs from the Conservative Party and from the Labour Party, from the old parties, who basically know that all they need to do every four or five years is get the vote of 20-30% of people in their community and then ignore everybody else all the rest of the time. And of course you then start getting trouble with expenses because of course if you give people jobs for life, these safe seats for life, no questions asked, then of course they start cutting corners. That's what we need to change as well as all the other things to do with expenses, House of the Lords and all the other areas which need to be reformed.

We're going to invest more in the NHS. And then, A'shani, thank you for studying in Manchester. I'm biased, but I'd say it's the greatest city. But I do think that there is an issue that, since the Tories took power, I was a home care worker and I represented public sector workers across the North West and nationally and the decimation of the social care services and the infrastructure around community services has actually not only left us without that community care but it costs more in the NHS. Far too many people, especially our older generation, we've just been talking about D-Day and our veterans, they're on trolleys for hours on end not getting the care they need but it costs more money to do that so we would put home care services in. it costs less to do that and we would have 40,000 new appointments every week in the NHS by making sure we close that non-DOM tax loophole.

This is not correct. The Taliban changed their tactics. We brought in helicopters from Iraq. We had to reprocess them because they were not suitable to the terrain in Afghanistan. We've got Chinooks in, we've got Merlins in, we've got Lynx in. All the different helicopters we need have been put into Afghanistan. And I would say, with the Chief of Defence staff, who said himself, we are the best equipped armed forces in our history as a result of the action we've taken. I'm not complacent. I want to do more. But we've put the helicopters in, we've put the vehicles in, and we are giving our troops the equipment they need. Thank you. And I'm very proud of our troops.

No, it's not about ISIS. But let me just say, Leanne, because I think it is an important point, we are currently taking military action against ISIS. I'm going to continue that as Prime Minister. And I think it's right that we continue that as Prime Minister. But you're saying it's a nuclear bomb? No, I'm not. No, of course not. The question is about trade and... Well, you raised the issue of ISIS, or rather I raised it, and then you talked about it. It is really important, this. Because, look, we have a battle going on against this organisation that wants to create an Islamic state across Iraq and across Syria, and it is right that we use military action against them, part of a coalition. And this is the point about the Iraq war. You see, in 2013, David Cameron came to me and Nick Clegg, and they said that they wanted to bomb Syria. And they hadn't thought through the consequences. It wasn't a last resort. So I said no. And as a result, the British Parliament said no. And then President Obama decided not to go ahead with that action. So it's right to be judicious about these things. Military action, a last resort. But in the case of ISIS, I'll continue it.

Well, thank you Nicola for the question. My party has presided over making sure that people on lower wages are able to keep more of the wages they earn. We must take it within context. Since 2009 we had, up from the run to 2009, we had one of the largest peacetime recessions. and we've managed to rebuild the economy since then. But my party is focused on making sure that we help people like Nicola, which is why we're going to continue to take people out of taxes, making sure that the amount you can earn before you have to pay tax rises to £12,500 and we're going to live within our means. Some of the offers you will hear tonight are just fanciful. In order to have a strong economy, to support Nicola and others, we need to make sure we have that strong economy. And the way to make sure that we have it is to have a strong negotiating position as we go into leaving the European Union. You're going to hear me say that a lot tonight because the worst outcome would be if we're not able to negotiate a strong deal with the European Union.

But it's important to me, because people are asking about the election, right? That is the important issue at stake in this election. I'm going to continue cutting taxes for people at every stage of their lives, whether you're a pensioner, whether you're in work, whether you're setting up a small business if you're self-employed, whether you're a young couple trying to buy your first home. I want to cut your taxes and deliver financial security. The alternative is Keir Starmer, who is going to put your taxes up, as we've seen over the years, which he disputes, I have to say. He's also just been here. Well, he's spent the last three days avoiding telling anyone about putting up council tax. All right.

On the 8th of June, I'm asking you to vote green, not because this election is about me or indeed about any of the party leaders here tonight. I'm asking you to vote green because this election is about you. It's about our country. It's about what kind of future we want for our children. And it's about standing up for our values of openness, cooperation, and compassion. And we face challenges that we can't possibly pretend to fix in the next hundred days or even the next thousand. We face challenges to our climate, and I'm sorry we haven't had more time to discuss that tonight. We face challenges to our economic future and our security. But we face opportunities, too, to be innovators and pioneers. So as you're watching this at home, I ask you this. Don't just think about who you'd vote for tonight. Think about who you want standing up with you and working alongside you. Together we can build a bigger, more confident and caring future. I'm ready and I hope you'll join me by voting Green on June the 8th. Thank you. Tim Farron.

I'm going to answer the first question, if I may, about the Paris climate change agreement. And I must tell you, I am disappointed. Caroline is right. I led the UK delegation to get that international agreement of 180 different countries pledging to that reduction. And it is disappointing that the Americans are pulling out. But I hope that we can use our relationship with President Trump and our close relationship with the US to try and influence, to try and make sure they nevertheless take the right step.

Well, Dennis, the NHS has two fundamental problems. One, Penny mentioned, caseloads up by 43%. Why? because we have an exploding population. All of our public services are under pressure because the population has increased by 6 million since the Conservatives came to power. It's impossible to keep up. Secondly, everyone talks about more money, more money. We've massively increased the amount we spend on the NHS. We've gone from spending about 7% of the national cake to over 11% of the national cake for worse and worse returns. And we need to look are countries like France who spend exactly the same sum of money but get far better services by people paying in to an insurance fund. It acts on a mutual basis. Those that can't pay don't. They get better results. Let's think more broadly.

Paul, I just don't know how you feel when you hear a Prime Minister say, having heard what you're going through, that the plan is working, it's all all right. I meet so many people who are struggling with their bills and the Prime Minister just keeps saying, he lives in a different world, that it's all all right.

Everything I've said during these three television debates is driven by my simple belief that if we do things differently, we can build a better, fairer Britain. As you decide how to cast your vote, of course you'll be told by these two that real change is dangerous, that it can't be done. But don't let anyone scare you from following your instincts. Together, next week, we can change Britain for good. Just think how many times you've been given lots of promises from these old parties, and when they get back into government, you find that nothing really changes at all. We can do so much better than that this time. Of course, I can't guarantee that all the problems you face will be solved overnight, but I can guarantee you that I will work tirelessly to deliver fairness for you. Fair taxes so that you pay less, but people at the top pay their fair share. A fair start, smaller class sizes for your children, a different approach to the economy and decent open politics that you can trust once again. I believe all this can happen. This is your election. This is your country. When you go to vote next week, choose the future you really want. If you believe, like I do, that we can do things differently this time, then together we really will change Britain. Don't let anyone tell you that it can't happen. It can. This time, you can make the difference.

I certainly do, yes. Actually, when it comes to a lot of grazing animals, sheep, for example, well-kept pastures, believe it or not, absorb carbon dioxide. So some of the arguments around meat are actually, I think, slightly false arguments. There are, I know, arguments about methane. Perhaps we won't go there right now. But just to think, that all meat farming is a disaster in terms of CO2 is actually factually wrong. I would just say this about climate change and, you know, you've come to me first on this. I mean, this will be a competition. Do you believe in it? I believe the climate's warming now, just as it was cooling 40, 50 years ago. But is it warming? Yes. Is that something we've got to think about seriously? Yes. But let's just bear this in mind.

Do you know how much the National Grid thinks that net zero is going to cost? Is it £2 trillion or is it £3 trillion? I'll tell you, it's £3 trillion, which is way more than the sort of sums I'm talking about. The reason that no one agrees with me from the establishment is because they don't want to talk about it. They don't want to admit it. We're the only country that is obsessed with net zero, and it's killing our jobs as we spend our jobs and our money.

Our country is crying out for change, and it's not hard to see why. Day in, day out, all over the country, people are striving just to get on in life. But under this Conservative government, too often it feels like we're fighting a broken system. Nothing works. Under Ed Davies' leadership, the Liberal Democrats are promising a fair deal for the British people. We will fix the NHS and social care, tackle the cost of living crisis and stop filthy raw sewage being dumped into our rivers and seas. A vote for the Liberal Democrats is a vote for a strong local champion, a vote for a fair deal, and a vote to help deliver the change that we all so desperately need. And in so many parts of the UK, a vote for the Liberal Democrats is the best chance of finally voting this disastrous Conservative government out of office.

Tonight, we've seen the real choice facing our country. between a Labour government and a Conservative government. This election will decide whether young people are saddled with debt or freed from it, whether we invest in our National Health Service, schools and social care, or they continue to be cut, whether older people get the dignity they deserve or see their incomes fall. On June the 8th, you have the power to decide. Vote Labour for the many, not the few.

Are you going to have a meeting with her? Are you going to try and bring her back? She used to be a Labour donor. Of course I'd meet with her. Of course I would. And, you know, again, my approach in life... I came into politics really late. I'm not as tribal as everybody else. I've run out of, you know, operations. I ran a public service. My view is, if there's an issue or a challenge, roll up your sleeves, get people round the table, and talk through the issues and find a way forward. So I'll always talk to anyone about this. The only rule, I suppose, I'd set is we've got to treat each other with respect. J.K. Rowling, I respect her and I don't suggest for a minute that she hasn't... She's made some really important points. I'd welcome that discussion because I do think that we've made huge progress on women's rights under Labour governments and equality, massive progress. There's more work to be done if we are... privileged to come in to serve this country, and I want to make sure that we can bring people together and, you know, take our country forward as well. Do comments like that get to you?

Let me say, first of all, my pride and my admiration for the armed forces and our questioner who was in the territorial army, all those who serve our nation, and particularly at this time when we're at war in Afghanistan, we've got to remember and we've got to do our best, not just by the armed forces, but by the families. And let us remember all those who lost their lives in Afghanistan. The important thing is that we're doing the right thing by our troops. And that's why we've increased the spending on equipment dramatically over these last few years. A thousand new vehicles, new helicopters brought into Afghanistan. We used to spend 600 million on Afghanistan three or four years ago. It is now 5,000 million this year. Now, this, every time, you know, I've got a right to a family where someone's died, I've got to consider all these issues. And I would not send our troops into battle unless I was absolutely sure that they were properly equipped for what they're doing.

To me, the NHS is personal, not political. My mum, who had ovarian cancer, from the point of her diagnosis, to her treatment, to her care, to her passing away in a ward just two floors away from the one where she'd given birth to me, like everybody out there, our experience with the National Health Service is personal. And let me finish. And the point is simply this. If you look at and you use National Health Services, you know, as we saw in Manchester just last week, how utterly brilliant, caring, dedicated and professional they are.

Thank you. Linda, I share your frustration. When I was younger, I had little to no faith that politics had the potential to make the country a better place. But let's remember politics at its root is about people. It's about people's lives, people's bank accounts, people's future, people's health and happiness. And that's why after meeting some Green Party members in my 20s, I realised that the Green Party is different. That's why I joined, that's why I got involved. Now as several of the other panellists have already pointed out, as Labour drop pledge after pledge, policy after policy, slide further and further towards the Conservatives so that you can barely get a cigarette paper between them, the argument for voting for a bit of diversity in the House of Commons becomes ever stronger and in terms of answering your question Linda about parties keeping their promises, The Green Party, when I joined it in 2011, was a pretty small and young party, but we're coming of age. We have increased our number of councillors nearly five-fold over the last few local elections, and we're grateful to everyone who's put their trust in us.

Because that was at a time when the budget deficit was forecast to be bigger than the deficit in Greece. That's what we inherited. So making that sort of promise then was meaningless. Now, having spent five years as Prime Minister, knowing what needs to be done, and let me be clear about... what still needs to be done. We've got to save one out of every £100 the government spends for each of the next two years. That's the extent of the efficiencies that are needed. And then we can start to see public spending growing again to make sure that we go on putting money into our schools and hospitals and all the important public services that we have. We may come back.

As Janet knows, and everyone knows, the NHS is still recovering from COVID. We went through the best part of two years where the NHS couldn't conduct all the treatments it normally would. And it is going to take time to recover from that. But we are now making progress. The waiting lists are coming down. But what Keir Starmer didn't mention to you, which you did, Julie...

Can you clarify that? There's absolutely nothing to support. They did nothing for 13 years. Do you support that? No. What I support is something I've supported all my adult political life, which is a complete clean-up from top to toe of politics, direct elections to the House of Lords. They shouldn't be there just because they've done favours to politicians making the rules that you need to abide by, getting big money out of them.

Well, first of all, what I'd say is that we've got a plan which is working. We've taken 3 million of the lowest paid people out of tax, and we've got almost 2 million people back into work. But I think what's absolutely crucial here is recognising that what our plan involves is balance. We're going to go on investing in the NHS every year, as we have done under this government under the last parliament. We'll go on doing that in the next parliament. We're going to find savings of one out of every hundred pounds that the government spends. And we need to do that for two more years, just as we've done for the last five years. But the alternative to that plan is actually putting up taxes. And I don't want to do that. I think that if we go back to the tax, the waste, the spending and the debt, all the things that got us into a mess in the first place, we wouldn't help working people, we'd hurt working people. That's what Labour did last time and we mustn't let it happen again.

I have to deal with these decisions every day. And I say to you, Nick, get real. Get real. Because Iran, you're saying, might be able to have a nuclear weapon, and you wouldn't take action against them. But you're saying that we've got to give up our Trident submarines and our nuclear weapon now. Get real about the danger that we face if we have North Korea, Iran, and other countries with nuclear weapons, and we give up our own.

You asked about it. It's very clear, pensioners under Keir Starmer will pay tax on the state pension for the first time in our country's history. It is a retirement tax and Keir Starmer can talk about the past all he wants but in the future pensioners have got a tax rise coming under your government. Why do you want to put up their taxes?

Well, I want to say to Paul Nuttall that immigrants who come to this country are making a huge contribution, both to our economy, to our communities, to our societies. And the idea that the problem that Paul sees with everything is always immigrants, I think is absolutely outrageous. You know, if you are trying to see a doctor, it's more likely that you will be treated by someone from a EU country, then you'll find them in front of you in the queue, trying to get through to the NHS. So those people in our countries are our lovers, they're our families, they're our plumbers, they're doctors, they make a vital contribution to our society. And the idea that we're going to be a better-off society by closing the door to immigrants I think is just clearly wrong. And it's clear that that's the case for the economy too, because right now you've got plenty of immigration coming from outside of the EU, The government's not stopping that because it recognises it's so crucial for our economy.

Can I go back to the original question? It breaks my heart to hear that you're being trained in the UK, and rather than thinking about going in to support the NHS, you're thinking about looking overseas. So one thing that we're promising to do is to lift the pay cap for public sector workers, including all the people that work for the National Health Service. At the moment, it's capped at 1%. We want wages to increase in line with inflation. So that's one promise we're going to make. In terms of funding the NHS, as an interim measure, in addition to the money that the Tories are promising, we're promising to put an extra £6 billion a year by putting one pence on the rate of income tax, 1% extra on the rate of income tax. Now you might think, well, why should everybody pay for that? What it means is the top 50% of earners will be paying 95% of that money. It's a very progressive way of doing it. We're being honest with you. We can't just keep borrowing. This has got to be paid for. We think it's worth paying for. It's an interim measure until we get an independent body to say, this is actually the amount of money each year that the NHS have. You then have a debate in Parliament, and then the Parliament either agrees with that independent body, And there'll be a separate line in your tax return that says, this is the money that's going to the NHS and social care. And that is the sustainable long-term plan that we have to fund the NHS and social care.

Thank you. Nick Clegg. Well, I think, Frank, you're right on the simple assertion you're making, that when the country's facing very big issues, we just talked about social care, but the one that you're highlighting is, of course, the black hole in the public finances. It is better I know it's an unfashionable thing to say, but it's better if politicians actually try and work together. That's why I proposed several weeks ago now that irrespective of the outcome of the next election, why don't we set up something which I call a Council for Financial Stability, and you'd gather in that Council for Financial Stability the Chancellor and the Shadow Chancellors, the Governor of the Bank of England, the Head of the Financial Services Authority, to do one simple thing. which is to come clean with you about how big this black hole is, because there's still dispute about exactly how bad it is, and then have an agreement on roughly how long it would take to fill it, so that everybody, in a sense, is being open with you, which hasn't really happened until now, about how bad this situation is and how together we're going to get out of it. So I do think there is potential for politicians to work with each other. Don't believe all these ludicrous scare stories about markets and political Armageddon if that is what happens.

Look, Jonathan is absolutely right. Look, the level, the scale of the ecological and climate emergency we're facing is the biggest challenge that human civilization has ever faced. You know, politicians often collapse into hyperbole, but everything that I've just said is true. I mean, we are facing the extinction of one million species on this planet. That's one in seven of all the species on this planet. And so you're absolutely right to call out each and every one of us to rise to that challenge. There's a positive here as well. We're on a wet and windy island, actually, which has huge potential resources in terms of marine energy. If we had the leadership that we deserve, you know, negative interest rates, There's never been a better time to borrow to invest in creating the green infrastructure, the platform for the future, not only to address the environmental catastrophe that we will face if we don't change course, but also the economic dividend, because the whole world is asking the same question. How do we decarbonize if we actually build the future faster and first? We will reap an economic dividend because we will create the innovation that will solve the world's problems and that will create an economic opportunity for Wales, Scotland, England and Ireland too.

We need, in Wales and across the countries and all the countries, we need an end to austerity. The way to achieve that is to get a Labour government and I do wish that Adam would realise that the people who have made the lives of people in Wales a misery is actually the Conservatives who have been in government for 20 years. Thank you. I just want to bring in Nigel.

This is about the courage of your convictions and principles. I believe when it comes to these matters, sex means biological sex. Of course, we should have compassion and tolerance for people who are going through questioning that. But fundamentally, when it comes to women's safety, women's only spaces, sex means biological sex for me. That is the only way to make sure you can deliver the protections that Vicky wants. And on this question, Keir Starmer, when one of his own female MPs made that point a while ago, he said that she was wrong. She was hounded, right? He said she was wrong. Now, he's now changed his mind on that question, that it goes back to trust, it goes back to having the courage of your convictions. I get that not all of you will agree with my position, right? But I'm being clear with you. Sex means biological sex. You have to change the Equalities Act to deliver the security of women's spaces and women's services. That's what I believe the right thing for our country is.

Nicola, what we will do in government is put further record levels of funding into schools. But we also do want to look at ensuring that the money is being distributed in a fair way. And as you probably know, there are some schools in the country today that may receive twice the amount of funding for a pupil that other schools receive. I think it's important that we see a fair way of funding our schools. What we're committed to is ensuring that as we introduce that, schools won't see any cash reduction in their level of funding. But I actually want to do more than that because I believe that education is absolutely crucial. We want our youngsters to get the best possible start in life. And that's why I want to see a more diverse system of schools available for people. I want to bring in some of the expertise that we see in universities. For example, in supporting schools, there are some good examples of that at the moment. Let's do more of that. Let's make sure that every child has a good school place, and a school place that is right for them. Because that's about ensuring they get the best possible start in life, to be the best that they can. Because I want our society to be one where it's down to your talents and hard work how far you go, and not where you came from or who your parents are.

Well, to be candid, my party's not going to be running the Treasury after this election, but we may well hold the balance of power. And therefore, we will be pressing for more investment in the National Health Service, an end to welfare cuts and austerity, more funding to tackle climate change. And we would bring to bear on that our experience in government in Scotland, where we have already reformed the income tax system. I, in Scotland, pay more tax as a high-paid person than I would do if I was living in England. But partly because of that, in Scotland, if you're a student, you don't pay tuition fees. If you're an older person, you don't have to pay for your personal care, we already have free prescriptions. So maybe it's a bit of that value-based approach to spending that we need to see right across the UK. Thank you. Joyce Winston?

I'm sure you recognise there are huge problems in the funding of our public services, health, education, and these have to be addressed and dealt with. We have a fully-costed and fully-funded manifesto here, and that will cost, yes, and we will raise corporation tax up to 26p by the end of the Parliament. And that will be actually 2% lower than it was in 2010 and will actually be less than the G7 average. What it will also do is not raise corporation tax for small and medium businesses by that amount. Some will have no rise whatsoever. And we have had very interesting and very good discussions with a lot of small businesses about their problems of accessing capital, their problems of growing because banks don't like them and are not interested in them, and the need to have an investment strategy for this country. So on top of that, we will also be forming a national investment bank to improve infrastructure all across the country and develop new high technology industries, which I think are the future of this country. Let the people with the skills actually develop the jobs and develop the industries in this country. And so, yes, we are asking the very biggest corporations to pay a bit more. But I tell you what, I think it's worth it. It's worth it so that any young person can go to university and not leave with debt. It's worth it to make sure school headteachers don't have to collect at the school gate in order to pay the teachers' salaries. I think it's worth it for a better society in which everyone can achieve something. Okay. Well done.

Let me respond to this point about the £6 billion as directly as I possibly can. £6 billion saving this year, so we stop the jobs tax next year. That means saving one out of every £100 that the government spends. That is the glossy leaflet that comes through your door from the local council. That's the one in £100. It's the fact that managers in the NHS got a 7% pay rise this year - that's the one in £100. We have the leaders of Britain's biggest and most successful businesses, Marks and Spencer's, Sainsbury's, Mothercare, Corus, the steelmakers, all saying that the risk to the recovery is not cutting waste. The risk to the recovery is putting up National Insurance on every job in the country, which is what Labour propose. So we say roll up your sleeves now, let's save the waste where we can to stop the taxes. It's the right thing to do, and it will help get our economy moving.

We wrote this manifesto, which I'm glad you approve of, by consensus within the Labour Party and our affiliated groups and organisations and from the contribution of hundreds of non-affiliated political groups who have very important views to communicate to us. We put those forward. In government, we will carry this manifesto out. This manifesto is about investing in our future. It is about investing in our young people for the future, so that we don't have supersized classrooms, we don't have massive waiting lists in hospitals, we don't have a million people waiting for social care, we don't have thousands of people sleeping on our streets. The choice is quite clearly there. This manifesto, investing for the future, taxing more, a bit, for corporation tax and for the very wealthiest, 95% will pay no more, or you can go down the road of continuing austerity, continuing cuts in all areas of public expenditure and an ever-widening gap between the richest and the poorest in our society. I am very proud of this manifesto, proud to lead this party, and you know what, I'll be very proud to put this manifesto into action by legislation and by government. And I'm looking forward to the chance of doing it.

Yeah, first of all, I mean, I was laughing. It was so much to laugh at Nigel's response. But I mean, the fact that he said the science wasn't settled, I mean, science literally is there to settle things. It's empirical data. Nigel, it's empirical data. You can't argue. All right. Could we just hear your take? The point I would make to Aidan is, look, in Scotland, and there's a young man here who also mentioned this point, that we should be at the forefront. We should be global leaders. Yes, we know China emits and pollutes more. We know India pollute more. But I would like Scotland as an independent nation, but of course, while we're still part of the UK, to be leading the way. And we're doing that. Our targets actually for net zero are five years ahead of the UK, we're 2045. Our 75% target at 2030 goes far beyond what experts say is needed. But let me also just make this point to all of you as young people. that I'm exceptionally proud of the role that you've played in bringing this to the agenda at the front of the agenda right to the top of all the politicians sitting here because I know the Conservative government ticked you off and chided those young people for skipping school and how dare you and it was terrible. I was really proud to be part of a Scottish government that said you know what bloody well done because you brought the issue right up the agenda exactly where it needs to be so well done for that.

The climate crisis is even more important than Brexit. But Brexit is a climate crime. It is morally wrong to leave our seat at the table and give up our influence to create the change we need right across the world. We have two paths ahead of us. one where our children inherit a poisoned planet, or one where our children breathe clean air, where going green is the easier, cheaper option, where we protect our planet for future generations.

It is exactly how we have done them that means we are attracting the investment and creating the jobs, which is already making a difference to so many people's lives. That is a practical example of what we have done. The choice at this election is about the future. We are not going to go back to Brexit. This is about the future. When it comes to the economy, And when it comes to making sure people who are working hard have the opportunities they deserve, what I want to do is build on the progress we've made and give you and every other young person that you're concerned about the opportunity to get a job with a new apprentice, if that's the right thing for them, and then keep more of their hard-earned money by cutting their taxes. That is what I want to do for people. And actually, outside of the EU, we're able to do things that will drive more growth, create more jobs that will allow me to cut more taxes. That's the choice at this election.

Jo, the Green Investment Bank is succeeding in the private sector. If you don't think there's a role for the private sector in meeting our climate change obligations, then you've gone the same loony direction as the Labour Party. We need tens of billions of pounds of private investment to meet our climate change obligations. 92 billion pounds of private investment has gone already into renewables. We need hundreds of billions more. Robert, can I take offence with your language?

Well what we're going to do Faisal, I'm sorry it's exactly the same as I've just said on the social care cap, is once again I think this is an issue where we need not just to pull out a figure in an election campaign, but actually to listen to people, to talk to people, to take people's views and then make a proposal as government. So there's a proper consultation. So we're actually hearing from voters, we're hearing from organisations and charities and others who work with older people about what they think is the right level to set that threshold for winter fuel payments at. But the key thing, the key principle that would drive what we do is that less well-off pensioners will be protected.
I think that it's a desirable form of energy myself, providing, er...

We want a bank levy to get the money off the banks that all of us have had to put into the banks. Do I want to cut taxes on all businesses, particularly small businesses to get the economy moving? You're damned right I do. We've got to get this economy moving otherwise we're not going to get the jobs, we're not going to get the investment and the wealth that we need. The Prime Minister's got to face up to the fact that, right now, it's not working. Small businesses come to my surgery and say, "I've never gone over my overdraft limit, I've never broken my covenants, but I cannot get a loan." We've stuffed these banks full of money. They're not lending. We need some action from a government that's going to roll up its sleeves, stop trying to defend its hopeless record and recognise we need change to get our economy moving.

Well, do you know, it is what we would do if we win a majority. Right? I just think we have to level and be straightforward about it. We are a pro-European party. We always have been. And if we fight an election on the basis of stopping Brexit and people give us a majority, don't be surprised if we then stop Brexit. But as I say, we have led that campaign for a people's vote. And we, with more Liberal Democrat MPs in the next Parliament, will campaign for a people's vote if we're not able to revoke.

Thank you. Nicola Sturgeon. I mean, nothing is more important to me than education. And as First Minister of Scotland, I've got a big job of work to do to make sure we are really improving education in Scotland. We've got a good education system, but we've also got some challenges that we are working really hard to address. Before I come on to school education, early years is really important. Giving younger children the best start through quality early years in childcare is something we're prioritising. So we're going to double the provision of that over the life of this Parliament. In schools, we're trying to reduce bureaucracy that teachers are working under, because they've told us rightly it is too heavy. So we're trying to take that out. We've got a recruitment challenge in teaching, like many other countries do. We're trying to encourage more teachers into education. But we're also doing something that is new for Scotland. We're giving resources directly to headteachers, so not to councils and then to headteachers, but directly to headteachers and freeing them up to decide how that money is spent, because teachers know best how to improve education in our schools.

I'm happy if there are charities, if there are charities or independent organisations that can provide a great service free as part of the NHS. then I think that if it's good health care, that's what matters to me. I love our NHS. It has done amazing things for my family. I will never forget taking my desperately ill young son, Ivan, to hospital night after night, getting into A&E, getting amazing treatment, everything that they gave him. I want to make sure that that is always there for families in our country. Now, that would always be predominantly A national health service provided by national health service providers. The private sector, the independent sector, is a tiny proportion of the total. I think it's gone from 5% to 6% under this government. A tiny increase. But if there are organisations, like for instance Marie Curie Cancer Care or Macmillan Nurses, who provide brilliant care for loved ones, or hospices, which aren't part of the NHS, if there are great organisations like that, that are helping our loved ones, and they're free, and the NHS and our taxes are helping to pay for them, that's OK by me.

What it means is Theresa set out 12 points. for the negotiations. He gave a very important speech at Lancaster House a few months ago. So controlling our borders, making sure that we have the best trade deal possible with our European partners. We remain European. We're just coming out of the EU institutions.

Tonight, I'm going to correct one big lie and tell one big truth. First, the big lie, get Brexit done. Boris Johnson's rotten deal will not get it done. It's just the start of years more wrangling. His oven-ready meal is made of chlorinated chicken and it will make us all sick. And when we turn up at the hospital, we'll find it's been flogged off to Trump's America. That's why the Green Party supports a people's vote on Brexit and will campaign wholeheartedly to remain. Now, the big truth. From the Arctic to the Amazon, the world is on fire. Scientists say we have just 10 years to stop runaway climate chaos. This emergency requires us to rise up and to be our best. An opportunity not just to avert disaster, but through an ambitious Green New Deal to fix Britain for good. This election is the greatest, perhaps the last, chance to change course. Children can't vote. The planet can't vote. It is up to you. And I ask you, if not now, when?

I think the best way, first of all Cara, thank you for the question, we were just getting into it with Jeremy back there. Look, I think Britain is at its best when we're out there trading and cooperating and working with others in the world. We are an open country, we're a trading nation, we're part of all the most important networks, whether it's NATO or the G8 or the European Union, and we need them to work for us. But I think the problem with the European Union at the moment, it's got some good aspects, but too many things that drive people mad. People see that it's trying to become too much of a state rather than an organisation. It's trying to take too much power. And I think what we need is a new deal with Europe where we say, look, We want to be in Europe for trade and cooperation. We want that common market that we were promised all those years ago. But we don't want to be part of an ever closer union. Maybe the Eurozone countries with their own currency, maybe they need to do that. We don't. And we're going to keep the pound as our currency. Well, we will at least if I'm prime minister. So if I can get a new deal, from Europe, and I believe I can. I've sat around that table for the last five years with those other countries. If I can get that better deal, I think there's then a real prospect of saying to the British people, you choose. You can stay in on this reformed basis, which would be good for Britain, or if you want, you can leave. But the British public have got to be given another choice. We haven't had one since 1975, and it's time we did.

Well we say we want to get to that point but we will invest in our green prosperity plan because as Lucy's question was put to us actually we do need to look at economic growth and it's not divorced from also looking at climate policies. We can't ignore the fact that we do have to change and Nigel says we have to use oil and gas and you're right and we've recognised that that will be part of the future but it won't be forever. So we do have to have a green transition and that's what our Green Prosperity Plan is all about, about creating those jobs, insulating homes and having our self-reliant energy needs for the future for your great British energy. Stephen Flynn, the SNP.

Well, all of this is about giving you financial security, which is why I want to keep cutting people's taxes so they have more money in their pockets at the end of every month. Now that we've got the economy in a better place, we started that at the end of last year. So this year, everyone in work is getting a tax cut that's worth about £900 on average. So that will really help people with their bills as well as inflation coming down. And if I'm re-elected, we're going to go further. And we're going to keep cutting your taxes at every stage in your life. So if you're in work, you'll keep getting more tax cuts. If you're self-employed, we're going to abolish national insurance, self-employed people. So all of that is going to put more money in everyone's bank account, give you financial security. But crucially, again, this is a choice. This election is a choice. We can build on the progress we've made, keep cutting taxes, or as I've been pointing out, if Keir Starmer is your Prime Minister, all of you are going to face higher taxes. That is not going to help anyone with any of the bills, Linda, that you're talking about if the Labour Party is taking more tax out of your bank account every month.

If you end up with a privatised service, that's what you get. If you end up with that US trade deal, that is what you get. Now, the Prime Minister says he's not going to do that sort of a trade deal. If that's the case, why did the talks go on for two years? It doesn't take two years to say no to privatisation of the NHS. OK, thank you, gentlemen. Thank you very much indeed. Thank you very much indeed.

Well, at the present time, our problem is we're losing people. I don't know how many people saw the reports last week. The issue for us is people are now leaving, and that will have a detrimental impact on our NHS and our public services.

It was a conference decision by the Labour Party, and as the leader of the party, I accept the democracy of our party. Is that morally right? In answer to the questions put earlier, I made the point that as Prime Minister, I will do all I can to bring about a nuclear-free world because I'm horrified at the very idea. You promised to renew. of a nuclear attack. He promised to win you a nuclear weapon. Is that morally right? Listen, it's there in our manifesto because our conference voted for it. I have to accept that decision. Do you think it's morally right? Can I finish? Can I finish? What I want to see... I'm asking you perfectly simply, do you think it's morally right? What I want to see is a nuclear-free world. Of course everybody wants to see that. But is it morally right? I'm not so sure about that. I'm not so sure about that. If that's what you believe, is it morally right to renew a nuclear deterrent? That is the decision that's been taken. We will work for a nuclear-free world. I note you don't answer. We will work through the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty to achieve that. That surely is something well worth doing.

I hope so, that's my intention. I want to be in the next parliament, leading our much much bigger parliamentary party for the Liberal Democrats. I don't know how far our ambitions can go because we've got a very perverse voting system when it's three party politics as it is in this election. But I think it can be substantially bigger, the opportunity in the next parliament is substantially greater and when you've devoted your working life towards that objective, heavens above, you don't want to shirk off that opportunity. I'll be in there with enthusiasm, particularly as I do really genuinely feel, probably like every parent in history that ...that all the things I've argued for, I've now got this additional stake in the future, that I didn't have until a week ago.

Look, we have had some very difficult times. We have had to spend a lot of money on things like furlough. That was the right thing to do, to keep jobs and livelihoods going, not just hand out welfare payments. The pandemic has been expensive and we've also had terrible global shocks. We've had to step in and pay people's energy bills. But we are coming through that, thanks to your efforts. What you need now is disposable income in your pockets. You need taxes to be cut. We have announced already we're making cuts on your taxes regarding your home, your pension, work. We need to continue to do that. If you get us, we will reduce your taxes. That's in our DNA as conservatives. That's not what is in Labour's DNA. They always put up taxes. They're going to put yours up by £2,000. They're going to increase your energy bills, and you're going to get a whole lot of costs with their net zero plans. My constituents cannot afford that.

What about the thousands of scientists who actually agree with me, but of course the establishment doesn't want to talk about? The truth is, I'll come back to my original point, climate change is real, but it's cheaper to adapt to it than think you can stop it.

No I think that's a fair point but what I'm saying to Scotland right now and as I said if I come to that point then I will set out exactly to people in Scotland what I think we should do then but what I'm saying to Scotland now in a general election campaign is we have the ability to to send that message and make that view and that voice very clear. And that's what I hope people in Scotland will do for that reason. But I also hope they'll vote SNP so that we can contribute to all of these other social and economic policies that I've been talking about. OK, there's a few hands up.

As you sit on your sofa with your cup of tea, watching this election unfold, you might be thinking, surely we deserve better than this depressing duo of Boris Johnson and Jeremy Corbyn. Boris Johnson, caught lying again and again. He thinks the rules don't apply to him. And frankly, he only cares about himself. He doesn't deserve to win a majority and the Liberal Democrats can stop him. You have the power to stop Brexit and create a brighter future. Vote Liberal Democrat.

Gordon Brown. My first thoughts will always be for our troops, for our dedicated forces, for our professional forces. And every day I meet people who have got people serving in Afghanistan or have served in other parts of the world. And we've got to do everything we can to support them. Yes, there will be a defence review in the next parliament so we can do our best by our forces. But, Luke, what happened on Christmas Eve? It was a bomber who would have been in Detroit and bombing that plane, came from Somalia. That was really where he was given his orders from. So we have got to deal with al-Qaeda in Somalia and Yemen, as well as in Pakistan. And we've got to be clear that we cannot allow terrorists to have territory in the world that then they use as a base for attacking the United Kingdom. I will do everything in my power to protect people, but we rely on the most brilliant, the most dedicated, the great forces that I want to pay tribute to this evening.

We have a UK Government that is saying to Ireland, rightly, that Brexit does not mean a hard border between the north and south of Ireland, it does not mean interruption to trade. The Prime Minister went to Dublin and said Ireland does not have to choose between trading with Europe and the UK. How is it that they say that Ireland but try to pretend in Scotland that something different would be the case. We should protect our trade with the UK but in my view also try to protect our trade within the single market. The biggest single market in the world that is eight times bigger than the UK market. That's the best of both worlds that Scotland should be seeking to secure.

The only thing I want to add is that Graham used that word, it's a powerful word - that you find it "galling"; one of the reasons, Graham, you might find it galling is that when you've retired - did you say you had retired? - is when you're retired that of course you then find that the benefits that you get, particularly the state pension, are not as generous as I think they should be. One of the things we need to do immediately after this general election is restore the earnings link in pensions. There's been a huge amount of talk about it from both the old parties about doing that, lets get on with it and do it. It was broken some years ago by the Conservative government, hasn't been restored under 13 years of the Labour government. Lets get on and do that, the least that you Graham and everybody else deserves when you retire, having worked hard, having paid into the common pot, is that you get a decent state pension when you retire, and I hope that will do something to make that experience you're talking about, feel a little less galling.
I don't use private health. I use the NHS. That's where my wife works, in one of the big hospitals. It runs through my dear...

So real leadership is about putting the country before you put your party and I think we all now know that this was a totally unnecessary election and the only reason Theresa May called it was that she thought she was going to have a massive majority as a result of it. whatever happened to strength and stability? Where's strength and stability gone? Weak and wobbly is where we are, not so much the Iron Lady as the U-turn Queen. That is not the leadership that we require. In contrast, In contrast, as leader of the SNP in the House of Commons, the third party in the House of Commons, I am proud that we have led the effective opposition when sadly the Labour Party has too often dropped the ball, asking questions not just about things that matter to people in Scotland but the rest of the UK, in Europe and the world. And I am confident that the SNP will be returned to Westminster as the third party because we need to protect our country from the Tories who sadly I fear are going to win this election. I am working hard to win in every single seat in Scotland, Misha, that is our plan, but I believe people in Scotland realise that it's either the SNP or it's the Tories, and whether they're Labour voters, Liberal Democrats or Greens, they understand that if they vote for those parties they'll be doing nothing but helping the Tories.

If you go for a three-year settlement it means the available money can be used better because any council leader will tell you that trying to make the best use of the money within the constraints of a 12-month period doesn't work. So it's better use of existing money. But look, I'm not going to stand here and say there's some magic wand that I can wave the day after the election and find money that isn't there. Huge damage has been done to our economy. It is going to take time. That's why our manifesto is a manifesto for growing our economy, for creating wealth, to making sure that we can make sure all of our public services, whether that's councils or others, are properly funded, providing the services that people expect and actually, frankly, deserve, which is not the case at the moment.

Well you can call it what you like. I think it's fair play. I think it's common sense, and I think it's fair play. These people have all paid their taxes. They've paid towards the NHS. I think it's right that if they shorten the waiting lists on the NHS, if they enable the NHS to treat someone else, it's right that the NHS should pay a proportion of what it would have cost the NHS to treat them.

You don't bring about nuclear disarmament by spending £200 billion on new nuclear weapons. I would never press the nuclear button because it would kill tens of millions of people. I think nuclear weapons are a huge waste of money that would be better spent on health and other public services, but I think they're immoral. We don't countenance countries using chemical weapons any longer. Why on earth should we countenance weapons that could wipe out swathes of civilisation? They are morally wrong and it's time to get rid of them.

There are 17,000 more nurses now than there were under the Labour Party. And that's because we've had a strong economy that's enabled us to make investments. And we are now going to step those investments up, because we believe passionately in the NHS. And the way to take this country forward, the way to get it moving, is to get rid of the blockade in Parliament, get Brexit done, and move on. There are 43,000... Jeremy Corbyn... Let me just ask you, Mr Corbyn...

Sian Berry. I'm sorry, I've spent a lot of my life listening to politicians list green things they want to do and even list green policies and it isn't about lists. So many of these pledges end up on a dusty shelf after we stop shining a light on them. And a light's on them today, and they're saying an awful lot of things. But I just cannot sit back and watch this happen again. I mean, Greta Thunberg is only a teenager. She's already sick of hearing broken promises. Think how I feel at my age. Now, in my own life, I'm a private renter. I still do not have the ability to do my own home. So there's not a lot I can do, but I can carry on working to change the system. And it does need to be a comprehensive system change. The science demands it. The emergency demands it. And it isn't about listing things. It's about changing the way things work for good.

No, absolutely and emphatically not, because it would lead to the deaths of possibly tens of millions of people and wipe out swathes of our civilisation. So no, under no circumstances would I use nuclear weapons.

Well, I would say in answer to that question, judge us on our record. On our record, we have We have cut the deficit, we've reduced taxes for the lowest paid, and we've made sure that we've continued to invest in the NHS, which is getting another £8 billion a year by the end of this Parliament. But the only way we can have that money to invest in our public services, and we know the importance of our public services, our schools, our hospitals, is to make sure that we have a strong economy, which does sometimes main making difficult choices, those sort of choices that no other party here is prepared to face up to. We have to make sure that we control our spending so we have that strong economy. But the sure way to bust our economy, to lead to danger, is to have false negotiations with the EU, to have the sort of leader like Jeremy Corbyn, supported perhaps by the people here, who would create just confusion when trying to deal with the 27 different states.

You're absolutely right to say about trusting politics. That's why, when I've spoke on this panel, there's things that we'd really like to do that we can't do, and we've been honest and upfront about that. But I also think we need our politicians who make the rules to play by the rules as well. So bringing back integrity and ethics into our politics, which is what Keir Starmer will do in Labour, is exactly what we need to do, because people cannot go around, as they have done, breaking the rules and think they can get away with it. The Prime Minister did. The previous Prime Minister to that has done and I think that's undermined all of us as politicians.

The second thing you ought to do, after scrapping net zero, you can save about £35 billion a year by the Bank of England not voluntarily paying taxpayers cash interest on the money it printed, enriching the citizens' institutions. So between those two, that's £60 billion. And then, look, everybody knows there's government waste anyway. Let me tell you what you've got to do. You've got to cut out the back office waste and invest it in frontline growth and public services. That's how you get the economy growing again.

Thank you. Caroline Lucas. I just want to thank that so much for that question, because the Green Party has been trying to get climate change onto this election campaign agenda for the past six weeks. It has been impossible. So thank you so much for that. It is a vital question. It is the greatest threat that we face. And I think, first of all, in terms of what we do to President Trump, I'm trying to think of a polite answer. But I think essentially what we need to do actually is ignore him, because the economics behind renewables It's already such that we know that renewables are going to be cheaper than the fossil fuels that they already replace. So that revolution is already happening. But what we need to do in this country is an awful lot more than is being done by this government or, indeed, previous ones. First of all, we need to leave 2 thirds of all known fossil fuel fossil fuels in the ground if we're to have any hope of avoiding catastrophic climate change. That means a massive investment in renewable energy, energy efficiency. Amber Rudd, of course, was the Energy Secretary for a while, but under her we did not see the kind of transition we urgently need to see if we're serious about climate change. So we do need to see keeping fossil fuels in the ground, not giving fossil fuel subsidies. We need to see no fracking, not the kind of frack tracking that is being planned all around the country under this government.

The question was aimed at obviously how we look after our elderly and the point is the elderly in this country at the moment get a really raw deal. What we need is a flexible state pension that allows people to take their pension younger if they choose. We also should be committed to keeping the triple lock to ensure that pensioners get a fair deal and we will fight tooth and nail the new announcement by the Tories today which basically amounts to a death tax which punishes people for simply living.
Absolutely. That's a Labour police crime commissioner as well.

Let's just be really clear about our position. As Liberal Democrats, we want to stop Brexit. I don't think that's news to people. And so we said, if we win a majority Liberal Democrat government, we would revoke Article 50, going from 20 MPs to 326. I think it would be a pretty seismic electoral shift. And therefore, there would be that democratic mandate.

Well, I said very, very frankly that, you know, if it's a hung Parliament, we'll do our best to make it work. If we win the election outright, I'd do everything I can to try and take the country with us on some of these difficult decisions, and also try and take other politicians with us, because there will be some hard times before we get out of this and actually get the deficit sorted. But there is a problem in that you do need to have agreement, and there's a fundamental disagreement between me and the other two parties about what we do this year. I think we badly need to roll up our sleeves and make some savings this year so we can stop the jobs tax, the national insurance tax rise that is coming down the road. Because I think when you've got an economy that's trying to recover, the worst thing you can do is put a tax on every single job in our country. And that's why over 1,100 business leaders have said, don't do this. They've said very clearly, the threat to our recovery is not cutting waste. The threat is the jobs tax.

Well look, there's been no vote cast at this general election and Susannah, I absolutely understand what you're saying. The cost of living, you can't escape what's happened and Liz Trust crashed the economy which sent mortgages going sky high. We've been at the mercy of the global energy prices and therefore energy prices have gone significantly high for people and also food prices. So Labour's first plan and priority is to secure the economy. We will never go fast and loose with public finances because it's working people who are paying the price for that. and Great British Energy, which is putting a windfall tax on the big oil and gas companies, which will allow us to set up the publicly owned energy company, which will give taxpayers money back, bring down bills and make us secure for our energy needs for the future and create thousands of jobs here in the UK. That is real change. That's how we'll deliver an economy that works for working people. And the Tories should never be allowed to forget that the reason they're in the cost of living crisis now is because they crashed the economy and went fast and loose with the public finances. Penny Mordaunt.

Labour seem to confuse the economy with the government. What we're saying is, save government waste to put money back in people's pockets. That's what - if you think about this, saving one out of every £100, that is something every small business, every large business, many families have had to do in this country, and government should do the same. Gordon's argument, in a way, is "Let me go on wasting your money, so I can put up your taxes next year", and it's taxes on people earning £20,000, £21,000. These are not rich people. They shouldn't be paying for the mistakes of the bankers and for the dreadful record of Gordon's government.

I think people will see straight through that as an attempt to try and frighten people. I have two children. My eldest is at a state school in London. I want every penny of the education budget to follow children like mine across the playground and into the school. I say after 13 years of a Labour government, there's a lot of waste we can cut out. There are quangos in education that spend £300 million a year. There's the fact that headteachers get 4,000 pages of information a year. The Department of Children, Schools and Families spent £3 million on itself, including, and I'm not making this up, a massage suite and contemplation room. I know that working in this government can be tough, but we don't need those sorts of things. Cut the waste, get the money into the classroom, and please stop trying to frighten people.

Boris Johnson. Andrew, thank you very much. And we will certainly be making some big investments in, as I say, in the NHS, in putting more police on the streets, in lifting up our education funding for every school in the country. We're going to continue with that. But what we won't do is borrow hugely to fund day-to-day investment. And that is the mistake that the Labour Party would make. They would rack up are debts, they would put up spending to 1.2 trillion. To answer your question, what that would mean is a tax burden of £2,400 per taxpayer. You hear from Labour sometimes that the burden is... I think Mr Corbyn said it just now, the burden is going to fall only on the 150 billionaires or the top 5%. Actually, people on as little as £20,000 a year under their plans will be paying £1,000 a year more in tax.
You're denying climate change. Your spokesman was. It's how you adapt to it. Your spokesman was. It's about how you adapt to climate change. And the point is that net zero is the one answer.

I think, again, Adam, it's really disappointing that you're playing the Tory game by blaming everybody apart from the Tories. In Wales, the Labour Party has done a very good job with the National Health Service in the dreadful situation of all those cuts to funding from the Conservative government nationally. It might suit your electoral purposes in Wales to blame the Labour Party on everything, but let's not pass the buck. Thank you.

Jeremy, I know there is no extra payment you don't want to add to, no tax you don't want to rise, but the fact is we have to concentrate our resources on the people who need it most and we have to stop thinking, as you do, that there's a magic money tree. You have to be accountable for the money you want to spend.

Let me be plain. We're not going to do a deal with the Scottish National Party. We're not going to have a coalition. We're not going to have a deal. And let me just say this to you, Simon. If it meant we weren't going to be in government, not doing a coalition, not having a deal, then so be it. I'm not going to sacrifice the future of our country, the unity of our country. I'm not going to give in to SNP demands around Trident, around the deficit, or anything like that. So I just want to repeat this point to you. I'm not going to have a Labour government if it means deals or coalitions with the Scottish National Party. And I just want to say this to voters in Scotland, David. There's no easy route here to vote SNP and get a Labour government. If you want a Labour government, you need to vote Labour.
So as you saw on the program, on the clip earlier, the 2015 police budget was protected.

Well one of Labour's policies is free personal care for older people as a start to create the kind of care service we need for our older people and other people suffering from dementia and other illnesses and also we will be, of course, properly resourcing our National Health Service with £26 billion extra every year in real terms. That's £40 billion cash. Because our society is, thankfully, ageing. People, by and large, are living longer. So we need extra investment to ensure that everyone can live in dignity.
But I may well do so to the Metropolitan Police when we get the inquiry.
But it is in Strasbourg, Fiona. That means it's not a British court.

It's very dangerous in elections to disagree with people. But I don't agree with you, sir. I actually think... Well, you're wrong. Well, OK. I should be there. I should be there. APPLAUSE I think actually if you look at our health system and compare it with others in the world it comes out very strongly on its performance and it also comes out very strongly on how cost effective it is and as we become a wealthier country we should put more money into our NHS so it's always there for us. Look there's a lot we can do to take pressure off the NHS, we've got to do better in terms of public health, we've got to do better in terms of health education, there are all sorts of things about diabetes and smoking and other things where public health issues can really take the pressure off the NHS, but I'm a profound believer that our model is the right one and if as long as I'm Prime Minister it will be free at the point of use, available to all on the basis of need and we will go on putting the money it needs into it, not least because we have taken the difficult decisions elsewhere that some people have been talking back to me. The lady behind me here.

I think the Health Service is a special case, but look, in, in the end people again are going to have to make a judgement about it. I can't, I can't sit here and write the budget for every year of a, of a Labour government. What I can say

You have a black hole of $38.5 billion. Their manifesto was published today. I don't know how you can talk about black holes. Labour's manifesto was published today. $38.5 billion is the tax gap in their plans. And the only way, because public spending is going to have to go up, that's the demand. The only way they are going to be able to do that is to raise taxes. They've only declared about a quarter of the taxes they're going to put up. They're going to have to put up a lot more, and they haven't yet told you how they're going to do that. We have absolutely... You can't afford that.

Lucy, I grew up in an NHS family. Dad was a GP and my mum was a pharmacist. I spent my entire childhood working in her pharmacy and I saw firsthand what an unbelievable difference the NHS made to my parents' patients and their lives. They became part of our family. And that's why I and my party are deeply committed to the NHS and investing in it. We've outlined a plan for £34 billion more. That will go on putting 50,000 nurses on our wards, 50 million more GP appointments and new and upgraded hospitals. But I say this, it's because we all care so deeply about the NHS that we must get Brexit done so that we can focus on it properly and give it the support and the investment that it deserves.
But the issue here is, will you continue to fund the police?

Jonny, I think it's all about balance, isn't it? That's what I don't think that you should be faced, Jonny, with the stark choice of either cutting too much, George Osborne and David Cameron's plan is for £50 billion of cuts, way beyond what's needed, or borrowing too much, which is Ed Balls and Ed Miliband's plan. They want to borrow £70 billion more than is necessary. That's a dismal choice, cutting too much or borrowing too much. It's a balance, and it does mean, yes, you need to reduce spending, but you also need to make those with the broadest shoulders the wealthiest to pay a bit more through the tax system to balance the books. That's the way you can balance the books, do it fairly, also then, of course, put money into public services, perhaps most especially the NHS, which does need more money because of the fact we've got an ageing population. So the Liberal Democrat plan is a very simple one. We'll cut less than the Conservatives and we'll borrow less than Labour.

Rhun ap Iorwerth. Absolutely. I mean, we're already seeing the impacts of climate change. It was disturbing, if I may say, hearing a spokesman on behalf of the Reform Party speaking on television in Wales last night with all the classic climate change-denying tropes being...

This is important to clarify. is damaged and we need to fix that. But one thing I would say that I think is really positive about this general election is more people have registered to vote and young people are engaged. It's fantastic that we've got so many young people here tonight that are engaging in this question time and whether you're in what's considered a safe seat or not, you can engage and change what happens in your local community. Not just with a vote, but by being active and get your voice heard. Just like we found with the students with the broken promise by the Lib Dems on tuition fees, they will make their voices heard and they will make it count.

Of course there are pressures on public services. There are pressures on public services, and immigration is one of the pressures on public services. But the reason why our national health service is failing is because of David Cameron's government. Look, we've seen five years of the NHS going backwards. We've seen five years of a top-down reorganisation that nobody wanted and nobody voted for. And people's experience in A&E, people's experience waiting longer for operations, That can be turned around. And we've got a plan to turn it around. And a shout from behind me that Labour started that. I'm proud of our record on the NHS. When we took over in 1997, people were waiting 18 months. Then it was 18 weeks. And now we've seen the NHS going backwards and backwards under David Cameron. And I really fear for the future of the NHS with his extreme spending plans. That's why we've got a plan to turn it around. And it's a funded plan.

Look, we've heard lots of warm words here about the NHS. Of course, we all love the NHS, but the NHS doesn't need warm words. It needs hard cash. And I'll give you the precise number of how much extra cash it needs. It needs £8 billion by the end of the next Parliament. That is what a man called Simon Stevens, who runs NHS England, he's independent of politics, has said it needs. Now, the Liberal Democrats have a plan about how to find that £8 billion. Unlike the Conservatives, we'll ask the richest to pay a little extra in tax for the NHS, closing the reliefs, for instance, in capital gains tax. Unlike Labour, we'll also be able to put money in the NHS because we'll actually get the job done of balancing the books and then we can invest in the NHS. That is the way to balance the books and invest in the NHS. It means £8 billion more here, £800 million more for the NHS in Scotland and £450 million more in Wales. And my challenge to the leaders here is if you love the NHS so much, why don't you put your money where your heart is? Nick Clegg, thank you. Leanne Wood.

I've made the position clear. We will have a referendum. We will have negotiations, and we will abide by that result. It's the British people who will make that decision. Yes, we've heard that. Thank you, Mr. Corbyn. We've heard that. I do think, though, that since the Prime Minister has claimed the NHS is not under threat, why then did his government agree with the US that there should be, in the terms of negotiations, full market access for United States medical companies into our national health service.

Thank you for raising this and for everybody who probably does know this. I accept that many families, parents work hard and save hard to send their children to private school because they've got aspirations for their children and they think that's the best place for their child. And I've got nothing against that, nothing against private schools, and I completely understand that for some parents that's quite hard to do because there's not a lot of money going around. But I genuinely believe that every single parent has aspiration for their children across the whole country, including every single parent who sends their children to state school. And this is really important. And at state secondaries at the moment, we don't have enough teachers in core subjects like maths. Now, my boys just done GCSEs, and so I know that if you don't have a maths teacher, at the moment they've got, in many schools, supply teachers or PE teachers doing maths. That means you don't do as well in your exams, that will inhibit you in your A-levels and apprenticeship. So we've got to recruit the numbers of teachers we need in our state secondary schools, which is why we're going to get rid of the tax break, which it is for private schools, and use it to fund the teachers we need in our state secondary schools. That is a tough choice. It's a difficult choice. I do understand parents who... have worked really hard. I would just additionally say this. The schools don't have to pass this on to the parents. The schools could absorb the cost themselves. But if you ask me, am I going to make a choice to make sure that every single child, whatever their background, wherever they come from, and whichever school they go to, has the right opportunity for core subjects like maths, I'm going to make the choice every single time to make sure that every child has that chance, every single child, whatever their background.

You're right, Nick, they are under-equipped and they are underpaid. I think there's something seriously wrong when you've got 8,000 bureaucrats in the Ministry of Defence who work on communications. When we have too many top brass in some of the services, there are, I think, two admirals for every warship. We have 17 brigadiers for every brigade. And we also, of course, have this consensus from the Conservative and Labour parties that we should spend £100 billion renewing the Cold War nuclear Trident missile system. I say, if we change our priorities, we can provide our brave servicemen and servicewomen, who do the most astonishing job in the most extraordinarily difficult circumstances, we can give them proper pay.
