I think it's a scandal that someone who starts in the army on a junior rank now gets paid £6,000 less than someone starting as a firefighter or in the police service. I want them to have the same pay and I also want them to have proper body armour, proper helicopters, proper vehicles and you can only do that if you cut out spending elsewhere which isn't being well spent.

Well, it depends what those climate policies are. And at the moment we're pursuing completely unrealistic climate policies. I mean, the Labour pledge to decarbonise the grid by 2030 will not happen, just as the Conservative pledge to decarbonise the grid by 2035 will not happen. Not only is it impossible, it's actually unaffordable. So we have to be grown up and sensible. If we get new technologies that give us cheaper energy or better ways of manufacturing things, that's great. But what we're doing in this country is we're sacrificing economic growth. We'd be massacring British manufacturing. And we say to ourselves, isn't it marvellous? We've reduced carbon emissions more than any other Western country. But we haven't. All we've done is to export carbon emissions when our steel works go to India, or our car manufacture moves to China, or to Turkey, or wherever it is. We're living in a complete fool's paradise on all of this. I mentioned earlier to the lady at the back about the cost of living. You know, we've been chucking 20% on people's electricity bills now for 20 years. to pay for very inefficient wind energy. So what's really happening with net zero? What's happening with the attempt to deal with climate change? Not that we can, because we produce less than 1% of the world's carbon dioxide, and the Chinese are building 80 new coal-fired power stations every year. We should not We should not forget we're going to need oil and gas for the next 30 years. We will be using oil and gas.

I don't have answers to your questions. Amber Ruddy's not answering. Yep. You see, what we have is a general election in eight days' time, Theresa May assuming a colossal landslide. That's why she thinks she can ignore it all and not bother turning up tonight. And she's telling you, we will take you, we will take your house off you. And we will allow you to know how much we'll let you keep after you've given me a colossal majority. So if tonight you resolve to vote Conservative on the 8th of June, you are resolving to give Theresa May permission to do what she likes. And that's with education cuts. That's with NHS cuts. Do not give her a blank check. is how you make things add up. The Liberal Democrats have got a fully costed manifesto. I'll tell you what, though. There's a long-term economic plan underlying the whole of the Liberal Democrat manifesto. And that is, don't leave the European single market and throw away 15 billion pounds every single year in revenue.

I mean, Paul is talking about taking money from the poorest people on planet Earth in order to fund his proposals. That stinks, because this election is about deciding what kind of country are we. And I am a patriot. I am proud to be British, and I'm proud of our values. And it shames me when we have people in this country and people in politics who are prepared to attack the poorest people on planet Earth to make a political point, but let's also address the person who isn't here and should be here. See, I have a plan to rescue the NHS by putting a penny on income tax for health and social care. Theresa May is calling this election because she assumes she has already won it. And she assumes that when you vote for her, if you do, on the 8th of June, you will be endorsing her plans to cut social care and health.

Well, I think we should try and work together where we can. I've always thought that's important in politics. I helped Tony Blair get his education bill through Parliament because I thought it was a good bill. Even though a lot of Labour MPs weren't backing it, we did that. That helped it go through. On the issue of Trident that we've covered already tonight, we put the possibility of defeating Labour aside, backed them to do the right thing. And obviously, if there is a hung Parliament, We must be responsible, we must try and deliver the best government we can for this country. But actually, if you want my frank and honest answer, I don't think a hung parliament would be good for Britain because I think we do need quite decisive government to take some of the difficult decisions for the long term. Now we've set out some of the things that need to be done to get the debt and the deficit under control and I fear if we put them off, We could have a situation where we see interest rates rise, we see confidence taken out of our economy, and I think we need change to get on top of the deficit, start solving these problems so we get our economy moving.

And then people wonder why there's a problem with trust in politics. We argued this at the referendum. I think we'll be better off. You think we'll be worse off. What we think is irrelevant. The country voted and your party, I remember the late Paddy Ashdown saying we will respect the voters and we will implement the result and you have broken your word. You've broken your word.

Yeah, it is too hard to own your own home, and I want everyone to feel what I experienced when I got the keys to my first flat, because it's a very special feeling, and we need to make it easier. And that's why we've delivered a million homes in this Parliament, and we'll keep building homes in the right place, but not concreting over the countryside, as Labour would do. But crucially, we need to make it easy for you to save for that deposit. And that's why I want your hard work to be rewarded. I don't think we should be taxing you more, because that's just going to make it even harder for you to get on the property ladder. Thank you.

If he'd been in as many support centers that I've been in for those that have survived sexual violence and domestic violence, he would know very well it is possible to do it. It is being done. I've supported it being done when I was chief prosecutor. I've always believed in that principle of women's spaces. That's by going back to 2008, way before this latest iteration of the debate, because I could recognize for myself just how important it was to have that space, women-only space, biological women-only space, particularly in that area, but also in other areas.

Well, the first thing I would say is that governments have choices about the kind of economic policy they pursue. And the Tories now alone in government and previously working with the Liberal Democrats introduced austerity measures which are hitting those in society with the lowest incomes the most. And frankly, I think the time has come for that to end. And politicians can make different choices. And every SNP MP elected to Westminster will stand up for those different choices and an end to austerity. It is about choices. The Tories have chosen to support those who are wealthiest in society. We don't agree with them. We think those who have the most should pay a little bit more. And that's why we're in favour of the highest earners seeing their taxes rising from 45 to 50p. We're also incidentally, also supportive of looking at changes to the pay restraint that we've seen through these times of austerity. That's going to happen in Scotland under an SNP government. We will press the UK government to do the same in the rest of the UK. Lastly, I want to make this point. Some of those people on the lowest incomes have been massively hit by welfare cuts. And I think the time has come to end punishing disabled people, end a bedroom tax, and leaving people with the lowest incomes with too little to pay for the essentials.

This is pretending that somehow there are billions and squillions of pounds around that we can continue to pour into our NHS. Every man, woman and child in this country spends £2,000 on the NHS through our tax system. I want to judge the NHS about how it helps me and my family when we're ill and we're sick and we're in need of NHS care, not just by numbers plucked out of thin air. David Cameron, you simply cannot seriously suggest that we should believe that you can cut the deficit immediately as you want, then get a whole blizzard of tax breaks, including a great big tax break for double millionaires in the inheritance tax system, and provide huge lashings of extra money to the public. You might be able to do one of those things. You can't do all three. So I want to say to people, let's be straight with you. We're going to have to find savings in the NHS. I want to see, for instance, strategic health authorities, which is a layer of bureaucracy, stripped away altogether and use that money on the frontline NHS services, which are so important to us.

Well, look, the point is, Jo, that we... The answer is no. The point is, Jo, that it would be for the people to decide. It would be for the people to decide, not the leader of the Liberal Democrats with 20 MPs or whatever. It would be for the people to decide. That's the point.

It's really ironic, isn't it, hearing Nick Clegg and David Cameron argue when they've been hand-in-glove imposing austerity on the people of this country for the last five years. David said in his opening remarks that everybody apart from him had been proved wrong over the past five years. That's actually not the case. David Cameron has missed his own borrowing targets by £150 billion. His policies are pushing children into poverty. There's a million additional children estimated to be going to be living in poverty in this country by 2020. That is not right. So what I want to do, I back Ed, on raising the top rate of tax. I also want to see us invest in the future of our children, not in nuclear weapons. But I take a very clear view. This country cannot afford more cuts in the next Parliament. We need to see spending rise so that we can invest in the things that matter for us.

I was about... First of all, Zane, thank you very much for your service in the police. I know that you put your lives on the line every day to keep us safe, and I have huge respect for what the police do for us. But I was about to say what a brilliant example the police have been. Over the last five years, we did have to make reductions in spending on the police, so police budgets came down by some 20%. But at the same time, the police have done such a good job that crime has come down. by almost 20%. We managed to get officers out from desk jobs onto the streets. We put more civilians into some of those roles. We made sure, and the police did this themselves, they combined, forces were combining and ordering equipment and things together. So I think there's still some more efficiency that we can get out of that, but in the end, we've got to back the British police, who are the best in the world, who do a great job, and they'll always have my support.

Well, I'd ask everyone to judge me by my actions. You saw what I did during the pandemic. When disaster struck our country, I implemented the furlough scheme because I knew the devastating impact it would have on people. And you know what? Every week when I'm out and about, someone comes up to me and tells me how furlough saved their family, saved their home, because that's who I am. And I don't want to put that progress at risk. I want to keep delivering financial security by cutting people's taxes. Thank you. Whereas the Labour Party want to keep putting people's taxes up, and that's not going to help anyone overcome the challenges they're facing.

I understand that. I understand that, but I want to draw a lesson. Because the reason why that hasn't happened is because living standards have fallen, so tax revenues have fallen. So the key to getting rid of the deficit is that third element. The tax changes we've announced, the spending reductions, but that key third element of raising living standards. If we raise living standards in this country, we'll also get the tax revenues in and the deficit down.

I've offered cuts of £32 billion a year that will not affect our public services. Ed has offered peanuts. And as for the rest, Charlotte, back to your question, you're going to be paying for this lot for a very long time to come.

Of course you don't need a new treaty, but you do need to at least work with people in Europe who believe that climate change exists. That would be a helpful start. On the point that Gordon Brown keeps saying of anti-Americanism, I have a very simple attitude towards our relationship with America. It's an immensely, immensely important special relationship. But it shouldn't be a one-way street. We shouldn't always automatically do what our American friends tell us to do. We've got to make sure that we act on the world stage in our interest, not simply at the beck and call of anybody else.

I'm delivering what I believe the British people want their government to deliver. And I think it's very important. I think it's not just an issue about Brexit itself. It's actually an issue to me about trust in politicians.

Well, it was very scarring. I lost my seat. The party was severely punished. In 2015, that's democracy. And we didn't do well in the two subsequent elections. And when I became leader, I said to my party, you've got to wake up and smell the coffee. We've got to really connect with people. And we've really tried to do that. And it's a work in progress. But I think the parliamentary by-elections we've won in this parliament, in Tory heartlands, the local elections results we've had. Just this May, we pushed the Tories into third place. It hasn't been done for 30 years. That suggests that some people are coming back and trusting us.

Good evening. Our economy is stuck in a rut, and we need change to get it moving. Let me tell you what I would do. First, we've got to reward work and tackle welfare dependency. Second, we've got to fix our banks: tax them to get our money back, regulate them properly and get them lending again. Third, we've got to start making things again in this country. It's no policy to just borrow from the Chinese and buy goods made in China. Fourth, we've actually got to get value for money in our public services. I want good public services for everyone, and we can only do that if we save and stop the waste. Let me tell you one thing I wouldn't do: with Greece so much in the news, I can guarantee you that I would never join the euro, and I'd keep the pound as our currency. That's the change we need. If you vote Conservative next Thursday, we can start to get to work on Friday.

Thank you, Julie, and to everyone watching this debate tonight. This election gives you a real choice about your future. the future of your community and of our country. Labour is offering real change and real hope. We will build a fairer Britain that cares for all, where wealth and power are shared. The Conservative government is failing. It's failed on the economy, on the climate crisis, the National Health Service and on Brexit. We will get Brexit sorted by giving you, the people, the final say and implement whatever you decide. Too many families are without a proper home, struggling to make ends meet, while tax cuts are handed to the super-rich. We can do better than this, with a Labour government on your side. A Labour government for the many, not just the few.

No, no, I'm sorry. If you're going to do your research, get it right. Go on, you tell me. We're going to scrap net zero, which will make no difference to global climate change. That will save at least £30 billion a year. I'll come to net zero.

Who created the crisis in our National Health Service after 10 years of Conservative government? Who is ensuring most difficult winter for patients in the NHS for years? What about this issue around the...

I think discipline is important, of course. I think creativity, which I think is the point you're saying, Joel. I'm not allowed to ask you questions, that's against the rules, but just nod if... Yeah, okay, good. I think creativity is important in the classroom. I think freedom for teachers and headteachers. Do you know one thing which I think would really help in all of those things? Discipline, creativity, freedom for teachers is quite simply good, old-fashioned, smaller class sizes. We have 8,000 infants in this country now, between the ages of five and seven, who are in classes which are so big, they're illegal. They're technically illegal. And it's just logical. If you're a teacher, I mean, friends of mine who are teachers just sort of say, they can't really keep an eye on the troublemakers, but they also can't support the brightest children if the classes are huge. And that's why we've got a plan, fully costed, to provide schools with additional resources so that they can bring down the average class size in a primary school for instance down to 20 and the average class size in a secondary school down to 16.

The risk to the economy is this year, and every country, America, the rest of Europe, including Britain, is saying we've got to make sure we invest in the economy this year so that we can have the growth we need. Now, pull out the money, and you've proposed it at every point during this recession. Pull out the money, and you'll have less growth, you'll have less jobs, and you will have less businesses. Now, that's the fear. We've got to take an overall responsibility for the whole economy.

As it is, it doesn't work. As it is, if it goes through... If it's oven-ready and it goes through as it is, we're headed for years of misery. Now, Boris Johnson said, after the election was called, that we would go back to Brussels and negotiate a free trade deal in line with the Canada plus plus agreement. If you stick to that and you amend the political declaration, then we will get something like Brexit. If you carry on with the deal as it was, it is not Brexit, it will take years to deliver and millions of people will be very upset and disappointed. So the question is, is it oven ready or are you going to go for a free trade deal?

I'm happy to bet with anybody that the Rwanda scheme will not happen at all, and not just because the Tories are not going to be in government in any event. It's a nonsense, it's a gimmick. It panders to the worst kind of instincts of people. It's not going to happen at all. So to go back to the lady's question, working with immigrants, it is going to have to be about resources being put into the Home Office, and also into the housing and the other facilities available for people, whilst we have this huge backlog of refugees because they've not dealt with it. But the one thing I would say, the most important thing in this election is to bear in mind, the money's not going to be there. both the parties, Labour and Tory, £18 billion short. That's what the IFS says. They're not going to have the money to do these things. That's the biggest problem we've got, and it won't deal with the long-standing problems of refugees, it won't deal with policing, it won't deal with crime, because the money is not there, either from Labour or the Conservative Party.

And the second thing we'll do for you, for all those people like you who are self-employed and others in small businesses, I want to abolish your national insurance so that you have more financial security. Now, I'm the person that has made sure that we have good relations with Europe. You can all see that. And we will continue to do so. But the best way I can help your small business and the hundreds of thousands of others like yours is to continue to cut your taxes, because that's how we'll give you financial security. That's the choice for you at this election. He says he's going to cut National Insurance.

Janet, I'm really sorry to hear about your own position and that of your relatives. My wife works in the NHS in one of the big London hospitals. My mum was a nurse, my sister was a nurse, so the DNA, it runs through my DNA. And you can imagine just how angry I am that it's been broken, and it has been broken, exactly as you describe. It's unforgivable, what's happened to the NHS. And to come into power for 14 years and leave the NHS in a worse state than when you found it is unforgivable in politics. And Rishi Sunak stood 18 months ago, Janet, and said that those waiting lists, which are now nearly 8 million, he said he'll get them down. He made a promise. He said he'd be held accountable. They were 7.2 million at the time. Now they're 7.5 million. Thank you. And that bears down on the NHS. Vishy Sunak.

Well, I couldn't agree more with that. Yes, we would absolutely lift the pay freeze. It is quite unacceptable that our public sector workers have been taken for granted for so long, essentially, and not being properly rewarded. And that's one reason why many are leaving the profession. But another reason why is because every time we have a new education secretary, they come up with their latest harebrained scheme and impose it on teachers, thinking that teachers don't have the professionalism themselves to make those decisions. So I come back to the point I made before. It's an important one. We need to trust teachers as the professionals that they are. And that has signally failed to happen both under this government and the previous one as well.

I think, Alan, this is one of those rare issues where The issue is so big, and the costs are potentially so great, and it affects every family, it affects every individual, that I think this is one of those issues where I would say it's worth the politicians just setting aside their political differences for once and trying to come up with a solution that everybody can agree with. We've all got different ideas. We have ideas, proposals, that there's a contribution from the individual and the state. David Cameron has ideas which help some, but doesn't help people in their home. Gordon Brown has some ideas which help some of the most needy, but not others. Why don't we for once, given this is something which I think is bigger than any other party, actually work together? There are some things, however, that I think we can do immediately. I, for instance, would like to use the money that the government has allocated for its latest, I think slightly flawed proposals, to give respite care to the carers who look after loved ones, those who look after loved ones for the greatest amount of time, give them respite for at least a week every year. That's something we can do now. Let's come up with a longer proposal together, a longer-term proposal together.

Daisy, you'll have to make it very brief, because I need to get to Carla Denya. The Hill Foundation and the King's Fund have done an analysis of different funding models. They don't materially make a difference to people's health outcomes. What makes a big difference, most of all, is the amount of money you put into your health service.

Immigration has not been cut to the tens of thousands. No, it hasn't. You failed. What we did is we cut immigration from outside the European Union. That is down by 13%. We closed down about 800 bogus education colleges, which were really visa factories. But inside the European Union, immigration has increased. I'm afraid not least because we've actually created more jobs in Britain than the rest of the European Union put together. The European Union's economy has been so stagnant that people have come here to work. So what we need to do now is keep the economy working, but fix the broken welfare system. And I'm going to just make this point, if you'll let me. There are some key changes I'm going to make, which is that if you come from Europe to Britain, you cannot claim unemployment benefit. If you don't have a job in six months, you'll have to return to the country you came from. You'll have to work here for four years paying into the system before you get tax credits or benefits out of the system. And while you're here, you can't send child benefit home to the family if they're living in another country. Now, those changes taken together, key welfare changes, will reduce It's not what you said last time.

These debates are the answer to people who say that politics doesn't matter. I want to thank everybody who's been involved in these debates over the last few weeks. They show that there are big causes we can fight for. They also show that big differences exist between the parties. I know that if things stay where they are, perhaps in eight days' time, David Cameron, perhaps supported by Nick Clegg, would be in office. But I've had the duty of telling you this evening that while we have policies for the future, the Conservatives would put the recovery immediately at risk with an emergency budget. I've asked David and Nick questions all evening. David has not been able to confirm, but it is the case that inheritance tax cuts will go to the richest people in the country. I believe he's planning to cut the Schools Budget, and he hasn't denied it. I believe also that child tax credits would be cut by both parties if they came into a coalition. I believe too that policing would be at risk from a Conservative government, because they have not said they would match us on policing either. And the health service guarantees that we have that gives every cancer patient the right to see a specialist within two weeks would be scrapped by the Conservative Government if they came into power. I don't like having to do this, but I have to tell you that things are too important to be left to risky policies under these two people. They are not ready for government, because they have not thought through their policies. We are desperate to get this country through the recession and into the recovery, and that is what I intend to continue to do. But it's up to the people to decide, and it's your decision.

No, we're not going to go back to austerity. I ran a public service for five years, the Crown Prosecution Service. I know how hard it is. Thank you very much indeed. But you don't have enough money. I believe in our public services.

I think the only way we're going to instil optimism is if we wipe the slate clean for the next generation. We have to release Rebecca and her generation of the debt and the deficit of this generation. I don't want Rebecca, I don't want my own kids, I don't want any of our children to pay the price for this generation's mistakes. And if I can leave Rebecca and everybody with just one figure in mind, one statistic, £46 billion. That is what we as a country will spend next year just paying off the interest on our debts. Just imagine the hundreds of thousands of homes we could build for £46 billion. £46 billion is the same as £700 for every man, woman or child in this country. And that is why when I hear some leaders implying that we shouldn't somehow get rid of our deficits, I say, look, if you don't do that, it's a bit like Miriam and me saying we're not going to pay off our credit card bill and we'll get our little kids to pay it for us. It's not fair.

I still don't know. You know what the business organisations tell me is? They can't stand the uncertainty of this government. They can't stand the idea of a no-deal exit. They want to make sure they're able to continue that trade and continue that just-in-time delivery system that is so important in manufacturing industry. And they are constantly concerned at the lack of understanding of how industry works from a Conservative government that claims to be in support of those industries.

Yes, Omar, it's an awful situation. And I'm pleased, actually, that Keir Starmer supported, alongside me, Israel's right to defend itself in the face of the atrocious attack that they suffered. But right now, you're seeing that US have put a deal on the table, which we strongly support, together with our allies, to bring an immediate end to the hostilities, release hostages, get more aid in, and then build towards a lasting settlement with a two-state solution. We've led in providing more aid to Gaza, something I'm proud that we've done. Our troops have helped build the port that's being established. But more generally, the world is a more uncertain place. What Hamas is doing is indicative of the dangers we face, which is why I've made the bold decision also to lead, to increase investment in our country's defence, because I think that's necessary to keep you safe, everyone safe. It's something that the Labour Party have not matched.
What's the difference? No, the position was not the same, because the international community was so shattered, not just at Security Council level, but within the UN generally. OK, you, sir.

We didn't have to for the commitments that we had, but it's true. We got the report that said, if you want to get your health service spending up to the European Union average, you're going to have to put more money in. Now, even with that additional money, I think it's worth just pointing out that the National Health Service, as a result of that, is getting the largest increase investment it's had since it was created. And I think the National Health Service is improving as a result. And that is surely better than going down the path of forcing people to take out private medical insurance.

You and other audience members, and it goes back to Elizabeth's question, will have to make a choice. There are some parties in this election saying no cuts at all. I'm not saying that. Mr Cameron, or he didn't say this earlier, wants to double the cuts next year. It's not just the same cuts as in the last parliament, double the cuts. Now I think that would be incredibly dangerous for our National Health Service. Because you want to borrow more.

Harry, let migration leave. Can you name me a single other year ever where we have reduced the number of visas by 30%. These numbers are on your watch, but it's clear that you were coming into slightly late to this. No, you just said... You just said... So, whose fault is it that it's so high? I've been Prime Minister for 18 months. So, Lestrade's fault? Was it Boris's fault? The numbers were too high. Whose fault was it? Well, the numbers were too high. Oh, go on. If it's not your fault, whose fault was it? Hang on. Well, obviously, the numbers are too high. I've got the job and I've brought them down. And you just said, oh, no, you haven't done very much, and said, tell me another year where someone has reduced the number of visas issued by 30%. Never happened in our country before, because I took strict action. I'll give you one example. We raised the salary that you need to earn to be able to come here to work to £38,500. We've made the very reasonable point that actually if you're going to bring your family dependents to this country, you should be able to support them. Economically, again, I think that's a relatively common-sense, reasonable approach. We've tightened up the social care visa, the student visa, where people were bringing lots of dependents to our country. They weren't working. They weren't accounted for. I made all those changes. The numbers are forecast to halve in 12 months as a result of them, right? And I want to go further with a legal cap and give you the guarantee that they will keep coming down. Again, the choice for you at this election is everything that I've just said, or a Labour party that opposed all of those things, no cap, and Keir Starmer, who, back in the day when he was running to be leader, said he wanted to bring back free movement of people.

The Chancellor says the money's been spent. It isn't there, Prime Minister. The money isn't there. The Chancellor, not me, your Chancellor said the money's been spent. No, that's not what he said at all. You can't fund things from nothing. The money has been spent. And this is why, if we go on for another five years like this, we're going to end up exactly where we are now, or in a worse place. We have to turn the page and start rebuilding. And that means building those 1.5 million houses. And that's what we're determined to do with a strong plan behind it, already talking to the partners who will build those houses with us, because I'm absolutely determined that every single young person in this country under a Labour government will feel that the future is better for them. For my parents, it comforted them, even though they had tough times as parents, they didn't have a lot of money, but the thoughts, the idea that it would be better for their children comforted them and made their lives worthwhile. We've lost that, and we have to get it back, because in the end, the future is about our children and young people. We have to build a country, rebuild a country, that is fit for them to live in and to absolutely realise all of their aspirations and their dreams, and I'm determined that we will do that.

Well, they were given every opportunity to pass it. They were given every opportunity to pass it and they passed a law to insist that we extended beyond October the 31st. And what we have now is a situation in which the other three party leaders either want to block Brexit or absurdly want to have a second referendum on the EU without... And that's, I think, Mr Corbyn's position. It seems to have mutated tonight. He's now going to be neutral on the deal that he proposes to do. I don't see how he can do a deal when he's going to be neutral or indifferent about the deal that he wants to do. Never mind that. I do not think whatever deal Mr Corbyn proposes... I don't think it's sensible. for this great country of ours to spend next year in yet more delectable disputations about the EU, and then another referendum, if I understood correctly what happened earlier on, another referendum on Scotland. How can that be right for our country? I want next year to be a fantastic year for our country. We're a very ambitious, one-nation Conservative government. We have amazing plans for this country, investing in education, investing in health, and that's what we want to get on with, and we need to get Brexit done to do it.

Well, I think a cap is necessary because we're not going to control immigration unless we actually take some quite positive and concrete steps. And I think we let down everyone if we don't do this properly. I was in Plymouth recently and a 40-year-old black man actually made the point to me. He said, I came here when I was six. I've served in the Royal Navy for 30 years. I'm incredibly proud of my country. But I'm so ashamed that we've had this out-of-control system with people abusing it so badly. So if we don't address immigration properly, we're actually letting down immigrant communities as well as everybody else. So we do need a cab. As well as border controls that Gordon talks about, and I support strong border controls, I think we need to go one step further and actually have a proper border police force. So we combine at our borders customs, immigration, security and police. So we make the most of the fact that we do have policeable borders and we could stop more illegal immigration that way as well. Nick Clegg.

Let me take on, Robert, this argument directly. The idea that if you cut waste this year, you endanger the recovery. Now, just this week, we've seen two, I think, pretty hideous waste stories. The first is that civil servants have been given credit cards, funded by the taxpayer, to go out and spend that on food and wine and other things, and that's cost a billion pounds. The second story was that managers in the National Health Service, many of whom are paid over £250,000, have had a 7% pay rise. Now, are we honestly saying that if you didn't have that sort of waste, that sort of excess, that our economy would collapse? I think it is nonsense. It's like saying that giving up smoking is somehow going to be bad for your health. Giving up waste would be good for our economy and it would mean that we could stop this tax rise that's coming down the track that Britain's biggest business leaders all say will cost jobs. Cut the waste, stop the tax. That's the right answer.

Nicola, we had this debate. We got a deal. Well, you didn't answer it then. No, no. We got a deal. You said, and I quote, you said it would be unachievable. Nobody thinks. You said it would be unachievable, your words, for Boris Johnson to get a new deal. But he did achieve. I'm talking about the future trade deal. Hang on. He did get a new deal, and you were wrong then. Do you concede that you were wrong? He did get a new deal.

Everyone who's had the opportunity to own their own home remembers what it feels like when you get the keys to your first flat. And it's an incredibly special feeling. I believe in a country where all young people can aspire to that and realise that ambition. It's a very special thing. I know it's too hard. Of course, it's become too hard to buy your own home. I've got two very practical, specific things that we will do, if I'm your Prime Minister, which will help make it easier. The first is to reintroduce a new form of help to buy. You may remember this. It means that you won't have to save for the entire deposit. just 5% and then you'll be able to get a help to buy loan from the government which covers another 20% so that potentially can save you tens of thousands of pounds. And the second thing that we will do is effectively abolish stamp duty for first-time buyers. For the vast vast majority of first-time buyers we will abolish stamp duty. Those are two very practical things that will save you thousands of pounds. Now, the reason that's important is, when I talk to people like you, the most important thing they tell me is, how can you help me with a deposit? Because, actually, I'm probably OK and into a decent job, and I can probably afford the mortgage. Right, that's actually similar to my rent, but what I can't do is save up for the deposit in time.

I'm sorry, I'm hearing half the panel saying that we have no economic problems with debt at all, and the other half of the panel saying that they've been prudent in government. Look, there's no question that spending got completely out of control under Labour, and there are many that thought this coalition would bring it back under control. We have doubled the national debt in the course of the last five years. Our debt repayment is bigger than our annual defence budget, and that's with interest rates close to zero. We have a massive problem here, and it seems to me that nobody's prepared to admit that what we've done is we've maxed out the credit cards. Yes, there's growth in the economy, but actually, at some point, we've got a dreadful debt repayment problem. We've got to get real, and we can cut budgets like foreign aid with, I think, popular public support.

Look, I'm incredibly sorry to hear about the challenges that you've faced and it's the same for millions of people right across these aisles and it's simply not good enough. We know the damage that the Tories have caused to the economy as a result of Liz Truss's But I'm afraid the challenges facing the economy are much deeper than that. Firstly, there's austerity. 14 years of austerity. And I must re-emphasise the fact that there's £18 billion worth of cuts baked in, which neither the Labour Party nor the Conservative Party are being honest about. Instead, they're having the worst of Westminster argument between them tonight, instead of being honest with you about the fiscal outlook going forward. But there's also a conspiracy of silence on one other issue. And that's Brexit. Because Brexit has impacted the economy more than the COVID pandemic. It has put your food bills up completely unnecessarily. It has been an unmitigated disaster for the economy. And... And that... That is why, at this election, you need to ignore the snake oil salesmen who delivered Brexit, and you need to challenge the Labour Party and the Conservative Party about why they aren't seeking to rejoin the single market, why they aren't seeking to double down on making sure that we return to freedom of movement, and why we aren't investing in our renewables future.

Under our plans, the deficit will be cut from £90 billion to £30 billion by 2020. We see no reason to put arbitrary deadlines on cutting the deficit. The austerity experiment has failed. We were told that the deficit would be eliminated within this Parliament, yet debt has gone up. We've faced all of these cuts, so much pain for so little gain. The banks have had a bailout, it's time now for the people to have a bailout and it's time for us to invest in public services and job creation and to see an end to austerity and cuts.

I'm going to disappoint you because look we've got seven days to go and I'm going to fight everything I've got those next seven days to get an overall majority and the reason why I think that's right is look I think a strong decisive a more accountable government where you're not trading away, that's the trouble with coalition. You've got a great manifesto, you don't quite win the election, you go into some dark room with Nick Clegg or someone else and you start giving away some of the things that were in your programme. I don't want to do that. I think I've got a great programme about building these houses, creating these jobs, funding these apprenticeships, helping people with pensions. And crucially, this European referendum, which I've said absolutely that is a red line, I don't want to give any of these things away. So I'm going to spend the next seven days flat out for victory, and if enough people watching this programme are back at home, back me, we can have that victory and have the whole of the manifesto rather than have it bartered away in a darkened room.

Johnny, cutting the deficit is important, of course it is, but economic policy shouldn't be an end in itself. It should be a means to people living better lives. And the fact is, austerity is pushing people into poverty. It's undermining our public services and it's holding back economic growth. And when economic policy is doing all of that, that policy needs to change. So I don't agree with the cuts proposed by the Tories, Labour and the Liberal Democrats. I take a different view. We should have modest spending increases over the next Parliament. It will take slightly longer to eliminate the deficit completely, but the deficit would continue to fall in each year of the next Parliament. But crucially, that alternative plan would mean that we had resources to invest in infrastructure and skills and innovation, the things we need to get our economy growing, resources to invest in our public services and in lifting people out of poverty. That kind of alternative plan must be better than a blind commitment to austerity that doesn't take any account of the damage being done to our society.

Yeah, absolutely. I mean we need parity of esteem with mental health and physical health and also social care as well. Three strands of the health service. And I think that's something that we all agree on across the political spectrum. But I've heard a bit of unreality here from the two main parties. I mean Labour seems to have a magic money tree which doesn't exist and they're not going to. Jeremy Corbyn comes out like Santa Claus saying I'm going to give this and I'm going to do this and I'm going to do this but there's no money there to do all of these things. The Conservatives, you know, talk about having a strong economy but they're cutting the NHS to the bone.

Thank you very much. Carla Dania. Christine, you're right. Our economy needs migration. Migration does change communities. In my view, that is a positive. However, I understand it can be unsettling, especially when change happens fast. And it can be particularly unsettling when the government doesn't invest properly in our public services. We think it's shameful when some politicians scapegoat the people who become our neighbours, our friends, our colleagues, to distract from their chronic underinvestment in public services. The solution isn't scapegoating or pulling up the drawbridge. The solution is investing in our communities to make sure everyone has access to the services they need. My party will never engage in that scapegoating rhetoric.

The Conservatives now say they want to renegotiate but they won't tell you how they'll renegotiate. They float different dates about when they want to have a referendum. 2017? No, sometimes they say 2016, sometimes they say they're going to leave the European Union if they don't get what they want, sometimes they say they'll stay. I don't know what they're going to think on Europe next Tuesday, let alone on May the 8th. I think there should be a referendum, I say this as a pro-European, whether we stay in or when we go out, where new powers are given up by us as a country to the European Union in the future.

It's really important to get it right. then believe the several generals who wrote just this week in the newspaper saying precisely what I've been saying all along, why take a decision now to commit that amount of money on replacing an old Cold War nuclear missile system when that system has still got several years to run, when those military people themselves say that there are cheaper and better alternatives, and of course most importantly when the world is changing. President Obama said last week, I think quite rightly, that now the greatest threat to us is not the Cold War threats of old, it's terrorists getting hold of dirty bombs. Trident isn't going to help you with that. So let's move with the times, take decisions when we need to take them, and at least have this review, which I talked about after the election, where we then consider everything that is possible.

You know I didn't. So what we've got here is you see the professionalism and the decency of those people, but have we got the best funded health service in the world? No, we haven't. We spend less on our NHS than in Portugal, than on Spain, than on Greece. It's important that we put a penny on income tax to fund the National Health Service and the recovery of it.

I want you to know that I have been in office for about 120 days or so as Prime Minister. I do understand, I have been to many hospitals and many schools in the time I have been Prime Minister. I have talked to nurses and doctors and teachers about what is going on in their schools. That is why I We're now levelling up funding for education across this country, putting more money into both primary and secondary schools, and I want to see that going directly to your school. But I just make the crucial point...

I don't know why I'm moving. I think the first rule of leadership is to show up. You don't call a general election. You don't call a general election and say it's the most important election in her lifetime, and then not even be bothered to come and debate the issues at stake. Now, I feel quite unique here tonight as a co-leader, and I'm proud that the Green Party pioneers new ways of doing things. We want to see job sharing in all kinds of aspects of our lives, because we believe that two heads are often better than one, And so I'm very happy to be there with my co-leader, Jonathan Bartley. But what leadership is about, it is about listening. It is about recognizing that all of us have leadership qualities. And in particular, I think it's about trusting the public. And on that issue, I do not understand why this government will not allow the people to have the final say on the EU referendum. Why can we not have a ratification referendum when we see the final deal of what Theresa May or whoever it is brings back from Brussels? Why can we not trust the British people to have that final say? If they like what they see, that's great. But if they don't, the way to have another look at that is by voting Green, because we will say on that ballot paper that you should have the right to remain in the EU if that's what you choose when you see the small print. Because since this referendum was called, We have so many lies told.

Try and take photos of these questions if you like. Hamza shows an admirable concern for the wider global issues which are going on. If you look at Mexico today, for example, or previously in Venezuela, or coming out of Africa, and it's the same thing that underlies the concern about climate change, which is inequality of resources and poverty throughout the world. That's what's driving it. And if you want to have an influence on that, you have to have some moral authority behind you. And the UK doesn't. During the Brexit time, they struck deals which he did not believe they would even want to fulfil. He said that afterwards. Boris Johnson said he struck a deal and had no intention of fulfilling it. So the level of trust, the level of regard for the UK is diminished hugely and that's detrimental. And of course, the other side of it is real money going into foreign aid and support. rather than spend £3 billion a year on nuclear weapons with the new Trident, why not spend it on trying to reduce inequality around the world? And in Scotland, the only chance you've got to come out of the Brexit fix in which you've found yourself is to vote SNP. Chris Philp.

An awful lot of truth in it. I think the thing we need to remember, particularly when everything is so raw just nine days after Manchester, that there's a lot of finger pointing going on. We all have ideas, and there's some of them different, some of them similar. The critical thing we should remember at this point is that knee jerk, new policies, and new laws tend to do more harm than good. More resources for the security service and the police we have already will do a lot more good than harm.

Well, there's a pattern here, isn't there? We've just been talking about the NHS, and Janet says it's broken. We've now gone to education, and the teachers say that's broken as well. And I know the Prime Minister has already said in the first however many minutes of this debate that he doesn't want to have anything to do with the last 14 years. I'm sorry, Prime Minister, you may want to just cast it off, but everybody else is living with it. When it comes to schools, we desperately need more teachers, because in secondary schools, we do not have the teachers we need. So, for example, on key issues like maths, We have maths being taught, and parents watching this will know, by supply teachers, by PE teachers, but not maths teachers. So we would have in place, one of our first steps would be to recruit 6,500 teachers to fill those gaps, which will help the existing teachers who are struggling to cope with the mess that this government has left them in.

Well this is an absolutely vital question and I'm glad it's been asked because we've got to get this economy moving, we've got to get this economy growing. And what we say is save £6 billion in the coming current year in order to stop the jobs tax which we think will derail the recovery. Because if you put a tax on jobs, that I think is a jobs killer, it's a recovery killer, it's an economy killer. And a hundred of the leading business people in this country, people who run companies like Sainsbury's and Marks and Spencer's and Mothercare, have all said that the threat to recovery is not cutting out waste, as we say, the threat to recovery is Labour's proposal for a jobs tax. So I think we've got to remove this dark cloud of the deficit over our economy and it makes sense to make a start on that now. Make a start this year to avoid the tax next year, and then we can go forward with further plans to remove our deficit and our debt that's going to hold our country back if we're not very careful.

You've seen tonight the real choice you'll have in this election, and your voice must be heard. If you haven't registered to vote, please go online and do it now. This is a once-in-a-generation election. To end privatisation and give the National Health Service the funding it needs. To give people the final say and get Brexit sorted. To tackle the climate emergency that threatens our futures. To invest in good jobs in every region and nation of our country. I ask that you vote for hope and vote for Labour on the 12th of December.

Just explain how that would work. Well, our taxes are all absolutely revenue neutral. Every single penny from the tax that we put on carbon and the tax we put on methane at the level of meat and dairy would go straight back into changing what's going on. We're not about making money. We're about changing how things happen. And so, yeah, we want the industry to change. We don't want any of those taxes to reach people's plates. We want the industry to reduce the amount of meat that they're producing, to produce the greener, healthier choices, and make those the cheapest choices for everybody. That's how the green values always work on these things. We're about changing things.

I think everybody's agreed that if we were to do this again, which is Stuart's question, we need to make sure that we've got the right equipment, the right resources. And that's why I think it is essential that after the next election, whoever wins, there is a full defence review. So we have a real good look at where we're spending money, whether we're spending that wisely and whether we are providing the troops on the front line with the resources they need. I personally think that we're not spending money on some of the right things. I wouldn't carry on spending money on the Eurofighter Typhoon, the third tranche of that you're a fighter project, consuming billions of pounds. I don't think it's right to do what both David Cameron and Gordon Brown want, which is now to commit before we even need to make a decision, to spend up to 100 billion pounds renewing, exactly in the same old way, the Cold War trident nuclear missile system. If you take decisions like that, then maybe we can equip our troops so they don't get so terribly overstretched as they were in fighting two wars on two fronts in Iraq and in Afghanistan.

We have to encourage people to make good choices for our climate and our environment, first of all, at an individual level. And actually, my young kids are much more aware of that than what my generation were. And we have to get real, because climate emergency is a real problem. Climate change is a problem, despite what Nigel thinks.

Paula, I'm really, really sorry to hear the position that you're in. It must be really, really difficult on a daily basis. And there'll be millions of people watching this who feel in a similar position to you that the cost of living crisis is really bearing down on them with constant worry. I'm not going to pretend that Ukraine and COVID hasn't made things worse, but this government has lost control. Liz Truss crashed the economy. They made it worse here than in other countries. And it's people like you that are paying the price, because if a government loses control of the economy, as they have, It's working people that pay the price. And Liz Truss's advisers are in the House of Lords. She's going to be a candidate at the next election. Rishi Sunak is putting her forward. We've got to stop this chaos, got to stop the division, turn a page and start to rebuild under Labour.

I've changed Labour's position and let me tell you what we're going to do. Under a Labour government, when people come here, they won't get benefits for at least the first two years. We're going to ensure that in our public services, people learn English. In fact, I think everybody who comes here should learn English. And thirdly, we're going to stop something which I think, frankly, should have been stopped a long time ago, which is employers bringing people into this country, exploiting migrant labour, and undercutting wages. Let me just give you a fact, Brian. There have been... There have been two prosecutions for failure to pay the minimum wage in the last five years. Now, the notion that that reflects what's actually happening in the world of work is, of course, nonsense. And even the government's own Migration Advisory Committee have said that is one of the things that is bringing low-skilled labour into here. The notion that some rogue employers, and I emphasize some because most employers don't do this, can get away with bringing migrant labour in and undercutting wages. So, the last thing I want to say to you is this. You know, I don't believe it's prejudice to worry about immigration. Some people say it is, I don't believe it is. I think people's concerns are real. I see it in my own constituency in Doncaster and we're going to deal with people's concerns with real controls.

So this is what I mean. This is what I mean when I say this election is about the cost of living. And what you have been going through and many other people have been going through, it's exhausting and we need to bring it to an end. We have supported people through some very difficult times. That has been expensive. But we are now in the recovery. The economy is doing much better. We're outperforming America on growth. We're outperforming Europe on inflation. Those are your achievements. But inequality continues to get worse. The only way you're going to keep the recovery going is to give you more money in your pockets. That is why this election must be about us cutting your taxes. We have already started doing that and you have already heard some announcements. You'll see more in our manifesto next week. We have got to cut people's taxes and we have got to alleviate burdens on business. Angela Rayner and the Labour Party, Keir Starmer confirmed this earlier this week, they are going to put up your taxes. No it won't. By 2,000 pounds. That's a lie. By 2,000 pounds per working household.

to pay for it? No, we've set out... Our manifesto has got a schedule at the back of it, costing everything we've said we're going to do, and saying where the money is coming from, because we know from Liz Truss, if you don't do that, then you damage the economy and working people are paying the price. And I think it was a question earlier from somebody about mortgages. And I've met so many people who have come off their fixed-rate mortgage in the last couple of years and now having to pay hundreds of pounds more because of what the Tories did to the economy. I'm never going to let a Labour government do that, which is why everything is fully costed and fully funded.

Can you confirm... He obviously has put a bet on interrupting me, hasn't he? ..will the state pension be subject to income tax under a future Labour government? The only way for it not is for you to match our triple lot plus. So maybe you can change policy here tonight. Will you match our triple lot plus and ensure that state pension is never subject to income tax?

So that is a choice at this election, right? I know people are frustrated about lots of things, but you've got to focus on that choice ahead of you and think about the next five years, think about what I'm saying, allow me to deliver all those things I just talked about for you.
No, no, David Cameron, what would the cut be?

Lucy, we have put forward... We've put forward from the beginning of this campaign the things we want to do for our country. Our five commitments are cleaner hospitals, school discipline, more police, controlled immigration, lower taxes. Let me tell you a little bit about the importance of school discipline. School discipline is crucial. I went to a terrific school this afternoon. It was a joy to behold. And there are some many tremendous schools in our country. But there are many where children just are having their education wrecked because of the activities of one or two disruptive children in the class who spoil the opportunity for others to learn. So we have to do something about that. And what we do is give headteachers the right to exclude. So we've been putting forward our plans. It's a choice at this election. It's a choice between parties with different programmes. And one of the things that people have to take into account when they decide is not only the programmes that the parties are putting forward, but whether those parties are likely to deliver on their promises. and if you have a situation in which, as is the case with Mr Blair, that before 1997, he said there'd be no need to increase taxes, and he did immediately afterwards, and he did the same thing before the last election, and he promised, he said he was opposed to... I'm not sure you're answering Lucy Allitt's point. I think I am, Liz.
But just my point, Jim, just to say that it is actually the NHS staff themselves who have been working with me on the NHS reinstatement bill who do say that this, over time, is the right way forward.

There speaks the ambassador for Theresa May, so he would know I'm sure. Look, democracy did not end on the 23rd of June last year. If you believe passionately that our children's future is better alongside our neighbours than separated from it, then you keep fighting. And it's a matter of democracy that this deal, that none of us know what the outcome's going to be, what will it mean for all of our families here tonight, you should have the final say. Because somebody will sign off that deal. It will either be the politicians or it will be the people. I trust the people.

You're absolutely right, the taxpayer has had to pay more and more and more as the Government has spent more and has been so careless at trying to stop wasting money. We see waste all around us, and the Government's done so little about it. Obviously, with the terrible situation we have in our public finances, with the mess left by Gordon and Labour, where out of every £4 the Government spends, £1 is borrowed, it's not possible to make great big tax giveaway promises. Even if it'd be a lovely thing to do, you can't do it. But what we've said is, let's try and stop the one tax that will hit the lowest-paid people, and that's the national insurance tax I was referring to earlier. We can't stop all of the other taxes, the top rate of tax, the extra tax on the pension. Labour have put up tax something like 178 times. But we are going to stop that one tax that will hit the lowest paid the hardest. Let me say this about tax credits. They will stay under a Conservative Government, and Gordon Brown has to stop misleading families in this country, like he's been misleading older people, and cancer patients as well.

Ritchie Soonack. Nigel has been a tireless campaigner for leaving the EU. I have, absolutely. But the difference between Nigel and me is that only my party, the Conservative Party, can actually deliver Brexit.

We'll be still planning for all the changes that'll come once we leave the European Union, like putting in a new immigration system. That's something that, of course, we have to plan for. But the point is this. People said we couldn't get a deal. We have got a deal. OK, you've made that point. And it's ready to go. No, we're going to bring in Rebecca Long-Bailey.

I don't think that any politician deserves your trust, and you talked about credibility, deserves any credibility, until everybody has come clean about what has gone wrong. Now, there have been some changes to the rules and all that, the changes to the expenses rules, but you know there are still people who haven't taken full responsibility for some of the biggest abuses in the system. There are MPs who flipped one property to the next, buying properties paid by you, the taxpayer, and then they would do the properties up, paid for by you, and pocket the difference in personal profit. They got away scot-free. There were MPs who avoided paying capital gains tax. And of course you remember the duck houses and all the rest of it, but actually it's the people, it's the MPs who made these big abuses, some of them profiting hundreds of thousands of pounds. I have to stress, not a single Liberal Democrat MP did either of those things, but they still haven't been dealt with. But we can only turn around the corner on this until we're honest about what went wrong in the first place.

We can look at it from two sides in Wales, actually. We have suffered, as everybody is, as a result of the public spending cuts by 14 years of the Conservatives. But also in Wales, we have had Labour health ministers who've mismanaged health for 25 years. At the heart of the future of the NHS has got to be sustainability. Sustainability of funding, which is why I want to see fair funding for Wales. but it's also going to be the sustainability of the workforce. The NHS is nothing without its workforce. That's why we say we want to recruit 500 extra GPs into the NHS. But two core principles. One, we absolutely have to pledge to keep the NHS free at the point of need, and also to keep privatisation out of the NHS. And it pains so many people to see Labour's West Streeting talking so warmly about bringing the private sector into the NHS.

In 2019 I campaigned for the Labour Party, as I've always campaigned for the Labour Party. I wanted good colleagues to be returned to Parliament. I knew we had a job and a half to do as a Labour Party because I didn't think we were going to win that election. Afterwards, because we got the worst results since 1935, the electorate clearly gave their verdict to us. And we did a lot of work on how we needed to change the party. And that started with what went wrong. And in all the analysis we did, people said they quite liked some of what was in our manifesto. But they thought it was too much. And they wanted to see something which was fully costed and fully funded. And that's why my manifesto going forward to this election is fully costed, fully funded. Everything that we say we're going to do, we say how much it's going to be and where the money is coming from. Because that's the sort of stability that we need. The other thing that's obviously happened since then is Liz Truss put forward a number of unfunded commitments, in her case, tax cuts, and look what it did to the economy. And there will be people in this audience, people who are watching, who are still paying the price for that. And I said it about Rishi Sunak's manifesto, because he's now included unfunded commitments in that. So he's repeating the mistake. That is a political point going into an election. I grant you that. But it is also really important, because if you lose control of the economy, It's ordinary people, working people who pay the price. So many people, for example, still pay more on their mortgages.

I think the first thing people want to know is that they are going to be voting for a cleaned up Parliament. Everywhere I go in this country during the election, it is simmering and bubbling below the surface. There is just anger about the expenses fiasco. People say, I pay my taxes to have decent politics and yet the money has been spent on all these appalling things. They want to be absolutely sure this is never ever going to happen again. So strict limits on what can be spent, total transparency so you the voters can see every penny. That I think is the absolutely first thing that needs to be done. I would go on and say we also need to cut the cost of politics. Everyone else in this country has had to make economies and had to make their businesses work better. Why shouldn't politics be the same? So I would do that. Then some simple changes that I think would put people back in control. I want to see many more people involved in choosing the candidates for the parties, holding open primaries. I agree that we need to have a situation where you can sack your MP, but please don't let's give up the way you can sack your government and throw them out. Let's not have permanent hung parliaments.

This is genuinely the biggest problem that we have at the moment and all of us all of the parties here are coming out with different figures of how much we're going to invest in the NHS and in social care and I just to say that Labour has the biggest figure but it's still a drop in the ocean. But it's still a drop in the ocean. All the independent people are saying that what we need is more investment than we're able to financially commit. So what Labour is looking at is, as well as putting the money in, we're looking at overhauling the service. So we're looking at making sure that staff are both recognised, respected and rewarded, not demonised as they've been in the last couple of years. We're looking at how we can integrate social care and the NHS. so that both of them have parity, because to be quite honest, if you go into hospital for an operation and you can't get home again because there's no one in the community nursing to take care of you, or there's no health worker to come and take care of you, you then get stuck in hospital, which is the worst place for you, and then bed blocking begins. We're looking at very actively. Labour created the NHS, we're still incredibly proud of it. We're looking at making sure that we get back down to the 18 weeks to have your appointment, to have your operation. We're looking at getting back to four hours wait in A&E. And we do that by radically trying to rethink. We do that by talking to the staff. We do that by having an independent regulatory body that looks at the budgeting and that makes the right decisions for the NHS. So whether that's, well you all know, community pharmacy is being cut to the bone now. Whether that's negotiating the best deals on the price of the drugs we get. And to be quite honest, unless we take the politics out of this and get really grown up, we're not gonna have an NHS left. And the one thing that we're particularly passionate about, and I'd love to hear Nadine's views on this, is the Tories are going for something called the Naylor Report, which is looking at selling off land and assets for the NHS. We paid into this. We created this. We own this. It should be us, the population of the UK, that makes the decisions about what we do with our NHS, not just the politicians. You're promising a large amount of money. 30 billion over five years. How is Labour going to pay for that? Well, we all pay our taxes. So we have a pot of money and we've based the projections that we've made on the figures that the government are using at the moment. And then we've made specific sort of recommendations. So one of them is the top 5% earners in this country will pay 5% more. The 95% rest of us won't. And we're also looking at getting corporation tax back to levels that it was before. 2010, it was at 28%. The Tories last year brought it down to 17%? 19%. 19%. We're looking at putting it back up to 26% because, to be quite honest, we all need to be investing in our country. Conservative Party.
79,000 people... The books aren't balanced. We've got a £90 billion deficit. 79,000... I mean, what's going on here? Can we get real, please?

I am indeed, for a complete nonsense story, but let's put that aside. Complete rubbish. Mary, I think one of the most heartening things, back to your question about people sort of feeling disaffected, one of the most heartening things over the last week, and it's one of the great sort of effects of these leaders debates, these televised leaders debates, is that more and more young people appear to be rushing to register to vote. before it's too late. Now that is immensely exciting, because there are lots and lots of especially young people, I hope I'm not saying anything out of turn by saying you look fairly young, who might be in your position, who feel, who have felt for so long completely turned off by the old party politics. And they're now quite excited, they're beginning to hope, they're beginning to think that we can do something different this time. That's what I think. I think we can do something different this time, but get stuck in. If you didn't vote before, if you didn't register, register. It's your country. It's your future. Assert your right to vote. Assert your right to shape your own future. And I think then we could make this election one of the most exciting elections we've had in a very long time. Mr Brown.

Well, there is a crucial difference on this occasion. We are the government. We've been able to look very carefully at the books. We know what is necessary in the next Parliament. And our plans do not involve tax increases on VAT or national insurance or income tax. It's very clear about that. We know what needs to be done. And let me explain, because I think people watching at home want to know, what is the scale? of what needs to be done. We need to save, for the next two years, one out of every £100 that the government spends. Now I think it is the right approach to try and find £1 of waste in £100 of government spending, rather than to put up everybody's taxes, which is what my political opponents want to do. So you've got a choice. Bear down on waste and get public spending under control, or put up taxes with my opponents. That's the choice.

I think you make a really good point. Actually that local hospital here does meet the target of seeing 95% of people within four hours and it's important to have that target because we want to know that we're getting the best out of our NHS. The problem we've had recently is just the numbers of frail elderly in our country and the growth of the population of our country has put a lot of pressure on the NHS. And I think my solution of having this seven-day opening for GPs and making sure GP surgeries are easier to access for people, I think that will take pressure off the NHS. I also think making sure social care works together better with the NHS, so we combine the budgets and make sure we help to get elderly people out of hospital, often blocked in beds when they could be looked after at home. Those things, I think, will keep the pressure off. But it's always going to be hard work to make sure we deliver these

Well, yes, I do. And one of the reasons I think we can negotiate a good deal is because actually a good deal in trade terms is not just of benefit to the UK, it's of benefit to businesses in the remaining countries, remaining in the European Union. So this isn't just about us. It's actually about a relationship that matters to them as well as it matters to us.

Look, what happened after 2006 is that the Taliban changed its tactics. Before, they were in a one-to-one confrontation with us, face-to-face. They couldn't win that armed battle, so they took to explosive devices, guerrilla warfare, and we had to respond to that, as all our allies had to do. And we've got 1,000 new vehicles in Afghanistan as a result of the decisions to get Mastiffs and Ridgebacks and other vehicles into the front line. and we've also got more helicopters.

You know, it's been a great opportunity to exchange ideas this evening. And I know we're not up against the X Factor or Britain's Got Talent, and I hope people have been able to stay with us in the exchange that we've had about the future policies of this country. I was really struck with a number of questions, but particularly the one from Robert about the future of his healthcare trust and about the jobs that may be at risk. And I've got to come to this central problem that we've got at the moment. We have got to make a decision now about how we secure the recovery this year. We've got to make a decision about whether we put funds into the economy or take funds out of the economy. Now, I'm very clear, we mustn't make the mistakes of the 1930s or the 1980s when unemployment rose for five years after the official end of the recession. So we've got to make sure the money is in the economy this year so that the recovery is secure. And then we've got to make sure that as we cut the deficit, we are fair to our National Health Service, our policing, and fair to our schools. And that's why the National Insurance Rise is necessary to protect our health service, our schools, and our police. And I think it was very interesting, when David Cameron was asked, he couldn't give a guarantee that we are giving about the funding on schools. He couldn't give a guarantee about the funding on policing. And when it came to the National Health Service, he couldn't give the same personal guarantees that we're giving about cancer specialist care, about seeing a GP at the evenings and weekends. Now these are problems he's got to address in the future. I look forward to the next debate so we can get all the issues raised, aired about the future of our country. David Cameron.

I would just say to Farhan, look at what I have said I'm going to do as a politician and look what I've delivered. Whether it was as Mayor of London or now as Prime Minister, I said that we would put 20,000 more police officers on the streets of our city, of our country, and we are recruiting them now. I said we'd upgrade 20 hospitals and have 40 new hospitals. That programme is going ahead. We are uplifting funding for education across the country. We're lifting up the living wage by its biggest ever expansion. And I said we would deliver Brexit, and we will deliver Brexit. Thank you. But we can only do it if we clear this parliamentary blockage.

You bet I would. And you bet I would. Not just because if we were in a position to do that on our own, we'd have won under the existing system. but that doesn't justify the existing system. So you would do a deal on that? No. We would put forward... I hope that all politicians would recognise something. There's one thing that's a bit more important than the self-interest of our parties, and it is the equal value, whatever constituency, whatever political persuasion they may be, of voters where their vote is concerned. At the moment, too many people's votes are corrupted by having to vote tactically. I want people at this election, and I believe they are, going to go out there and vote for values and principles and a bit of hope in our country.

Gordon Brown mentioned spending. Absolutely, too right. I don't think we're really going to get those smaller class sizes, that one-to-one tuition that Joel, I think, agrees is necessary. The catch-up classes, unless we find money from savings elsewhere, we've spelt that out in our manifesto, so we can provide, under our plan, £2.5 billion extra to our schools.

What we're saying, very precisely, is that we need to make another £30 billion of savings. That's the one in £100. So £5 billion of that should come from the tax evaders and the aggressive tax avoiders. We've gone after them every year in government. We'll continue to do that. We then need to go on making savings in government departments through efficiencies, and then the welfare savings that I've talked about. That's my plan, and that gets us to a budget surplus. It means we're paying down the debts, we're passing on a better country to our children, and crucially, we're not putting up taxes. Indeed, I want to cut people's taxes. People talk about the cost of living. The biggest element of the cost of living is the tax you pay at the end of the month. and I say you should be able to earn £12,500 a year before you start paying income tax. That would take people on minimum wage working 30 hours a week out of tax altogether. We should stop taxing poor people in this country in the way that we do today and that's part of our programme for the future.
