I absolutely respectfully want to make your point, because it is a concern. It's a concern to you and to other people. In our state secondary schools, we've got thousands of teachers missing in the subjects that we need. So, in a state secondary school, let's take maths. Too many students, young people, children, are being taught maths by sports teachers, by supply teachers, and that's not acceptable. And for those children of state secondary school who don't have the teachers they need, that's not a one-year problem or a two-year budgeting problem, it's a lifelong problem, because if they don't get the qualifications they need at 15 and 16, they can't go and do what they want. So we need to recruit 6,500 teachers for our state secondary schools to make sure every child, wherever they come from, whatever their background, whichever school they go to, has the same opportunity. And I'll be pressed When I say we're going to recruit 6,500 teachers, Beth and others will say to me, where's the money for that? And I say we will get rid of the tax break for private schools and use that money directly to fund those teachers in our state secondary schools.

APPLAUSE So... We live in a really dangerous world. I think it feels like it is a much more uncertain world than even a few years ago, when you have President Trump in the White House with his volatile decisions in geopolitics. I don't think this is the time to get rid of our nuclear deterrent. I think that we need to keep our nuclear deterrent and that it keeps us safe. I do think the best use for nuclear weapons is to use them to negotiate away other nuclear weapons. And the UK should be playing a leading role in all of those international diplomatic discussions about how do we create a world without nuclear weapons, because that is what we want to see. But we are not there. And while we are not there, then I do not want us to scrap our deterrent. Merlin, the blue shirt.

Plaid Cymru does want to tackle the deficit, but not at any cost, and not according to artificial deadlines. We want to invest in the future generation, and we want to invest in job creation. Our welfare state was hard fought for. And remember, that investment was put in to building that welfare state in the 1940s, when there was no money in the coffers, when the country's debt was massive. But that time delivered public services for all. And remember, it also delivered a generation in the 1950s that were told that they'd never had it so good. So continuing on the path of austerity will deliver a very uncertain future for the next generations. Investing in job creation and public services now will offer them a much better future. OK, thank you.
Richard Tice. Let's get a quick trade deal done.
Have you seen the footage of him boasting about sexually assaulting women?

Absolutely. And that deal means that the whole of the UK can come out and do free trade deals around the world and do a great deal with our EU friends and partners, Northern Ireland included. What the people of Northern Ireland don't know.

leave the country, they're leaving already. Even under a Conservative government we've got a brain drain, the likes of which we haven't had since 1978. Can I come back on that specifically? And if Labour are in power, maybe with your support, what will happen is even more will leave. Just taxing the rich doesn't solve this. And everyone here is talking about more money, more investment. I'm telling you, we've massively increased investment in the NHS for a worse return. It's not just about money.
Well, there we are. Exactly. I wanted to believe Boris when he said they were going to go for a Canada-style trade deal.

You've got to go in, and you've got to do it street by street in a comprehensive way. Now, at the moment, more than half the emissions from the UK are from our buildings and our homes. And the reason for that is because each successive government we've had has neglected this issue of the homes that already exist, because it's not sexy going around and putting insulation in people's homes. Now, we've got a massive backlog, and we have to make up for it. And I hope you're fact checkers. We'll be checking the amount of money each of us are committing to this big gap that closing this loophole that's been left by everybody so far. Of our 100 billion a year that we're putting into our Green New Deal, 38 billion needs to go into homes because this is the biggest thing we've not cracked so far. We need a deeper retrofit than Labour are proposing. They keep talking about loft insulation and windows. It needs to be much deeper than that. And that's because, and your question again about how can we change everyone's boiler. We can change everyone's boiler to a heat pump, but only if we insulate first, because then the heat pump needs to be smaller and we don't have to change everyone's radiators. And I know how annoying it would be if we went round to everyone's house and said, we need to change your radiators as well as your boiler. So we do think about things more deeply. We are putting in the resources and none of the other parties have anything like our plans for home.

Well, we all want to see those things happen, and I think it's an absolutely vital year that we're having in Afghanistan, and you can see, I hope, progress being made, but difficult, difficult times, I'm sure, lie ahead. I think there is something more fundamental that we need to do in order to answer your question properly. and that is we've got to have a fundamental defence review of all that we spend and all that we do and all the equipment that we have. Because if you think about it, over the last decade, since we last did this, we've had 9-11, we've had 7-7 in our own country, we've had the wars in Iraq and Afghanistan, and yet we haven't fundamentally asked again what should be the shape of our army, our navy, our air force, how can we best defend our country, how can we best protect our servicemen and women. And we need urgently to do that so we make sure if we get involved in these conflicts in the future we don't have the situation where we've had troops on the ground without enough helicopters. We all know that happened after 2006 and it really wasn't good enough.

Jeremy, there are going to be negotiations over all of that. I've seen the figures that Michel Barnier has bandied around. I don't recognise those figures. Others may not also. We will obviously negotiate them. Where we have a legal obligation, it must be met. Where the EU has a legal obligation, it must be met. But we have to recognise... So you've done some sums? We have to recognise that we're leaving the European Union. The priority has to be tariff-free trade access because we have a lot of manufacturing industry... Haven't you done any sums? Can I finish, please? Really? Just for a second?

There's the point about the 5.3 billion fund that will help fund this vital social care that Natalie's talking about. But I also want to say how important I think it is that we have a seven day NHS with your GP being open. eight in the morning, eight in the evening, seven days a week, because the areas of the country where that's already happening, some of them here in Greater Manchester, we're seeing a lot less pressure on hospitals because people can get to see their GPs when they want. Now, all of this has taken the extra resources that we put into the NHS because the long-term economic plan is working. David, you promised seven-day waiting in your last manifesto and you failed to deliver it.

And can I just say that, obviously, if there's more Liberal Democrats in that next Parliament, then that gives us more power to deliver that. But we also need to make sure to get there, that we stop Boris Johnson getting a majority. And it's Liberal Democrats who are able to win seats from Boris Johnson and the Tories. There's lots of places in the country where that can happen. From what we're seeing, it's not the case that Jeremy Corbyn and the Labour Party are going to be able to win seats from the Conservatives. And that's why voting Liberal Democrat is the way to deprive Boris Johnson of a majority. The man in the red tie.

Well, Linda, I have to say, I think this has been one of the worst general election campaigns so far between the two main parties I've ever seen in my lifetime. It isn't just that their leaders are dull, don't clearly... Well, very dull in the case of Labour. I mean, sort of Blair without the flair. I mean, the real leader of the Labour Party is here tonight on the stage. At least she's got some personality. We've got Rishi's slippery sunak, you know, he's fed all the lines to Penny Morton, don't believe any of it. I think it isn't just our public services that are broken, I think our political model is broken. The arguments that are going on on the other side of the room here, when the truth is, they don't really disagree on anything. fictitious numbers like £2,000, £3,000. And that's why reform has risen so quickly in the polls. There is a sense we need a revolt against this system. We need an electoral system where we get some proportional representation in Parliament. We need to break up the current model. If we get a new kind of politics in Britain, we might just get some real change. What we're being offered, frankly, are two parties promising the earth and nothing much will change, regardless of who gets in. Although, I think the breach of trust on manifestos with the Conservatives means they're probably sunk below the waterline. New politics, fresh start. We want a revolt from the British people. That's what I'm after.

No, I think that you need to be absolutely clear that the funding that you're suggesting you're putting is nowhere near and even the amount you've taken out of schools, and will just about reverse some of the cuts. But 83% of cuts will still happen in schools. That's before you even think about carbon neutral.

Ed Miliband? I do think we've seen the choice tonight, Julie, because I've said I'll cut the deficit every year and we will balance the books. I've said they'll have to be spending reductions outside key areas like the NHS and education. David Cameron has an extreme plan. He's failed in this Parliament and wants to go much, much further. Now, I'm not going to stand on the stage tonight and say there don't need to be difficult decisions. Cuts will have to come, but we can do it in a balanced way, we can do it in a fair way.

Well, let me just address that, if I may, because we have set out in our manifesto all of our plans. They're fully funded, they're fully costed and we've set out that we will not be increasing income tax, national insurance or VAT. What the Prime Minister is doing, he's got a manifesto that's got promises to you and everybody watching which are unfunded because his Chancellor has said that the money he's relying on has already been spent. Now, that is the mistake that Liz Truss made. That's what cost so many people so much across this country, and now we've got to repeat unfunded commitments, lots of promises, promises, promises, promises, but where's the money coming from? The Chancellor himself has said the money was spent last year, it isn't available. That's a... And if there's five more years of this... OK. We're going to have the same as we've got now, a cost of living crisis bearing down on people across this country, really struggling because the economy is broken. And this is the chance at this election to turn the page and start to rebuild our country with a stable economy.

And I am focused on the future, right? Because this election, as I said, of course we can talk about the past and we have done, but for all of you, the choice for you is who do you want to run the country for the next five years? Who is placed to deliver the security that you and your families want? You have to ask yourself, as Kate said in the intro, I don't think any of you can tell me what Labour are going to do if they're in power. And when governments and politicians don't tell you, it's because they think you probably won't like it. And I'm telling you, your taxes are going to go up. It's as simple as that. And that's the choice for you. Can you afford £2,000 worth of tax rises? Can your family, can your loved ones? We heard from Linda about the challenge of bills. Because that's what's coming down the track if Labour are in power. And that's why I want to fight very hard.

No, no, no, he was denying climate change. He was saying that scientists don't believe in climate change. OK, let's tie this off for now. But this, this is going to be at the heart of the movement of people...

We're standing in an election and saying what we would do if we won the election. I think that's democratic. But you haven't answered the question whether you think it's possible that there could be a Brexit deal that is better than remaining in the European Union.

Nick Clegg. Nick Clegg. Hang on a minute. Before we bandy about these things, let's be absolutely clear. We were completely exonerated for that. It was years ago. I'm talking about what's been going on now. Have you paid the money back? And listen, none of this is going to make any difference if we allow this rotten system in Westminster to carry on, where MPs have jobs for life, where they basically only need to get 20, 30% of your votes in their areas, No questions asked. They don't even need to bother until the next time there's an election. And there's a direct correlation between the hundreds of Labour and Conservative MPs who've got these safe seats, these jobs for life, and the levels of abuse in expenses. And neither of you want to clean up the system from top to toe in the way that we've been advocating for years. Thank you, Mr Clegg.

So, £75 billion we're going to inherit. We want to balance the books by the end of the Parliament, and we want to cut the deficit every year. And we're going to do it by doing three things. First of all, we'll have fair taxes. We're going to reverse the change that David Cameron made, cutting taxes for those earning over £150,000 a year. Secondly, there will have to be spending reductions in areas outside some priorities, like health and education, because we do need to get the deficit down. And I'll make no bones about that to you. That's going to require difficult decisions. But thirdly, really important other thing we've got to do. Why is it that the Conservatives promised to eliminate the deficit and haven't done so? Let's not talk about the Conservatives. Let's talk about what you do.

We've said that for pensioners with incomes over £42,000, we'll take away the winter fuel. That's a difficult decision. We've shown how we can save hundreds of millions of pounds in local government, in policing as well. But I just want to take this point. Let me bring one or two of the others in. Let me just say this to David about the spending cuts. Because I really want the audience to understand this. because Nigel hasn't denied that he wants to double the spending cuts next year. Now, he's got these fantasy figures that somehow getting out of Europe is going to save all this money, when it's a disaster for jobs in our country. The reality is that for the NHS and education, Nigel, your plans are dangerous. They're like David Cameron's plans and they are dangerous for our core public services, which Charlotte and many others rely on. All right, Nicola Sturgeon, do you agree with what Ed Miliband's saying?

Well, we certainly don't fix it by putting our faith in Labour, because the NHS is in dire crisis in Wales. We've got the worst A&E waiting times on record. It's so bad that on Friday night, even the shadow Secretary for Business, the Labour Party, called for an inquiry into our own party's record in Wales, where we've got a crisis in terms of maternity services in the south, a crisis in terms of mental health services in the north. The truth is, we need different approach which first of all recognizes we have to diagnose the problem properly. We're not going to solve it by putting our faith in either Labour or Conservative parties. We've actually allowed one of our greatest treasures in these islands to waste away under the lack of leadership under both Labour and Conservative parties and that has to change in this election.

We have had to take some hard choices across the public sector in relation to public sector pay restraint. We did that because of the decisions we had to take to bring public spending under control, because it wasn't under control under the last Labour government. And I'm being honest with you in terms of saying that we will put more money into the NHS, but there isn't a magic money tree that we can shake that suddenly provides for everything that people want.

The Liberal party want to cut child tax credits as well. We're talking about tax credits paid to children, we have fought hard for a long time to make sure that we can give children and families decent incomes. The Liberals will cut child tax credits and so will the Conservatives. The Conservatives want to save £400 million, the Liberals £1.2 billion. The Institute of Fiscal Studies said that David's proposals, and what he said was "were misleading, incomplete and regressive". And I come back to this question, why cut children's tax credits for middle-class families, when you want to give a big inheritance tax cut to the richest estates in the country who do not need that money at the moment?

May I just... I mean, Terry asked how we're going to safeguard the NHS for future generations, and in view, as Terry and we all know, of an ageing population. The NHS is under greater pressure than ever before. But there's a simple question. Who has got the plan to put £8 billion of additional money into the NHS? That is what all of us standing here have been told it requires. You're not going to get it in Scotland. The SNP have actually reduced the amount of money compared to what we've done in... Robert. ..south of the border. And you're only going to get it if you're going to ask the wealthiest to pay a small additional contribution. One other thing I'd like to say, mental health has for far too long been the poor cousin of physical health and the NHS. The more we can do to treat mental health in the same way that we've long treated physical health, I think that'll put the NHS in good shape for the future.

Well, Geoffrey, first of all, austerity Tory austerity is happening, and the cuts you're talking about in the councils are happening because of the Conservative government's decisions. Our spending plans are very different from theirs for the future. They want cuts bigger in the next three years than the last five. But let me go to the other point, which I think is implicit in your question about GAFs, so-called, right? I'm not going to win a contest for who looks best eating a bacon sandwich. I think we can all agree about that.

Well, if you have a household income in the region of £42,000, when you begin to get above that, you will pay more. That's about a quarter of people in this country do pay more under our local tax proposals. But the Institute of Fiscal Studies today has been looking at all three parties, their views on these and other matters. They say that 70% of people will benefit from our local income tax proposals. The big winners, let's be honest about this, will be pensioners. Six million pensioners will be taken out of local tax altogether. But there will be losers, and we've been quite honest with people. You've got two parties on offer at this election, Labour and Conservative, applying different forms of sticking plaster to council tax. We say you've got to do something about the wound. you've got to heal the wound. And at least we've been honest enough, and I think brave enough, to come forward with a new local tax proposal. We argue it's favour, it's fairer, but we don't try and pretend, whatever system you have, that some people will be better off as a result, others will be worse off, but the vast majority will be better off.

Well, listening to Richard and Rishi, frankly, I think you've got your own answer as to why there is so much distrust. Because once again, these issues are being used as political footballs. I really feel, you know, so depressed that people are thinking that perhaps it's not worth voting because they don't have confidence in us. All I can say is that it makes me very angry when all of us are tarnished with that same brush. I can say that as someone who has been I've been banging on in the Green Party since 1986. If I was going to go for a career in politics, I might have found a faster route. I'm in the Green Party because I passionately believe in what we stand for, and I hope others will do as well.

The point is, we have made a special exception for the NHS for exactly the reason that Mrs Neville gives, which is that there are more older people, there are more expensive treatments, there are drug budgets that are going up, and we say you need that extra money to even keep going with the NHS and that's why we make the exception of the NHS and say that's the budget that has to go up. Now, what Gordon Brown is not telling you about the situation with cancer, cancer drugs and cancer outcomes, is after all the things he's talked about, all the money that's gone in, our death rate from cancer is actually worse than Bulgaria's. So all that's happened has not actually improved the outcome, which is what matters. What matters... Gordon Brown.

Nigel Farage. Well, I mean, the fact that there are some very good foreign doctors and nurses that work in this country is not the point. It does make me, of course, ask the question why we're not training more of our own nurses. But it isn't the point. The point is that health tourism to this country costs us a very great deal of money. People in government have to make tough choices. And if the choice is that we increasingly say to people, particularly older people, we cannot treat you for breast cancer, we cannot treat you for prostate cancer, we haven't got the money, whilst at the same time we allow people to fly into Britain who have got no link with this country and contributed nothing to this system, but we're prepared to give them the drugs for being HIV positive, you then get yourself to a situation where you ask yourself a sane question. Is the job of the National Health Service to look after people here, or is it to be an international health service? And I can assure you, whatever you may think on this panel, the vast majority of British people want this to be a national health service.

I've never been called timid in my life, but coming back to the point that Nigel made, and Daisy was making the same point, look, I was a home care worker. We gutted out home care services. It costs so much more money now that people are stuck on trolleys in A&E for 40 hours plus. These are our elderly relatives. that are treated really poorly, and it costs us more money. So if we put the money in the right place, we can actually make savings. I did that when I was a home care and trade union rep. I know we can do it again. But at the moment, we're hemorrhaging money because the money is being used in the most inappropriate way, and people are not getting the care they deserve. That's my question. All right, thank you very much indeed for that. But Angela, I'm just going to raise your taxes. Thank you.

Well, Darren, in answer to your question, obviously it's wrong that that was able to happen. No terrorist should be released without it being properly assessed whether or not they still pose a risk to the public. So somebody like that just should not have been on our streets. clearly these questions need to be asked. We need to learn the lessons of this instance. I think we also need to listen very carefully to the loved ones because there are families who are grieving and mourning and the father of Jack Merritt I think was very powerful in what he said which was His son wouldn't have wanted this to be used as a pretense for some kind of draconian change in the law and I think we do need to listen to those loved ones too.

And I was actually just going to come to that, helpfully enough, because what Angela has said there in relation to oil and gas, so there's three positions at the moment. There's a Conservative Party position that oil and gas is going to exist forever. I'm afraid it's not, and I can say that with some certainty given where, of course, I live. The Labour Party position is to shut down the North Sea oil and gas sector, which would risk 100,000 jobs. Not my numbers, independent experts in what the Unite Union call putting people on the scrap heap. There is a sensible middle ground which allows us to invest in net zero, protect the jobs of tomorrow, and that's what the SNP are focused on.

I know many Muslims, but the reality is the Henry Jackson survey with J-O partners showed that in 18 to 24 years over 20% believe in jihad. Almost 50% didn't think that people, that Jewish people... And what do Muslim friends you're talking about think about? Almost 50% didn't think that Jewish people were raped and murdered on October the 7th. And I just don't think those represent British values. If you believe in jihad, if you want Sharia law in the United Kingdom, that's not who we are.

Well there is the question of wages and benefit freezes, but also we need to think about how costs can be reduced for people. So the point about rent is a fair one. There's also costs like council tax, VAT on goods could be reduced to make some things easier for people potentially. So we just need to be thinking about how we A, reduce people's costs but also increase people's income and the real living wage, not the joking living wage that the Tories have introduced previously, the real living wage is the way to do that.

Well, what I want to make sure, and it's really related to the point that I answered Sam about a moment ago, is that the legitimate decisions of the people of Scotland in the election in 2021, when a Scottish Parliament was elected with a majority of members favouring independence and a referendum, that that can actually be respected by the United Kingdom government.

And we will have a strategy for child poverty. But I think people are fed up with politicians who, before the election, say, we'll do everything. And then after the election, they don't deliver. So I've taken a decision, which is before the election, I'm going to tell you what we can't do, even though some of these things are good things to do.

Sorry. Please. Very quickly, all those candidates have either apologised or are now subject to investigation. And, you know, I don't want to unnecessarily draw comparisons with the Labour Party, but I do think it extraordinary that in this election we've heard for the first time in history the Chief Rabbi say that he fears for his community in this country were Mr Corbyn to be elected. And I think it's a quite extraordinary state of affairs.

No, I would consider other things in terms of red tape and bureaucracy that are stifling so much of business at the moment to be worthy of the term, sun setting on business. Now that's going to be our approach. Look, we're sitting in a city here in Liverpool, great city, run by Liberal Democrats, and one of the things you hear every visit I make to this city, you hear business, who for years operated under municipal leadership for Labour, saying thank goodness we've got the Liberal Democrats on the city, it's prospering, you just need to look at the construction going on. That's because where Liberal Democrats have power, we work sensibly with the business community.

Well, look, you've heard my arguments. I'll give you the final say over the Brexit deal. I will put a penny on income tax for the NHS. And I will stand up to the Conservatives over the dementia tax and everything else. Now, Amber Rudd is up next. She is not the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister is not here tonight. She can't be bothered. So why should you? In fact, Bake Off is on BBC Two next. Why not make yourself a brew? You are not worth Theresa May's time. Don't give her yours.

I'm glad you did mention the word Islamist and Islamism because this is the root of the problem and we have to be honest about that and this is an ideology, it's an idea and there are some writings, Islamic writings that are problematic. We have to admit that. There are some writings that appear to advocate violence. And Nadeem, you mentioned the Ahmadiyya Muslims earlier. I'm glad you mentioned them because they are a very peaceful group of Muslims and they practice Islam in a very peaceful way. But we do have Wahhabis, Salafists and other groups that I would call Islamists who would take all of these writings which are problematic and say that they have to be applied literally in all times and all places. not just 7th century Arabia, but 21st century Britain and France and Germany and Russia. And everywhere else there have been Islamist terrorist attacks. And we need to deal with the problem. And we do need to put police back on the street. As Brian said, I would agree, we need 20,000 more police to do community policing because that's a big hole that's been left after the 20,000 police that have been cut. But we do need to deal with Saudi Qatari funding of Wahhabi and Salafist Islamist mosques. and to stop the radicalisation.

First of all, reflecting back to what Nigel Farage said, how cold-hearted do you have to be to not want people who are coming here on work visas to be able to bring their husbands or wives or infant children with them? You would not want to cut immigration? The Green Party takes a totally different approach to immigration. When it's hard to get an affordable home, when it's hard to access public services like a GP, when the roads are poorly maintained, I can absolutely understand, Lorenzo, why you're angry about these things. I am too. But I am clear that the reason for these services being run into the ground is not people coming here, whether they're coming here fleeing violence and persecution as asylum seekers, or whether they're coming here to work in many of our understaffed sectors like health and social care. If you meet a migrant in the NHS, they're more likely to be treating you as I was the last time I saw a GP than being ahead of you in the queue. Migration's been a good thing for this country and so in the Green Party our immigration policy is not about arbitrary numbers. That's why we're not talking about caps. Instead it's about making our immigration system fairer and more humane.

Well, we're very, very behind on biodiversity and nature. I mean, credit to the movement, first of all, for getting this debate heard and for bringing climate change and new targets forward through so many motions, through councils, starting with Carla Denyer in Bristol, but now there's 389 climate and ecological emergency motions passing through councils. A lot of them were 2030 as their target. But it's the ecological stuff where we're behind. I have far too much trouble, I've spent years trying to argue against road schemes that are going to go straight through. sites of scientific interest. I've got HS2 going through ancient woodland. People have been very, very careless of our natural world. I have too much trouble getting councils off pesticides. We've got resistance from some farmers to using pesticides. We have to think more carefully about the actual world. I'm looking for your actual talk. I mean, Nicola Sturgeon. And this is something where we're very, very far behind.

We are in the transition away from fossil fuels and that transition has to accelerate. Scotland is one of the most renewable energy rich countries in the world. If we were to stop tomorrow, in terms of oil production. We would make ourselves more reliant on imports. The carbon intensity would increase. So that transition has got to be done in a way that cuts our emissions. But the one other thing that's not been mentioned here right now, which is where we've taken action and other countries haven't, is about the justice of the transition. not doing this in a way that leaves people behind, that decimates people's jobs. We have a just transition commission that's looking at how we do this transition fairly and justly. And that's really important as well. I grew up in the west of Scotland in the 1980s. I saw the effects of deindustrialisation and leaving people behind. The legacy of that is still there in too many of our communities and we can't make that mistake again.

I think this is, and certainly should be, one of the biggest issues in this election. Experts say inequality is now rising at a faster rate than any time since the days of Margaret Thatcher. I was reading a report yesterday that says by 2021 the incomes of the poorest third in society will have fallen poverty. That will take the total to 5 million. The equivalent of the population of Scotland across the UK of children living in poverty. It is obscene. So what do we do about it? Well, Tim is right, we need to do something about it. We need to do something about it. We need to do something about it. We need to do something about it. We need to do something about it. make sure we don't damage the economy by coming out of the single market. I would also want to see the minimum wage over the next Parliament start to rise in line with the living wage so it becomes a real living wage. And I tell you what else I would do, I would end the freeze on working age benefits because social security cuts are penalising the poorest in our society, many of them working. Let's bring some decency back to the social security system of this country.

Tonight's been a little bit like Groundhog Day for me because you've heard the same arguments, the same excuses, the same platitudes a hundred times before. UKIP will always be the outsider in British politics. It'll be the one that the Westminster elite and the establishment media want to mock and ridicule. But we have been proven right. Proven right on Brexit. Proven right on immigration. Proven right on grammar schools. Proven right on protecting our police and security services. On June 8th, it's your decision, your country, your vote. Vote UKIP.

Well, I think one of the things that we need to do is to rebalance the spending within the United Kingdom. So what we want to see is the UK overall increasing its level of infrastructure spending by at least 1% of GDP, but seeing that investment not going as it's as has happened decade by decade into the already affluent southeast of England, but into Wales, yes, into the north of England, Scotland, places that have been starved of investment so that we can be there at the ground level of this green jobs revolution rather than being where we've been for too long, which is at the wrong end, the long end of the queue for investment. We do it by having a fairer tax system, absolutely, but the crucial thing is that investment needs to get to the right places. It hasn't been coming to Wales.

You know, if you go through all my articles with a fine-tooth comb and take out individual phrases, there is no doubt that you can find things that can be made to seem offensive. And, of course... And I understand that. I understand that, but what I will say... Do you not think bubble is offensive? Just on the point that I think the lady was referring to... Olive. Olive was referring to. I defend my right to speak out, and I do defend my right to... And if you actually read the piece, which I don't know how many people have, if you read the piece, what I was really doing... Which one? I suggest you read any of them. or all of them. What I was really doing was mounting a strong liberal defence of the right of women in this country to wear what they choose. And I think most people, and not to be oppressed into wearing something they don't want to wear. And I think most people in this country would agree with that.

We do. That's the point of this. And I will say that I think the most important thing that politicians can do is to put in place the policy frameworks, whether it's in terms of getting rid of single-use plastics much sooner, the regulations that are going to make it easier, because what we want to do is to make it easier and cheaper for everybody to do the right thing so that the obvious decision to make is the one that is good for our climate.

Thanks, Christine. And what we need at the moment is a skills strategy. We've not had an industrial and skills strategy. So what we've had is we've been over-relying on our economy from overseas workers to fill our skills gap. And they've done a tremendous job in doing that. And we have needed that. But what we really need is unemployment levels have gone higher again. is we need to really match those skills and give people opportunity to take those jobs and that's what Labour's industrial and skills strategy is about. It's about not relying on overseas workers and we'll always have overseas workers that come and help us but we also need to have a strategy on how we skill up people here in the UK. Thank you very much indeed Angela Rayner. Nigel Farage.

Absolutely. These figures are two years old. They are what sparked me making that commitment and I will be when we come to contest the next Scottish Parliament elections, if I'm asking people to vote for me again as First Minister, I expect to be judged on that. But it's absolutely legitimate for me to be asked these questions and answer them. But on Thursday, we're not choosing a Scottish government. We're choosing MPs who will go to Westminster and vote on public spending. Whether the money available to invest in our schools and hospitals goes up or down, we'll elect MPs to go to Westminster to decide whether the social security cuts that the Tories want to impose that are going to drive hundreds of thousands, perhaps a million more children across the UK into poverty. We need to make sure we have strong voices.

Jacqueline, you asked what can we do to stop burglary happening over and over and over again. Two things. Firstly, quite simply, more police on our streets. This government wants to waste billions of your money on an ID card system so that you have to pay for the privilege of having lots of your own details on a piece of plastic car that you carry around. For pretty well exactly the amount of money, your money, that the government's pouring into that, we could put 3,000 more police officers on the streets. That is the absolute priority for me. Second thing is this. There are too many young offenders who start first getting into trouble with low-level nuisance, antisocial behaviour. who become the hardened criminals of tomorrow. What we've got to do is stop the young offenders of today becoming the hardened criminals of tomorrow. And in my city of Sheffield, where I'm an MP, we've done some great things to do exactly that. That's the way to get burglary and crime down.

And what we've seen is there are lots of people who are prepared to come to our country because it's a fantastic country to fill those vacancies and don't need to bring their family members and those are the people that we will welcome here. But what we can't do is support an unlimited number of people coming here who are not working. That's just simply not right, and it's not fair. So we may disagree on this, but I do think migration has to come down from the levels we've seen. That's what our migration cap will deliver. That's what we're already doing. And I think basic things like expecting people to support their family if they're bringing them here is actually just fair to everybody, and that's why I believe it's the right thing to do.

Can I take the gentleman's questions in turn? There are a lot of questions in there, let me try to take them in turn. Firstly, with the greatest of respect, Europe did not say that in 2014. Europe did not make any comment about what would happen if Scotland voted for independence. It certainly didn't say that Scotland would not be allowed to be a member of the European Union. Some may have taken from their body language, they weren't that keen on it, but they didn't actually say that. Nor has Spain ever said that it would veto the membership of an independent Scotland. What Spain has said is that if it is done constitutionally and legally, they would respect that. and I travel a lot around the European Union and there's been a sea change in attitudes towards Scottish independence since Brexit, both in the institutions and in member states. The European Union, since it was first established, has sought to enlarge itself. I think the idea that it's going to try to kick out or not allow in part of a member state that has been there for 40 years is kind of inconceivable. On the deficit, can I firstly make this point? Exclude London, because London skews a lot of the economic calculations for the UK. Scotland's deficit is not out of sync with the rest of the UK. But the thing is, London is in the UK. OK, but in terms of... Because Scotland is often treated as an outlier, London is actually the outlier and Scotland is in line. But that is... We'll leave that point to one side, because I can see I'm not convincing you. Well, what about you? I mean, it doesn't really matter if it's convincing me. OK, but the other point is Scotland's deficit is falling. It fell by £1 billion in the most recent year. Our onshore revenues went up by £3 billion. But my more fundamental point is this. If the argument against independence is that Scotland has a big deficit, that is a deficit that has been accrued under the Westminster system of governance. And if we've got a big deficit because of that, that's not an argument for Scotland staying the same.

Well, I certainly hope so. Look, the reality is trust in police is woeful at 50% plus or minus. That is a disaster. We have to improve that. The police themselves have to do much more. But the time that crimes take, or potential crimes take to go through the criminal justice system, is an absolute disgrace. We have to accelerate that. Only 5% of crimes are now resulting in a charge. And violence against women and girls is absolutely abominable. And women and girls have got to feel much, much more confidence. We've got to have a much faster criminal justice system. And there has to be proper sentencing that go right through until the end of the sentence, not to be let out early.
I will write the appropriate letter to our commanders, who are obviously very responsible, very loyal naval officers.

Yes, you will. And we're talking about something like Roosevelt's New Deal, like the Marshall Plan. And frankly, you know, if the climate were a bank, we would have bailed it out by now. When there's an existential threat on your doorstep, you pull out all the stops. As we saw in the financial crisis, and this Green New Deal that we're putting forward, not a narrow green industrial revolution, but a Green New Deal that covers agriculture, meat, transport, energy, every sector of the economy. We put this forward at the time of the financial crisis. We should have done it 10, 12 years ago. And if we had, we wouldn't be in this fixed with austerity, and we wouldn't be facing the same environmental destruction on our doorstep.
Nigel, would you rule out here and now? Would you rule out? ever taking a seat in the House of Lords.

I was talking to representatives of the Republican movement. Yes. Actually, so was the government at the same time. Ian Paisley was indeed thrown out of Parliament when he told the government, told Margaret Thatcher, she was actually talking to them. Do you know what? I voted that Ian Paisley shouldn't be thrown out of Parliament because I thought his voice should be heard in Parliament. You have to bring about a peace process by talking to people that you don't agree with. If you just talk amongst your friends, you're not going to get a peace process.

Well, again, we see potential for wind farms. We actually see greater London term potential, actually, for off-shore harnessing of energy. And we look as any ...party, any set of representatives should where wind farm proposals are concerned, on the merits of each case. But I think we should...

Look, as I said, some people... Could you live on one? I want to create a country where more people have the opportunity of the full-time work that they want. Could you live on a zero-hours contract? Look, that's not the question. The question is... Well, it's the question I'm asking. Yeah, but the point is, some people choose a zero-hours contract. If you're a student, for instance, and you want to do some part-time work, a zero-hours contract can work for you. That's why we haven't outlawed them altogether. What we've outlawed is exclusive zero-hours contracts. And no, I couldn't live on one of those, and that's why we outlawed them.

Yeah so on the question of buying property we want a low deposit scheme, which is what we'll put in place, so that people who've proved they can pay, because they're often paying more for their rent than they pay on their mortgage, so the ability to pay isn't the issue, but they haven't got the ability to save for a mortgage, because mum and dad haven't got the money. And they're paying it all out on rent. So that's the first thing. The second thing, which is also important for those that are in rented accommodation, is we have to stop the landlords ripping off tenants who are doing, you'll probably be familiar, this bidding war, so that when you go to get a rented property, the landlord just does a bidding war. Who will pay more? Who will pay more? Who will pay more? Until people are paying through the roof. We have to end that.

So you abuse immigrants and those with HIV and then complain that UKIP is being abused? Now, there is a risk to public services, but I don't believe that that comes from immigration. I believe that that has come about as a result of cuts. So, the answer to that is to end austerity. There are scarce resources. There aren't enough to go around. Now, our public services, especially the NHS, would collapse if we were to end immigration tomorrow. Now, there are real issues in terms of the driving down of wages, and that has to be addressed. And the way to address that is to raise the minimum wage to the living wage and to strengthen trade unions. We should be looking at repealing the trade union legislation that Margaret Thatcher brought in because if you have stronger trade unions, then you have a stronger defence of our public services and against the exploitation of workers.

It is a very, very important question and bullying in our schools is a hugely important problem. It's a part of the discipline problem which I was talking about earlier. And we have to tackle this if our children are to get the decent chance in life that they need. I'm very lucky. I come from an ordinary family but I went to a school a good state school, and I've benefited enormously from that. And I want every child in this country to have the same kind of chance that I had. So, to come directly to your question, if you give... I'm not sure that you need to ring-fence the money. If you give the head teachers the power to enforce discipline, what happens at the moment is if a head wants to exclude someone from school, someone who's been guilty of bullying, for example, That headteacher can be second-guessed by a panel who take the appeal. They may know little about the school. They will know nothing about the child. And if they override the headteacher... The Tory idea, that, wasn't it? They undermine the headteachers. The Tory idea. And we learn from our mistakes. All right, good.

Knife crime has almost tripled. Let's remember, you talk about stop and search, but actually stop and search under your government has reduced by two-thirds in the last 14 years. Per 100,000 people, we're almost 10% less. officers, police officers across England and Wales. So the reality is that you've presided over a massive reduction and that we've got to get more police out on the streets, away from the back office, away from their desks, get police officers on the streets, preventing crime, reassuring people. Stop and search works, we know it works, we need to dramatically increase the use of it.

We will have a plan for social care, and like everything else in our manifesto, it will be fully costed and fully funded. It starts with the workforce, because as you know, one of the biggest problems in social care is that the workforce is fragmented, poor conditions, and many, many people. Thank you. You talk about the workforce. If I could just pick up on this workforce.

This election is our last chance to tackle the climate and environment emergency. The climate movement has changed our politics forever and Labour has listened. Just 100 companies are responsible for 70% of emissions. And you at home must not pay the price of the transition to a net zero economy. Labour will kickstart a green industrial revolution, bringing new wealth to all parts of our country. Labour is on your side and on the side of the environment. It is time for real change.

So what I've outlined there is quite clear. Now, I have in recent days proposed a bill to prevent any privatisation of the NHS. The Labour Party were outraged at the very concept. If they wanted to back that, that would be most welcome. We would also want to see a rejection of the £18 billion worth of public sector cuts that are coming down the line. so that Scotland's public services could be funded properly. We want to see a rejoining of the European single market. We want those discussions to happen. It's good for business. We want to see increased migration.

17 times. in Parliament, we put down amendments to try to secure a people's vote. If Jeremy Corbyn and the Labour Party had backed that with sincerity, then we might be having a people's vote right now instead of a general election. But we are in a general election. This is the essence of democracy. I am standing here and telling you honestly what I would do if I was elected as Prime Minister, I would revoke Article 50 and if you vote me into that position, I will do what I've said I will do. And that's the essence of democracy.

to be a minority government again. So we don't have that overall control of the political system. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, passed by the Scottish Parliament. I can't just instruct my colleagues to vote for it. I've got to win friends and allies. So on every piece of legislation, on every budget, we've got to win friends and allies. And one of the things that I think...

If you look at the numbers we put on the side of us, look at the gross figure we pay to the EU, the number is going up. And it is one of the advantages of our deal that we will take back control of our money, our borders, our laws, our immigration system. It's very, very clear. It's there in black and white after 45 minutes. We still do not know what Mr Corbyn's deal would be. And we still don't know whether he would back it. Nor do we know who in his shadow cabinet he is proposing to deploy to negotiate that deal. Thank you very much. But not a single one of them actually backs it. Thank you very much indeed.
And that's why we have an exception for health care and social care. But we have to train our own people. That's the right thing for a government to do for British citizens.

Brown. Well, I've just got to say that my commitment to the NHS is that we will give people these personal guarantees. The main source of employment in the NHS is more nurses. We've got 90,000, 80 to 90,000 more nurses across the United Kingdom. We've got 30,000 more doctors. We've got the best equipment now in some of our hospitals. Remember the situation before 1997 when people had to wait two years for operations. It's now a maximum of 18 weeks. And yes, it is our priority that we will support the frontline services, health, the National Health Service, education and policing. These are the frontline services that people depend on. We will make sure that that finance is there. David says he will support the National Health Service, which assumes he will not give the same guarantees to education and policing as I asked him earlier this evening. But I think the main point to recognise is this. David will not give you the guarantee that you'll see a cancer specialist in two weeks or the guarantee you'll have a GP in the evenings at weekends. These are personal guarantees written into the NHS constitution that we will give.

Let me address that, because the Prime Minister says, I think he's just said the economy is all very well, going very well. I don't think millions of people across the country feel it's going very well. I was saying our export performance. They think it's going very badly and it's hitting them. But Julie, In relation to the EU, I did vote to remain, I campaigned to remain, but we left. We've left the EU, we're not going back to the EU. But I do not accept that we can't get a better deal than the one that we've got, because too many businesses are in the position you're in, which is, you know, you put yourself into your business, you put your money into your business, you take huge risks for your business, and I think you should be supported. So we want to... to get a better deal in trade relations with the EU. I think Boris Johnson botched it. He just wanted to say he could get a deal across the line. So we can get a better deal. I think a trading deal, I'd also like a better deal on research and development, where I think if we work together, it will be better for the country, and a better deal when it comes to security and defence, particularly in light of what's happened in Ukraine.

We actually all agree with that. We agree that benefits should be conditioned, we all agree that benefits shouldn't be just dished out for free if people refuse to take up work. We agree on that. Where we disagree is that I have a plan to make sure that taxes reward work when you start work. Particularly to get you off benefits. David Cameron's priority is to give tax breaks to double millionaires, and Gordon Brown has n plans to lower taxes for people on ordinary incomes an ordinary incomes. That's a big difference. That's a big choice.

I want to see a reformed House of Lords. I think the House of Lords should be predominantly elected. And Gordon, you've had 13 years to sort out the House of Lords. If there are still hereditary peers sitting in the House of Lords, if you're not happy with the House of Lords, why on earth haven't you done something about it? You've had all this time. and to suddenly now talk about electoral reform, about changing the voting system, which you started doing just weeks before the general election, I think people will see that as a bit of a ploy. But let me defend once again this idea of cutting the size of the House of Commons. Who in business, who in public services, who in their family life hasn't actually had to try and get more for less hasn't had to trim their budgets, hasn't had to work a bit harder. Why on earth should MPs and Parliament be any different? We could quite as well get by with 10% fewer MPs, we could cut the cost for you, the taxpayer, and we could do a better job at the same time.

You see, David wants a referendum if there's any future change. Nick wants a referendum now on the European Union. I think what people want is us to solve the employment problem, the economic problem, and get on with the job. And I need to work with these other countries in Europe. President Sarkozy, Chancellor Merkel, David's walked away from the European People's Party, which is an alliance of the centre progressive parties in Europe, and gone in with a group of right-wing extremists. I want to work with the sensible people in Europe to get jobs for our British economy. If we don't trade with Europe, we'd lose jobs, we'd lose businesses, we'd lose growth. Let's make sure our priority is trading with Europe, sorting out the problems of the European Union, yes, but let's make sure we get a recovery that's stronger than ever.

Sally, what we're doing, the grammar schools will be within the state sector. They might be free schools, they might be other types of schools. We want a diversity of education because education is so important. I want every youngster to get the best possible start in life and that means every youngster getting the education that is right for them. We are actually putting more money, you said we're putting money into the possibility for grammar schools to be set up, yes, but we're also increasing the overall amount of money that goes into schools and ensuring there's a fairer distribution of that money across the country. But I believe it's important that if we know that there are good schools out there, at the moment we have a law that says you can't set up any more of these types of schools despite the fact that they're good. I think that's wrong. I think we should allow those new grammar schools to be set up so we ensure every child gets the education that's right for them and the best possible start in life.

We had all these debates several years ago, I'm not going to re-litigate them, we had a referendum and the country decided and now the job is to get on and make sure we realise all the benefits of that and actually don't have to go very far from here to see it in a very tangible way for young people. In Teesside there's something called a free poll. which is a special investment zone that we created using our freedoms from Brexit. It's attracting billions of pounds of investment, creating thousands of incredibly high-quality jobs. I was there recently talking to young apprentices who are now doing fantastic careers in the growth industries of the future, incredibly excited about what that is for them and what that means. And that's just one of the examples of things that we're doing.

I want a majority Labour government, and it'll be for parties in the House of Commons to vote for it. But you ask me about deals, you ask me about coalitions, they're not going to happen. I couldn't be clearer with you.

Yeah, Harry, it really does. Look, you've asked me this for another five years, but you're saying that the last 30 have been a failure. We went over this earlier, right? So I'm not going to pretend that we've got everything right. Of course we didn't, right? No government does. Of course I'd wish we'd made more progress on areas. And I know the last few years have been particularly frustrating, given everything that's happened. But I'm not going to sit here and say that 14 years hasn't made this country a better place. Let's just go back 14 years, what happened. We were left a note by the Labour Party laughing about the fact that there was no money left in this country. They have bankrupted this country, and what have we done since then? We've delivered the third highest growth rate of all the large major economies in the world. We've created 800 jobs a day. We talked about our children. We've halved crime, and there's more to do on all these things. We've increased defence spending so that we can stand tall in the world. I mean, I could go on and on and on, right? The point is, that's 14 years. There's lots of things that would have made a difference to you, some things you would have wished we'd have done differently, but this election is about the future. If you want your taxes cut, if you want your pension protected, if you want to get to net zero in a sensible way that doesn't saddle you with bills, if you want action on legal migration, on stopping the boats, I am the only person who is going to deliver that for you, and that is the choice at this election.

If you look at the security services, what do they need? Do they need more powers or do they need to make use or be given the resources to make use of the powers they already have? The temporary exclusion orders which exist now, only one has been used in the last two years. But undoubtedly, the shadow that we are standing in now is that of Manchester. And Manchester is my capital city. It matters to me. As Jeremy said, I was there the night afterwards. All four of my kids knew people who were in Manchester that night. And it just reminds you of how safe our police and our security services keep us. The countless times that those kinds of outrages had been prevented. So to answer the question, How do you deal with it? How do you tackle that terrorism? Well, we will give an additional £300 million to police to make sure that we deal with the community policing that is so necessary. We will also ensure that we restart engagement with communities where terrorism can be fostered, so you get on the inside to prevent it and nip it in the bud. But there is undoubtedly an international angle to this too. And here's the thing. The terrorists that hate Britain, do you know what? They hate Belgium. They hate France. They hate Germany. They hate our way of life. They hate everything that we stand for. And just as we in Manchester stood together against terrorism, and across Britain we stand together against terrorism, But we must stand together with our neighbours, not just in this continent, around the world, with shared values, to fight it. And that will mean diplomacy. That will mean all sorts of things. But sometimes it will take real, serious action. Sometimes it might even mean military action. You have to do the tough thing to defeat the terrorists.

I mean, I'd actually be interested to hear some of your views as well, but let me give you mine, but do come back and give me some of your ideas. I mean, when I talk to some young people, including my own constituency, they talk about their fear of knife crime. And I think that's really terrible, that young people are worried about going out of their homes to enjoy themselves, and some of them carry knives. Now, they shouldn't, but they're worried about that. And so we have to get beside those young people, obviously through education and schools, but also through community police. One thing that used to work really well until, I think it was Boris Johnson, when he was mayor of London, got rid of them, we had lots of community police in my area. and they were highly visible, they got the sight of young people, and they got the trust of young people. And it was very effective in tackling that sort of crime.

I'll leave you a copy of this afterwards. Well, where are you going to put them? Well, I'll leave you a copy of this afterwards, and this sets out in great detail the whole proposals we've made, both on tree planting... You didn't need to print it off, I could have looked online. ...as well as on biodiversity. Yes, that tree planting will take place in national parks, in existing forests, and on farmland all across the country. We have the lowest level of tree cover of almost any country in Europe. It hasn't grown very much over the past 50 years or so, and it should have done. So you will pay farmers to plant trees? We will do exactly that. We'll also be planting in new forests as well as the new national parks that we've proposed. We have to restore the levels of biodiversity in this country. Whole species of birds have completely disappeared. Sparrows, reduced. House martins, reduced. The cuckoo, almost unheard of in many parts of the country. That biodiversity eventually catches up with us and damages food production and farming itself. So it is about restoring peat bogs and salt marshes which have been destroyed. It is about upstream river management and that's why we want to bring the water industry into public ownership to work alongside the Environment Agency so that we do use upstream floodplains as a way of reducing flooding as well as, of course, flood protection. If we don't do this, then we're all damaged. Our plant life is damaged, our natural world is damaged and eventually we're all damaged as a result of that.

No, if you're a liberal, you fight for everybody's right to be who they are. And I honestly don't understand the conflict that people think is there. Why was it so hard to answer the question? Because, you know, I'm not somebody who wants to go around talking about my faith all the time. I've never made it... You've had lots of interviews.

I live in a part of the world, up in South Cumbria, where one in three young people leave and they don't come back, despite the fact that it's the most beautiful place on planet Earth, because it's so expensive. And that is not unlike many other parts of the United Kingdom. The mismatch between wages and house prices, particularly for young people, is staggering and it is getting worse. That is why we will build every year, 300,000 new homes, the bulk of which will be council homes, and we will allow councils to do it, and we will make sure that the government directly commissions house building if those developers won't do them themselves. But there's something much more going on for young people at the moment. It is bound to be said by people in my position at least, that you've got three quarters of the young people in this country went out to vote in the most important election of their lives last June, and they voted to remain in the European Union. And their dismay, and the dismay of their parents and their grandparents, of the narrowing of their opportunities and of their futures by this extreme Brexit government under Theresa May is something someone has to stand up to. I will stand up to it. I will give you the choice on the final deal. And if you want to remain, you should be able to remain.

I think one of the biggest issues which is I think what you're touching on, Graham, is how do we encourage people who are on benefits to move into work? I was really delighted at the Institute of Fiscal Studies when they compared the three parties' manifestos this week said very, very clearly, and very directly, that our proposal to lift the income tax threshold to £10,000 is the best incentive to work. Because if you keep more of your money when you start working, particularly on low-paid and part-time work, then of course you have an incentive to get off benefits. There are too many people I meet who say, well, to be honest, it's not worth my while to go off benefits because Housing Benefit, in particular, gets withdrawn so quickly as soon as you start earning that actually when the sums are done, and quite rightly, people look at this very closely, they think, "Well, it isn't worth me working." We need to give incentives to work. Our plan would do that. I believe in work. I think work is one of the most important things in society, it gives people self-respect, and I want to encourage it. That's what our tax proposals would do.

How about the Prime Minister showing a degree of honesty about the arrangements he's actually made with Northern Ireland? He spoke at a DUP conference and said there would be no restrictions whatsoever. We now know there are restrictions. He should and could have said that at the time. And so it is a question of openness on all of this.

If Scotland had become an independent country, we didn't vote for that last year, our policy was that Scotland would have been a member of NATO. 25 of the 28 member states of NATO don't have nuclear weapons. Can I say, I mean, Ed and I don't agree on Trident, although I think some of his members agree with me on Trident. But can I say, I thought the Tory attack on Edds last week was absolutely disgraceful. This is a Tory government that sent P45s to soldiers while they were serving on the front line. But this is an issue about priority. Ed's right when he says we live in a dangerous world, and Leanne's absolutely right to say that Trident will not defend us against ISIS. I take the view that the world we live in is not made safer by us renewing our nuclear weapons. If we want other countries to give up their nuclear weapons, surely we should lead by example. We should then make sure we've got strong conventional defences so that we can protect ourselves in a dangerous world, and we should make sure we are investing the £100 billion in things that build a better future for our children.

Bush. Look, if I'm the Prime Minister of this country, and I - and President Bush needs to work with me, which he does, of course we'll work together, of course we'll have a good working relationship. Britain and the United States have many things in common, common interest, common values, and I would have a very effective working relationship with President Bush. What I'm not prepared to do is pull my punches in criticising Mr Blair, because someone else wouldn't, doesn't like me doing that. I am going to do what I think is best for this country, I'm going to say what I think is best for this country, and frankly, no one is going to stop me doing what is best for the people of Britain.

Nicola Sturgeon. Well, firstly, my condolences and thoughts remain with the victims and all those who were affected by what was a horrific attack. Clearly, the responsibility of politicians is to do everything we can to keep people safe. He shouldn't have been out. I think we can all agree on that. There are lessons that will require to be learned. But I don't think we do any service to victims or to the wider public to rush to those conclusions or to have knee-jerk reactions and I frankly don't think Boris Johnson has done any service to victims in the crass way he has sought to politicise this issue during an election campaign. The most serious offenders like this one should be in jail for lengthy periods. In Scotland we have ended automatic early release for prisoners sentenced to more than four years in prison. I think that is right. It should be up to the parole board to determine the safety of releasing somebody. So there must be lessons learned here, but let's do it properly in a way that actually does do a service to those affected by this atrocity. A quick response from Rishi Sunak, then I'll come to Sean Barry and Adam Price.
John McDonnell stood there and said very clearly that it would apply to everyone. Are you now saying that he was wrong?

Yeah, look, seriously, Humza, right? You're standing there advocating independence. Emma asks a perfectly reasonable question, which is that the Scottish deficit is 7% and across the UK it's 1%, right? So there's an obvious question as to what would happen in an independent Scotland. There's a perfectly reasonable point of view that says, I want Scotland to be independent and recognise that there would be a big hit to the economy and that we would be poorer. But what you're trying to say is that somehow there'd be magic tools that you would do something different Final thought, we still have a minute.

Indeed. But as a party, things have moved on. That was four years ago. We are against fracking. We want a ban on fracking because the climate emergency is such that we shouldn't be starting new fossil fuel industries. We need to be changing the way that we produce our energy, the way that we heat our homes. We need to be getting the carbon out of our everyday lives rapidly. And that means no new fossil fuel industries such as fracking.
You'll close down factories all over the country. We won't close down factories.

The problem both in hospitality and in the NHS is a skills shortage and so it comes back to what I said before, Liberal Democrats would get rid of the arbitrary salary threshold and work with every single industry to work out what skills we actually need and do that alongside a skilled strategy to make sure that we're training our domestic workforce at the same time. Specifically, I think your question about the NHS, I'm the health spokesperson for my party and I'm very passionate about our NHS and our social care systems. We need to look at recruitment, but we need to look at retention as well, and we're talking about having a 10-year retention programme. Part of that needs to be retaining people that we have here in our domestic workforce, but we do need to retain people who have come from abroad and need to make sure that our recruitment from abroad is ethical recruitment as well. And one of the reasons why people are leaving our NHS is because of burnout, because of the workload. And so we need to improve not only the working conditions in the NHS by the NHS being a more flexible employer, but we can reduce the pressure on the NHS by fixing social care. OK. Liberal Democrats have a comprehensive plan for doing that and we will keep banging the drum until it's fixed.

Thank you, Gordon Brown. You see, Nick, we already have what's called the Business Council for Britain, and that brings together all the leading businessmen and women in our country with government ministers to look at what we can do. We have the Tripartite Committee for Financial Stability, which brings together the Governor of the Bank of England and the Head of the Financial Services Authority and the Chancellor of the Exchequer. We already have these things, but the key thing is that we've got to have an agreed plan to reduce the deficit, protect our public services and get growth in the economy. and I'm afraid that we don't have agreement on what needs to be done even this year to sort the economy out. I want to see our economy grow this year. I want to see unemployment come down. I want to see businesses thrive and that means we've got to keep the government support in the economy for the time being, something the Conservatives don't want to do. But I also want to say we should never take the votes of people for granted. The public must decide and then it's up to the politicians to do what the public wants them to do. So it's for the voters to decide, you're the boss, it's your decisions that will count and you have the vote to make a difference.

Darren, thank you for the question. Of course, my thoughts and prayers are with all of those affected by the tragic events on Friday. And we saw the extraordinary bravery of the emergency services and the incredible resilience of those who responded from the community. And the overriding job of government is to protect us. That's why we're recruiting 20,000 new police officers. It's why we've increased counter-terrorism funding by a third. And it's why, to your question, within weeks of taking office, the Prime Minister announced a toughening up of sentences for violent criminals. Now, to the case in point, the man in question, he was released due to a law that was passed in 2008 under the last Labour government, where people were released automatically halfway through their sentence. We changed that law in 2012, and if he had been sentenced under our rules, he would still be in jail today. But what I can tell you is, we will always back the police and the security services with the resources and the powers they need to keep us safe.

Thank you. The Conservatives keep shouting out, let's move on and we got a deal. And it's move on to the next cliff edge, they haven't got a deal, they've got a transition agreement and then we've never negotiated a trade deal in less than about five, seven years. It took seven years to negotiate a deal with Canada. These are more lies and more slogans and people won't be fooled again. Greens have been completely consistent in saying we need a people's vote, we need a ratification referendum and we will campaign for Remain. I think that makes us represent the majority of people in this country. Okay, Adam Price.

I think the reason that that position changed, and in fairness it wasn't actually agreed by the party, it was Nicola's position, it wasn't the party's position. The reason it changed is that because in order to achieve independence you have to have a referendum, you have to have the gold standard, which is recognised by our friends and allies in Europe, of course, who we want to join as an independent European nation. That's why the party took a pragmatic decision to say that when we win the election in Scotland, which I believe we will, we will win a majority of seats in Scotland, we can therefore have discussions with the UK government to put that into democratic effect. And it's for the UK government and Keir Starmer to outline whether he's going to respect Scotland's democracy or not.

What I would say to Graham very simply is we should have a very straightforward approach: we should say to people, if you can work, and if you want to work, we'll do everything we can to help you. We'll get you the training, we'll get you what you need. But if you're offered a job that you can do and you don't take it, you cannot go on carrying on taking your benefits. You've got to say no to that. Now, the Prime Minister has just said, I wrote it down, "No life on the dole." But we've had 13 years of ha Labour government and there are five million people on out-of-work benefits. There are still three million people almost still on incapacity benefit. They have had so long to do something about this. Here we are tonight, talking about the need to cut waste and the need to cut budgets. Shouldn't we start with people who can work but refuse to work, so people who have done the right thing don't have to feel as Graham does? I've had so many people in this campaign who said to me, I go to work and I walk past houses where I know people could work but choose not to. That's just not acceptable and we need change to make that happen.

Of course. I don't want you to be misled by David Cameron. I'm not advocating an amnesty. In fact, the only politician in Britain who is advocating a blanket amnesty is Boris Johnson, the Conservative Mayor of London. I think we do need to do something about the fact that there are lots of people living in the shadows of our economy. Gordon Brown and David Cameron just want to deny it as a problem and pretend that somehow, it'll go away. It won't. I'll tell you who benefits from this layer of people who've been living for years and years in the shadows of our society. It's the nasty criminal gangs who exploit them, exploit us, and create crime in our communities. Can I say one other thing? When we deal with something as sensitive as immigration, let's at least be open and honest and straight with you. David Cameron says you can put a cap on immigration. It is complete nonsense, since he knows that 80% of people who come into this country come from the European Union. You can't cap those numbers, so you shouldn't pretend to people, give them false hope that you can bring numbers down when you can't control them in that way. It's wrong to raise false hopes on such a sensitive topic.
You were trying to dog whistle racism and you're a disgrace.

In 2015, when we were out of coalition, The thing that the Conservatives did on their own is they removed maintenance grants, the grants that were there for the poorest students to give them a shot at going to university. And we have said that we would put those back in place.
