But when it comes to education, we will not take lessons from the SNP, because under your watch, schools have fallen down the international rankings rapidly.

It's right that those people are held to account. Now, the Met in particular, there are issues. And actually, we instituted a report, which was done, which now the new Met Police Commissioner is working through to improve relations and make sure the Met is focused on policing in the right way. But I also want to make sure that our communities are safe. safe for you, safe for everyone else, safe for my two girls to walk around in, which is why we're putting more police officers on the street, giving them the appropriate powers they need, and toughening up sentencing, particularly for the most violent crimes, and focusing especially on violence against women and girls. So that's what we're doing on policing. Police numbers today are at a record high. And if we're elected, then we'll put 8,000 more police officers on the street, warranted officers, which one extra one for every neighborhood. Because I think, to that lady's point, that neighborhood policing is really important. And having police officers who know their communities well, and I was out with some not that long ago, where they can build those relations is really important.

That's a sore subject for us for sure. That was a very difficult decision. But let's remember that it was the Conservatives who in 2015 withdrew the maintenance grants for young people from the lowest backgrounds, which has now stopped many of them from going to university. But my point is that this election is about hope and it is about change. Many of us do come into politics for the right reason. And for any of you who have seen the party political broadcast by Ed Davey talking about his experiences as a carer for his disabled son, you will be in no doubt that he is in politics for the right reason and that we are committed to fixing the NHS.

offshore wind is now half the price of nuclear. I don't know why Labour and the Tories are still supporting new nuclear power. We also need to get more support for onshore wind, which the Tories disgracefully have taken away. These technologies are here now and we have to do much more to support them.

We have made it very clear that we will provide more police. This government has taken 20,000 police officers off our streets. But you don't deny that John Donald said all that. We'll put 10,000 more on our streets. We've also said quite clearly that... How can we believe that when the Shadow Chancellor believes in disbanding MI5 and the special branches? The Shadow Chancellor is the one who signed off that pledge in the manifesto.

I'm saying austerity has cut youth workers and there are fewer people around to challenge the root cause, the ideology that spurs these people on. I used to work as a probation officer. Can I just finish making this point? Of course. I used to work as a probation officer. If we are interested in tackling the root cause of this problem, we have to understand what it is. We have to understand those ideological drivers. And they have to be challenged by people qualified to be able to do that. And by cutting youth workers and the public services, then you are reducing your ability to do that. And that is one of the reasons we are less safe. We have a small amount of time left.

No, if I'm Prime Minister, we will not pull out of international agreements and international law, which is respected the world over. Because I want the UK to be a respected player on the global stage, not a pariah who doesn't agree with international law. And those laws were put in place after the Second World War, after the horrors of the Second World War, when all governments, all countries came together and said, never again. And it was a Conservative government. Churchill's fingerprints are on this instrument. So important was it for the Conservative Party. This Conservative Party is a million miles away. from the Conservative Party of Oldham.

and what you are going to do to protect them? Hold on a second, it is not in our manifesto for a Westminster election because it is a responsibility of my government right now. We don't need a Westminster election, we are doing it. There are people outside Scotland as well. I think people would expect me to take responsibility for what I can do directly. We have increased funding for peatland restoration and it is important that we continue to do that. We want to restore by 2030 40% of our degraded peatlands. In terms of tree planting, Scotland is absolutely leading the way. Last year, 22 million trees, more than 11,000 hectares That was 84% of the total number of trees planted across the UK. We need to increase that number. And yes, we have to work with our farmers. I agree with Adam. We should see farmers as allies in this, not as enemies. But peatlands are being burnt still, aren't they? sometimes. We don't deliberately burn peatlands, we are in the business of restoring peatlands. Again, I am not standing here, I agree with others, I think the ecological crisis has to be given the same priority as the climate crisis, they are obviously connected and we have all got more to do here, but I do think that others could look to some of the work we are doing in Scotland here and to some extent learn from it as we continue to seek to go further.

I think what you raise is really important, because for young people, the dream of home ownership is all but gone. The average age for owning a home now, Miles, is late 30s. That's shocking, because I know what your own roof over your head means for an individual. It meant the world to me when I was growing up. So we will build 1.5 million homes, picking a number requires a plan. And if those homes are going to be built, we're going to have to change the planning laws to make sure we can get on with it. We're going to have to make sure our mayors and combined authorities have the powers they need to put the homes in the places. And we need to make sure the amenities are there, the schools, the health clubs, et cetera, to support those homes. So we've got a clear plan to deliver on the homes that will make sure, Myles, that you get that chance for the dream far earlier than you do under this failed government.

Ian is absolutely right. It is completely unacceptable what has happened, and we need to grip it very, very hard to sort this out for the future. The first thing we need to do is actually regulate these banks properly. We would put back to the Bank of England the power to regulate the banks, including having a big say over the appalling bonuses that have been paid. The next thing we need is a bank levy. We say don't wait for the rest of the world, put that levy on now to start getting back the money from the banks that so many people have had to put in. We also want to see the banks lending again, particularly to small businesses, and so we need to make that happen. Something else we need to do is this, retail banks, banks that you and I put our deposits into, they should not be behaving like casinos, taking wild bets. So we agree with President Obama's plan, which is actually to say those banks shouldn't be able to take part in the most risky activities. That, I think, would start to get this under control, and make sure the banks serve the economy and serve the people, rather than the people and the economy serving the banks.

And in the areas of procurement, I think I was right. I think the NHS could do better. However, what I will say is we're going into this and the last general election with a clear commitment to keep the NHS in public hands and put £9 billion extra in. And that's taking money from a foreign aid budget, as I say, which is costing us at the present moment £13 billion every single year.

Well, UKIP has just claimed that people voted to leave the European Union, and in so doing, they also voted to curb immigration. I don't think we can read that into the result. Some people may well have voted to curb immigration, but there was only one question on the ballot paper, and immigration wasn't on it. And I'm afraid that UKIP keep using this issue. They want to whip up people's hatred, division, and fear. And that's why they talk about immigration all the time. I've got to come back to this. Right. Stop lying about immigration. Stop lying. Listen, listen, listen, listen.

I'll answer the question I was asked, if I may. Do it your own way. If we look at the situation at the moment, if you need care, then if you've got more than £23,000 in savings, you have to pay for that care. And if you need residential care, the value of your house will be taken into account. And so it's today that we see people sometimes having to sell their house in order to pay those bills. Now, what we say is that under the system we introduced, which is important, because we need a sustainable system for the future, given the ageing population. If we do nothing, our social care system will collapse. We say that we will ensure that people are able to protect more of their savings, the £100,000, that they will also not have to sell their house during their lifetime to pay for their care bills. But I also wanted a system that was fair across the generations, and I believe this is too. We said in our manifesto we'd consult on the details. I heard the scaremongering that came out after our manifesto was published, and I set out one of the details, aspects, that would have been in the consultation, which is about having a cap on the absolute level. So there's a floor of 100,000. You can protect 100,000. And we'll consult on what should be the cap of the absolute cap on the level of care costs people have to pay.

And indeed nobody would seek to dismiss the challenges which exist in our NHS right across these aisles, primarily driven through two issues. One, the backlog from Covid. but secondly the austerity agenda which is impacting us from Westminster and has done over the course of the last 14 years and there's 18 billion pounds of cuts coming down the line agreed to by Angela Rayner and Penny Mordaunt. The Tories and the Labour both know that they're going to cut public sector investment to come and that's going to have dramatic consequences on the NHS and the devolved nations because as Wes Streeting himself said when it comes to NHS waiting times, all roads lead back to Westminster. It's why the cuts that are coming down the line aren't acceptable and it's why the Labour Party need to change their stance.

But also, I mean, I think that makes a good point, because we discovered today there are about 70 Labour MPs that employ people on zero-hours contracts, and they obviously haven't got to the bit about practising where they preach. But there is a bigger point here. Yesterday, 100 of business leaders from some of the most iconic business brands, large and small, said that the plan that we have is getting the country back to work, is getting the country on the right track, and if we go off that with Ed Miliband's plan, we put the country at risk, the recovery at risk, and jobs at risk. And for young people, that's the most important thing of all.

Yeah, so let me address it straight on, tuition fees. Very expensive for students, don't work very well, students come out with a huge debt around their neck. And five years ago, I said, we should get rid of them. Now, five years down the line, The economy's been very badly damaged. We don't have the money to do everything we want to do. We do need to change the tuition fees. But I had a choice to make, which was, of the available money, do I use it for getting rid of tuition fees, which would help students, I accept that because I know how hard it is, or do I use that money to get our waiting list out? That's a political decision that I took. I'm not going to do the tuition fees abolition because I want to put that money to get our NHS back on its feet. That is my decision. It's a political decision. I accept it's different to the position I put forward five years ago when the damage hadn't been done to the economy. But that's the position because I don't think until we get those waiting lists down we can afford to do the tuition fees as well, so we had to make a choice about it. And it's a straight choice. And, you know, I'm saying this to you before the election. If you think that waiting lists down is more important than getting rid of tuition fees, if that's the right choice, then you'll go along with me. If you say, no, I'd rather the waiting list stayed where they were, bad luck to all the people on the waiting list, but at least students aren't paying fees, that's a different choice, not the one I would take. The other example you put to me is nationalising energy. When we had the energy crisis two years ago, when everybody's prices went through the roof, both at home and in their businesses, I asked my team to mock up what the options were. One option was nationalising energy. That would have cost tens of billions of pounds. That money would have been spent on shareholders to buy them off. And not a penny could have been used to lower the bills. The alternative was a windfall tax on oil and gas companies to cap the bills of many people in this room and across the country, and to ensure that people actually survived through the last two years. I took the second choice. I don't think I could have stood here today and say, I didn't do anything about your bills, sorry about that. You've all had to pay a fortune, but I did pay off the shareholders and nationalise energy. I'm a common-sense politician. I work through the issues, and to me, it did not make sense to nationalise energy and not get the bills down. So I decided we should go for getting the bills down. They are political choices. I'm telling you what they are before the election, and people can then make their mind up according to what they think.

No, it needs to come down. It needs to come down by getting people back into work with a clear plan, and it needs to come down... we're tackling the waiting lists so that more people can get back into work, which is why one of the first things that we will do if we're elected in to serve our country, serve being the word I choose, is to put in place 40,000 extra appointments, NHS appointments, each and every week, that's two million a year, to drive down those waiting lists, to give people the comfort and operations that they need, and to allow them to get back into the economy so that we can grow the economy, which is the thing that's been missing so desperately over the last 14 years.

Well, Rob, look, I can understand your frustration, everyone's frustration, because the last few years have not been easy. We've dealt with a lot as a country. I'm not saying we've got everything right. I certainly would be the first to acknowledge that. But I've been your prime minister for 18 months. And in that time, we've got inflation down from 11% with all the damage it was doing to the cost of living back to target. The economy in the first quarter of this year, fastest growing in the G7. Wages rising faster than prices for almost a year now. And my primary job was to deliver that economic stability so I could start cutting your taxes. That is the journey we're now on. So whatever your other frustrations, the choice for you at this election is about the future. If you want lower taxes, If you want your borders secure, as Steve was asking before, if you want a sensible approach to net zero, if you want your pension protected with a triple lock plus, that is what I will D for you. This is a serious election with consequences for you and your family. I get the frustrations, but think about the choice. Allow me to finish the job I've started, cut your taxes, protect your pension, secure our borders. Because if Keir Starmer and the Labour Party are in charge, your taxes are going up, our borders are going to be insecure, you're going to be saddled with bills as he reverses my net zero changes, and every pensioner is going to face a retirement tax for the first time in history. And that is the choice that you should have in your mind. Robert...
The industrial base. No, that was happening under Thatcher when you were in that party.

I've done this. You can't trust him on this issue. Do not surrender our borders to him. This is the difference between me and the Prime Minister. Before I did this job, I was running the Crown Prosecution Service. I was the Director of Public Prosecution. And it was my responsibility to take down criminal gangs on behalf of everybody in this room, everybody watching to protect the country. So I know that this can be done. You share the data, the analysis, the intelligence. You have to have a strategy to arrest everybody at exactly the same time. You need to know what evidence is going to be used in which court, where, and which country is going to have a lead on the prosecution. We did it to take down really dangerous terrorist gangs. There was one terrorist gang that involved bringing down nine planes in the Atlantic at the same time with bombs. That was one of the cases that I had to deal with as a prosecution. That's a very different kind of threat, isn't it? No, no, no, but these were very sophisticated terrorist gangs. I do not accept that this can't be done. for the gangs that are running this wild trade.

Well, frankly, I've said nothing should be taken off the table. As far as I'm concerned, you know, when MI5 tell us that there's a possible 23,000 jihadis out there who want to do us harm, I'm sorry, but I would always... Let me finish. Let's not give away our freedom. I would always put British lives over the human rights of any jihadi any day.

What Conservative governments always do is look after the very richest within our society. They've done very, very well in the years of austerity, while it's communities all over the country that have paid for it with closed libraries, closed swimming pools, out of reach pricing on sports facilities, lack of housing, increased stress, increased mental health stress. We have to redress the imbalances within our society. And our tax proposals for corporation tax would be to raise it to 26% at the end of the first period of that Labour government, which would actually be lower than it was in 2010, lower than it is in France, lower than it is in the USA. What we're proposing is a serious set of plans in order to start addressing the imbalances within our society. I think it's important to do that.
We are talking about misuse of information.

There's two things I would say to you back, Nigel. The first thing is that your plan in relation to migration, I believe we do need to try and get net migration down, but your plan This is your plan. It's to get out of the European Union. Now, that's your way of doing it. Now, that is a disaster for our country. That is a disaster for jobs. That is a disaster for our economy. So I'm not going to go along with your plan. But look, the second thing is this. We can do more for local people when it comes to housing. Take this issue of planning permission. Planning permission is granted locally, and then local people find themselves locked out by buy-to-let landlords from out of the area. I say give councils the powers to set aside homes for local people in an area because planning permission is more likely to be granted and local people can get homes to live in.

Well, first of all, Shirley, I want to thank you for all of the work that you've done in the NHS, and I want to say first and foremost that I think we should be properly rewarding all of those amazing people who work in our NHS. They have had pay freezes, they've had an incredibly difficult time recently, and I just want to thank them from the bottom of my heart for all they do in the NHS. I think it's clear that our health service is in crisis, that waiting times are up, people are being treated in corridors, hospitals are closing and it simply can't be right that big business is making money out of that mess. So as well as putting more finance into the NHS, the Green Party is really clear that we would take the private sector out of the NHS. We think it's quite wrong that we have this costly contracting-out process, which is hugely administratively burdensome, hugely costly. We'd give an emergency kiss of life to the NHS using funds that would otherwise be used for our redundant nuclear weapons programme. We think it's a no-brainer to use those funds to look after people rather than to create more weapons of mass destruction. Thank you. Tim Farron.

I'm still suffering from the shock of having found one thing I agree with Paul Nuttall about, which is that HS2 is a disaster. Thank you. But that apart, when it comes to what kind of economy we want, then it has to be a green economy. Otherwise, frankly, there won't be an economy at all. We can't simply go on growing and growing with more and more production and consumption in a way that creates more pollution that costs more money to clear it up. What we can do is to invest in the greener economy. For example, why did the government flog off the Green Investment Bank to Macquarie, this company that has a horrendous track record when it comes to things like fracking and bad environmental practice. We need things like a Green Investment Bank to be investing in the new green technologies of the future. They are hugely labour-intensive. They get people back to work. We want to see a Green New Deal. That means investing and insulating in every single home in Britain. It will get hundreds of thousands of people back to work. It will get our fuel bills down, and it will tackle our climate. targets as well and nobody so far I have to say has even mentioned the climate crisis. Thank you very much indeed Caroline Lucas.

It's perfectly clear that the Liberal Democrats do propose an amnesty. They can't get away from that. And Refugee Action, one of the very respected voluntary bodies, has criticised them for that, and I think for a good reason. We should in this country be trying to encourage people and reward people for doing the right thing. If you have an amnesty for illegal immigration, you're basically saying to people who came here illegally, who didn't have the right to come here, "That's OK". And to people who are queueing up who want to do the right thing and obey the rules, they're being punished. That's one of the things that's wrong in our country at the moment. We need a different set of values, and this is not them. It doesn't make sense.

First of all, I'd like to say a big thank you for what you do, because I think teachers just perfrom the most incredibley important work in our society. We should do more to value them, to respect them and to raise their status. One of the most important things we can do is give the teacher and headteacher, in particularl, control back over their school. Discipline is the absolute foundation of a good education. Right now it just doesn't work. We have something like 17,000 attacks each year on teachers. You get kids who can be excluded from school, including one in Manchester who is excluded for having a knife, who then gets put back into the school by the appeals panel. That's just not right. We say make the head teachers captain of their ships, let them have proper discipline, change all the crazy rules that stop teachers searching for banned items. And make sure you have proper discipline. Then we need to raise aspiration, as I'm sure you do in your school. And open up education, have the big society where we say new bodies that want to come in and set up great schools, including in inner city areas, come on in, we want choice, diversity and excellence in our state sector. That's where I send my children. That's where I know we've really got to do better than we do today.
Well one is happening as an immediate redundancy, I mean they've actually been handed their redundancy notices. The other will happen over time and actually we've still
The NHS is not for sale, never has been, and never will be.

I've said I'll serve the full term, and exactly what situation happens then and how you hand over, well you can leave that to a later time. But it's not unknown you know for, for - there are countries with fixed term parliaments

to make up the pension from what you've had as a result of your earnings being interrupted during the course of your life. Now, what are we doing now? We're going to link pensions to earnings in 2012, so every pension will be linked to earnings and not just prices. Secondly, women, and you are one of them, who have not had the full state pension, we are making it possible for all women in future to have that full state pension. And thirdly, of course, we've got to deal with the poverty that people face as pensioners. And that's why the pension credit, the winter fuel allowance, all these things have been introduced, the free concessionary travel as well, the free television licences over 75, all of these have been introduced over the last few years because the one thing that scandalises our society above all else is that we cannot give dignity and security to all pensioners in retirement. That's what I want to see and that's what I want to do. David Cameron.

As Sian rightly says, we would cancel the third runway at Heathrow. We would also cancel HS2. It is a vanity project. It is kind of obscene. The suggestion is that England is going to get a high-speed north-south railway we in Wales haven't got any kind of railway link north to south within our country. We want to rectify that, bring back the Aberystwyth to Carmarthen railway link, take it up all the way to Caernarfon and Ynys Môn and see the proper investment in our transport infrastructure within Wales. We only get 1% of the transport infrastructure investment in the UK, we've got 5% of the population and it's been like that for decades. I have to say, Jeremy, under Labour governments as well.

Well, Nigel's going to keep your fact checkers busy for a little while. Nigel Farage has been misleading you. I'm an engineer. I used to work in the renewable energy sector. I know very well how wind farms work. So much of what he said there is simply untrue.

That was on the program. I'm not disputing that. I'm just saying that on counterterrorism, the police asked for more funding, and we delivered that. On cyber, another $1.9 billion is going to cyber security. I think the lady in the front mentioned what's happening on social media and so on. And your very good point about the criminal aspect to this, what Theresa May wants to do is work with other countries to get to a place so that actually our security agencies, our security services can work with the online platforms so that we can have a way where your information is secure and you can talk to your friends and everyone else in a secure place. But those people who are using those safe places can't use them anymore. We need to make sure there are no safe places. on the internet for these people. And that's a big area that we want to focus on as well. We want to look at legislation in terms of how do you, if you know someone's about to be radicalised, how do you try and bring them back away from that? How do you curtail their movements? How do you deport those who shouldn't be here in the first place? Those are the sort of things that we want to look at if we are back in government after Thursday.

I think that the rhetoric on immigration has not helped the economic situation that we're in. There are gaps in the Welsh economy that need filling, and this debate does not help. The one thing that I would agree with Nigel Farage on, and I never thought I would ever say that.
No, I'm reiterating what he said before, which is the idea of people working a four-day week at some point in the future, in maybe ten years, is something which could be considered.

We are the party that wants to back our police to have the resources to actually do something about it. Because at times like this, it's very easy and tempting for a politician to come up with a knee-jerk, sounds-good response. I want to do some good. I realise, at this point, people are seeking answers and want to see action. But, for example, you talked about the anti-radicalisation strategy, and you asked the question before about people we know about and aren't tackling. We know that the murder in Manchester was reported by his community on five separate occasions. That is a reminder that the community out there are desperate to tackle terrorism, and our police and security services do not have the resources to enable them to do it. That's where the priority must be.
Is it 10,000? Is it 10 million?

Mary, let me just say, I was ashamed by the behaviour of some of the MPs in the House of Commons. What they did was completely unacceptable. And no punishment is too great for them if they have to go before the courts and answer for their crimes. And I think we've got to be very clear, we want nobody standing at this election who is not transparent and open about what they're doing, and anybody who breaches the guidelines that we've now laid down, the Independent Standards Authority, should be thrown out of the House of Commons, and indeed out of the House of Lords if they're a member of that as well. But you know, when I was talking to young people only yesterday, and talking about politics, if you ask the question, politics don't make a difference, people will probably say yes. But if you then ask the question, ah, but my job may depend on decisions that are made by governments, then people say, ah, I might be interested. When people then say my health service may depend on how much resources government is prepared to invest in the health service, then people say, yes, politics may make a difference. And then when you ask about crime and it depends on how many policemen are on the street, then it makes a difference. And if you ask about schools and what the standard of your schools are and how you're investing for the future, that makes a difference. So I ask Mary to think politics can make a difference.

Scotland already produces 75% of its electricity from renewable sources. We absolutely now must prioritise transport, which we have spoken about, and heat. We already have a home energy efficiency programme. We are already investing hundreds of millions of pounds in trying to help people insulate their homes and heat their homes better. There is also a role for regulation here. We have given a commitment already to have a new home building standards in place. By 2024, all new buildings will have to have non-fossil fuel boilers in them. There is a big programme of work here.

Well it's rather difficult because Gordon says Nick agrees with Gordon and Nick says Nick doesn't agree with Gordon so let me try and find something we're all agreed on that we could change and will make a difference. I think it is time that when an MP breaks the rules that those constituents should be able to throw that Member of Parliament out of Parliament without having to wait for a general election. I think we all agree about that and whoever wins the next election we can put that in place straight away.

Last autumn, George Osborne made a speech at the Conservative Party conference, and he said that the Conservative Party, in a radical departure from the sensible way which we have adopted over the last five years to... I'm not going to ask the very wealthy in society to pay a single extra penny in tax to balance the books and instead only the working age poor are going to pick up the tab for the mistakes made by the bankers. I think that's unfair. They've said they want to take the equivalent of £1,500 off the 8 million poorest families in this country. They won't tell you how they'll do that. £12 billion is about exactly as much as we spend as a country on disability living allowance. Are they going to scrap disability allowance? They're not going to touch child benefit. Are they going to scrap this allowance? It's about the same as we spend on employment support allowance. Are they going to scrap that? I think the point that Danny was making, quite rightly, is we've had five weeks of this election campaign. The Conservatives have a very unfair plan to balance the books, which departs from what we've done in coalition. And I think we are entitled to say, what are you going to do? Who are you going to hurt? Who is going to bear the pain? OK, the woman up there, the back row.

You never attacked Obama, not once. We've got strongman politics. Not once. We've got Nigel Farage, we've got Boris Johnson, we've got Donald Trump. That, in my view, is not what the UK, or any part of it, needs. Donald Trump's attitudes, I think, are wrong and they're dangerous. And in Boris Johnson, we've got somebody who's trying to be like him. That, I think, is the wrong way to go. And people in this country, people in Scotland, have a big choice to make. Are we going to allow our future to be dominated and decided by the Trump-Johnson-Farage axis, or are we going to choose something much better? for the future of our country, of this generation and those who come after us.

Penny Mordaunt, done well, both should help the other. What is critical, though, is if you want to reach net zero by 2050, you have to do it with people. You have to ensure that people can afford to get there. If you don't do that, you will fail to reach your targets. We are going to do this at a pace that people can afford. 2050 sounds a long way off. And in this election, you're looking at the next five years. At the end of this parliament, if they get in, you will not be able to buy a petrol car. If your boiler goes, you'll have to spend tens of thousands of pounds on a new heat pump, other things that need to be done to your home to convert it. My constituents cannot afford that. You heard about GB Energy. You heard about GB Energy. This is an energy company that your money is going to be put into that doesn't produce any energy at all. It doesn't produce a drop of energy. Oil and gas, this is the insanity of Labour's policy. They are expecting a load of people to invest in oil and gas, at the same time saying they're not going to expand the sector. So they're not going to get the money in. And the final thing, if you do this too fast, All you do is destroy our supply chain. We should be building supply chains in this country to produce batteries, to do all sorts of things. Let's hear the response.

Yes. Of course, the deal will be better because it couldn't be much worse than the deal that we've had whilst we've been within the European Union. And I encourage the Prime Minister and David Davis, when you go into these negotiations, be bold, have courage. Promote how great our country actually is and accept that in many ways the European Union needs us far more than we need it. We have a huge trade deficit with the EU. There are 6 million jobs on the continent which are dependent on British trade. And I want to say to the Prime Minister, be bold on immigration, because when people voted to leave the European Union, they didn't just vote to control our borders, they voted to control immigration. Say no to any divorce bill that is put on the table, because since we've been members of this club, we've paid in over £180 billion in membership fee alone. The deal will be better, but only if Theresa May and David Davis have backbone. Paul Nuttall, thank you.

We don't have to take any more of this. The Tories' heavy austerity has cut deep into the lives of those who can least afford it. Labour isn't challenging austerity, just offering a slightly lighter version of the same failed recipe. What does that leave? The Lib Dems. Many people voted Lib Dem in 2010 to keep the Tories out. They won't be doing that again. Tactical voting has given us the kind of politics that we have now. Instead, I urge you to vote for what you believe in, for our future and for future generations to come. If you agreed with me tonight, vote green. Don't listen to the people who tell you that you'll have to settle for a pale imitation. If you want real change, you have to vote for it. It's time to vote for hope. It's time to be bold. Vote green.

Anna's absolutely right, the house building industry has really not served us well in this country. When the crisis happened, the building firms didn't have enough capital, weren't able to survive and so many went under. Yet, there is a pent-up demand for housing in our country. There are one million more home owners than there were just over ten years ago, so more people are buying their homes. What I would like to do, we have extended stamp duty relief for first time buyers, so that is available now. Shared equity is something that might be considered because that's a chance to buy up a part of your house, and it's become a more popular way of doing things and we are able to help finance that and work with the building societies and banks. The third thing is, of course, getting the building societies and the banks to lend again. We've signed these agreements that require them to lend £92 billion this year, a lot of that for mortgages. The fourth thing is, of course, as has been said, using up empty property, giving local authorities the power to build and making sure housing associations have also the power to build. I want to create an owner-occupied majority in this country and I want to increase home ownership and I want to do it quickly with the measures that we are taking.

This election is the most important in our lifetimes. At stake is not just Brexit, but what kind of society we want to be. The SNP wants Scotland to be an independent, internationalist country. And we are determined that the people of Scotland must have the right to make that choice. But we also want to play our part in locking Boris Johnson out of Downing Street. Make no mistake, if he wins a majority then the consequences will be dire. Brexit will not in any sense be done. Trade talks haven't even started and these issues will cause chaos for years to come. In the rush for a trade deal with Donald Trump, the Tories simply can't be trusted not to put our NHS on the table. They're also threatening to rip up rules on food standards, environmental protections and workers' rights. To build a better future, we need to stop Boris Johnson and the Tories. And in Scotland, that means voting SNP. Adam Price.

With respect, more council houses were built in this country last year than in 13 years of the last Labour government. We are building 240,000 homes now every year. That's the way to resolve this, not by making, with respect, cheap political points, but working to get houses built in this country of all types, from ones by the private sector to council properties so that more people can get a safe and secure home.

Tonight, I hope you have seen and heard why a strong SNP voice at Westminster matters, and why there needs to be strong and effective opposition to a Tory government that is increasingly in hock to UKIP. We have seen, sadly, that Labour simply isn't strong enough to hold the Tories to account. the SNP can provide strong opposition. My message to those of you watching tonight in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland is that SNP MPs will always work for jobs, fairness, and progressive values. And to those at home in Scotland, my message is this. Now more than ever, as we face big challenges, it is vital that we have strong SNP voices at Westminster. It is only with a strong group of SNP MPs that we will have a strong enough hand to protect our public services from Tory cuts, stand against an extreme Brexit and continue to make our country the very best that it can be.

Let's be honest with each other. Net inward migration is falling. It has fallen three years ago, two years ago and it is falling this year. It is falling as a result of the action we are taking and will continue to take. To stop illegal migration, which is what Nick has referred to, border controls are being brought in and we are counting people out and in from the end of this year. It was a policy that the Conservatives scrapped before 1997. We've got ID cards now for foreign nationals so an employer can see whether they're legal or illegal. so we're taking precise and specific action. But what I wouldn't do is have an arbitrary cap. An arbitrary cap means that an employer, and I've been visiting a lot of employers in this region, who wants to bring someone in from abroad, needs that skilled worker, the skill is not here, cannot do that. Net inward migration will continue to fall under our policies. 40,000 less students, I suspect, this year because of our tightening of visa controls. Now, these are the things practically we can do. We're a tolerant country, but we want proper control over migration. David Cameron.

That's not quite how diplomacy works. The reason why I am optimistic is because there has been such substantial investment in renewable energy. And yes, in solar, it is increasingly doesn't need a subsidy. So because of that investment, it makes good economic sense as well as good renewable energy.

Well, that is totally untrue. We're doing 20 upgrades. And yes, it is true that we are starting with six, but the seed funding has got in. So where did the 40 come from? hospitals. And the investment is going in now. And the only reason that investment can be made is because we have a robust economy. And let me make another point. The single biggest threat to our economy and our ability to fund the NHS is our failure to get Brexit Ask the Governor that. It's absolutely true.

I don't want to be too technical here, but the stats are not directly comparable. More young people in Scotland than in England do higher education and further education colleges. That's not included. We have more Scottish students going to university now than ever before. We have more places than ever before for Scottish students and the gap between the richest and the poorest is starting to narrow. We need to do more. Young people in England are coming out of university with £27,000 of debt just from tuition fees. Young people who come out of university in Scotland have much lower debt to take into the start of their working life. I think education should be based on your ability to learn, not on your ability to pay. And I think it's important we protect that principle.

Thank you, Tim, for that pass. So you've heard the squabbling and discord of disagreement here tonight. You've seen the coalition of chaos in action. But in the quiet of the polling booth, you have a clear choice. A vote for anyone other than Theresa May is a vote for Jeremy Corbyn and that coalition. Our government needs to be at its strongest to take us through Brexit. It is only Theresa May that can deliver that leadership.

Yes, of course I know what's in his mind. And what's in his mind is to carry on running the strongest economy in the Western world, and thank goodness he's been doing it. Or to become Prime Minister. Well, that may be there too. But before we get to that stage, David, we've got to win this election, and there's a long way to go.

I just feel sorry for Adina who must be completely lost by all this political points scoring. The fact is, Adina, you are right, taxes are unfair, we have a plan, we think it's a great plan, to switch taxes so that you get more money back in your pocket. That's what I think we need to do to make taxes fair.

It's hardly leaked. Danny Alexander said it to The Nation. I mean, he's just saying, quite rightly, that unless the Conservatives come clean, and I don't think David Cameron's done it this evening, where they're going to take the equivalent of £1,500 off eight million of the most vulnerable families, we can only assume that they're looking at the kind of plans which they floated some years ago. If they're ruling that out now...

I think the commitment we've given on the foreign aid budget is an important one. And I think it's an important one for two reasons. First of all, we are one of the, we're the fastest growing, second fastest growing economy in the G7 last year. We are one of the most significant economies in the world. I think it's right that we say that we help those people who are less well off than we are in those developing countries. There are millions of children millions of girls being educated today who would not be educated were it not for the foreign aid money that we're giving. But it's also about something else, because it means that in certain states it's possible to help to develop the economy, the governance of that state, And that is of benefit to us in protecting us as a United Kingdom for a whole range of reasons. If we can stabilise certain countries, then that's going to be better for us, both in terms of security, for example. But we do look very carefully at individual countries and individual payments.
And I think we should also encourage people in marriage to have some tax benefits as well. I really do. So that's a yes? A lifting of the two tariffs? We've got to help people.

this time last year I campaigned passionately for the UK and Scotland to stay within the European Union and I still believe that for all its faults we are better off in the European Union than outside of it and I believe not now but when the time is right at the end of this process Scotland should have a choice about our own future but the priority now is is the Brexit negotiations. Because Theresa May is not just pursuing Brexit, she is pursuing a hard, extreme Brexit. Even today when she published her manifesto, she threatened again to walk away with no deal. That would be an economic catastrophe. That would cost in Scotland alone 80,000 jobs. So my message to people in Scotland, whether you voted remain or to leave, is to vote SNP to strengthen our hand to get a deal that does not sacrifice Scottish jobs and the economy. Because that kind of deal is not just in the interests of Scotland, but in the interests of all of the UK.

These are no ordinary times. And this is no ordinary election. We've just been going through the biggest global financial crisis in our lives. And we're moving from recession to recovery. And I believe we're moving on a road to prosperity for all. Now, every promise you hear from each of us this evening depends on one thing. A strong economy. and this is the defining year. Get the decisions right now and we can have secure jobs, we can have standards of living rising and we can have everybody better off. Get the decisions wrong now and we could have a double-dip recession. And because we believe in fairness, as we cut the deficit, over these next few years, we will protect your police, your National Health Service, and we will protect your schools. I know what this job involves. I look forward to putting my plan to you this evening.

We want to get the numbers right down. To what? With a plan to get people back into work. partly that's supported, partly it's the changes we want to make to the job centres to make sure that where businesses and public services, the NHS doesn't go to the job centre when it's got vacancies, they should do so we want to merge those two services but the other thing if we're going to get people back into work is we have to tackle these terrible waiting lists because nearly 8 million people are on the waiting list and so it's not their fault that they can't get back into work. It's the fault of the government that's put us in this terrible position. We have to address these bases.

No, I mean, there are two issues here, and it's not about... You've got to have the police there to respond, as you said, when an incident like this happens. And luckily in London, there was a great number of armed response vehicles, they're called, who were able to respond really quickly to deal with the incident. I'm a bit concerned that in other parts of the country there might not be enough armed officers because there's like a thousand less than there were 10 years ago. A thousand less armed officers than there were 10 years ago. So that's one aspect of it. But the most important aspect as far as we're concerned is is this erosion of community policing. Because as well as the 20,000 police officers that we've lost since 2010, we've also lost 24,000 support officers. And the police community support officers are a big part of those community teams. And what that means is that some communities begin to lose trust and confidence in the police because they don't have a local officer that they can relate to. And when that happens, it means that you don't get the intelligence. I don't know whether people saw the reports the day after the London Bridge attack. One of the neighbours of one of the guys involved said, oh, I remember now, earlier on, on the Saturday, he was being over-friendly, and then he started talking to me about hiring a van. Of course, it now all makes sense. Well, maybe if he'd had his suspicions about that guy, there'd been local community officers that he could have talked to, maybe he might have talked to them about his feelings. We know that that guy was already on the radar of the security services, so maybe the security services might have put two and two together. So we don't think we need more powers. We don't think we need more draconian powers. There's lots of powers that the police and the security services have that they're not using at the moment, but we need to make sure that they use them effectively.

Well thank you Jenny for that question. No, I don't want to do that. This report that was out today is something I rejected at the time as Prime Minister and I reject it again today. But I do think it's important we go on reforming welfare. It's worth remembering when I became Prime Minister we had a situation where some families were getting £70,000, £80,000, even £90,000 of housing benefit for one house. And think how many people watching this programme were going out, working hard, paying taxes to keep that family in that house. A house they could never afford to live in. So we've got to go on reforming welfare. Let's make sure that work always pays. And let's go on doing what we've done for the last five years, which is get two million more people back into work. That's the most important thing we can do, helping people off welfare into work. That's the sort of country I want to build the next five years.

I'm really grateful for everyone in the NHS for working so hard and we have now settled pay rises with everyone in the NHS except for the junior doctors and of course I want to do that to make sure it's fair and affordable though but despite being offered the most generous pay settlement anywhere in the NHS and indeed the public sector the Union are demanding a 35% pay rise and I don't want to raise your taxes to pay for that because I don't think it's right but I will say this So when Keir Starmer says he'll resolve it, he hasn't explained to you how, but I will say this... OK, perhaps you can answer that, Keir Starmer.

Paul is talking about taking resources from some of the poorest people in the world. And we contribute through foreign aid, not just to help the poorest people in the world, which I think, as it happens, is the right thing to do, but also to make this country safer. Now, Paul says that's where he wants to get the money. The question he still hasn't answered, if he's going to stop EU migration, where's he getting the staff for our national health services? It's simple.

David, will you not answer the question? Why are you cutting investment allowances for manufacturing industry? Why are you also going to take away the regional development agencies and scrap their functions? What good does that do to the West Midlands, that wants investment in manufacturing and wants the regional development development agency to work for them?

I think people at home watching will find it extraordinary that Gordon Brown is really saying you've got to go on wasting money to keep the economy going. Why not cut the waste and stop the tax rise? How is a 7% pay rise for NHS managers essential for economic growth? I'm going to have to park it there.

On Thursday next week, this country reaches a turning point. We can get Brexit done. We can end the paralysis. We can transform our country with massive investments in health, 50,000 more nurses, more funding for every school, 20,000 more police. We can spread opportunity fairly with fantastic infrastructure, education and technology. And the only way to unleash the potential of this whole country is if we get a working-majority Conservative government. Because the only possible alternative is another hung Parliament, with Jeremy Corbyn and Nicola Sturgeon, with an economic agenda that would take this country back decades, and two referendums, one on Scotland and a second, toxic and divisive, referendum on the EU. Let's give that nightmare a miss. Let's end the chaos, the deadlock. Let's get Brexit done with a one-nation Conservative government and take this country forwards.

I find this extraordinary and I'm also deeply depressed by what I've seen and no wonder people are turning off politics and there's collapsing trust you know I mean because you know that the level of rancor in the debate we should have sincere disagreement and I can understand you know some people sincerely disagree with me when I say that Brexit is going to hurt the Welsh economy and they still want to continue with it. In terms of the Labour Party's position, the great merit of Jeremy Corbyn, and I knew him when I was at Westminster, he spent more time voting with me than he did with his own party back then, he was a conviction politician who said what he believed clearly, without fear or favour. And yet on this central question, it is a heart, whether we like it or not, of this election, he won't even say where he stands. And no wonder people don't have faith in politicians when the leader of the Labour Party, the candidate to be Prime Minister, won't even tell us where he stands on this central question that's going to cast its shadow for a generation. LONG-BAILEY: Very briefly, Rebecca Long-Bailey. LONG-BAILEY: It's right that Jeremy acts as an honest broker in this, because he needs to get a good deal with the European Union, and I think stating how he would vote in a final public vote would undermine that. LONG-BAILEY: But also, he wants to heal a divided nation and bring people together, because whether you voted Leave or Remain, you didn't vote to be poor, you didn't vote for industries like steel to be undermined, you didn't vote for a trade deal with the US that would put our NHS on the table.

I want to particularly pay attention to the plight of young people in this country. The fact is housing benefit being cut for those under 25, minimum wage lower than anybody else for those who are young and this is a situation we find ourselves in 12 months since the referendum when we remember that three quarters of our young people voted to remain and it seems to me that our leader in Theresa May and Jeremy Corbyn going along with her are deciding to put together a system which allows our young people's futures to be trashed and taken for granted. That's why the answer surely has got to be to fight that we do stay in a single market, that we do meet the needs of those young people, that we give them the housing benefit they deserve.
I've never taken illegal drugs, no.

Anti-Semitism is wrong and totally unacceptable in our society, in our parties, in our political life, and the history of the awful treatment of the Jewish people in the 19th century in Germany indicates exactly where that kind of language and that kind of racism leads us to. There is no place for it whatsoever anywhere in our party or our society, and there never will be, as far as I'm concerned.

Right. Very important question, Bob. We can control immigration from outside the EU in the way we do now by closing down these bogus colleges, and we've closed down about 800 of them, and we can do it by having a cap on migration for economic reasons coming into the country. What can you get that done? I'm going to do it inside the EU. The key changes that I'm going to make, if you re-elect me, are these. First of all, anyone coming from an EU country cannot claim unemployment benefit while they're looking for a job. Second, if they're here after six months and haven't got a job, they have to go home. Third, you'd have to work here for four years paying into the system before you get tax credits and other benefits out of the system. And fourth, if you're living here but your family's back at home, you will no longer be able to send the child benefit home to your family. Now, those four changes are big changes. They'll make a real difference and help us to control immigration. And if I'm prime minister, that's what you'll get.

Yeah, well I think this is about demand and supply. Markets are about demand and supply. If you're having to build a new house every seven minutes to cope with 300,000 people a year net coming into Britain, you have a problem. So that side of it. A government, an independent government, can deal with that and reduce the numbers coming. But we need to build lots and lots of houses. The problem is that developers want to build on green field sites. It is cheaper for them. It is better for them. And indeed, changes in the planning laws mean it's actually much easier for them to build on those sites. I think what government needs to do, and I don't always want government to intervene, but I do think here government should supply grants and actually make decontamination of brownfield sites something that is a big opportunity for developers and we could build 200,000 new houses a year on brownfield sites and solve much of this problem.

Yes, and I put them before the membership of our party. Most of them are still in place. I said that they were bound up with... I think you've dropped six or seven of them, actually. ..economic justice. No, that's not entirely true. But, look, I'm not going to shy away. Have I changed my position on those pledges? Yes, I have, because I've asked myself, Honestly, are these country first, party second? And if the answer to that question is no, then I've changed them, because I think this party should always put the country first. But the other thing I'd say, Beth, because I do think this is important. But do you think they're wrong in principle? Well, let's take... energy nationalisation, which a lot of people think we should nationalise our energy companies. They're making a huge amount of money, lots of people are paying a fortune on their bills. Two years ago, at the height of the crisis, when people were facing very high bills, I said to my team, well, mock up for me, tell me how much it would cost to nationalise the energy companies. That ran into tens of billions of pounds and I had to take a decision, which is, do we use tens of billions of pounds to pay off shareholders, to nationalise the company, but not have any money to reduce your bill? I thought that would have been the wrong decision. I think most people watching this here or on the television would say, for heaven's sake here, if it's a choice between keeping my bills low or paying off shareholders tens of billions of pounds, there's only one choice you can make. And that's what I mean by country first, party second. I changed my mind. It was too expensive. We need to get to the audience.

David Cameron, your chance. I just think it is disgraceful to try and frighten people in an election campaign, as Gordon Brown has just done, and as the Labour Party are doing up and down this country. And I'd like to take this opportunity to say very clearly to any pensioner in the audience, anyone listening at home, that we will keep the free television licence, we'll keep the pension credit, we'll keep the winter fuel allowance, we'll keep the free bus pass. Those leaflets you've been getting from Labour, those letters you've been getting from Labour, are pure and simple lies. A politician shouldn't say lies very often. I say it because I've seen those leaflets and they make me really very very angry. You should not be frightening people in an election campaign. It's just not right.

Daniel, in the short term, Help to Buy has helped about 350,000 families to bridge that gap in terms of deposit, because you probably can pay the monthly installments on the mortgage, but it's the actual capital that's needed to put down the down payment. In the longer term, I think to Arif's point, We pledge to build a million homes by 2020. We're running at approximately 189, 190,000 at the moment, so I think we'll hit that target by 2020. In the manifesto, we said we'll add another half a million by 2022, and what we want to do is work with councils and housing associations, so we build more council housing, and if people live in those houses for 15 years, they can actually have a right to buy those, because I think that's a good thing to do, to own your home. Tarek, to your point, about homelessness. There's a brilliant colleague of ours, I think we both, both parties support him, Bob Blackman. Shout out to Bob, he's a candidate this time around. He delivered the Homelessness Act, the bill that went through Parliament that had cross-party support and the government supported it. And the government, the Conservative Party in their manifesto, in our manifesto, we've pledged to cut homelessness by half in the next five years. And I hope we can deliver that on that pledge.

Thank you very much. This election is all about a choice. More of the chaos and division we've seen for the last 14 years or turning the page and rebuilding with Labour. I have ambition for our country and have a practical plan to deliver it. I've changed the Labour Party and put it back in the service of working people. Now I'm seeking your backing to change our country, to make it work once again for you and your family. Thank you. Rishi Sunak.

Thank you, Nicola Sturgeon. They are on. Brexit will cost people the length and breadth of the country. And the alternative to giving people the chance to escape it is just to continue with the chaos. I asked Rishi a question in the last debate we did. Can you guarantee that next year, if a trade deal hasn't been negotiated, that we won't have a no-deal Brexit? Will you take a no-deal Brexit off the table right now, yes or no?

When we talk about the special relationship, we're particularly talking about foreign policy, we're talking about defence, and indeed we're talking about security. And I would argue that the special relationship is more important now than ever, because NATO itself is now directly threatened by a European Union that wants to do defence on its own. You've only got to listen to what President Macron has said over the course of the last few weeks, what Mr Juncker's been saying, what Mr Verhofstadt's been saying. The European Defence Union effectively has been set up to replace NATO, and we play a key role in this. Boris Johnson needs to make a big decision in the next few days. Are we staying with the European Defence Union, or are we staying with NATO and America? And I hope we choose the latter.

I couldn't agree more with Caroline on that. We should not get into... the situation where we are choosing between supporting young people and supporting our pensioners. And coming back to this issue of university tuition, I agree university is not for everybody, and I agree that free tuition in and of itself is not enough to give people access to university, but it is essential. And I take a really simple view. I benefited from a free education as a working-class young person. That's why I can stand here today. I have no right to take that away from another young person.

No, but the fact is there were reductions in police budget because we had to make changes in order to live within our means after 2010. But the police responded incredibly well. Crime has fallen by a third between 2010 and 2015. And we've protected the police budget going forward. So government can make sure that we live within our means and protect the communities. But particularly on terrorism, if I may, we have to make sure we also have the right legislation. And I am shocked that Jeremy Corbyn just in 2011 boasted that he had opposed every piece of anti-terror legislation in his 30 years. in office. And I really think he must be held accountable for that, because I find it chilling.

You know, these are incredibly worrying times, aren't they? We see the axis between Mr Farage, Mr Trump and Mr Johnson. And that's the really frightening thing. It's not just our NHS that is a threat. It's of fundamental values. The President stands accused in the impeachment process of trying to use his political influence in Ukraine. What are we seeing even this week? The Prime Minister of this country, when he doesn't turn up to a channel for debate, saying he's going to revoke their licence. It's the Trumpian future that's going to happen to this country unless we call it out at this election. That's not the values that we want to see at the heart of our politics, but it's what's coming as a result of the special relationship, not between two countries, but between two men.

Well, Charlotte, you're quite right. And what nobody wants to talk about, and it's a great shame that David Cameron is not here, is that in the last five years, our national debt has doubled from £850 billion to £1.5 trillion. The interest repayments on that are already bigger than our current level of defence spending, and that's with interest rates at rock bottom on the floor. So you're right, it is not fair because we are burdening future generations. All I can say is, listening to the promises that I've heard from the people here tonight and the other two who went somewhere else this evening, is they are going to drive us into even greater debt, frankly bankruptcy, for your generation. UKIP is the only party to come up with a credible plan by cutting our foreign aid budget by cutting our EU contributions, by stopping white elephant projects like HS2, and by recalculating the Barnett formula so that less money goes over Hadrian's Wall to Scotland, we can save £32 billion a year. And if you ally that to tax cuts and growth, UKIP is the only party with a plan to get this debt down.

I think the truth is that there is good immigration and there's bad immigration. I was in a hospital, a paediatric hospital in Cardiff a few months ago, treating very sick prematurely born babies. I was being shown around and there were a large number of babies who needed to be treated. I went into a ward, it was standing completely empty. It had all the latest equipment, but it was standing completely empty. And I said to the ward sister, I said, what's going on? Why are there no babies here being treated? and she said no, new rules mean that we can't employ any doctors from outside the European Union with the skills needed. That's an example of where the rules are actually stopping good immigration, which actually help our public services to work properly. And that's where I want to see, not an arbitrary cap, I mean, you can't just say a cap, what is it, 10, 10,000, a million, what happens if you reach the cap in the middle of summer and someone wants to come and play football for Manchester United or Manchester City. Do you say they can't come? No. Let's have a regional approach where you only make sure that immigrants who come go to those regions where they can be supported.

Nigel Farage, this stranglehold of the big developers, do you agree with him? I don't disagree with that for one moment, which is why I want a Brownfield revolution. But Ed, if you have net migration into Britain running at 300,000 a year, that's 300,000 more people that need somewhere to live. And the point I'm making is that, I mean, our plans would be, with the Brownfield revolution, for 200,000 homes a year. But even that isn't enough unless we cap the demand side of this equation. OK, let me respond to that.

Jeremy, this manifesto reflects what would be a profound and very good change in our society. Because for the first time you'd have a government that was saying you cannot go on loading our students with debt. You cannot go on overcrowding our classrooms. You cannot go on underfunding our hospitals. You have to invest by all of us for the future of all of us. I don't want to live in a country of food banks. I don't want to live in a country of homeless people. I want to live in a country that actually does seriously care for all.

If we want to talk about pledges then, in a Scottish context I can provide you with a few that have been delivered. 100,000 children in Scotland are being lifted out of poverty thanks to what's called the Scottish Child Payment. It doesn't exist anywhere else on these isles, the SNP has put it into place. Our young people, of course, do not pay a single penny in tuition fees. We've got something which I know the audience in Scotland would love to have, which is a nationalised water company that delivers clean water.
I think she also dropped the F-bomb in that phrase.

But he's repeating the problem. He's repeating the problem. Who said unite behind her? She's one of his candidates. And his election pitch to you seems to be, my predecessor, a Tory Prime Minister, broke the country, did huge economic damage. Please vote Tory. Now, you're going to have to decide whether that's persuasive. He's now openly admitting the damage that the Tories have done to the economy and saying, please vote for him. That's the last thing we need in this country after the damage that's been done. My pitch is straightforward.

David Cameron. But why do you think it is, I would say, that 100 of the leading business people in this country, people who run some of the biggest businesses, like Corus, like Logica, like Mothercare, why do they say, and they couldn't be more clear, the risk to the economy is not cutting waste, the risk to the economy is Labour's proposal of a jobs tax?

Labour's policy is to renew Trident. I have to say, in addition, I find it really troubling in a general election campaign where politicians are asked, yes or no answer, would you press the button? And politicians smirk and say, yes, I'd press the button. This macho politics to get votes. I can't envisage what killing being prepared to kill millions of innocent people would do to protect or make safe or more safe the British people. Well, why have it then? And I think... Exactly. Why have it then? I think... I think that politicians should stop boasting about their willingness to kill millions of innocent people just because they think it'll get them a few extra votes. Well, why waste the money on Trident if you're not prepared to use it? If you're never going to use it, don't waste the money on Trident. Where's your bargain? Come on, make your mind up. My own views on the renewal of Trident are well known, the same as Jeremy Corbyn's, but the Labour Party's democratic incited policy is to keep Trident as a deterrent, not as something to boast about in election debates as being willing to press the button.

And, look, I have huge sympathy for Mr Merritt and the relatives, the families of both victims. And it was an absolutely terrible thing. But I still think it's wrong that somebody like Usman Khan, who was sentenced to 21 years or 16 years plus five on licence, should have been out automatically on eight years, after eight years, when the judge, when he was first convicted, made it clear that he was a very serious jihadi and a risk to public safety. And under our government, under my government, of course we won't want to be compromising people's human rights. Of course we won't, but we must put public safety first. It was the courts that quashed that, wasn't it? Automatic early release in such cases is, I think, completely unreasonable and we will get rid of it.

Well, Johnny, I think what we're offering is not cuts. We're offering the reversal of austerity, investing in your future and the future of everybody in this room. And what we've been doing in looking at the deficit is looking at this the wrong way round. We've been slashing away at essential public services. And let's really think about what austerity actually means. Probably somewhere near you, there's a children's centre or a library that's closed. Think of a worker in, say, that children's centre. She used to be providing an essential public service to your community. She used to be paying tax and national insurance. She had a modest amount of income to bring to spend in the community. Now that essential service is gone, She's on jobseekers allowance and everybody is much poorer. So what we're saying is we do need to raise taxes on those who aren't currently paying their share. Multinational companies in particular and rich individuals, if they pay their share in the world's sixth richest country, we can afford to have a decent society and accord to have decent public services.

but every school's got to be a good school, and we've got to insist on the highest standards for every school. Now, since 1997, 1,600 underperforming secondary schools in 1997, down to 250, next year down to zero, as a result of the fact that we're allowing federations, academies, to take over underperforming schools. Now, that's the way forward, to insist on the highest standards, to make sure an underperforming school is taken over, and to make sure that we spend enough and invest enough in the education system to ensure our children are properly taught. And what I would be very worried about is in this difficult and straitened time, we were to cut our budgets for education at this point in time. I think that would put our children at risk for the future and it's very important that we continue to invest in the education of every child in this country.

Our own leaders are not optimistic. They don't think this country is good enough to make its own laws. I want to see a self-governing, self-confident United Kingdom, a country in which we have pride. For young people, we are living in a global economy. We have to forget this obsession. with our, frankly, failing in many cases, next door neighbours in Europe and let's re-engage with a bigger, wider world and the best place to start would be the 2.2 billion people that live in the Commonwealth and are our real friends. Let's have a Britain that governs itself and looks outwards to the world.
You are now hitching your wagon to UKIP. You said you wouldn't trust Boris Johnson to drive you home.

The so-called economic plan was meant to eliminate the deficit by five years, but now we're told we can expect it to take two decades by the Tories. The UK's debt has increased by £555 billion since the Tories took office in 2010, and the UK is now the only advanced economy in which wages have declined despite the state getting richer. So the cuts that have taken place at the bottom are putting real pressure on people but we haven't seen those same cuts to wages at the top of the scale. So that inequality has to be addressed and one way to do that is through introducing a real living wage but we also have to have a proper job creation plan, a proper industrial strategy that enables people to have well-paid work. Because another scandal that's taking place at the moment is in-work poverty. And that rise that you see in the numbers of people going to food banks, so many of those people are in work, not just in work, but working for the public sector. Teachers, nurses, doctors, absolutely outrageous, and it shouldn't be the case in 2017. Thank you very much, Lenwood.

So I accept that the premise of what you're saying in respect of your area. I'm afraid I don't know it very well. But let me say a few home truths, particularly to some members of this panel. Migration is absolutely essential to our public services, it's absolutely essential to our businesses, and it's absolutely essential to our economic growth. And what we need to do is end the demonization of migration. In Scotland, we have a declining working age population, despite a net number of people moving from the rest of the UK to Scotland. We need migrants. And there's a race to the bottom on migration, driven by Nigel Farage. followed by the Conservative Party and hotly chased by the Labour Party does not serve Scotland's interests. And it does not serve your interests either. So rise up against it. Do as Keir Starmer did in 2020 and talk about the benefits of migration. It's important to our fundamental aspects of life.

I mean it's astonishing. How much energy is it going to produce? It's astonishing because the Tories have been in power now 14 years. How much energy is Great British Energy going to produce? And we haven't had an industrial strategy or a manufacturing sector in the UK that is about creating. You just said it's going to produce. We're just listening to you Penny. I think it's pretty important.

I'm interested in doing what's best for Britain. I'm interested in doing the right thing for the people of this country. I believe that we have to bring immigration under control, that we have to limit the circumstances in which people apply for asylum in this country, and if that means that we have to withdraw
I think that the UK has a long tradition of welcoming people who are fleeing torture or persecution or poverty and that's a tradition we're proud of and it needs to be done in an international way.

I think you say there's no such thing as a hard Brexit. Wales has 200,000 jobs connected to tariff-free trading with the single market and we are net beneficiaries from the EU because in Wales we have some of the poorest areas in Europe when we're looking at GDP. So a good deal with the European Union is absolutely crucial for the people of Wales and what Plaid Cymru want is a mandate from the people of Wales to go to Westminster to put Wales on the political agenda and make sure that our voice is heard in those negotiations. OK, let's get a few more closing thoughts on Brexit from you guys.

I'd like to see what the deal is that we get. But if we get a deal that protects our economy and jobs then I would vote for Brexit because that's what people wanted and I represent my constituency. But it has to be a deal that doesn't put jobs and the economy at risk in my constituency. That is my red line.

Well, our plan is for them to serve the full mandatory term of sentences. The reason they couldn't be given indefinite terms before was because of the Human Convention on Human Rights, which overruled a UK ruling, and that is something that we've said. If we were elected with a majority government, we will re-examine human rights law to make sure the balance between security and human rights is properly matched in favour of our safety.

Well, Sue, I completely share your anger. I was as frustrated as you are furious when I learnt about these allegations. That's why, alongside the Gambling Commission, we initiated our own internal inquiries into what had happened and, as a result of that, have suspended two candidates. And I've been crystal clear. Anyone who has broken the law, broken the rules, should not only face the full consequences of the law, I will ensure that they're booted out of the Conservative Party.
