Well, tonight you've heard it from the mouths of the other party leaders, or at least the ones who bothered to turn up, that they simply don't believe in Brexit. They don't believe we're big enough, We're strong enough and simply they don't believe we're good enough to be a free, democratic and independent state on the world stage. Unlike them, I do. I believe that this is a fantastic country. So if, like me, you believe immigration needs to be reduced, you believe that foreign aid needs to be cut, you believe that our National Health Service needs more investment and that our armed forces need to be bolstered, then you know what to do. If you also believe we need more Bobbies on the beat, and that our elderly need to be looked after, then please go out and vote UKIP on June 8th, because together we really can put the great back into Britain.

I visited a manufacturer today who is involved in selling to the rest of the world, including to China and Asia, with the most advanced precision manufacturing. And I believe over the next few years, we can create 400,000 jobs in low-carbon industries. I believe we can create half a million jobs in the digital industries of the future. I believe biotechnology, and this region is very good at biotechnology as well as well as advanced manufacturing, can see 100,000 jobs, but we as a government are investing with these companies in the equipment that they need for the future. So I'm optimistic about the future of the British economy and optimistic about this region. What I would insist on, however, is that the banks do lend, and that's why we've appointed an arbitrator. Where people are dissatisfied with what the banks decide, they can go to them and we will back them up. Secondly, we will continue to give investment allowances which would be abolished by the Conservatives. And thirdly, we will maintain the regional development agencies which again the Conservatives want to remove. It's very important we back regional manufacturing in this great centre of manufacturing industry for our country.

What I'd say, the biggest promise we made about the NHS at a time when we were going to have to make difficult cuts in public spending, as anyone, Prime Minister, when I was Prime Minister, would have to do, we said, we will not cut the NHS. And we haven't. We've increased spending on the NHS by over £12.7 billion over the last five years. And because this is a really important point, can I ask quickly, What we did in terms of changing the NHS is we got rid of 20,000 bureaucrats in the NHS and we put that money into 9,000 more doctors and 7,000 more nurses and as a result we're treating more patients. If you take something just like cancer, We're seeing 460,000 more people and looking at their potential cancer than we were five years ago. So the biggest promise we made, more money, safeguarding that money and treating more patients, has been kept. And if you elect me again as your Prime Minister with a strong economy, because that's the key to afford this, we'll go on investing in our National Health Service.

Well, we actually engage in a lot of dialogue with many Southern African countries, for example, about the loss and damage activity, where we became one of the first funders of a loss and damage program. I had a meeting yesterday with the president of Zambia, who was in Edinburgh. And we were discussing the developments of the Scottish-Zambia joint exchange program, which is heavily focused on improving energy sustainability in Zambia. It is part of the lost damage work that we are undertaking and also part of the international partnership work that we take forward. I accept we have a domestic responsibility for tackling climate change and net zero, but I also accept that we have a responsibility to reach to other countries and to work with them and to support them, given the fact that as a developed Western European country, we've created our fair share of the damage to the planet and we've got to address that in the loss and damage programmes with others. Christina Rivas Graver, you've got a question.

Because I've spoken to lots of people in the armed forces, and they themselves have been working on schemes to make it easier for young people to engage with the armed forces. That's something that they themselves have been working on. Those are the conversations I've been having with them for many months. And it's really important for people to know the military option is optional. Right? No-one's going to be forced to do it. It's actually going to be very selective, competitive. Well, the whole thing's going to be optional, isn't it? National service will be compulsory. The military option will be something that people choose to do.

I watched bits of it whilst I remained awake. I mean, it was pretty dull, wasn't it? Two forms of socialism. No one answered any questions. Neither of them... Who won? Neither of them, because neither of them had a plan for growth. We've got the longest recession per person since records began 70 years ago. Neither of them talked about growth. Neither of them talked about the impact of mass immigration making people poorer and reducing the quality of our life on housing, on health,

On the subject of the right of EU nationals who've made their lives here in good faith to stay here, I just think it is unbelievably cruel to be using those lives as bargaining chips in these negotiations. The government right now could be saying, those people should be able to stay right now. But I want to be able to make the case proudly for Free movement. I think free movement has been the most wonderful gift, the ability to travel and work and live and love in 27 other member states, and for them to come here. And I have to say, I am sorry that the Labour Party now doesn't support that, because I think our country is enriched by people coming from other countries. I think that the Britain that I love is a confident, outward-facing country, recognising that migration makes a massive, positive contribution. And I think right now we have people like Paul and his hate-filled rhetoric. will make people feel, that makes people feel as if the reason that they can't get a leg on the housing ladder or the reason they can't see a GP is because of migration. It is not because of migration, it is because successive governments haven't invested enough in our public services. If you are trying to see someone in the NHS, you are more likely to find a migrant actually treating you as a doctor than a head of you in the queue. Jeremy Corbyn, could you respond to the point on giving up on freedom of movement?

Nigel Farage? Well, at admission of the truth, that's fine. I tell you, very interestingly, Anne, this actually worked rather well. The free movement of peoples, when we were in with countries like France, Germany, the Netherlands, roughly similar living standards, roughly similar education and health systems, it didn't really pose any problem. The problem was, irresponsibly, stupidly, we let in ten former communist countries, where the minimum wage, say, in Romania, is about a tenth of what it is here. And if you say to people in poor countries they can move to relatively rich countries, they do. The Labour government, and I think Ed's admitted this, got it horrendously wrong on the figures. But we now face the potential of a collapse in the Eurozone, and we have no control over the numbers coming to Britain.

Well, we have tried to do something about respite care because there are six million carers in this country. I've met many of them and talked to them about their needs. One need is respite so that they can have a break, as Nick said, and we're introducing and have actually made provision for more measures for respite care. But the question I was asking about also how he could be sure that he could be less worried about having to go into an old people's home or what would happen if he got ill later on in his life. But that's why the urgent needs measure that we're introducing from April next year is so important. It means that you can stay in your own home, have the help that's available, the equipment but also the home helps and the health visitors and at the moment it has been means tested, now in future it will be free of charge.

Absolutely not. The figures on health tourism and, indeed, benefit tourism that Nigel Farage is often citing, they do not reflect the reality. The situation is that people come here as immigrants to work or they come here to seek asylum as refugees. And we've had a really dangerous... damaging debate about immigration, fuelled... You know where it's been fuelled from, but sadly followed by the others. You know, we need to look at the fact that our NHS is hugely dependent on foreign-born workers. It couldn't operate without them.

Well, Harry, I don't think the choice at the last election was a good choice. I accept that... But you backed Jeremy Corbyn. I didn't. You said his manifesto embodied decency and hope. Harry, I didn't think we would win. I don't think we're in a position to win. But what's happened with Jeremy Corbyn Look at my leadership on this. I picked up the Labour Party. I recognised many people had not voted for us last time, possibly people in this room, and I said we're never going to put that proposition in front of the electorate again. I'm not going to tell the electorate what you think you're doing. I'm going to say to my party, we need to shake this up. and change it. So I've changed the Labour Party. So there's a fundamentally different choice at this election. And Jeremy Corbyn not only lost the whip as Labour MP, he's now been expelled from the Labour Party. No Labour leader has ever done anything as serious as that about changing his or her party before. And so I'm really proud of the fact that I'll be judged on the last four and a half years. I've picked this party up. I've changed it. And now I hope that there is a Labour Party which people feel that they can vote for this time in a way which I accept. wasn't the case last time round. And I don't look at the electorate and say, you were wrong in 2019. I look at my past and say, we were wrong, that's why we've changed.
...to dictate from the centre.

Ed Miliband is scaremongering, and he said in an interview that he wanted to weaponise the NHS. Now, for most of us, the NHS is a service for our families, not a political weapon. And I think, let's be clear about social care. We are starting, it started on the 1st of April, a new fund, the Better Care Fund, with £5.3 billion in, bringing health and social care together so we can unblock those hospital beds, make sure that people get the care they need in the community. Nick was talking about the inheritance we had. There was something else we inherited in the NHS, which was a culture, too often from Labour, of cover-up. Just 60 miles from where we are is the Mid Staffords Hospital, and we all remember what we uncovered with that public inquiry, that elderly people were being left uncared for, some of them drinking out of water vases because they weren't being looked after because the target culture had run rampant. We've changed that. and we've made sure there are more nurses on wards, more doctors in charge and standards of care are going up in our NHS.

Mr Cameron. On that point, let me take Nick back to his manifesto and one pledge that's in there that worried me a lot. My mother was a magistrate in Newbury for 30 years. She sat on the bench and she did use those short prison sentences that you're talking about. And I've got to tell you, when someone smashes up the bus stop, when someone repeatedly breaks the law, when someone's found fighting on a Friday or Saturday night, as a magistrate, you've got to have that power for a short prison sentence when you've tried all the other remedies. And the idea that you never send someone to prison... Brown on that point, please.

No I don't. I think one of the stories if you like, of this parliament, that has just come to an end, and this election campaign now underway, is the extent to which people have seen and are seeing the Liberal Democrats as actually being most in tune with public priorities, and those public priorities have ranged in the international level and the opposition in the war to Iraq, to the domestic level, the priories that we're putting in front of people.
The best way to deal with that is to make sure that we don't have another terrorist attack. And that means we need to deal with what causes these things to happen.

Well, let's allow Mr. Corbyn to respond to that. Thank you, Mr. Johnson. That's what I've said, and it's very clear, very clear. Three months to negotiate, six months for a referendum, and that will bring that process to an end. What we know of the government's proposals, what we know of what Mr. Johnson has done is a series of secret meetings with the United States in which they were proposing to open up our NHS markets, as they call them, to American companies. Freedom of Information Act request was made in order to find out what happened at these meetings. That's what happened at these meetings. Every single line of this document redacted out. A document here, a document here of US-UK negotiations. Summary of specific negotiating objectives. Full market access for US products to our National Health Service. You're going to sell our National Health Service out to the United States and Big Pharma.

In Scotland, of course, we have abolished the right to buy, a controversial thing to do at the time, but it helps to ensure that as we invest in building social housing, we're not then losing it later on, and we help people into home ownership in other ways. There's no doubt house building is key to getting the cost of housing down, and we need to build homes that are affordable for people. I was listening to Jeremy Corbyn talk at its manifesto launch the other day with lots of policies that the SNP is already implementing in Scotland, but one of them was restoring council house building. We've done that as well. Now, we've got challenges. I'm not saying we've got everything right, but you have to be prepared to build the houses and you have to be prepared to protect them for the use of the public in the future.
But having resolved all of the industrial action except the junior doctors. Except the junior doctors, what you're now seeing is that the numbers are coming down. And we are making progress.

Well, I don't accept that. Because if you look at what's happened with our economy, there are 1.89 million more people in work than when I became prime minister. We've got 900,000 fewer people on out-of-work benefits. Is there an increase in food banks? a mark of success? Look, obviously there has been an increase in food bank use. That's partly because of the difficulties we faced as a country. It's also, Jeremy, because we changed the rules. The previous government didn't allow job centres to advertise the existence of food banks. They thought it would be bad PR. I thought that was a wrong decision. I thought it was a poor decision. So we allowed them to point people towards food banks if they needed them. But look, the big picture is here, is we want to get more people back to work. We've turned the economy around, and it's jobs that are the best route out of poverty.

It isn't a small problem about people having to sell their homes to pay for care. I believe it is 45,000 families every year who have to do that. As I say, I think there is a deep unfairness in the system. All of us, when we get older, we want to spend as long at home as possible before going into residential care. anything we can do to help people adapt to their homes, to help people live in their homes, and also to help the carers. If carers stopped caring in Britain, whether for disabled children or whether for elderly people, if they packed up and gave up, that would cost us £50 billion, as well as all the hurt and the pain it would cause. So giving carers some clearer rights and saying if you care for someone, you should get a break. The thing that every carer always says to me, more than anything else, give me a break every now and again and I can go on doing what I do. And I hope as we try and seek some consensus, let's put the carer absolutely up front and centre. They are Britain's unsung heroes.
But you're not telling us a million people would lose child tax credits under both of your proposals.

The relationship with America is important, of course it is, but when it comes to Donald Trump, we should sup with a very, very long spoon. His rhetoric and many of his policies are deeply dangerous. And the last thing the United Kingdom needs is a Prime Minister who's trying to be like Donald Trump. We certainly shouldn't let him anywhere near our National Health Service, and I think it is at risk in a future trade deal, the Tories, and Nigel Farage will say that's not true. Well, if that's not true, let us put it beyond doubt. Let's have an NHS Protection Act that makes sure our National Health Service can never, ever be on the table in a future trade deal, because Donald Trump and Boris Johnson, frankly, cannot be trusted with it. Thank you.

So can I draw also the austerity point together because the two are for me two sides of the same coin When I was born 40 something years ago 48 wasn't it? Well, thank you very much You're younger than me, what's your secret? We're told that we're going to create all this wealth, have this technological breakthrough, we're going to get off our dependence on oil. Well, look, the economy is now three times as big. We've got three times as much wealth. We've had the technological revolution. We know how to harness the power of the sun, the wind, the tides. But instead of getting that kind of utopian vision of working fewer hours, spending more time with our family, having a cleaner air to breathe. We've seen rampant inequality. We've seen climate destruction and breakdown. And now we're on the verge of a mental health epidemic, stressed out, working longer hours than ever before. We can solve the climate emergency and not just stop austerity, but reverse austerity. if we are willing to make the right choices. And as far as I can see, we're the only ones that are saying we can have a hopeful vision where we can cut people's bills by super insulating their homes. We can get people out of their cars by cutting public transport.

I want an economic plan that gets the deficit down but protects the vulnerable. as I say, I think we should reprioritise government spending. We should put the British people first. And we should worry a bit less about propping up foreign regimes through a wasted £10 billion a year on foreign aid, which all of them agree to, for some reason that's completely beyond me. And really, frankly, costing £10 billion a year net to be a member of the European Union for no trade advantage whatsoever. These are massive savings we could make, and we could actually... You know, some of the concerns about social spending and everything else, that's not where the cuts need to come. Let's stop giving foreign money away.

It's a very, very important question. I'm an internationalist too. I chair the Welsh Parliament's branch of the Commonwealth Parliamentary Association, working with parliamentarians across the world. I think the important thing for me is that leaders at a UK level should recognise that there is an impact to decisions made in terms of foreign policy. And I would also make this point, Krishnan, that historically there's a reason why many people are choosing to come to the UK because of the English language connection and the fact that those years of empire created those connections. So those decisions made in terms of foreign policy many years ago are having an impact today. Carla.

Everyone knows that they can't trust the Conservatives or reform when it comes to climate, and that's part of the reason why the Conservatives are on their way out of the general election. Hang on. Let her answer. But what people have been saying to me on the doorsteps in my home city of Bristol and elsewhere is that they're sorely disappointed in the Labour Party, too, who've rolled back on their previously flagship climate investment pledge, one of the few good policies they had left. when it's totally clear that we need to tackle the climate crisis and that doing so brings so many other benefits. Insulating our homes, a nationwide home insulation programme will bring down our bills, give us warmer, more comfortable homes.

The reason that we supported the principle in London and we argued it, and supported Ken Livingstone in this, at a time when Tony Blai was not, remember, was because, as he recognised, and I think he was quite brave about doing it, providing you put in the additional resource for public transport, which he did in terms of buses, people will make more use of that alternative. Now, that's the right way to go about things.

With the greatest of respect Emma, it's not for you or anybody in Westminster or indeed not from Scotland to decide, it's for the Scottish people to decide. You've lost your material difference. No actually, the Brexit issue is just symptomatic of a broken Westminster system. We've had 10 years of Conservative stroke, Lib Dem austerity, we did not vote for. A bedroom tax, a benefits cap, a rape clause, all of which Scotland did not vote for. Are you surprised, any of you, looking at the Westminster system over the last few years, is it any surprise that Scotland looks at that shambles and goes, you know what, thanks, but no thanks.

We focus on action and solutions, not titles and nonsense like that. All the media are obsessed about titles. We focus on actually, how do you solve things? How do you make things happen? Because that's who we are. Business people who fix things.

Sure, I was. And what I said was that when, first of all, when we have our point system to decide who comes into the country with the skills that we need, we would give extra points for people who want to go to Scotland if their skills are needed in Scotland. The problem Scotland faces is that a lot of their young people are leaving Scotland. with unlimited immigration, uncontrolled immigration. The reason they are leaving Scotland is because the Scottish economy is doing much less well than the economy of the rest of the country, the rest of the United Kingdom. David, please let me answer the first question the gentleman posed because it is a very important one. We have never said that the only people we'd let in would be people with the skills that are needed in this country. That's not the policy. There would be three groups of people we'd let in. The first are those people whose skills we need, the second are those people who want to come into the country for family reunion, and the third are refugees, genuine refugees, We'd have more genuine refugees come into the country than come in now. The problem now is that we have a very unfair, inhumane system which rewards those people who pay the people smugglers who traffic people across the world. And I want to put an end to that.

No, what he's saying is exactly the same as me. There's no point in setting an - the Tories say, you set a quota and what they say is that parliament will set the quota. Supposing you get a major company that says, I mean here we are, company of law offices here, which is actually connected with a, an American company, supposing they need someone to come in from abroad for a short time. Why, why shouldn't you have them in. The point is to make sure that you have strict controls that mean the only people your economy needs to come in to this country
I was talking about Obama, and you're so Muslim about it.

Angela Rayner? Well, Nick, thanks for the question. And I think our young people don't feel safe either at the moment, never mind us as parents. And I say that, I've got 15 and 16-year-old sons at home. And I think it's really sad when I go around my schools and I talk to young people, they're frightened of what they think is crime on their streets and young people who are worried about knife crime generally tend to carry a knife to protect themselves but are more likely to actually be involved in knife crime because of that. We've got to educate our young people and that means that we've got to have those neighbourhood police. We've had them caught. We said we'd put 13,000 more neighbourhood police on the street. Penny talked about 20,000 police officers They cut 22,000. That's a minus 2,000 in my mathematics. So they haven't protected people.

Can I come back to your point? What we're proposing is £10 an hour by 2020. That would be a living wage. That would reduce our Department of Work and Pensions bill somewhat because you'd be reducing in-work benefit payments because people are being paid more. There are many big companies that could well afford to pay it and shouldn't be just paying the minimum wage. There are companies like, I suspect, yours, small companies that would have problems. We fully recognize that. We would therefore work with them, either to give them tax relief or support, in order to make sure the living wage, the real living wage, was paid, but they didn't close down as a result of it. Because as a society, we have six million people earning less than the living wage. We have a million people on zero-hours contracts. We have wage levels, the proportion of national income actually falling at the present time. I think more money in the pockets will mean people are better off, but it will also be an economic boost itself. I absolutely recognise the problems for small and micro businesses. That's why there would be a pathway to it for them with support from the government to achieve it.
And he could have been. You think that was a tragedy? I think the whole Afghan experience is a tragedy and I think he should have been put on trial.

Can I just make a point on the council houses, because it makes absolutely no sense to continue to build council houses, which I agree that you should do, and then sell them off, as Margaret Thatcher did under the Right to Buy scheme. That has left us with a lack of social housing, and that is one of the reasons that we see high levels of homelessness today. So if we are going to build new council houses, we have to stop the Right to Buy.

Nobody should suffer abuse, and many women Labour MPs, Ruth Smith included and others, have suffered the most unbelievable levels of abuse. And it was a Labour MP, Jo Cox, who was murdered because she stood up in public life. I simply say to you that bad behaviour, misogynism, racism in any form, is absolutely not acceptable in any form whatsoever in my party or in our society.

Well, I think like everybody here, it's very distressing to see an increasing number of people moving to food banks and that's why every day that I have been in government over the last five years as we've had to clear up this unholy mess that we inherited, this massive black hole in our public finances, a broken banking system, the biggest heart attack in our economy in a generation. I've always tried to take decisions where we spread the burden as fairly as possible. So, for instance, have resisted, time and time again, much, much deeper cuts to benefits, to the help given to the most vulnerable, those who fall on hard times, as advocated by Conservatives in government. When people get back into work, particularly those on low pay, I'd be very anxious to make sure they keep more of the money they earn. So when I came into government, you, everybody here, all of us, would start paying income tax the moment you earned £6,400. on the front page of your well-thumbed copy of the 2010 Liberal Democrat manifesto, you would have seen an absolute leading commitment, which we did stick to, which was that we would raise the point at which you pay income tax. So you pay no income tax on the first £10,600 you earn. That has actually meant that 3 million people, over 3 million people, on low pay pay no income tax for the first time ever. The final thing I would say is about the benefit sanctions which you mentioned. I do, I have become persuaded, listening to the Trussell Trust and others who have provided evidence about the reasons people are using food banks, I have become persuaded that we need a, in effect, a kind of a yellow card system, that some of the sanctions that are applied to people who don't meet the conditions of their benefits shouldn't be imposed quite as harshly and automatically as they are, and that is a change that I would want to introduce in the next Parliament.

My mother used to say, when I was growing up, that what was available for a mother and for a parent was maternity services when the child was born, then you were called for vaccination for these different vaccinations, and then you were told to come to primary school at the age of five. Now we've got nursery education, three to four year-olds, now we've got SureStart children's centres, and there's 3500 now round the country, and now we've got maternity pay and paternity pay, and Child Tax Credits, and we are moving forward by making the Child Tax Credit even higher for the parents of under three-year-olds. That's the sort of way that we can help give chances. I'm interested in social mobility. You have to help children under five develop their potential, you've got to have personal tuition at schools if people fall behind. You've got to encourage young people to stay on at school and get qualifications. This is the way that we can have a new generation of middle-class jobs in this country, where young people from poor backgrounds can get the opportunities they've never had before. That's what the social mobility that I want to promote is all about.

Christian, well, Simon, thank you for your question, because the father of a daughter myself, the story you told about what happened at your daughter's school is as concerning to me as it is to you. Nobody wants their daughter or their son or any of their relations exposed to violence of that kind. Now, what I can say by way of reassurance is that since 2010, violent crime, according to the Office for National Statistics, has come down by 44%, which is significant progress. But we do need to go further. Now, in relation to knife crime, The incident you mentioned involved a 14-year-old perpetrator, I think you said. Now, I remember attending a few months ago the tragic funeral of Eliane and Amin Croydon, a 15-year-old girl murdered by a young alleged perpetrator. So what I'd like to see is more early intervention to get those youngsters onto the right track. We're investing an extra... We're going to invest next year £75 million in doing that. I'm working to identify young people in local authority areas most at risk of getting onto the wrong track, so we can intervene early with mentoring and other interventions to stop people that young committing offences in the first place. But that is only part of it. We need tougher enforcement as well. I believe in stop and search as a way of taking knives off the street. Labour's Mayor Sadiq Khan in London unfortunately doesn't, which leads I think to London streets being less safe. We also need to invest in hotspot policing. We've now got hotspot patrols. They launched just a few weeks ago across England and Wales. Every single police force intensive patrols in areas where antisocial behavior and serious violence is a problem, including many right here in Essex, to make sure the police are on the spot when antisocial behavior or other incidents may occur. So while it's good that we've got violent crime down by 44% during this government's term of office, We need to go further. Those measures combined with record ever police numbers and the 8,000 extra officers we're going to hire, I hope and I expect, will make sure that fathers like you and me and mothers and fathers across the country can be confident that their children are going to be safe.
Well, neither of those things are privatisation. These are  BOTH TOGETHER
I'm very clear. We would ban fracking.
Parliament voted against getting it done to a sensible timeframe.

I was just trying to understand. I'm not going to apologize for having had a career before I went into politics. And it's a funny question from you, Emma, if I may, because I'm certainly not going to apologize for having a successful wife who has a career, earns money and enables us to buy nice houses. So that's... But the thing in politics is, what do you actually do when you're there? And I've made it my personal mission as Housing Secretary to help more young people and people on lower incomes onto the housing ladder. We're doing a number of things in our manifesto. One is to say that we're going to offer a 30% discount to local first-time buyers to get a property in your own area. Another is to say that we're going to help you with your deposit, which is the biggest challenge for most young people, and we're going to say that we're going to create a market for 95% mortgages, so you need a relatively small deposit, and then we're going to have fixed low interest rates for the lifetime of that mortgage. Through serious, good interventions like that, I genuinely believe we can make owning a home and aspiration that you can actually achieve in life and that's what the Conservative government will be trying to do if we're re-elected.

Thank you. Labour is ambitious for our country, and ambitious for you. I want to build a society where there is real opportunity for all, where everyone feels supported and secure, and where everybody has dignity in old age. There are now four million children living in poverty in our country. People can't get appointments to see their GP. Elderly people can't get the care they need. And millions of families are struggling to pay bills and make ends meet. They need an ambitious government on their side that will put money in their pockets, save our NHS, rebuild our public services, deliver free personal care for older people and free education for all. Yes, that does mean asking those with the broadest shoulders to pay a bit more in tax. A vote for Labour is a vote for hope, for real change and for a country that works for the many, not just the few. Mr Corbyn, thank you.

Thank you. Rhian ap Iorwes. I'm sure there'll be people who were thinking, why is this guy from Wales being invited on to a debate? It's being broadcast across the whole of the UK. Well, firstly, I hope some of what I have said has resonated with you, wherever you are, and that you agree we need to do politics in a different way. But the fact that some do think that reflects what Plaid Cymru is all about. We are here to offer a positive vision of what Wales could be, knowing that time and time again, our aspirations go unheard by Westminster governments, be that Labour or Conservatives. Now, the truth is, throughout the UK, There are communities who feel ignored and overlooked, and I hope you stand up for your community. And for me, it'll always be about standing up for Wales. Now, it's high time that the Conservatives were kicked out of power, but Labour will have to be held to account too. And Welsh voters can do that through Plaid Cymru MPs and their vision for fairness and ambition for Wales. Vote Plaid Cymru on July 6.

Sir, with the greatest respect, we do not need a foreign court to tell us how to police our borders and our security. And I believe that everything we're doing is in compliance with our international obligations. But if I'm put in a position where I'm forced to choose between those things, I'm going to put our country's national security first every single time. And I make absolutely no apology about that.
Just let me finish the example if I may. Contrast and compare with London where we supported the principle of congestion charging.

I'd say two things about what David Cameron has achieved as Prime Minister, which I admire. The first is his commitment to equal marriage. It was hard in his party. It's the right thing to do for the country. And second, and this isn't going to be universally popular, his commitment on overseas development, a 0.7%. And now, there'll be some people who don't like me saying that, but he took a risk in the Conservative Party by saying this is the right thing to do, and he's kept to that, and I think it's right that he did.

Well, my life... It's beyond reproach, is it? Nobody's life is beyond reproach. I'm always the last one to turn the heating on. In fact, we turned it on for the first time last week in my house, and that was very low temperature anyway, and I turned it off pretty quickly after that. I'm quite miserable, actually, on this basis, because I don't like to see the waste of energy that goes with it. And also, it is about... Everybody can grow something. If they've got a garden or a window box or whatever, You can grow appropriate plants which do improve biodiversity. So you can plant lavender or something which attracts bees and so on. So I do all of that in my garden and on my allotment and in my life.

Education today is the economy of tomorrow and skills, high levels of skills are vital to our economic performance. Plaid Cymru will ensure that our children are taught by the best teachers in well-funded schools so that they have the skills that they need to flourish. We will oppose any attempts to reintroduce grammar schools and we will develop a modern and well-funded system of comprehensive education. We'll invest in our teachers, supporting them with a premium in exchange for improving their skills. And we also have to value youth work and all of those things that happen outside of the school to support children's education. Applied Cymru will give every child a chance to reach its full potential and we will give everybody an opportunity for a life of education so that they can get to the best place that they can possibly be.
Thank you so much. You see, you won't apologize, will you? Amidst your vile and prejudice, you've made the point. Vile and prejudice. You've made the point.

Well there's a huge amount at stake in this election and I'm proud that we've worked with Plaid Cymru and with the Green Party to try to maximise the number of Remain MPs elected in this new Parliament. But I would love to have a system where that wasn't necessary, where catching your vote would count no matter where you were. It's important for people's votes to count. It's important because the very concept that there is a safe seat, that any politician does not get held to account, that will be voted in time after time, no matter what they do. That is fundamentally a broken part of our politics. And when you look at what is happening right now, it sometimes feels like a slightly geeky thing to be interested in electoral reform, but actually, it has a massive impact on the politics we get.

So he might as well say goodbye, goodbye to the coalition. You should have taken the opportunity when David Cameron was here to ask him about the Conservative position on Europe. I can only tell you about...

I believe in academic selection. Always have done. I believe that we don't have enough grammar schools in this country. We have 164 at the moment. We need more. But the problem that you have is that the majority of these grammar schools are in middle class areas. We need to get them into working class areas which then allow working class kids to break out of their communities. It is a disgrace. that there were more working-class kids in Oxford and Cambridge in the 1960s than there is now. However, what we also must do is promote vocational education, which has often been the poor relative of academia. And I would look at the German system for this, because they have something called the dual vocational training system, whereby you attend a vocational school, but at the same time you receive on-the-job training. It's why Germany has kept its manufacturing industry.

Well if they've got good quality broadband obviously that's a start and clearly there has to be full accessibility to public services online because there is, you're quite correct, a digital divide where public services increasingly, doctors appointments and everything else are done online and there are a significant number of people in the country that don't necessarily have smartphones, probably don't have computers and can't access those things and that becomes a form of social exclusion. So I would always make it a requirement that public services must be available also in the more traditional ways as well so that nobody is left out because there's nothing worse than people that are told, for example, to go online to sort out their universal credit, can't get online because they haven't got a computer or a smartphone. How on earth are they supposed to access universal credit in that sense? That's why we will end universal credit and bring back a serious system.

David Cameron has been talking about parties being too close to the city. The blunt truth is that both Conservative and Labour Governments now for ages have been far too close to the City, basically preferring the interests of the one Square Mile of the City of London, rather than the 100,000 square miles of the whole of the United Kingdom. What I think we now need to do is look fundamentally at what went wrong in the way in which banks were working. And my view is this, that as long as you have banks which mix up high-risk, free-wheeling casino investment banking on the one hand, and the sort of conservative, sober retail high street banking which we all depend on, then you're asking for trouble. That's why, as the Governor of the Bank of England says as well, many people are increasingly saying, we should split up the banks between investment banking on the one hand and high street banking on another. In order to ensure we that never, ever, ever again have the banks hold a gun to the head of the rest of the British economy. And still, still we haven't done enough to protect ourselves of that risk in the future.
I think, to the best of my knowledge, we have 5,000 more doctors. I appreciate it's not the same as GPs. We have 5,000 more doctors this year than last year. We are making progress.

Well firstly I will defend free university tuition. I got the chance to go to university because education was free. I don't think I've got the right to take that away from anybody and we've got more young people now in Scotland going to university than ever before including more young people from our most deprived communities. I stood for the Scottish Parliament election last year, seeking to be First Minister, and said that over the course of the Scottish Parliament, my priority was to raise standards in our schools and close the attainment gap. We are working to do that through a range of different reforms and additional investment direct in our schools to headteachers to allow them to deploy the additional staff or whatever other resources they think is necessary.

I just want to point out that Angela sat there and told us the most amazing amount of fudge, because the gentleman at the back said, look, what is the difference between Customs Union, Single Market and Remain? Well, a key difference is that we would be having the Single Market regulations, but we would lose our voice on them. The basic bottom line is there isn't a Brexit deal that protects jobs, that is good for our economy. And the people that are trying to say that that is the case are trying to peddle something that is simply not true.

The important thing is making sure we've got a trade deal that works for the UK and for Europe. We cooperate and make sure that we deliver defence and security for Europe. And we have 140,000 workers, for example, in our NHS who are European. We've got to secure them and Brits living in Europe.

Firstly, on the point of EU membership, Scotland, whether we're in or out of the EU, wants to get its deficit down to sustainable levels. Most countries across the Western world have deficits and we've set out plans to reduce that. It is reducing already. It's not a condition of joining the EU to meet those deficit levels. I want to see more investment in Scotland from whoever is in government in Westminster, which is why I've set out the policies that the SNP would want to see pursued. But our experience of Westminster governments over a long time is that that investment doesn't always come. It's always also the case that if you want in Scotland to deprive Boris Johnson of a majority, then the way to do that is to vote SNP, because we're the main challengers in every Conservative seat. On the issue of our grandparents, my grandmother was from the north of England. I've got many family members in England. I believe in independence so that Scotland can be self-governing. But the closest ties of family, of history, social ties between Scotland, England, Wales, Northern Ireland, will always be close. And I don't believe independence for Scotland is turning our backs on that. It's simply making sure that Scotland is in those relationships on the basis of equality. And I think that's a good and positive thing for Scotland. And actually, I think it would have benefits for the rest of the UK as well. Let's take a question now from Stephen Whitelaw.

Well, Mr Wilcox, I was in Parliament just a couple of years ago when the awful incident happened, and I saw firsthand the bravery and ultimately the sacrifice of your colleagues that day. So I pay enormous tribute and thanks to the work that you and your colleagues do to keep us safe. And as we reflect on today's events, of course, our thoughts are with all of those who are affected by these awful events. It's a tragedy and our prayers are with them. Now, the first duty of every government is to keep us safe. And, of course, a Conservative government will continue to do that, investing in our police, giving them the resources and the powers that they need to do that job. And you can rest assured on that. But today we saw something else. We saw ordinary members of the public, not the government, ordinary members of the public showing incredible bravery to help intervene and protect their fellow citizens. They represent absolutely the best of our values. Rebecca Long-Bailey. LONG-BAILEY: Well, I think today's event was horrific, and it will alter the lives of people involved forever, not to mention the families who were affected by the two people who were tragically killed. LONG-BAILEY: And our hearts and our prayers are with them all. LONG-BAILEY: And of course, as I've already paid tribute to the amazing police for their response and those passersby who intervened. LONG-BAILEY: But this was an extreme event. LONG-BAILEY: But we can't ignore that over recent years, certainly the last five years, we've seen increases in violent crime right across the country. LONG-BAILEY: At the same time, since 2010, we've seen over 20,000 police officers cut from frontline services. LONG-BAILEY: So it's right to recognise that that would have LONG-BAILEY: direct impact and of course we've got to invest more in community neighbourhood policing and Labour has pledged to do that, the eyes and ears of our communities, but we've also got to invest in counter-terrorism measures and ensure that they're properly funded as well as ensuring that our defence spending is at the level it should be and we've committed to that 2% NATO target. LONG-BAILEY: Well, the final message that I'd put to the audience on this is that we do need to have that comprehensive community approach. LONG-BAILEY: And that doesn't just focus on police and defence spending. LONG-BAILEY: It's right across social services, probation, education, and reversing the cuts that have happened there so that we can spot any alarming signs when they arise.
No, I've said to Gordon the same as I've said to everyone, urm, if you elect the Prime Minister, you serve the term.

Obviously, I want fewer people to be using food banks, and I want more people to have the security of a job. But we have created 1,000 jobs for every day this government's been in office. Now, that's a statistic. But behind that statistic are people who are able to provide for their families, who are earning a wage, who are able to build a better How many of those jobs are zero-hours contracts? About one in 50 jobs are zero-hours contracts. Could you live on a zero-hours contract? No. Some people choose a zero-hours contract, for instance, students, because they want the flexibility. But what we did is... Hold on a second. We outlawed, first government to do this, the idea of exclusive zero-hours contracts where you can only work for one business. So we passed a law saying that shouldn't happen. But it is a myth. Some people want to say the jobs that have been created are all low-paid jobs. That's not true. No-one's saying that. I'm not saying that. The hint you're trying to give is that...

We would take people from the United Nations. There are people all over the world. Mothers and children. who desperately need sanctuary. They are the people we should be giving sanctuary to, not people, I don't know how you came here, I don't know your circumstances, but the truth is...

Philip, thank you, because you've raised an issue which I know a lot of people are looking at and thinking about. And it's a really important issue because one of the great challenges that we face as a society, and that government faces, is the fact that our society is ageing. We see more people, it's great that people are living longer, but of course that has an impact in terms of the services we have to provide and ensuring people can have the dignity in their old age. Now the situation, as I'm sure you know at the moment is, that if you require care and you have savings of more than £23,000, you have to pay for that care. And if the care you require is residential care, then the value of your house is taken into account. So what happens is people are paying for care People are finding that they are having to sell their house, many people are having to sell their house to pay those care bills and many find that they're not able to leave the money to their families. Now I want to take those risks away and that's what the proposals I've put forward are about. It's about ensuring that nobody is going to have to sell their house to pay for care in their lifetime. It's about ensuring that £100,000 of savings of assets are protected to pass on to the family. We'll put an absolute cap on the level of money that people have to spend on care. And I think what we're doing is ensuring that we can have a sustainable solution for the long term. If I can just give one figure Faisal, in 10 years time there will be 2 million more people aged over 75. If we don't address the issue of our social care system now then it is going to collapse. We need to do it and do it in a way that's fairer to younger generations and that's what my plans are for.

We know there will be difficult decisions, and we'll have to go through every part of the welfare budget. but we believe just as we saved £20 billion in welfare in this Parliament, we will be able to find a further £10 billion of welfare savings in the next Parliament. Let me give you some more examples. We're going to cut the welfare cap we put in place, saying no family should get more than £26,000 a year in welfare. We're going to reduce that to £23,000. That's not within We think that's the right thing to do. We're going to say when it comes to young people that young people when they leave school they should be either earning or learning, they should be doing an apprenticeship, looking at higher education, they shouldn't be able to go straight on to unemployment benefit and housing benefit. All of these things... Changing welfare, Jeremy, isn't just about saving money. No. It's about trying to help people's lives. You give no deep.

Of course. And I believe in spending, investing massively in our public services because we support, and which is what we are doing, but because we support a dynamic wealth-creating sector. And that's why it's a good idea actually to believe in business, to support business, to support the wealth-creating sector of the economy. Jeremy Corbyn and the Labour Party have actually said that they want to overthrow capitalism and to destroy the basis of wealth creation in this country. I have to say, I think that would be disastrous for this country.

Robert, earlier in my career I worked in Northern Ireland with the police in Northern Ireland as part of the work out of the Good Friday Agreement and with a team I set out to change the police service in Northern Ireland to make sure it gained the confidence of all communities in Northern Ireland which was a really important part of the peace process. So I went, I said what we would do and we delivered. I then ran the Crown Prosecution Service for five years with 7,000 staff. I said we would improve the way we dealt with violence against women and girls. I said we'd do it. We made the change, and we got on with it, and we made that change, and it was more successful at the end. I came into the Labour Party to lead it four and a half years ago, and I said we will change our party to make sure that we are a party that can put a credible proposition before the country for changing the country, and that we'll always say, always say, country first, party second. I've been there, I've done it, I've changed it. What I'm now asking for is the opportunity to change our country for the better. Because we've had 14 pretty awful years. 14 years when I think most people would say we've ended up now with almost everything not working very well. Worse than it was 14 years ago when this government came in. Most people not feeling better off. And this is a choice election. Because if we go on for another five years of this under that is not going to change. That isn't going to change. So this is the opportunity to turn the page, to rebuild our country, rebuild our public services, rebuild our economy and make sure that we can genuinely say to ourselves we're a better country, better run, proper public services and people feel better off. I've done that sort of change throughout my life, and I'm asking for the opportunity to now do it for the country.

Tim, you've just made my point. for me when you were talking about school meals. The simple fact is too many of the grammar schools at the moment are in middle class areas. What we need to do is we need to put grammar schools in working class areas.

Exactly. And in the manifesto, what we said was that the commitments we had could be financed by the taxes that had been set aside. What happened after the election was, we got a report that said, no actually, if you want to reach the European Union average, the figures aren't as you supposed they are, you're going to have to raise it further, in order to do that. And that's why we did it, and that's why we've got the 1% National Insurance. Now that was a ¿

I'm afraid that you asked me what I would demand. I'm telling you what I would demand from a Keir Starmer government, which is progressive politics that align with the values of the people of Scotland. And of course, within that, we would want the powers to hold a referendum, because it is wrong that a Westminster parliament gets to determine our future and not the Scottish people's.

When illegal migrants come to our country, what will you do with them? It's a very simple question. I can tell people what I will do with them. I will put them on planes to Rwanda because they shouldn't be able to stay.

Let me just make one point after all that Nick said, because I thought there was a slight danger of a sort of holier than thou. I think we should all be frank. Politics has been a mess for all of us. We all had MPs with dreadful expenses problems. There are still three Labour MPs in court at the moment. There were Liberal MPs that were criticised. And when it comes to party funding, yes, there's been the union money going into Labour from the Unite Union. Yes, the Conservative Party's been too reliant for too long on rich individuals. And yes, the Liberal Democrats took £2.5 million off someone who's still, I think, a criminal on the run, and the money hasn't been paid back.

I think it can do. We've had a Brexit crisis created by the Conservatives, only one of the many crises they've caused, a crisis of living standards, homelessness, zero-hours contracts, and the rest of it. I think it can bring people together. And actually, our society isn't divided between people who voted Leave and who voted Remain. Some politicians try and exploit that and make that into a division. Whether people voted Leave or Remain, they need an end to 10 years of Conservative-Liberal Democrats' austerity, which has held communities back including those that voted Leave.

It is about reducing the working week all across the economy, paid for by productivity increases all across. Britain works longer than most. Well, people need to be aware that actually a shorter working week is probably a good thing for their health and well-being, as is decent pay. What I would also say to Mr Johnson is that his proposal for 40 new hospitals, which he grandly announced in July, turns out to be six reconfigurations

Yeah, on fuel bills, as I said earlier, we would take VAT off domestic fuel bills altogether and do away with green levies, which would put more money in your pocket. And one of the reasons that wages have stagnated in recent years, and have probably stagnated since about 2004, is because of the levels of immigration. We have an oversupply of labour. That's the answer to everything, isn't it? We have an oversupply of labour in this country. It is as simple as that. Now, as for Tim talking before about us falling off the cliff edge, It's simply not true. You were the same people who went round talking about project fear before the referendum. You know, let's just look. Unemployment is down. Manufacturing is up. The FTSE's at record levels. We're the fastest growing economy.

75% unaffected or better off. But do remember, it is part of the package. It is a deal. The lady with spectacles.

Yeah, no, they're not, and that's why we need to set up this border security command, which will operate across different countries. But just to explain, in the terrorist cases, which is why I passionately believe we can do this, we shared the data, each country as to what they knew, the intelligence all sitting in one room with big data panels, then agreed a strategy, how we're going to take this gang down, then an arrest strategy. Because if the French authorities don't arrest someone at exactly the same time as the authorities in Germany or London, then it doesn't work. I've seen that done in relation to terrorist cases. I simply don't accept people who say the only gangs that can't be taken down are apparently these smuggling gangs. I don't accept that. I think it can be done. I've seen it, I've been part of it, which is why I'm confident it can be done. I agree we've got to do things on returns, but the first thing... On returns, though... We've got to get security back to our borders, because at the moment, the government's lost control of our borders.
What I will be doing is, as we heard in the earlier session, what I'll be doing is being a difficult woman and ensuring that we're negotiating hard.
The Labour Party is saying other parties have got a racism problem. But you want to wake up and smell the coffee. You're quite right. You've got an anti-Semitism problem.

I just think, look, this is a really sensitive issue. People feel quite rightly, really strongly about immigration. People feel very unsettled that they have had no faith in an immigration system which would be run chaotically by both Conservative and Labour governments for a long time. All I would say to both David Cameron and Gordon Brown is, yes, let's have our differences, but please don't live in denial about what is going on. Don't live in denial about the fact that because of the chaos in the system, we've got lots of people here who are working for criminal gangs rather than for Britain. We've got to deal with that. You can pretend that we can sort of wish it away. It is a problem. They are here. Now, of course what I'm saying is controversial and people try and score points. It is at least an attempt to deal with an issue which we have to deal with. I don't think we can have another 10 years, another 20 years, in the old way, just ignoring these things. We can't come up with promises like caps, which don't work, and by the way...

Well, you're absolutely right. The previous administration chose not to proceed with that policy. Instead, they spent that public money on building more affordable homes. And we've built 450,000 affordable homes in this country since 2010. That's more on average per year than the Labour government did before us from 1997 to 2010. We've built in the last year more homes in this country than any year bar one in the last 30 years. There's a role for the state in that. We're now investing £9 billion in an additional 240,000 affordable homes. But we're also trying to encourage through reforms of the planning system and infrastructure the private sector as well, because you need the two to be working together to deliver the number of homes that we need to in this country. We think we need to be building about 300,000 homes every year. We believe we're on course to achieve that, but you've got to have a very vibrant private sector and additional government resources.

Tasneem, your question is fundamentally the reason I got into politics. My grandparents emigrated to this country with very little. Two generations later, I have the enormous privilege of standing here as your Prime Minister. I don't think my story would be possible in any other country, and that's why I feel an enormous sense of duty and gratitude to our country for what it's done for me. And I'm in politics to make a difference, to make people's lives better. And that starts with young people, making sure you have the opportunities you need to succeed in life, whatever you want to do, wherever you live. That's the type of country I believe in. And when you get that job, when you work hard, I want you to keep more of your hard-earned money, decide how to spend it for yourself. I believe in a society that's aspirational. That's why I want to cut your taxes. It's not just a technocratic thing. It's a type of country, I believe in. It's one where your hard work is rewarded, your aspirations are supported, and that's what I'm in this to deliver for you, and that's what I will do as your Prime Minister.

Two hundred and twenty thousand people last year, without any health insurance, people who were not by any stretch of the imagination rich, people who in many cases had to borrow, which they could ill afford to do, to pay for their operation, went in to the private sector. They've paid their taxes, they've paid towards the cost of NHS treatment. I think they deserve a better deal.

Thank you. Janice, you're right. The rich are getting richer, but the poor are getting poorer. We think it's time to bring fairness back into our economy by asking the billionaires and the multi-millionaires to pay a bit more and introducing a £15 minimum wage, an immediate £40 weekly uplift to universal credit. and lifting the cruel two-child benefit cap, which both the Conservatives and the Labour Party support, even though it unnecessarily holds 250,000 children down into poverty. It's not even that expensive to lift, but both the Conservatives and Labour have ruled it out.

Well, I'm the First Minister of Scotland. We're investing in a record number of apprenticeships, more affordable homes, and helping the poorest young people in our country stay on at school and college. But we've also kept access to university free of tuition fees. I grew up in a working class family. I wouldn't be standing here as First Minister of Scotland without the free education I had access to. As a politician now, I have no right to take that same entitlement away from the next generation of young people. So I will always fight, work, vote, do whatever it takes to keep access to university free. I think it's shameful for any politician who has benefited from that free education to take it away from others. So, SNP MPs in the House of Commons and the Scottish Parliament, anywhere else, will always support the principle that your access to education as a young person should be based entirely on your ability to learn and never, ever on your ability to pay. Nicola Sturgeon, thank you.

No, well, actually, if you look at what we've done just last year, we built 240,000 homes, which was more than any year in the last 31 years bar one. And of those, 57,000 were affordable homes. I don't want to go on my record. If you look at my record as Mayor of London, we outbuilt Labour by miles when I was Mayor of London. We will build the homes for young people growing up in this country. We will also make sure that they have the jobs to go to. And we will... I'll answer that.

The SNP have also delivered nationalised rail, 40% more affordable homes than in England, 70% more than in Wales and ultimately it's about keeping our promise to the Scottish people. But I would actually contest, I would say the Labour Party and the Conservative Party are being very honest with you about what the future holds. The problem is it's £18 billion worth of public sector cuts and a continuation with Brexit.

Well, the coalitions that are dealing with IS in Syria are complex and they're sometimes fighting each other as well. My whole point is that we have to cut off the funds for IS, cut off the arms for IS, cut off their publicity as far as we can but also bring about a peace process in Syria by reconvening the Geneva talks including all the neighboring countries as well as Iran but also bring about a constructive dialogue in Libya so that we don't have huge areas of that country with all its oil wealth underneath it ungoverned and a prey for this kind of thing. There are desperate people in Libya living in refugee camps. Let's give them something positive and some hope by bringing about stability in that country. It's up to all of us to engage with that because if there is ungoverned spaces around the world then actually everybody's under threat.
It decides... I think inevitably you will see more wind farms across the country. But I think also...

Nushka, our country desperately needs change. From the NHS crisis, to the cost-living crisis, to the sewage in our rivers, nothing seems to work properly anymore. Families across the UK are working hard, raising their families, looking after loved ones. They deserve a fair deal. And every vote for the Liberal Democrats in this election is a vote for a local champion who's going to fight for that fair deal. So people can see a GP or a dentist when they need one. So we value care and properly support carers, whether they're care workers or family carers. And so mortgages and rents aren't so high that families are terrified of losing their home and young people can't get one. and, crucially, a fair deal where we transform British politics so we can get this real change. Only the Liberal Democrats are fighting for that fair deal.

I'll be brief, Krishnan. Sarma, thank you so much for your work. I've been a fan and signed up on the mailing list of the JCWI since before I was a politician. briefly we would create safe and legal routes as I said we would scrap the Rwanda scheme which at 1.8 million pounds per asylum seeker is an incredibly expensive way to be cruel. We would allow asylum seekers to work in the meantime while they're waiting for their decision so they can support themselves We would restore legal aid, which is the issue that's brought up to me time and time again by those working in the legal profession and as volunteers to support those seeking asylum. We would end indefinite detention. Asylum seekers are not criminals. And finally, we would take expert advice from people like yourself.

Mr. Romance. But to be fair to my wife, she's a social housing lawyer and she's now on the council in my area in charge of housing. And she's now in charge of the largest council house building programme in 40 years in Kingston. So it can be done. You know, my wife's doing it. I'm very proud of her. And I think it needs political will. The Conservatives aren't interested. It's really clear they're not interested in that. They want to play to the developers' tune. I think if you play to the community's tune, it's better. And here's another reason. You know, some people say people don't want homes built in the area. One of the reasons they don't, if you ask them, is they say, well, there's no GPs already. We want more people here. There's not enough school places. Their water infrastructure can't take it. So what you want is a community-led approach which deals with those issues. The developers don't want to do it. So if you have a community-led approach, you can deal with the infrastructure problems and people actually will welcome more homes because they can see there's something in it for them.

The choice at this election is simple. More chaos with the Conservatives or change with Labour? Imagine waking up on the 5th of July to five more years of a chaotic Tory government. Years of high tax and low growth while everything gets more expensive. Crime going unpunished. NHS waiting lists getting longer. The Tories have left you and our country worse off. A vote for Labour is a vote to end the chaos. We know we can't tax our way to growth. Labour's plan for growth and wealth creation is about stability, investment and reform. Economic stability is change. We will bring down NHS waiting times with 40,000 new weekly appointments. And we'll secure our streets and our borders with more neighbourhood police and border security command. Vote Labour for the change.
Thank you. Is it not Parliament's job to challenge the government and to prevent the government simply sweeping things through?

Well, Nigel, those schools could. You know, our state schools have had austerity and have cuts and cuts. Those schools could absorb some of those costs, so they didn't have to pass it on to all of the people. So you're happy with more pupils going into the state sector? But we cannot afford to give private schools a tax break when our state system cannot have the right teachers in the classrooms that they deserve. OK, I must bring in Penny Morgan there. Thank you, Nigel Farage.

I hate to enter into private grief here, because both of them have got this wrong, I'm afraid. Nick is wrong to send a message to people that you can come here illegally, and then you'll get an amnesty. David is wrong to mislead people about his cap, because it doesn't include people from the European Union, doesn't include large numbers of people who are dependants and university students. What he would do is make it impossible for some businesses to recruit people from abroad during the course of the year, and he's never given us a number for his cap. He has to tell us what the cap is, or stop telling us there will be a cap when probably, there's not going to be.

It's provided for under the Equality Act. It is actually spelled out as one of the examples given in the Act. It always has been. and fabricating a difference here in a situation where actually we should be trying to find common ground and work together. Well, I'm proud of the record of my party on women's rights. We've done amazing things in terms of equality under Labour governments, made a massive difference to millions of lives. There's much more work to do on women's equality going forward. But the only way I think we can make that progress is if we change the way that we do politics, stop this business of always trying to divide everybody and actually use the opportunity of leadership, which is what I would do, to say the job of the leader of the country is to bring people together. These are difficult discussions, different opinions of course, but the role of the leader of the country is to bring that together so that the country itself is less divided. One of the issues for me is the, with sadness, is the fact that over the last 14 years there's been this tendency on almost every issue to try to find the points of difference and division rather than doing what we're actually really good at as a country, which is bringing people together.

I think actually the best thing we can do for the NHS is to build up social care, because it helps to take the pressure off the NHS. Now, I'm not saying we've got everything right in Scotland. We face the same massive challenges that other countries do. But as I said earlier, we have already integrated health and social care. difficult process, but the right one to do. We're also now doing the equally different thing of trying to take money from the acute NHS and put it into social care, recognising that if we've got more investment in GPs and community services, then that will help to keep people out of hospital. And just on Leanne's point, we also pay the living wage to people who work in social care as well, to value them for what they do.

I'm sorry, it was. And the great thing about this now for any member of the public is that thanks to the Butler Report, and remember we've had four separate enquiries in to whether this intelligence was misused or not, each of them have come to the same conclusion and people can actually go and look on the web site and they can go and study the Joint Intelligence Committee Reports that I got

Look, I think what happened was very wrong and I'm glad the Prime Minister has said that and he's apologised. He's apologised to veterans, but I think he also has apologised to everyone because he was there representing us. And what I hope is that our veterans will feel treasured and their families will feel treasured at the end of this week. I don't want this issue to become a political football because I think that does them a disservice. Well, it already is.

Well, we need to set a timescale for net zero emissions that's both ambitious but is also achievable. And if you look at studies, for example, such as the one by the University of Oxford at the end of last year, it showed that we could produce renewable energy in this country that exceeds our demands for energy if we really put that investment in. Claire, in the audience tonight in the debate, said that both the main parties, the Conservatives and Labour, have broken promises on climate. And she was absolutely right. And in the answers they gave, what they omitted to say is that they're not willing to put the investment in that's needed if we're going to seize the huge opportunity we have. To solve our energy crisis.

I think there's a separate issue though, you're quite right, which is we need the workforce coming in so we don't use agency staff in the same way. And that requires a workforce plan, which we've also got fully costed to come in to effectively be the cavalry that comes in and gives you the support you need on the front line. But that will also take time, won't it? It's going to take years. It will take time, but let me just... I mean, it'll take years. Yeah, let me just deal with this, because I've been leader of the Labour Party now for four and a half years, and every year we've had a winter crisis in the NHS. You'll be completely familiar with that. And what we do is we put a sticking plaster on that in about January or February, it just about limps through to the summer, and then the only guarantee is you're going to have another one next year and it's going to be worse. And everybody says, everything else will take too long, don't tell me things that are going to take three or four years, and nothing gets done. So we go round and round in circles. not fixing it, sticking plaster politics, is one of the biggest problems of politics now, which is politicians not being honest about how long it's actually going to take to fix things. OK, well, be honest with us now.

Well, I want to see our education improve as it has done over the last few years. We need teachers with better qualifications. We need young people with the aspiration to succeed. And we need to give people the chance to start education early. That's why nursery education starts at three, and to be able to go through to 18. And that's what we're saying in our manifesto. Education will be part-time or full-time till the age of 18. Now, as far as grades and standards are concerned, I, myself, believe in the highest of standards. I believe if we don't search for the highest of standards, then we will not, in the end, get the best pupils coming out of our schools. Now, yes, we've got to look at the different types of exams, and we will do so, but I think it's important to realise we're in this new world where we're competing with Asia as well as America and Europe, and our young people have got to have the grades, the qualifications, to be able to meet the best in the world. Now, that's what I want to achieve, and I hope I can work with you to do so.

And they're now on their way down. They are down. Yes, because the NHS was impacted by industrial action. And if it wasn't for that, half a million appointments would have been saved. So it's somebody else's fault. No, no. But let me address the question, because as you said, Julie, the NHS is devolved. So different parties are responsible for the NHS in different parts of the United Kingdom. Keir Starmer's party are responsible for it in Wales. Now, the NHS has been impacted by COVID everywhere. But things are difficult in England, I know that. But if you were in Wales, you would be waiting 40% longer for treatment. Ambulance and A&E times are the worst in Great Britain, and one in four people are on a waiting list. So yes, the NHS has challenges, but it has challenges everywhere regardless of the party that's in charge.

Those rules were difficult for everyone to follow, and the government should absolutely have gone above and beyond in following them. And I deeply regret what happened. I apologize for it at the time. And it was an extraordinarily difficult time for our country. And I know that. And I know that people will remember that. And all I can say is, look, trust takes time to rebuild through actions. And whether it was what we did during COVID to support the NHS, roll out the vaccine, support people's jobs, what we've done, what I've done over the past 18 months to deliver on restoring economic stability and start making progress on bringing migration down, that's how you earn trust, through your actions, making progress on things that matter to people. And that's what I plan to talk to people about through the rest of this campaign. And yesterday's manifesto sets out a direction travel for the future.

Where do you think Theresa May is tonight? Take a look out your window. She might be out there sizing up your house to pay for your social care. And why do you think she called this election? She wants five years as prime minister, and she thinks you'll give it to her, no questions asked, literally. even if she brings in a dementia tax, sacks your kids' teachers and nicks their lunches while she's at it.
