We can all agree that is a rubbish offer. Look, I know we don't all agree on Brexit, but she's off to negotiate a deal for you, for me, for all of us. Imagine if it's a bad deal. I mean, dementia tax bad. I know that to persuade you to vote Liberal Democrat I have to give you some really good reasons. So here's three. One, I'll rescue the NHS and social care by putting a penny on income tax. Two, I'll give you the final say on the Brexit deal, not Theresa May. Three, whatever power you give me, I'll use it to stop her taking you for granted.

Well, in a world that has barbarous countries like Iran rushing as quickly as they can to get their own nuclear weapons, it would be madness to unilaterally get rid of ours. And so, yes, we have to have it. And with reluctance, yes, there may be circumstances in which we'd have to use it.

Correct. Would you support Tony Blair? No, I mean, we wouldn't support Tony Blair. If you have a Parliament where there's no overall majority next time, a government's got to be formed. Now, let's not speculate which party's the biggest single party but without a majority. But that party, which has the first opportunity to form a government, is going to have to conduct itself in Parliament by taking account of opposition parties' viewpoints, also dissenting voices in its own party. Now, I think that's a much healthier way to proceed. than what we've had over the last four years in particular, which is a government with a big majority riding roughshod, not just over opposition opinions, but over the substantial opinions of its own members of Parliament, which is why you get student fees when they said they wouldn't, which is why you get a war in Iraq, which is why they say they're not putting up tax, and then they turn around and increase national insurance. No wonder, heavens almighty, people are so cynical about politics in this country. If politicians had to listen a bit more, it would be better. Yes.

The reality of austerity has been to hit the The funds of local authorities has meant the life chances of children have been damaged by underfunding schools, headteachers going out collecting money in order to keep a school going, young people with no after-school clubs, no youth centre to go to, and the levels of dislocation and poverty in so many areas of Britain deliberately, because of austerity, must and will change. Yes, we will invest in education.

Well, I support a second Brexit referendum, because I actually think everybody in the UK should have the chance to escape the mess. But fundamentally, it's for Scotland to decide our own future, because it's not right. And perhaps you can explain why you think it's right for Scotland to face exit from the EU when we overwhelmingly voted against that. I respect the fact that people here don't support independence. That's legitimate to oppose it. But what's not legitimate is to deny the people of Scotland the right to be in charge of that decision. That's a basic issue of democracy, surely.

Just this week, we've introduced a diverted profits tax to go after these companies that make money in Britain and don't pay tax in Britain. But here's what Ed Miliband isn't telling you. Because he doesn't support any of the spending reductions and efficiencies we've had to make, he wants to make a very big cut. He wants to put up taxes and cut your pay, going into your monthly pay slip at the end of the month and taking your money out because he thinks he can spend that money better than you. That would be a great mistake for our country.

and it certainly wasn't a day for a Prime Minister to decide, as Stephen said, that his priority should be to fight for his own political future. We have a proud record in Wales of sending young men and women to the armed forces and we need to make sure that the armed forces are properly funded for conventional deterrence and peacekeeping and we need to look after those young people when they leave the armed forces with so many problems in housing and mental health issues and so on. But we don't build that resilient conventional force by spending 200 billion pounds on a new nuclear deterrent.

Well, our manifesto, unlike the Conservative manifesto, is properly and fully costed. It's there online for everyone to see. We're one of the richest countries in the world. And in terms of jobs, we deserve better, and our people deserve better, than the low-paid, low-wage, insecure employment they've got. And that's one of the reasons that our green industrial revolution will not only tackle climate change, it will create a million new jobs, a million well-paid skilled manufacturing jobs to replace some of those well-paid manufacturing jobs that have been exported and lost since Thatcher came to power in 1979. I think we can do so much better as a country in terms of the economy and the quality of jobs in our country.

I remember vividly when your party wanted to cut spending for schools at the beginning of the last Parliament and I said no, because you don't make society fairer by cutting the money that goes to nurseries, colleges and schools.

There is no place for anti-Semitism anywhere in our society and certainly not in our party. Members have been suspended if they've committed any remarks that can be seen to be of an anti-Semitic nature. Those that have done that are suspended. We have a process that is independent of me within the party, which investigates these and makes a decision on it. I deplore racism in any form whatsoever. The way in which the Jewish people have suffered down the centuries, the Holocaust and all that went with it, was the most appalling stain in the history of mankind. And I believe we have to fight racism in any form, with every fibre of our being. A society that cannot challenge racism is a society that is heading for division. I will not tolerate it in our party or anywhere else.

On anti-Semitism, I spoke out in the Shadow Cabinet directly confronting Jeremy Corbyn. And to the voting public? I did. No, that's not right. I did. I was out on the airways also saying the same thing. You could vote with your feet rather than just, you know, going on to do the Today programme. Harry, we were trying to ensure that the day after Jeremy Corbyn, there was a Labour Party which we could pick up and change and put it back. The Labour Party has done incredible things. We set up the NHS. We've done incredible things on equality, Sure Start, that made a massive difference to people's lives. I'm proud of my party, and I knew there would be a day after where we needed to pick our party up, to say to the electorate in 2019, we got this wrong and rebuild. And we were all working to that end. I'm glad we had that opportunity because I genuinely think now at this election, you know, there is a choice. The Prime Minister was saying there's a choice. We've had 14 years now of chaos. of division and failure. And it's not going to change, because if you think their election campaign is anything other than chaos and division, it is characterising them. That isn't going to change. Or we can turn the page and start to rebuild our country with a Labour government. And the sooner, in my view, we can get on with that, the better for millions of people. I think most people, if you ask them the question, do you now feel better off than you were 14 years ago, most people would say no. Or, is the NHS better now than it was? Are our public services better than they were? Most people would say no to those questions. We have to turn this around.

What I've done is always wanted to see a peace process in Northern Ireland, and that is exactly what we've got. And thank you to the Labour government that negotiated it after 1996. Now, gentlemen, briefly. Thank you.

I am going to leave the immigration debate till later. I look forward to that one. But I'm going to take up your beloved market, Nigel, of blaming immigration. But we have a market-led housing policy over the past decade or more that hasn't delivered homes. That's why we actually need to build homes for social rent, build council homes. That's the kind of solution we need to actually get genuinely affordable homes, allowing private landlords to keep stacking up enormous profits. They've actually made 1,400% profit. for private landlords since 1996. That's far more than you get from investing in new business, investing in productive parts of our economy. The whole way our housing market has worked has utterly skewed our economy. We need to change that away. We need to get back to those houses as homes, not financial assets.

Because I'm a practical man. and because I want to do what I believe is right for the country. Now, I think that we have to spend quite a lot of money in future on some of the important things, particularly hospitals and schools, because we are the fourth richest country in the world and it's about time we had a standard of education and a standard of healthcare that matched that fact. Now, in order to do that, I think we need to change the system. I think we need to trust doctors and nurses and trust teachers and not have all these targets, not have the command and control system that we have now in health and education, and we'd make changes to get real value for the taxpayers' money, but the money's needed as well. So we've put forward our plans, we've said, where we would identify the waste and cut it out, and the choice between for the country next week is this. £4 billion of tax cuts from the Conservatives, £10 or £11 billion of tax increases from Labour, which every independent expert said they'd need. That's a difference of about £1,000 for a typical family in the country, so it's a pretty sizeable amount.

That changes about political changes. On the direct point of payments to help people who are disabled, we are a party who will always support those in most need and the Welfare Bill for helping people on disabilities has gone up 7 billion in the past seven years and is now at 50 billion. We will always provide that safety net where it's needed.

This rhetoric around resources completely misses the point and it fans the flames of the very rhetoric that you have mentioned. Lady on the front row, I didn't catch your name, I'm sorry. Under our migration system, under the Conservatives, we have the worst of both worlds. We have sectors like the NHS, social care, hospitality, engineering, so many different sectors that simply cannot get the people they need with skills because there hasn't been an investment in this country in apprentices and skills programs. And at the same time, the government is also cutting the supply from abroad as well. So it's hardly any surprise that our economy is flatlining. So what we need to do is get rid of these ridiculous arbitrary targets, get rid of arbitrary salary thresholds. We need to invest in a proper skills strategy in this country. Liberal Democrats are the only party saying that we will have a higher minimum wage for care workers. More than 105,000 people came to this country to work in our care sector last year. We should welcome them and thank them for propping up our care system and for looking after our loved ones. But we should not be doing that. at the expense of investing in our own workforce. And it is possible to do both in a way where we can invest in our own domestic workforce, and then when we need to get different people in from abroad for different sectors, we can do that sector by sector, depending on what we need. And that avoids that kind of resource-driven rhetoric that fans the flames.

Let's hear from Plaid Cymru and reenact yours. Talking about victims, that's what was at the heart of the question. I was attacked when I was about 13 or 14 by other teenagers. It left a deep scar on me for many years, actually. No knives involved, thankfully, but it didn't take away the fear in me at that time. And when the same thing happened to my son recently, I could feel his pain. On knife crime, we had the attack at a school in Ammanford in the south of Wales recently. Again, looking at the victims, Applied Cymru is talking about establishing a role of a victim's commissioner for Wales, because it's by listening to and hearing and acting on the fears and the wishes of victims that we build a better criminal justice system. On policing, by the way, and retention, It's like with teachers and nurses in many ways. What I think police women and men want to feel is to feel valued. And when you've gone through a process over the past 14 years of being told, we want fewer, police, public spending cuts slashes the numbers, then they gradually build it back up when they realize the errors of their ways. just undermines policing. Some of it's about pay, but some of it is about just feeling valued. But for us in Wales, of course, we need to get hold of powers over policing. Every bit of evidence suggests we should. Both the Conservatives and Labour ignore that.

No, we set out the principles in our manifesto, which are the ones that I've just set out in the answer, which is crucially that it's fair across the generations, that we enable people to have the knowledge and the comfort of knowing they won't have to sell their house in their lifetime to pay for their care bills.

But if this was such a deterrent, why is it obviously, obviously not working? You spend all this time, when I first got this job, saying, call an election, call an election. And then when we call an election, you spend all your time saying, why did you call an election? I'm just curious. This is about the future. I can tell everyone what I'm going to do with illegal migrants who come to our country. They will be on planes, be sent away, because they shouldn't be here. What will you do with the illegal migrants that arrive to the UK? What will you do with them? You've had three weeks to think of an answer. What is it?

Well I (laughs) Urm. (pause) It's not at the moment cos I still think we've got things to do. I'm not saying the, the irony of this job is that you're less popular as you go on, but in some ways you're better equipped to do the job. And I feel that, that we're just really poised on the Health Service, Education reform, a lot of the stuff we're doing on Law and Order. I think with the economy, there's a whole new series of things we've got to do about science, technology, apprenticeship, vocational skills and you know there's masses for me to do. And that's what gets you up in the morning. It's, it's recognising that whatever ghastly stuff is appearing from time to time in the media, you've still got the energy and the determination to do the job, and then I've limit on, my, my time doing it and then it's for somebody else to take on the batten and and and run with it. But I still feel there's things we've got to do and, and, and I think we can accelerate the change, drive it faster in, in this third term, but we've got to get elected first.

I think it's slightly ironic, because from the moment I got this job, Keir Starmer was telling me, call an election, call an election. And now that we've called an election, he's saying, oh, no, don't have an election, because I don't want to tell you what I'm going to do. And this is the choice for everybody. The plan is working, of course I know. Everyone is only just starting to feel the benefits of it, but inflation is back to normal, wages are growing, taxes are now being cut. Keir Starmer would put all that progress at risk. He would put up everyone's taxes by £2,000. £2,000 in higher taxes for every working family in our country after all the hard work and sacrifice we've been through. That's not the right course of action. I don't know why you want to put up people's taxes.

But what happens is there are a million people from Britain who are in the European Union and staying in homes and staying in countries of the European Union. And there are a million people from the European Union who are in our country. That is what being part of the European Union is about. And there are many, many British citizens who want the chance to stay for a period of time in other countries in the European Union.

In eight days, you have a vital choice to make about who you want to lead the country. Do you want Theresa May and her team? A team that has a plan, and it's one that doesn't duck the hard choices about living within our means, but is also a plan that will build on the success we have delivered. Success in cutting the deficit, stabilising the economy, getting more people into employment. It will build on our commitment to having a country that works for everyone. Now, there are seven of us here tonight, and I'm sure you will hear plenty of bluff, bravado, and tempty, shiny election promises. But the only question to consider is who should be in number 10 to steer Britain to a brighter future. Jeremy Corbyn, with his money tree wish list manifesto and no plan for Brexit, or Theresa May and her record of delivery with her clear plan for Brexit and the strong team behind her who can make sure the country gets to that brighter future. And next, it's Jeremy Corbyn.

Yes, but I think it's very important when you start those negotiations, you're very clear the lines you won't cross. And I've just set out the lines that I won't cross. But I'm not a defeatist like the Prime Minister, and nor will I say to Julie... I won't insult Julie by saying, it's all fine, everything's working, everything's great. Part of the problem we have with this Prime Minister is that his lived world is millions of miles away from the lived worlds of individuals across the country, the businesses and the families that they're trying to support. There's a massive gap. That is a problem.

Absolutely, it is the case that I think resources are always part of the answer. In Wales, in contrast to Scotland, we've seen a 9% reduction in police officers. between 2010 and 2018, we need to reverse that. We're calling for 1,600 additional police officers. A preventative approach, absolutely. It is true, as I think it was the father of Damilola Taylor was saying, knife crime and violence has become normalised in our society, in the sense that we are almost desensitised to it now. And that says something, doesn't it, about where we are. And a preventative approach absolutely, ultimately, is the way to address this, because crime, criminality, radicalisation in the case of terrorism, it has multiple roots, and we have to address them at all of those levels.

In our manifesto we said we weren't following the Andrew Dilnot proposals. We weren't going to follow those Andrew Dilnot proposals and there were two reasons why. The first is that those tended to protect people who were wealthier but didn't protect people who were on modest incomes and secondly it required payment out of taxation. And I actually think that if you're going to be fair across the generations, then we don't ask young people to be having their taxes increased in order to pay for the social care costs of people who may be sitting on a very significant value in their house.

My thoughts are obviously very much with those who were injured, the family and friends of those who were killed. And as others have said, really, it's impossible to imagine the pain that people are going through right now, particularly London Bridge, a place that was already hit once before as well. I think we've seen the best of human nature and the worst of human nature, as quite often has happened before, in the sense that, you know, we've seen the bravery of the public, the bravery of our emergency services at the same time as we've seen the utter criminality and hatred of whoever perpetrated this particular attack. We don't know much about it yet, but I think as political leaders, we all have a responsibility to ensure that incidents like this don't divide us, that I hope we can come together and show real leadership and promote the compassion and love that is more powerful ultimately than hatred. But on terms of what we should do, Terry talked about the police and he's right that any number of police can't prevent this from happening, but more police numbers would certainly help. I think we need to look again at the PREVENT program because I think we need to make sure that it is enjoying as much confidence as every single community in our country as it can, and I'm not sure right now that it is, and I do believe that staying inside the EU would be a better way of ensuring that we have that security information.

First of all, I want to say to Penny, decent investment in public services is not political dogma. It's common sense. It's what people want to see. I'm not saying that. I'm saying having a row about the sectors is wrong.

What Nick is saying, and we're scratching beneath the surface, now, is there would be VAT on new homes under a Liberal policy. What I think is however right is that we do, as we've done, encourage local authorities to build, and that's why lots of local authorities are again building. But, you know, I think the other thing that matters is what I call shared equity. That is part-rent, part-buy, but it's a housing association, a bank or a building society, working with the owner, and gradually you buy up your house. That's going to be far more popular, particularly for young people in the years to come. The key to all this is low interest rates. We've got to keep interest rates low. We have kept interest rates low even during this recession and have done so for the last few years. I want interest rates low for existing home owners and for people buying their own home. I'm afraid the Liberal and Conservative policies are too big a risk to inflation and to interest rates for the future.

First of all, there hasn't been unlimited immigration and it doesn't cut wages. I don't want to see a cut in inward migration because it would damage the economy of my country and one of the reasons I want Scotland to be independent is so we can take these decisions for ourselves rather than having damaging decisions imposed upon us.

So, William, actually, on national service, I have two young girls. I'm incredibly excited for them to do it, because I think it'll be transformative for our country. I really do, because it'll provide skills and opportunities for young people that will set them up for the rest of their lives, foster a culture of service in our society, bring people closer together, and contribute to our long-term resilience and security. And actually, everywhere I've been, people have been really positively talking to me about it, and actually parents, more than anyone else, excited about what it will do for their kids. But more broadly, so that's just one thing, right? A couple of days after that, we announced another big policy where I want to provide opportunities for young people, you and everyone else, which is why we're going to curb the investment into rip-off degrees, which let young people down, and take that money and use it to fund 100,000 high-quality apprenticeships, which actually can provide an enormous opportunity for people. And the answer to the question, I think, Ian's first question, wasn't it, Ian? There you are, Ian's daughter, with buying a house. Because when I talk to young people, that's the thing. They want a great job. They want to be able to buy their own home. And what we announced yesterday in the manifesto a couple of days ago is going to make a big difference. Because it's going to mean that it's much easier for young people to get on the housing ladder and buy their first home. So whether it's an amazing apprenticeship, whether it's national service, whether it's buying your own home, actually what you're seeing are bold ideas that will make a big difference and spread opportunity around.

Well I think the answer to that is to raise the minimum wage, which we're now doing so far, far, far faster than we ever have done before. Train up more apprenticeships, which we're doing on a scale we've never have done before. But Nigel Farage talks about remember history. I mean, the Farage family were foreigners once. What precisely? You are married to a foreigner. Precisely. Let's be open-hearted and generous-spirited. Should we control it? All I'm saying... Should we control it? That's the question.

Thank you. Natalie Bennett. There's been two issues covered here. One is the issue of the debt. And it's worth saying that over the past 100 years, about half of it, Britain ran a higher debt-to-GDP ratio, and no-one worried about it very much. Because the question you have to ask is, why are you borrowing? If you're borrowing, say, to build the new council and homes for social rent that we so desperately need, you know you're going to get a return far into the future if, as we want to, you end right to buy that asset is there for the future, for future generations. And the second point about the nature of the cuts, This has been borne overwhelmingly by the most vulnerable in this society. The bedroom tax. Two-thirds of the households affected by that have at least one disabled person in them. Independent Living Fund. 18,000 of the most disabled people, the most vulnerable people in our society, their support slashed away. We have to be a humane, fair, decent society. We have to support the most vulnerable. Natalie Bennett, thank you. David Cameron.

The schools got up and wanted to give the books and the teachers, so the nine million or billion that you're suggesting that you're giving to schools actually, they can't even stay open at the moment.

Do you agree with that? I don't think we necessarily would be poorer. I hope that we will retain, as I said, the trade access and I hope also that we will have a Labour government that will be investing in a growing economy in this country and challenging the terrible levels of inequality that exist in this country at the same time.

Jeremy Corbyn? I think it's important when people go into an election campaign, they put forward a policy of what they're going to do. They put forward a policy of how they're going to pay for it. And if they don't deliver, then there is a democratic process to deal with that in the future. That is what democracy is about. It's about holding people to account. and holding government to account in Parliament. And I want to see not a weaker, but a stronger House of Commons in the future that holds our government to account to make sure we deliver on the promises of reducing poverty in Britain.

Let me deal with that in terms of the trust issue. I worked in Northern Ireland, as you may or may not know, with others on transforming the police service in Northern Ireland so that it would have the confidence of both communities. I said I'd work on that. I said we would change it. We delivered. I then ran the Crown Prosecution Service, and I said, we need to change to deliver a better outcome, particularly on violence against women and girls. I said it, I did it. I came into the Labour Party, and I said, we got it wrong in 2019, and I'm going to change this party. I've had to be really tough to do that, and I've delivered it. What I'm asking for now is the opportunity to pick our country up, which is as broken as my party was in 2019, and change it for the better so that everybody across the country benefits from that. So my track record is to come in to say I know what the problem is, it may be difficult but I'm going to do it and I've meant when I started as Labour leader most people said it is not possible to get the Labour Party back to a position where it can put a credible proposition before the country. Said it isn't going to happen, you can't do it, Keir. I never believed that. I've delivered that change. I don't think anybody now says this is the same Labour Party as 2019. What I now want, humbly, ask for the opportunity to take a country which I do think has been broken under this government. Is anything better now than it was when they started? To take that opportunity to rebuild our country and take us forward and that's why I came into politics. I was a lawyer doing other things. Is this convincing you?

94 billion of that will come from borrowing and we're not afraid to say that because interest rates are low and if you are investing in assets and in jobs then the money comes back into the Treasury through higher taxes.

Nicola Sturgeon... Caroline's right. that point. But can I say, I totally get the frustration. I get that people are, to use a Scottish word, scunnered by this whole thing. And I know I'm in a slightly different position to everybody else here, because I'm the First Minister of a country that voted to remain by an overwhelming majority. And we face being taken out of the European Union against our will. But this get Brexit done is the biggest con of this election. The deal that Boris Johnson has negotiated, I think a bad deal, is only a withdrawal deal. The trade talks haven't started yet. And, you know, if they don't conclude quickly, then we will very soon, next year, face another no-deal cliff edge, and that would be catastrophic.

That's right. I've been a housing campaigner my whole life. It's something I'm very passionate about. And my God, do we have a housing crisis in this country. When I moved to Bristol about 15 years ago, I rented a one bed flat. The cost of a one bed flat in the 15 years since has doubled. And we can all agree that wages have not doubled in that time. And so, yes, the Green Party does have a plan. There is no one single bullet. There's no panacea. But there are a group of measures that we can do together. That includes creating more homes. We would create 150,000 council homes a year by the end of the next parliament. That's through a mixture of new build and giving councils the powers to buy the supply more easily from the market. It also involves giving councils the ability to make sure that private housing developments deliver the kind of housing we need, the right homes at the right price in the right place, and giving councils the power to keep private rents under control in areas where they're overheated.

I want to come back to Nicola's point because I think the situation that you described Nicola is something that people feel up and down this country you know and for Amber to say that this is a government that actually cares for those most vulnerable I think is downright insulting to the kind of people that I see in my constituency surgery. Letters from a man who can't actually get into the surgery because he hasn't got his mobility scooter and he hasn't had it for six weeks because the DLA payment going on to PIP has now been delayed. He is just stuck there. You know, I think there is just simply not a recognition from this government about the pain that it is inflicting on some of the most vulnerable people in our society. And it is a matter of political choices, because we are the fifth biggest economy in the world. And yet, we are a country that has four million children living in poverty. We are a country where a million food parcels were given out last year. That is just simply quite wrong. And so the Green Party is quite upfront about saying, We would make those with the broadest shoulders pay more. We would reverse the changes in corporation tax, which has allowed Theresa May to come up with this vision of the country as some kind of bargain basement tax haven. Corporations should be paying their way as well.

Trust is something that has to be earned. And as a public representative, you have to listen to the people that elected you in the first place. And you have to listen to people all over the country in all walks of life. I spend a great deal of time traveling around the country listening to people. be they chief executives, workers, people sweeping the street, or the homeless. Everybody you meet knows something you don't know. Leadership is about listening to people, trying to put their ideas into practice, and finally come to a decision in which you can take your country and our society forward. My style of leadership is actually to listen to people and try and bring consensus together in my own party and, obviously, within Parliament because, The issues we face of division in our society do have to be conquered. They're not going to be conquered by diktat, but only by listening and taking us forward that way.

And maybe it didn't, but I'm very proud of many of the reforms that we put in place. And we did try to abolish the House of Lords, and Labour refused to vote for it and to make sure that we had the time in Parliament to do it.

Well that would be a disaster for Wales if the Barnett formula was scrapped. I mean we already lose out because of the Barnett formula in terms of need. You have to recognise that in Wales, and the same is the case in Scotland, there are large rural areas, mountainous climate, you have to be able to provide public services across quite difficult areas. And that's why there's additional money in the Barnett formula. If that was taken away, then we're already disadvantaged. We would be disadvantaged even further, and it wouldn't be acceptable.

And that is under the Tories' watch. Point out the embarrassment here for Angela Rayner. We have Nigel Farage wanting to get rid of the two-child limit, and Labour is refusing to. Again, I ask, what's happened to Labour? Well, again, we've said that we'd have action on child poverty, but we will not... But you won't lift the two-child limit? But we will not... A Conservative policy that you are determined to press ahead with, even though it damages some of our poorest families?

I need a quick answer too. I need a quick answer, please. First, by absolutely being clear that I understand the challenges that you are setting out. And that's because I do something which I think is underrated in politics and leadership, which is to listen, to use these, to listen to the challenges that people are talking to me about. I go around the country the whole time. The most important thing I say to everybody I meet, please tell me the change you want if we're able to come into government. And I will mull that over in my mind on the train or in the car on the way back, because you have the right to put your fingerprints on what comes next. But just to address the specific things, we need good quality, high skilled jobs for the future for our young people. That's why I was so impressed you know, when I saw those apprentices here in Hull who had skilled jobs that they were proud of doing with a future to them. We need to make sure we're building enough houses so that young people can get on the housing ladder. At the moment, the idea that you have to be in your mid to late 30s before you get your own roof over your head it is a sign of a broken system, because I know from growing up how important it is to have your own home as a base camp for your dreams, your aspirations, for the security of your family. So we will build those 1.5 million. That will only happen if we partner with business, if we get rid of some of the planning rules that are in the way... OK, it's OK, I'm going to have to interrupt. ..and if we have a set of arrangements with mayors and combined authorities to get that building done.

Let's get Penny Morton to respond to that. So, Manchester's second only to Portsmouth, and we're just about to have both a dental school and a medical school very shortly, and good luck with your studies. Your future colleagues are currently dealing with an increase in NHS workload of about 43%. That's the legacy of the pandemic that we have to deal with. So there are many things we need to do, but there are two really important things. We have to keep the budget strong. You need a strong economy to do that, and public spending is going to go up. It will go up, and for the NHS, very substantially. The only people on this platform who have ever cut the NHS budget are Labour in Wales. They've cut it four times, and that's why their waiting lists are four times longer than they are in England. Not only can you not afford a Labour government, the NHS can't afford it either. But the second thing we also need to do is increase the number of health care professionals we have. We have been doing that. We have 70,000 more nurses, for example. But we need to retain people in the service. We've got to take the stress off people, but we also have to remove barriers to them continuing in service. OK, you do acknowledge, don't you, that the pandemic is not completely part of waiting lists? Labour's plan to tax your future pension, senior nurses and doctors, is going to get health care professionals to leave the service, that is going to lead to more waiting lists.

Let's take these in turn, because I think that is completely unjustified. Let's take Stephen Green, who was head of HSBC. He had been appointed by my predecessor, Gordon Brown, to run his business advisory council. I appointed him as a trade minister in a move that was welcomed across the political spectrum, across British industry, as someone who had run a bank responsibly. I don't know whether there was wrongdoing subsequently at HSBC. We'll have to see. But nobody criticised that appointment at the time. Did you ask him about it? I didn't ask him about that specific question, but we went through all the normal processes and procedures that you would with appointing a minister. Proper checks, including checks by the Inland Revenue into someone's tax affairs, and so it was properly dealt with. If you want to raise the issue of Jeremy Clarkson, I said very clearly I didn't know what had happened, and so I wasn't going to comment extensively. I just simply answered a question I was asked, explaining he was a friend of mine, he's a talent, and I hope it could be resolved. But I'm absolutely clear, look, treating the people you work with badly is not acceptable. The BBC have made their decision, and that's absolutely right for them. So I think the aspersion you're trying to cast I think he's completely ridiculous. What I have done for the last five years is lead a government that has got the economy growing, has got people back to work, has cut the taxes of the poorest people in our country. I'm not saying we've achieved everything we've set out to do, but the country is immeasurably stronger.

I think you are making a very important point and everybody feels it at the moment. This is a very rough time in politics. There is a lot of feeling at the moment. I think on prejudice, Islamophobia, anti-Semitism, what we are doing in our party is having an independent inquiry into anybody who is guilty of any such offence. They are out first bounce if they are guilty. When it comes to the protection, particularly of female MPs and women in politics. It is vital that we give them the security that they need and we are doing a huge amount in the House of Commons to deliver that. What we need to do is we need to come together as a country. We've been going at this for too long. And one of the things, and I'm afraid this is my key point tonight, the scratchiness of politics, the bitterness, the acrimony is a function of our inability to get Brexit done. Understood. But on this specific point of Islamophobia that we have... We have a plan to get Brexit done. We can do it and we will. And we can be out on January 31st and end what I think is misery for the country.

OK, thanks for your questions, and thanks for the way that you've put them. An incoming Labour government will invest a great deal across the UK, including 100 billion pounds will go into Scotland over 10 years. That will be a massive game changer in Scotland, where you have the worst levels of poverty and health inequality in Glasgow. You have serious levels of child poverty and housing stress. You know that as well as I do. So the question is, why will you not support it, or will you support a referendum? And, and I've said that I don't see a priority in Scotland of having an independence referendum. What I see as a priority is investing in the social and capital needs of Scotland. And so, in the early years of a Labour government, we will not be supporting an independence referendum. Instead, we'll be investing in Scotland and working with whoever the government is in Scotland to deliver that programme, deliver that improvement in in business infrastructure, in transport infrastructure and in the social needs of the people of Scotland.

That was happening under Thatcher when you were in the Tory party. You're good at being offensive, aren't you? You complain about him, and yet you're offensive to everybody. I thought you liked Margaret Thatcher. The fact is, we will close down every chemical factory, every heavy engineering plant. We've got to think about this. We cannot unilaterally go to reduce carbon ahead of everybody else without losing a huge number of jobs.

I've been on trains all the time. I don't think I've been on any more than one plane during this election campaign. I've been going round by train. And I think actually our high-speed rail network will allow people to get off the roads and also to get off domestic air flights, and I think that's incredibly important. I would say the other thing I've done, and I'm very pleased that we've managed to do this in our home in North Queensferry, is that we thought living on a hill with a huge amount of wind, not very good weather in Scotland, as you know, that a wind turbine would be the answer. In fact, we found that far better, even in this area where there's not much sun, is a solar panel. And I would recommend people, if they can, to use this form of energy because it allows us to heat our water in a way that is far more environmentally friendly. We've got the first climate change act in the world. We're due to reduce emissions substantially by 2020 and 2030. There is a lot more to be done both individually and as a community, but we've also got to get a world agreement. We've got to work, David, with Europe to do so and work with the rest of the world, and that's the way to get environmental emissions down. Thank you, David Cameron.

He's looking at the US economy, and he promised to look after the Rust Belt and working class people. And he knows that if they reduce energy bills, and they will do that by withdrawing from the Paris Agreement, then he will protect the American economy. He's putting America first. And frankly, I think Britain should put Britain first as well.

Jo Swinson. Well, this is exactly the kind of issue that does need to have cross-party working. And that has been set up in the past and then fallen apart. And the Conservative government says they want to work cross-party, but you have delayed again and again and again the social care white paper and are not bringing it forward. So it's all very well to now say you want to work cross-party on this basis, but Boris Johnson hasn't been bringing forward his plan on this.

Fair is where you bring people in when they've got jobs to come to or it's necessary for them to be working here or we need them to assist in the economy. That is fair but it's also important that we maintain the links of universities with universities across Europe. This government Is it going to go on? And Theresa May sent vans around Brent telling people to go home as referral to allow refugees and children in. There are people in this city leaving.

Well, what I will say is this, as I said earlier on, Diane Abbott wants to be Home Secretary, and she wants to wipe the records of criminals and terrorists from the DNA database. That would mean we could catch fewer criminals and fewer terrorists.

That's why there are 20,000 more people in prison as a result of the tougher sentence we've been passing. But you've got to answer this question. We will continue to match the funding of the police as of now. You are saying you're going to cut it. Now, be honest with the public, because you can't airbrush your policies, even although you can airbrush your posters.

We'll come back directly to Nicola Sturgeon. Why did you vote for £30 billion of cuts, Ed? that wasn't what the vote was for, in fact just two weeks ago we had a vote against Tory authority, your SNP MPs didn't turn up. Let me just say to you Nicola, you've got a plan to cut six billion pounds in Scotland for fiscal autonomy plans, and you need to explain what that will mean for the people of Scotland.

Well yes we've also said not only do we need that investment but we do need the reform. We need the technologies as well because our NHS is world leader and then we need to have those scanners and the new technology so that we can have the best possible care for people. That will also bring around with that reform efficiencies in the way that people work. So we're open to that to make sure that people get the support that they need but we've got to fix the social care crisis as well.

This election really matters for all of us across the UK. For Scotland, now more than ever, we need strong voices at Westminster. We need MPs able to stand up for Scotland and work with others for fair policies across the UK. The SNP will always stand up against Tory cuts because they are damaging our public services and pushing more people into poverty. We will stand up for jobs and against an extreme Brexit that will put living standards and jobs at risk. And we will make sure that Scotland's future is always in Scotland's hands. The next few years will determine the kind of country we become. We need strong opposition, holding a Tory government to account, keeping them in check and standing up for the values we hold dear. Values of social justice, tolerance and community. The more seats the SNP wins on June the 8th, the stronger Scotland's voice will be. Nicola Sturgeon, thank you.
But because they actually had the Attorney General there in person. And therefore, in a sense, what was important was that he was able to take them through the reasoning in person and say to them...

Well, I want to talk about the positive things that would happen for people's lives. I think that if you were to insulate every single home in Britain, you would create around a million green jobs, you would get people's fuel bills down so people weren't dying of fuel poverty in the 21st century, and you would also tackle climate emissions. So the real price here is that tackling the climate crisis isn't about hair shirts, it is about a better quality of life as well.

People across the UK are looking for an alternative to the grey, stale politics as usual. And people are seeing through the austerity myth. They told us that slashing services would deliver recovery for all. It has not. Plaid Cymru has a vision for a post-austerity society. Our MPs will work with progressives from other parties to deliver prosperity, investment and job creation, public services and strong communities. Cuts are a choice. Austerity and the old politics are not inevitable. Plaid Cymru will not apologise for speaking up for Wales at every opportunity. And we will work for a new politics, co-operating with others for the good of all.

Thank you. Natalie Bennett. I think we were talking about education, so perhaps we can go back there. And to come back to the point about education, and particularly the point that David Cameron raised about free schools, we have a system brought in, supported with the academies of the former government, that were based on competition. The idea is that schools compete with each other. They fight against each other. The Green Party, we don't believe that should be the foundation of schooling. We should have cooperation. We want to bring free schools and academies back under local authority control, have an overall cooperative system. And much more than that, we need a school system that isn't focused on exams, that isn't an exam factory that shoves children through exam after exam after exam. Children need an education for life and that means a much broader education, an education that includes things like first aid, cooking, sex and relationship education, personal finance education. We need a much broader education that prepares our young people for life. Thank you very much indeed. Leonwood.

This £2,000, he keeps saying it's going to cost, is absolute garbage. We won't go into the details of it. But Clare, let me address your point, because this is really important. A transition is coming that's very important when it comes, for example, to energy, a transition to renewables. I don't think we should just see that as a challenge. It's a huge opportunity, because if we go to renewable energy, that means cheaper bills. Renewables are cheaper, not just for the short term, but for the long term. It gives us energy security, so it means that Putin can't put his boot on our throat and the result you've all been feeling for the last two years. But it also gives us the next generation of jobs. Other countries have realised renewables is the future. They're in the race. I want the UK to be in the race. I want us to win the race. When it comes to hydrogen... offshore wind, solar, nuclear. Thank you. The question I ask is why not... Thank you very much.

No, these were some proposals that were produced that I rejected then, not least because... But you asked for them. No, I didn't ask for them. They were proposals that were... Where did they come from? In government, people produced... Look, you've got to remember, step back a second, what we inherited. I became Prime Minister at a time when there was no money left. And I bring this with me everywhere, the note that the Treasury Minister left, and it's... There it is. Dear Chief Secretary, I'm afraid there is no money. That is the situation I inherited. So we have had to make difficult decisions. over these last five years and I accept not every one of the decision has been easy for people but the truth is five years on the deficit is halved, two million people are back in work, the economy is growing faster than any major economy in the Western world and I think what this election is about is do we build on that foundation and build a really strong country where you can get a job, you can keep more of your own money to spend as you choose, you get that apprenticeship, we can build houses for people to buy and own, or do we go right back to the start with Ed Miliband potentially propped up by the SNP who want even more borrowing, even more spending, even more debt, all the things that landed us in that mess in the first place. I never want us to go back there again.
You haven't. You promised an employment bill.

We didn't build enough homes. The last Labour government didn't build enough homes. But I think Nigel said something very revealing. Because he basically said what Mrs Thatcher used to say, you can't buck the market. Well, the problem about the market in housing, Nigel, is it doesn't work. It's not working. It's not working because large developers have a stranglehold on the market, and I'm going to do something about it, and you're not. It's not working because we're not building enough homes in our country. And therefore, yeah, you're right, it is an issue of demand and supply. There's not enough supply in our country, and that's what we've got to do. The reality is we haven't done it for a generation and the next Labour government will get to grips with it because it is one of the biggest priorities our country faces and we are letting down our young people and we are letting down our families.

Well, firstly, we would scrap HS2, which would save £50 billion immediately. We would also scrap the Barner formula, which would give fairness to England, and that would save an extra £5 billion. But that's taking money away from Scotland and Wales, incidentally. Well, actually... You know, in Scotland, you're getting £1,700 extra per head than the English. It's all built on English taxpayers' money. That's why you have free prescriptions. Scottish people pay taxes as well. That's why you have free tuition fees. And that's why you have no hospital guard out to charge you. We're paying for it. So the simple fact is there would also be an EU dividend when we leave the European Union. 350 billion per week. Where's your bus?

I'm going to give you a stat. Let me give you a stat. Let me... because it's a really important point and I respect it. Under the last Labour government, we were returning people to their country of origin. Of course it's difficult, of course you've got to establish where they came from, but you can return people to their country of origin in very many cases. The number being returned has gone down 44% and we need to address that. But the most important thing, in my view, is stopping people coming in the first place. We've got to stop this trade across the Channel. We've talked about smashing the gates. No, I know. That's why we need to work with other... When we had terrorists... I mean, take your point, sir, completely. Yeah. When we had terrorist gangs operating, they too were operating across European countries. So we worked with other countries in Europe, we shared intelligence, we shared data. I've been in the room when we've done this.

I get this pious stance from Ed Miliband. This is the man who was part of a government that said no boom and bust in the economy and crashed our economy, jeopardising the future generations and life chances of millions of people in this country. I have apologised, I have taken responsibility for the mistakes I have made. Why don't you, in front of the British people, Ed Miliband, apologise for your role in We said, no, no, don't say nothing euphemistic. Say, I'm sorry for crashing the British economy.

I and the SNP and people before me and the SNP have argued against the common fisheries policy. We've argued for it to be scrapped or fundamentally reformed. It's the Tories, time after time after time, that have sold out Scottish fishermen. And believe me, they're shaping up to do it all over again.
Parliament

What we want to do is bring the national grid into public ownership to ensure that you can feed into it as well as out of it in all places and invest in it for the future. Gas is an important part of the energy basis at the moment. Yes, it may well decline over time but it's there at the moment and if you want to change it and also introduce a greater degree of biogas then it obviously helps. If the grid itself is in public ownership which can ensure that distribution and that supply system.

None? I think there should be what is necessary in order to save and protect life. I want to live in a society that is safe and secure. Ideally, nobody wants to see armed police officers anywhere. The reality is, at the moment, they are there, and they have been necessary. Just a quick comment, Callum.

We're always serious on this show. Look, you're going to have to make up your own mind about that. Look, there's no point in me sitting here and saying, oh, yes, I am a good leader or I'm not. You're going to have to decide who you want to lead this country. And on May the 6th, you're either going to have Michael Howard walk into number 10 Downing Street or me. And if you want to keep the economy strong and the investment in our public services there and the measures to help families in this country that we've introduced in the past few years, you should vote for the leadership that's delivered. Hang on a second.

Well, I mean, Jeremy Corbyn also stood here and said that he would be neutral in that referendum, so I don't think that we, and the millions of people who want to remain in this country, can trust Jeremy Corbyn on Brexit. But the other exchange that you had, which I thought was a very powerful exchange, was about what's happened in the Labour Party under Jeremy Corbyn in terms of anti-Semitism, that they are being investigated by the Equality and Human Rights Commission. And he said he was happy about that. I don't think a party leader should be happy about that. I think you should be ashamed about that. And I just don't think that we can ignore the genuine fear that lots of people feel about Jeremy Corbyn being Prime Minister. So when it comes to working collaboratively, absolutely. And what does that mean? Let's just look at what we've done over the last couple of years, right? We are here, it is November. we are still members of the European Union. And we were supposed to leave on the 29th of March, and we were supposed to leave on the 31st of October, and for people that wanted to leave, if that was you, then I appreciate you're going to be frustrated by that, but as a party that wanted to stop Brexit, we have worked collaboratively to make that happen. We've worked with people in the Labour Party, in the Conservative Party, in the SNP, in Plaid Cymru and the Greens. We have worked week in, week out, and we have stopped Brexit twice Just imagine if you elect more Liberal Democrat MPs what we will be able to achieve and we'll be able to stop Brexit for good.

Boris Johnson, with great respect, Mr Corbyn, throughout his career, has consistently opposed longer sentences. When you look at MI5, the body that keeps us safe, particularly from terror attacks, he is proposing to put in a Home Secretary in the form of Diane Abbott, who has called for MI5 to be disbanded. Mr Corbyn, with great respect to him, when you look at the huge issues of safety on public streets, knife crime. You have a Labour Party that is adamantly sceptical, opposed to stop and search, which I believe is an integral part of fighting crime on our streets. We're not only going to put 20,000 more officers on the streets of London, we're going to fight the streets of this country, we're going to fight crime in the way that I did in London, but we're going to back the police up. Let's get your response to that.

Boris Johnson's Tories are unfit for office, in my opinion. But unless people vote to keep them out, we could be stuck with them and their policies for years to come, with dire consequences for our economy and public services, especially the NHS. In Scotland, the SNP is the main challenger in every single Tory-held seat. So I vote for the SNP on December the 12th as I vote to lock Boris Johnson out of Downing Street, provide an escape route from the mess of Brexit and to put Scotland's future into Scotland's hands so that we can build a better, fairer country.

Adam Price. OK, what could we do immediately? We could pass a Clean Air Act. The Welsh Labour government could do it here. We would take them to court because the 2,000 people, 200 in this city, that die unnecessarily because of pollution in air. We could do that now. Your party could do that now, Rebecca. We are going to bring in a clean air act. Yeah, but you're saying you're doing England. You're in government here in Wales. People will judge you, not by your words, but your deeds. You're not doing it here. We could actually massively improve the level of procurement of local food. You know, our hospitals, our schools that are buying imported food, when we could actually be supporting local food systems, helping our environment and our economy as well.

And thankfully, of course, Nick, in relation to your direct question, I remember the figures correctly. Serious knife crime in Scotland is comparatively low compared with many other areas. Now that's not to be complacent, because as a dad, I share your concerns. I worry about what the future holds for my kids. But ultimately the solution lies in what Reem has said. What we need to do is make sure that we are investing in the areas that need support the most. Poverty is a major driver behind this and Westminster has failed generations of young people by not providing them with the hope for a better future. So they turn to violence when they perhaps shouldn't. And I think that's something we should all reflect upon going into this general election, where £18 billion of public sector cuts are coming down the line and the future Labour government has no answers.
Stop peddling hate in our country.

I'm going into this election with a very clear message. None of us can afford more austerity. Our NHS, our children, our economy can't afford the billions of pounds of additional cuts planned by Labour, the Liberals and the Tories. And none of us can afford the £100 billion they plan to spend on new nuclear weapons. Their priorities are wrong and it will be ordinary people across the UK who pay the price. unless you choose differently. We've seen tonight that Labour alone will not be bold enough to deliver the real progressive change that people need. A big team of SNP MPs can force the pace of progressive change. We offer an alternative, real investment in the NHS and housing and childcare and jobs. To voters in Scotland, I ask you to vote SNP to make Scotland's voice heard. and to voters elsewhere, I pledge that ours will be a voice to deliver real change for you too.

We should train nurses and doctors, shalln't we? I am proud to stand up for free movement. I'm proud to stand up for the free movement we already have and I'm proud to stand up for the principle too, that the thought of an Australian style point system strikes fear into my heart because I know what it's like in Australia for immigrants.

Stop and search. Stop and search. We know the areas in which knives are most prevalent. Stop and search. And, of course, if we don't do it, oh, gosh, if this area has got a high proportion of people from the black and ethnic minority communities, they might call us racist. We've got to stop doing this. We've got to completely forget the colour of people's skins, treat everybody equally, but we must do stop and search. We must do it in a very, very tough way. We have to have proper sentences. And it's but one area, isn't it? I mean, you can go shoplifting now, any of you. You can go out and nick up to 200 quids worth without being prosecuted. We are seeing a decline, a societal decline of law and order in this country, and frankly, government and the police forces have been too scared to do what needs to be done. It's yet another area of Britain that is broken and needs radical surgery.

The point is about the cohesion of our... What is it? The point is about the cohesion of our society. I hope the Prime Minister understands the hurt that people feel when they hear remarks and articles that he's written in the past. I hope he will regret those, and I hope he will understand the importance of all of us using respectful language of all faiths, all religions and all languages in what is a multicultural society which I'm proud to live in.

Well, there has to be a baseline of production of electricity from some totally sustainable source. Otherwise, if you rely solely on wind and solar and wave, it is obviously... could now be like new nuclear power. No, what I'm saying is that when we move into government, if any of us moved into government, we're inheriting a mixed base. I want that to go to 80% or 90% of renewables over time, and that is the direction in which we want to go.

Well, again, I would say to Michael that, again, I would be agreeing with you and against the Pope in terms of, for instance, the need to make advances in science. I don't think we should. Obviously, you need proper protections and proper rules, and we debated them at great length in the House of Commons, but I do want to see that go ahead. also on the issue of abortion. So there are lots of areas I don't agree but I don't think that means you should stop someone visiting our country or condemn them. We must try and build an open and tolerant country where we respect people for their different faiths, we bring faiths closer together with each other and we're prepared to have an open and frank discussion about these things. I think that's the only way to go and it's nice to hear that pretty much actually we all agree about that.

I've been your Prime Minister for the last five years, and all that time, I've tried to have one task in mind above all others, and that has been turning our economy around, putting the country back to work and clearing up the mess that was left to us. I want to stand for another five years because I want us to finish the job that we've all started. We've created two million jobs. Let's create a job for everyone who wants and needs one. We've cut the deficit in half. Let's clear it all together and have Britain back in the black. We've invested in our National Health Service. Let's keep doing that and make sure it's a genuine service, seven days a week, for you and your family all year round. What my plan is about is basically one word – security. Security for you, for your family, for our country. This is an amazing country and we're on our way back. And there's a fundamental choice at this election. Stick with the plan and with the team who brought that plan, because it's working and it's helping. Or put it all at risk by the people who gave us the spending, the debt, the taxes and the waste. I say, let's stick to the plan that's working. Let's not go back to square one. Let's finish what we started.

You've got £18 billion of cuts up your sleeve. The Scottish Government already receive much more generous funding than England and Wales because of the Barnett formula. And as I say, actually police numbers in Scotland have recently gone down. We have now hit record ever numbers across England and Wales, more in fact, as Nick knows, more than there were in 2010 by about 3,000.

I've got to deal with negotiations over Iran and we've got the problem over North Korea. If countries unilaterally decide to have nuclear weapons and break the non-proliferation treaty, then we need multilateral action with all of us working together. Now, we are nuclear weapon states. We can make a huge difference in the reduction of nuclear weapons overall. If we can persuade countries not to have nuclear weapons in the first place, or force them not to have them, if we can then have a reduction in nuclear weapons all round, but I don't favour Nick's proposal, which would unilaterally abandon our nuclear deterrent when we know that Iran and North Korea and other countries are trying to get there.

Let's try and disentangle some of these claims and counter-claims. Of course, Gordon Brown is right to say that the Conservatives, I don't know why they want to cut the schools building project programme. That's a silly thing to do. We need to continue to invest in our schools building. But equally, for Gordon Brown to constantly make these claims about tax credits, I come back to a simple thing. When money is tight, surely it's not right that someone on my salary, an MP's salary, could be entitled to tax credits. Why not focus the money on where it's really needed, and also use the money that we can save elsewhere, as I explained to Michael, to invest in those individual children who need that individual care. It could be Saturday morning classes, evening classes, one-to-one tuition, the smaller class sizes, all the things that I know as a parent and Michael no doubt knows as a teacher makes the most dramatic difference to a child's education.

I mean, that is something I will take away and give consideration to. We do try to consult patient groups and consult organisations that work with patient groups before formulating policy in any area, but it's particularly important in health matters, and I think probably particularly important when it comes to mental health. We're investing a lot to try and improve mental health services just now. One of the good things I think we should all be positive about is that more and more people come forward for mental health help now because the stigma around it is reducing, and that's something that is good, but it puts more demand on services. So we've got a real obligation to invest, but I'll certainly take your suggestion about looking at how we formalise the patient input.

I have not supported any of those organisations. What I've said is, and I'm sure you'd probably agree with it, that if you're to bring about a peace process anywhere, be it in the Middle East, be it in Ireland, be it in a different situation in, say, Colombia or anywhere else, there has to be a coming together at some point.
I think that we need to play our role alongside other richer countries around the world in being a place that people can go to and tackle the root causes of why people are seeking asylum.

No, that's not how they do it. That's not how they do it. They say, one person says x pounds a week. Someone says, I'll give you x plus. £10, £20, £30. They then go back to the first person and say, I've already had a better offer for X plus this. So you get this bidding war going on until the rents go through the roof. So you're saying they have to take the first offer. Is that what you're saying for private landlords? No, we can't have this bidding war like this.

the question is right, how can anybody believe these promises on cutting the deficit? Because this coalition was put together to reduce the annual deficit to zero. That's why these two guys got together. It is still running at 90 billion sterling every year. More remarkably, and what no-one talks about, is the national debt which has been going on for hundreds of years and in this five years the national debt has doubled from £850 billion to £1.5 trillion. We need to make cuts and there are some places we can start. We could easily cut £10 billion a year from the foreign aid budget. We could save another £10 billion a year by not paying over money to Brussels every single day. We could end vanity projects like HS2, that will only benefit a tiny number of people, thus saving, by the end of this Parliament, £4 billion a year. And we need to revisit the Barnett formula, because, frankly, English and Welsh taxpayers are getting a rotten deal, and we could save £5 billion a year in doing that. There's a plan and a promise that could be kept.
Angela Rayner. I won't lie and I'll call out the people that do. Robert Jenrick.

...for when, it is a yes. You've got to try that when people are incarcerated quite rightly, when they come out, they don't become part of the dismal reoffending rates, and that means trying to make them more responsible members of society, and if giving them a sense of participation in the political process helps that, I'm not against it. But I do say when I make the decisions along with colleagues about what we put in our manifesto for the next parliament, of course I don't include each and every item for the last four years that's been passed by the Liberal Democrats or we'd publish war and peace. So I take the things that really matter and that's...

It really is a threat to the NHS from Brexit and from Trump. And our manifesto commits completely to refunding it and also reversing privatisation, as Caroline Lucas put forwards in her bill. But the really key thing that we would do is prevention. I talked before about some of the side effects of our Green New Deal investment, being warmer homes, healthier people, cleaner air. But also, we want to bring in more social care investment, a universal basic income, the next step in social security, the best thing we can do that follows the founding principles of the welfare state. When the NHS was set up, it was sent round to everybody, a leaflet that said, this is not a charity, you're all paying for it as taxpayers and it will relieve your money worries in times of illness. If you're talking about mental health, we need that universal basic income to relieve people's money worries throughout their lives. That's the big idea in our manifesto.

But Ed, you have an opportunity to vote against zero-hour contracts and turn down that opportunity in Wales. They voted against a place and re-amendment to end zero-hour contracts in the care sector. So why should people believe what you say on zero-hour contracts?
Well, council taxes is too high for many people. It's been going up. It went up again this year. But that's because the government lost control of the economy.

What I would say to what the Prime Minister's just said is that if you look at the Labour record over the last 13 years, they did very much hitch the whole fortunes of the economy to the City of London. And we got into a situation where we ended up with the whole economy having to serve the banks rather than the other way around. I assume the banker we've just been told about was probably Fred Goddwin, so-called "Fred the Shred". It was actually this Government that gave this man a Knighthood for services to banking. He not only broke his own bank, he very nearly brought down the whole economy. So there are big, big lessons to learn, and I think the most important is that we put the Bank of England back in charge of regulating the banks, and give them the specific duty of calling time on debt in the economy. Things got completely out of control, the banks were regulated but badly and by the wrong organisation. And the one party that wants to scrap the current system and put the Bank of England back in charge and change things is the Conservatives.

I listened very carefully, as I always do, to Mr Corbyn. I didn't hear him say he was going to rule out a referendum on Scotland. Did you? We're ruling out a referendum. It's perfectly clear that the price of Nicola Sturgeon's support for the chaotic coalition that he would comprise is a second referendum.

Mr Corbyn. The problem is that the A&E performance is now at its worst ever. There are 4 million people waiting for operations. There are all the vacancies that have not been filled within the NHS. The Health and Social Care Act allows the privatisation of services and indeed we have the nonsense of our National Health Service being taken to court by private operators because they didn't get contracts from it. Let's end the privatisation within the NHS and instead have a fully funded NHS for all people.

There's a lot to this job, and as you saw yesterday, I don't get all of it right, but I do know how to run the economy in good times and in bad. When the banks collapsed, I took immediate action to stop crisis becoming calamity and to stop a recession becoming a depression. As a result of that, Britain is now on the road to recovery. But as we meet tonight, economies in Europe are in peril, and there is a risk of dragging us into recession. So I'm determined that nothing will happen in Britain that will put us back in that position, and I want to set out my plan, and why this year is so important. Support the economy now, and you will ensure that there are jobs and a recovery, and ensure that we can have the resources for deficit reduction. Shrink the economy now, as the Conservatives would do, and they risk your jobs, living standards and tax credits. So it's not my future that matters, it's your future that's on the ballot paper next Thursday. And I'm the one to fight for your future.
It's a fact. It's dangerous. It divides communities. Well, it's true. And it creates stigma to people who are ill. And I think you ought to be ashamed of yourself. Well, I'm sorry.

Well, I've used the NHS a lot as an emergency patient. through my life and it can be fantastic and brilliant. But let's stop the lies, shall we? Let's stop that everyone wants to privatise it, Donald Trump wants to buy it. He's not going to buy it, Richard. Nobody else wants to privatise it. Actually, we all want the same thing. I know your party gave us PFI deals, but if we all want the same thing, could this possibly be one issue that shouldn't be a political football and where we could perhaps work together?

I agree with Nick. There's got to be a right of recall for people who are in a constituency and find their MPs corrupt and Parliament doesn't act. I agree with that. I think Nick also agrees with me about a new House of Commons and a new House of Lords properly accountable with a new system of election that will be put to a referendum next year. But David's solution, just to cut the number of MPs, Cut the number of MPs. All of us represent a constituency of people. All of us represent communities. All of us represent neighbourhoods and localities which deserve to have their local representation. I would cut one thing. I would cut the numbers of the House of Lords, and not by 10%, but by 50%. A smaller House of Lords, directly accountable. And David, please, no more hereditary peers.

Just look at what's happened. I mean, since we started putting in place green policies, and this country's done it worse than any other country, we've finished up with electricity prices 60% higher than most of our European competitors. So no wonder our aluminium supply has dropped. I challenge the idea that we've... No wonder. No wonder. We've got more policies in place.
We are, no, we have said that we are against in principle...

But Nick, wind turbines and the development of the offshore wind industry is one of our priorities. We're now the world's leading offshore wind power, and there are four companies, including Siemens, which announced that they'll invest in wind power in this country as a result of government incentives. We're doing the same for digital, because we want 100% superfast broadband in this country that will serve every community, including rural areas. But you have to have some government finance to persuade people it's necessary to go to 100% and not 70%. Biotechnology - we're investing substantial sums in the leading cancer and research centre for the whole of Europe that will be in Britain as a result of the investment we're making, but I come back to this point: if you cut investment allowances, David, if you cut the regional development agencies which are a symbol of what the regions can do themselves, if you do that to cut corporation tax for banks, you're putting manufacturing industry at risk and doing the opposite of what is needed now.

David Cameron says you were a great team and now you're all slugging each other off. He keeps talking about darkened rooms as does Ed Miliband. I have to say to you if either of them still think they're going to win a majority they need to go and lie down in that darkened room. The point I'd like to make to you and to everybody is this, is that you are the boss, right? We are your servants. You give us, through the way you vote next Thursday, our marching instructions. And last time, the marching instructions were very, very clear. The only way we could create a stable government at a time of an economic firestorm which could have engulfed this country, we could have been the next domino to fall after Greece and Portugal and Spain, was the Conservatives and the Liberal Democrats.

Yes, well, the good news is that this Tory government is coming to an end. And thanks to Rishi Sunak, that date is coming sooner than many people had predicted. So the choice for voters in Scotland is now very simple. Who do they trust to put Scotland's interests first? at Westminster. For the SNP, we are very clear about what it is that we believe in. We believe in investment in our NHS, we believe in action on the cost of living crisis, we believe in rejoining the European single market, on ensuring that we create economic growth by delivering net zero. Within the Palace of Westminster, those policies are not particularly popular and they are not shared by the Labour Party. But we're not here to cosy up to the Westminster status quo.
