We're here to deliver for the people of Scotland and to ensure that they have a say over their own future. So on the 4th of July, I would urge people to vote for what they believe in, to vote SNP to put Scotland's interests first.

Well, yeah, I think farmers are very, very keenly engaged with this question. And indeed, of course, actually, there's a lot of evidence of the positive benefit of farming because, look, the ecology of much of Wales has grown up with farming. And indeed, that's why the RSPB, for example, has grazing animals on its land.

But, but do remember please, and you take Cardiff Central as an example of this. An awful lot of students there, an awful lot of students are going to benefit by our proposals for scrapping top up fees and tuition fees.

I want to set up Great British Energy. This is a publicly owned company that will generate energy, that will have investment alongside it. It'll be owned by the taxpayer, making money for the taxpayer, and drive us to renewables so that we can have lower bills, secure energy and the jobs of the future. I also think that we should insulate our houses because I think people are paying far too much in their bills. And that was a project that was started 10 years ago and cancelled under the Conservatives when they said cut the green crap. We've lost 10 years. Get on with it and get your bills down. Because people are fed up with energy bills that have gone through the roof.

Tonight I am here to debate the future of our country. The question in this election is whether we want a country for the many or just a few. The state of our NHS, our children's schools, social care for older people, our young people saddled with debt, none of that is remotely strong or stable. And now the Conservatives want five more years of cuts to our vital public services to fund tax handouts for the wealthy few. Labour will make very different choices. There will be no tax rises for 95% of taxpayers. But we will ask those with the most to contribute a bit more so no one is held back from achieving their potential. On June the 8th, you have a choice. More cuts in services and living standards with the Conservatives or vote Labour to transform Britain for the many, not the few.
The Tory government's forever by backing Scotland's independence. That's what we should do.

Let me answer that question directly. My priority is not to have an in-out referendum, and let me explain why. Because my priority as Prime Minister is to tackle the cost-of-living crisis, sort out our NHS and build a future for our young people. And look, the British people have a decision to make tonight and at the general election. David Cameron wants to spend the next two years deciding whether to exit the European Union, something he doesn't even want to do. He says he wants to stay in the European Union. So, look, I say there's better priorities for our country and they'll be my priorities as Prime Minister.

What about skilling up your own people? We have a great success here, says the Conservative representative on the panel, by cutting the number of visas to people accompanying workers. There has been a 76% drop in visa applications for social care because you have taken that decision. Now, in social care, I don't know who you want to look after you when you're in your old age, but we'll need people to work. well as training. Let Crystal answer that.

I've made it very clear we accept the results of the referendum and I think it's important to go from that point. I've made the point also about the need for trade access to the European Union and there is of course an interest in that on both sides of the channel. Most of our big manufacturing companies have supply chains here and in Europe and vice versa. There is a mutual interest in this. But we're not approaching these negotiations by threatening Europe with setting up some kind of low-tax haven for big corporations in this country. We're instead saying we want to continue that trading relationship outside the European Union, but I think a sensible relationship with them is very important. And I will approach those negotiations to build up a trust which gives us that sensible relationship in the future.

I'd like to know what kinds of jobs all these extra people are in because we know that the numbers of people on zero hours contracts has gone through the roof. My party Plaid Cymru has tried in Wales on seven different occasions to abolish zero hours contracts only to be voted down by the Labour government in Wales but it's It is a scandal that those people, especially in the public sector, who are on the highest wages get paid pay increases. Politicians have had pay increases, yet those at the bottom have been squeezed. And it's immigrants then who are scapegoated and blamed for those wages being squeezed, when it's Tory austerity that is the real cause of that problem.

Well, President Trump is doing what he set out to do. I mean, he is the leader of the free world, whether we like it or not. He said during his election campaign that he would withdraw from the Paris Agreement. What he's doing is he's looking at India and he's looking at China, who've got huge coal-fired power station building programs, which are taking place at this present moment in time.

The issue of your views on rights and freedoms in our society, I absolutely defend the right of free speech. I supported the introduction of the Human Rights Act. I support the Convention on Human Rights. Freedoms and rights in a democracy are very, very important. I spent my life getting into hot water for defending people because I believe their human rights should not be violated. And that's the kind of government that I intend to lead that does guarantee democracy.

I freely acknowledge that. But, Fiona, this is an important point, right? Inflation yesterday hit 2%. When I became Prime Minister, it was 11%. Now, that is something that I said I would do, and we've done it. Now, these things don't happen overnight. They don't happen in a straight line. But if you have a plan and you stick to it, you can get the results. You've seen that on inflation. You've seen me stick to a plan. And yes, during that period, it popped up a couple of times, and everyone said the same. And I said, if we stick to it, we will get it there. We did. And on waiting lists, we haven't made as much progress as I would have liked. But because of the things we are doing, What you have seen over this year is waiting lists are now starting to come down. There is still a way to go, of course there is, but we will stick to it and I will get it done.

It's a zero-hours contract. Thank you, Ed Miliband. It's necessary for our economy to succeed. That's what you're saying, David. The zero-jobs approach that we got under Labour. Never mind the soundbites, David. You're defending zero-hours contracts. Thank you, Ed Miliband. And yes, there is a big choice. There is a big choice in this general election. He thinks that as long as a few corporations and individuals do well, the richest and most powerful, the wealth will trickle down to everyone else. Well, he tried that experiment over the last five years and it failed.

What we say is, we don't know what the final deal is going to be. And who is it, once we know what the final deal is, who is it who should decide whether that is an acceptable deal or not? Should it be politicians or should it be you? And we think you should decide. We think that we should stay in the single market because that's going to be best for the economy, it's going to be best for jobs, and it's going to be, if you want to go and Move to Europe. If you want to go and work there, if you want to fall in love with somebody from the European Union, then you have got to have free movement. And that's what we stand for.

and the Green Party the party that's ready to be honest about the need for investment because Ishana you're quite right to ask the question and thank you for stepping into this important vocation. The NHS has been chronically underfunded for decades. It's on its knees. This cannot be allowed to continue. Most of us agree about that. But the answer is not, as the Conservatives and, surprisingly, Labour think, to invite big multinational companies further into our national health service. The answer is investment and protecting our NHS from privatisation. And the money comes from? Well, we announced our health and social care policy just yesterday, and that included a £30 billion increase in funding for our health services, £20 billion on social care and it's really important that we understand how closely those are linked because if we have better social care it takes the pressure off the NHS and a one-off £20 billion capital investment. These are large sums, they come from where? They come from reforming the tax system because currently the UK tax system is unbelievably unfair. It puts more onus on lower-income working people to contribute to the Treasury than it does towards the super-rich, the millionaires, the multi-millionaires and the billionaires. And so the Greens would make some adjustments to that system so that those with the broader shoulders who can most easily afford to pay quite a modest amount, and that will provide plenty of funding for decent investment in public services that benefit all of us.

What Jeremy Hunt was talking about and what we referred to in our manifesto was a specific proposal. This proposal was put forward by Andrew Dilnot a few years ago when he was asked by government to look at the social care issue. And if I just explain why we rejected that Dilnot proposal, Jeremy, it was because for two reasons. First of all, because it was going to be paid for out of general taxation. And secondly, because that Deal Not proposal protected wealthier pensioners, but did nothing to protect pensioners on modest incomes. Now the proposals I've put forward actually do that. They do help pensioners on modest incomes. And crucially, they ensure we have a long-term sustainable solution for social care.

This great country of ours, it's being so badly run at the moment. Everyone knows that we're all feeling worse off. It's harder to see a GP or get an operation. And our immigration system, legal and illegal, is completely and utterly out of control. It requires bold solutions to these serious problems and it's only Reform UK that has got these bold plans. Imagine not paying any income tax below £20,000. Imagine zero waiting lists within two years and keeping them there. We've got plans to achieve this and do this. And imagine freezing immigration so the country can catch up, that things can work properly. And imagine stopping the boats, stopping the deaths. We've got those bold plans, but it requires real successful people, achievers, doers, to fix the crisis, the problems this country's got. And if you're not happy with the status quo, if you want change, you've got to vote for change. You've got to vote Reform UK on July 4th.

No, it's not about that. We have a set of rules for people from outside the European Union. We will look at the requirements for... One of the things we do is ensure that it's possible for people to be brought in where there are skill shortages, where there is a need for people to come to work.

I don't regret saying that. that. First of all, I think it was right, with France and America, to stop Colonel Gaddafi when he was going to butcher his own people in Benghazi. If we had not stepped in, if I hadn't ordered those aeroplanes into the sky, we would have seen a massive catastrophe in Benghazi of people butchered. It was the right thing to do. I don't accept that we left the Libyan people after that. We put in aid, we put in military training, we put in political assistance. It just hasn't been possible to date to get the different parties in Libya... Christians are being beheaded on the beach. Well, it hasn't been possible to get the different Libyan parts of government together, to get the warlords to put down their weapons, but we're still trying, even now, with people out there, trying to bring that about. But it has been, I accept, a very difficult situation.

Let the Prime Minister answer. Look, I'm not going to make any excuses for the experience that you've had. That's why I think it is so important that we actually, we do deal with mental health. Now both of you have raised two different issues. One is how we deal with mental health issues in the health service and the other is actually the work capability assessment. And this is something where we are, we do look at improving how that assessment is taking place. But I know that the issue of mental health is a particularly difficult one to address in terms of those work capability assessments. On the National Health Service and the other things we want to do with mental health, one of the things I want to do is ensure that there is better support in schools so that we have individual members of staff trained in schools who are able to better identify mental health problems and then know how to address them. I was talking to a young woman only at the turn of the year who, in school, said nobody had really known what to be able to do because of her mental health problems. So she'd suffered as a result. If we can give people the support they need at an earlier stage, it's better for everybody.

As it happens, I agree - I think we all must agree on the points about investing in new technologies, investing in our young people. I was at a college here in Birmingham today, young people developing vocational qualifications which we need for the skills of the future. I think that - it's a good thing that basically, all the parties agree on that. But I come back to this point I made to Jean earlier: unless you've got banks helping businesses, it is extremely difficult for them to expand their products, invest in their factories, and actually invest in creating new jobs. Who would have thought, here in Birmingham of all places, that a bank that you own - RBS - should have been involved in lending money - money that you've given to them in the bail-out, the taxpayer bail-out - should have used that money to help fund Kraft, the American multinational's, takeover of Cadbury's, leading to job losss in Britain. When you lent that money to the banks, did you think that money would be used to put people out of work in Britain? No, and it was wrong.

Some of the richest and most prosperous countries in the world are countries of a similar size to Scotland, but if that is your view, that is a choice you would make. That is my point, it is a choice for people. But in this election on Thursday, I think we have a more immediate opportunity as a country to make our voice heard more loudly in the Brexit negotiations, to make sure that our interests are not ignored as Theresa May it is Theresa May, takes those negotiations forward. Somebody mentioned freedom of movement earlier on. Immigration is a really difficult thing for politicians to talk about because people have concerns. But one of the biggest challenges facing Scotland right now is the need to grow our working age population. If we have the Conservatives putting more and more restrictions on the ability of the best and brightest from around Europe and the world to come here, that will be damaging for our economy, not just now, but for decades to come. And I don't think we should simply accept that.

Our choice is very simple. We can get Brexit done, or we can spend another groundhog year with another referendum when Mr Corbyn, you've heard tonight, cannot answer the fundamental question. Is he for remain or leave? And what price would he pay to secure Nicola Sturgeon's support to enter Number 10? And if he can't answer those questions tonight, I don't think he's fit to lead our country. Let's end the dither and delay the deadlock and the divisions. And if we have a working majority, a conservative majority, I pledge we will have a parliament that works for you, that focuses on the NHS and the cost of living. Because when we get Brexit done by January the 31st, we will go forward as a united and confident nation that has shown our faith in the judgment of our people.

Let Mr Cameron answer your point, Mr Brown. It's answer time, David. What matters is what comes out. I went to Hull police station the other day They had five different police cars, and they were just about to buy a £73,000 Lexus. There's money that could be saved to get the police on the front line. The Metropolitan Police have 400 uniformed officers in their Human Resources Department. Well, our police officers should be crime fighters, not form thinners, and that's what that needs to change.

That's the point also to Nick Clegg. I would suggest from this discussion we need three things to safeguard the NHS, which was Terry's original question. First, money, the £8 billion. My party has a plan to do so. Secondly, give mental health the same importance and emphasis in the NHS as traditionally given to physical health. And thirdly, make sure that where people, particularly elderly patients, are discharged from hospital, the social care system has a place for them to go. If we do those three things, we will protect the NHS for the future.

The industrial strategy is key, and as we leave the European Union, those jobs that we currently have, and some of them are not as well paid as they should be, are at risk. And that's why Plaid Cymru's positive post-Brexit plan, which safeguards those jobs that are currently reliant upon the EU, but also creates the conditions whereby we can develop better paid jobs over the long term. And there are great opportunities in the fields of renewable energy and providing those services that people need. We've lost actually lots of public services and I'm very cherished public servants and a proper industrial strategy could make sure that we take people's full potential and those people can be brought back and make a contribution to our society.

I am, because research from the Runnymede Trust has shown that black people are 18 times more likely to be stopped with stop-and-search than people who are not black. So it's really clear. The way that stop-and-search works, whether it's intended to be or not, is racist, and the Green Party would stop the routine use of stop-and-search.

Well, first of all, really good luck. I hope it goes incredibly well. We need lots of people signing up and studying to become the medics and our NHS staff of the future. Look, under the Conservatives, our health and social care services have been driven into the ground and the Liberal Democrats have a very clear plan as to how to fix this. We're going to fix the front door to the NHS with 8,000 more GPs, giving you a guarantee of an appointment within seven days. We're going to put an end to dental deserts. We'll have a mental health community hub in every single community. And we've made a very bold pledge to fix and to offer free personal care. That means you can see, you can get the help you need when you need it. We can fix the back end to the NHS by making sure that if you need to leave hospital, there will be care in place. What's the total annual cost of all the changes you're making? So we have set out in our manifesto exactly how every single pledge is going to be funded. But we've said right across the piece, across all of our policies, we will not tax struggling families during a cost of living crisis, but we will be looking to those really big companies, raking in billions of pounds of profits to support struggling families and to fix our public services.

Look, we've had to change our tactics all the time because of what the Taliban has been doing. Originally they wanted to win a face-to-face war with us, fighting person-to-person. Now they use explosive devices. And these explosive devices are designed to scare as well as to maim our own troops. We have had to change our tactics and therefore bring in the explosive experts, bring in the metal detectors, bring in the drones to survey the land, make sure we have the proper intelligence, go into Pakistan and deal with the making of bombs as well. All these things have had to happen. And we've had to change our tactics again because we want to train up the Afghan police and army. So we are partnering with the Afghan police. Now, I'd like to say to our troops, come back into the barracks and you'll be safe. But I can't say that. because our strategy in Afghanistan depends on contact with the local people, persuading them that they are safe with us and safe in an Afghanistan free of the Taliban. So we've had to change, yes, but it's in response to the tactics of our opponents.

No, there's nothing wrong with that, and I just want to address Beverly's point. Hang on, you opposed it when I announced it. You literally opposed it. If you listen to people in the audience across the country more often, you might not be quite so out of touch. Beverly. Beverly, can I just address the point you make, because it's a really important point, which is very many people with illness or disability do want to get back into work for the very reason that was alluded to before, the respect for dignity and, you know, the support that it gives them. But it's difficult to do so because they're worried about whether they can do it and whether, you know, they'll last a period of months. So what a number of firms are doing, businesses, and I've been to see this myself and I support this, is having a supported scheme where people can be supported back into work for a number of months to make sure they can make that transition and get back into work. I think that's a really good scheme. That's what we would put into place. But I do have to address this question. The Prime Minister keeps saying he's going to cut taxes. He has raised tax 26 times. Nobody's raised more tax than this Prime Minister. This is not a question about tax.

Firstly, Mary, you need to be given the power to sack any politician who's proved to be corrupt. It's something I advocated in the past, it's something I put forward in Westminster. Both David Cameron and Gordon Brown's parties didn't support that. They now, which is good, say that they do welcome that because you're the boss. You're the boss. The other thing we need to do is clean up all the murky business of party funding. We've all had problems with party funding. Again, there was a deal. There was a deal on the table. We supported it to clean up party funding. Yet again, the old party said no. Gordon Brown wanted to protect his trade union paymasters. David Cameron wanted to protect his paymaster in Belize. We all agree on the rhetoric, we all agree on the rhetoric of cleaning up politics, but we actually have to act. I'd say one final thing. One of the reasons why your friends and your neighbours are perhaps right in saying that they feel ignored is that because we have this very odd electoral system which allows Gordon Brown, the Prime Minister, to be in power when only 22% of people voted for his party last time, many people are being ignored and we need to change that as well.

Well, I think this is one of the most important questions of this election. I see it in my own family. I see my 80-year-old mother having to try and negotiate the complexity of a means-tested system to care for, properly, for my 84-year-old father. It comes to the basic question of whether we are a decent society. And again, I don't mean to bang on about this, Richard, but why is it when you're in government in Wales, you're not implementing, we have free social care in Scotland, why can't we have it in Wales? You're talking about a national health and social care service, free at the point of need for social care in Wales. You could do it in Wales now, why don't you?

Nigel Farage. I think there's a section of young people in this country who are having a fantastic time. Life is great. They're full of optimism. They're the rich. They're the rich. And they're the ones who go to the 7% of schools where their parents are wealthy enough to pay for their education. And they are now dominating politics, the media, the arts, sport, in a way I haven't seen before. And their families get richer and richer. And the gap, the social inequality in Britain, grows with every single year. By abolishing grammar schools, by abolishing selective education, what we did actually was to pull up the ladder on tens of thousands of young men and women every year who would have done better had they gone to a grammar school. With university education, we encourage lots of people to go to university who actually weren't academic and have come out with debt and would have done better with trade and skills. And to give optimism on housing, what I've said earlier, We have to build a new house every seven minutes to cope with migration. Let's cut the numbers and let's have a brownfield building revolution.

You know, elderly people shouldn't have to choose between the home they own and the care they need. And we have to devise a better system for the future. That's why the first stage of it is urgent needs care. So you can stay in your own home. You don't have to go into a nursing home. You get the support you need to stay in your home free of charge. We're introducing that from April next year. The second stage in the next Parliament is to say to people, if you're in an old people's home and you're in that home for more than two years, it will be free for personal and medical care from then onwards. and that will take away the worry and the anxiety people feel that their own home will have to be sold to meet the costs of residential care. The third stage will be to move to a more comprehensive system where people can be guaranteed that their needs will be met in the long run. I agree with Nick, we want consensus on this, we want to proceed in a way that every party, but every part of the country is with us. That's why we're consulting the social care groups, we're setting up a new commission to look at the funding for the future, after the next Parliament, but we are committed to urgent needs being met now.

We were trying to get the NHS reinstatement bill, which would precisely have rolled back the role of private sector in the NHS. Huge amount of support for that in the NHS itself by the NHS workers. So to call that politicking, I think, is wrong.

Mr Brown. I honestly think we should raise the standard of debate here. There has been awful things that have happened. We've had to take action against lots of MPs who've betrayed the public trust. We're in politics, I hope, for serving the public, not serving ourselves. But we've got to take action that makes a real difference in the future. Now, Nick supports me in reforming the House of Commons and the House of Lords.

And you've not wanted to answer the question. So no is the answer to that question. But the issue is... No, you don't think it's a sin. No, but the point you... Do you think abortion is wrong, is the other question that's asked? The question is, do you act in a way, because of what you believe sincerely in your heart, in a liberal fashion and defend people's rights? So I joined the Liberal Party, as it was then, when I was 16. One of the very first campaigns I was involved in was trying to abolish and get rid of Section 28. That homophobic piece of legislation was introduced by the Conservative government at that time. And right through my time, passionate for LGBT plus rights, particularly most recently when we were in coalition government, introducing equal marriage. That is massively important to me, but the job is not finished.

I will try to do it as briefly as possible. On the question, very good question, about are we prioritising independence over other issues, I would say that the evidence says no. Don't get me wrong, Scotland has challenges, all countries have challenges. As First Minister, I face challenges in our public services every single day and my job is to confront those. But if you look at some of the policies... Well, if you look at some of the policies in Labour's manifesto published this week, If you take things like free prescriptions, free university education, restoring the nursing bursary, opposition to fracking, for example, these are all things that my government in Scotland has already done. So we're getting on with delivering those progressive policies, but we want to be in charge of our own future, not to turn our backs on the rest of the UK. We'll always be the closest friends and neighbours, but so that we don't get dragged down paths by Tory governments that we don't want to go down. The second question on if there is a second EU referendum and the UK votes to remain. I hope that happens. I've campaigned and worked as hard as possible, as has the SNP, to try to find a way for the whole UK to stay in the EU, because I think that's the best future.

I want to pick up on something that's very important to young people, which is renting in the private sector. Lots of young people have this experience. It's incredibly insecure. It's sometimes substandard accommodation. Now, we're the only party with a plan to get a fair deal in the private rented sector. Three-year tenancies, not one-year tenancies. Rent stabilised during that time. and stopping letting agents charging tenants, because at the moment they charge tenants and landlords fees. And that is a massive issue for young people all across our country, and we will act. But it's all part of saying, look, we've got to stand up to some of these powerful interests and actually make our country work for young people again.

Well, Fiona, I think housing is possibly one of the biggest issues in this election campaign. In Scotland, we have a target of 30,000 affordable houses by 2016. We're on track to meet that. We're also considering rent caps to help people in the private rented sector. But I think we need to go much, much further. SNP MPs in the House of Commons would vote for a target of 100,000 affordable homes a year across the UK. But we also need to make sure if we're building new homes for rent, we protect them for people who need to rent. I think the Tory idea to extend the right to buy to housing association properties is one of the worst ideas I've ever heard. In Scotland, We've abolished the right to buy, not because we're against home ownership. We help people into home ownership through help to buy and shared equity. But because it's a policy that's had its day, we need to build more houses for rent, and then we need to protect them for those who can't buy and need to rent.

Although everyone knows that Plaid Cymru is all about Wales, our vision and our values can benefit people in all parts of the UK. Our country faces some real challenges, so we must grasp any opportunities that come our way to give ourselves a voice. Plaid Cymru's positive post-Brexit plan is an alternative to Theresa May's way. And I have a message for the Prime Minister, who I'm sure is watching tonight. You may be too scared to come here tonight for your U-turns to be highlighted, for your cruel policies to be exposed. You want this election to only be about Brexit because that means you avoid talking about the real issues like the NHS, the economy and the cuts you've made to our public services. That's weak leadership. weak and unstable. And I hope all of us here will show you tonight that real leadership means being willing to defend what you stand for, not hide from it. Leanne Wood, thank you.

Nigel Farage. Scotland was world class at one point. It certainly isn't now. It's gone right down the international rankings. Look, there is a problem. We know the buildings, the racked concrete, all of that, but we are back to the subject that nobody wants to discuss. The exploding population means our schools, our class sizes are getting bigger. Of course, we've recruited more teachers. We need more teachers with an exploding population. And it's a constant problem that none of the other panellists even want to discuss.

I don't know what Ed Miliband is talking about. The question of independence was for the referendum last year. This election is about getting rid of the Tories. Ed, whatever differences you have with me, surely they are as nothing to the differences both of us have with the Tories. This is about how we deliver better politics for people not just in Scotland but right across the UK. This election is an opportunity to seize an alternative to austerity, to protect our public services, to create more and better jobs, to have a higher minimum wage, to protect our National Health Service. It's going into this election not promising the money that the National Health Service needs. What I'm saying is I can help Labour be bolder to deliver the change that we really need, because that's what this election is about. For me, it's about making Scotland's voice heard. But secondly, it's about delivering real change, not pretend change, but real change for people right across this country. Don't turn your back on that head and let David Cameron back into Downing Street.
You can't sit here and predict what will be the number of individual wind farm developments.

The principle is to ensure that all people that work in the NHS are employed by the NHS on similar contracts across the NHS. The internal market... So, none. ..must go within the NHS. And what is happening is privatization of services across the NHS, and I think that is very, very damaging to the whole principles of the NHS. We also have problems of social care, which are not being addressed either, and there are about a million people waiting for social care at the present time.

There is more to do. So some of these policies will be starting to have an effect, but it will take longer in some respects. So I'm not going to stand here and make promises that I can't necessarily deliver in the year timescale that you're talking about. What I can say with absolute conviction is that there is a real determination to tackle this and to do what is required over whatever period is required. I don't want, not just to be First Minister of a country, I don't want to be a citizen of a country in which we've got people dying unnecessarily of drug use. If you take alcohol misuse, for example, which is not the same, we've implemented, for example, minimum unit pricing. So we have a record as a country of doing really courageous things when it comes to public health and we have got to show that same determination here too.

Well Fiona, I'm going to come back to your situation and your struggle because it's the struggle of so many people in Britain. The fact is private landlords last year got £9.3 billion from housing benefit. and 38% of people living in private rental accommodation, that rent is being paid in part or full by that housing benefit. We've got a huge problem that houses have become primarily financial assets and only secondarily places for people to live. That's why the Green Party wants to build 500,000 homes for social rent, genuinely affordable homes over the course of this Parliament. Maybe one of them could be yours. We also want to put a cap on private landlords raising the rent. five-year security of tenure, a cap that your rent goes up by no more than inflation. We really need to turn this around and turn homes into somewhere that's secure, affordable, that you're not six months and you'll throw it out and you have to look for a new place and have a whole new huge deposit to pay.

Well, I explained that part of our funding would come from, as I say, our nuclear weapons programme. If you were to get rid of that, you would have £110 billion minimum over the next 30 years. You could certainly be putting £3 billion and more every year into the NHS. And on top of that, clearly, we would also say more progressive taxation. We're very clear, if we want good, world-class public services, we have to be prepared to pay for them. When we put far less into our public services, into our NHS, by proportion of our GDP than many other countries. But I want to say one thing to Tim, if I might, because I'm very glad that he's so proud of the NHS, and rightly so, but I do find it puzzling that his party did support the increasing role of the private sector in the NHS by supporting the Health and Social Care Act when they were in coalition government. And I do wonder if, in his manifesto, he might re-look at that, because it seems to me that that role of the private sector that fact that you can have 49% of hospitals now, 49% of their budget being used on the private sector is deeply damaging.

Well, as you know, that's not the case. That's not been the party policy for a considerable period of time. What we would seek to do through this election is to win a majority of seats in Scotland, and then seek to enter discussions with the UK government following that. The big question there, of course, is for, likely, Keir Starmer to determine whether he's going to respect the democratic will of the people of Scotland or not, or whether he's going to copy Boris Johnson, Liz Truss, and Rishi Sunak.

I actually voted for the Brexit deal. And, you know, so I voted with my feet on this, notwithstanding I personally had voted to remain, because I accepted the outcome and I walked through the lobby in support of the deal that Boris Johnson put on the table. I don't think it's good enough. I do actually think we can do better than that, but we are not going back in because we're not going to reopen what was actually a pretty awful period for us politically, Between 2016 and 2019, I think, in Parliament, we were arguing about nothing else. And I think many people in this room and across the country, by the time it came to 2019, said, for heaven's sake, we've got to move forward. I agree with that and we're not going back.

No. Very simply, no. Why not? And the reason for that, and I'm glad you asked the question, right, the reason for that is a vote for reform ultimately is a vote to put Keir Starmer into Number 10. So, if you're someone who cares about cutting taxes... If you're someone who cares about cutting taxes, protecting pensions, a sensible approach to net zero that prioritises security in your bills, or indeed bringing migration down, both illegal migration and stopping the boats, I'm the person that is going to deliver that for you, not Keir Starmer. Keir Starmer doesn't have a plan to tackle illegal migration. Right, Keir Starmer is not going to cut your taxes. Keir Starmer's going to reversal the reforms I made to getting us to net zero in a way that saves you money, and that's going to put up everyone's bills. So that's the choice, right? And Keir Starmer's certainly not matching the Triple Lock Plus, which is going to protect pensioners, and instead there's going to be a retirement tax for every pensioner in our country.

That's a political choice. It's actually a tough choice. I do understand what you're saying. I don't think it will lead to lots of people going to state education. We have done some analysis on this, but it's a choice. I think every single child, whatever their background, wherever they're from, should have the chance and to believe, genuinely believe, that success belongs to them. Zakir, I think he's saying he wants to come back.

I think Ed is trying to defend a record that wasn't all that good. You were the people that saw the massive growth of MRSA. You were the people that presided over the Mid Staffs Hospital. But my question to you, if you become Prime Minister, will you run a national health service, or will you continue it to be an international health service? And that is what voters want to know. I'll answer you directly.

and should be. Big legal firms, big aerospace firms would invest in our country if we were sort of bobbing along somewhere in the mid-Atlantic, friendless, unable to sell into our own European neighbourhood. Of course not. Jobs are at stake.

Yeah, well, it took you 15 years, exactly. That makes the point, doesn't it? At the Green Party, we take a totally different approach to refugees, because this is ultimately about what kind of society we want to live in. And I'm clear that the majority of Brits want us to live in a welcoming and civilised society. So we think the UK government needs to do three things. Firstly, to create safe and legal routes for those fleeing danger, persecution and war so that the smugglers with their small boats taking advantage of people in a desperate situation lose their business model. That's the first thing. Second, we need to speed up the asylum system so that timely and fair decisions are made and we're not unnecessarily keeping people in detention and preventing them from integrating into their new communities. And third, we want to allow asylum seekers to work to support themselves while they're waiting for their decision. I visited a refugee centre in my home city of Bristol recently, and I met a woman who'd been practising as a nurse in her home country. She would need to do a little bit more training in order to start practising here. She wasn't able to get started on that. She wasn't able to work. She'd been waiting in this country for 10 years. She wanted to work in the NHS, contribute to our country, to our public services and to our tax system and our stupid asylum system stopped her. We are a civilised country and we should act like it.

I am so proud of the values that have made our country so great. Democracy, human rights, the rule of law. But the sad truth is that in recent years our governments under the old parties have let those values down. We shouldn't have sent soldiers into battle without the right equipment. We shouldn't be facing allegations of complicity in torture. We shouldn't have invaded Iraq. So I want us to lead in the world. I want us to lead in Europe, not complain from the sidelines. I want us to lead in creating a world free of nuclear weapons. And I want us to lead on the biggest challenge of all, climate change. Now my family knows what British values really mean. My mother was freed by British troops from a prisoner of war camp. And I think if we do things differently, we can be proud once again of the role we can play as a force for good in the world.

Thank you very much. OK. So if the polling is to be believed, one third of the folks in here support the SNP, and enough respect to all of you. And two years ago, you had six MPs. And whether you like it or not, whether I like it or not, you've made a significant difference in that time. So don't write off people because they've got a current number of MPs in single digits. There was a bunch of questions at the back about what happens next and all of the rest of it. My view is simply this. As somebody who is a Northerner, who is committed to my collective British identity, I want us to be a united kingdom. I utterly respect those who believe in independence, but I politely disagree. The issue about there being untruths or wrong arguments on either side, do you know what? I mean, I am most critical about the arguments used by those at the top of the Remain campaign.

I've been Prime Minister for 18 months, give or take. When I first came in, the priority I had was to make sure that we could restore economic stability. And that was the challenge that everyone was facing with the cost of living. And I said, look, this is what I'm about. Those are the things I want to focus on. You can judge me. I want to be very clear and transparent about what I'm wanting to do for you. And so the first of those was to bring down inflation. And I'm pleased that We've just got the news recently that inflation is back to normal, and that is a sign of progress. I know people are only just starting to feel the benefits of that, but it shows that we're on the right track, and that's why we're able to start cutting people's taxes, and that's why I want to go further.

Thank you. Gordon Brown. I want to answer the question directly. Let's be honest, and Nick didn't say this, we've already got Al-Qaeda in Somalia. We've already got problems with al-Qaeda in Yemen. We are having to take action with our multilateral partners to deal with these problems and will continue to have to do so. So why are we in Afghanistan and why have we got to be vigilant all the time? The reason is there is a chain of terror that links these al-Qaeda groups in different parts of the world to action that could happen in the United Kingdom. And, you know, every week I get a report, sometimes every day, of terrorist plots, most of which arise in the Afghanistan-Pakistan area, and we have got to deal with. So to keep the streets safe in Britain, we have to take on al-Qaeda wherever it is. I also have to say about Afghanistan that this is a mission that can work. And the reason is we are training up the Afghan soldiers and the Afghan police. So whenever we're in a mission abroad, we've got to make sure that we have a means by which in that country we can build up their own security staff so that we can bring our troops home. That is my mission and that is my aim.

Housing is hugely, hugely important. Scotland, as I know is the case in other parts of the UK, we are investing in schemes like help to buy and shared equity and taking a range of steps to help people, not just young people, but young people in particular, get their first foot on the housing ladder. But we are also protecting a affordable housing as well, and investing in greater numbers of affordable homes. Because there are some people, even with the help of shared equity, will not be able, in the short or medium term, to buy their own home. And we have a duty and an obligation to make sure we're providing good quality houses for rent as well, and that's really important. Thank you very much, Ed Miliband.

Jeremy Corbyn. Socialism carried out in a democratic way in this country, in Scandinavia and other places, has raised the living standards of the very poorest. And it was the post-war Labour government that did massively to improve the living standards of the poorest working-class communities in this country. And our Labour government will do the same again.

Well, as time-seekers, you can't be absolutely sure of the numbers because it depends on what's happening in other parts of the world. What we have said is we need to make sure we go after those international criminal gangs who are trafficking human beings and stop it at source. And then, when working with the UN and other countries, take our share of refugees.
Well, we need to stop FGM. Which creates segregation. We've got to stop FGM.

Yes, tell us more. Is that changing the system? Yeah, it's changing the system. It's about saying that, I mean, there's lots of different things that need to be done. So there does need to be much more social housing built as well as more housing built overall, because the fundamental problem, you know, I mean, the contrast between those of us who are a bit older on the panel and the audience is that that just demonstrates how much more difficult it is now for young people to buy a property than it was 15 years ago. And obviously not everybody will be in that situation of being able to buy a property but what we have now is lots of young people who would be able to afford the monthly mortgage repayments but wouldn't be able to have a deposit and so the scheme that we have is basically a rent to own scheme so you would pay your usual rent but over time that actually builds up equity within the home and at the end of 30 years you would own it outright.

So, Jon, I completely... It was Jon, wasn't it? Jonathan. Jonathan, completely agree with you. We've had a massive spiral in the welfare bill since Covid. It's gone up by 60%. almost 800,000 more people who are of working age are now been signed off sick. I don't think that's right. We were making great progress before the pandemic and we need to get back to that. So we set out a plan to reform the system to do that, have people provide more medical evidence, reform how the sick note, fit note process works because it's not working, make it clear that if you don't accept a job, when your job coach tells you there's a sensible job for you, you won't be able to keep receiving your benefits, all very common sense things. And that's going to help us make savings in our welfare budget. And we can use those to help cut your taxes and support people into work. Because I completely agree with you, everyone who can work should work. Our reforms are going to deliver that. And in contrast, again, because all elections are a choice, What has the Labour Party said? We think we can make savings in a welfare bill that's gone up as much as it has. £69 billion today we spend on people with ill health, disability benefits, a working age. The Labour Party think that you can't make one penny of savings from that bill. They don't think you can make one penny of savings, and that tells you the choice ahead of you at this election. If you think we can do something about this, we're the only people who are going to do that for you.

Leadership is about understanding the people you represent, is about being prepared to learn, is about not being so high and mighty you can't take advice. It is also about bringing people with you. It is also about ensuring that your responsibilities protect the safety and security of everybody in this country and to lead a government that cares for everybody in the country and doesn't walk by on the other side when there are people that are homeless, there are people that are starving and we have millions of our children living in poverty. It's leadership to lead a government that is prepared to say to our society we cannot go on like this, we have to start putting more money into our public services and our resources. We have to have an economy that works for all and we don't have to have a spiv economy that hands tax relief to the biggest corporations and the wealthiest people whilst ignoring the desperate cries for social help of so many people in our country.
The industrial base will disappear. That's the point. It's not true. And it's happening in the North already.

Stop lying. UKIP believes in the National Health Service, free at the point of delivery. It was you that privatised a large chunk of the health service and you are lying and you are lying and you are lying and you keep on doing it.

In response, just as we don't believe the real divide in our society is between people who voted Leave and Remain, neither do we think the fundamental divide in our society is between whether someone lives in Newcastle or in Glasgow. We don't support independence of Scotland. We would argue against it. I also believe that when austerity is ended under a Labour government, when Scotland gets its fair share of a growing economy under a Labour government, that may make people realise that there is an alternative in Scotland, in the United Kingdom, with a progressive Labour government looking after the many, not the few.
And just briefly, no wonder people are fed up. It's the PPE corruption, it's the Downing Street parties during Covid. We need to hold politicians to higher standards than we've seen in recent years.

Nadim, you're quite right, there are going to have to be cuts, and we need to be frank about this, and we were the first to say that cuts would have to be made. I really want to explain to people that, if I'm your Prime Minister, I will do everything I can to protect the front line services. I want to see the police officers on the beat, the money go into our children's state schools, the money on the ward in the hospital - that's absolutely essential. Now, we do have to say some of the difficult things we're going to do, and we have done that. We're not just relying on waste. We've said, for instance, there will have to be a public sector pay freeze for one year from 2011. That's not popular, but it is the right thing to do. We've said that people are going to have to retire a year later, starting in 2016. These are difficult decisions, but I believe, also, we've got to get our economy growing. We've got to get it moving. That's why we say start now with efficiency savings to stop the jobs tax next year. Let's get employing people again in Britain; let's get the economy moving to help us with the deficit.

No, we would always respect... So we are responsible in Scotland for health policy and education, for example. I don't think it's right that we would seek to... You know, no more right than it would be for Westminster to dictate to Scotland that we would dictate health policy or education policy in England, except where that affects Scotland, because often the decisions that are taken around Westminster on the health service, for example, have implications for our budget. and one of the big issues in the next Parliament potentially for the health service is trade deals and we all have to come together those of us who don't want our NHS sold off to make sure that that doesn't happen so we will seek to exercise influence of course in Scotland's interest but where it is appropriate in the interests of people right across the UK.

We all express our condolences, but nobody apologises. Apologises for the fact the liberal elite have given us a ridiculous sentencing system. I don't care whether these people were in jail for six years or 12 years. If you have committed mass murder, or plan to commit mass murder, you're not just an ordinary criminal, you've got the virus of jihadism, and I think these people, Darren, should never, ever be let out of prison unless we're absolutely convinced they do not have the jihadi virus but of course political correctness stops us from doing this.

That's where we'll leave it. Kate Forbes, SNP. So we respect across the UK that a majority voted to leave the EU. But in Scotland, 62% voted to remain. And so over the last year, Nicola Sturgeon put forward some compromise solutions for what, in a way that would recognise the majority vote to leave, but also Scotland's position in voting to remain. All those proposals and discussions have been soundly rejected. And so we're now in a position of saying, well, once we know the deal, whenever that might be over the next two years, we will give people in Scotland the opportunity to choose between a hard Brexit that doesn't take into account Scotland's interests and independence in the EU. Jonathan Bartley, the Green Party.

Yes is the answer to the question. It's very important that we protect women's spaces. I saw this firsthand when I was Chief Prosecutor back in 2008-2013. I was working with groups who were supportive of women who'd been subjected to domestic violence and sexual violence and saw for myself just how important it is to have those women's spaces. Same in hospitals and Um, in sport. So, yes to that answer. But what I will also say is that I do recognize that there are a small number of people who are born into a gender that they don't identify with, and I will treat them as I treat all human beings with dignity and respect. And I'll tell you for why... I'll tell you for why. Because if you don't, you end up the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom standing in Parliament making an anti-trans joke in front of the mother of a murdered trans teenager. I will never, ever allow myself to be put into that position.

Well, Daryl, I agree with you that we need a safe and beneficial future, but I absolutely don't think we're going to get it through the kind of extreme Brexit that Theresa May is pursuing. I think we need to be really clear that she has no mandate for this kind of Brexit, when we're out of the single market, out of the customs union, putting at risk key social and environmental protections. You know, we were clear that that was not on the ballot paper. The vote was to leave, but leave can look very different. There was no clarity as to what leave actually meant. And that's why the Green Party believes that you, the general public, should have the final say on that final package. Now, if you like what you see, then that's great. Go for it. But we believe that you should have the right to be able to look at the small print and decide if you don't like it, then you should have the right to be able to remain inside the EU. You know, I am passionate about freedom of movement. I'm passionate about staying as close to our European neighbours as possible. And I feel very let down by Labour, which has not only not turned up tonight, but basically left the field empty too when it came to opposition to this kind of extreme Brexit in Parliament. Caroline Lucas, thank you. Paul Nuttall.

My thoughts, too, are with those attacked on London Bridge today and with all those who responded. This election really matters. At stake is not just Brexit, but what kind of society we want to be. The SNP wants Scotland to be an independent, outward-looking, internationalist country, and we want the people of Scotland to have the right to make that choice. But we also want to play our part in locking Boris Johnson out of Downing Street. If he wins the majority, then the consequences will be dire. Brexit will not, in any sense, be done. Trade talks haven't even started yet. These issues will cause chaos for years to come. In his rush for a trade deal with Donald Trump, Johnson simply can't be trusted not to put our NHS on the table. He also threatens to rip up rules on food standards, environmental protections and workers' rights. To build a better future, we need to stop all of that. And in Scotland, that means voting SNP.
Look, I believe in a democracy, and we live in a democracy. We have a titular head of state as the monarch, but without political power.

Thank you very much indeed, Mr Brown. To help people live at home, to give them the urgent care needs that they have since either met, for example by home helps and health visitors, so that people who want to stay at home don't have to go into institutional care. We've seen a revolution. Large numbers of people living longer and that's a great thing, but people need the help in their homes so that they don't have to go into nursing homes or into old people's homes. My priorities for the health service are that we give people now personal guarantees. And that is that every individual patient will know that they will get a cancer specialist seen within two weeks if they need it. They'll get a diagnostic test within one week and the results to them. They will also be able to know that their operation will be within 18 weeks if you're any patient in need of an operation. You'll be able to see a GP in the evenings at weekends, something that wasn't happening. You'll be able to get a free health check up on the National Health Service. I want people to know that public services are personal to people's needs and that's why we need to give these guarantees to individual patients and that's what we're going to do from now on.

This general election has become farcical. Every morning, billions of pounds being offered, showered around like compessi. They're trying to bribe you with borrowed money. Now UKIP has come up with a fully-costed, verified plan that shows we can cut the taxes of working people in this country to bring about real economic prosperity. And we do it by cutting the bloated overseas foreign aid budget. our contributions to the European Union. Let's take back control of our country, take back control of our borders, put in place an Australian-style points system to control immigration, and take on the big corporate giants who've driven down salaries and made life so difficult for our five million small businesses. It's only UKIP that is prepared to talk straight. I'm not frightened of upsetting the very politically correct class who were afraid to touch difficult issues. No, we'll tell it as it is. And I have a feeling that I'm the only person here saying what a lot of you at home are really thinking.

My thanks to you as well, Shirley, for all your service to the NHS. The NHS is undoubtedly our most cherished public service. We all love it. We all want it to be there for us when we need it. In Scotland, since my party took office 10 years ago, we've increased the budget by 40%. The number of staff in the NHS is up by 10%. And we've also, unlike other parts of the UK, taken the step of integrating health and social care. So today, our accident and emergency departments are under pressure, but they're the best performing anywhere in the UK. And we abolished car parking charges in 2008. Health services around the world face real pressure, and Scotland's is no different. So we have a commitment that by the end of this Scottish Parliament, the budget will be £2 billion higher than it is today, more than inflation. Money not just for hospitals, but for primary care, mental health services. and community services as well. We won't open it up to private competition, but the most important thing we need after this election is voices arguing against more needless Tory cuts and austerity, because that is putting our NHS at risk.

Yeah, because the Tories, they talk about a cap, but we've had net migration going up and up because our economy has relied on overseas workers to... Well, it's flatlining. But what we've said is that we need an industrial and skills strategy because we've got unemployment going up in the latest figures, so we've got to make sure... You've had 14 years to come up with some ideas on this. You've had 14 years in government!

No, what we do think... Wow, you're not going to one word. No, no, no, no. What we're trying to do is to make sure we get maintenance grants for students. OK. Students from poor backgrounds need to be able to go to university. OK, but no. And the Tories scrap that.

OK. Can I deal with the broadband question first? Thank you for it. The issue is, as I said earlier, 95% of people in South Korea have access to broadband free. We have, in this country, 10% access, and it costs people roughly £30 a month. And it's quite selective, the areas... It's not free in South Korea, though, is it? For some people it is, I'm not sure about the whole country, but it is available for the whole country. Our point is that by investing in it, it actually promotes economic growth and brings about a degree of social justice. Listen, everyone in this room probably has a computer and uses access to broadband if they've got it, or some form of internet all the time. It is the normal form of communication. Yet there is a big digital divide between those that can afford the best and those that don't get any whatsoever. I just want to make sure it's equally available for all. And over 10 years, we would make sure it is. And I think that's actually a sensible, up-to-date, modern way of looking at communications within our society.

When I was Home Secretary, crime fell by 18%. It hadn't happened before, ever, it hasn't happened since; so I've proved that I can get a job done and that's what we will do. That's one of the ways in which we'll be different. We ...  BOTH TOGETHER

When I was young, my father ran a youth club with my brother for young people. And the more people who do voluntary service and give of their time in the community, to getting young people off the streets to doing purposeful activity, the better, whether it's sports or whether it's dancing or music, or whether it's other activities that get people off the streets. But the one thing I'm absolutely sure of, we've got to maintain the numbers of police we have in this country. We've built up the police force from a period when it was understaffed to a period where they now have more police than ever before. And I want to make sure that that continues, with neighbourhood policing accountable to you, with you able to direct what happens in your own local police force. and if a police force is not performing well, let it be taken over by another police force so the job is done properly for you. So I would not support those and I'm afraid the Conservatives are not prepared to guarantee, as we are prepared to do, that we will continue to fund the police force and the spending on police will continue to arise so that we have enough police there on the beat for you.

I don't think the Labour Party can be trusted to keep this country as safe as the Conservatives. The world is a more dangerous and uncertain place than at any point since the end of the Cold War. I've made the decision to invest more in our defence, taking it up to 2.5% of GDP. Labour Party have not matched that. And worse than that, the person who would be Deputy Prime Minister under Keir Starmer does not believe in our nuclear deterrent, the ultimate guarantor of our security. So no, you can trust the Conservatives with our country's security, and my track record and our commitments prove that.

Of course, I agree with that. There are about a million carers in this country who care, I think now, for 50 hours or more for their loved ones, for members of their family who need care. They are the unsung army of heroines and heroes that keep our society together. They desperately need some time for themselves, time to go on holiday. Under our plans, what we could do immediately is give those million carers who care for the greatest length of time at least a week off. at least a week off, just to have a breather, spend some time on their own, go and visit friends, go on holiday, just have some time to themselves again. But as I say, I think we've all got some ideas, but I don't think any of us, and you don't hear this from politicians very much, I don't think any of us, actually, if we're really honest with you, have got the perfect solution. That's why I think this is so important. Let's, for once, put people before politics and come up with a solution that works for you and your family in the long run.

Well, the first duty of any prime minister is to keep our country safe. And that's why I'm going to keep our independent nuclear deterrent. And let me explain why. I think we live in an uncertain and unstable world. We only need to look at the actions of Russia in regard to Ukraine over the last year or so to know that we can't predict the threats we face 10, 20, 30, 40 years ahead. That's why I think it's right we keep our nuclear deterrent. But there's so much more we need to do to keep our country safe. Look, we need to work with our allies in the European Union, in my view. We need to work with the United States. But I think we need to work not for our allies. And I think it's very, very important that we learn the lessons of the 2003 Iraq war. Because Britain's national interest will often coincide with America's national interest, but not always. And you need a prime minister who's willing to say no when it's appropriate. And that's what I'll do.

Tonight, the choice at this election has been clear. You can vote for the same old parties and get the same old politics. More cuts and more misguided priorities. Or you can vote for something different, better and more progressive. I'm going into this election with a clear message. None of us can afford more austerity. None of us can afford an additional £30 billion of cuts. And none of us can afford the £100 billion that the Tories, Labour and Liberals intend to spend on new nuclear weapons. Their priorities are wrong, but they won't pay the price. It will be ordinary people across the country who pay the price. The SNP offers an alternative, a clear alternative, a plan for investment. Yes, it is fiscally responsible, but it will also allow us to invest in infrastructure to protect our public services and to lift people out of poverty. To people in Scotland, I say vote SNP for a louder voice for Scotland. To people elsewhere, I say ours will be a voice to help bring about change for you too. Nicola Sturgeon, thank you. Nick Clegg.

When it comes to tax, the Conservatives have made a very clear choice to increase taxes on working people, on struggling families. That's not true. That's happened more and more times. You have raised taxes to the highest level in almost 80 years. The tax burden...

Steve, the biggest threat to our borders at the moment is the very many people arriving by small boat over the Channel. And there are record numbers. Since Rishi Sunak became Prime Minister, 50,000 people have come across by small boat. and the government has effectively lost control of our borders. And that means it's not just a border security issue, it's actually a national security issue, because the government's lost control of who's coming and what their background is. Now, I think that nobody but nobody should be making that journey across the Channel. And we have to smash the gangs that are running this vile trade. There are gangs that are making a fortune putting people into boats across the Channel, an absolute fortune. and I think they can be taken down. And Steve, before I was a politician, I was the chief prosecutor for England and Wales, so I ran all criminal prosecutions in England and Wales, in all of our criminal courts, and we had to deal with terrorist plots and other plots, drug plots, and gun plots, which were across various other countries. We grouped together with police and law enforcement across Europe, and we took down those terrorist gangs. I've seen it done. I've been part of the operations to do it. I do not accept, I will never accept, that the only gangs that apparently can't be taken down are the gangs that are putting vulnerable people in boats to go across the Channel.

The point is that today, actually, the number of managers is going up five times faster than the number of nurses in our NHS. And the government has had 13 years to fix these problems and it hasn't done. Gordon Brown talks about cancer, but what he's not telling you is that there are people in our country, there was a case the other day of someone who had to sell their home to get the cancer drugs. And the Prime Minister, the government, is about to hit the NHS, Britain's biggest employer, with this national insurance rise. It's going to take £200 million out of our National Health Service. We say stop that national insurance rise and instead spend the money on a cancer drugs fund so people can get the drugs they need. Talk about guarantees. But the fact is, for some people, waiting two weeks to see a consultant is too long. We need a faster, choice-driven system, but the drugs have got to be there when you need them. They're not always right now. This is a phony debate.

Yeah, well I'm going to come to that absolute specific point in just a sec, but I thought the lady in the second row here on my left, you know, hit the nail on the head. It is always a few days before an election that the dog whistlers in chief, and you have the dog whistler in chief on this panel here, Nigel Farage, blame everything on immigrants. In fact, Nigel, I mean, I mean, I'm not lying to you, I'm not making this up, but I mean, Nigel was once late to a UKIP reception and he blamed the traffic on the M4 on immigrants. I mean, that's how ludicrous it is that he blames every social ill on the world. Do you not accept that population growth of 8 million has made our roads busier?

The problems that we have of exploitation of workers in this country and groups of workers that have been brought in wholesale from lower paid economies in Central Europe is a serious one. Those people are themselves grossly exploited. They're brought here to undermine existing pay and working conditions and create... Hang on, can I finish? That will make the situation worse in any community where that takes place. I was demanding that we have a proper regulation whilst we were still members of the European Union before the referendum. That whole strategy has to stop. There has to be a managed migration that isn't designed to undermine working conditions in this country.

Yeah. Linda, look, we talked about it right at the beginning of this interview. That was my number one priority when I got this job. Interest rates are at a very high level. And why were they at a high level? Because inflation was at 11%. I haven't experienced anything like that in this country for decades. That's why I made it my number one priority to bring inflation down, because that's what will then allow interest rates to come down. And as you've seen, inflation just got back to target just last week, which is very, very promising news. You heard from the Bank of England, so we're now on track to start seeing interest rates and mortgage rates be cut. And that's how I give you financial security.

It's not a question of whether or not we should go along with somebody else, David. We take independent decisions as the UK. I spoke to Donald Trump. I've told him my views from the UK's position last week. I told him last night what our views are. We remain committed to the Paris Agreement. We continue to think it's important for dealing with climate change internationally.

Prime Minister. Fliss, I'm so sorry to hear about your dad and if you let me know afterwards which trust it was, I'll make sure that we look into it and someone gets in touch with you so we can find out what went wrong. I can't imagine how difficult that's been for you and your family. I think as probably most of you know, I grew up in an NHS family. I know how important healthcare is. I've worked for my mum in the pharmacy, helping people myself. When it comes to cancer, the most important thing we can do is, as you highlighted, just diagnose it as soon as possible. And that's the best chance we've got of helping someone, getting them the treatment they need and make sure that they survive. And one of the ways we're doing that is by rolling out things called community diagnostic centres. So we've done 160, and if we're re-elected, we'll do many more. And these are units in your local community where you can get all your checks, tests and scans done. and that way get your diagnosis much quicker. We're hiring more oncologists, we're using technology as well and new forms of testing to identify things. But fundamentally, what happened to you and your family isn't acceptable. And I can only apologise. You know the challenges we're dealing with with COVID. Cancer in particular was one where for two years, lots of people didn't present with symptoms and we're catching up with that now and that's an awful tragedy, one of the many tragedies of Covid. The key thing now is to just keep working through the backlog and as I said more oncologists, more community diagnostic centres and new technology is going to help us build on what we're doing which is improving the survival rates.

If I could make a bet, I had to draw attention to the analogy with the cats and the mice here. What we've seen under the Conservatives for 14 years is the fat cats. getting rich and getting the cream. And I fear that with privatisation in the NHS, we're going to see the same under Labour.

Well, I think we have to represent people, take decisions and be honest with people about why you're taking a decision that you are and how to bring people together. My whole strategy on Brexit has been to recognise whether you voted remain and live in a private rented accommodation and in poor and in London, voted leave and you live in the North and are in the same situation, your issues are the same. The problems of poverty, inequality and division within our society. Bring people together, don't divide them.

I've got fundamental disagreements with you, Nicola, because in the last couple of weeks, you've revealed that you haven't ruled out having a second referendum on independence in the next five years. Now, look, we're just very different. We've got very different views. I mean, I respect your views that you want to break up the country, but that's not my view. I think that would be a disaster for the working people of Britain So we have profound differences between us, David. That's why I'm not going to have a coalition with the SNP. Because I'm never going to put at risk the unity of our United Kingdom. And why? Because I care too much about the working people of our country. And I think it would be a disaster for us to start competing against each other, driving down wages, dismantling the basic state pension, which is the foundation of security in retirement, and dismantling our country. So it's a no, I'm afraid.

Yeah, and I think we need to look at how we invest in supporting particularly people from low incomes going into university to get more people doing that. But if we really want to improve life chances, the best place to put that money is in the early years and in schools, that's how you get that social mobility, that's how you give young people opportunities and that's where the priority for our spending is going, it's to make sure that we give our schools the money they need. I mean at the moment in schools, right, you've got teachers being sacked, you've got some schools not opening all day on a Friday, you've got schools crowdfunding for print sticks and exercise books. I mean, if that doesn't tell you that our schools are in dire need of more funding, what does?

Thank you very much. My message to you is simple. If you want your NHS back, you have to vote for it. If you want a growing economy, you have to vote for it. If you want more police on our streets, more teachers in our schools, you have to vote for it. If you want to end 14 years of chaos and rebuild our country, then that power is in your hands. On July the 4th, vote change, vote Labour.

I suppose what I would say, Melissa, is having looked at the last three and a half years, is there anyone seriously who thinks this is going well for our country? It is a mess. And it's a mess because back in 2016, they weren't straightforward about what it would mean. And, you know, we know that remaining in the EU is going to be better for our public services, it's going to be better for our NHS, for our economy, for jobs, for our environment, for workers' rights. And I think it's right that people have the chance to choose a different path now that all of that is so much clearer. And, you know, we're in an election. Liberal Democrats are absolutely standing to stop Brexit. We've led the campaign for a people's vote and we want to stop Brexit. And the final thing I would just say is, you know, you said that we're so far along. I'm sorry... I'm sorry to have to say this, but we are so not far along on this Brexit journey. You know, the idea that Brexit is going to be done, we're like in episode one of a ten-season box set, and if you don't like what you've seen up till now, you don't have to watch the rest.

I don't think we can afford quite that yet. But one thing we can do to help families with children the ones you were talking about, sir, I think the two-child limit on universal credit is just wrong. I think it has to go. If you look and talk to the experts, they say the quickest and most cost-effective way of tackling child poverty is to get rid of that two-child limit on universal credit. And that's something that could happen really quickly. It's costed in our manifesto, because it is a bit pricey. But I think it's the best way. And hopefully, that means that family that you were talking about would have the money and wouldn't have to do that thing.
