One million people on disability benefit across the UK are going to lose £1,100 of their benefit. That's not the kind of economic plan I want.

What are you going to do? We need to smash the gangs that are running this vile trade, making a huge amount of money, putting some of the most vulnerable people in boats across the Channel. They're making a fortune. Before I was a politician, Stephen, I was the Director of Public Prosecutions, and I worked with the police and prosecutors in other countries to bring down terrorist gangs who were running across borders. I do not believe it's impossible to bring down these gangs. What I won't do is engage in an expensive gimmick, the Rwanda scheme. If he believed it was going to work, he wouldn't have called an election before it can be tested. He wouldn't have done it.

No life on the dole. That's my policy. We've got to get people off unemployment benefit and they're going to be forced to work if they've been on unemployment benefit for a period of time. Now, we're giving an offer to young people at the moment. Under 25, if you've been unemployed for six month, we'll give you worker training because we don't want the unemployment of the 1980s for young people. We don't want to lose a labour market for the future, and we don't want a lost generation. But we're saying you're compelled to work, you cannot take this as an option. You're going to have to take up the job and the training. That's true for the long-term unemployed, it's true for young people who have been unemployed for six months. We're also doing a great deal to try to get people on incapacity benefit back to work. The record of doing that is large numbers of people are now moving back into work, not enough, but we're trying to do it. Now, I believe in work, too, because I've been brought up that work is the way that you reward people but it's also the way you find self-esteem. My Britain is one where I want more people working, more people working without being on benefits.

Thank you. Tonight, I'm going to be straight with you. I'm going to say what I really believe, not just what I think you might want to hear. Never in my lifetime has our future felt so uncertain. Brexit, climate change and NHS in crisis. But when people come together and reach for a bigger future, we've shown we can change the course of history. With courage, we emerged from the rubble of the Second World War. With vision, we forged a continent that's lived at peace for the past 70 years. And with ambition, we built a free and universal NHS. And we did this not in the good times, but in our darkest moments. And together, we can make big things happen again. We can end the scandal of four million children living in poverty in one of the richest countries of the world. We can transform our energy system to make it the cleanest and greenest. And we can forge a relationship with Europe which is open and says no to racism.
We would end the non-DOM loophole so that we can bring that money in and have 40,000 new appointments every week. But you're familiar with what Stephen is referring to.

Well, I call it democratic socialism, that's what's on our party card, Lyn. Yes is the answer, because I think, I believe in a fairer, more equal society. Now, each generation of Labour politicians puts it into practice in their own generation. Now, what does it mean today? And it goes back to the question that this lady asked. which is what I see in Britain. And you know, this is the thing that countries all around the world are grappling with. This is a central question we face. Are we going to be countries that work for the richest and most powerful? This is what America is facing, President Obama as well. Are we going to be a country that just works for the richest and most powerful, or is everyone going to get a fair shot? Is everyone going to play by the same rules? If you're going to do the right thing, go out to work, are you going to be able to build a life for you and yourself and your family? Lots of people are doubting that in Britain. To the gentleman who asked me whether I was too gloomy, that's why I think it sometimes might sound that way. Because I think people are asking, hang on a minute, this country was supposed to work for me. Why doesn't it work for me anymore? I'm doing what everyone tells me I should do. So that's what democratic socialism means, a fairer, more equal society, making this country work for working people again.

Like Adam, I've been campaigning for what I believe in for all of my life, even in the days when it wasn't as electorally popular as it is today. I'm also the leader of a government in Scotland. We're not perfect, we make mistakes, but I'm not asking here to be judged just on my promises. I'm asking people to look at what the SNP government is doing day in and day out. Much of what Labour is promising in this election, free prescriptions, free personal care, for example, are things the SNP in government in Scotland is already doing.
We have to rebuild trust and You know, Paul, that the murder last Monday was reported five separate occasions by the Muslim community.

Mary, there's a clear choice for you and everyone who believes, like you do, the situation isn't right. Because if you want lower taxes and lower welfare, that's what I'll deliver. If you want higher taxes and higher welfare, that's what Keir Starmer will do. So I strongly agree that people are better off in work. It's not just for the financial security it brings you, it's about the purpose, the dignity that it brings to people which you alluded to and I strongly agree with. The number of people currently out of work on ill health and disability benefits is too high. It's gone up by 800,000 since the pandemic, it's gone up by 60% the bill. in the last few years. We've got to do something about that. I made a speech about this a few months ago. I set out a comprehensive plan to reform the welfare system, to tighten it up so that those who can work do work. We're stricter about those eligibility criteria. That's fair for all of you who are working hard paying your taxes. It's better for those people and that's what I'll do. But when I announced that, And just again recently, Keir Starmer and the Labour Party opposed every single thing that I set out to deliver what you want. Every single thing. They do not think that you can save a single penny in the growing welfare bill. That's what their numbers show, and that's what they've said. So the choice for you is clear. If you want lower welfare and lower taxes, I will deliver that for you. Higher taxes and higher welfare is what you will get with Keir Starmer.

People are £1,600 a year worse off than they were when this government came to power. It's the worst record on living standards since the 1920s. And that is the argument of this election. Look, David Cameron wants to say... Do you want to say unemployment has fallen? Well, hang on a minute. You asked about wages. You said I was wrong that wages had fallen. No, I said four costs were wrong. No, you said that I forecast that wages would fall, Jeremy. You did. And they did, yes. And I was right, right? They did fall. They did fall.

Well, and against fracking. So why did you vote against a ban on it in 2015? So, when we were in the coalition government, my Liberal Democrat colleague Ed Davey was the Energy Secretary and he put in place very stringent regulation.

It's a difficult choice. We will have an anti-child poverty strategy, of course we will. The last Labour government did, we will have one. But the easiest thing in the world, it's what Rishi Sunak is doing, is to simply say you can have everything. Unfunded commitments, unfunded promises, that's what got us into this problem in the first place. And working people are paying the price with higher bills, their mortgages. I'm not prepared to have a Labour government ever make that mistake again.
The Conservative government have been very very weak on sentencing over the last 40 years just as Labour have been.

You cannot afford another five years of the Tories. You're promising tax cuts when you've already done that once and crashed the economy once. £2,000 is what £38.5 billion means to every working household in this country.

It is in the bottom, of course it's not. And I think our proposals are responsible, they're fully costed. I think other parties, frankly, are not putting the money in we need to rescue our public services, particularly the NHS. But our schools need better funding too. I've talked about the mental health provision that we want to make, but I think if you look at the cost of living, I'm very worried that children are going to our schools with empty tummies. that they're hungry. They can't learn if they're hungry. And when we were in government, we provided free school meals for infant children. Actually, we used a pilot that Labour had done, performed to give credit where credit's due. But we put it in. The Tories didn't like us doing it. You know, George Osborne did not like giving free school meals to infants, I can tell you. But now we think we need to go further. OK. And the Kosselin crisis, any child that's in poverty really should be getting a free school meal.

I thought I'd never utter these words, but I agree with Gordon. You cannot put off this decision. General Mike Jackson said today, we can't go on putting off this decision. You have to make it early. You have to keep your country safe and secure. You cannot rustle up a nuclear deterrent at the last minute, as the Liberal Democrats seem to think you can.

Yeah, just... I've been through life-threatening situations with my mum many, many times, holding her hand on nights we thought we were going to lose her. Too many times to not know exactly what that feels like. We had to go through it again, tragically, with my wife's mum in the leadership campaign, and we lost her in that particular case. I do know what I'm talking about when it comes to acute care in high dependency units. I know what it looks like, I know what it feels like, and I absolutely know it's the best place if you've got a life-threatening situation. Look, I'm not going to say to other people that's the choice that they should make, and the bigger choice, the bigger discussion that we're not really having here, which is important, is why are so many people having to now go private because they are having to wait so long on the NHS. The hard-working people are having to use their money, their savings, for operations which they ought to be able to get on the NHS much more quickly. It is the hidden tax in the NHS that we have got from this government. I want to end that. That is my choice, based on my experience, mainly on my mum, I accept, but I do know what the inside of a high-dependency unit looks like.

When it comes to the economy, I was right then. And since I've been your Prime Minister, inflation is back to normal, the economy is growing faster than our competitors, wages are rising, energy bills are falling, we've started cutting your taxes and I will keep cutting your taxes. That is my pitch to you, that's a choice at this election, their taxes that he wants to put up on you are coming. He won't rule out revaluing your council tax bands, every pensioner that is watching should realise that under a future Labour government, for the first time in our country's history, the state pension will be subject to a retirement tax under Labour. That's what their plans are. Those are the numbers, black and white. We're not going to do that. We're going to have the triple lock plus. But for the millions of pensioners that are watching, be very worried, because you're going to face a retirement tax under the Labour Party.
Well we have to see. Cost is, is one aspect to it. But I think it's important this country retains a strong defence at all times.

Well Nick is wrong about our plans because of course we are going to raise five billion from tax evasion and aggressive tax avoidance as we've done in this Parliament and that's part of the balance plan that also involves putting more money into our NHS and cutting taxes for working people. The very wealthy include some of the tax avoiders and evaders. But here's the point. We've got to understand why the deficit matters and why we got here. And the problem in the real choice is with Ed Miliband, who still thinks the last Labour government didn't tax too much, borrow too much, and spend too much. And if you don't understand the mistakes of the past, you can't provide the leadership for the future.

Once again, I think there's this complete confusion between the government and the economy, and Gordon Brown doesn't seem to understand that actually, to get the economy going, you've got to help help businesses employ people. You've got to cut their red tape, cut their regulation. Let me tell you one thing government could really do to help: government is an enormous purchaser of goods and services, and yet it hardly buys anything from small and medium-sized enterprises. We say government should give a quarter of its contracts to the small firms, the ones that are actually going to be the success stories of tomorrow. Let's make it easier for firms to register with the government, so they can buy services and sell services to the government. That would actually help to get them going, the great businesses of tomorrow. That's what we need to build.
No, you reduce the number of courses. I mean, there are a number of non-courses in our university, right across the board at the moment, that are nothing to our economy.

Look, I think the fundamental imperative, right, the first commandment in politics is to be honest. And when people ask me honestly whether I believe Brexit will hurt Wales or help Wales, I have to be honest. It will hurt Wales. You know, it'll rip the heart out of the economy in Wales. And that's what we sincerely believe. And I respect people, actually, that sincerely disagree with us. But I can't say something that I don't believe, even if it might be politically more convenient for me to do so. In terms of the broader question, I agree with you, right? And I agree with Catherine. There is a deep dysfunction at the heart of our democracy. Brexit, if you like, has brought it to the fore. It's thrown it into sharp relief, but it was already there. I mean, when I got elected, to Westminster, you know, in 2001, I couldn't believe what I saw, you know, men in tights opening doors for you, you know, it felt like a gentleman's club, and I used my words very advisedly, you know, I say instead of spending five billion pounds on refurbishing, you know, that crumbling pile on the banks of the Thames, shut it down, turn it into a museum, it's full of dinosaurs anyway, move the Parliament, move the Parliament, move the Parliament to the north of England.

We have a deal that keeps the whole of the UK together as we come out of the EU. And of course, Jeremy Corbyn and the Labour Party, be in no doubt about it, in order to secure power and the keys to number 10, are going to do a deal, and they probably already have done a deal, with Nicola Sturgeon and the SNP. to form a Corbyn-Sturgeon coalition. And the price of that deal, the price of Nicola Sturgeon's support, let's be in no doubt, she's made it absolutely clear, would be a second referendum on the union with Scotland. I don't think we need another referendum on the union with Scotland. I've ruled it out. We had one in 2014. The people of Scotland voted very substantially to stay in the most successful political partnership of the last 300 years. Why put this country, this great country, through another referendum on Scotland? And why have a second referendum?

Well, you know, firstly, let me explain my position, and then I'll perhaps speculate on Jeremy Corbyn's, if I may. Well, you don't need to speculate, because he told us right here, so would you accept it? I'll speculate on what his position might be after the election, as opposed to what he said here today. Oh, I see. OK. But my position is one of principle. There'll be differences of opinion in this room on Scottish independence. Of course there is a difference of opinion in Scotland on Scottish independence. I would not be asking Jeremy Corbyn to support independence. but simply to accept the principle that that is a question that should be decided by the people of Scotland, not by Westminster. Now, I guess, if I can turn to the position that I think Jeremy Corbyn might take after an election, I've heard some of what Jeremy Corbyn has been saying to you this evening. He spoke with a lot of passion. I don't agree with him on everything, but I do agree with a great deal of what he was talking about, about public spending, health, austerity... But the key thing, as you said, not for the first two years. But that's the point I'm coming on to. I'm asking you, having heard Jeremy Corbyn, do you think he's going to walk away from the chance to end austerity, to protect the NHS, to stop universal credit, simply because he wants, for a couple of years, to prevent Scotland to have the right to self-determination? Jeremy Corbyn supports the right of self-determination for almost every other country in the world. I'm not sure he's going to compromise the chance to have a Labour government for that issue.

To boost manufacturing, we have trebled the number of apprenticeships from 70,000 in 1997 to over 200,000 now. We want to raise that number considerably over the next few years. At the same time, there are more students going to university than ever before in our history, and I'm pleased to say that the majority of students are now women. To help manufacturing, the regional development agencies have the power to support individual businesses, and there's more than 20,000 firms in this region receiving help with cashflow under our programme Time To Pay. But the problem is now, you can't take money out of the economy now, as David proposes, and hope that businesses can survive with orders. You've got to keep the money in the economy. You can't take investment allowances away from businesses, as David proposes to do to pay for his corporation tax cut for banks, without manufacturing suffering. And you cannot help the region if you take away the Regional Development Agency that's doing so much good. So we've got to face up to the fact that we've got to act now. We cannot take money out of the economy, and we've got to support manufacturing and not withdraw the support that David would do with his policies.
Well I, we've talked about the ERM. We're the only party to have learned our lesson from that. We'll talk - I'll tell you about the Poll Tax if you like.
Angela, which of your party's U-turns are you most proud of? The ditching of the 28 billion climate investment plan, keeping the cruel two-child benefit cap, or one of the others?

Well, we've got a plan for our social care workforce with a fair pay agreement so that we retain the good people we've got working in care. We've got a plan for construction, for example, to build those houses. But how do you know it'll be the right number of houses if you don't know how many people will live here? But we need 1.5 million houses because too many people are waiting for a house that they want to live in. The dream of home ownership is real for so many people. They want to own their own home. The average age now for somebody to get on the housing ladder is 36 or 37. It is impossible or near on impossible for young people to get their first home. We have to build more houses. That means we have to take tough decisions on things like planning. I was at housing development here just in Outer York this morning, 650 houses being bought by locals, but it took 15 years from the plans being submitted to the houses being built.

Yes, so, Hamza, thank you for the question. And your question seemed premised on a concern that perhaps we'd see mass migration movements in response to some of these challenges. Now, I don't think mass migration would be particularly good either for the country where people were leaving or indeed potentially Western countries to which people might be going. So we should try and find ways of avoiding that happening. And there are two components I think to that. The first of those is peace and the second is prosperity. Now in relation to peace, we've seen, you mentioned Libya as an example. Now we've seen Russia actively stoking up conflict in Libya. We've seen Russia intervening in Syria in the past as well. We've seen China intervening in a number of African countries. I think we need to be very mindful of the inflammatory effect that particularly Russia and China have, and as the West seek to contain that and prevent it causing instability in countries like Libya that you referenced. Now, the second component besides peace is prosperity. And I think one of the best ways that we in the West, including the United Kingdom, can promote prosperity is through trade. People can become more prosperous by building businesses and by exporting and importing. Now, you know, the UK has recently jumped up the export league table. We're now, I think, fourth in the world for exports. But I'd like to see us, you know, using our free trade freedoms we now have to trade more with other countries, particularly in the developing THOMAS-SYMONDS: Nick Thomas-Simmons. THOMAS-SYMONDS: The climate crisis and the deep war, I think, will continue to be drivers around the world of people being unable to stay in their homes. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Their home's going to be extremely unsafe. THOMAS-SYMONDS: First of all, in foreign policy, which the other question was directed at, we need to set an example ourselves, first of all, that we are a country that abides by international law. THOMAS-SYMONDS: To have conservative cabinet ministers, no less, talking about breaking international law in a limited and specific way in the House of Commerce THOMAS-SYMONDS: Commons did us a great deal of damage. THOMAS-SYMONDS: I used to be shadowing international trade and I travelled the world and people were just astonished that Britain, famous for its rule of law, could have a cabinet minister doing that. THOMAS-SYMONDS: And secondly, on our international aid budget, it also is very, very important that we use it where there is war, where people are driven from their homes, so we can help people in their region so they can actually return to their home as quickly as they can.

I think one of the crucial things we need to do in this country is ensure that actually we are enabling people here in the UK to be skilled up to do jobs. I think sometimes what we've seen is people being brought in from overseas because we haven't done sufficient in this country. That's why I think technical education is so important.

OK, Flair, Ellen from Plaid Cymru. Plaid Cymru would like to see policing devolved to Wales so that we can better invest in our policing and to keep our communities safe in Wales. But what we also do need to do, as you were saying, is prevent extremism of all forms from happening. so that we can tackle this and investing in youth services, in community services, so that we are, you know, promoting community cohesion rather than division is crucial and politicians have such a responsibility here when you have a public platform not to, you know, target individuals or target a specific religion because by doing that we are increasing the division and hate within our communities and within society and we have to be so careful We also need to look at how we are combating extremism, so the Prevent Agenda which is currently being run by the Conservatives. Many people say that they are feeling targeted. That is not going to promote community cohesion and that is not going to help bring people who maybe are feeling isolated back into community.

Be honest about the risk. Why are you doing a jobs tax? You're going to take £1 billion at least out of the schools this year. Simply not true. If you were elected in a budget in July, you've got to take £6 billion out of the system, other than health and defence. Where does that money come from? You've promised you'll take £6 billion out. It can only end up with the loss of thousands of jobs, including teachers. You will not back us and support us on keeping education spending rising. Why won't you support educational spending as we do?
Well, 16 year olds at the moment, er, are able to get married, so we do have a rather inconsistent approach to the age of maturity.

No, I mean the big problem is numbers. I'm afraid this generation are paying the price for a population explosion. Eight million more people living in the country than there were when Tony Blair came to power. We've not been able to plan for public services or housing because we just didn't know how many people would come. I do think the argument about affordable housing being built. At the moment, frankly, that is not going to happen.

Look, I'm not proud of some of the votes we did, Fiona. I'm not proud of them. and I want to overturn them, and a lot of our policies are changing some of the things that we had to agree to back then. OK. But look what happened when we left office. I lost my seat. The Conservatives were governing by themselves. The first budget with George Osborne, they cut welfare spending by £12 billion, hitting the poorest in our country. We stopped that, and people often... I don't blame people, but they forget what we stopped as well.

Nigel Farage and I'm sure your good self over the last decade or two have taught this country saying Britain should be more like Norway and Switzerland. What are Norway and Switzerland? They are out of the European Union and in the single market. Leaving the single market is a political choice and let's remember the impact of that choice. Half of the things we sell as a country go to the single market in Europe. The impact on living standards, on jobs, on our ability to create a future for our children massively depend on Britain being in the single market. For Theresa May, with Jeremy Corbyn's help, to take us out is to damage our children's future. It must be resisted.

Well, firstly, what we would do is we would scrap HS2, which is nothing but a white elephant, which is only there to benefit London. That will save us £50 billion. We would then have an industrial strategy which would promote employment. But let's not forget the opportunities that Brexit will give us once we leave the European Union. We'll be able to sign trade deals all over the globe.

Angela, all of us are aware of what Wes has said. And you may deny it, but the reality is he said he wants privatisation. It goes beyond just hospitals as well. And when we talk about staffing, because staffing is an enormous issue, the elephant in the room is the fact that it's not migration is the problem. It's the fact that we need more people coming in to work in our hospitals and to work in our social care sector, which has been decimated by your Brexit.

in the next five years? I think the nature crisis is as critically important as the climate change crisis. What we need both nationally and internationally is legislation to tackle the extinction crisis, placing a legal obligation on this and every future government to to act for the recovery of nature. The two crises, which are interconnected, they have different drivers, deforestation internationally, pollution, and hunting in some areas as well, but they need to be now treated with the same level of seriousness, because obviously, as was said in the film, we are facing the mass extinction of a million species. It is what you are going to do about it.

Politics can make a difference, Mary, but I would say the politicians in this country have been treating the people as mugs for far too long. They've been sort of saying, look, we can do everything. Vote for us and we'll solve all your problems. Just let us pass a few more laws, spend a bit more money, pass some more regulations, and all the trouble will be fixed. And it's not really true. It's actually quite a big lie. The truth is, if you really want to change things, If you want safer streets, if you want better schools, yes the government's got its role, but we've all got our responsibilities too. We need to bring up our children properly. We've got to make sure we work with the school. We've got to make sure we help the police. We've got to make sure we have discipline in our schools. Real solutions to the problems of the country I think come when we all say, I've got responsibilities as well that go beyond paying my taxes and obeying the law. It's my society, it's my country, I'm going to elect some politicians but I want to join with them to change the country and make it a better place. I think that's a much more honest answer for how we really get the change we need in our country.

Well I think we are in a dangerously unstable world. But trident nuclear weapons don't make me feel any safer. I do not, under any circumstances, in which anyone is even seriously thinking about using those hideous weapons of mass destruction. We've followed a lot on international policy in recent years. We followed America into Iraq and Afghanistan, disastrously for our forces and for the state of those countries and the state of that whole region. How about we take a lead and say we will rid Britain of these hideous weapons of mass destruction? Weapons that surely everybody thinks we can never use. Instead, let's focus on increasing, and here I go in entirely the opposite direction to Nigel Farage and so many other things, increasing aid, increasing diplomatic efforts, and making this a safer, secure world, tackling hunger, supporting democracy. That's how we get safer.

What I can guarantee is that we'll bring an end to this debate and we will have a final say for the people of this country. We cannot go on forever saying we represent the 48 or the 52. I want to lead a government that represents the whole country and the whole community. So we will, within three months, negotiate a leave agreement with the European Union that will protect trade and jobs and the peace process in Northern Ireland. and put that alongside remain in a referendum within six months. And that will be the end of the matter. My role as a leader of my party, as the Prime Minister would be, to ensure that decision is carried out whatever it is. But nobody voted to lose their job or to lose trade with Europe. people voted for many, many reasons, but I think we've got to come together and bring this issue to an end, not go down the road of sweetheart deals with the USA. Boris Johnson.
We agreed that not just the normal armed officers should be increased, but actually the counter-terrorism specialist officers as well.

Well, Obama brought him a so-called Muslim ban, but no one criticised him. He was the one that named the seven countries where a total stop on anyone coming into America should come from. Trump implemented it. And so these really, in many cases, are very, very false arguments. Look, Donald Trump's style is very American. It may not be to everybody's taste, but just think about this. He is our most important friend in the world. And at a time, at a time of huge global uncertainty, we should think about not just trade with America, but security, making sure that NATO is not destroyed by the European Union. These things really matter. And can I just say, the biggest lie of this entire election campaign is that Trump wants to buy the NHS. He does not. And he will say so, I'm sure, this week when he's here.

re-offending. There are 74 convicted terrorists out on our streets. There are 400 returning jihadi fighters on our streets who've come back from Syria. Surely whether they serve six years or 12 years isn't the point. Why should they ever be let out again into society?

You've completely rubbished your policy. You built 57 council houses in Wales last year. You've just said you didn't build any. The year before that, you built zero. Robert. The year before that, you built zero. A year before that you also could have still been a Robertson. I'm holding you to account for your record.

Anna, I have every sympathy with you because, frankly, today in our country, people who try and work hard and save, and obey the rules, and do the right thing. All too often, they just find hurdle after hurdle put in their way, whereas people who actually don't play by the rules, who don't think about saving and don't think about their behaviour often get rewarded and that's not right. What can we do to help you? First of all, we've got to get spending under control so we stop putting your taxes up. We also say we should have no stamp duty on the first £250,000 that people... if you can buy a property for less than that. I think that would help. But above all, we've also got to build more houses. I think there's no doubt in my mind that we've got to change the planning system right now. It so militates against actually people building houses. We think you've got to scrap these top-down targets that make local communities so angry but reward councils that go ahead and build homes for families like yours.

Well, you know, people understandably ask me that all the time, and what I say, and I know it sounds like a typical politician's way of avoiding the answer before I say it is... Oh, no, we don't want that. Well... Not tonight. I know, because I genuinely don't like to answer questions that way, but if I come to that point, then I'll set out how I'll cross that bridge when I come to it. And, yes, I've thought a lot about that, but I don't want to talk about that right now, because that is me conceding that these Westminster politicians actually have a right to block Scotland's ability to choose our own future and actually we're in an election campaign right now and people in Scotland have an opportunity to send a very clear message that they want to be in charge of their own future and that's what I'm asking them to do. But the government does have a right to block it. Your government has the veto on it. I don't think they have a moral or a democratic right to block the right. It has never been legally challenged. I have always accepted and I continue to accept that the transfer of power is necessary and desirable. But I don't believe they have a moral or democratic right. We are in an election campaign and there is an opportunity for Scotland to make that abundantly clear.

and indeed we're making significant progress in relation to that. But of course poverty and the attainment gap are interlinked and the levers of power over poverty are determined by Westminster because all roads do indeed lead back to Westminster.

I believe the gap between our career, professional and political classes and ordinary people has never been wider and that has been exemplified this evening. They basically are not very different at all and they are not prepared to stand up and fight for ordinary folk in this country by confronting the tough issues. I am. I'm unafraid to say what I think. And whether you agree with everything I say or not, know that when I say it, I do so because I believe in it. I'm patriotic. I believe in this country. I believe in the people of this country. I believe we'd be so much better if we governed ourselves, controlled our borders, and gave ordinary workers a chance against the takeover of the giant corporate companies who've now basically taken over our political parties. I'll fight for the little man, for the little woman. I'll fight for this country. This is the chance for the most radical political change we've seen for decades. Vote UKIP if you want things to change.

I think we all have to think about the balanced, healthy diets that we eat. We also have to think much more carefully about where our food is sourced from. Absolutely, the practices that are used in agriculture and farming have to change, have to become much more energy efficient and environmentally friendly. But of course, again, talking about Scotland, we have the climate and the environment to actually support sustainable, high-quality produce, and we should be supporting our farmers to do that. A lot has already been done just now, but we need to do more of that. Land use, I absolutely agree about land use. I've been listening to all of the parties here today talk about the number of trees they want to plant. Last year in Scotland, 22 million trees were planted, 84% of the UK total.

Well, thank you, Joan, for your question. What we need in our country is to recognise that people who come here and work hard and contribute to our companies and to our communities, they help make this a great country. But we do need immigration that's controlled and fair. And in recent decades, it has been too high, and I want to see it come down. Now, we have reduced immigration from outside the EU, not least by putting in place a cap on people coming to work here. But inside the EU, not least because we've created more jobs than the rest of the European Union put together, immigration has been very high. So we need to bring that under control. And here are the proposals that I will put in place as Prime Minister. First, if you're coming from the European Union, you won't get unemployment benefit. Second, if you've been here for six months and don't have a job, you'll have to go home. Third, if you've come here and worked, you'll have to work for four years paying into the system before you get out of the system. And finally, if you leave your family at home, you won't be able to send child benefit, as you can now, back to your family at home. Those are fair changes that I can deliver.

Perhaps what Caroline was trying to do was not so much restructuring, Tim, as renationalising the health service in England, because it should be in public hands. That is where our National Health Service belongs. On the issue of funding, I don't want to see our NHS in Scotland broken up and privatised in any way. One of the first things I did when I was Scottish Health Secretary was bring a hospital that had been contracted out to the private sector back into the NHS. family. The question was about financing. We have a costy plan in Scotland to increase the budget by 2 billion by the end of this Parliament. Health spending per head in Scotland is 7% higher than it is in England and if the other parties were to match that, that would be a significant increase for health elsewhere in the UK. But on this question of tax and austerity, be under no illusion, the Tories are going further than they need to on austerity. If we had a more sensible fiscal plan that still got the deficit and debt down, that would free up more money to invest in our health service. And if the Tories weren't giving a tax cut to the top 10% of earners and planning to cut corporation tax even further, that would be even more money.

Well, let me explain. We've said that there's a £30 billion adjustment that needs to be made, and the other political parties have voted for this too. And we've said that breaks down into £13 billion that needs to be saved in government departments, £12 billion in welfare, and £5 billion from cracking down on tax evasion and aggressive tax avoidance. Now, when it comes to the welfare, we've identified for instance, freezing in-work benefits, like unemployment benefit, for two years to raise some of that money. But the £12 billion, right, that, the £12 billion... £10 billion of which you've yet to explain. That compares with £20 billion that was saved in the last parliament, in this current parliament, by this government on welfare. So this is well within the range of what we can do if we continue with the sort of plans that we put in place. Do you know where these cuts will fall? Well, we know that it is possible to make the savings in welfare, like we made. We made 20 billion in welfare savings.

Well, let me perhaps set out some of the things that we've done. So since we came to office, for example, GDP per capita in Scotland, the growth of the economy, has been faster in Scotland than in the rest of the United Kingdom. Productivity in the Scottish economy has grown faster under the SNP government than it has in the rest of the United Kingdom. I said a moment ago the measures were taken to tackle child poverty, which is keeping 100,000 children out of poverty in Scotland today. We've seen also the significant expansion of early learning and childcare, which is delivering the best start for children and young people in our society. So what I would say is that there's a whole range of decisions that have been made in Scotland that are good for Scotland, and that's an illustration of what an independent country could do if we had the powers, the broader range of powers, because I accept we need to improve economic performance in Scotland, we need to eradicate poverty, child poverty within our society and we can only do that if we have the broader range of powers and we've demonstrated with the limited powers that we've got how we can make a difference.

No, they should not be frightened of an incoming Labour government because our country's economy relies on many small and medium-sized enterprises that are the motor of our economy that often have great difficulty in accessing capital and accessing support for what they're trying to do. The biggest businesses will be asked to pay a little bit more in corporation tax, but it will be lower than it was in 2010, and indeed lower than the average for most industrial countries. What I want to do is ensure that there is an economy thriving in every part of the country. And so that means the resources. It means an infrastructure system, roads and railways all across the country. It means broadband in every part of our country that's free to everyone to use. And that will mean that businesses can be set up anywhere. It also It is also clear in our manifesto here that what we're keen to do is ensure there is a better trained workforce in our society, so we will be promoting apprenticeships and skilled training at every level and give them the same value as academic education in universities. And so I enjoy working with all the business organisations, setting out clearly our views on public ownership, our views on taxation and our views on investment for the future.

Well, of course, the country I represent voted to remain in the European Union by more than 60%. Even some people who voted to leave, and I suspect this is true across the UK, are utterly fed up of the mess that Brexit has become, of the chaos that we've seen played out over the last three and a half years. My policy on Brexit, of giving people across the UK the opportunity to think again and to escape the Brexit mess, yes, I do think can help to bring people back together, because the biggest con in this election is the slogan from the Tories, that if they win this election, Brexit gets done. We only move into the next and probably more chaotic part of the process, and, of course, a no-deal Brexit would be back on the horizon next year. On the point about the union, it's not the sign of a strong union. where Scotland can be dragged out of the EU against our will. If Scotland is independent, we get to be in a partnership of equals with the rest of the UK, which is a much better position to be in.
No, they're not going to be frozen because they'll be uprated every year, as they should be.

So democracy does not stop a single election, but for the people of Scotland, 50% of whom believe in independence, they have to determine whether they want to vote for a party who will put the NHS first, action on the cost of living, rejoining the European single market, and of course seeking to develop net zero, and who also believe in Scotland having the right to determine its own future, or whether they want to vote for a unionist Labour party that doesn't want that to happen, that wants to stop Scotland's democracy.

Let me just bring in Nicola Sturgeon. Well, health services everywhere are under real pressure. That's true in Scotland. It's true right across the UK. Firstly, we've got to invest more in Scotland. We spend £136 more per head than elsewhere in the UK. We've got to have reform. We've already integrated health and social care. And we've got to keep recruiting professionals into the health service. One of the debates in England is about bringing back the nurse bursary. In Scotland we never abolished the nurse bursary in the first place, so that's why we've got 2,500 more nurses today than we had previously. So investment and reform and support for the health service is absolutely crucial.

I think it did create a problem when free movement became kind of the same as the freedom to claim. I don't think the freedom to move is the same as the freedom to claim. And so, even though I am pro-European, not because I think it's perfect, but I think it makes sense for an open economy to be part of the world's largest marketplace, even though I am pro-European, I decided as Deputy Prime Minister in this coalition government to break that link so that people couldn't arrive here from elsewhere in the European Union and claim benefits, no questions asked, on the first day they arrive. I, by the way, would also point out it is a two-way street. There are roughly about as many Brits living and working elsewhere in the European Union than there are Europeans working in our country. So we kind of got to remember that it's also a freedom which many, many, many of us British people also benefit from elsewhere in Europe.

I'm actually very proud of our record. We are the country that has decarbonised faster than any other major economy. We're leading the way in banning the use of single-use plastics. And this year, we passed a very important milestone. For the first time ever, we generated more of our power from carbon-free sources than fossil fuels. So that actually is a record. I'm very proud to stand on it.

Well, high housing costs are a result of a shortage of social housing, and that has come about partly as a result of Margaret Thatcher's right-to-buy policy. Not enough housing has been built. And the problem is exacerbated when more people are out of work, because then more people require social housing. And on the question of immigration, When we have more immigrants paying into the tax pot, if they were to be removed out of the system, then there would be even less money in the tax pot to build more houses. So I don't accept the argument that immigration is part of the problem. I think in some ways it can be part of the solution.

What we have said is that we want to see the working week come down. The average working week across the country is 38 hours. How many days? And we achieve bringing it down by improved productivity, industries and services. There is no plan to bring in a four-day week in the NHS, but there is a plan to properly invest in our NHS because I am fed up with seeing people queuing and waiting and being in desperate straits trying to get an appointment to see a GP or to go into hospital.

Well, listen, those were... I have to say it was very difficult governing with the Conservatives. We couldn't get everything we wanted. But we were faced with a choice as a minister. You either had to stay in and fight inside the government or leave. I think the easy choice for me would have been to leave the government, vote against it and tour the media studios and complain. The hard choice was to stay in, roll my sleeves up and really fight. And as explained earlier, the Tories didn't want us to bring in free school meals. We fought for that, and we got it. They wanted, actually, to cut spending on welfare far greater than happened. For example, in the second budget, when inflation was about 5.2%, they wanted to freeze benefits and pensions.

Thank you. Natalie Bennett. I think David Cameron raised the issue of social care. And this is a really critically important one that has huge impacts on the NHS but is important in its own right. Help the Age says there's 2 million people aged over 65 who need social care. 800,000 of them are not getting any help at all. And it's worth thinking for a second about what social care actually is. You might need help getting out of bed and bathing in the morning. You might need help eating. You might need help just living your life. And you're not getting that help you need. And that's why the Green Party is calling for free social care for the over 65s to be provided to everybody who needs it. Thank you, Natalie Bennett. David Cameron.

If people get early detection, and that means screening, I had a lady write to me and said that she would not be alive today if we hadn't introduced screening and we hadn't given the chance to see a specialist within two weeks. What David is not telling you is that while we are using the national insurance to pay for health, to pay for policing and to pay for schools, he won't give him the guarantee on policing and schools. Mr Cameron. health service.

Thank you very much for what you're doing and I believe passionately we need more nurses in the NHS. I've been in power in office for about 130 days and everything I've seen convinced me this is now the time to invest massively in our NHS. So what we're doing is putting in the biggest cash boost, £34 billion more, 50,000 more nurses, and I should unpack that figure if people complain about it, but it means retaining the 19,000 who might otherwise drop out of the service and then recruiting another 31,000 new nurses, plus we're bringing back the £5,000 bursary. One thing that we're doing that I think is sensible and that our Australian-style points-based system allows us to do is to encourage nurses from overseas to come to fill some of the gaps by shortening the time for their visa application. Now, what I think is totally incomprehensible is to have a system where you try to get more nurses and more staff into the NHS and simultaneously ask the whole of the public sector to work a four-day week. Thank you very much. That would be bad for patients and bad for the NHS.

Well, actually, the IFS also said that it's right to identify welfare as a challenge that needs to be addressed, and the IFS also acknowledged that when the Conservatives previously set out savings that they would get from the welfare bill, the IFS didn't think it would happen, and, in fact, they were delivered, and I'm sure BBC Verify will put that quote on their website. But, look, two years ago, I warned repeatedly about what Liz Truss's economic policies would do to our country, even if it wasn't what people wanted to hear at the time. I was right then.

But the argument is, of course... If you're a Democrat, why not give them another go? So I fully respect whatever people feel on both sides of the issue on independence. I'm bound to say, as a Northerner, it breaks my heart the thought that you want to leave us lot saddled with the Tories. Please don't go. But the second thing I'd just say on this, is that we as a party stood in the Holyrood elections, the Scottish parliamentary elections last May on a manifesto committed to Scotland as part of the United Kingdom and so keeping to our mandate it's right that we say that we continue to support the Union and then not being a rerun of the Scottish referendum.

Very tough one, this. You know, people with savings and houses are terrified that years in care mean they can't leave money to their children. And yet, for the government to pay for all of it appears to be insurmountable. I don't think anybody has got an answer to this problem. Yet, I really don't think they have.

I recognise that you genuinely believe that, but let me say, from the people that I've spoken to in the Labour Party, when I speak to Luciana Berger who joined the Liberal Democrats, when I speak to Louise Ellman who felt that she had to leave the Labour Party, and when I speak to Jewish people right across the country, They do not feel. They do not feel that Jeremy Corbyn is fighting racism and anti-Semitism in the Labour Party. They know what that feels like. And I'm going to listen to them and trust them on this issue.

You know what? I really want to address this, Michelle. We passed a law in Parliament. People have to bear in mind, right? You're right. We do have to smash the gangs. What does that mean? We passed a law in Parliament. We passed new laws so that our law enforcement agencies could use them. You know what? They've arrested 1,000 criminals and people smugglers. They've sentenced them to hundreds of years, exactly as you would expect us to do. That's what we did. Do you know what? The Labour Party voted against that law. So when you stand there and say, smash the gangs, like on every other issue, you say one thing, you do another, you just don't know which Keir Starmer you're going to get.

They don't, actually, because our deal is a great deal. Let me just make one point. I do find it slightly curious, to say the least, Nick, to be lectured about the union between Great Britain and Northern Ireland by a man who all his life, political life, has campaigned to break up that union and actually supported for four decades the IRA in their campaign violently, violently to destroy it. I must say I find it a curiosity. Jeremy Corbyn, what is your answer to that?

Certainly not under Boris Johnson. This Conservative Party is off with Nigel Farage. Boris Johnson's been endorsed by Tommy Robinson. I mean, my goodness, they are so far off the charts. Absolutely not.

Well, I'd also like to express my deepest sadness that I feel for those that have lost their loved ones. Look, I think there are serious questions that need to be answered seriously. The 2016 report, the Aitchison report, which looked at this question of parole for convicted terrorists, it seems from the report authors' claims that the majority of those recommendations haven't been implemented. The former director of public prosecutions here in the Northwest says that he raised these issues directly with the Prime Minister. I don't know what happened as a result of that. and I think it would be wrong to speculate. We need sober reflection, not a rush to judgment in an election campaign. And, you know, I think that maybe I'm old-fashioned, but at a time when terrorists are trying to divide us, I want to be the... I want the Prime Minister, even in an election, to be the Prime Minister of all of us. And I think the language that he used this morning, talking about a lefty government, not only showed a huge disrespect to those that have lost their loved ones, it did a disservice to a democracy, and he showed yet again that he's unfit to be Prime Minister.

Let me respond to that directly. Ultimately, if we are going to move this conversation on to economic growth and what comes next, because that is probably where it is going to go, Angela said she wants economic growth but she didn't mention the single market or freedom of movement or the fact that the Labour Party have walked away from £28 billion of investment in net zero technologies, whilst the United States, Europe, and everyone else is going down that route to grow the economy. The Westminster consensus is alive and well. And it doesn't serve you well. And ultimately, it will be the people who need it most, the people who can't afford the shops, the people who are ultimately living in poverty who will feel the consequences of Westminster's priorities.

I was pointing out that the IFS said that neither Labour or the Tories' spending plans We are credible. Let me be candid, my party is not going to be running the Treasury after this election, but we might hold the balance of power and therefore we will want to see investment in the right priorities, investment in the National Health Service, an end to the welfare cuts that have caused misery. more investment in the transition to a net zero economy, pension justice, particularly for WASPI women. And how will we help to ensure that? We'll bring to bear the experience we have in government. In Scotland, the SNP has reformed the income tax system, making it more progressive. Those at the top pay a bit more, those at the bottom pay a bit less. And we'll also argue that we shouldn't be spending money on the wrong priorities. Why should we be investing £200 billion in new Trident nuclear weapons, for example?

I absolutely accept the way that you feel, and I might not be able to convince you on this, and I recognise that. I do think that scrapping universal credit on its own isn't some kind of magic bullet. We need to have a system that provides support to people. I think the biggest problems with universal credit is the lack of money that's in it. not the principle that you try to have a simpler benefit system instead of there being multiple different payments that creates more complexity. So I think the principle of making it simpler is a good one, but it needs to have the money behind it, and that's what we're setting out to do.

Sue, thank you very much for your question, which I think is on a lot of people's minds, this question of respect and integrity in politics. And you have to lead from the front on issues like this. I think over the last 14 years, politics has become too much about self-entitlement and MPs thinking about what they can get for themselves. And one of the important choices, and the thing that I would like to do if I'm elected Prime Minister, is to reset politics so that politics returns to public service. In relation to these gambling allegations, the instinct of these people to think the first thing they should do is try to make money was the wrong instinct and we have to change that. But it is also a question of leadership because this isn't just, you know, what's happened this week, last week in the Gambling Commission. We saw Partygate earlier in this Parliament. The Prime Minister himself was convicted and fined for breaking the rules which he brought in and imposed on everybody else. And of course, we've seen what happened with the COVID contracts and the huge amounts of money that were made. This takes leadership. What I did when one of my team was alleged to have been involved and investigated by the Gambling Commission, they were suspended within minutes because I knew it made it really important to be swift. the Prime Minister delayed and delayed and delayed until eventually he was a bullied into taking action.

No, that's not true. Because we're not raising the basic rate of income tax at a time when inflation is rising. I don't think it's right to ask low and middle income earners to pay more. But we have decided to take a different approach on the higher rate of income tax. So again, unlike the Conservatives at Westminster, we're not giving a tax cut to people who pay the higher rate of tax. Instead, we're investing that revenue in our schools and hospitals, and I think that's absolutely the right thing to do.

Listen, American politics is in fact more divided in many ways than our politics. But all these false claims, you may not like him, he is president of the USA and that relationship matters. You are so anti-American, you're prepared to put your hatred of Trump above our national interest. And that's a big mistake.

Boris Johnson is going to spend years negotiating with the USA. He's going to spend years ruling the fact he didn't reach a proper agreement with the EU before leaving. And as the documents we've shown today indicate, he hasn't been exactly straightforward on the issues surrounding trade with Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland. And so I do think we need a bit of openness from the government. We forced them to release the documents, or we released them for them, actually, a week ago, which showed the access that the US were demanding to our public services. And today, the news came out of what's happening with Northern Ireland. for people who didn't hear the news, what are you claiming the documents show? They show quite clearly that there are going to be charges, there are going to be customs checks, there are going to be restrictions on trade between Britain and Northern Ireland. Thank you very much.

Well, I do think it is about dignity and security in old age to be able to say to elderly people that you should be able to, if you've worked hard and saved hard, you should be able to pass your home on to your children rather than have to use it to pay for your care. And that's why we have this plan that if you put aside £8,000 when you become 65, then you'd have that guarantee that your home would be there for your children because you've done the right thing. Now, that doesn't solve the whole problem. But it does seem to me that's a very good start, ending the unfairness of all those thousands of people who've done the right thing, who've worked hard in their lives, who then get penalised and punished by the system.

I want to live in a world that's free of the danger of a nuclear holocaust. Any nuclear weapon used anywhere in the world is a disaster for all of us. And I've spent my life working with peace organisations, working with the United Nations over the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty in order to bring about global disarmament. I would see my responsibility as our Prime Minister to contribute to a world of peace in the future. That means being very serious about the dangers, very serious about promoting what China is trying to achieve in Korea with the six-party talks, be very serious in what President Obama achieved with his agreement with Iran, but also look at the other threats that we face as well. Cyber attacks are very serious. We saw that with the attack on our National Health Service only a couple of weeks ago. Random acts of terror, such as we saw in Manchester, are a real threat as well. We have to defend our way of life, our society and our country, by an intelligent engagement with the rest of the world. And I want to bring about our contribution to a more peaceful world.

I'm very clear that what we need to do here, we need to get our security, our borders back. They've lost control. 50,000 people have come. We've got to smash the gangs that are doing this in the first place. Smash the gangs.
Channel 4 have highlighted what racist party you are and what you've been doing and what you've been saying in the north of England.

The truth is when it comes to ending welfare dependency, Labour have had 13 years to deal with it and haven't, and the Liberal Democrats have got virtually nothing in their manifesto. I think one of the big senses of unfairness people have on this issue is people who've worked hard, saved, put money aside, when they go into residential care they have to pay every penny including selling their home, whereas people who haven't saved get the whole thing paid for free. Now I'd love to make it all free - but you can't - there's no money - so we say if you can put aside eight thousand pounds when you turn 65, you should get your care for free. I think that would remove one of the major unfairnesses - it doesn't solve the whole problem - but it does remove one of the big unfairnesses of our system right now.

The whole purpose of this was that people used to be able, used not to be able rather, to get an appointment with the GP within the 48 hours. Now obviously it shouldn't work in that way because that would be absurd.

Two things we should do and one thing we shouldn't do. First of all, we should build more homes that are part rent, part mortgage, because it gives people - I've seen this in my own constituency - the chance to get one foot on the housing ladder. As circumstances change, they can increase the mortgage and reduce the rent. I think that helps. Also, rewarding responsibility. People who live in housing association homes, or council homes, with a record of good behaviour, give them a stake in that house, so when they move, they have the start of some capital to move up the housing ladder. One thing we shouldn't do, and this is in the Liberal Democrat manifesto, is putting VAT on building new homes. That would just lift the price of new homes even higher out of people, like Anna's reach. I think it would be a big, big mistake. Once again, it just doesn't make any sense.

Hang on, Richard, we know. I'll bring you in, Richard Tyson. And in the last few months, these are applications which are a leading indicator, are down 30%. People we need. The measures we are taking already will reduce net migration by 300,000. OK. And in addition to that, we will come to the measures in detail. We're going to be imposing a parliamentary cap, so those are very clear, tangible and credible actions.

I accept it's been tough in these last two years with the recession, but what we've tried to do, when people are in difficulty, is provide tax credits. A half million people have got tax credits when they've been on short time and are trying to get through this recession. We've brought down the basic rate of tax from 23 pence when we came in to 20 pence. At the same time, we've raised the top rate of tax above £150,000 to 50 pence so that that's fair to ordinary, hard-working families. I believe in fairness, but one thing I don't believe in is the Conservative policy which would cut child tax credits, but at the same time give an inheritance tax cut to the 3,000 richest people in the country of £200,000. That's not fairness, that's the same old Conservative Party, tax cuts for the very rich, and cutting child tax credits of the very poor. It's simply not fair.
Because it was overturning an awful lot our traditional liberties and ways of going about things. What they've now got at their disposal and it's going to be reviewed.

Well, I believe that is still the right ambition, and we achieved a cut from outside the EU, but inside the EU... It was a no ifs, no buts promise, not an ambition. Inside the EU, we haven't achieved it, but we need to make these welfare changes in order to do it.

Let me tell you. OK. Let me tell you. In the summer of 2013, this government proposed action in Syria, the bombing of Syria, right? I was called into a room by David Cameron and Nick Clegg because President Obama had been on the phone, the leader of the free world, right? I listened to what they said, and over those days, I made up my mind, and we said no, right? Now, I think standing up to the leader of the free world, I think, shows a certain toughness, I would say. Are you proud of what's happened in Syria since? I'm not proud of it, no. It's a failure of the international community. But what I'm not going to... I'm not a pacifist. So I did support action in Libya, and David Cameron talked about how I supported action against ISIS. But am I tough enough? Hell, yes, I'm tough enough. APPLAUSE

Well, yeah, and we need the power to do that, you know, so that we can actually use the huge advantages that we have in terms of the green jobs revolution, the fantastic marine energy resources that we have across all over our coastline. But of course, at the moment, we don't have the power to tap into that potential. We want to see that investment. I have to say to Jeremy, of course, it was great to see the Labour motion at your conference coming out for 2030. That's absolutely the right target. But unfortunately, the manifesto says you want to get the majority of emissions by 2030. A couple of countries that are already in the world have gone for 2030. We need to show that same level of ambition. We have the technology now to reach carbon neutrality by 2030. Unfortunately, what's lacking is the political will. That's the monuments to the lack of political leadership that we have either side of us here. We've got to change that. And the people that aren't represented here as well are the future generations. We've got to be their voice and get to 2030 carbon neutrality.

Right now, there are criminals and people smugglers that are spending hundreds of years in our jails because of the laws that the Conservatives passed. When it came to it, the Labour Party opposed those laws. They voted against it. So when he's standing here before you tonight saying, yes, he cares about this, he's going to do about this, just look at his record. This is someone who defended free movement. He campaigned against the deportation of foreign criminals. He can't be trusted on this issue. Do not surrender our borders to him.

Yes, and I'm very concerned about the situation because I think anybody looking at a council which has run out of money is concerned about the basic services, whether it's libraries or other support that councils have to put in place. And this is a position for councils of all political stripes. There are Conservative-run councils that have struggled for money and gone bankrupt as well because of the lack of adequate and proper structured funding from the government. You asked what we would do about it, and I think it is important for us to set out what we would do if we came into office. We would change the way the payments are made to councils. At the moment, council leaders are saying they're made on a yearly basis, so we can't make the most effective use of the money that's available to us. If they're on longer term, we could make much better use of that. Secondly, they're saying if you could do what the Conservatives promised but didn't actually do, which is to stop no-fault evictions, that would have a massive help for councils as well. Because when people lose their home through no fault of their own, quite a lot of them end up having to have support of a local authority. I don't think you should have no-fault evictions. I think we should actually get on and get rid of them. That's what the Prime Minister used to say, but he doesn't do it.

Thank you very much. Rishi Sunder. Tabitha, I'm not sure how old your pupils are, but my younger daughter's seven, and she's the climate change champion in our house. And she brings home how important this is in all those practical everyday ways that you were talking about, the immediate steps. She turns off all the lights. She makes sure that the tap is off when she's brushing her teeth and she's going through making sure that everything goes in the right recycling bin. And now there are more than one recycling bin. So those are all the practical immediate things. But for the government the most important thing for us to do if we have a strong economy we can then invest in innovation because ultimately it's going to be innovation. It's going to be cheaper car batteries. new forms of renewable offshore wind or, indeed, green infrastructure that solve this problem. We can only make those investments if we have a strong economy and that's something that we're very, very committed to.

Nicola Sturgeon. In Scotland, we've already ended hospital parking charges. Terry, this is a really important question. The NHS is the most precious public service we've got. I was Scottish Health Secretary for five years. In Scotland, we've protected the budget of the NHS and we will always do so. We also believe passionately that the NHS should always be run as a public service, not for private profit. That's why we oppose the privatisation that is happening in England, we oppose it in principle but also because it poses a risk in the long term to Scotland's budget and that's why SNP MPs in the House of Commons will take the opportunity to vote against any extension to privatisation in England and it's one of the many ways in which we'll seek to build progressive alliances to protect things that matter to all of us. But the best thing we can do for the NHS is to end austerity. You'll hear the other parties tonight talk about increasing funding, but if they try to do that within the framework of overall spending cuts, then they'll have to cut even deeper elsewhere. And anybody that tells you that cutting the social care budget or the welfare budget is good for the NHS is wrong. Let's lift austerity, invest in the NHS, but without making deeper cuts elsewhere. Nicola Sturgeon, thank you. Natalie Bennett.

No, more than just the Conservatives, 90% or so of MPs at the time voted to hold a referendum. I was one of them. Angela, I think, was one of them as well. We voted for it and we gave the question to the people to vote in the referendum. As it happens, I voted remain, but the majority of people in the country voted to leave. And I've always felt that it is absolutely essential if we're going to respect our democracy, that we carry out the wishes of the public in that vote. And going back to Katrina's question earlier, why does one's vote not count? Well, this was an opportunity for every person's vote to count in the country. And now we're suddenly told, actually, your vote didn't count. You know, Jo is saying that she's now, well, she was in favor of revoking Article 50 altogether. Then she says she wants a second referendum, but she would ignore the result of the second referendum as well. What we want to do is get Brexit done. and then move on with the life of the country.

The cost of living in crisis is a complete catastrophe, and it's affecting people in every single community across our country. There was a point at which we used to say people were choosing between heating and eating, but there are people now who can't afford either, and it's devastating. So we Liberal Democrats were the first party to call for a windfall tax on the big oil and gas companies because we saw the raw injustice of those companies raking in billions of pounds in profits whilst people couldn't afford to heat their homes. And that's why we're still saying we would close the loopholes in that windfall tax to raise more money so we didn't increase the warm home discount and start insulating people's homes. We would spread free school meals to every single child living in poverty in this country so they can afford to have at least one meal a day to help them sleep and study. And we must invest £1 billion in our farmers to reduce the price of healthy, locally grown food. OK, thank you very much. Angela Rayner.

Our NHS, our care service, hospitality, engineering, you name it, almost every single sector in this country is absolutely crying out for people with the skills that they need. And under this Conservative government, What has happened is they always pull the lever for immigration, and people come here and we should welcome them when they do and contribute to our economy, but they haven't invested in our domestic workforce. Liberal Democrats have a plan to give a higher minimum wage to care workers, £2 higher than the minimum wage for everybody else, so we can get people back working in the care sector. We want to create a Royal College of Care Workers, so they've got a bigger, stronger voice, and give them genuine vocation and training throughout their lifetime. We also need to have skills lifetime account as well because people need to retrain and reskill throughout their life but when people come here to look after our loved ones we should welcome them but we must also invest in our domestic workforce at the same time.

Nigel Farage. I'm on a platform tonight with six other people whose parties have been wholly unconcerned with the issue of immigration. They think that to even question it somehow makes you a bad person. Let's get back to Lorenzo's question. Let's deal with some logic, shall we? From the end of the 1940s... Probably a novelty for you. ..until Mr Blair got to power, listen to the numbers. You probably don't know them. For 50 years, net migration into Britain was 30,000 to 40,000 a year. Then Tony Blair gets in, decides to open the doors. We get net migration for the period of the Labour government of 2.7 million people come in that period. Then we get the Conservatives elected in 2010, 2015, 2017, telling us they'll reduce net migration to tens of thousands a year, 4.3 million the Conservatives have let in. Most of those that come in are not directly productive members of the economy. Most of those that come in are actually dependents. And Lorenzo, the truth of it is, this ought to be the immigration election. Because whether we talk about housing, whether we talk about the fact that rents are up between 20% and 30% in most of the country in the last four years, whether we talk about the roads, whether we talk about infrastructure, we are living through a population crisis, an increase of 10 million people since Mr Blair came to power and frankly it's making us poorer, it is diminishing our quality of life and it's nothing to do, nothing to do with race or any of those issues, it's to do with actually putting the interests of our communities first. We need to get net migration and that means by the way people come and people go. We need to get net migration down to an even figure for the next few years and maybe then we can hope to catch up with housing, with health and many of those things.
We did unilaterally give up biological and chemical weapons. We showed the leadership, and that led OK, thank you very much. That should happen with nuclear weapons, too.
Well, I want to say to him that actually the health professionals themselves are with me on this one, because I piloted through Parliament the health reinstatement bill.

Yes, absolutely. That is a fair point, Fiona. For most of that time, I was running London. When I was running London, we reduced the gap between rich and poor. and one of the things I did, which we have now brought into government, we introduced the Living Wage, we massively expanded the Living Wage when I was Mayor of London. That became a national policy. George Osborne nicked it from me, an act of theft, I was perfectly happy to condone, and it has become a national policy. I am proud to say that under us Conservatives, we're going to raise the living wage to its highest ever level. And I guess if you ask me, do I want to tackle poverty in your area? Yes, I certainly do. And yes, I certainly want to give every kid in the country the opportunities to make the most of their talents. And that is what I believe in passionately as a Conservative. I want to see equality of opportunity in this country. I believe that you have talent, genius, ability, uniformly distributed throughout the whole of the UK. OK. I'm going to get them on side with one like that, I don't think. To unleash that potential. OK. That's what I want to do.

I have made it absolutely clear to everybody that under no circumstances will that happen. The biggest privatisation of the NHS, of course, took place under the last Labour government, which put in the ruinous PFI I have gone round many hospitals talking to fantastic staff and management there who are bearing the burden of that debt. We will now have to deal with it.

Rhun ap Iorwerth. Nothing is more important than looking after the environment that you will be living in in future. There is not a matter of making a choice in Wales as it happens. We can create that economic prosperity that we need to build a brighter future for our country hand in hand with looking after the environment. It is why Plaid Cymru calls for the devolution of the Crown Estate, as has happened to Scotland, but they won't do it to Wales, so that we can invest in making the most of our natural resources, in extracting power out of the wind and the tide.
