It's why we need to see Wales being paid the £4 billion that it's owed of the investment in HS2 Rail in England. Wales is contributing towards that, even though there's not a mile of that railway in Wales. And we need that in order to drive people onto public transport through investing in connectivity in Wales. And you know what? I'm not surprised hearing the Conservatives refusing to make that commitment to Wales, but it's galling when you have a Labour government in Wales that Labour won't either.

I think it has been a great opportunity to have this debate and I think one of the things I've heard during this debate is just repeated attempts to try and frighten you about a Conservative government and I would say choose hope over fear. because we have incredibly exciting and optimistic plans for the future of our country. A great vision where we build a bigger society, where we get our economy moving, where we stop Labour's jobs tax which could destroy that economy. I think it's been shown tonight, the idea you have to go on wasting money to secure the recovery is simply wrong. You've heard a lot about policy tonight, but I think as important as policy is your values. Let me tell you mine. If you work hard, I'll be behind you. If you want to raise a family, I will support you. If you're old and you become ill, we will always be there for you. This is an amazing country. We've done incredible things. I think we can go on and do even more incredible things. But we need two things, a government with the right values and also an understanding that we're all in this together and real change comes when we come together and work together. That's the sort of change and that's the sort of leadership that I would bring to our great country.
Thank you very much on that. I have to add that it is unthinkable to use nuclear weapons, and I want to put my name down on that side of the aisle.

No, it is absolutely not a wish list. It's a programme for government. And when I answered Alison earlier about the costings, and I would urge people, they're on the website, go and see them for yourself. We've put a lot of work into it to make sure we can afford something. Let me explain. One of my lessons from when I was in government before, and we didn't deliver everything, was that you shouldn't promise something you can't deliver. What, like cancelling tuition fees? Indeed. And what I learned from that process was two things, actually. First of all, don't promise something you can't deliver. And so when you have a cost of manifesto, make sure the sums add up. And I'm very confident that they do. Secondly, sorry, we lost some trust during that period. And we've had to rebuild it. And since I became leader, we've spent the last few years trying to rebuild that trust by listening to people. in communities that we seek to represent, trying to understand their concerns. And in our manifesto, we've tried to reflect those concerns. That's why I've put health and care right at the centre. I have some experiences of care, maybe we'll come on to that. But I've also put the cost of living as our key priority. I mentioned free school meals, talking about reducing people's energy bills through insulating people's homes. That's good for the environment, it's good to keep energy bills down, they're way too high.

You keep saying cutting corporation tax for banks. I want to cut corporation tax for small firms, for every firm, whatever they do, to try and help them keep more of their profits, to reinvest, to expand, to take people on. That's what a growing economy needs. Right now, we're stuck. The Prime Minister talks about renewable energy - after 13 years of a Labour government, we've got one of the lowest shares of renewable energy of any country in Europe. They talk and talk. We've had nine energy ministers - I think two of them were the same person - had nine energy ministers, several energy strategists, but nothing ever happens. What we need is change from a government that understands business, that feels the beating heart of entrepreneurs within it and gets things moving.
What, the Crown Prosecuting Service is an extremist organisation?
Amber, you've tried to take personal independence payments away from people with disabilities and then you turned yourselves around in a few days on that. You're not credible on this issue.

Well, if you're referring to MRSA, it is a real problem and we're tackling it. We're putting in the matrons and the cleaning that's necessary. But let me just say this to you. I don't think that should, as it were, stain the whole of the health service. I honestly believe that our National Health Service, and I probably visit more hospitals and talk to more people in the health service than any Prime Minister before me, I think that of course there are real issues and real difficulties. All healthcare systems have that. But I think when you go into, for example, an accident and emergency department nowadays, you can see a change and an improvement. I think for many people who used to wait, for example, you take cardiac care. people used to wait months and months, years even, to get the treatment that they need. They don't wait that long anymore. If you take cataracts, for example, as a result of the changes we've brought in, big change has happened. In cancer, we now save, literally, I think it's something like 10,000 deaths a year as a result of changes that have been brought in. Now, I'm not saying it's all perfect, don't misunderstand me, but I am saying it's a darn sight better than what we inherited from the Conservatives in 1997.

Living standards have fallen in the past seven years under the coalition government with the Liberal Democrats and the Tories and now under the Tory government itself. Six million people are earning less than the living wage. A million are on zero-hours contracts. Public sector workers have had a pay cut of at least 14% over the past seven years. We will lift the pay cap on the public sector. We will introduce a living wage of £10 an hour by 2020 and we won't punish workers who want to take their rights to a tribunal where they have to pay charges and fees. We will abolish that. Our determination is that those who produce the wealth of this country should benefit from the wealth of this country. What we've seen is a massive gap growing between those at work and those at the top. It's times 186 between those on the shop floor and those in the boardroom. That has to change and we will ensure it does.

Yeah. I mean, the international flights, obviously, the biggest amount of carbon released in terms of aviation, and we've said we would start with an international frequent flyer tax for those taking more than three return long-haul flights a year. How much? What it would mean is it would be more expensive for those taking more than three return flights a year. What would you pay? We've got the whole costing set, obviously it's on a scale. So people who are flying very, very frequently, the people that are racking up those long haul flights will pay a lot more. I haven't flown internationally on holiday for a very long time, but I do fly domestically and that is because I represent a constituency of 400 miles from London. And at the moment, we do not have good enough transport infrastructure. We've got Victorian railway infrastructure, and we're in the 21st century. That's why we need the investment in things like High Speed 2 to bring our domestic travel in the UK up to the standards it needs. Hardly anyone would think of flying between London and Paris or London and Brussels these days since the Eurostar came in. That's the kind of 21st century infrastructure that we need.

Jacqueline, as long as anybody feels unsafe, and as long as anybody feels insecure, even though crime is falling, official crime figures show it's falling, and violent crime is falling, I feel that we have got to do far more. And that's why there's three things I want to suggest this evening that will make people safer. One is, we've got to have effective policing on the streets. Police have got to spend 80% of their time now on the streets. We've got record police numbers in this country and we want to maintain that level of police force over the next few years. The second thing is parents have got to accept responsibility for their children. If an order is passed against a teenager then the parent has also got to accept responsibility and we're bringing that in now. And the third thing I would say is this. If you are dissatisfied with the way the police are treating or the police are dealing with your case and you are persistently denied the rights you have, then we'll give you the right to take an injunction against the police so that you can be sure that your rights against anti-social behaviour and crime are upheld.

John McDonnell supports the need for more police officers. Does he still believe in abolishing MI5? John McDonnell also accepts the need for the accountability to Parliament of our security services, which is important. But he also accepts, as I do, the need to have security services that do protect us. Manchester is a good example. And I think when all the inquiries have been completed in Manchester, I think we need to look at those needs.

People can remember the record of 13 years, they remember who it was who abolished the 10p tax that hit some of the poorest people in the country the hardest. They remember the measly 75p increase on pensioners that Gordon Brown was responsible for. And let me say this, the whole reason we're having this debate about how difficult it is to get taxes down, how difficult it's going to be to cut spending, is because this Prime Minister and this Government have left our economy in such a complete mess with a budget deficit that, this year, is forecast to be bigger than that of Greece. That's why we're having to have this debate. Let's not forget whose responsibility it is.
I don't have the exact figures but I know that usage of food banks has gone up and there are many amazing volunteers who man those food banks and provide an important service.

I think, Julie, on that, look, this is a difficult issue. As you said, 20 years, different governments of all stripes haven't been able to solve this problem. We need to work together, we need to try and solve the problem. But I can say this, if you think about next year, should we spend next year focusing on this kind of problem, social care or climate change, or should we spend next year arguing about Brexit with more referendums and years more gridlock? The choice to get on to these issues is to get Brexit done and then we can start focusing on this kind of stuff. Thank you.

Listen, it's not on anybody's agenda. It's certainly not on my agenda. And do you know what? I had a very nice chat with the Queen. But you don't like her, do you? You don't like what she represents. We got along absolutely fine, perfectly fine, but I don't think she should be brought into political discussion.

Well, you know. All I can say to you is to repeat what I've said. Actually, in this party, in the Conservative Party, unlike other parties, we have zero tolerance of Islamophobia. We do. And people are asked to stand down if they're guilty of Islamophobia or any other prejudice or any other hate speech. And that is what we... That's what we do. And I'm delighted. And we are going to have an inquiry into prejudice of all kinds. question.

Housing has become a speculative commodity and we've forgotten that it should be about people's homes. We would introduce rent controls, absolutely. We would also build 100,000 council homes a year. We've got 4.5 million economically inactive people, so I support Angela's point about we can get the workforce. but also would introduce a land value tax, which I think is the most radical proposal for bringing that land back into use. We desperately need that greater supply of land. You've got a lot of developers sitting around just waiting for the land to increase in value, benefiting from the infrastructure that we put in around it that we all pay for through our tax money, and then they just cash it in maybe in five or ten years, and that land is lying empty. So a land value tax would abolish council tax and business rates and replace it with a land value tax, move the shift onto the land owners to increase the supply of land. And I think all the other parties are going to be talking about this in three or four years, where Greens lead, others follow, but it's the future.
OK, no, I'm going to look for manifesto where the school support staff negotiating body which is around the whole of the support staff as well as teachers which I believe will make a difference.

Okay, Kate Forbes, SMB? Yeah, well, I think if you ever wanted an example of how austerity hasn't worked for the last seven years, employment and youth employment is a good place to start. And the Conservatives like to style themselves as looking after the economy and meeting these financial targets, and yet they've singularly failed to do that. And so the economy has not grown at the extent it should have. And austerity has actually kept the economy back, which means you haven't got the same extent of job creation. But it isn't just about people in employment. It's about what they're getting paid as well. And again, we in the SNP feel very, very strongly that people should be paid a fair wage. They should be paid the real living wage, not the rebranded living wage. And the cutoff should not be age 25. Why should somebody age 26 earn something that's a different wage from somebody age 24 who might be doing exactly the same job? So we've said in our manifesto that we're committed to raising the minimum wage in line with the real living wage so that it's over £10 over the next parliamentary term. But it really does come back to ending austerity, making sure that people have money in their pockets that they can spend so there's more money going round the economy and that means that our economy grows, we're more productive and it's better all round.

Yes, we can afford it, because if we don't afford it, then what's the effect on areas that don't have access to broadband, on their economic development, on the investments that might come there, on the businesses that won't go there? Listen, talk to a business about investing in, say, Cumbria, or, say, parts of Northumbria. Say, would you open a factory or a shop or a small business here? They'd say, No, there's no broadband, there isn't good train access, there isn't good bus access or whatever else. We have to invest in social infrastructure and transport infrastructure for the whole country. Otherwise, the North-South divide, the inequalities between regions in England and between Scotland and Wales and England will simply get worse as a result of it. This is a serious proposal in order to help bring economic development to all parts of our country.

I'm absolutely sure that they add up. absolutely clearly about this. For example, if we want to remove university tuition fees, and we do, so our students are not saddled with debt, and restore maintenance grants, so that all children, whatever their background, have a chance of going to university, that will cost 11 billion pounds. I think that is money well spent. I think that is the kind of investment this country needs. Why should... Why should... Why should... Why should a child's future be determined by the postcode in which they're born? That's why we are determined to bring in free preschool 30 hours a week from two to four-year-olds and make sure that every primary school child gets a free lunch every day so they can learn while they're properly fed.

Back to the question Robert put. The PCT, the health authority, was finding it very difficult because of the situation at the moment. Take thousands of millions out of the economy now. Take six billion out of the economy now and think of the risk to jobs and businesses. And I say to the Conservatives, of course we want efficiency savings and of course we want to deal with waste, but we cannot afford to see private investment so small and then public investment cut at this time and lots of jobs put at risk.

Well, it seems like there's three people in the special relationship, Boris Johnson, Nigel Farage, and Donald Trump. We obviously have a hugely important relationship with the United States, but make no mistake, the current occupant of the White House is not somebody who shares our values. This is someone who has boasted about sexually assaulting women, whose policies are discriminatory against different people from different religious backgrounds, such as Muslims, and is separating parents from their children at the border. And we should be very careful with that relationship. The last thing we should have done was rule out the red carpet for a state visit. And I think it's shocking that that's the way the Conservatives have chosen to develop that relationship with Donald Trump. Nigel Farage.

We are going to be talking about immigration later. I had family that migrated into culture, so they were working in the prison service at the time many years ago. But I fear that whatever problems there were in the prison service back then still exist within our prisons now. We want a radical overhaul of the way that we build the criminal justice system in Wales. It should be devolved to us. I'm sure most of you would agree with me that the way policing and criminal justice works should be as close to the people it affects as possible. What we've seen in some of the highest incarceration rates anywhere in Europe is a failure of the people that the criminal justice system is meant to serve. We've had the building of a huge super prison in Wales, causing more problems than it resolves. We need to move away from, you know, using prisons, which are absolutely needed for the most serious of criminals, towards a system which is restorative, which does deal with crime at its source. And for prisons themselves, that would allow us then to concentrate on investing in the kinds of prisons that work. You'll have heard about Park Prison, a privately run prison, dogged with problems in recent weeks and months. The number of deaths there are consistently rising. We cannot have the prison service in the hands of the private sector and we need to invest in it for the future.

This may have the feel of a TV popularity contest, but in truth, This is an election about Britain's future, a fight for your future and for your jobs. If it's all about style and PR, count me out. If it's about the big decisions, if it's about judgement, it's about delivering a better future for this country, I'm your man. Ahead are huge challenges, delivering the economic recovery in jobs, bringing our brave troops safely home from Afghanistan, keeping our streets free of terrorism, building alliances in Europe against nuclear weapons, against climate change, against poverty, and to deal with our banks. Now not everyone has the answers, but I say get the big decisions wrong and Britain's security and jobs are at risk. Get the big decisions right and we can have a prosperous, fairer, greener and better Britain. Like me or not, I can deliver that plan and the way to do it is with a majority Labour government.

Decent investment in public services is common sense. It's what the public want to see, an NHS well-funded in public hands so that it can do its job. And Dennis was trying to get out of the panellists tonight what our big ideas are. The Green Party's big idea is decent investment in public services, and we're not afraid to say where it will come from. We want to make the economy fairer so that the billionaires and multimillionaires, those with the broader shoulders who can easily afford to, pay a bit extra to fund the public services that we all rely on.

In my view, you should always vote if you are doing well in life for the politician who will make sure that others can get on in life. And if you're not doing so well in life, you should always vote for the politician who's going to make things better for you. I think that's a nice and good starting point in politics. The Westminster system, the way it operates, is broken. It's been built on failure, Brexit being the most obvious one of recent times. Hold your politicians to high standards and vote for what you believe in.
Just like Obama before him. Obama brought the ban. You never attacked Obama, did you?
And you've got legal constraints upon you as a Minister...

It is every business leader. Every leading business leader is saying that we've got it right, and the government going on wasting money is wrong. Let me tell you where I think we should start: we should start with welfare. Under this government, there are now five million people on out-of-work related benefits. There are people who could work who we'd train and offer work. We should say in our country "If you don't accept work, you can't go on claiming benefits". That's something Labour have left us with, this terrible mess. And the Liberal Democrats have almost nothing to say about welfare, so as we try to get public spending under control, let's start with people who can work, who are offered work, but who don't take it.

The best way to provide optimism is to create the conditions whereby everyone can have a job, whereby we can create the conditions of full employment and Plaid Cymru wants to create 50,000 new jobs with our job creation plan by supporting small businesses through business rate relief to take on extra people and also changing the way that the public sector contracts out to the private sector to guarantee more jobs to be local jobs.

Thank you for your question Sam and I accept it's an important question and one that's on the minds of many people. I think politics nowadays needs to have people setting out what they believe in and I honestly believe that Scotland would be a stronger country and would have better prospects if we were an independent country. That's been my That's been my honest view over my entire adult life. But I'm a Democrat, so I accept that we can't be an independent country until the people of Scotland vote for us to be an independent country. And I do believe in the right of the people of Scotland to decide their own future. I think, generally, there's an accepted view that self-determination of the right of people to decide how they should be governed is an important political principle. And I think what politics today needs is politicians who will set out what they actually believe in, set it out openly to the public, and ask the public to judge. And one of the frustrations I've got is that today, in the Scottish Parliament, there is a majority in favour of there being a referendum on independence, and that Scotland should be an independent country, but we're not being permitted by the United Kingdom government to put that idea and that concept to the public. And I think we should have the right to do so.

Thank you. Nicholas Sturgeon. the economy means doing more to support business. In Scotland, we are taking 100,000 small businesses out of business rates to try to give them more money to employ people. In this election, we will be arguing for exemptions from national insurance for companies who take on more staff. I don't support a headline-cutting corporation tax, but I think things like the investment allowance should be increased to encourage companies to invest in plant and machinery, because improving productivity is a key objective as well. But, you know, supporting some of the key sectors, certainly of the Scottish economy, is partly about making sure we don't pull the rug out from under them. And so many of these key sectors, from food and drink, from our energy sector, will really be hurt by coming out of the single market. And that underlines the point, if we don't want Theresa May to have a free hand to do whatever she wants, regardless of the cost for ordinary people, we need voices in the House of Commons standing up for these kinds of interests. Caroline Lucas, you wanted to come in there.

We've got a record number of police officers. We have seen the decimation of our neighborhood policing. And actually, when police are in the neighborhoods, they get to know the young people. They can work with them. And we can really get our young people feeling safe on the streets again. Because I think that is one of the real scandals at the moment, is our young people feel that they will be protected if they carry a knife. That is completely untrue. and we've got to educate them and make them feel safe again. Reena Pureworth.

Well, good evening, everyone. Fiona, thank you for inviting me. And Kevin, was that right? Yeah. When I first got this job, I stood on the steps of Downing Street and spoke to all of you as Prime Minister for the first time, and I acknowledged that. I said very clearly that mistakes had been made, and that's why I was standing there in front of you. In fact, you may remember I spent the summer before that arguing against the policies that my predecessor had suggested were right. I didn't think they were right for the country, and I said so. But what I'd ask you to do is judge me on my 18 months that I've been in office. I came in with one primary job, and that was to bring back economic stability after the difficult few years that we've had. You saw yesterday, we had the very welcome news that inflation is back to target. Now, I know that that's only starting to make a difference to all of your lives and everyone else's, but that's the path we're on. And if we stick with that path, I can continue to cut your taxes, give you more financial security, and actually deliver on the things that I know you want me to.

Labour is very clear that we want to maintain an incredibly strong partnership with Europe. We're very aware that the terms of that are going to be changing, but we cannot be going into this all bluster, throwing the baby out with the bathwater. What we need to be looking at is workers' rights, environmental protections and security, as well as forming a really strong trading relationship.

about half a million people leave the UK every year. So we can welcome about half a million people, highly qualified, highly skilled, that we can accommodate. But the truth is that we are in the longest recession per person, and that's what really matters to all of us, the longest recession per person since records began 70 years ago. economic growth and we're dividing the cake by three quarters of a million more people every year. The right thing to do is invest in our own people as we did in the 1990s and 90s.

Yeah, I think it's probably worthwhile starting by addressing the question head on. Net zero is economic growth. And it's not GB energy. It's Scotland's energy. It's Scotland's wind. It's Scotland's waves. It's Scotland that's going to provide the carbon capture and storage. And it's Scotland that's going to create the green hydrogen economy of the future. The Americans are investing in it. Europe's investing in it. The Middle East and beyond is investing in it. And the UK needs to invest Two, the opportunities are huge but we have to grasp them and I'm afraid that what we're seeing at Westminster is a betrayal of the future generations. A Labour Party walking away from £28 billion worth of investment which would have been transformative. With the SNP you will get that commitment to a just transition, to reaching net zero, to protect and create new jobs for the future.

Yes I have changed my mind. I think it's important to learn lessons as life goes on and to look at things again, and I, and that's right. I have changed  BOTH TOGETHER QUESTIONER_0: So if you've been wrong on all these things in the past, we don't have slightest guarantee you'll be right on anything in the future.  My mind about that. Well you, you know we can, we can talk about the past, you can - about the time that ur, that, that I brought in the Poll Tax, Tony Blair was a member of CND, he was opposing all the reforms to the trade unions that we brought in. He was describing our plans to give trade union members the right to vote for their leaders as scandalous. So we can argue about the past to your heart's content. I think most people watching this programme are interested in our plans for the future of our country.

Well the first thing is I imagine at the forefront of your minds, and I'm sure I speak for all of my colleagues here, at the forefront of all of our minds are all of the families who suffered the devastation to their loved ones and relatives with the attack on Manchester. What happened there was a horror and sadly it's happening far too often, both here and round about the world. So I think the first thing that we need to do is that we do need to take safety and security seriously. We need to invest in the police, not cut them as has been the case in England. In Scotland, we've managed to maintain police numbers. So that's my first step. Let's make sure we back the police, give the resources that they need, back our intelligence services who do a tremendous job. There's been some discussion, has there not, about the link between UK foreign policy and becoming perhaps a terrorist threat. And I think that's a dangerous link to draw. However, I would say this. I think we are right to question our intervention in different parts of the world. In Afghanistan, in Iraq, in Libya. And the lesson I draw from that is not that it is wrong sometimes to intervene. We agreed that helping people in Libya was a good thing. But what was wrong was spending 13 times more bombing that country than helping to rebuild it. on leaving areas of the world. Leaving areas of the world with ungoverned spaces is where terrorism and extremism thrives. So we need to do everything that we can to counter extremist thought, give the police and security service the tools that they require. So not cutting the police would be a first step that I would take from Scotland's experience. And I think we want to be very cautious about how we intervene internationally. And if we're going to do it, we have to think long and hard about how we make sure that those places remain stable and safe for years to come.

Well, when I said to my party we had to get real about the environment and we had to be a party that was a blue-green party, not just a blue party, I did actually once get a letter from someone who didn't really agree with this and said, Mr Cameron, if you're so concerned about carbon emissions, why don't you just stop breathing? And that was a moment that I realized I still had some persuasion to do. In terms of my own life, the biggest thing we've done is actually to have proper insulation in our house and actually really can cut your energy bill and make life cheaper as well as greener. But I would say in the last six months, the biggest change or the last year, the biggest change that I've been able to make is actually coming out very strongly against the third runway at Heathrow. I think it is wrong to do that. I think we should be going for high-speed rail instead. We should be having a high-speed rail hub at Heathrow. And trying to make sure that all those flights that people take where you could take a train, it's possible to do that. I think it would be a really big step forward.

We will, over a period, over a parliament, increase corporation tax to around the levels it was in 2010 in order to reinvest properly in education, in order to pay for the ending of university tuition fees, to give every student a decent chance of going to university and life. And we will start to address the grotesque levels of imbalance in our society. We're a society of billionaires and the very poor, neither of which are right.

Well, David, we've supported the right to buy, but the problem with the Conservative plan is that they've got absolutely no idea where the £4 billion that they're supposed to be paying for it from is coming from, and that means that we're going to lead to fewer houses available for rent in our country. That's why the plan doesn't work. And look, any plan based on right to buy has got to mean there are going to be more houses, not less. That has got to be a fundamental principle, given the problems we face. Look, the answer is we need to invest in housing in the future. That's why I say that we make capital investment the priority for the future.

of the public sector. half an extra teacher in every school in England will make a difference to the standards of education. But I'll tell you one thing, we need, and both my parents were teachers, we need, just as in health, to make sure that our workforce feel supported. It's in good wages, but it's also in knowing that government is on their side, and neither in Wales nor in the rest, nor in England, can they say that that's the case.

First of all, I should say that this election will not change the administration of the police in Scotland. It's dealt with by the government, currently the SNP government. And to make some suggestions for Simon for the terrible situation he described, first of all, you should pay your police officers more. So in Scotland, every single rank of police officer is paid more than they are in England and Wales. You should have more police officers. So we have around 20% more police officers per capita in Scotland than they have in England and Wales. And thirdly, we had a very... successful initiatives called No Knives, Better Lives, which instead of taking what you've heard from the Conservatives, the hit them hard, stop and search stuff, it was about public health. And it oversaw a massive reduction in knife crime. It worked. I mean, Glasgow in particular, the reputation, as you may know, for many years has been a knife capital, certainly of Scotland, perhaps even the UK. We've dramatically reduced knife crime since 2007. However, all those things are put at jeopardy by what you've not heard from the two speakers, the £18 billion of cuts which they're both going to bring in. And that will affect policing in Scotland because the cuts which you've seen already under austerity will come from Labour, they'll come from the Tories, whoever gets in, and that is the biggest threat to continued effective policing in Scotland. And I have every sympathy with the situation you've described. Chris Philp, that's right, isn't it?

I mean, to me, it is my life's work. I profoundly believe that the NHS grows with a Conservative government like what I've been leading. We put the money in. I care about it passionately. And I've got seven days left to prove to people that the NHS, to get... Also, I'd make this point. You only ultimately have a strong NHS if you have a strong economy. We've got that strong economy now, don't put that at risk. You know where they cut the NHS? Portugal, terrible economy, cut the NHS 17%. Greece, catastrophic economy, cut the NHS 13%. It's the economies that tank and bomb where you can't support the health service that our country needs and I think we all deserve.

Michael, in specific answer to your question, what want to do, and we've set it out as one of the biggest pledges in our manifesto, is to take £2.5 billion from the £15 billion of savings that we've already identified elsewhere in Government spending, so that we can raise the money given to pupils, the million poorest children, some of the children that Michael's talking about, to the same level which children get if they go to fee-paying schools. Becaue I think the issue that Michael's risen is one of the biggest that faces us as a country. At the moment, you know, a poor but bright child, the latest evidence shows, gets overtaken in the classroom by a less bright but wealthier child by about the age of seven, and after that, the gap tends to widen. It even affects life expectancy. A child born in the poorest neighbourhood of Sheffield, for instance, today in 2010 is likely to die a decade-and-a-half before a child born in wealthiest neighbourhood up the road. That needs to change. We've got a plan to deliver more one-to-one tuition, smaller class sizes, to help those children the most in the crucial early years when they start school.

pay progression conditions, and all of that could be negotiated on the 35%. I don't think we can afford that as a country because of the damage that's been done to the economy. I don't want to pretend to you that we can. I'll sit down in a room and negotiate and find a way through this, just as has happened in other countries, because it's intolerable that we go on. But I'm not going to start with the proposition that we simply accept whatever the other side is asking for. That's not how negotiations go. And you wouldn't respect a government if it operated that way. And other people in other... Walks of life have got constraints and pressures on their lives that have to be balanced. But will we be grown? All that's happened this year, and it's so frustrating, is the government say, we want a deal, but we won't go into the room first. It's for the doctors to... They've never wanted a deal, have they? I think you may be right, and the fact they've left this to the election without fixing it and just booted it the other side of the election, I think is disgraceful. It doesn't do you any good, it doesn't do patients any good, and it doesn't do the country any good. But we've had a government that's chaotic, that's divided, has broken just about everything they've touched. I don't know a period in history, and you will all think about this, Almost everything is now in a worse state than when they started in government. And this is not about Labour or Tory. If you leave your country in a worse state than when you came into government, you should be booted out.

You know on all these big issues I think we're proving ourselves to be a tolerant and understanding nation. We introduced civil partnerships and therefore we changed the laws on homosexuality and said to people if you're gay or straight you have a place in British society. And I think for many people that was a great liberation and I'm pleased that it has happened in Britain in these last few years. On human embryology, I've disagreed with the Catholic Church, because I believe if you can treat a disease by using embryos that are enabling us to make sure that people can be free of some of the conditions that afflict their lives, we should do so under the right rules. And I'm sorry that in principle there is a disagreement with the Church on that. On the question of contraception, I think we know that in Africa, we know around the world that it's important to give women access and choice so that they can make their own decisions. And I regret the fact that the Catholic Church does not do that. But the Pope should come to Britain, we should have these debates, and we should welcome all religions because bringing religions together is the key to making sure that we have a more peaceful world.

Nigel Farage... We need to do something, something with vision. And if we increase the level at which people start to pay tax to £20,000, that takes 7 million people out of tax. That's up to a £1,500 benefit for people on lower wages. But it helps get people off the benefits trap. because the benefits trap is if you start to go back to work and earn money you lose benefits plus we'd rely less on unskilled foreign labour. That's the kind of vision that we need to help those who are lower end of the income scale or on benefits in this country.

yes, yes, I believe that if people have paid their taxes, have paid towards the cost of their NHS treatment, but find for whatever reason that they have to, or want to go private it's right  BOTH TOGETHER

Well, I'm afraid, Leon, I entirely disagree with the premise of your question. What's put our public services at risk is austerity, failure to invest, and privatisation, particularly of our NHS. Now, I am an immigrant. I've chosen to become a British citizen to make my life in Britain. I came as a visitor, I loved the British way of life, I loved the traditions, the culture, and I decided to stay and make this my home. I went into politics because I want to improve that, I want to protect it, and I want to make sure the vulnerable and our natural environment are protected. Now I live in Somers Town in central London, a very diverse community. There are migrants there, Some of them are cleaners, some of them are doctors, some of them are grandmas. They're all contributing to the British way of life, to the British life in their own way. Now there's someone here on this platform who wants to utterly demonise those migrants and you know who I mean. I want to celebrate the contribution of migrants to Britain and I believe we all should be doing that.

Well we've got to retain our nuclear deterrent, and we've had an independent nuclear deterrent for a long time. Now that decision is for another time, but in principle, I believe it's important to retain our own

The expenses scandal made people incredibly angry. People also got angry by politicians saying, I only obeyed the rules. I think they're now starting to get angry by some politicians saying, well, my party was much better than all the others. Frankly, Nick, we all had problems with this, whether it was moats or whether it was politicians claiming on phantom mortgages or whether it was kitchens and cake tins and the rest of it. Don't anyone try and put themselves on a pedestal over this issue. Let's actually sort it out and clean it up and recognise we were all in this mess.
But the reality is that the UK is a country of migrants and migration is a good thing for our country, for our culture and for our economy. It's absolutely vital in supporting our public services.

And possibly nuclear. Adam Price. It is a genuine question. I can understand and I support actually taking the energy distribution companies into public ownership, though we'd like to see that within Wales with a national energy company. But why are you nationalising the gas distribution network when you're going to decarbonise? You're going to spend billions of pounds on an energy form that you say you want to get

David, can you understand, though, that when a mainstream party like UKIP is putting out its so-called integration policy, which says that... We need to integrate. Sorry, can I speak? Which says that Muslim women shouldn't be able to wear headscarves... No, we didn't say that.

Thank you, Dennis, for your question. The NHS is one of our proudest achievements, and it'll be come Labour that we'll have to fix it again. Before I was an MP, I was a Unison rep, and I was a home care worker. And in 2010, the Tories, aided and abetted by the Lib Dems, created austerity, which meant many of our workforce were made redundant. And now we've got the crisis in the NHS. So we've got to fix the workforce problem, and we've got to invest. That's why we're done the non-DOM tax status, so we can put 40,000 new appointments, and we'll use up private sector to bring down those waiting lists but the NHS will remain a public service under Labour and will also fix the problem in our social care because if you don't take the two together then you won't fix them and we've also said on the VAT on private schools to give us 6,500 qualified teachers in our classroom to fix our education as well.

because I believe that we are the party that is going to do the most to help you to get a fantastic high-wage, high-skilled job... What about all the debt I'm about to graduate with? And help you to get a home by building record numbers of homes, help you to get onto the housing ladder, With great respect, sir, we built a record number last year and we have a plan to build a million homes in the next five years.

I'll answer that directly. Wes Streeting has said in order to bring the record high waiting list down that we would use up capacity in the private sector to bring the waiting list down rather than keep people on waiting lists waiting for years for an operation that they desperately need. Angelo. We think that's the right way. But it will always be a publicly owned NHS under Labour.

If he's elected president of the US, then we will deal with him. It's up to the people in the US to decide who they want as their president. Even if he's a criminal? The special relationship transcends whoever fills the post of prime minister and president, because it's such an important, strong relationship.

Reena Fullworth. Change is a key word. It's a change election, isn't it? The sadness is there'll be a change in Downing Street. I'm quite sure that Keir Starmer will become the Prime Minister in four weeks' time, but what change does that represent? I will always argue that you need a plurality of voices making the case for exactly what you're looking for. What are the answers when you have two parties converging on public spending? converging on taxation, who's going to make sure that you're able to see the investment in housing for your children? To see the investment in getting rid of the two child benefit cap in order for parents to be able to look after their children and take children out of poverty? I believe that the real frustration in this election is the lack of change on offer from the Labour Party. Whether you liked him or not, there was a feeling. I was still working as a journalist back in the 90s when Tony Blair came in. At least there was a feeling of something different happening. I'm just not sensing that, which is why we in Plaid Cymru say the Tories are gone, Labour are going to come into power, but let's, for goodness sake, make sure that people who feel ignored, as you do, are heard by that Labour Party when they came in, because change has to mean something. and that's what's important.

Phil, Keir Starmer said, smash the gangs. We put new laws in Parliament that have now led to almost 1,000 criminals and people smugglers being arrested, serving hundreds of years in jail. Because we do need to smash the gangs. Keir Starmer voted against those laws. So as ever, you say one thing here, but your track record says something completely different, and you can't be trusted to tackle immigration.

And this is an issue right now. Tonight, there are families who are living in makeshift accommodation, living out of plastic bags with whatever of their possessions remain, because they have experienced those horrendous floods in Yorkshire and Derbyshire. So this is an issue right now. So some of the things we can do to rapidly cut carbon dioxide, the things we know best are insulating our homes, a rapid program of home insulation. And the new homes that we build should all be zero carbon. We know how to do this. And making sure we take the fossil fuels out of our electricity generation. renewables, we should be so much more ambitious. The cost has already come down significantly. Offshore wind is the cheapest electricity that you can now create, thanks to Liberal Democrats supporting that technology.

To me, helping someone to get a job has a moral dimension. It gives them the dignity and pride that comes with work. Helping someone to get an apprenticeship, that has a moral dimension. It gives someone the chance of a career and success. Building a house that a young family can afford to buy and own, that's got a moral dimension because it gives them a stake in the country they live in. That's the country I want to build. These plans I've got for the next five years are about taking this economic foundation that we've built these last five years, all the shared sacrifice that we've been through, but not putting that to waste, but saying let's now turn that into jobs, into pay, into houses, into good school places. And yes, also, into that sense at the end of your life that this is a country where if you work hard, you save and you do the right thing, you should get that dignity and security in retirement that I think is the right of everyone who lives in our country.

Jo Swinson says she will pay for low-income homes. When you offer people either an interest-free loan to convert their house or you do it for them in the case of social housing or much in the social rented sector, then I think you've made a massive step forward. We can and do retrofit homes. We can generate electricity from solar wave and wind. We don't have to destroy our countryside with fracking. We don't have to go on in this way. But it does mean a determination and that's why our manifesto quite clearly sets out a very large Green Transformation Fund which will allow that investment in zero carbon homes will create a very large number of jobs in retrofitting those homes and actually give people cheaper electricity as well. Who will qualify for government help? those that are in social housing will get that done because it will be done by their landlords like the local authority or the Housing Association. Those on low incomes will have it done for them. Everyone else will be offered an interest-free loan to ensure that it's done. thousands of pounds on their homes. No, but they will get the benefits of it in lower bills for their gas or electricity or whatever form of heating they do. It is actually possible to construct homes that use very, very little energy. New homes should all be done like that and at the moment they're not.

So to help people with mortgages, if you go and talk to your bank, what we put in place is a mortgage charter that covers the industry. So if you talk to your bank, what we've organized is for you to be able to get a holiday on your mortgage, move to an interest-only mortgage, stretch out the term, All of that you can do without affecting your credit rating. It can save you potentially hundreds of pounds a month in mortgage costs, which is really practical support, and your bank has to do that all for you if you like it. So that's the solution that we've put in place. The combination of those things, extending your term, moving to interest only, those types of things will save you hundreds of pounds, enable you to keep your house, and the bank can't repossess your house. We've made that clear. for a very prolonged period of time after you first told them that you're struggling. So all of those predictions, we didn't have them in the past. They're new, something that the Chancellor negotiated with all the lenders in our country. So it's called the Mortgage Charter. For anyone struggling with that, please talk to your bank and say that I said that that's what you're entitled to, because that's what you should be able to get. Prime Minister, we're talking about solutions.

You're quite entitled to speak out, but the Prime Minister ought to get his facts right, and as so often, he gets his facts wrong. We all remember when he told us the defence budget went up every year, when in fat it didn't. It didn't go up every year when he was sending troops to war. On this issue of tax credits, we are saying, we like tax credits, we'll keep tax credits. But for families earning over £50,000, we think we can't afford the Child Tax Credit. That's one of the savings that we're being upfront and frank about. But for Gordon Brown to say that actually the changes we're making would hit low income families is simply not true. As I say, last week in these debates he tried to frighten people, saying the Conservatives would take away benefits, when we will keep the winter fuel allowance, we will keep the cold winter payments. He's trying again to frighten people, and actually he should be ashamed of what he's doing.

Well, first of all, thank you, Terry, for your incredible service to the NHS. And you're absolutely right. This is the most important national institution and national public service that we have. And I'll never forget, as a dad of a desperately disabled child, what I got every night when I took him to hospital, worrying about his health. I got unbelievable care. And I just want that for every family and for everyone in our country. Now, we've been talking about the difficult decisions we had to make to turn the economy around, but a difficult decision we made also was to go on funding the NHS, putting more money in every year, and we'll go on doing that in the next Parliament. That meant that we've trained 7,000 more nurses, we've got 9,000 more doctors, and we also managed to take out of the NHS 20,000 bureaucrats, because I want the money spent on patient care. Now, it's key that we keep a strong economy in order to fund a strong NHS. And I want to see the NHS move to much more seven-day operation, like your GP being open eight in the morning, eight in the evening, all the way through the week. There's one point I want to end on. There's only one group of politicians anywhere in this United Kingdom who've cut the NHS in the last five years, and that was the Labour Party in Wales. So when you hear Ed Miliband's promises, think about that.

I wouldn't attend a state dinner with Donald Trump. I think Jeremy was right at the time to do what he did. What we need in a Prime Minister and in a government is a government that, yes, has a good relationship with the United States, America and other countries, but also a government and a Prime Minister that thinks for themselves. Jeremy Corbyn was correct to oppose George Bush's order that we should join the Iraq war. In the same way, Jeremy was correct to oppose the NATO-led bombing of Libya, which hasn't made things better in Libya, it's made things worse, it's caused a refugee crisis, there's human trafficking there, slavery has come back in Libya. So yes, we need to be allies with the United States of America, Our quarrel and difference isn't with the people of the United States of America. Our quarrel and difference is with some of the policies of Donald Trump, who, like Boris Johnson, is a politician who's giving the green light to sexism, homophobia and racism. OK. And that cannot be allowed to flourish.

But the question is, we've got a multiplicity of issues to wrestle with here about energy security and about the journey to net zero. The key point I would make is that the journey to net zero is absolutely inescapable. We've got to do it for the sake of the planet. But the judgments we've got to make have got to be made on a case-by-case basis where we explore and examine whether any new activity could be compatible with our objectives on climate change.

Well, it's all very well to say these things, but if what actually happens in practice is that we produce these, as I say, these colleges of crime, where we have now, what, about 4,000 people going into our prisons on short-term prison sentences. They sit around. They learn some extra tricks of the trade from some more experienced criminals and then they go out and nine out of ten of the young men on short-term prison sentences just commit more crime. And it's that, I think that's what Jacqueline's talking about, this desperate feeling, this hopeless feeling, it keeps happening over and over again. I met a young man in London the other day, his flat had been burgled five times. And one of them, would you believe it, Jacqueline, was when he was away at his father's funeral. And all he said to me, he said, why can't this stop? And unless we do something different, not the same old remedies, but do something different to stop the youngsters of today who are getting into trouble from becoming the hardened criminals of tomorrow, all this stuff I don't think will make the difference that they say it will.

It already is because the veterans themselves are speaking out saying he's let the country down. And to say that it's a mistake, if his instinct was the same as the British people, he would never have contemplated for a moment not being there for the big international ceremony. And it shows how disconnected he is with the people of this country.

Well, I think Theresa May's attacks on education are doing real damage, both the way in which she's slashing school budgets and with her obsession with the divisive grammar school system. But coming to Alex's point, I think he's absolutely right to point to demoralisation of teachers right now, because it seems to me that schools should be about nurturing potential and inspiring a love of learning. I remember when I was at school, coming across a poem I loved so much that the teacher basically made it the subject of a whole lesson and had the freedom to do that. Right now, education, I think, can feel a bit like a production line, creating children-shaped pieces to fit the latest gaps in the jobs market. And when we do that, then I think what we do is to stifle the freedom to let children play and flourish and grow. We've replaced that with endless testing and measuring. It demoralises teachers, it adds yet more stress onto our young people. So the Green Party thinks it's time to scrap SATs, to trust teachers. Really, it's quite simple. Let kids be kids and let teachers teach.

And I would not countenance any such thing. By the Conservative Party. Yes. But this gets to the heart of the question. Because it isn't just our voting system that's wrong. I'm with you. I think millions of people say, what the hell is the point of voting? I'm living in a safe seat. What it also leads to is parties saying, don't vote for them. They're awful. We're not quite as bad. I think a degree of proportionality would lead to us saying, look, this is what we stand for. Please vote for us. And I, in the history of UK politics, I'm the person that achieved the most votes as a party leader for the least reward. Four million votes in 2015 and one seat in Parliament. So that needs reforming. But getting back to what Emma was talking about, the idea that the upper chamber The amending chamber that has legislative power in this country is filled with 600 people who were put there by Cameron and put there by Blair. We should abolish the House of Lords and make that elected too. Let's modernise our whole democratic system. It'll be a better, more positive country.

Corporation tax was 28% in 2010. This government has reduced it and proposes to reduce it further. We're going to put it back up to 26%. And why are we doing that? Because this country is badly divided between the richest and the poorest. You put corporate tax and tax at the top end down, the division gets greater. Are you happy that so many of our children are going to school with supersized classes, so many of our children are going to school hungry. Are you happy with so many people waiting for hospital operations, a million waiting for social care? You don't address these problems by ignoring them. I appeal to you, as obviously a traditional person who supported our party, recognise that we're all better off when everybody is better off. And your children want to get a home of their own in the future. If we build the homes for them, both to rent as well as to buy, then they've got a better chance. Your children, I'm sure, would like to go to university. I do not believe we should charge fees for our students to go to universities because we all benefit from them graduating and becoming well-qualified citizens. So what we're proposing in this manifesto is transformational because for the first time for many years we're saying this younger generation should not be worse off than we are or ones that went before. They should actually be better off. This is about rejigging the way that we run our economy. But I also say to anyone running a small business I work with small businesses, I talk to them, I listen very carefully to what they have to say. They are often very badly exploited by much bigger business who delay on payments, often don't pay at all and force them out of business. We want a society and an economy that invests and a legal system that protects those that are working really hard. Surely a £10 an hour minimum wage, a living wage, a real living wage by 2020 would mean we actually spend probably a bit less on working tax credits, but it would also make an awful lot of people a lot better off. And do you know what? They'd spend the money in the local economy. It would be something that would be good for all of us. I appeal to you, think again. How did we get our welfare state in Britain? How do we get our National Health Service? We got it because the Labour Party was bold enough in the post-war period to invest in the future. Our Labour government will do the same.

If he's allowed to shout out like that, I'm perfectly happy to answer it. And we will, just to complete this point, and we will put in the transport infrastructure that, as I say, and improving education, that will allow us to unite and level up this country. That is what I want to do.

Because we're talking about two different things, aren't we? On the flaw, I think it's important that we give people the protection of their savings, which is greater than it is today. That's why we've set that figure at 100,000. But on the cap, as to where you set that figure as to the absolute figure that people pay, amount that people pay, I think it's right that we have that consultation. We will consult, as I've just said to Barry, with individuals but also with organisations that deal with these issues. Charities that work with older people to make sure that we get that at the right level.

Angus Robertson. Can I just say I think this debate shames and demeans us all. I don't think there's anyone in this room or anybody watching this debate from Cornwall to Caithness who does not understand the positive contribution that people have made to this land who've come from the rest of Europe and the rest of the world and demonising those people is totally unacceptable. Scotland's experience, Scotland's problem has never been immigration, it's been emigration. Now, it may well be the case that there are different realities in different parts of the UK, I accept that. But in Scotland, we value the contribution of people who've come to our land. We value refugees who sought refuge in our country. And there is much, much more that we should do to make sure that people are able to stay in Scotland, but we should also be able to protect people who've come there. So the first thing that I would do is make sure that every EU citizen who is here is guaranteed the right to stay. It is totally unacceptable that this Tory government hasn't been prepared to give them the guarantee, the promise that they can stay. I think that's totally unacceptable. If it is perfectly possible to have different immigration policies in countries such as Canada and Australia, who have different policies between different provinces in their country, it's perfectly possible to have different immigration rules for different parts of the UK, and that's what the SNP supports.

I mean, I love the spin on that. 14 years and you've failed, trebled net migration figures. But what I would say to Lorenzo is that the problems with housing, the roads and GPs and non-public services is decimation that the Tories have done to our public services.

Good evening. This election is about the kind of country we want to be. Now, more than ever, Scotland needs strong SNP voices at Westminster. And SNP MPs will work with others to promote fairness across all of the UK. A vote for the SNP is a vote against Tory cuts. that will harm our public services and push many more hard-working families into poverty. It's a vote for jobs and against an extreme Brexit, which will put jobs and living standards at risk. It's a vote to reinforce the right of the people of Scotland to decide their own future. We can't afford a Tory government which thinks it can do anything it likes without being held to account. And we can't give Theresa May a blank cheque to pursue any kind of Brexit, no matter the cost. In Scotland, the SNP is the only party strong enough to stand up to the Tories. So vote SNP for a strong voice for Scotland at Westminster. That voice is needed now more than ever.

Reform our political system, bring back faith that we vote for things that are representative, we haven't got postal fraud ballots and we do not have a House of Lords filled up with the friends of current Prime Ministers.

look, I've made, I think at the end of the Brexit process people in Scotland should have a choice and I've already set out why I think that's the case. You happy with the answer? And, you know... No, no, what?
We've witnessed nine years of chaotic coalitions already.

We have seriously overcrowded prisons. We've lost a lot of prison officers. And when a prison is overcrowded and there's a shortage of officers, the first thing that goes is education, the next thing that goes is rehabilitation, the next thing that goes is medical support and so on. So you end up with a breeding ground for all the horrors of the future. If we go down that road, we all pay a price. So I'm simply saying this, let's invest in our prison system, invest in rehabilitation, invest crucially in a probation service that works. It was privatised by this government and we're saying it should be completely in public ownership.

Well, Ed, I just want to say to you, just look at some of your promises and some of the things you have made clear. You want to cap child benefit for two years. By contrast, the Green Party wants to lift it to £40 per child per year. You want to, in a classic kind of Labour halfway house, cut university tuition fees by a third, which is better than what we've got now, but just a very modest improvement. You also haven't committed to what extra money you're going to put into the NHS. The NHS that we're being told this morning so desperately needs that extra money. And the other thing that you're really not focusing on is making big multinational companies pay their way. And that's what we desperately need to do at the moment. Rich individuals, that's why the Green Party wants a top rate of tax of 60p. for earnings over £150,000. We need to rebalance this society. You're just holding things at the same kind of level.

Thank you Francis and thank you to all those that have served in our armed forces and continue to do so in what is a very, you know, insecure world at the moment. First of all, Labour's committed to the triple lock on nuclear deterrent, especially in the climate that we're in, which means we will keep our nuclear deterrent, that we will make sure that the four submarines are built in Barrow, and that we will continue with the upgrades. We will have a commitment to the 2.5% spending of GDP, which obviously is very important. And I think one of the other things that is a real scandal at the moment is our armed forces and the accommodation that they're given. So we will look at that issue as well. We want to make sure that everyone has secure, decent homes in the UK.

Thank you very much. Penny Mordaunt. No, and if you have a Labour government, they will take you back in. They will tie you on defence, on migration, on regulation, without any of the benefits of membership. Carla Denyer.

No. We're not proposing that you should be able to purchase alcohol at the age of sixteen, no we're not. We would however like to see, for example, another area, we'd like to see a lowering of the voting age. I think that people are now mature enough at an earlier age to be able to vote at an earlier age also.

We're offering people these incentives. Hold your horses. Gordon Brown asked me about nuclear power. I don't have a sort of theological opposition to nuclear power. I just think it's extraordinarily expensive. very, very expensive indeed. There are some calculations that it would lead to average energy bills actually increasing in this country rather than decreasing, and it takes a long, long, long time to build these new nuclear plants. All the experts agree that it would take well into the next decade to create new nuclear energy, which would be too late to deal with the energy problems we have now. For a fraction of the money that I think both David Cameron and Gordon Brown want to spend on subsidizing the nuclear industry, we could develop a mass insulation programs of our homes of our schools and our hospitals. Remember, 27% of all carbon dioxide emissions in this country go straight out of your window through the roof of your house. If we only use energy more efficiently and also, of course, invested some of the money which would be wasted on big nuclear projects, on wind energy and other renewable energies, I think that is the way towards a sustainable future.

Leanne Wood. Plaid Cymru believes that we need to invest in our young people, particularly in education, as that is the best route out of poverty. You're right when you say that young people today are going to fare worse than the older generation. And it's the first time for a long while that the generation of today is going to be worse off than the generation before it. Plaid Cymru wants to provide free tuition fees for students, but because of austerity, we are not in a position to do that, even if we were running the Welsh Government. We want to keep the tuition fee subsidy that is available for Welsh students, but we would like students to study in Wales so that we can invest that public money into Welsh universities. but we would like some courses to be made available for free and I've talked earlier on about the need for doctors in the health service in Wales and we believe that we can attract more doctors by providing free tuition for those particular skilled group of workers. Liam Wood, thank you. Ed Miliband.

Nick, we all have the right to feel safe in our homes and on our streets. My home city of Bristol has seen an epidemic of knife crime. One of my colleague's friends was one of those victims, so this strikes very close to home for me. There are obvious things that could be done in terms of making sure that there's more officers out and about, but the other parties will also, I expect, tell you that they'll be tough on crime.

We are in dark and dangerous times, and this election is about trust. I know how many casually broken promises you've heard from politicians. I joined the Greens after hearing too many of these myself 18 years ago. I know you want real change this year, and things can change dramatically when you use your right to vote. I'll be honest and say that Brexit would change things for the worse. And I'll be hopeful and say that I believe you want that final say. And that you and your children are crying out for real action to stop climate chaos. That's what I stand for today. For good ideas that build up communities for the common good. For the Green New Deal we need to prevent disaster. Our efforts together to do politics differently mean you have the chance now to elect more Green MPs, a louder voice for you and future generations, adding to the volume and the clarity that Caroline Lucas has been providing for a decade. You can make history, shine a light in the darkness with your vote. If not now, when?

Well, I think meat consumption is falling anyway and more and more people are recognising that you need a balanced diet. It doesn't mean everybody's got to become a vegetarian tomorrow and neither will they, but it does mean we have to look at the balance of our food sources as well. Therefore, more UK production of fruit and vegetables is perfectly possible. Looking at the issues of the damage we do to our environment by but flying vegetables in from all over the world when they could be produced here. There's a whole lot of issues surrounding that. But we also have to recognize that many people in this country are actually obese because of the poor quality food that they eat, or hungry because they can't afford to eat properly. So it is also about food pricing. And with our farms, yes, The farms are absolutely crucial to this. It is about extending biodiversity on our farms. It is about expanding headlands around fields. It is about rewilding part of it in order to strengthen our levels of biodiversity. If we go on damaging our soil at the rate we are, destroying the insect population at the rate we are, the levels of agricultural production will actually start to go down, not up.

There has never been a time when confidence in politics, politicians and our institutions has been lower than it is right now today. And that's because three and a half years ago, all the parties on this platform told you to vote Remain, but you didn't. You said, we want to leave. And three and a half years on, we find most of the parties here still don't want to honour the vote, want you to vote again, some even want to revoke Article 50, and the Conservatives have had three and a half years to sort this out, yet they sort a half-in, half-out fudge. I formed the Brexit Party because I want to get Brexit done properly. I want us to become an independent nation. That is what the vote was for, to leave the institutions of the EU and to be self-governing. Once we've done that, that's just the beginning of a political revolution that needs to happen. The Brexit Party are the new radicals. We've got to reform our rotten institutions, the House of Lords, our voting system, corruption with postal voting. Let's get back to being a proper, functioning democracy.

I think the key is of course urgent needs payments for people so that they are sure they can stay in their own home. I agree that we need to do more for carers. I want carers to be able to manage their own budgets as well and that's something that we're really working on for the future as well. but we've got to find a solution to this big problem and the big problem is people don't want to have to make the choice between owning their home and getting the care they need and that's why in the next Parliament I think it's important to try with the Commission to reach a consensus on what the funding will be for the future.
Well, Fiona, as you said, these investigations are ongoing. They are rightly confidential. One of them is a criminal investigation that's been conducted by the police. These are very serious matters.

had chemical and biological weapons. That he could manufacture new stocks of VX and sarin agent within months of doing so. That he could use those weapons against, not just military targets but strategic targets; you can go and read it.

OK. I've always paid my tax in full. But, you know, and I accept I earn a lot of money in the job I am now in, but when I grew up, my dad was a toolmaker. He worked in a factory. It's true. My mum was a nurse and, actually, we couldn't make ends meet, which actually isn't a laughing matter. We couldn't make ends meet. We couldn't pay our bills. I know what it feels like. to feel anxious about the next bill which you can't pay. And what I have recognised is a lot of people today are in precisely the same position as I was in. Politics is about your policies, of course it is, but it's also about who do you have in your mind's eye when you make your decisions. Who are you thinking about when you make your decisions? I'm thinking about the people who are struggling with their bills. I'm thinking about working people who deserve I want to come on... I'm thinking about working people who want their living standards to go up, and our plan for the country, which we will set out tomorrow, is a confident, hopeful manifesto for change.

Yes, I met with the local branch of Citizens Advice in my home city just earlier this week, and they told me the exact same story. They're helping people who've never needed their help before, and the key message that they wanted me and other prospective MPs to go away with is an understanding that, for too many households, the problem is extremely simple. Money in is less than money out. The government could choose policies that would fix this. It's choosing not to. So's the Labour Party. The Green Party thinks differently. Daisy Cooper, you wanted to come in.
So I've explained the reasons not only when a referendum will take place, but more than that, I'm the first Deputy Prime Minister, we're the first government to ever put that into law.

Well, I think we can learn a lot, actually, from Scotland, where they seem to have things like free personal care, and the Green Party would like to see free social care as well, and we would pay for that by, for example, having a more progressive taxation system, things like a wealth tax. Essentially, this does come down to what kind of country we want to be. And as I've said before, we do want to be the kind of country that makes sure that we look after people all through their lives, including when they get towards the end of their lives. But can I just raise one issue that hasn't been talked about yet, which is really vital, and that is about how we actually prevent ill health in the first place. If we could take more action on things like the air pollution emergency, which is linked to 40,000 early deaths in this country, we could save our NHS a huge amount of money and, indeed, social care too.

So we believe there should be a cap in terms of what you pay for care. That £72,000 cap that once upon a time was a cross-party solution under Andrew Dilnot. We believe the dementia tax is an appalling attack on the most vulnerable people in this country. Nine out of ten houses will pay for it. If you have dementia or your loved one has dementia, your house is at risk. And on Thursday you've got the chance to vote to stop it, to vote Liberal Democrat.

Well, Amy, thank you. Well, thank you for all your previous volunteering and service to the party. And when it comes to D-Day, as I said, I absolutely did not mean to cause any offence or hurt. That was not my intention. I made a mistake and I unreservedly apologise for it. And all I can do is ask for people's forgiveness. And when it comes to trust in my commitment to our veterans community and the pride that I have in what they've done for our country, What I would say to people is you can look at the things that we've done. There is a veterans minister sitting around the cabinet table for the first time with more funding than we've ever had, focused on supporting veterans with their mental health, physical health, employment opportunities, housing, because it's something I care deeply about. But more generally at this election, as you said, you're undecided. This election is about the future and the future that you want for our country and I believe that the manifesto that we put out yesterday contains a very clear plan, a bold set of actions that will deliver a more secure future for you and everybody. And that starts with cutting everyone's taxes, because I believe people should keep more of their hard-earned money. It means bringing migration down. It means a more sensible approach to net zero that prioritises our energy security and your family bills. And it means protecting pensioners, because I think if you've worked hard all your life, you deserve dignity in retirement. So those are some of the things I talked about Yesterday, that's a choice at this election. It's an important moment for our country. But given the opportunity, I can deliver a secure future for you and everyone else.

Well, Charlotte, I'm greatly concerned about the debt that your generation faces. But that debt for about the 50% who go to university is tuition fee debt, which we want to reduce to zero and pay off the student loans.
