Students are leaving with 44,000 pounds worth of debt on average. 73% of them will never pay it off. And of course, you're going out into a workplace where one in five workers is on less than a living wage. People aren't paid enough money to live on. They're indeed stacking up debts just to meet their basic costs. The cost of housing is enormous and that's why we do want to deal with the debt and the deficit and we reduce it to about 1% of GDP from 5% of GDP in a manifesto now. But if we invest some of that money in building a social home, a home that you might be able to live in and a genuinely affordable rent, If we invest that in renewable energy, in providing warm, comfortable, affordable to heat homes, then you are much better off. We've invested in your future, in your entire generation's future.

Yes, it has. Yes, I was very good. We've not made as much progress on cutting waiting lists as I would have liked. And that was something that I was keen to do. And it has proved more difficult. And it's been proved more difficult for a number of reasons. Obviously, recovering from the pandemic is not easy. But we are now making progress. And actually, the number that you gave is just over 200,000 lower than it was a few months ago.

David, I'm wondering what world you live in. I think we all wonder that. You talk about your negotiating skill in Europe. You made a big stand against the President of the European Commission, Jean-Claude Juncker. You lost by 26 votes to two because you had no allies. And the problem is David Cameron has marginalised us in Europe. Now, I just want to be honest with people at home. There are false promises you get from David Cameron. He stood on this stage five years ago and said, net migration into the tens of thousands. And he actually said, kick me out if I fail to deliver. Well, I suggest you do. But he failed on that promise, and he's going to make the promise again. And there's false solutions from Nigel Farage. I've just got to be frank with people. I don't think our place lies outside the European Union. I think that would be a disaster for jobs. I think that would be a disaster for families and businesses. Let's change Europe so it works better for us, including on immigration. But let's not sacrifice jobs, businesses, and families.

But what Angela's refusing to admit there is what was referred to earlier, which is the shadow health secretary essentially saying that he's going to hold the door open to the private sector. Now, that is not... He did say that, and that is not... That's not true. ..where the answer lies. The answer lies in investment. Well, I'll answer that directly. And when it comes to staffing...

Tonight, this country stands at a crossroads and the choice is stark. You can stand up for what really matters, values of openness, tolerance and compassion. Or you can turn inward to isolation, division and hate. You know, one more MP from the other parties makes no real difference. But more Green MPs would be truly transformative. On June the 8th, I urge you, don't look back. Choose what kind of future you really want. Vote for change. Vote Green. And together, let's build a caring and a more confident Britain. Jeremy Corbyn.
I don't run a sofa style of government. There were over twenty cabinet discussions of the Iraq war.

Correct. And I've said that to George. and what's more President Bush knows that because when I met him in London during a state visit a year and a half ago we had this very discussion and I confirmed that was a view and interestingly although we disagreed Rather revealingly, he remarked to me during that discussion that he'd enjoyed it, although he didn't see eye to eye on Iraq, because, as he put it, you're one of the few guys I'm meeting on this visit that actually doesn't agree with me. He was only allowed to meet people that were singing from his hymn sheet. OK. You, sir.
Continuing to invest in renewable energy will make our energy cheaper. Investing in energy saving will save everybody money on their bills.

I just want to say that when we're talking about money not going so far that isn't happening by accident and it's a great shame that Theresa May isn't here to defend the government's record or to try to because she couldn't. Because what's happening here is a very deliberate policy of targeting some of the most vulnerable people. And I see them in my constituency surgeries. I see people who are desperately trying to fill in the forms to go from disability living allowance to the personal independence payment, and they are absolutely overwhelmed by the bureaucracy and they are also overwhelmed by the fact that there are deliberate targets which actually mean that some of them simply can't get the funding that they deserve. So let no one be under any illusion that this is happening by accident. This is a cruel and callous government, and the sooner it goes, the better.

I think the NHS is a wonderful and brilliant institution but it is suffering under the most incredible pressure. The staff are unbelievably stressed by the job they have to do and the insufficiency of facilities to deal with it. There are 33,000 nurse vacancies at the moment in the NHS. I'll give a story. Yesterday, a woman, a friend of mine, died at 6.30 yesterday morning from secondary breast cancer. The day before, she'd gone to hospital at the recommendation of her GP in order to get urgent treatment. She waited eight hours. The nurses that were trying to help her were unable to get anyone to see her because they were under such strain and stress. And so she recorded a video saying, please, in my memory, make sure nobody else goes through this pain. We've got to fund our NHS properly and fill the vacancies and make sure it's there for all time. It's one of the most civilized things about this country. Thank you, Mr. Chodron.
Listen, we require migrant workers to maintain our health service and much of our industry. We have a skills gap because we haven't invested enough in skills training.

It's very old thinking. Yeah, I mean the more diverse a grid is, the more you have different sources, the bigger the grid is, the more you have battery power in people's homes, the more you're investing in long-term storage, the easier it is to do without nuclear power. We cannot be investing in any more nuclear power stations and they take longer than ten years.

People want to see more Green MPs in the House of Commons holding the feet to the fire of the other parties. And under a broken political system, we have to do this. We have big differences with the Liberal Democrats as we do with other parties. But we're always willing to work with them in a more collaborative, grown-up way where there's common ground. And to get more MPs in Parliament, more Green MPs in Parliament, that's what we have to do. We got 2 million votes. in the European elections. In 2015, we got one MP off over a million votes. That's not a fair system.

Well, I think the real danger here is we've gone from a vote for Brexit across the UK, Scotland voted to remain in the European Union, but we've gone from Brexit to this extreme form of Brexit where we are running the real risk of jettisoning many of the benefits that we get from participating in the European Union on an equal basis with other partners. Now, you know, I was in St Andrews University last week, one of the best universities in the world, and they are worried about losing access to research funding, they're worried about losing some of the best academic staff in the world, they're worried about losing students. And those concerns are replicated across so many sectors of our economy. And why are we running the risk of a hard, extreme Brexit? It is around control of immigration. And Theresa may not have had the guts to be here tonight, but her spokesperson in the form of Paul Nuttall certainly appears to be here in her place. And I know immigration is a difficult topic for politicians. People have understandable concerns. But when we talk about European migrants, We're talking about people who work in our National Health Service, we're talking about people who serve us in our restaurants, we're talking about people who make a contribution and it is outrageous that their futures have not yet been guaranteed.

Well, you say resist the temptation to get onto Brexit, but the reality is that neither Richard nor Rishi can guarantee that they're going to be able to make any spending pledges, not that Rishi's got very many, as far as I could see, because Brexit is going to make a real difference to our economy. It's going to absolutely trash our economy. It's going to make us wealthier, yes, that's right. Every single analysis shows that it is going to trash our economy, it's going to throw people out of work, it's going to be a disaster. So, what you need... is a set of policies, first of all, to remain inside the EU. And that is what the Green Party is proposing at this election. But also, we are proposing a significant investment in a Green New Deal. And we're completely upfront about that. That would cost 100 billion pounds a year. We want to do that for the next 10 years.

We're definitely not expecting employers to fund the welfare bill. But my point at the moment is that the welfare bill is actually spending billions and billions of pounds subsidising low pay in our country. And I'm afraid that's the sort of reality. Now we've got to raise the minimum wage in a way that is cautious, because we've got to do it so it doesn't put people out of work, and that's what we'll do. But I think we've also got to confront the fact that because we're one of the low-pay capitals of Europe, we are spending tens of billions of pounds on welfare as a result of that. So it's about working with the private sector to make sure you can raise wages. Alright, on that point let me bring in Chris Magee, please.

Well thank you for raising that because we've got, what we've done is we've set out the big change that we want to make but also what the first steps will be that we can do on day one. So the day after the election I want to start on our first step stabilising the economy to make sure we never go through a cost of living crisis like this before. Set up Great British Energy so we can keep your bills down. I want to start on the 40,000 appointments each and every week that we need to get the waiting list down in our NHS. OK. We will be doing that the morning after the election.

They've been in power for 14 years, and after 14 years, with a few weeks to go before the election, they come out with this desperate idea. If it's such a brilliant idea, why for 14 years has your government not lifted a finger to do it? It's a desperate attempt, now the election's being called, to get some attention and put something on the table. The other thing I would say is this. It's very important, Miles, is that the money the Prime Minister says he's going to use for this national service, he's going to take away from levelling up. Remember that promise in 2019, we're going to level up? Well, now he's going to use the money for national service, and the army say, and the military say, this isn't going to help us because we don't have the wherewithal to train people, and it'll take away from our own budget. Allow Rishi Sunak to respond very specifically. It's not thought through, it's not sensible, it's desperate.

Let's start with private schools. Look, on private schools, I completely understand that families up and down the country work hard and save hard to send their children to private school because they're aspirational for their children. But all parents are aspirational for their children, every single parent. And I want every single child, wherever they come from, whatever their background, and whichever school they go to, to have the same opportunities. At the moment, in our state secondary schools, we don't have the basic teachers that we need for things like maths. Now that's really shocking, because if you don't have, some people here and watching will be familiar with maths being taught by the supply teacher, or the PE teacher, but not a designated maths teacher. That really impacts on the lives of people in our state secondary schools, because if you don't do well at maths, then that inhibits what you can go on to do in A-levels, and it will affect the job you can get, and apprenticeships, et cetera, et cetera. That's intolerable, so I want to recruit the staff we need to teach core subjects like maths so that every child, for example, has a maths teacher to teach maths. In order to fund that, we're going to remove the tax break on private schools. That's a tough decision. It's not an easy one. I do know how hard people work, but I've got to look at it across the board. I've got to make sure those teachers are there for all children and make sure that everyone has that chance, so that wherever you come from, whatever your background. On the question of rent and mortgages, this is a real... I mean, you're obviously living it. But, you know, young people who are working hard, you know, on a reasonably decent wage, but then having to pay a huge part of it for rent over and over again, because rents are through the roof, the housing isn't what it should be,

It won't be privatised by UKIP, we'll keep it in public hands and we're going to be able to fund the bursaries for nurses and midwives. Well, no, absolutely. It isn't, actually. And we are not going to privatise it. And we will, as I said, we're going to give the bursaries to you and we're going to... Can I go back to the original question?

Well, I just think it's a great shame that governments and others are basically trying to pit different groups of people against each other. I want to see a good life for people who are older, and that's why we would make sure that they had a proper citizen's pension that was a decent, good pension for them to live on. But at the same time, we need to make sure that our young people are properly set up for life too. And to say that we have to, and again, I come back to the fact that we are the fifth largest economy, to say that we have to make choices between whether it's our older people we look after or whether it's our younger people, I think is completely wrong. We should be doing both.

So you are going to freeze it? Every single budget, we have supported the freeze on fuel duty. And you will continue to do that as Prime Minister if you would win? We have to concentrate on growing the economy. On fuel duty, that has to be decided budget by budget, but my track record is... Do you think he's going to do it or not? I don't know. We've supported the cap on fuel duty every single time it's come up. OK. I want to do things differently. If we just do things in the same way as we've done for the last 14 years, we'll end up in the same mess. I'm not prepared to do that. This election is an election about change, about doing things differently, about growing the economy, and making sure we create the wealth so this country can run our public service. OK. I'm going to have one more go, and then we can end it. No, no, no. I do want to finish this. I ran a public service for five years. I ran a public service in the austerity years. I know what it means to be a public servant. I absolutely care about our public services. My wife works in the NHS, my mum worked in the NHS, and I know what it means to the NHS. So I absolutely want our public services to function.
People tell me the most effective use of it is not to use it because it's there.

Well, we want to do that, but let's be clear Anushka, the Conservatives have ruined our economy, Liz Truss's budget has trashed it, and so we can't do everything we want to in the first instance. We've got a set of priorities and they are costed.

...in this country. We're not recommending it as a course of action for 16 year olds, but what we're saying is that at the moment this country, in terms of the way the laws have evolved over many years, does have a rather inconsistent approach to what age you are allowed to be considered an adult, and what age you're not.
You know what? Very simply, when you get your job, I'm going to cut your taxes. You're going to keep more of your money and spend it on whatever you want. There you go.

You invited Hamas to the House of Commons. I think we have to recognize we live in a multi-faith, multicultural society. An attack on any religion is totally unacceptable. Thank you. And we should recognize that we should bring people together and have much stronger communities as a result.

I believe there is still a decent Britain worth fighting for. The vision of Theresa May and Paul Nuttall is not the only choice. The fact that Theresa May is not here tonight tells you that she is taking you for granted. She thinks she owns this election. She owns the result. She owns your future. Your children's future. You deserve a leader who cares about the things that you care about. A leader who will stand up for you and your family, for your NHS, for your schools, for your children's future. You need a leader who will stop Theresa May's extreme version of Brexit. You don't need a difference of opinion, you need a different option. You need someone who will step up and fight for you. I am determined to offer you the chance to change Britain's future because the Britain I love is open, tolerant and united. This is a country worth fighting for. It's a country I want to lead.

You know, we have an enormous amount to be proud of as a country, but we do need to do more to make sure our young people, like you, Miles, get the opportunities that they deserve. So that's why we will introduce a modern form of national service. And I think it's going to be transformational for young people in our country, giving them the skills. and opportunities they need to succeed in life, fostering a culture of service in our society, bringing it together, but also strengthening our resilience and security for the future. And I think it is going to be something that's incredibly positive. But beyond that, I want you to have financial security. So when you get that job, when you save for that house, we make it easier, which is why I want to keep cutting your taxes so you can keep more of your money, build for a deposit for that first house, and not saddle you with £2,000 of higher taxes.

Yes, our manifesto has addressed this issue. It is very painful some of the decisions that are made where the frankly arbitrary level of income is chosen. Couples are split apart, families are split apart, and people like the person you're talking about have invested a huge amount of time and energy into this country are then removed from this country. Who loses? We all lose. The family loses, the children lose, we lose as a society. And we will change those immigration laws so that there can be proper family reunion. I think that has to be the right and sensible thing to do.

Offshore wind is a huge opportunity. 5% of the UK's electricity needs could be met from the west coast of Scotland alone. Add that to the major projects like the Mersey Barrage or the Swansea Bay Lagoon, you get a huge way down the line of getting totally sustainable electricity production in Britain. You have to plan for it, you have to invest in it, and you have to make sure... We have to make sure that the jobs are there to produce it, such as the BiFab works in Scotland are very much part of that industrial revolution for the future.

Well, let me tell you very specifically what I think we need to do for businesses like yours. And this does go to the big choice at this election. There'll be some people who tell you that the way we succeed as a country is as long as a few people at the top do well and large corporations, that's what powers the economy. Look, I've got a different view and that's your choice for the next five years. I think it's when working people, when every person in our country succeeds, that Britain succeeds. Now for your business, we'd make a different choice from Mr Cameron. He wants to cut taxes further for the largest businesses. I would cut your business rates if I was Prime Minister. Now that's important, I hope, for your business, but I think it tells a bigger story about how Britain succeeds. And as I say, I think it succeeds not just with a few successful corporations, but with millions of businesses and millions of working people.

I feel passionate about opportunity for infants and young children. That's why we introduced the Child Tax Credit. That's why we've created the children's centres that are now focuses for the community. That's why nursery education is at three now and not, as before, at four. That's why we are financing personal tuition for people in the schools so that if they fall behind, they can catch up. But I do say all this is at risk, because David is not answering the question. He will cut child tax credits. He's going to cut the Schools Budget. He's going to cut nursery education by charging for it, and he has a coalition for cuts with Nick on child tax credits. They're both going to cut child tax credits for the future. That is not the way to deal with the problem that our questioner, who is so concerned about opportunities for children, has raised. You can't solve the problem by taking away all the advances we've made.

Briefly, Chris Philp. Yeah, so look, Petra, you're right to raise this. Women and girls across the country should feel safe to walk the streets at day or at night without feeling any fear. And as a father of a young daughter, I feel that as strongly as anyone. We've recruited 1,000 independent sexual violence advisors to support victims through the criminal justice process. There's 1,000 extra judges to speed up the legal process, rape prosecutions. are 56% higher than they were in 2010 under Labour. And there's a whole range of things that this government has made illegal. Coercive and controlling behaviour. We've banned upskirting. We've made stalking illegal. Revenge porn and deepfakes also banned. These are all things that almost entirely affect women and girls, that it's this government that has taken action by banning. We'd like to go further. We're going to outlaw spiking. We're going to make sure there's a 25-year minimum sentence for domestic homicide where someone murders their partner. We're going to make sure that victims can give evidence quickly on videotape. It's called a Section 28 evidence. It's almost like you say everything's OK. To make sure... to make sure that evidence is given quickly. Rape sentences have gone up from six and a half years under Labour to about ten years now and we have legislated to make sure, to Richard's point, that people who are convicted of rape are not automatically released at the halfway point as they were previously. Labour, shockingly, voted against that measure during the passage of the police crime and sentences. THOMAS-SYMONDS: How astonishing. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Chris stands there when 97 of every 100 reported rapes in this country today, absolutely nothing will happen. THOMAS-SYMONDS: And he talks about early release. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Chris and his government are presiding over people being released 70 days early, a number of whom posed a threat to the public. THOMAS-SYMONDS: I mean, come on, Chris, you should be apologising to this audience, not boasting.

Oh, go on. Let me just explain briefly what I want on overall majority. Look, I think this is important. We put a manifesto before the British people You get to decide in three weeks' time which is the government of this country. I think, frankly, this coalition government has become an excuse for broken promises. Nick Clegg broke his promises on tuition fees. David Cameron broke his promise on VAT. Now, what I want is a majority government to implement my manifesto. To run this country again for working people, not for the richest and most powerful. To raise the minimum wage to £8 an hour. To ban those exploitative zero-hours contracts. To have a mansion tax for the NHS, clamp down on tax avoidance and have more doctors and nurses. And look, on this question of what happens after the election, I've got an old-fashioned view. Let the people decide on May 7th. But I want a majority Labour government because I think it's the best thing for the country.
Right, well it's a very serious problem and one of the worries which older people have is that they will have to sell their home in order to pay for the costs of long-term care.
I think you had a question earlier from, I think there was a junior doctor here. And I think everyone knows the impact the industrial action has had. That's why we haven't made as much.

And that's what we can do immediately. And Mr Johnson will spend at least seven years negotiating with the USA on access to our public services, on the price of medicines in Britain, on the access to our National Health Service. And he knows he can't get a deal quickly with the USA because of the way in which the US political system works. And so what he will do is walk out of a relationship with the EU into a relationship with nobody. And that is where the chaos will come.

I think we do it better by offering a sensible alternative which is also, and I'll finish on this point, also about improving bus services all across the country. They're pretty good in London, they're pretty poor in most other parts of the country. There has to be full regulation and demand for provision of adequate bus services all over the country to cut down on air pollution from cars.

We've said that we will be spending more on the criminal justice system and that is as a result of nine years of economic growth, of the very high level of employment we have in this country, the highest levels of employment since records began. As a result of that, as a result of a strong economy, we are now able to invest more in our public services. So is that a yes? That will be in the criminal justice system, it will be in the police. Is it specifically legal aid? We will the criminal justice more broadly. We will certainly be investing more money at the moment 20,000 extra police officers for example plus the extra investments in the health service and education but Disregard as you will it is important. I think it's important to make the point So we can only afford to do this as a country if we have a strong economy. If we risk that by having an old-fashioned far-left Labour government, there will be less money to fund all forms of public services, particularly services like the criminal justice system.

You know, I've not been talking here tonight about things that I'm simply promising. I'm the only one here in government. The things I've been talking about are the things we're doing. OK. We've got to do more. We've got to leave it there.

Well, I've been trying to explain that we want a very different approach, a bigger focus on social homes. That means the state investing in council properties. My wife, I met her on a Liberal Democrat housing policy working group.

Well, he was, as you say, investigated by the gambling commission because he bets on himself to lose. As soon as the commission said they were investigating, I suspended him. And I've made very clear to my candidates that I expect high standards in politics. I said last week that the Prime Minister should have taken decisive action. And I said last week, should one of my candidates be investigated by the Commission, then they would be suspended and their feet wouldn't leave the floor. And that's what I did. And so my candidates know I expect the highest standards. and that I'll take decisive action if they fall below those standards. But do you know that... I am... Back to Sue's point. Do you know... I'm absolutely determined... OK. ..to return politics to public service. Do you know that none of your other candidates have placed similar bets? That integrity in public service is so important for our country.

Well, I don't like these words because we are bringing it under control. Net inward migration is falling and will continue to fall as a result of the measures we've taken. We've brought together the police and the immigration officials and the customs officials in one agency. We're doing that already. Illegal immigrants are deterred because we've got ID cards for foreign nationals now. So an employer cannot say to someone, you can come and have this job. They've got to ask for the identity card first. And there are big fines if employers break the law. Now, we've got to do more. And that means we've got to tighten the number of skills that we need in this country. And that's why we're moving from care assistants to chefs, right through other occupations where we train up British people to do the skills.

The point is this Angela, you're in power, you're in government in Wales now, you could do This is nothing to do with austerity. These are laws that you could pass where you are in power now and you're not doing it.

Well, Alex, as a father of four kids myself, nothing is more important than the education of our children. And it is a reminder that as this election takes place, we are just eight weeks off a time when two out of every three headteachers will be dismissing and getting rid of at least one teaching post in this country. And that is an outrage. That is what Theresa May hopes you don't realise as she seeks to do to our schools what she has already done to our hospitals. And there is nothing more important than developing skills and education and a love of learning from the earliest possible age. That is why, for example, The forced sitting of SATs for young primary school children can often put those young people off learning for life. What I want to do is have a government that does not interfere with our teachers on a daily basis but trusts our teachers to teach our children and to let them get on with the job and give them the £7 billion extra that we will give them to enable them to do so.

Paul Nutter. Of course, we are a growing population, and as we spoke about earlier, immigration plays a large part on that. And if we continue on the road that we're on at the moment, the population will be 80 million by the middle of this century. It's unsustainable. It puts pressure on the NHS. It puts pressure, of course, on social care. What the NHS needs is more money. And my party is committed to putting six billion extra every single year into the National Health Service. This will fund 20,000 new nurses, 10,000 new GPs and allow us to scrap hospital car parking charges which are nothing more than a tax on the sick. We also will put 1.4 billion a year into social care. And how would we pay for this? Well, we would take that money directly from a foreign aid budget, which is costing us around 13 billion pounds every single year. We would cut that back to 0.2% of GDP, which is exactly the same as it was in the United States under the Barack Obama. Paul Nuttall, thank you. Carolyn Lucas.

Plaid Cymru would oppose the Tories' plans to extend the right to buy because we believe that there should be more social housing and not less. Tackling rising homelessness will not be addressed by allowing more people to become private home owners. There does need to be investment in housing, and the Prime Minister's announcement suggests that there's an additional £5 billion to be spent on housing. We would like councils to build more council housing and for the profits to be reinvested back in that housing for more people. We would also like to see rent caps in order to make housing for rent more affordable for people in all communities. and we'd like to double the council tax on holiday homes.

Fair point. But we have to do everything we can, obviously to protect ourselves, but the best protection is having good and reasonable relationships with the rest of the world so we don't descend into a mentality where there are military blocks starting threatening each other. And I'm utterly determined to do everything I can to bring about a more peaceful world. I've spent a lot of my life with UN and other organisations trying to bring about principle of no first use, principles of disarmament and principles of bringing about peace around the world. That surely is something we can all work for. I think we have to recognize that there is deep inequality and injustice in the world, but the threats are, as I said, cyber security and terrorism. The other threats are actually of environmental disaster around the world and large numbers of people fleeing from environmental disaster, in effect, environmental refugees, which is why I was so disappointed in President Trump's decision particularly in view of what has been achieved by people working together to try and protect and sustain this planet. We've only got one planet. Let's get together when we live on it and, above all, let's not destroy it.

There's all sorts of things that people do across Europe, whether that's looking at driving licenses, other access to finance, all sorts of other things. The right thing to do is to allow... Access to finance? Yes.

Well we have a situation as you know where there's a devolved government in Scotland and they have the ability to make separate decisions on a whole variety of issues affecting people in Scotland and they've been given extra powers in relation to dealing with welfare so it is up to them to make that decision as to how they choose to apply certain welfare benefits. We will, we have said and we will in government means test the winter fuel payment and the reason for doing that is this, at the moment we have a situation where pensions who are better off are getting that help and support where you see working families who might be struggling not getting that sort of support and I think it's right and I think you know I've met pensioners who've said to me that they don't think they should get that winter fuel payment. What we will do with the money that's released is we'll put it into health and social care. So it will be going towards actually ensuring that those systems are providing better quality care for older people and others.

And that is the case for so many people. You just need to look at the prices on the shelves, you look at the prices of the supermarkets, and wages have stagnated. For me, in my own personal experience, I said earlier on, I grew up in Preston, a little further north from here, and I remember, in the years following my childhood, realising, I guess, the poverty that I had been brought up in. You only realise, on the mark of good parents, is you only realise that you were poor many years afterwards. And what I, looking back, saw about my community was the way we were taken for granted by a government in London that cared nothing for us. And what I want is a government, and I want to lead a country, I am determined to do that, that makes sure that everybody is decent to one another. And that means fair taxes. That means that everybody pays, but it also means that rich are taxed properly. And it also means having a government that does not take for granted large swathes of this country because it assumes it's going to win a landslide on the 8th of June. This is why opposition is so important in this election. If you want your hospital, your well-being, your daily, your weekly budget to be taken for granted, then a Theresa May majority of Margaret Thatcher proportions is the best way to achieve it.

Firstly, I do want to say that I don't trust the Conservatives with migrants' rights. They created a hostile environment. They deported black people in the Windrush scandal. And now they're chasing the votes of Brexit Party supporters in order to get Boris Johnson back into Downing Street. We need to value migrants. The NHS would flounder overnight if it weren't for people who have come to this country as migrants.
No, it is building six new ones immediately but a programme over the next ten years with seed funding already going in to build 40 new hospitals.

if you are in, but he wasn't responsible for police. There was a police crime commissioner and it was Labour. If you live in a Labour area, you are 40% more likely to be a victim of crime. That is a shocking statistic. We need more police and we need police who are embedded in communities that can follow up with people. If kids are doing stuff, they follow up with the school, they follow up with the parents. That's what conservative police crime commissioners are doing. In Hampshire, we've got 600 extra officers. Every single one of my wards has a named Bobby. People know, build up intelligence. That is how we get crime down, and we have got it down by 55%.

Thank you. Sian Barry. It's easy to fear the future. The shadow of Brexit hangs over us. Runaway climate chaos is getting closer. And all around, there is inequality. But despite all this, Greens don't fear the future. We are the future. You can trust us to keep our promises and hold the other parties to account. Greens in Parliament will campaign for remain and let no government get away with more failure on climate chaos. We can't afford it. This election could be the last chance we have to transform Britain. It's time to change course. So vote Green, if not now, when?

No, frankly, I'm not. And firstly, congratulations for achieving citizenship and for voting in your first election here. Obviously, you're taking it seriously. And well done for reaching that milestone and participating in this fantastic democracy of ours. Look, there was a question earlier about safe and legal routes. And I think You were asking about this as well. Where there are crises, this country, the United Kingdom, is generous-hearted and open. So when there was the crisis in Syria, when Assad was murdering his own people, we set up the UK resettlement scheme. We directly and legally and safely resettled 25,000 people from Syria and from refugee camps on Syria's borders into the UK. That was the largest legal resettlement scheme in relation to Syria. I've mentioned Ukraine already. 200,000 Ukrainians have been welcomed here to the UK. A similar number of people from Hong Kong fleeing oppression by the communist regime in Afghanistan. Of course, your own the Taliban.

I missed that, but let me respond by saying that I think that Mr Corbyn, I'm sure, is very well-intentioned, but I think that in his handling of this particular issue, his unwillingness to take a stand, to stand up for Jewish people in the Labour Party, his unwillingness to protect them, to put an arm round them, Is it, in my view, a failure of leadership? You cannot be neutral on questions like this any more, in my view, than you can try to lead this country and be neutral on the issue of Brexit. And it is a failure to take a stand, to have a point of view that, in the end, will cost this country gravely and deeply. And I don't want to waste another year, next year and more, in vacillation and confusion about what sort of position Mr Corbyn is willing to take, what sort of leadership he's willing to... A failure of leadership is when you use racist remarks to describe people in different countries or in our society.

You've seen the same polls as I have that put the Labour majority well into the three figures, if not close to 200. So the people of Scotland have a choice to make. Who do they think is going to best stand up for Scotland's interests in Westminster? And it's the SNP who will do that. That is our USP. It's what we do. We put Scotland's interests first, growing Scotland's economy, delivering for our public services, and making sure that we can decide our own future.

Urm. Well let's, let's talk about those things. The ERM was indeed a terrible mistake. When we went in to the ERM, we were supported by the Labour Party, the Liberals, the TUC, the CBI, and we are the only party that's learned our lesson ..

Well, I think that under the United Nations Charter, if our country is attacked by another country, we have a right to defend ourselves. What we don't have a right to do under the United Nations Charter is simply, without UN authority, go into another country and change its government. Because if we didn't like governments, and there's many around the world we don't do, where do you stop? Are we going to start a war in Zimbabwe, for example, because Mugabe is a murderous dictator? Are we going to invade North Korea? Where does it stop? The world order breaks down.

Labour policy to scrap zero-hours contracts. First of all, zero-hours contracts for many people mean a lifetime of stress and a lifetime of great difficulty. Imagine what it's like if your sole source of income is a zero-hours contract job. You wake up in the morning, you look at your mobile phone and see if your employer wants you that day or not, that week or not. you don't know what your income is going to be. pay you X number of hours per week on a flexible basis so that you will have an average pay of, say, 10 hours per week. That means those people are loyal to the company, those people know what their income is going to be, and that seems to me a much fairer way of doing it, so you then have some security of what's going on. And there are some employers that do that in towns with a large student population, with students, and it works quite well. What's your reaction? I'm not going to stop you working, it's okay.
OK, I'm sorry, I'm sorry. Or the Russian report that Jeremy Corbyn read out to us the other day. OK.

I'm going to be very nice with Ashani. My life may be in your hands one day, but thank you for studying medicine and do enjoy it. I mean, I have a privilege of speaking for Wales here this evening, but hopefully what I say will have relevance for people wherever they are in the UK. I think one thing you want is an assurance about what kind of NHS we will have in future and my party is absolutely clear. The NHS should and must remain free at the point of need. Another key principle for me and Plaid Cymru is that no profit should be taken out of the NHS. We will not consider ever privatising the NHS, something that of course the Conservatives want to do but remarkably Labour are happy to see privatisation within the NHS now too. But, you know, we in Wales have had a double whammy. We've had the impact of the cuts, of austerity that absolutely comes down to hitting public services, education and health and everything, plus a very badly managed NHS by Labour.

Nicola Sturgeon. This election is a chance to change the Westminster system so that it serves you better. The SNP will always stand up for Scotland's best interests. We will make Scotland's voice heard. But I know that it's not just people in Scotland who feel let down by Westminster politics. That's why my message to people watching in England, Wales and Northern Ireland is one of friendship. I won't pretend I don't want Scotland to be independent. I do. But as long as Scotland remains part of the Westminster system, the SNP will seek to work with others of like mind across the UK to deliver positive change. Like many of you, we want an alternative to the pain of austerity. an end to the bedroom tax, a halt to the privatisation of the NHS. And we believe the scarce resources of our country should be invested in the future of our children, not in new nuclear weapons. A vote for the SNP is a vote to make Scotland's voice heard, but ours will also be a voice for new, better and progressive politics at Westminster for all of us. David Cameron.

I urge you to read it. I think it is a serious and realistic document that addresses the issues that many people in this country face. And we've been brave enough to put it out there with all the policies that are in it. How we deal with the school funding crisis How we make sure preschool all children, 2 to 4, get 30 hours childcare per week or opportunities for preschool, play and education. How we bring back the educational maintenance allowance so that youngsters who would be deterred from staying on at school or going on to get A-levels or other qualifications and that those who can get into university won't end up with debts of 50 or 60,000 at the end of it. This is nothing unusual in many other countries in the world. I think it's time for us to invest in our future. The other crisis I may mention, David, is the question of health, the question of mental health, and the question of social care. If we don't address it, what happens? What happens is more people suffer. What happens is more women usually give up work to care for those who cannot be cared for because we're not putting enough money into it. And those with a mental health crisis suffer alone and don't get the help and support that they want. We have to respect the needs of people, and, frankly, challenge all of us to say, if we want to live in a society that genuinely cares for all, we've got to be prepared to deal with issues of inequality and pay for it. And I'm prepared to do that.

Yes, absolutely. In fact, the more I look back on it, the more proud I am that I think it was a difficult decision. I think it was a brave decision for the Liberal Democrats. It's come at short-term political cost. But I'm absolutely clear in my mind that if you think back to 2010, we could have been Greece. We could have been Greece. Our deficit was almost as big as Greece's. Our banking crisis was a whole lot worse. And I certainly wouldn't have wanted on my conscience higher interest rates, higher unemployment, higher youth unemployment, which I'm absolutely sure would have happened if we hadn't stepped up to the plate to create a stable government, without which an economic recovery is not possible. And my great concern at the moment is that having got this far over five years and millions of people across the country having made huge sacrifices to get us this far after that terrible heart attack in our economy back in 2008, that we undo it all by lurching off to the right with ideological hardline cuts or lurching off to the left borrowing pots of money that we can't afford. I think we need to remain anchored in the centre ground so that we can finish the job of balancing the books but do it fairly.

No, it's not. Have you worked out the geographical effect of this, Mr. Kennedy? It's all detailed on the website. Can you give me the answer? I have the south-east income figure for you, which is the one you can base it on. The average salary in the south-east is an awful lot lower than people imagine. It's £27,000 a year. The woman here.

Fundamentally, there's a very clear choice and a very clear dividing line. I think you've got to change the eligibility criteria in the welfare system. You've got to make it clear that people who can work should work. That means looking at all the gateways we have and actually being more rigorous about asking people to provide medical information when they're making these claims, reforming the sick note process, being stricter about saying to people who don't accept a job after 12 months that that's not OK. Those are all the practical things that I've set out. I think that's fair. Now, Keir Starmer can stand here tonight and say what he wants. When I made that speech, and you can all go and read it and see what they said, they opposed every single measure. Just two weeks ago, they were asked about it. They said, we oppose all those measures. So it's one thing to stand here and say all these things. You should at least be upfront with people. You don't think my approach is the right one. You are going to have higher welfare under you, and that's why you can't deliver tax cuts the way that I can. And people should not surrender to this.

Natalie Bennett. David Cameron, since you're talking numbers, let's look at the £6 billion that last year went to the private health firms. Now Leanne is absolutely right, this privatisation is causing huge damage. And what we're doing is actually racing towards the American healthcare system system that uses twice the percentage of GDP that we use on health care for actual worse outcomes. And just one more point that I really want to pick up is we also need to think about the nature of our society and if we're going to take pressure off the NHS we need a healthier society, deal with air pollution, encourage walking and cycling, take the stress off people.

Well, I wish I'd seen through the lies. I've said sorry for not seeing through those lies. There were many ministers of all political parties during this 20-year period who didn't see those lies. The courts didn't. And here's the issue, because I've obviously reflected on this, because it's such a scandal. What I've come to the conclusion is our system of government, whether you're a minister or you're in the courts, it depends on people telling the truth. And in a number of scandals, the contaminated blood scandal, The Hillsborough scandal, the Horizon scandal. We've seen people not telling the truth and there's been a conspiracy.

..or seven-figure bonuses, if you get regular hours, you know, and you've got some security if you're going out to work and doing the right thing for the country, if you're having an NHS that you can rely on, that's better for the whole country. I think we all benefit from that.

Yeah, look, migration is at record levels at the moment under this government. They've completely lost control. We need to get that number down. We need to get it significantly down. I'm not going to put an arbitrary figure on it, because every single politician who's put a number on it has never met that number. But we need to get it down. We need to get it down. You're asking, is that a reason not to do it? We want to get it down significantly. It needs to be balanced immigration so that it works for our economy and works for our country. We need to get it down. But if we're going to do that, we need to understand what the problem is. And the problem at the moment is we haven't got the skills that we need in this country. So many, many people are coming on work visas. So we need a skills strategy to ensure that we have the skills in this country to ensure that the workforce is available here for the jobs that we need doing. One specific example is social care. Many people coming here are coming into social care and working hard in social care, by the way. But that's because we haven't got a plan for the staff within social care. They're all fragmented. My sister's a care worker. They're on fragmented contracts, poor terms and conditions, and many of them leave. Most of them leaving social care go to work in the NHS, because they've got a better framework for pay and poor progression. So we've got a problem we need to fix here, and we would have a fair pay agreement for all the workers.

First of all, let me say, I don't want anyone to have to rely on a food bank in our country. It's important that they're there, but I don't want anyone to have to rely on them. The most important thing we can do is to get more people into work. That's the best route out of poverty. Now, two million more jobs is more than the rest of Europe put together. But have we finished the job? Someone said zero-hours contract. One in 50 jobs is a zero-hours contract. Two-thirds of the jobs we've created are full-time jobs. Now, if you're saying the job isn't finished, I absolutely agree, and that is why I'm so passionate about completing the job. And, madam, you asked, what country do you want to pass on to our children? I don't want us to go on borrowing year after year, racking up debts that we then ask our children to pay because we didn't have the courage to pay them off ourselves. And this goes to the heart of the election campaign because Ed Miliband will stand here in a few minutes time. He is saying go on with the budget deficit forever. I'm saying once the economy starts to grow you should be putting money aside for a rainy day. Otherwise we will burden our children with too much debt and that's not right. What was that?

We have to move away from that. The reason you can't do it immediately without reducing demand is that you'd make yourself more reliant on imports, which would increase your carbon emissions. But the transition away from fossil fuels is important and is underway.

Look at the Muslim ban that Trump brought in. Look at the caging of migrant children. Look at the way he threatens war. And of course, he wants to conspire with Boris Johnson and the leader of the Brexit Party in order to get his fat cat friends to have access to our National Health Service. That's not true, is it? are what's happening in the United States of America under Donald Trump, then they should fear that that could happen here. The only way to stop the UK becoming a new version of Donald Trump's United States of America is to ensure that Boris Johnson does not become Prime Minister at the end of this general election process on the 12th of December.

ensure that we've got good, well-paid skilled jobs here for you and other young people who I do think have been let down in recent years of government, and make sure that we build the houses necessary to ensure that the dream of home ownership lives again. Because at the moment, it's being killed under this government. The average age for young people to get a house is in their 30s. now. I think it should be in the 20s, and if we have five more years of this Government it will go up to the 40s. But we've got to build more houses, 1.5 million houses across the country. That means tough decisions about planning and where those houses are going to be, but we also need to deal with the very high rents that young people are often paying. So many say to me, I'm paying more in rent than I've actually paid on a mortgage. huge proportion, but I can't save for the mortgage deposit that I need. And that's why we will have a low mortgage deposit scheme in place to allow young people, first-time buyers, to get on the housing ladder. But we've got to build the houses, 1.5 million houses. The Prime Minister's taken down the targets for house building. They're going off a cliff, sinking, and that is taking down with it your dreams and the dreams of young people across the country who can't get the job that they want. Very often, if they've been to college or university, are returning back home. We have to give them the future that they deserve. That means that we have to go out with our manifesto for growth, for wealth creation, and seize the opportunities of the future. Other countries are doing this. I think we should be in the race for the jobs for the future. I think we could win that race. But also, roll up our sleeves, start building the houses that are necessary to make sure that you can afford to buy a house in your 20s, not as it currently is in your 30s. That's the tragedy of the last 10 years. And it'll simply go up if we have five more years of the current government.

We have not made as much progress as we would have liked, as I would have liked. But what I can tell you is we are putting record investment into the NHS. We've got more doctors and nurses working in it than we ever have and we're training more and we're doing different things, making sure that we can diagnose people's cancers earlier by doing scans closer to their homes. allowing you to do more things at your pharmacy to get the treatment that you need quicker. And those things are starting to make a difference. And what we've seen over the past few months is waiting lists now are starting to come down. They've just gone up. over the past few months, over the past five months, they've gone down by around 200,000.

And I'm answering that question. If, if I am persuaded that there are areas where the private sector can deliver a better service for people in this country than the state can, I'm in favour of it. I'm not, I'm not wedded to any ideology.

for larger businesses and what the Labour government is proposing. don't look at the problems throughout our school and education system and invest in it properly. Where are the skilled workers going to come from tomorrow? Where are going to be the consumers of tomorrow? I think it's time that we looked at inequality in our society and used a public investment in order to improve services and give real chances to everybody. Poverty is a waste. People who can't get the education they want and the qualifications they want, we all lose. It's a question of whether the community gets together to support everybody or we just let the rich get richer and the rest suffer.

Paul Nussel. Well, it's quite clear that the war in Iraq and the bombing of Libya was fundamentally wrong. Because all we were doing is we were removing, yes, evil dictators. But it was quite clear that what was going to come next was going to be even worse. But that isn't, our foreign policy isn't an excuse for what went on in Manchester. And what politicians need to do is at least have the courage to name what it is. It's Islamist extremists. Sorry, OK? It's Islamist extremists. but nobody seems to have the courage to say what it is. How do we solve it? Well we put 20,000 police officers back on the beat, 4,000 more on the border force, we tighten our borders, 7,000 new prison officers because radicalisation is rife within our prisons and I must say I can't believe that we've allowed 350 jihadis to return to our country from Libya and Syria. I'm sorry, if you go out and fight or support Islamic State, you should have your passport revoked and you should never be allowed back into this country. Now, what we've also got to do is look at radicalisation within our own mosques. And I will say, Amber, I think you need to look at Saudi and Qatari funding of mosques in this country. And finally, we need to get the Muslim community itself to sign up to the Prevent programme. Only one out of eight referrals to Prevent come from within the Muslim community.

that whoever becomes Prime Minister improves the nature of debate in this country. The debate in Parliament before Parliament was dissolved was vicious and horrible and some terrible remarks were made particularly to and about women MPs. I asked the Speaker to convene a meeting of all party leaders, Mr Johnson sent a representative there and we agreed that this was unacceptable conduct and I absolutely agree with that. Thank you. Will you both make a pledge to improve things?

Yeah, well, absolutely. I mean, I would say to anybody watching, you know, it's an ideology, it's an idea. It's nothing to do with the colour of your skin, and everyone needs to realise that. You have Hindus and Sikhs as well, same colour skin, but completely different religion, different ideology. We need to deal with Islamism, and we've got to be honest about that.

that's not true. We recognise that we need a transition from oil and gas and that's what the Green Prosperity Plan is all about and that's why we put the levy on oil and gas companies to then have Great British Energy which will give us the jobs for the future, thousands of jobs across the UK including in Scotland where Great British Energy's headquarters will be based. I'm going to now turn to Daisy Cooper.

Either Keir Starmer or I will be your Prime Minister, and a vote for anyone else makes it more likely that it will be him. Agree or disagree with me, you know what I stand for. I'll always have your back, as I did with Furlough. But with Keir Starmer, apart from higher taxes, you don't know what you'd get, and neither does he. And we've heard a lot about change from him, but change to what? Keir Starmer is asking you to hand him a blank cheque when he hasn't said what he'll buy with it or how much it's going to cost you. In uncertain times, we simply cannot afford an uncertain prime minister. If you don't know what you're going to get with Labour, Don't vote for it. On the 4th of July, choose lower immigration, lower taxes and protected pensions. Choose a secure future with the Conservatives.

Well, Sandy, I'm very happy to talk afterwards about what we can do to help your specific case, but I do think that universal... I know that some people want to scrap universal credit without putting anything in its place. It has worked in the sense that it has helped many a couple of hundred thousand people at least into work. And one of the features of the economy at the moment, whatever the problems we have, is that we have more people in employment than ever before. We have more women in the workforce than ever before. And actually incomes are starting to grow faster than any time in the last 12 years. And that is what we want to focus on.

Also, everything produced has to be measured against the strike price that could be obtained at the time of its production. Everyone has agreed that wind power has become much, much cheaper. There was a time when it was more expensive. It isn't so. Likewise, solar has also reduced in price. So actually, the future is in sustainable green energy spaces.

Harry, as I said, I share absolutely in people's... I'm as angry as anybody when I learn about these things. Of course I am. I think the voters are probably angrier. Yeah, well, no, I'm incredibly angry about this. And the right thing to do, and, again, you talked about that letter, to do things properly is to get to the bottom of what happened, to investigate things thoroughly. Now, we have to do that separately to the Gambling Commission, who don't report to me, I don't have the details of their investigation. We have to do that sensitively and carefully so that we don't compromise the integrity of a police and other investigations. But let me be clear, if we come across findings or information that warrants it, we will not hesitate to act. I've been crystal clear that I will hold people to account. Whoever they are. I will kick any... Whoever they are. If anyone has not upheld the standards that I expect, if they have broken the rules, they should face the full consequences of the law and they will be booted out of the Conservative Party. I couldn't be clearer about that.

Why did you call the election early? I called the election because, going back to the second question, my number one job when I became your Prime Minister was make sure we had economic stability. When we had that, when inflation was effectively back to normal as it was, the economy was growing healthily, wages were rising, I thought that was the right moment for us to consider our future. You have a choice. You and everyone else has a choice. Do we build on the progress we've made? I want to keep cutting your taxes. The alternative, if Keir Starmer is your Prime Minister, is that your taxes are going to go up. That is the choice for everyone at this election and I'm going to fight very hard to make sure that I can continue the work that we've started, continue delivering financial security, continue cutting your taxes. Are you glad you called the election when you did? How do you think it's going? It was the right moment to call the election, for the reasons that I outlined. And Fiona, I am. Look, of course these things are difficult. But to actually answer the first question over there, when I was standing against Liz Truss in that leadership election, I had the same questions from everybody. You're behind. Why are you bothering? Isn't this all... You know, this is all very difficult. I said, no, well, I'm fighting for what I believe is right. Even though people didn't want to hear it at the time, I kept going, I kept saying what I thought was right for this country, I kept going to the end, and you know what? I was proved right then. And that's why you can trust me now when I say that what Keir Starmer is promising you is the same fantasy that Liz trusted, and it's just going to mean your taxes are going to go up, and that matters to me because I don't want that to happen, and that's why we're having this election, and that's what I'm going to keep fighting for.

I have to say, this is pure Bermuda Triangle stuff. We have heard it time and time again from the Labour Party during this election campaign. We will be hearing about little green men next. I believe very passionately in a... I use the NHS. I love it. It's one of the most incredible things about this country. It is admired around the world. The central idea that if any of us get sick, all of us take care of them. It's a fantastic thing. Now, we believe in an NHS free at the point of use, and under no circumstances will we sell it off to anybody in any kind of trade deal.

In fact, when the lady first spoke to me, she talked about learning disabilities and mental health, and she'd actually raised mental health herself. But I recognise that the particular issue that she had was about learning disabilities. But look, both of these are areas where we need to ensure that we've got the ability for people's needs to be identified at as early a stage as possible and then the support goes in. Obviously all the evidence is the earlier you can get that support to somebody, the earlier you can identify an issue, then the better it is for that individual but also the better it will be for the rest of their lives. That's why on the mental health front, as I say, I'm so keen to ensure that we get more support in terms of training in schools. There are other things I want to do as well, but including in the workplace, actually, because too often I think there is discrimination in relation to mental health in the workplace. So I want to – I'm going to bring in a new Mental Health Act, scrap the old Mental Health Act, but also change the equalities legislation so that discrimination on mental health will not be possible in the workplace.

Where are you, Robert? I can hear your voice. Ah, there you are, right at the back. Sorry, behind the camera. Now I can see you. Robert, I think we need to just be open with you, straight with you. and we've sought to do that. We have specified, and we're the only party in politics now, in our manifesto, look at the back of our manifesto, which says, here are the figures, this is the way that we would find cuts and savings of £15 billion. How would we do that? By things like removing tax credits for the top 20% of recipients of tax credits, ending the child trust fund, which gives £250 to all 18-year-olds. I'd love to give everybody £250, but I just don't think we can afford it right now. Putting a cap of £400 on any pay increases in the public sector for the next two years. But also some long-term choices. I'm the only leader here who's saying very clearly, I don't think we can either justify or afford the like-for-like replacement of the Cold War nuclear missile system, the Trident missile system, over the next 25 years. It'll cost you, all of us, £100 billion. We can't afford it.

But Nick, you're right. There is no British-only solution, and David's got to face up to that. But you've also got to face up to the fact that the cooperation that we have achieved, a hundred countries have now signed carbon emission reduction plans. We're trying to persuade China and America to do so. We need America on our side. Your anti-Americanism will not help us. We need to work with all the different continents to get a climate change agreement.

and I persuaded the Americans to be part of a G20 that dealt with the banking crisis, and I'm still pushing the Americans to take action on climate change as well. But David, I mean, your anti-Europeanism becomes more and more obvious as this debate goes on. It's the big society at home, but it's the little Britain abroad. I think you've got to rethink your policies.

Well, James, you asked who's going to pay. So I can tell you under our plans, it won't be our children, because debt under a Conservative government will be lower at the end of the Parliament. Under Labour's plans, it will be higher. And it also won't be the lowest paid. Our plans are to cut taxes for the lowest paid through cutting national insurance. We now know from the IFS and everyone else that Labour's plans mean tax rises for millions of married couples up and down the country, for millionaires of pensioners on modest incomes, and for millions of small businesses. It's not rich people and companies that will just pay the higher taxes under Labour, it's everyone.

You have to run a prison system and a judicial system in a proper way. You have to have a prison system that works. One day, pretty well all prisoners are going to come out at some point. But if you don't run a prison system that includes rehabilitation, and you don't have a proper assessment of people before they're given any form of early release and a proper review of those cases, then there's obviously a danger to all of us. And so what happened on the streets of London was utterly appalling. And I was very moved by what Jack Merritt's father said about what his son was trying to do, that he wanted a society where you did address the huge problems where somebody committed awful acts like that. Yes, of course, you must imprison them. Yes, of course, you must try and rehabilitate them if you can. But there has to be a monitoring process to achieve that. We need the security on our streets. Security doesn't come on the cheap. And if you cut the number of police officers, cut the number of PCSOs, privatise the probation service, underfund all the other aspects of the criminal justice system, then we all pay a price with our own security.

Well, what we're doing here is we're putting forward a proposition of fairness, in terms of what you contribute locally. At the moment, everybody knows it is not fair because it's not related to your ability to pay. Now that's an argument of the principle we've got to win in the course of this election.

Well, I hope very much that people will vote for a one-nation, conservative government and get Brexit done. And I think, actually, if you look at our agenda, it's positive, it's progressive, it will unite the country, involve massive investment in public services, and take us forward.

Five years ago, we inherited a situation where the previous Labour government had wasted £250 million, you can't deny this, Ed Miliband, on sweetheart deals with private sector contractors in the NHS, which didn't help a single NHS patient. We outlawed that use of public money for private sector contracts which were only based on price rather than quality. And as I said earlier, 4% under Labour was already devoted It's only 6% now. It is simply not the case to allege that privatisation has happened in an unbridled fashion since this government came into power.

I've said it's unlikely to happen. I've said that the lot for the British people... That's not a definite no. No, it isn't. No, it isn't, because of the criteria I've set. But I'm making no bones about it, Kate. It's very unlikely to happen. OK. Because I've said that if there's a transfer of powers, it will happen, but that doesn't seem likely at the moment.

I think not for the first time tonight, David Cameron's going to want to talk about the past, but he doesn't want to talk about the future. But, David, you just said that you were tackling tax avoidance. Let's look at the reality on this. You haven't acted on the tax havens. You haven't acted on the hedge funds. We've shown how you can raise over a billion pounds as part of our £2.5 billion Time to Care Fund for the NHS. It'll hire 20,000 more nurses, 8,000 more doctors, 5,000 more care workers and 3,000 more midwives. You have to ask yourself at home, why won't David Cameron act on those hedge funds? They fund his party, he won't act. You need leadership that will stand up and act to tackle taxable... David Cameron... Once again, he's wrong.

I think that's, well, one, it's untrue. But secondly, it's cheap because, actually, Boris Johnson has very serious questions to answer about his and his party's relationship, financial and otherwise, with Russian oligarchs.

I believe the way things are is not the way things have to be. Now, you're going to be told tonight by these two that the only choice you can make is between two old parties who've been running things for years. I'm here to persuade you that there is an alternative. I think we have a fantastic opportunity to do things differently for once. And if we do things differently, we can create the fair society, the fair country we all want. A fair tax system. Better schools. An economy no longer held hostage by greedy bankers. Decent, open politics. Those are the changes I believe in. I really wouldn't be standing here tonight if I didn't think they were all possible. So don't let anyone tell you that the only choice is old politics. We can do something new. We can do something different this time. That's what I'm about. That's what the Liberal Democrats offer.
We would stop the routine use of stop-and-search. If there is a reasonable suspicion...

Would you say to them, please try and limit the amount that you eat to a certain gram a month? We would give people the information for them to make healthy choices. And actually, all of us have to take responsibility for our actions in that. But if we're giving people an informed choice, then that helps them make those choices. But in terms of state, and we come back to that, and Nigel's point around China and everything else, if we create a million green jobs, and we create solar and wind farm and the technologies around that. We can export that. And that's the industrial revolution, the million green jobs we want to create.

There will be a cap, yes. What we set out in the manifesto is the principles of our position. I then saw a lot of scaremongering about that. I heard a lot of scaremongering about it. and recognise that people would be worried as a result of that. 100,000? 500,000? We clarified that there will be a cap. But what we will do, Faisal, is not just put a figure on it. What we will do is publish a green paper, a consultation document. We'll listen to people. We'll listen to voters. We'll listen to charities and organisations working with older people. We'll take people's views on where that cap should be. And I think that's the fairer way to do it. OK. Amanda.

The reality is... So you're wrong on two counts. So first of all, having a 7% deficit while we're part of the United Kingdom is hardly a great advert for staying within the United Kingdom. We're not independent. If we had the full powers of economic fiscal levers, of course, we could make a difference. Hold on, I didn't interrupt you. The second point is incredibly important as well. Actually, you're wrong about that being a barrier to joining the EU. Croatia, Poland, Hungary all had a deficit higher than 3% and joined the European Union. Please, I absolutely respect that on this panel and in this audience there will be differences of opinion on independence but please do not patronise Scottish people by saying that you are far too poor you know every other northern European country can be independent but somehow Scots are going to be too poor to be independent I think it's really important that there's an honesty about this

I want what's best for Scotland and the alternative to not having a choice over our own future is that we have to put up with Brexit regardless of how damaging that is and that could be narrowed horizons for our young people and lost jobs, tens of thousands of them.

Well, I do need to answer that. That's not the start, and that's not the end. I do need to answer that, because he put that figure out there. Very briefly. Well, what's happened here is it's a classic. They put in pretend Labour policies to the Treasury, and then they get a false readout. So I'll just tell you, what they've put in for this analysis is a mental health policy that isn't the Labour Party's policy. He's put in one of his own policies. He's asked the civil service to cost it, and they've come up with this figure of £2,000. The expert... They are an independent civil service. All right, I think... He's put it twice. I do need to deal with it.
That was later in the year. You didn't want the amendments. Parliament voted for it. But you had a deal.

My kind of socialism is one where you don't leave people behind. You don't ignore the fact that four million of our children are living in poverty. You don't ignore the fact that there are thousands of people sleeping on the streets of this country, the fifth richest country in the world. The inequality has got worse. 150 billionaires in Britain, more people poor than ever. Jeremy Corbyn, thank you.

Brexit done right is an amazing opportunity. Let's go for it with ambition, with energy. Lower prices, higher wages, training our own people, building roads and rail projects all over the country. But make your vote count to get Brexit and stop Corbyn bankrupting the country. Only the Brexit Party can take on Labour in seats Tories have not won in decades. Together, let's change politics for good.

Well, we have a dedicated visa for social care because we know how important it is, and we need to make sure that we do get the right workers here. That's why, unlike almost every other profession that you can think of, there's a dedicated visa for social care workers.

The national insurance increase, which Gordon Brown has said is definitely coming in, that will take £200 million out of the NHS. He is not replacing that money, so he would have less to spend on cancer drugs. I have a man in my constituency called Clive Stone, who had kidney cancer, who came to see me with seven others. Tragically, two of them have died because they couldn't get the drug, Sutent, that they wanted, that was on the market, that people knew was a good drug. That's a scandal in our country today. So stop the national insurance rise, use that money for the cancer drugs, and help people so our outcomes can be amongst the best in Europe rather than, sadly, amongst some of the worst.

Well, I don't believe it is wasted. Look, I visit schools and hospitals the whole time. I go in there, I see investment in schools, I see it in new school buildings, in computers, in additional teachers. I go into the health services. There's barely a health service site you visit without new buildings being put up, new wards, scanners, extra nurses. I don't I don't misunderstand this at all. There are still big problems that we need to deal with. But I think if you look at our National Health Service today, we can be proud of it again. It's improvements happening in the National Health Service. If you go into the schools, you can see the extra investment in there. That's on the basis of a strong economy, and that's why I want to keep that investment going in. And I don't accept the Conservative charge it's all wasted or it's all going on bureaucrats. Actually, it's going to give our kids a better education and people a better deal on health. OK.

Karen, my first job in government was being local government minister, so I was responsible for councils, and they do an extraordinary job. And much as people focus on national politics, actually, most politics is local. It's what's happening in your street, your neighborhood, your community. And the services that you mentioned are so important, particularly those that concern families and children.
