One of the things that I do want to do is actually roll out family hubs. as we've been doing over the past few years, which are special places where whole families can come and get the advice and support they need, particularly the most vulnerable families. Again, in terms of funding councils, we've recently announced just over half a billion pounds of extra funding for councils, particularly for social care, which is where some of the big pressures are, and children's services, which you alluded to. And we'll continue to work with councils to provide them the support they need. But you mentioned Nottingham, right? And that is a glimpse of the future. Nottingham City Council bankrupt under Labour. Birmingham City Council, the largest council in the country, in fact the largest in Europe, not just bankrupt but as a result everyone in Birmingham is now paying a 20% higher council tax bill. And that is a snapshot of what will happen if Keir Starmer is in power across the country. As Labour always do, they run out of everyone's money, they bankrupt the economy in 2010, they bankrupted Birmingham, they bankrupted Nottingham, and that's why everyone in Birmingham is paying a 20% higher council tax. So that is just an appetiser of the higher taxes that are going to come under a future Labour government. Do not surrender your local council and our finances to them.
That's one tenth of what we're putting forward. It's one tenth.

OK, so the blaming of migrants is a cover-up. I'm not blaming migrants at all. Please get it right. The blaming of migrants is a cover-up for policy failure. We've had successive governments that have not invested. migrants. Have not invested in the NHS, have not invested in our public services, have given us the lie of austerity and in return they said actually it's the migrants that are to blame for our housing crisis, it's the migrants to blame for the pressure on social services. That is a lie and we need to call that out as much as we need to call out the racism.

No, if you vote for the Conservatives at this election, you'll get a government that will take action on the challenges that face the country and on the things that really matter to people and I'll give you one example - crime. Let me give you another example - clean hospitals. Five thousand people a year die in our country from hospital acquired infections - as many people as die on Britain's roads. We have an action plan, which will deal with that problem, which would bring it under control. There's no reason why in this country of ours, we should have that problem worse than it is in almost any other country in Europe.

Well, as I've said, health and social care need to be integrated and working together. The social care side of things has been disadvantaged compared to health. Money has gone into health in most areas, whereas local authorities have seen their budgets cut quite severely, and of course social care is provided through local authority budgets. So stop the squabbling in terms of the budgets, make those budgets and departments all come in under one roof and then you can put the patients at the heart of the system instead of the bureaucracy.
Yes, they will, and we think that nobody should pay for their cost of their social care by selling their home, and everybody should have dignity and security in their old age.

They can't hear. On the specific question on the housing. So I'm pleased in the Scottish Government we have responsibility for housing. We have built 87,000 affordable homes, the vast majority on social rent and we're on target to meet our 50,000 home target by 2021. But one of the best things that we did was abolish the right to buy when it came to council houses. Because when the Conservatives sold off council houses, when Labour sold off council houses, you want to know how many they replaced? They replaced one in five.

I think, as I say, it's how do we make sure that the youngsters of today don't become the hardened criminals of tomorrow. It's that conveyor belt from nuisance at the beginning, anti-social behaviour in our communities, you know, yobs on the street corner who then become the hardened criminals of tomorrow. And I think what makes me so angry is that Again, it's a bit like the immigration debate. So much tough talk from different governments of different parties for so long and actually has turned our prisons into overcrowded colleges of crime. I mean, do you know that young men going into prison now on short-term prison sentences now come out and nine out of ten of them re-offend? So they're actually reproducing more crime than actually cutting it. And what I've seen in my city of Sheffield is that you get these youngsters Not when they've done serious crimes, but when they're first starting to get into trouble. To face their victims. Explain why they've done what they've done to their victims. Apologise for what they've done. Make up for what they've done in the community. Cleaning up parks and streets. It has a dramatic effect on their behaviour. I want to change people's behaviour before they become the criminals of tomorrow.

Well, and I think we should, yes is the simple answer, because we need to have a system that is fair to everybody. We need to think about our Commonwealth partners and friends, for example, not just people from the EU.

Absolutely not and there isn't people lying idle at the moment. There's a lot of people not being given the skills and that's why I'll create a national education service and we can put people online to have those construction jobs for the future and I make no apologies for a hundred thousand council homes that we need or the making sure that the contracts for tenancy is a minimum of three years and that we'd have rent controls in line with inflation. So you think you can do that next year? I think we can start putting that online straight away, yes we can.

It's going to be a UK policy and it will mean that the money is made available there by increasing tax, not for the first 95% of the population, but the other 5% to ensure the funds are there. Because I am fed up with the idea that so many children growing up in poorer households and poorer communities don't get the same chances as others to get to university and get on in life. They lose and we all lose because we lose those skilled, brilliant people that want to contribute to our community.

I think there's a very clear choice at this election when it comes to keeping us safe. He's talking about the tone of the conversation. We're trying to increase use of stop and search. We're investing billions in our criminal justice system, and we want tougher sentences. Jeremy Corbyn has opposed pretty much every single piece of anti-terror legislation that's come to Parliament. They oppose the use of stop and search. And John McDonnell signed a letter saying, and I quote, disband MI5, disarm the police. There is a very clear choice about who's going to keep us safe.

We should absolutely adhere to the Paris climate change agreement. And we should urge the American people to press their government, their Senate, and their House, and their president to adhere to it as well. In this country, we are determined to achieve 60% of renewable energy by 2030. We're also determined to reduce emissions. But it's not just about climate change emissions. It's also about our attitude towards the environment, levels of air quality, and air pollution in our cities, which are not being addressed by this government. And also, when we did have a growing and thriving solar power industry in this country, what did they do? cut the feed-in tariffs, which destroyed and damaged a lot of that industry.

I mean, I was 10 years old during the poll tax, Angela. I don't really think I can take responsibility for that. The bedroom tax, though, you can. The bedroom tax. Yeah, and austerity. But you can't accuse me of not being honest about where we stand on Brexit.

I think one of the biggest things we must do as we try, and I think it's right to try and forge a consensus because this is a long-term issue we must deal with, is to try and give the carer and those they care for more power and control and influence over their lives. Form an individual budget for each one, make sure that if they want to they can take that as a direct payment, that they can make decisions about the sort of respite they need. We tried to do this with my son and when you try and get a direct payment system, so you're in charge of the money and you can try and get some help, it's unbelievably complicated. You've got to set up a separate bank account, you've got to read about four lever arch files and, you know, I found it testing enough and what someone who's recently had to start caring, who's under huge pressure, who may be getting ill because of what they're doing, to try and get direct payments, let's make it easier because we ought to be trusting people to do this.

It's all very European Union, isn't it? If you don't get the answer you want in the referendum, you have another one to get the answer that you do want. They did it in Ireland, they did it in France, they did it in Holland. It's what they do. The bottom line is this, the people have voted, they voted to leave the EU, they voted to leave the single market, and Tim can cry about it all he wants. You know, that's just a fact. That's democracy, Tim. You know, but the bottom line here is the single market isn't the be-all and end-all. If we're members of the single market, we can't sign our own trade deals. And there's a big world out there. There's the Anglosphere. There's the Commonwealth, which has over 2 billion people in it. This is where our future lies, not tied within the sclerotic EU bloc. Do you really think people vote to lose jobs?

I know many parents who were really very scared about the safety of their children. It's not only an issue here in London but particularly in the home counties on county lines there's a big issue with knives as well. I think we need to change the model of policing and we need to get back to good old-fashioned community policing. where the police know their communities, where the communities know their police, where community groups, where faith groups, where local families all work together and they get their intelligence from the ground up. That's been all but wiped out in parts of our country. There's been cuts of 4,000, 4,500 PCSOs as well. And let me say about stop and search in particular. Stop and search can be a useful tool in some circumstances, but suspicionless stop and search, which has been used to target particular groups of people, is not valid and should not be used. So where there is a suspicion of somebody, it can be used, but it has to be used in a targeted way based on evidence, not on profiling.

Well, the NHS budget in Scotland has increased by £3 billion since the SNP took office. It will go up by £400 million next year. You know, look, I think, well, one of the things we've learned is that there's not anything that Nigel Farage won't blame on foreigners. Actually, the pressures on our health service, many of them are things that we should celebrate. People are living longer. We've got new treatments, new technologies. How do we deal with that to protect it for future generations. First, we've got to reform. As of yesterday in Scotland, health and social care services are integrated. And, yes, we've got to invest. I think it's right to accept the Stevens report, but Nick Clegg's £8 billion figure is an England-only figure. That would be £9.5 billion for the whole UK. And if you follow the plan I'm putting forward of modest, fiscally responsible spending increases, we can invest more in the health service, but we can do that without cutting welfare and social services.

Well of course we need an energy balance and we need nuclear, we need renewables, we need a full range and it's a great opportunity actually for our country to do these things but I think one of the biggest opportunities we've got is actually with our own homes and we have a proposal called the Green Deal where we want to say to everyone in this country that you could spend up to £6,500 on your home to insulate it and better protect it. And then you can see your energy bills come down. And we will have companies, and Marks and Spencers and others have expressed an interest, to come and carry out that work and to pay for it and to share with you the reduction in the bills. I think that would show people that going green actually can save you money. It can actually get Britain working again. It can cut carbon emissions. It can cut fuel poverty. For all those reasons, I'd want it to be a really big part of the first Queen's speech. if I'm elected as your Prime Minister.

Well, it's a very important question. Stephen gave a resume of what the problems were, but without any of the solutions. We need to grow our economy. We haven't had growth in our economy. The Tories have taxed us the highest burden in 70 years. We also need to build the houses that people need. The Tories haven't built those houses. That's why we've said we'd build 1.5 million homes, including social housing that we desperately need. We need a new deal for working people, which I'm incredibly proud of, to make sure that people have secure, good-paid jobs. And we need great British energy to bring down energy bills so we're not reliant on people like Putin for our energy needs, which has meant people's bills have gone sky-high.

I mean, for the NHS, for example, I think job number one is to put it back on its feet, get it working again, get those waiting lists down. But we are ambitious about actually changing the NHS. so that it's fit for the next 75, 76 years, for example. That's a big job. The Labour Party, Labour government set up the NHS. That's one of the proudest things we've ever done. I don't want forever to be looking back. I want to make sure that for future generations, they can say that the 2024 government got to grips with making sure the NHS was fit for the future.

The answer is in your question, of course, and it's about stopping the demonisation. Of course, the Conservatives have made a right hash of processing asylum applications, and people caught up in that backlog are the people who are then demonised. We have to remember that the UK has international obligations under the 1951 Refugees Act to play its part as a global player. And you know what? The UK's standing on the international stage has been damaged so much by the actions of this Conservative government in undermining those obligations. We have to change the language. My daughter worked for a youth organization welcoming Afghan refugees to Wales. It enriched her life so much. I'm so proud of her seeing those refugees settle in our communities, learning English, learning Welsh. That's the nature of sanctuary principles that are important to us. And they're just our international obligations that we should be proud of stepping up to the mark to deliver on.

The only reason I came into politics was because I saw what was happening in my local community. I've got the good fortune of being the member of parliament for the people I grew up with and the people I went to school with. The reason I want to be in politics is to create jobs. When it comes to immigration, I want to see a situation where we increase the number of jobs that people trained in Britain can take, as we lower the numbers of people coming into this country. That's why we have banned unskilled workers from outside Europe from coming into Britain. That's also why we're cutting the numbers of semi-skilled and skilled workers who can come in. That's why we have a list of occupations we now want to reserve for people in Britain and not for people coming from abroad. But what we are doing at the moment is training up people so that in the next few years, as we move forward out of this recession, the jobs will go to people trained in Britain, who have the skills in Britain, and that goes right across from chefs and care assistants to nurses and of course teachers. That's where we want to be by training people up for the future.

I don't know better than anybody. I listen to people and amend, if necessary, the rules and processes. I'm the leader that introduced an investigation process, introduced a disciplinary process, introduced a process of education, introduced a process for dealing with egregious cases. And, yes, we have suspended or expelled members where we have found them guilty of anti-Semitic behaviour, as, indeed, other parties have done the same. But there are problems of Islamophobia within our society also, as you quite rightly draw attention to, and there are problems of language that is used. I do not ever use racist language in any form to describe anybody in this world or in our society. Let's be clear, you're saying that he does. We need a world where we treat people with respect.
That is going to cost all of you thousands of pounds of tax rises. That's why I say Keir Starmer and the Labour Party are not being straight with you. So do not surrender to their tax rises.
And see the intelligence that was given to us.

So there's a great point. And first of all, you are right to say that calling it the dementia tax, Theresa May's dementia tax, does not fully explain the situation. Because people with multiple sclerosis... What, he says it's nothing to do with dementia, he says it's not a tax. I'm afraid we're short of time. It's got everything to do. What Theresa May is planning to do is to say to everybody in this country who gets a long-term condition, whether it be Alzheimer's or multiple sclerosis or whatever it might be, that if you have to get care, then your spouse or your children, that house will have to go on the point of your death. That is not something that is the case now. She says, Theresa May says there'll be a cap, but she says, give me a colossal majority first and then they'll tell you what it's going to be.

Will you do that? I think this is an utterly depressing conversation. It does not serve the people of Scotland, certainly, and I would argue the people of the UK. Immigration can be an economic benefit. That's the way we should look at it. It should be fair, it should be rights-based.
The deficit's halved. The deficit's halved. 79,000 people... It's doubled! Thank you, Ed Miliband. 30% of young people in my constituency are unemployed.

10. Me and my mum and sister lived in a two-bedroom house in Preston. My mum's mate separated from her husband, and her and her two kids moved in with us. So six of us in a two-bedroom house. Felt like great fun at my age at the time. But I found out later on, the reason why my mum's mate couldn't get a house for her and her kids was because Margaret Thatcher had sold off those council houses that weren't any available. So what we will do is lift the borrowing cap to allow councils to borrow to build. allow housing associations new access to finance so they can borrow against the value of their stock to build and then you allow the government, you give the government the power to directly commission to buy brownfield sites and you raise the money by the sale of that land at the end. You actually make a profit whilst housing Britain's future.

Well, as I was saying to the gentleman at the back, the thing I learned from that process, the whole process, fees but other things too, was you shouldn't promise what you can't deliver. And I think we did in 2010.

We have a housing shortage across this country. But you know what, Nigel Farage? It's not caused by immigrants. In your world, every problem is caused by immigrants. We need to build more houses. We have a situation where certainly the Tory plans are Ed's may be slightly different, but the Tory plans are to spend a fraction building new houses of what is spent every year on housing benefit. It's the shortage of houses that drives up rent. We need to build more houses, substantially more houses, and then we need to protect them for the people who can't buy their own home. and need to rent. Now, Nigel, you're obviously setting out to win friends and influence people tonight, but if we can get away in this debate from the idea that every problem in this country is caused by immigrants. You know what? Immigrants from the European Union into this country make a net contribution to our country. So if we can maybe just put the bogeyman to one side, we can actually debate these issues for real and in substance.

Unlike the rest, I don't need a script. I've come out of retirement, back into politics with passion in my heart. And I'll tell you, Britain is broken, everyone knows it, and Britain needs reform. The election is over under our system. We know that Keir Starmer's going to win and probably have quite a big majority. The question is, Who will lead the opposition? Because for democracy to work, there has to be a voice of opposition. And I put it to you that Ed Davey of the Liberal Democrats won't do that, because he actually agrees with Labour on most things. Rishi Sunak won't do it. Well, he'll probably be in California by then anyway. And the Tory party is split down the middle and about to implode in this election. I say to you, I will stand up against open borders. I will stand up and say those that come illegally across the channel should be deported. I will stand up and fight for the millions of men and women running small businesses. I have the courage to take on the mob. Please join the revolt.

The reason for making this pledge is I really want to be clear with people. Over the last five years, we've made difficult decisions. We've talked about some of that tonight. It hasn't been easy, I know, for many people in Britain. But it's, I think, been the right thing to do. I now know what is in the books. I know what needs to be done. And I know we can finish the job without putting up people's taxes. And my fear is this. Those people who've opposed every step that we've taken, every cut we've had to make, I think they will make a cut of their own, which is to put up taxes to reach into your pay packet and to cut your pay. I don't want to do that. So if you want a government that finds more efficiencies in government spending, that goes on reforming welfare and doesn't put up taxes for working people, indeed cuts taxes for working people, that's me. If you want a government that goes on with unreformed welfare, that doesn't find savings in government spending and puts up taxes, that's the other guy. That's the choice.

Well, I can absolutely... Of course I can understand people's frustrations. It's been a very difficult few years. We've been through two once-in-a-century shocks, which have had an enormous impact on everyone, on public services, on people's standard of living. Of course it has. When energy bills double, when inflation goes up to 11%, when a pandemic shuts down our economy, with all the impact on everyone's livelihoods, of course that's taken its toll. But this election now is about the future, and I do believe that we've turned a corner. And I appreciate that people want to see positive change, And my only point would be that you don't get any change unless you've got a plan and you're prepared to do bold things. And that's what our manifesto sets out, a way to do that.

Carla Denyer. We can all see the Tories are toast, thank goodness. But we deserve better than a Labour Party that is offering more of the same. Angela says that Keir has changed the Labour Party, and she's right. He's changed them into the Conservatives. Frankly, our children deserve better. The Green Party is on the cusp of breaking through in seats up and down the country. Green MPs will never stop defending our future, protecting our NHS from privatisation and pushing the next Labour government to do better. We deserve real hope and real change. Vote Green.

Well, I can't understand what everything is going on in your mind that makes you say that, but whatever. Maybe we can talk about it later if you like. I want to live in a society where we do deal with the worst aspects of poverty that exist in our society, we do invest properly in our schools, we do give all our children a real chance and we do have a health service that is really there for all of us at all times. That's the kind of agenda that I've spent my life dealing with. The gentleman that asked the question about businesses, On the questions of public ownership, which you're quite right to raise, our proposals are that we will take our railways, which after all were invested in and built by the rest of us, back into public ownership. We'll take Royal Mail back into public ownership as it's a natural monopoly and our water industry which has been asset-stripped to within an inch of its life by many of these water companies and wastes a great deal of water will come back into public ownership which will be run with consumer and worker involvement. The new announcement we made last week was on broadband because I believe that the government's proposals are insufficient Only 10% of households in the whole of the UK get access to fibre optic broadband. We will make that 100% within 10 years, starting in the areas, the remote areas of the country, that have the least access at the moment. Hence, we can get the business development done in those places.

What we are saying is that democracy doesn't start and stop with one election. That has never been the case. If that was the case, we wouldn't be having an election here today. We are standing on a manifesto which is going to have a clear commitment to obviously have those discussions with the UK government, should we win that election. I'm confident that we will be able to, but the big question for the Unionist parties, and for Keir Starmer in particular, is whether he's going to follow the tact of Boris Johnson, Rishi Sunak and Liz Truss, and deny Scotland her democracy.

Thank you for inviting me here tonight. I'm very sorry this is not a debate, this is a series of questions. I think it's a shame the Prime Minister hasn't taken part in the debate. First, we're very clear on Brexit. A referendum took place, a decision was reached, we're leaving the European Union. Secondly, we will immediately legislate in office to guarantee rights of EU nationals to remain in this country. Secondly, we will negotiate with the European Union to guarantee trade access to the European markets and protection of the conditions that we've achieved through EU membership, because it's crucial to protect our manufacturing industry. Your point about our negotiating team, we have a great team. We have a great team of very experienced people. Keir Starmer is one of the leading lawyers of this country. I think I can trust Keir Starmer with negotiations more than some other people who are undertaking those negotiations.

In Wales there will be more cuts to education. There will be a difficulty continuing the tuition fee grant beyond 2017. If Labour win, they will cut The Welsh block grant by 2.2% according to the IFS. That's a billion pounds over the course of the next term. And the Tories will more than double that. So affording good education and job creation will be difficult under those circumstances. That's why we must end austerity and if we are in a situation where there's a hung Parliament, Plaid Cymru will do all it can to end austerity, to rebalance power and wealth and to win for Wales parity with Scotland and then we too can afford free tuition fees for students in Wales.

Some of it has got to be about understanding what is going on here. These people who are carrying out these terrorist attacks, they believe in a violent political ideology that wants to overthrow democracy, that wants to overthrow our liberal values. That is completely different from the Muslim religion. Muslim religion isn't about overthrowing democracy. Muslim religion is, you know, is about worshipping Allah and living a good life and so forth. And so part of it, you know, we've had instances in the past where people following an Islamist attack, you know, this violent political ideology, people have started attacking Sikh good warriors because they don't even know the difference between Muslims and Sikhs. So we've got to clamp down on all sorts of hate crime.

Well, the buck stops here. If you're in the job I'm in, you've got to take responsibility for the decisions. And we've talked this evening about Afghanistan and the responsibility I take every day making sure that our forces can do the job in Afghanistan and can soon, as a result of the efforts we're making, bring our forces home. And we've talked today also about security and how we need to be part of multilateral disarmament negotiations so that we, Britain, do not give up our weapons unless others are prepared to do so and reduce their weapons as well. And we've talked also about the economy this evening, and I've heard a number of people ask questions about the deficit, ask questions about how the economy is going to fare as businesses in the future, whether in or out of Europe. And the issue for me is, don't do anything that puts this economic recovery at risk. We have fought so hard for so long over these last two years to make sure we get through what has been the worst global financial recession since the 1930s. And we've had to take ownership of our banks. We've had to call the world together in London to have a big summit so that we can make decisions that everybody will work together. And we all decided we'd put resources into the economy. Unfortunately, the Conservatives want to take these resources out far too early, and they put thousands of jobs at risk. So I would say this evening, David, you're a risk to the economy. Nick, you're a risk because of what you're saying on Iran and on nuclear weapons to our security. Nick, you would leave us weak. David, you would leave us isolated in Europe. I think these are the problems that these parties have got to face up to. We have a credible, long-term plan for the future of Britain.

It has been a turbulent time and I would be the first to accept that and admit that. So my party has asked me to lead it through this election campaign and beyond the 2026 parliamentary elections to essentially do two things. to first of all rebuild trust between the electorate and the Scottish National Party and I've always worked throughout my political life to build trust and confidence with the electorate that have chosen me and secondly to provide the direction that's necessary in the Scottish Government to change people's lives and that's my primary duty as First Minister of Scotland. to make sure that we change lives and to improve lives of people in Scotland. And I've set that programme out by establishing a focus on eradicating child poverty, on achieving the transition to net zero, on strengthening the Scottish economy and improving our public services. And by doing that, I hope to rebuild the trust of people in Scotland, which I accept the SNP has got to earn on a constant basis.

No, it isn't. And it's been a really difficult decision for all the reasons you outlined, because the last Labour government had a laser focus on dealing with child poverty and did a huge amount of good work, including Sure Start and the programmes that were set up.

The right of recall, yes, but I want a more fundamental reform. I want an MP to be elected with more than 50% of the vote and I want a House of Lords that is not hereditary but elected on a proportional representation list system. Now that's what we want to put to a referendum next year.
I've been in government since 2010, so seven years in government.
But it doesn't get into the manifesto, does it? Jeremy, what we seem to be struggling with here is an understanding of a process that brings about a manifesto. No, no.

Well, it wasn't a huge number, but the fact is everyone that was, was being paid for by dozens of people going out to work, paying their taxes. Look, if you believe, as I do, we should go on reforming welfare, making sure that work always pays, helping people back into work, and keeping working people's taxes down, that's my programme. If you want unlimited welfare, more increases in welfare and higher taxes for working people, that's Ed Miliband's programme, vote for him. But I say keep reforming welfare, it's the right thing to do.

Well, at the moment, people coming from countries like Bangladesh are not being removed because they're not being processed. Well, the only way to stop the boats is to smash the gangs that are putting people in boats in the first place. Is it really the only way to secure... Fundamentally important.
I don't think it is right for any politician to dictate to a country what its future should be. I think that should be a choice for the people of Scotland.

And I would say that it is one nation conservatism which understands the vital importance of a dynamic market economy as the only way in which you can pay for fantastic public services and for looking after the poorest and neediest in our society. That is the balance and symmetry at the heart of what I'm trying to achieve with this government. and I think that to attack capitalism, as Mr Corbyn and Mr Macdonald do, is absolutely senseless. And by the way, to continue to attack all free trade is, I also think, ignorant of the fact... Just to be clear, Mr Johnson, you think he wants to destroy capitalism? Is that your view? Well, John Macdonald, who is the Shadow Chancellor, and some would say the intellectual driving force behind... this type of polio socialism is committed, he says, to fomenting the overthrow of capitalism. I think that is a mistake. I don't think that's the right way forward for our country. I believe in supporting businesses. I believe in cutting... Let him respond.
If there is a reasonable suspicion, then, sure, you need to search somebody. But the routine use of stop-and-search, whether it's intended to be or not, is a racist endeavor.

Every government that comes into office does a strategic defence review. There are serious issues about the shortage of ships in the Navy. There are serious issues about supplies of frigates. There are serious issues about maritime surveillance aircraft that are not available, that have to be addressed. There's a crucial issue of cyber security and cyber attacks that are taking place on us. And there's a crucial issue about terrorist attacks. And I don't believe you make a society safer

I am saying that we need to bring immigration under control and we need to limit it, just as Roy Jenkins said, in the quotation that I've just put to you - because of the importance of good community relations in this country, because we need to have a proper grip on security, and because we need to manage effectively the demands on our public services, the demands on housing and the demands on other things which are associated with immigration. At the moment we have a number of people coming in to the country, the size of a city like Peterborough every year. Over the next few years, the governments own figures show that there will be five million more people coming in to this country, the population will grow by five million because of immigration, that's five times the city of Birmingham. I think that although I recognise this is a country which has benefited from immigration, we are a better, stronger, richer country because we are more diverse, there have to be limits and there have to be controls.

Well, there's lots of roads already, actually, and a lot of the roads we have already are in a terrible state. There's a lot of people who really don't like potholes. Indeed. And that often doesn't get the funding. That's in the road building budget. OK, so potholes will still be filled. Road maintenance, of course, needs money. But you can spend huge amounts of money on new roads, which if we put that money into public transport measures, which benefit people who haven't got cars as well as those who have, and we bring the price down, I mean, so many people don't have access to a local bus or a decent train service and we need to invest that. We need to bring train prices down to be affordable for people to be able to use.

Nick Clegg. I strongly agree that apprenticeships which is an old idea, the idea that you sort of earn and learn, that you learn the tricks of the trade while you're actually working on the shop floor, in the office or elsewhere, is a great old idea that we've given it new life. We've got two million new apprenticeships created over the course of this last Parliament. It's never been expanded on such a scale, and I think it is something that I hope all future Parliaments will continue with, because it is a fantastic way to allow youngsters to get their feet on the first rung of the jobs ladder.

I chose my words carefully. It's the warm words which Wes Treating has used to describe the role he sees it for the private sector in the NHS. And the problem with that, you see, is that if you're allowing the scarce funding that we all agree there is in the NHS to be leaked out of the NHS in private profit, rather than doing things like setting up the cancer system that Plaid Cymru proposes in this election, you're not serving the people that we're meant to be serving.

Let's look at that record. We've decarbonised our economy faster than any other major economy in the world. By outsourcing everything to China. We've been the first to phase out coal, and we are leading the world in banning single-use plastics. It's actually a record that I'm very proud to stand here on. And we talk about renewable energy. We are now the world leader in offshore wind. That is something that this government has supported. LONG-BAILEY: Thank you very much. Thank you, Rebecca Long-Bailey. Rebecca Long-Bailey, please. LONG-BAILEY: I think Rishi's heart is in the right place but his party is led by a Prime Minister who called climate change a primitive fear without foundation and the policies that they have driven forward so far underline his true belief in this. LONG-BAILEY: But ultimately this election LONG-BAILEY: is critical to tackle the climate crisis. LONG-BAILEY: We are running out of time. LONG-BAILEY: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change have said that we have to take the vast majority of action to decarbonise our economy by 2030. LONG-BAILEY: And that's why we've rolled out a very detailed Green Industrial Revolution to outline how we're going to do that.

We are putting forward a proposal which I think will be very beneficial to all parts of the UK, including Scotland. And I think you have to look at the situation of the income and expenditure levels as they affect Scotland. Surely Scotland needs to be able to address the problems it has, and the industrial and green infrastructure investment that it absolutely needs. That's all it means, Scotland, they're telling me.

What Keir Starmer is not telling you is actually most of the people who we're talking about, who Sue asked about originally, aren't on an NHS waiting list. So talking about that isn't an answer to this problem. Nearly 8 million. The answer to this problem is actually being stricter about the eligibility criteria, making sure that everyone who can work does work, because that's fair to everyone like Sue who is working very hard and putting in, that's what I will do. I promise you I will deliver on it. We will. cut the welfare bill, the growth in the welfare bill. It is unsustainable. We spend too much on it. I set out a plan to do so. Labour oppose all of it. So the choice for you is simple. If you want higher taxes and higher welfare, that's what Keir Starmer will do. If you want lower taxes and lower welfare, that's what I will do. Do not surrender our welfare system to the Labour Party and Keir Starmer.

Can I make this point about how social carers are paid? Because so many are on zero-hours contracts and that is a real scourge in our society at the moment. Thank you very much. And I want to make a further point because Labour have claimed that they will get rid of zero-hour contracts. Seven times Plaid Cymru have given Labour in the National Assembly for Wales the opportunity to vote to abolish zero-hours contracts. Seven times they've failed to do that. So what does Labour really stand for in terms of zero-hours contracts?

This is the honesty that we need in this debate. We can't have a decent NHS. We can't have good schools unless we have a fair taxation system that brings the money in to pay for those services. The taxation system has to be fair. We have to have honesty. And the taxation has to be fair in that the weight is borne by the shoulders of those who have the broadest shoulders, be it corporates or the very, very wealthy.

I was shocked and I was sickened by what I saw. I've been brought up to believe by my parents that you act honestly and you act fairly and you act responsibly. And just as the bankers were irresponsible, so too were Members of Parliament. And nobody should be standing for election at this election who is guilty of the offences that we've seen in MPs. Now I want to do three things to change the system. First of all, I want to give the right of recall to constituents. If your MP is misbehaving and is guilty of corrupt practices and Parliament doesn't act, you should have the right to recall that MP. The second thing we've got to do is give people the right to petition Parliament so that your issues can be raised in Parliament, and that's what we propose to do. And thirdly, and this is quite fundamental, and I don't think David will support us on that, but I hope Nick will, We've got to reform the House of Commons and the House of Lords. We need a new House of Commons and new House of Lords. We will have a referendum to elect members of Parliament with more than 50% of the vote and to have a House of Lords that is elected rather than hereditary or unaccountable. These are the changes we need.
...for wind farms alone.
We've already given social care an extra $8 billion because the pressures are immediate. And the pilot reforms are there for charging.

Liam Wood. But which students would you stop from going to university then? Would you stop the ones that are coming from the poorest backgrounds perhaps? How do you decide to reduce the number of students going to university?

Yeah, because I think we do have to invest in our public services and we've said that. Austerity was very damaging for our public services and actually cost us more in the long run and people are not getting the care that they need. But we do need reform and we do need the new technologies that will help as well to bring those efficiencies in.

I mean, I absolutely recognise the issue that you're raising. And the bottom line is that there are far, far too many people in our country living in poverty and life is too hard. And we did not get everything right.

I actually worked for the man who was sent by Margaret Thatcher, would you believe it, to bat for Britain in Brussels. And what I learnt when I was there was this, the European Union is not perfect, of course not. I mean, this is a club that took 15 years to define chocolate in a chocolate directive. Anything that takes 15 years to define chocolate is not a model of democratic efficiency. But what I also learnt was this, that there are a whole lot of things, whether we like it or not, whatever your views on Europe and the European Union, which we simply can't do on our own. We can't deal with international crime, which touches and affects every single community in this country on our own. We can't deal with climate change on our own. The weather doesn't stop at the cliffs of Dover. We can't regulate these wretched banks that got us into so much trouble in the first place, which now sprawl across countries. I don't think the European Union is perfect. I want it reformed. That's why I want to lead in the European Union. But we're stronger together and we are weaker apart.

Well, we are under threat from people who don't want to attack in the conventional sense, but want to attack us, maim fellow citizens. It's because we're meddling in their countries. Can I... Well, I don't actually think there's any excuse that any rational or reasonable person can give to those people who want to kill innocent British citizens through terrorist acts in our cities. And whilst... Whilst... Whilst as a good old-fashioned liberal, I'm the first person to defend our civil liberties and our freedoms, I think there is no inconsistency between defending our freedoms and keeping ourselves safe. We don't make ourselves any less free by making ourselves any less safe.

Start putting it online? It means that you can't build 100,000 houses tomorrow on Friday, but we can certainly start to create the national education system so people can get free education. How many houses are you building in Wales while you're in government? To get those jobs. How can you talk about houses you're building?

I'm going to wrap this, because... But I will just... I've got so many questions for you. But I think that's pretty important. Where I just very respectfully disagree with you, actually, based on the clinical advice that we have from all our leading medics, is that we can improve care for people by doing things outside of hospital. And the Community Diagnostic Centre, one of which is here, is a great example of that, where you can get your checks and tests and scans quicker. Another example of that is pharmacies, right? Because I think that you should be able to go and see your pharmacist to get treatment rather than your GP for common ailments. That's a change that we've brought in that already millions of people are benefiting from. And again, it's just an example of doing things differently to get people the care that they need.

My challenge to everybody here was, of course, ignored and brushed aside for chiefly politically correct reasons. Terry wants to know where the money's coming from. And yes, you've got to put money in. You've got to stop money being wasted. I mentioned health tourism. OK, here's a fact. And I'm sure there are other people who will be mortified that I dare to talk about it. There are 7,000 diagnoses in this country every year for people who are HIV positive. which is not a good place for any of them to be, I know. But 60% of them are not British nationals. You can come into Britain from anywhere in the world and get diagnosed with HIV and get the retroviral drugs that cost up to £25,000 per year per patient. I know there are some horrible things happening in many parts of the world, but what we need to do is to put the National Health Service there for British people and families who in many cases have paid into this system for decades.

Thank you. I'm standing here for a very simple reason, that I love this country, and I think we can do even better in the years ahead. We can go on, solve our problems and do great things. But we need a government with the right values. We need a government that backs families and understands that the family is the most important thing in our society. We need a government that backs work, and people who try to do the right thing. We need a government that always understands that keeping us safe and secure is the most important thing of all. But there's something else you need to know about me. I believe the test of a good and strong society is how we look after the most vulnerable, the most frail and the poorest. That's true in good times, but it's even more true in difficult times. And there will be difficult decisions, but I want to lead us through those to better times ahead. I think I've got a great team behind me. I think we can do great things in this country. If you vote Labour, you'll get more of the same. If you vote Liberal, as we've seen tonight, it's just uncertainty. If you vote Conservative on Thursday, you can have a new, fresh government, making a clean break, and taking our country in a new direction, and bringing the change we need.

Thank you very much indeed. Well, the reason I focused on a fair voting system... I mean, there's so, so much that could be better in politics, but the reason I focused on that is that when MPs have safe seats, they're not held to account. short of a serious breach of the law and being found to be a criminal, you can do pretty much anything if you're an MP in a safe seat and you'll be re-elected. Holding MPs to account, a big part of that is making sure that they are properly answerable to the electorate.

Well, you're right to ask the question. This is such a serious issue and the continuing abuse, harassment and worse of women and girls in this country is an absolute stain on our society. The Green Party would make it a priority to end domestic abuse and violence against women and girls. We want to free up police time to ensure that they can focus on those issues. and we would make sure that police are trained to deal with domestic violence because too often we hear reports of women who have brought these cases to the police and have not been taken seriously, so that's the absolute top line for me. We would also, and this has been a long-running Green Party campaign for a few years set up by our former deputy leader Amelia Womack, is to make misogyny a hate crime. It's amazing that it isn't already. I think that would help the police and our justice system to take it seriously. And finally, we would develop and implement a new UK-wide strategy to tackle rape, sexual abuse, FGM and trafficking. OK. But to undermine, just briefly... You need to be really quick. ..to underpin all of this... We need proper accountability so that women can regain trust in the police force because too many incidents in recent years, police officers have been part of the problem rather than the solution.

Thank you, not over each other. Thank you, not over each other. We've committed to the Triple Lock Plus. Under us, the state pension will never pay tax. Under you, there's a retirements tax coming for British pensioners. I think it's appalling. Explain to people why pensioners should pay tax on the state pension.
No, I think they should, on that particular issue, they have to be fuller in order to allow us to use them solely in Scotland with confidence.

I want the Scottish people to have the right to choose their own future. Fundamentally for Scotland, we have put up with... Scotland has not voted for a Conservative Westminster Government for 60 years. For 36 of those 60 years, we have had to put up with a Tory Government. the European Union against our will. I want Scotland to determine our own future. But in answer to your question, I will never put a Tory Prime Minister into power. I grew up in the west of Scotland. I saw the damage. I grew up in the Thatcher years. I saw the damage that Tory governments did to Scotland and to communities across the UK. I will never support a Tory government. But if Jeremy Corbyn wants the support of the SNP, I don't think it should surprise people to hear me say that I would ask him to respect the right of the people of Scotland to choose their own future. And I don't think he'll turn his back on ending austerity in order to block that right.
It has. I'm being very clear about that. And now it's coming down. OK. Because we are now, the plans are working. You made the pledge in January 20th. We've resolved the industrial action.

by bringing the country together. I think this is such an important point because politics is about the policies that you put forward at the election, obviously. But it's also about how you do politics and how you deal with difficult topics. I want to do two things if we get into government in two weeks' time. and they're about the mindset of government. The first thing is, I want to return politics to service, because I think it's got far too much into self-entitlement. Look at the gambling stuff that's going on today. Politicians who think that making money or self-entitlement is the reason they're in politics. So we need to return it to service. The second thing, to go directly to your question, is I want to take the opportunity to bring the country together, because What's happened in politics, in my view, over the last 10, 15 years or so, is they've become more and more divided and toxic, and everybody's trying to find the difference between us rather than the points of connection. And Jo Cox, who tragically was murdered, was a good friend of mine. We came into Parliament at the same time. Our children are broadly the same age. What she said in her maiden speech, that we have far more in common, and we do, which divides us. That is what I carry. That is what I want to have the opportunity, if we get into power in two weeks, to implement so that we actually are good at this as a country. During the pandemic, people were looking out for each other. Yes, you know, food, medicine, of course, but just the gentle knock on the door of a neighbour that perhaps we didn't know very well, to say, are you all right? We're a good country, a reasonable, tolerant country. We're pretty live and let live if we don't go down the politics of division. And I want to stop that.
Well, I would agree with you that the Tories are damaging, so that's why I would ask you to work with us to bring an end to further Tory cuts and another Tory government.
I'm opposed to privatisation. You know I didn't vote for the bill.
What matters to you more, economic growth or successful climate policy?

Tonight, this is what you'll see. Every leader on this stage will agree, with me. They will all work hard to sound a little bit like the Green Party. Some might even sound convincing. It's progress. But as you watch, ask yourself two important questions. Do their plans match the scale of the emergency we face? And do you trust them to get this done without more Greens in Parliament making sure of it? If the answer is no, then vote Green. If not now, when? Thank you. And next Jeremy Corbyn.

Please. Well, thanks very much, Tom. And there will be all sorts of ways. I've mentioned the ways we can cut VAT on sanitary products or regulate differently for bioscience or have free ports. ports in areas that need investment. You can trigger growth in investments in some of the left-behind coastal communities by having free ports, as 130 countries do around the world. We can't because of EU rules. Another major advantage is, of course, that we take back control of a lot of money that we'd otherwise send to the EU. But I might mention also our ability to control our immigration, which is not unimportant. I'm a believer in immigration for people of talent who want to come here, fantastic, but we've got to control it democratically.

Leanne Wood. Tonight you've heard that Plaid Cymru is the only party putting Wales on the agenda during this election. But our MPs will play their part for the whole of the UK. We will stand up for justice and fair play on pensions, on social care and for our cash-starved public services. So I urge people back home to give us the mandate we need to defend Wales. Liking us isn't enough. We need your support and your vote. Don't let our country be invisible in Westminster. Vote Plaid. Vote for Wales.

We will cut the costs of politics more by halving the House of Lords and making it a far smaller chamber, making it accountable and making it democratic. David, let's be honest. You voted against taking action against removing hereditary peers from the House of Lords. You don't want that to happen. You blocked it only in the last week. The key issue here is will we take responsibility for a better form of politics

But I think at election time, a lot of people talk about supporting mental health. And again, it goes back to actually reviewing people's records. And we need to raise awareness. We need to fund it. But we need to put the money where it's most important. In Scotland, we were, under the SNP, we're one of the first governments to establish a minister for mental health. And we have doubled the number of places for child psychologists, for example, going back to the point. about putting the money where it's most critical from a young age, and making sure there is that support. But it comes back to this idea of everything being integrated. So education, health care, we've also integrated health and social care. So that means that we aren't, there isn't the same extent, for example, of bed blocking, because we're able to support people. from the point that they come into contact with social care or with the health service. And there's that streamlined support for people, so that when they ask for help, they're not left hanging for a very, very, very, very long time before they get help. They're able to get help quickly.
When the population goes up, there is more demand for houses. It's not difficult, really.

I think it was probably my turn. I do want to say something to you, Ed, that you are on the record about. You're comfortable with a 5 per cent private profit for private companies operating in the NHS. We should have zero per cent profit in the NHS.

Well, cannabis, gives some people, a very, very small proportion of people who have a propensity to schizophrenia to trigger it. But it's a very, very small proportion of people. I've been the champions of mental health. Norman Lamb, when he was in the coalition, was the champion of mental health. We got the Tories to a greater parity between physical health and mental health, which meant that... And we actually introduced waiting times for mental health that never existed before. As far as this extra six billion is concerned, we want to earmark one billion of that for improvement in mental health, and a billion to improve GP services, because we know that by spending money on those issues, you get the best return on that investment by investing in those things.

I mean, to answer Nicola's question, I mean, economics is about people. It's not about figures. It's about people's experience and how they can afford to feed their children and look after them. Like many people, I have known in my life what it's like to not be able to pay the bills. It matters to me that we build a country for all of our children where people are decent to one another. A strong welfare state is essential. I've been helping two people in my constituency with their PIP payments in the last month. both of whom were chief executives of different companies in the not-too-distant past. Everybody out there who thinks that welfare is some kind of a burden needs to remember that any one of us is only one or two steps from being in need at any given time. And just to answer the question specifically, what will we do? We will end the benefit freeze. Since the crash in 2007-08, the public sector pay freeze as well. But since that crash, public sector workers have probably borne the brunt of that. those cuts, whether it be nurses, teachers, doctors, social care workers, but you also need to support people to be able to go to work and have the ability to choose it. That's why we'll invest that money, £3.6 billion, into childcare for two-year-olds, £5.6 billion for two-year-olds, to make sure parents have got the ability to go out to work.

I mean what was negotiated was even much less close agreement with the rest of the European Union than what Theresa May put forward because you watered down any of the what were inadequate protections on workers' rights, on environmental protections and the only possible conclusion can be is that's because a Conservative government would want to rip up workers' rights. It's absolutely the case that that's what the Conservatives want to do and that's why it's so dangerous for people in this country who are wanting to make sure that their hard won rights are not going to be sold down the river.
You can't be prescriptive about how many wind farms, because this will have to be...

Well, related to that, and I think we are, you know, related to that of course is those people who look after loved ones who are elderly and need help with cleaning, washing, getting dressed, getting fed. And I do think, and I think in the past we've all agreed this is something we need to look at, is we need to provide more respite, more time off for people who care for their loved ones in that way. We have got a proposal which would take the money which the government is allocated to its rather flawed bill on social care and use it instead to say to those who provide a lot of care every week look here's money so you can take a week off and spend some time with yourself on holiday just getting a break because I think speaking to any carer as we know that makes a huge huge dramatic difference to their lives.

over my political life, a number of powers have been devolved to the Scottish Parliament. When I was born, the Scottish Parliament didn't exist. I was one of the founding members of it 25 years ago. So I've welcomed the development of that Scottish Parliament. I was also part of a commission that secured more powers for the Scottish Parliament in 2016. So we've strengthened the powers of the Parliament. So I'm never going to say no to more powers for the Scottish Parliament. But what I want to be able to do is to do the things that will transform people's lives. I want to live in a fairer, more equal country. I want to live in a country that prioritises the improvement of our economy and strengthening the rights and the responsibilities of people in our country. And I don't think that can be achieved for us in the United Kingdom, which is why I want Scotland to be like Denmark or Ireland or Sweden, as an independent country, And when you look at those countries, they are more prosperous, they're more equal, they're fairer than Scotland and the United Kingdom. And that's a prize that I think people in Scotland are attracted to and want to make sure happens for our country.

Where has it all gone wrong? There have been challenges about the issues you have set out and we have got to recognise the importance of delivering on the priorities of the public. When I look at some of the issues we have delivered on, for example, we have recently doubled the availability of early learning and childcare for families in our country so that every three and four-year-old and for many two-year-olds get access to full-time early learning and childcare which saves a family about five and a half thousand pounds a year per child and it's policies like that that make a real difference to the lives of children and young people and families in our country that we need to deliver on. We've established a Scottish child payment which puts about £26 every week into the family, into households of low-income families, to try to help with the cost of living and to eradicate child poverty. Now, these are examples where I think the SNP is delivering to individuals and to households around the country to make that difference. And we've got to do more of that as we take forward our agenda to transform lives within Scotland.

It's obvious there is civil unrest in Bolivia. There are very strong feelings on both sides about people that feel they've been let down because Evo Morales is no longer president, others that probably don't support him at all. Surely the function now is to try and bring people together. I met Evo Morales last year. I've met him over the years. recognise that Bolivia, before he became president, was probably the poorest country in Latin America. It isn't now.

I'm going to be straight with you. The choice you make on the 4th of July isn't just about which party you vote for. If we look beyond the finger-pointing and squabbling, politics is about choosing the kind of future we want for our children. We all know the Tories are toast, and Labour will form the next government. But are they offering real change, or is it the same broken politics dressed up with a red rosette? We deserve better. So as you watch this with your friends or family, think of who you want on your side in the next Parliament, making sure your voice is heard. The Green Party is on the cusp of breaking through in seats up and down the country. And Green MPs will never stop defending our future, protecting our NHS from privatisation, and pushing the next Labour government to think bigger and act bolder. They'll tell you that's not how it works. They'll tell you you have to vote for more of the same. But a vote is a choice. And on the 4th of July, you can choose real hope and real change.

I do not think this is putting a cap. We should encourage immigration that is right for our needs. Natalie and Leanne are right to point out the way in which people who come here from overseas contribute to our National Health Service. We should not be ideological about this. We should be pragmatic and practical about it. What does our economy need? That is what should drive our decisions on immigration. I fear, increasingly, that it is Nigel Farage who is wrongly driving the decisions that some of the Westminster politicians are making.

I do agree that the first-past-the-post system has led to the two big parties. Well, one was big, not after today's polls, but there we are. They're both mushy sort of SDP parties in the middle. I would change the voting system. I think the House of Lords is an abomination. The fact that it's filled up with party donors is a complete disgrace. There must be an elected element. And finally, let's give the people the ability to call more referendums so they can decide the biggest issue of their lives.

This is, I think, one of the, I'll be perfectly honest about it, it's one of the trickiest balances we've got to strike. We have a police force, not just in Scotland, but across the UK, that is routinely unarmed. And my feeling is that is how the majority of people would prefer that it stayed. But we must make sure we've got sufficient numbers of armed police officers to respond to incidents like the one in London. I mean, frankly, the response of the police on Saturday night was exceptional. I mean, all of us should be full of gratitude for the speed and effectiveness with which they responded. Frankly, the police saved, I think, a lot of lives through their response on Saturday night, and we must make sure we support them to do that.

So, I absolutely recognise that so many people feel that this division that we have in our country right now is really horrible, it's really uncomfortable, right? In this room, there's people who are Leave, Remain and so on, and I'm sure we all feel uncomfortable about that, and we kind of crave for and long for a feeling where that isn't there. All I would say is I don't think there's an easy answer to this. In Scotland, we've been living with this now for eight years because of the run-up to the independence referendum, and the society is still very divided on those lines. So I don't think there's a quick fix, but I do think that many of the issues which people are unhappy about, that things don't... things are too tough, that, you know, the economy doesn't work well enough for people. I genuinely fear that those are going to be made worse by leaving the European Union. There's going to be less money about it. I mean, to take up the point about austerity, you know... Because what this questioner is saying is that you will make austerity worse by running a budget surplus. But what my point is, is that... it's leaving the European Union that is going to unleash austerity on our country because we are going to be poorer, people are going to lose their jobs, there will be less money for our NHS and it will be worse, whereas if we remain then we will actually have more money to spend to be able to invest in our communities and in resolving many of the issues that people are unhappy about. So, you know, that's why I'm standing on that platform to say I want things to be better in our country because I recognise that for too many people it has been I think that's a really good point. I think that's a really good point. I think that's a really good point. But I'm not going to stand here and promise that we're going to abolish tuition fees. There's a lot of numbers that are getting thrown around by different parties in this election. And a lot of the stuff sounds good, but can it actually be delivered? Do you actually, in your heart of hearts, believe that those sums add up when the IFS says that it's just not credible when you look at the Labour manifesto? So I'd rather be straightforward and honest with people.

One of the points I wanted to make, going back to the discussion about targets, is the UK government's target right now, the 2050 target, actually excludes aviation emissions from it. Scotland's target includes aviation emissions. And I suspect if the UK was to include emissions from aviation, Heathrow expansion would not align with that. So we've got to make sure that these targets, if they are to be meaningful, are actually including all of the emissions and the biggest emitters along the way. You have to include aviation.

I'm a litte bit unusre about which country Gordon Brown thinks he's Prime Minister of. Because in Britain today, there are actually 900,000 young people not in employment, not in education, not in training. He's caused record youth unemployment. We've seen unemployment today 40% higher than when he came to power in 1997 after the longest and deepest recession in our history. So, to talk about as if somehow he's got a magnificent economic record is nonsense. To answer Nick's point, of course you need better incentives. It's outrageous today that a single mum trying to go back to work gets 95p of every extra pound she earns taken away by the state, but you do need a penalty, so we say to people, if you go on refusing the offer of a job you can do, you should lose your entitlement to benefits for up to three years. I think that is important. You need to have that as well as the incentive to encourage people out to work.

We've got to be honest and stop blaming immigration for everything. So what we are planning to do is to promise 300,000 new homes a year. If the private sector, as the Tories rely on, does not deliver that, then the government must directly commission housing where it's needed to make sure we get up to that level. In terms of people who can't afford a deposit, We've got a scheme called Rent to Buy. What it means is that you rent a property from a housing association, you pay market rent, but gradually you'll build up, just by paying the rent, you gradually build up a share in that property. So at the end of 30 years, where you're just paying the rent, you will own that property. And if you want to move, then you take whatever you've built up in terms of the deposit when you move. So you need no deposit at all. It's as if you're just renting, but you're renting at market rent. But because you're doing it from a housing association, the property will be yours in 30 years time.
Average earnings of a nurse, I would say, in about the same region.

As a new father, the climate crisis feels personal now. My son is only one, but one day soon he's going to ask me, you had power, so what did you do? What I want to do is to combine hope with urgency, to make a trinity of zeros, zero carbon, zero waste, zero poverty, our common goal by 2030. To turn Wales into the world's test bed, to make this moment not a tipping point of disaster, but a turning point of change.

Paul, this is still an incredibly divisive way forward, and there is no educational evidence that suggests that this is good for the vast bulk of our people. Quite the opposite. To the contrary. And what you're doing, essentially, is making a decision when a child is just 11 years old. You are forgetting and ignoring huge amounts of evidence about how boys and girls develop at different stages, and how many children don't actually develop quite so fast. I have to say, relying on private tutors, which richer people can afford, poorer people can't. Let me bring in Leanne. Thank you, Caroline Lucas.

Our ambition is that we desperately need to push forward on the bold action that's needed, both on addressing the climate and nature crises, the defining issues of our time, and on creating a more equal society. Where's your influence in Westminster? All the polls suggest we're on track for a Labour government. The question is, do we want Keir Starmer to have such a big majority that he can continue to make U-turns all the time on climate or on funding for public services? If there's a group of Green MPs in Parliament, and we're standing a record number of candidates in this election, going into it in better shape than ever before. We've got our top four target seats. If we win those, we'll be able to push this new government in the right direction. If they don't have that voice in Parliament, they'll be able to just continue to pretend that these little tweaks that Labour's proposing is actually going to address these big issues of our times. Do you really think that the Labour proposals that we've heard tonight are going to enable us to tackle the climate crisis, to address the fact that...

The question was, how would you raise the money? We've been quite detailed about how we raise the money. Firstly, we'd scrap HS2, which is only there for the benefit of one place and one place alone, and that is London. We'd also look at the Barnard formula, which gives the Scots 1,700 pound per head more than us, the English. It needs to be scrapped. We'd also get an EU dividend. And finally, finally, finally,
You talk about right to buy and everything else, but you've not created the council housing and the social housing that we need in this country.

So as I was saying, We didn't get everything right. We also had plenty of fights with the Conservatives. And we won some of those fights, and we lost some of those fights. And I am sorry that we did not win more of those fights in coalition because of the reasons that you are outlining. Now, we did stop the Conservatives cutting 12 billion more from welfare. We did stop them bringing in the two-child cap, which they did, of course, when they were on their own in 2015. But I absolutely think that we have lessons to learn from that. I've learned those lessons. And if you look at what we're setting out, we are identifying six billion extra to put into universal credit to make sure that you don't have that five-week wait, which is hurting so many people, so that we get rid of that two-child cap, that we get rid of the bedroom tax. So I get it. We got stuff wrong. And we, in the future, going forward, are determined to get it right.
Penny, keep spouting this rubbish, but we've got more working people.
With America, with Donald Trump, and have almond-filled beef and chlorinated chicken. Why not? That would be fantastic for the American people. Why not, Sam? Why not, Sam?

Well, look, I want everyone to be able to own their own home. It's a really magical thing when you can, whether you're a daughter and everyone else. And the best way for us to do that is to get interest rates down again in the first instance. And that's why I made a priority to bring inflation down, because that's how we will enable the Bank of England to then start cutting interest rates. And as you can see, inflation is down from 11% back to normal. So hopefully, we're on the right path then. And you will see that reflected in mortgage rates. But actually, in our manifesto, there were a couple of things that will help your daughter. And hopefully you can tell her about them, and hopefully she's watching. One of them was stamp duty. So what I will do if we're re-elected is to abolish stamp duty for first-time buyers, like your daughter, buying homes up to £425,000.

Yeah, absolutely. I mean, 15 a year, ridiculous. So 20,000 is what we need. And we need to continue at that rate over the course of the next 15 years. And it's absolutely right. There's this issue about affordable homes and help to buy. I mean, the average price of many of these, even in Wales, by the way, is 150,000 something, 200,000. These are not affordable by any kind of stretch of the imagination. So we're putting the resources in the wrong area. We need to do something on social housing. In the meantime, we've got to do something about generation rent. Those who are renting in the private sector, we've got to bring back rent control. We've got to have they end to no-fault evictions, we've got to have open-ended tenancies, those are all things that are in the Labour manifesto in the Westminster election, but you could do...
We've heard overnight about 12 new taxes that Labour are going to bring in. It's not rubbish. That's rubbish.
Renewable energy is now the cheapest thing we can possibly invest in.

This election is really quite boring so far, isn't it? And yet it really matters, because it's our chance as a country to say what sort of future we want. And people I speak to on the doorsteps every day in East Anglia really want a change from this Conservative government, which has let us down for so long. And yet the changes that Labour are offering are just making small tweaks. They're really timid. Changes that are not going to tackle the big issues of our time, that our NHS is creaking, that people can't get access to affordable housing, that our rivers are polluted, that we're all suffering from a cost of living crisis. We need real hope and real change if we're going to respond to the challenges that our country faces. And that's why we need a group of Green MPs in the next Parliament to push the next government to be bolder, both on restoring our public services and on protecting our environment. And the environment has been far too left out of this debate. So I'm asking people to vote Green on the 4th of July.

But what I was absolutely set about doing was making things that little bit easier. Well, look, let's go... And that's why inflation was a priority. Let's go through the pledges. And it was precisely because the bills were continuing to go up that we needed to bring inflation down, and I'm pleased we were able to do that.

I have just spoken about the home energy efficiency programme which provides financial support, we are going to update that. But people have to pay some money themselves. Absolutely, this is a collective effort but government has a big part to play here. One other point I was going to make, which Scotland cannot do on our own, so we need to have cooperation from whoever is in the UK government, is around decarbonisation of the gas grid. There is not enough priority being given to that by the current UK government and I hope that changes.

I think Mary's saying, can a vote make a difference? And I believe it can. Yes, we will give the right of recall, so if you don't like an MP for being corrupt and Parliament doesn't take any action, then you can remove them. Yes, we'll give you a right to put petitions to the House of Commons, so they have to be debated if you put them in sufficient number to do so. And yes we want a referendum on the future of the House of Commons and the House of Lords and you the people should have a vote on a new voting system for the House of Commons and ending the hereditary principle in the House of Lords and making it for the first time elected and accountable.
