But you know your vote matters as well because this is a big choice election. We've got to secure the recovery, and it's put at risk by Conservative policies. We've got to make sure we have decent public services, and that's put at risk by Conservative policies. We've got to build the jobs of the future, and that's put at risk, actually, by the policies of both these parties who are here this evening. So your vote matters. Please use it. It's the most important and decisive election for years, because our future depends on how you vote in the next few weeks.

But what about pressure on public services? Yes, exactly. Caused by a failure to invest in public services. You have to plan, don't you? How about looking at what our actual needs are? What I'd like to do is look at how we have visa powers in Wales, for example, to make sure that we can bring in people into shortage occupations. give visas to those that we need here.
Yes, and even the Prime Minister has said that he's not prepared to commit to that level of expenditure. The expenditure on defence is 6% and what is allocated can be spent better than it is.

Look, the reality of it is this. We were already a divided so-called United Kingdom. We were divided by a huge chasm of wealth between the North and the South, between the West and the East of the country. And many people, there will be many, many different reasons for voting Lee, but many people in the former Caulfield communities, like the one that I'm from, voted for leave because they were exasperated at a Westminster political establishment that has never delivered economic change for them. I have to be honest to people in the communities like the one that I represent. Look, Brexit will not deliver the economic justice that we want. It'll make matters worse. It'll take the division that's already there and make it what it'll do to our economy, what it's done to our politics, divided the rich from the poor. It will not be the answer to our problem. Certainly don't take power from Brussels. They're putting it in Westminster. We'll do the opposite of economic change for Wales. Thank you. Jo Swinson.

Well, listen, I take... We're talking, obviously, falling off the paddleboard. I'll come to the paddleboard in a second. Going down a water slide. I'll come to the paddleboard. Going on a roller coaster. You name it, you've done it. Yeah, I'm happy to talk through all of them. And I don't know what they've got in store for me afterwards, I have to say. We did wonder if you were going to paraglide in here tonight. But it's a serious question, I'm grateful for it. I take your concerns and the concerns of voters very, very seriously. And if you look at our manifesto, we've got some very... well thought through policies to try to do things like rescue the NHS. But I don't think politicians should take themselves too seriously. I think that's part of the problem in politics actually. People are really disillusioned. They think politicians don't get them. And yes, it's been to grab a bit of attention. But when I did fall off that paddleboard in Lake Windermere, the serious message was about the sewage problem. The Conservative government, the water regulators, have allowed water companies to pump their filthy sewage into our precious environment, our lakes, our rivers, seas. And that's got to stop. I want to end the sewage scandal.

A sort of Conservative leader who's not afraid to criticise the Prime Minister of this country when he thinks that those criticisms are justified. I've made criticism of Mr Blair's conduct of the war in Iraq, and I, I will carry on making those criticisms where justified, and er, you know, if that offends certain people, that's though.

Well, like you, I was incredibly angry. Incredibly angry to learn of these allegations. It's a really serious matter. It's right that they're being investigated properly by the relevant law enforcement authorities, including, as Fiona said, a criminal investigation by the police. I want to be crystal clear that if anyone has broken the rules, they should face the full force of the law. And that's what those investigations are there to do. And I hope that they do their work as quickly and as thoroughly as possible.
The way that you've done it so far, you've been balancing the books on the backs of the poor. 79,000... No, we've actually done it in a balanced way.

What the cats have said is that we would not increase taxes on struggling families during, of course, the living crisis. That is not true. But we would look to the big banks, to the big tech companies, who can pay a little bit more to fund our public services.

Yes, I think the agreement should be that you come to a discussion with the employer. I can work for three hours on a Monday, six hours on a Thursday, two hours at a weekend, something like that. And that's my work pattern. It's not simple to manage for any employer. I fully understand that. But it does give the security to the individual. It also gives the employer a wide range of workers that are working and will be loyal to that employer. All right, you sir at the back there.

Well, that's a very good... Can I just clarify, firstly, not to dodge the question, because I'll come on to it. I don't envisage being in a coalition government with Labour. I think it would be a less formal arrangement than that, if we're in that situation. But, you know, many of the areas of England suffer from much the same neglect as Scotland does and much the same contempt from Westminster as Scotland does. So the SNP will always try to stand up for decentralisation from London. Much of what we have been able to do because we have a Scottish Parliament some of the policies that Labour are now promising in their UK manifesto, I think with more power, with more devolution to some of the regions of England, would be possible there as well. So the SNP will always be a voice for decentralisation and more powers to be spread around.

And if you support the Labour Party and if you want socialism and that's your viewpoint, then we're not a socialist party. I'm a liberal. And it's not some annex to the Labour Party. a liberal party. We want an open and a fair and inclusive country. We're an internationalist party. That's why we want to remain in the European Union and stop Brexit, because we believe that you get further, you achieve more if you work with your closest neighbours. That's what we are standing for. That's what our values are. And sometimes we do agree with the Labour Party, but we are not the Labour Party. We are a different party. And that's why we're standing separately. We stand on our own two feet in this election. The woman right at the back there.

Well, you're absolutely right to recognise that child poverty is a scourge. And as someone who grew up in poverty, I absolutely know that we have to tackle it. And that's why we'd have a strategic number 10 unit that would look at tackling child poverty. But it's not just about one particular lever. We talked before about housing. That is a problem for people. We've talked about employment. Insecure work is a problem for people. Low wages. So we're going to tackle all of the issues that will help. And the growing of the economy will give us more money in our public services so we can do those things. But uncosted spending commitments is not going to help our economy, because the Tories did that and they crashed our economy. So our fiscal rules are absolutely cast iron.

because I think that what we didn't do enough of was build up other industries as well. Sure, financial services is important, including here in Leeds. But look, the reality is that we didn't do enough on apprenticeships, we didn't do enough on a modern industrial policy, and we're not doing enough now. Because you can't simply rely on one industry. And so my point is that actually you need a more diverse industrial base, and that is also at stake in this election.

And the reality is this. If on May the 8th there are more anti-Tory MPs in the House of Commons than there are Tory MPs, then if we work together, we can lock David Cameron out of Downing Street. So tell me tonight, is it the case that you would rather see David Cameron go back into Downing Street than work with the SNP? Surely that cannot be your position, Ed.

Because we will take action on the things which matter to the country and the things which matter to people, and that's why we've been spelling out our plans to bring to this country school discipline, clean hospitals, more police, controlled immigration, lower taxes; the things that people really do care about and the things that are important for the country's future and unlike Mr Blair who talks a lot but does very little, we will carry out the promises we make.

system where people feel it is complete chaos. I agree with Nick, an arbitrary national cap will not work and I think the Conservatives are not even giving the number for that cap so they can't tell us what they will do. Take the controls we are putting in, add to the points system, restrict more occupations in future where we can train up people to have the skills. My vision of Britain moving forward is we train the young people up to get the skills for the future and we will need less people to come in to meet the skill shortages of the past.

The lady makes an important point, there are people who come out of our armed services who do have difficulties and that is why we should be putting money into the armed forces charities that help homeless people and people also with mental health problems when they come out of our armed services.

You haven't done it. Well, why don't we, instead of dealing with perception, we deal with the reality. And if you doubt the position that I'm saying on the intelligence, as I say, the great thing is you can go and read it for yourself and make up your own mind about that. But I like to think that also there are certain changes we've made in this country that we can be proud of as a government. I don't disagree with that.
I've signed the Citizens UK pledge for a counsellor in every school to help with young people's mental health and with youth violence. Will the others on the panel today join me?

David and Nick are not addressing the question we face now: if you shrink the economy now, if you contract it, if you make the mistake of the 1930s, you lose jobs, you lose growth, you lose businesses. We've got to support the recovery until it's fully established. Then my deficit reduction plan, which is a four-year plan, comes into place. But take money out of the economy now, David, for ideological reasons, and you put the recovery at risk. I do fear an emergency Tory budget in a few weeks' time, putting the very work we've done to secure the recovery in jeopardy, and no other country in the world is prepared to do that now.

Thanks, Carly, for this question, because I think it goes to the heart of the dysfunction in a democracy at the moment. Brexit has brought it to the fore, but it was deeper than that. It was already there. And, you know, we've got a 19th-century political system which is unable to address the 21st-century problems. And, you know, no party has a monopoly on the truth. We'll all have some valid perspectives, and we have to find a more collaborative system. We have to adopt the sort of Citizens' Assembly approach positive in Ireland as they looked at the change of the constitution over rape. There are different mechanisms whereby we can have better conversations with each other and as a result of that we'll get better solutions for all of us.

Look, there are rules in place, and they've got to be enforced, so that when people come here from other countries, we collect the money from them. But Nigel, the thing you've got to answer is this. Because people at home need to know this. You don't want the National Health Service. You want a private insurance system of health care. You've said it on the record. You've said you want to retire. And just to come in on something that you, Ed Miliband. That's your real agenda. And you'll support David Platt with his plans to privatise the NHS. And Ed Miliband. You cannot conduct this debate.

Well, trust is an issue. But then people are going to have to decide you know, are we to be trusted when we say we've run a strong economy, and we'll continue to do so with low interest rates and low unemployment and low inflation. I said that we would put investment in to the public services, we've have. You go and look at any school or hospital, you look at this city here, and how it's been transformed in the past few years. Look at the investment in, in the local regeneration of an inner city such as, as Leeds. Now, if you're talking about Iraq, yes, there's an issue because people, some people feel they were misled over the war and some people powerfully disagree with the, the reason why we went to war.

Well, no. We support the current planned levels of spending on our military over the term of the next Parliament to 2020. There are important roles for our military in self-defence and an important role in UN peacekeeping. Instead of being the world's policeman with America, as we have been over recent decades, we should be taking our part in terms of UN peacekeeping around the world. But I particularly want to focus on the point about ISIS in that region. Because what's really important is regional countries are feeding ISIS. Money is coming through them through countries in the region. What we need is strong diplomatic efforts to put pressure on that region. And we also need to stop pumping our own arms into that region, particularly like countries like Saudi Arabia, which are huge human rights abusers, hugely fueling conflict in the region. That needs to stop.

Well, I'm just listening with open mouth to what Jeremy Corbyn had to say about what he's done to stamp out anti-Semitism in the Labour Party. Because I'm afraid it's a complete failure of leadership, what's happened in the Labour Party with anti-Semitism. But the failure of leadership is even worse when you look at what is happening on their Brexit policy. Mr Johnson, before we go any further on this, what the people of this country want to hear is clarity about whether he believes in the deal that he is proposing to do.

Last week, a middle-aged Asian man on his way home from work was abused and called racist names and called a terrorist. He had just finished 50 hours saving lives after the Manchester bomb. He was a doctor. And that is what happens when you demonize immigrants. That is what happens when the language... So, does immigration cause pressures that need to be met? Do we need to invest more in infrastructure to make sure we invest the taxes that immigrants pay to make sure that we provide for everybody? Of course, yes. Do we need, as I'm afraid the Prime Minister has done for the last seven years, first as Home Secretary and then Prime Minister, set completely varmi, bogus targets that she fails to meet every single year, No we don't. What we need is an immigration policy that is good for Britain, good for our businesses, good for our farmers, good for tourism, good for our health service, a wise immigration policy, good for Britain, not a concerted immigration policy written to appease UKIP.

Richard, by taking the position that he is, Jeremy Corbyn shows that he's a bystander, not a leader. But can I ask you this? Do you think it is possible for Jeremy Corbyn to negotiate a Brexit deal that is better for our country than remaining in the European Union?

But Mrs Merkel, who is the real boss in Europe, as we all know, has made it perfectly clear you can negotiate lots of things over the next couple of years, but you cannot renegotiate the free movement of peoples within the European Union and that is backed up by the Commission President and the President of the European Council and the overwhelming majority of the European Parliament. Do you accept or not that in your renegotiation free movement is not up for discussion?

Corbyn needs to catch up. The Conservatives and Liberal Democrats passed the Health and Social Care Act after 2010 and that has led to £10 billion of private sector involvement in the NHS, with often different wage structures and a difficulty in management. Labour will end privatisation with the NHS and return it to what it was always intended to be, a fully funded, fully functioning public health service in this country.

Well, look, on the biology, I agree with what Tony Blair said the other day in relation to men having penises and women having vaginas. But what I will say is... So, you've changed your position? Just, on the biology, that doesn't help on the gender. There are some people who don't identify with the gender that they're born into. And they go through a lot of anxiety and distress. And my view in life is to respect and give dignity to everyone, whatever their position. And I will always do that. And I was worried at the time, and you referenced that particular debate, by the way in which the debate was being conducted, because it got very toxic, very divided, very hard line, and we lost sight of people in that. So much so that that drags you to a place where we end up, as we did end up, with the Prime Minister of the United Kingdom making a trans joke in Parliament when the mother of a murdered trans teenager was watching on. That is a shocking place to get to.
The weapon is there and I would say no first use of the weapon and I think that has to be the basis on what we do. Adam, does that answer your question?

Can I just perhaps put the polls into context? All of the polls suggest the SNP is on track to win this election in Scotland fairly convincingly. Now, I take nothing for granted. I don't take a single vote for granted. But I think we have to see that in context. But the point that was made by the gentleman here about the responsibilities of the Scottish Government. Now, I don't stand here and say that we don't have challenges to address. But I was hearing, just as I was waiting to come on, the discussion about the dementia tax. In Scotland, we support free personal nursing care so that people have less burden on their personal assets when they need care. Our NHS, if you take our accident and emergency departments, beds performing of any of the NHS systems in the whole of the UK by a considerable distance. Health spending in Scotland per head of population is 7% higher than it is in the rest of the UK. We have more doctors, nurses, health professionals than anywhere else in the UK. So I think we do a good job on these things but of course we have to continue to make sure we address the challenges we face.

Well, Frances, you're right to ask the question. We're spending less on defence than we were in the last year of the Labour government, pro rata. The army has shrunk from 100,000 to 72,000. Recruitment is catastrophic. So rather than having a weird concept of national service, where 30,000 youngsters would do a year, we need to recruit 30,000 people into the army full-time. And yes, the veterans point's been made already. Respect our veterans, including those with the average age of 100 who were deserted by the Prime Minister in Normandy yesterday, which I think was a complete and utter disgrace and shows us we actually have a very unpatriotic Prime Minister. It was dreadful.

Before I start, I want to pay tribute to the bravery of our emergency services, and my thoughts are with everybody affected by this afternoon's horrific terrorist attack. My dad taught me always to ask questions, to challenge the way things are and work to change them for the better. And I believe things can be better. That we can remain in the European Union and have more money to spend on improving your life right now and more to invest in our children's future. Imagine a country where you are valued for who you are, no matter what God you pray to or none, how much money you have, the colour of your skin or who you love. where we nurture the bonds in our family of nations, our United Kingdom, where we work with our closest friends to save our planet, where we look after the most vulnerable and our children have the best start in life. If you share my vision for our country, if you want to stop Brexit and build a brighter future, vote Liberal Democrat.

That's £46 billion. £46 billion has just been spent in the last minute without saying where that money is coming from. That money on National Insurance is used for our NHS and for our pensions. So, Prime Minister, if you're going to cut that money, that National Insurance, those £46 billion, is it the NHS or is it our pensioners that are going to pay for it?

That is not in our manifesto. That is not a choice that we are making. It will not be unveiled tomorrow. There's no surprises in our manifesto, because our manifesto is a manifesto for growth. It's about how we tackle our planning laws, how we get the skills for our young people, how we set up great British energy. This is going to be a publicly owned energy company so that taxpayers' money that goes into investing in energy comes back to the taxpayer. OK, so, Kit, the reason I...

I will look into that. The purpose of the target, just so you know, is that it used to be the case that people would phone the GP and not be able to get an appointment for a week or two weeks when they wanted it quickly. I agree, the target obviously should not work in that way. That is a perfectly sensible point. I will have a look at it. It obviously should not work like that. The purpose of these targets just so that I explain the situation, is to try and make sure that you get minimum quality and standards and are seen quickly. Now, obviously, it should not work in the opposite way.

Our electricity prices are 60% higher than most of our competitors. We've done it through green taxes and guess who pays? The average family of four pays an extra £130 a year for their heating, increasing fuel poverty and rich landowners get given

No I wouldn't because I think the private sector couldn't do that job. I want people to get the best deal they can in this country. Everyone in this country. And if they can get it from the private sector rather than the state, then I think they should be allowed to. All I'm interested in is getting the best deal for the people of Britain and I think if you ask people what they're interested in, they'll tell you they want the best service they can get. They want the best health care they can get.

We would slash a foreign aid budget which is costing the British people £30 million every single day. And we would transfer that money to the National Health Service and that's why, because we're prepared
Well it will be lower under the Conservatives than it will be under Labour

Do you know how many inquiries we've had? You decide the wording every time. The Hutton inquiry was a six-month inquiry. The Foreign Affairs Committee decided their own terms of reference, so did the Intelligence Committee. We have had four inquiries, we could have another four. And frankly, after that, we could have another four.
The problem with what Mr Johnson just said is that he said exactly the opposite when he spoke to a DUP conference. You said there would be no border down the Irish Sea. Well, there is!

Leanne Wood. Plaid Cymru won't go along with the scapegoating of migrants. It was not Polish care workers or Estonian bar workers who caused this economic crisis. It was bankers. And we shouldn't allow the rhetoric that blames immigrants for all of our ills. There's a reason why there's a strong anti-immigration feeling in areas where there is little immigration and that's because often those areas have not shared in the wealth that has been generated. There are gaps in the Welsh economy and there are certain skills that we need but sadly the debate around immigration has stopped those gaps being filled. We talked earlier on about the Welsh NHS. We have fewer doctors per head of the population in Wales than other parts of the EU and this immigration debate has not helped that problem, it's exacerbated it. I would like to hear more about that.

if you want to deal. I don't make a working assumption about it, what I do is I concentrate on the bits that are absolutely vital to concentrate on, which is - hang on, just let me finish, which is the numbers who are coming in, the numbers we're removing and the backlog. The only long term way of dealing with this issue however, is to introduce the proper controls of borders through an electronic visa regime, and the other thing is identity cards.

I agree with the questioner. Immigration in this country has been too high for too long. That's why we have a very clear approach to cut it, and cut it substantially. In these last 13 years, over a million extra people have been given citizenship. That's like another city of Birmingham all over again. We say you need to control it properly. That's why we say that new countries that join the European Union, they should have transitional controls so not everyone can come here at once, and when it comes to immigration from outside the European Union for economic reasons, we believe there needs to be a cap. I want us to get back to a situation where the net number of people coming into our country is in the tens of thousands, not, as it's been in recent years, in the hundreds of thousands. One of the benefits of that, and it is an important benefit, is that we can better integrate people into our country, build a stronger society, and we wouldn't hear on the doorstep or on the streets as we go about this election campaign people worried about immigration, because they'd know their government had listened to them, gripped it, and got it under control. That's exactly what I'll do.

The 50% best paid people in this country would be paying 95% of the burden. But this is a colossal problem and either we solve it or we don't. And the Liberal Democrats have a plan to solve it and we're going to be honest with you and tell you how we'll pay for it.

Anti-Semitism is an absolute evil and scourge within our society. Racism in any form is a scourge in our society. I have taken action in my party where anyone has committed any anti-Semitic acts or made any anti-Semitic statements. They are either suspended or expelled from the party, and we've investigated every single case. We do take this very, very seriously indeed, because I do not want to live in a society where racism is rife. I understand and recognize the desperate history of the Jewish people in the 20th century, which came about from an unrestriction of anti-Semitism in the 1920s. We've got to stand up against racism in any form in our society.

I'm not going to make a false promise to you, Leanne, on the stage tonight, because it's not responsible. That's the reason. Because we have a deficit that we have to get down. We do want to see fair funding for Wales. And we've said that. But you know, look, it does go to the big issue at this election, which is, can you believe the promises that are made? And aren't people just going to promise you the earth? Now, you've got different people on the stage tonight who want to make the case for different parts of the United Kingdom. I want to stand up for working people in every part of the United Kingdom. And I think it will be damaged, frankly, by trying to break up our country. All right. Natalie Bennett.

I think it's incumbent on the Prime Minister of this country and a party in an election to explain to people how they will keep them safe if we form a government. And on this issue, the Prime Minister, as I said, has been a long-standing critic of letting people, violent criminals, out of jail before their sentence is due. He spoke about this three weeks after taking office.
Stop peddling hate.

Yeah, can I first agree with you that work is, of course, about pay and being able to pay your own way and, you know, support your family. But it is also about dignity and respect. And my dad worked in a factory all his life. He was a toolmaker. And having a secure job for him was really important to his respect and integrity. In relation to those that are out of work, my rule is this. If you can work, you should work. And we've got a plan to get people into work, working with businesses to support people in. I think we need to change the job centre. and the career service, because at the moment, when I ask businesses, if you have a vacancy, are you going to the job centre to get that vacancy filled? They say they're not, so we need to do that. The other thing, Mary, that we absolutely need to do is to get the waiting lists down in the NHS, because very many people aren't able to work or to work in the way they want. because they're waiting for an operation because of the catastrophic level of waiting lists under this government.

First of all, to be very clear also, we would do nothing to prop up a Tory government in any way at all. But what voters are increasingly doing is looking towards voting green. In the last election in 2010, about 1 in 100 people voted green. The polls are showing that at least 1 in 20, and maybe many more, will vote for the kind of policies we're putting forward for the fair economy, for a publicly owned and run NHS in which the profit motive has no place, and for tackling the pressing issue of climate change that, yet again, we've not heard any other person mentioned on this panel tonight. A strong group of Green MPs can build on the brilliant work of Caroline Lucas. We prepared to work with Ed on a vote-by-vote basis, but as Leanne and Nicola have said, what we need is a much stronger alternative to the Tories, and sadly that's not what Labor is offering.

Dennis, you're right, the NHS is in crisis and the Conservatives' answer of a workforce plan doesn't help when they're refusing to pay healthcare workers properly. They're pouring water into a leaky bucket because people are leaving the service for better pay and better conditions elsewhere. It also doesn't help what Labour are proposing, opening the door to more privatisation in our health service. The answer is investment. And that's why the Green Party is clear that we would make transformative investment into not only the NHS, but also social care, which helps take the pressure off the NHS. The Green Party will always stand for an NHS for people, not profit.
And the points. And the points system that we, we're introducing will make sure that only people get work permits, who actually come in ¿

I didn't go to D-Day. I think what happened was very wrong. I think the prime minister has apologized for that. But what I also think is important is we honor their legacy. They fought for our freedom. Unless we are spending the right amount on defense, we can't honor that legacy. The baseline for our equipment program is 2.5 percent. If you're not committed to spending that over the period it needs to be spent, you are not funding defense. And I have to just mention also The cornerstone of our defence is our nuclear deterrent. And you need more than submarines, sailors and warheads to deliver that. You need credibility. And it's too late for Labour on credibility. Angela Rayner voted recently, along with the guy that wants to be your Foreign Secretary and half the Labour frontbench, to end our nuclear deterrent. If you are doubting that they would use that force, and she might be prime minister in four or five years' time, if you are doubting that she or David Lammy or Keir Starmer would use that, imagine what Putin is thinking. Without credibility, we become a target. If we become a target, you are less safe. It's too late for this generation of Labour politicians. That credibility is shot do not vote these people in.

We will not... We will not do unfunded spending commitments because... We're talking about the poorest families. Why not fund them, Angela? Why not fund them? Just let me finish. Because unless we can identify where that money's coming from, we have identified... Just identify where the problem is. Poor children living in poverty. Can you let me finish? Yeah, that's why housing... response to poverty so does people's work we've already put things in place that we said we won't do unfunded spending commitments but we've put free breakfast clubs in schools because actually as a child that was on free school meals as I was growing up I had to wait to dinner time to get fed.

Thank you, Nicola Sturgeon. Nicola Sturgeon is absolutely right. You have a choice in the two largest parties here between austerity heavy and austerity light. and I think we need to, we've been talking, David Cameron's been talking a lot about the jobs he's created and let's think about the state of employment in Britain today. One in five workers, more than 20% of workers is on less than a living wage. People have been forced into self-employment not by choice because there's nothing else, 80% of people in self-employment are living in poverty.

Let me repeat again. There is a problem that you have created of people who came here illegally because of the chaos that your Conservative and Labour governments created in the immigration system. We have to deal with it. It would be much easier for me to pretend it's not a problem, as David Cameron and Gordon Brown are doing. It is a problem. It's helping criminals. I don't want to help criminals. I want to get those people into the hands of the taxman. And if they shouldn't be here, of course they should be deported. But neither David Cameron or Gordon Brown even know how to do that. So let's get real with the way the world is, rather than the way we would like to think it should be.

It's our process that is being looked at and I'm very happy about that. We've laid ourselves completely open to it. We're the party that after all set up the Equality and Human Rights Commission and in government we will fund it properly and I think it's right that our processes should be examined. We're the only party that actually has a full process to deal with abuse, racism in any form. within our party or our society is simply not acceptable. We have suspended and expelled members that have been guilty of any racist activity.

I think it is profoundly misguided. Nick has talked, not tonight, but has talked about 600,000 people having this amnesty, being able to stay. And they would be able to bring over a relative each, so that's 1.2 million potentially. And all of those people would have access to welfare and housing. I think this could make...

This is ludicrous. The Labour Party clearly don't understand the difference between buying and selling. The NHS buys lots of products, beds, curtains, sheets, drugs, all sorts of things from private providers. Nothing is for sale in the NHS. It provides incredible frontline care and great outcomes. I would say to Lucy, the NHS is not failing. But what we do need to do, as well as investing more money, we have to manage it better, that's crucial, and we have to cut out the waste. My mother sadly died in April and it absolutely broke my heart and broke the heart of the nurse who came to collect the drugs that were still unopened. The seals hadn't been broken. And the nurse wept as she said, we have to literally pay to bin these drugs. We can't reuse them in this country. We can't give them to third world countries. That is a lack of common sense. Well, let's get better. We need to manage it better. Understood. Cut out the waste. And we need to stop the drugs. But what we do need to do is we need to stop the drugs companies from ripping off the NHS.

Where is Radley? I could hear his voice...ah, there you are. Sorry, I had this problem last time. Of course you're right. We are there to serve you. And of course, we are there to respond to people's concerns. I hear people's concerns about immigration all the time. The reason I think people have become so anxious is because the immigration system itself, the way it works has become utterly chaotic under a succession of Labour and Conservative Governments who talked tough about immigration, but for instance, it was Conservative and Labour Government that removed the exit controls which I want to see introduced, so we don't know only who's coming in, but who should be leaving as well. I think there are additional things we need to do. I do think we need to have a dedicated border police force. I think we have to have a regional approach so that if people come and work here, they're not allowed to work in regions where there isn't work for them to do or there is an unreasonable strain on public services. And we need to deal with the criminal gangs who've been exploiting the illegal immigrants who came in because of the chaos in the system. That is a fair, effective, workable approach, dealing with something which is of immense public concern.

Well, there is more money going into the NHS today than there has been in its history. And I'm sure BBC Verify after that, this debate, will verify that for you. But that is the fact. But I don't dismiss your concerns. Well, inflation might have something to do with that, but yes. I absolutely acknowledge your concerns. You and your colleagues are working very hard. You were doing more than we've ever done before, but the situation is challenging because we are dealing with the backlog of missed appointments that you are very well aware of, and I'm sure you were working hard during COVID. You saw what didn't happen during that period, but you talked about parents and children. I'll give you one practical example of something that we're doing that is already making a difference. Now, Because of the changes that we've made, if your child has an ear infection, a sore throat, sinusitis, instead of having to make a GP appointment, you can now go direct to your pharmacist to get the medicines that you need. That change that we have made is making an enormous difference already. saving parents an enormous amount of time, making sure their children get the care and the medicines they need much quicker. And if I'm re-elected, we will expand that Pharmacy First programme to cover more different illnesses. And again, these things all add up. No one thing is going to solve the problem, but it's just a demonstration to you of my commitment to making sure you and everyone else has an NHS that they can rely on, having the peace of mind that it's there for them when they need.

You know, if you're asking about why you can trust me in particular, I'll tell you. Actually, very simply, it's because my grandparents came to this country with very little. And in two generations, I'm standing here as your Prime Minister. So this country did something extraordinary for my family. And I feel an enormous debt of gratitude to repay that. And I'm in this so that I can make the same difference to as many people as possible as the difference that this country made to my family. So I can happily talk to you about lots of policies like national service, helping your children get onto the housing ladder with cuts to stamp duty, getting them great apprenticeships. But fundamentally, I'm in this because of what this country has done for my family, and I will work very hard to repay that and make as many people's lives better in the way that my life has been transformed by being here.

Well, the papers that were leaked the other day do set out what the evidence is, the fact that these discussions have already been happening. And I'm going to be pretty blunt here. When Boris Johnson says that the NHS is not on the table in a future trade deal, I simply do not trust him, and I do not believe him on that point.

Look, I can appreciate people's frustrations. Of course, we haven't got everything right. I don't think any government does. And I know it's been very difficult for many people. But what I can do is work as hard as I can to deliver the stability that I said I would. And I've had this job for 18 months. And I think, particularly on the economy, As I'm sure we'll get into, people can see that inflation is back to normal, the economy is growing, wages are rising, and that's why we're having this election now, to talk about the future. And yes, I've got a track record over the past 18 months, which I'm sure we'll talk about, but also I want to build on that and continue the progress we've made, whether it's cutting people's taxes, bringing down migration, or indeed protecting people's pensions. These are all things that now we're in a position to make more progress on.

First of all to say that the way to really prevent and detect these offences is with a community who doesn't feel discriminated against working alongside the police in a cooperative way and we do need intelligence-led stronger policing in the community. I work in London in the London Assembly I know that police cuts are real the police do not have time to get out of their vans into the community and and work with people in a way that would really prevent this. Now when it comes to people who've been recruited, who've been exploited, who are vulnerable to this kind of political ideology, we do need to have proper rehabilitation for them while they're in prison. It's no good just leaving people to fester for longer in an overcrowded prison system. We need real rehabilitation and it breaks my heart that Jack Merritt, who was murdered was actually working on this exact thing of education and rehabilitation. Those are the things we need to be funding more of, as well as real policing in the community that doesn't just alienate people. OK, and Adam Price?

I may address that directly because it was pointed towards me, that we should not be grateful in Scotland for a headquarters, it is our energy. 100,000 jobs are on the line as a result of your policy, you should be apologising.
Even Liz Truss, on her worst day, still recognised that we need a nuclear deterrent in this country, Angela, so she's still got the edge on you.

First of all, child tax credit we increased by £450. And it's not going to fall? Not going to fall. Child benefit? to me is one of the most important benefits there is. It goes directly to the family, normally to the mother. £20 for the first child, £14 for the second. It is the key part of families' budgets in this country. Now that's not what we need to change. What we need to change is, again, what did I inherit? A system where if you worked an extra hour or an extra shift, you could sometimes find you actually lost money from that extra hour or that extra shift. So universal credit that we're introducing, coming in now, means every hour you work, you keep more of the money that you earn. And we're going to introduce that right across the country. That will save money and form part of the welfare savings we need to make.

Well, if, for example, it passed an assessment in relation to energy security and also in relation to the sourcing of alternative products, that might well be a justifiable case. But that would have to be assessed rigorously in a climate compatibility assessment. And when you say energy security, it would be sold on the open market. It wouldn't make us any more secure, would it? We're also taking into account all of the issues about the utilisation of that oil supply, if we use oil as an example. So there would have to be a comprehensive assessment to do that. Now, the Prime Minister has said that he would authorise 100 new oil and gas licences. I think that's completely and utterly irresponsible. It is essentially to deny the climate emergency and I would have nothing to do with that. But what I do want to make sure is that we can work with the oil and gas sector to support it in making the transition to net zero because we will actually need lots of the skills in the oil and gas sector to be available to help us with the transition to net zero and that's a key part of how that agenda would be taken forward.

Well, Ian, specifically on bonuses, I would say we need to do the following. Firstly, it sounds draconian, but I think it's now necessary, we should say no bonuses whatsoever for the directors of banks at board level. Why do I say that? Because I don't want people who are actually running those businesses, which they should be running for the long-term interests of the business and, indeed, for their clients, to be kind of susceptible to the temptations of the bonus incentive. By all means, pay them lots of money, give them a fancy membership of a golf club, but don't give them these bonuses. Then I would say absolutely no cash bonuses at all above £2,500. And finally, I don't think banks which are making losses should be handing out multibillion pound bonuses at all, full stop. No bonuses in banks who make a loss. No bonuses for people at director level, and no cash bonuses above £2,500. That's specific, it's tough, but it'll finally root out this outrageous abuse of bankers' bonuses.

Well, as an observer, it'll be impossible, I think, for Ed Miliband to be Prime Minister without Nicola Sturgeon's 35 or 40 MPs. And that is something, I think, very deeply concerning to a lot of English voters. They're very, very worried about the Scottish tail wagging the English dog. I'm not sure I'm not sure that's what we're going to finish up with. Well, that's the reality. Scotland is 10% of the House of Commons chamber, but may well, in coalition with Ed Miliband, lead to a situation where we continue with the Barnett formula, because Ed has promised to keep that. So I think English voters are worried about that. My view, David, for UKIP is this. I would happily say to you that UKIP could have worked with a Labour party that believed in the British people having a say on the greatest constitutional question of the day. They've turned their backs on it. The Tory government...

You need a just transition, I agree with that. But offshore wind right now is about half the cost of nuclear power, and yet these two parties here are still investing in more expensive nuclear power. That doesn't make sense. I think working together is a really good thing, but you've got to be working together for the right things. So I don't think it was right for Joe Swinson and the Liberal Democrats to be in coalition with David Cameron's Conservatives and impose austerity and welfare cuts and vote for things like the bedroom tax. So let's work together to do the right things

We're not in principle opposed to right to buy but as I say I don't think the Tory plan works because there's no money for it and all that is going to mean is the social housing stock being watered down and even fewer homes to rent.

Hang on, hang on. Actually, if we reduce foreign aid to 0.2%, as I said earlier, it'll be the same as the United States under Obama. The point is... No, it's stopping refugees coming in. This is about the law hanging fruit. There's no need to put up taxation to pay for this. We can simply take it from the foreign aid budget and put British people first. Charity begins at home.

I wanted dialogue in Northern Ireland during the 1970s and 1980s. I did make contact with Sinn Féin when they were not allowed to travel, their leadership was not allowed to travel to Britain, for example. I wanted there to be a process. And that peace process came about by the actions of people such as John Hume, such as Gerry Adams, such as David Trimble, who eventually brought about the ceasefires with both governments, both the Conservative and later with the Tony Blair-led Labour government. And that brought about the Good Friday Agreement, which respects all the historical traditions of Ireland, which is obviously fundamental to bringing about peace. And I think we should all be pleased that we've achieved a great deal through the Good Friday Agreement. And do you know what? That Good Friday process, recognising those differences, has become a bit of a model for peace processes around the world, where people who were not talking to each other, worse than that, now travel the world together in order to promote peace and dialogue in the Middle East, in Colombia and in the past in South Africa. I think we should be pleased about those achievements.

That really helps a lot of people stay in employment. that local councils have the resources to do this. We've just established this new fund, the Better Care Fund, that starts on the 1st of April. It's got £5 billion in it to try and bring health and social care together more in our country. And that could help with some of the issues that you're raising. But thank you for the question.

Caroline Lucas. Well, I agree on that last point, a second one, which is a bit scary. But I do have real concerns about the policy that the Conservatives have just announced today. And there are real concerns that if you have dementia, for example, if you might be unlucky enough to have those big costs, then there's nothing really going to protect you from that. Instead of having a system which could have pooled risk, instead that risk is being put onto the individual.

Your party opposed the formation of the NHS in the first place. It is factually so, but the more important thing is that your party has presided over cuts in our NHS, increased waiting times and waiting lists, a record number of people delayed in getting appointments and a social care system that is in crisis.
You're on the record as saying that you saw the case for replacing our National Health Service with some kind of American-style insurance.

Well, we're saying in our manifesto that we'll invest another billion on top of the £11.4 billion into mental health. And, of course, also look at the Mental Health Act to make sure the legislation is fit for purpose, as well as getting people back into work. Our target is a million people with disabilities, whether they're mental health issues or physical, back into the... Jonathan Bartley, what would the Green Party do?

And it goes on to talk about the - asylum seekers arriving in the UK and other EU member states, could be transferred to a transit processing centre where their claims could be assessed, that centre would be located outside the EU. Now the difference between Mr Blair and me is that he talks about things but he doesn't do them. He starts things but he doesn't finish them. He pussy foots around.

Ed Miliband. I'll just say on this, Leanne. My two sons were born in a PFI hospital. It was an old, falling down hospital. But you're right, there need to be limits to privatisation. That's why we said we'll roll back the tide of privatisation that is starting under David Cameron and is about to go much, much further. We'll cap the profits of the private sector. want to come back to David Cameron. He said he's protected the NHS and he said he kept his word. David, you made a whole series of promises at the last general election to people. You said no top-down reorganisation, you didn't listen to the staff and that's exactly what happened. You said no going back to the days when people had to wait hours on end in A&E. That's exactly what happened. I don't think people will take seriously your promises on the NHS because you broke all the promises you made at the last general

Well, I just want to say it is unfortunate there isn't a single question to which the answer for Paul Nuttall isn't immigration. But on the substance of what we're talking about here... Paul Knuckle says it's going to help people like Alnus to get rid of the so-called green levers. Actually, the green levers are shifting us to much cheaper fuel sources. It is cheaper now to get your fuel from wind and from the sun than it is from fossil fuels. So the idea that that's going to help us is wrong. But let me say something about rent, because you mentioned that too. And we want to see rent controls. Rents are spiralling out of control right now. Private rent people, private landlords, are basically taking all of this money. We want to see rent controls that would stop that, which would mean that people could afford a decent rent, and they could then afford to save up for a deposit. Let me bring in... Right now, it's the opposite. Thank you. And also... Thank you, Caroline. Sorry to conclude you there.

Well, look, as I said a moment ago, if the union is as strong as some people say it is, then how can Scotland be in a position of facing being dragged out of the European Union against our will? We stand at a crossroads right now. Down one path, Scotland has the prospect of our future being decided for us by Boris Johnson and Nigel Farage, or we can have the option of deciding a different future as an independent country where we're in charge of our own destiny. I think that is a much brighter prospect. On the question of Labour, I'm not asking Richard Burgon or Jeremy Corbyn to support independence but I would be asking them if the SNP hold the balance of power to respect Scotland's right to choose, to respect the fact that that is a decision not for Westminster but for the people of Scotland and I don't think they'd turn their back on the chance to govern just to block Scotland's right to choose.

Just to pick up a couple of points that have come up. On public service investment, just to be clear about that, the NHS is receiving more money than it has ever received in history. Not per page. In terms of the economy, we had the highest growth in the G7 Q1 this year. Let me address the question about migration. We have about 6 or 7 million, maybe even 8 million, UK citizens of working age who are not active in the workforce. Now some of those have caring responsibilities or there are good reasons why they're not working, but for others of them, there are reasons why they could get into the workforce. So priority number one is to encourage those UK citizens here already to get into the workforce and Mel Stride, the Welfare Secretary, has set out some plans to get that done. So that is priority number one. Now, of course, where there are skill gaps, like particularly for high skilled jobs, scientific researchers, IT professionals, people working in financial services, doctors in the NHS, nurses as well. Of course those are people we would like to welcome here, but it needs to be in the areas where there are genuine finished Chris Richard, in areas where there are genuine skill shortages and people with high levels of skills who will add value to our economy. So that is the strategy that's being pursued. Now the levels of net migration we've recently seen have been frankly too high and that's why specific measures have already been taken, not in the future, they've already been taken that will get those numbers down by 300,000 and we've seen that drop in visa applications already. So we're going to get hang on we're going to get the right balance. We don't want mass low-skilled immigration. It's not good for the economy.
Nobody should... Nobody should suffer any abuse in public life or privately.

People say that politics doesn't change anything, but it can. It was a radical Labour government that created our National Health Service and rebuilt Britain. Today, we must be ambitious on the same scale. The future really is ours to make together. We can tackle the climate emergency, end child poverty, and properly fund our National Health Service. On Thursday, you can choose hope and vote for real change. Thank you, Mr Corbyn.

So, I mean, I think about my childhood and those types of birds that you would see in the garden. I think about bringing my children up and I want them to be able to see that kind of wildlife. Hedgehogs, I remember having a little hedgehog box in the garden and putting out food for hedgehogs. They've halved the hedgehog population in the last 20 years. I don't think my five-year-old has actually seen a hedgehog. We need to protect our amazing wildlife and biodiversity. So what do we need to do? strict legal targets in a Nature Act where we have very clear legally binding targets. We need to be able to enforce them. We'd have a new Office of Environmental Protection and we need to invest. So we do need to plant those trees. We do need that to be in national parks. We also need to look at how we do that within our urban spaces and greening our cities as well, which has an obvious benefit in terms of reducing carbon dioxide by taking that out of the atmosphere and public health benefits, making those places greener and more pleasant for people to be in. And that's, I think, one of the key things about this. This is something which makes people's lives better. Of course it's good for the environment, it's good for us in the longer term, but so many of the measures that we can take will make the places we live more pleasant places to be.

But, David, the biggest unfairness is that the biggest beneficiary from your manifesto is the 3,000 richest people in the country who would get £200,000 each from an inheritance tax cut. Now, if you want to be fair, you don't give people an inheritance tax and then cut child tax credits for middle-class families. You don't cut the child trust fund. You don't cut the school's budget. You don't have your do-it-yourself society, which means you cut the school's budget.

You can have more money, but you can't have more power. the police and security services, what they want more than anything else is resources to catch people. There's also, though, a point made here. What do the terrorists want us to do? To turn in on ourselves and be divided as a country. What do they want us to do? To give up on our freedoms and our liberties. And those are the things we must not sacrifice, otherwise the terrorists would have won.

Well, absolutely, 11 billion pounds. We know where we're going to get it from. We know we're going to get it from Brexit, from not paying our EU membership fees. That's 10 billion a year. And we're also going to cut the overseas development budget back to the useful part of it and not spend the wasteful part of it giving money to countries with space programs and nuclear weapons, rather than spending it here at home on hospitals, on mental health care, and on investing in our doctors and nurses. And we will be able to. re-give out the bursaries to nurses and midwives, and we'll be able to pay back the student fees of students doing STEM subjects, studying medicine, so that if you do study medicine, pharmacy, science, and things like that, you will get your fees paid back for you if you work in the NHS for 10 years after you've graduated. So I think that's a very good deal that we will give. We're the best on the NHS.

Yes, sorry, what was it? I couldn't quite hear. Is it Beverly? Beverly. Beverly, I actually believe in a compassionate welfare system that supports everyone who genuinely needs it. And you saw me do that during COVID, making sure that we've got support to the most vulnerable. That will always happen under a Conservative government, always happened under me. But what we won't do, is have a system where people are not supported into work if they can work. So what we're going to do is make sure that we reform the fit note, sick note process, which isn't working. It's signing far too many people off as not fit for work. We're going to look at, when you asked about benefits and sanctions, we're going to provide active support for people into work. But I also think that if people have been offered jobs that they can do, and that they don't take them, then that's not okay. So after 12 months, what I've said is, we will do something about that. If your job coach says to you, look, there's work that you can do, that everyone agrees you can do, there's a job for you, that you're fit enough and well enough to do, and you refuse to take it, I don't think that's fair on everyone else after 12 months of support, so we will tighten that up. I think that's fair for everybody, because fundamentally, I think that those people who can work, should work, that's, I think, fundamental tenet of fairness in our system, which most people would agree with. That's why I think we can save money in the welfare bill, because it's forecast to grow at an extraordinary rate over the next few years.

You look at the businesses we're talking about are actually doing very well. They've done extremely well over the past six years because their tax have been cut a great deal. I think we have to look at the problems of our public services. We have to look at the issues of what kind of economic future we have. We cannot go on being a low-waged economy, underspending on our public services, investing less than any other industrialised country in our future. I would much prefer that we develop a national investment bank and strategy regionally based to improve rail, broadband, road communications, that we improve investment in our industry for the future. We have a lower proportion of manufacturing industry than Germany. We have a lower level of productivity than Germany. We have a lower skill base than Germany. Why is there a difference? Because successive German governments, almost irrespective of which party it is, have been prepared to invest in their infrastructure and their future. I think we should think about that and improve the economy of our own society. And do you know what? That would lead to better economic growth. It would lead to better opportunities for everybody. And I think this is an offer that's important. And I think it's time that we actually invested in our future rather than presided over our decline.

The case for independence doesn't rest solely on Brexit. Brexit is one example, it's a very extreme example of the way in which Scotland is treated with contempt often by Westminster. And we've seen that over the past three years. We've tried to compromise, we've tried to find ways to protect Scotland's interests, and that hasn't happened. So I think Scotland does need to make that choice about taking a future into our own hands. And thirdly, because this is a very good question about, you know, will independence be as difficult as Brexit? And, you know, I... You've probably noticed this. I oppose Brexit. I think Brexit is a big mistake. But what I don't think is that the mess that Brexit has become was inevitable. That was down to the fact that the Brexiteers, if I can be undiplomatic for a moment, told a lot of lies, one of them on the side of a bus. It was down... down to the fact that there was no honesty or frankness about the implications, no planning done in advance, and then Theresa May came out with all these contradictory red lines that could never be reconciled. Contrast that with the independence referendum in 2014. Whether you agreed with the proposition or not, there was a very detailed proposition. We had done the work, we had done the thinking, we had a whole transition programme ready to go had the result gone the other way. I don't think we should allow the Brexiteers, who made such a hash of Brexit, to tar all positive change with their brush. They messed it up. It doesn't mean others have to mess it up as well. And you want a confirmatory referendum on Brexit.

Let's refocus us onto the issue of social care, Paul Nuttall. Yeah, I mean, social care has often been the poor relation, hasn't it, to the NHS? I think that Natalie's absolutely right. What we need to do... Will you call me Natalie? I mean, Leanne, sorry.

Well, Janet, thank you for sharing your story. And I'm glad that you've recovered. And I'm sorry for your loss as well. Now, my dad was a GP. My mum was a pharmacist. So I'm speaking as someone who was brought up in the NHS, surrounded by people who dedicated their lives to it. And the evenings in my house, growing up, my parents spoke about how they could provide more care for their patients, like you. And that's how I think about the NHS. And of course it's difficult, and you're right, and we have to be honest. about the damage that Covid has done and the time it will take to recover from that. But we've put record funding in, we're training more doctors and nurses, we're doing more checks and scans, and what I want you to know is that the NHS is safe in my hands. I will always stand up for it to give you and everyone else the peace of mind that it will be there for you and your families when you need it.

Some great words have been said. I mean, utterly, utterly heartbreaking is how all of us must feel. It is impossible to imagine, if you receive that phone call, that a loved one, a daughter, a son, a father has been murdered in a terrorist incident. Utterly impossible to imagine. But we do also have to just recognise the extraordinary bravery, as people have said, of members of the public, And I think that does also remind us that all of us, in our daily lives, we have to remain incredibly vigilant. Because yes, we must keep investing in more police and more technology. But however many police we have, and we need as many of those police as possible on the streets, everywhere, in our towns, villages and cities, making us feel safer. The more police on the streets, the safer we feel, but we must remain vigilant.

So when we look at our National Health Service and we realise that a quarter of the people working in the National Health Service are from overseas, the vast chunk of which are from the European Union, we realise that when we refuse to give those folks in the EU the right to settle here with their children, not only are we doing something utterly immoral, we're doing something that is damaging our own future. And this is the problem. with Theresa May's Brexit plans backed by Jeremy Corbyn, is that she is putting together a plan which appeases the Paul Nuttalls and the Nigel Farages, but damages our children's future. That is why you should have the final say on the Brexit deal, not the politicians.

You see, I warned you at the beginning. I said they were all the same. And what you've seen tonight is the politically correct political class. Oh, they're very keen to be popular on the international stage. They don't understand the thoughts, hopes and aspirations of ordinary people in this country. They are detached. Most of them have never had a job in their lives. What we represent in UKIP is plain-spoken patriotism. We believe in this country. We believe in its people. We think Britain can be a lot better than this. But if you want things to be shaken up and to change properly, you've got to put more UKIP MPs in Westminster. We won two by-elections last year. We can outshine all expectations on 7th May. Let's do it.

I think what the people of Scotland want right now is to get rid of the Tories. The people of Scotland haven't voted Conservative since 1955, and they want to make sure there's not a Conservative government. But this election is, of course, about much more than that, because change has to look like something. We're clear about what change looks like to us. Investment in the NHS, action on the cost of living crisis, making sure that we rejoin the European single market, and, of course, delivering on net zero. The Labour Party do not agree with those things. We can hold their feet to the fire.

Nobody earning below £20,000 will pay the national insurance rise. The reason for the national insurance rise is to ensure our health services, our police and our education, and David can't guarantee funding for police and education that will match what we are doing. That's the reason for the national insurance rise. But nobody below £20,000 will pay it. Six million people in this country receive tax credits, and the Conservatives and Liberals have a plan to reduce tax credits for middle-class families. I come back to this central question about fairness in the tax system. If David wants fairness in the tax system, why does he support this inheritance tax cut for only 3,000 families, worth £200,000 each? The biggest beneficiary of the Conservative manifesto is, as always, the richest estates in the country, and not the ordinary, hard-working people of this country. If the Liberals want to cut child tax credits with the Conservatives, then I can say one thing - I will never form an alliance with a Conservative government that cuts child tax credits.

The response of the people of Manchester was absolutely magnificent. All communities of all faiths came together and I utterly deplore the language that Paul Nuttall uses and the subliminal attack the whole time on people of Muslim faith in what you say. You invited Hamas to the House of Commons and called them your friends.
No, hang on a second. I raised £100,000. I raised £100,000 for the London Taxi Charity to send veterans back to Normandy. That's why I was there. Thank you.

...for civil libertarian grounds, identity cards, compulsory national identity cards, but also and I've been asking the Prime Minister about this, on many occasions over the course of the last six months or so, when you get in to the detail of what it means for you, and for me. Having to go to a test centre, having to pay out a significant amount of money. Problems for old people, the government not even being able to tell us, despite the fact they introduced to this extent, how the system will work and exactly how much it will cost. I think it's a responsible opposition party that at that point says, hold on a minute, this is not a route we should be going down. We'd be far better putting more police on the streets.

I can defend our record, which is clear. 400,000 fewer people are in absolute poverty today than in 2010. Child poverty is higher. 700,000 children are now not living in a workless household. And the best route out of poverty... Child poverty is increasing. The best route out of poverty, heartbreaking as it is, especially when it concerns children, the best way to help people like that is to find a secure and well-paid job for them and their families. Thank you. That's what we're doing with our National Living Wage, which is making sure that wages are rising at the fastest rate for those at the bottom and employment now is at a record high.

Look, first of all, we respect the result of the referendum. I know that the SNP never do, but we do, and that's what we've said on that point. On the second point, on the second point, I absolutely believe that people come to this country and work hard and contribute but we cannot have an economy that relies on just overseas workers. We have to also have an industrial and skills strategy when unemployment is going up in the UK that we can train and fill our skills gaps as well.

Mr Johnson, your closing statement, please. This Thursday we can choose another hung parliament under Mr Corbyn and Nicola Sturgeon with two chaotic referendums or else we can get Brexit done, get out of neutral and get a parliament that works for you and on your priorities. The NHS, the cost of living, 20,000 more police, more cash for our schools. Let's get it done. Let's move on and vote Conservative on Thursday. Thank you, Boris Johnson.

May I ask a direct question to David Cameron? You're proposing, David... You're proposing an additional £12 billion in welfare cuts. Where are those cuts going to fall? Who is going to pay the price of those cuts?

Well, the thoughts of everyone are with the family and loved ones of the two innocent people who lost their lives. It's not true that the only option at the time was automatic release halfway through the sentence. There were other options available. It's now not the case either. But people don't want history lessons after this. terrorist atrocity. What people want is to put victims first and put keeping communities safe first and so we can't avoid asking tough questions in general about the Conservative decision to cut more than 20,000 police, about the chaos in our prison system where there are more and more assaults on prison officers and by the disastrous part privatisation and breakup of our probation service which is looking after a quarter of a million people across our country in order to keep our communities safe.
Yes, because having a strong relationship with our closest partner and ally in the United States is critical for keeping everyone in our country safe.

Well, I think, well, I would probably leave a copy of, since you want a literary effort, a copy of my brilliant Brexit deal, which allows us to come back. Non-political. Non-political. I will wait. Maybe by next year, we will be able to see his own Brexit deal. Non-political, Mr. Johnson. Non-political. What I think, Mr. Corbyn, Mr. Corbyn shares my love of plants and trees. I think maybe some... Maybe your little cardboard buses, maybe. Some dams and jams. Some dams and jams. Some dams and jams.

You are going to take money out of us. Thank you, Mr Brown. Mr Clegg. I'm not sure if you're like me, but the more they attack each other, the more they sound exactly the same. Let's go back to Joel's question. Joel asked, why are you being tested so much? How can all pupils in our schools feel that they're being supported and getting the best out of education? And I come back to this need to combine two things. Firstly, more freedom for teachers and headteachers. Remember this crazy thing I told you about headteachers getting 4,000 pages of instructions by email? And secondly, smaller class sizes, more one-to-one tuition, Saturday morning classes, evening classes, so that you can help those children in particular who perhaps aren't being supported at home as much as anybody else. Because you know, I certainly know from my two sons who go to a school, a local school, excellent state-funded school in my area, if a whole class can move together, then that enriches all children. I think what goes wrong is when classes get so big and classes actually fall apart.

Dennis, thank you for your question. The NHS is quite an important topic for me because I physically wouldn't be standing here if it wasn't for the good work that the NHS did. I was a disabled man for 18 years. I know the importance of our public services but our NHS in particular. The thing the NHS requires more than anything is clear financial support from government and a plan for the future. And I'm afraid that Westminster doesn't offer that hope and that certainty. What's baked in under a future Labour government is £18 billion worth of public sector cuts. That's the equivalent of the entirety of Scotland's health and social care budget. You deserve better and the people of Scotland deserve better, which is why people should vote for the SNP at this election in Scotland.

because people are getting poorer. It's quite simple. I mentioned a few moments ago that GDP, wealth per capita, has fallen for the last six consecutive quarters. And it's very much... And it's been the same. It sort of began with Labour, but it's continued. It's very much a country in which the big corporates and the big banks and a certain group of people have become richer exponentially, And everybody else has fallen behind. And things that perhaps we could have expected when we were younger, our kids and grandkids now find a house unaffordable. Rents have risen by 20%, in some parts 30%, just in the last three years. And I'm sorry, but we are back. to the population explosion and the massive impact it's having on the lives of people. And we've got to try to get the population to steady, and we have to build more houses, and we have to take the poorer people out of tax, increase the tax rate... Thank you very much. ..for £20,000, get people back to work.

Thank you. Great British Energy will be an owned company by the UK taxpayers that will bring energy, clean energy, to the country which will bring down bills and give people good skilled jobs. We've heard from the Tories for 14 years that they were going to do something about skills and manufacturing in this country. I asked the people to look at their record and they constantly spout these lies that we're going to stop people's cars, that we're going to do this, going to do that, when really the reason they say that is their 14 years of abstract failure. And are your green policies going to be in your manifesto? You have failed the British people.
I mean, Robert, you literally abolished the Department for Climate Change. You sold off the Green Investment Bank, and you stopped onshore wind.

I never said that raising energy bills was a way to solve climate change. Let me explain. It was a levy. Well, let me explain. Look, I always said, when I was Energy Secretary, that there will be upward pressures on bills as a result of the need to transition to a green economy. But I also said that we need to make sure the energy market was fair. And you can't use climate change as an excuse for ripping off the consumer. And that is what's been happening with the energy companies. And look, it goes to another big choice at this election. Who's actually going to stand up to the energy companies and say, we are going to freeze bills until 2017 so prices can only fall. We are going to give the regulator powers to cut prices so that actually wholesale price reductions get passed on. Sure, there are upward pressures on bills in the long run. But that makes it all the more important that you reform the market to make it fair.

Well, Almaz, thank you for your question. And for you and for everybody else in this country, I have a long-term economic plan. It's called Stay in the Single Market. The European single market is where we sell half of our goods to. It is vastly significant to the creation of jobs, well-paid jobs, the ability for us to grow as an economy. Since June last year, let's not pretend that things have not got worse. The value of the pound has crashed. The average family is £500 a year worse off now, and we haven't even left yet. We've seen the inflation rate go up already. So I love my country. I want to do what's right by my country. And that is why, when that deal comes to you in the next 18 months, you should have the right to the final say. Because if we can fight and stay in the single market, we can protect incomes, we can protect jobs, we can keep down prices. Let's do the right thing.

Well, we'll get those operations going as quickly as we can with the staff. We've got the models from the London Hospital, the Leeds Hospital. We're asking those teams to go around the country to talk to other hospitals, maybe the one that you work in, to say, this is how we've done it. This is what we would recommend. Now, however long that takes, but we will get that. How long will it take? We will make a start on that absolutely straight away. You've made that clear. Everybody here will either be on a waiting list or know somebody who's on a waiting list. It's incredibly discomforting. And it means people aren't able to go to work, and that's not helping the economy.

Thank you Omar and I agree passionately with what you've said about the NHS. It's absolutely vital that we support it and it is one of the single most beautiful and brilliant things about Britain and about our society and we are determined to fund it not just now but for the long term. In this One Nation Conservative Government we're putting record sums in £34 billion. We are, as I said earlier on, we're not just upgrading 20 hospitals, but we are building 40 new hospitals as a result of the decisions taken by this government. We will invest in GPs.
