We'll have tens of thousands more nurses, 6,000 more GPs. And our objective is to have a health service that is not just better for doctors and nurses, but better for patients as well. Thank you. We can achieve that only if we have a strong economy. Thank you. And I'm afraid, which is what we do.
But don't you think cuts to social services make that situation even worse, put even more people at risk because you've got fewer social workers inspecting?

Well, it's certainly not somewhere that I would suggest that is somewhere that one holds up as a paragon of virtue. I think what the DPRK, what North Korea has been doing in terms of its missiles and its nuclear situation and, you know, we had another ballistic missile test only a few days ago. We're very clear that we want to see those changes in North Korea. I think it's important for China to be influencing those.

Well, we all try to be as green as we can. I do have a car. I use it when I need to. Petrol car? A hybrid car, because when I got it, I lived in a home that didn't have the charging infrastructure. And we've got to improve the charging infrastructure.

Tonight you will see that there is only one party that is going into this election which is truly committed to the Brexit that people voted for on June the 23rd last year. And that is a Brexit whereby we are back in control of our own laws, of our own borders and of our own money. It is also a Brexit whereby we take back full control of our waters and sign free trade deals all over the world. UKIP is also the only party going into this election with a clear and honest commitment to reducing immigration through a policy of balanced migration. You will also see that we are the only party that is committed to cutting a foreign aid budget that is costing you, the British taxpayer, around £30 million every single day. And we want to reinvest that money into our national health service. And it should be a national health service, not an international health service. Finally, Brexit would never have happened if it wasn't for UKIP. We have taken you so far. Elect UKIP MPs and give us the tools to see this through.

First of all, please don't hate me, but my first house cost £32,000, which you're saying is the average deposit. So I can't actually imagine how you look at having a home of your own. And I think that's one of the saddest things, because to have that security of something that's yours, whether that's a long-term secured rent, whether that's a council house or whether that's somewhere you buy. I mean, that's a fundamental human thing to do. And the flip of that is seeing the homeless levels rise and knowing that's to do with government choices around pay, around accessibility, around building houses. So what Labour is looking to do is basically fundamentally build more houses. It's also about getting young people so that they have the skills to build those houses. It's then about having a million new houses over a five-year period and making sure that half of those go into social housing, so whether that's with housing associations or with councils, so that they're there, they're secure and they're an asset of this country. It's also about making sure that you have landlords who are honourable because the level of rent increases is ridiculous. So we're going to make sure that that's capped to inflation. We're also going to make sure that landlords have to provide a safe home so that all of the checks are done for you. We're going to have to try and also make sure that this just getting rid of tenants when they don't want them, or to get more people in and bigger deposits. That's got to stop and we will bring in legislation to make sure that that stops. But again, with everything that Labour's proposing, what we're trying to do is think big, think how we can help everybody to have a secure home and also to help the economy by putting those skills and the money back in the country. So the final thing, you were talking about foreign investors. If people want to invest in this country, fantastic, we'll take their money, but we also want to make sure that they do invest in this country because they are using our roads, they'll be using our education and they'll be benefiting from the economy that we want to see growing.

Well, I just... I don't accept that. I mean, I think, you know, I understand... Is he wrong? ..that they're under pressure. Is he wrong? Well, I think if you look at Lincolnshire, in that county as well, you know, crime has come down because the police are doing a great job, and there's more we need to do, using modern technology, to make sure that crime continues to fall under this government. And if we do that, then we should be saving money as well.

It is about priorities, and isn't it interesting how there are always unlimited billions of pounds to spend on foreign wars. There's five billion pounds now that are going to be spent on refurbishing the palaces of Westminster. Yet we can't put people and health services and social care services first. Now Plaid Cymru's got a costed plan to increase the number of doctors by 1,000 in the Welsh NHS and 5,000 extra nurses. That money is available within the Assembly budget but the problem with that plan now is that if there are to be restrictions on freedom of movement it's going to be very difficult to attract those doctors. Without doctors and without nurses we have no NHS. Let's turn to Paul Nuttall.

When we were in coalition, we earmarked 25 million a year for children's mental health services. The reality is that that money, we know now, is not getting through to the front line to delivering services. You raised the issue around bursaries for nurses and midwives, which this government has now stopped. I mean, it is a ridiculous thing to do. We will reinstate those things. Why is it ridiculous? Because we're already seeing lots of EU nurses and midwives already going back to continental Europe because they're so uncertain about the future because of Brexit. So it is a ridiculous time to do it. We would reinstate it. I mean, I've suffered from clinical depression a couple of times. You know, I know what it's like. And that is, you know, so I'm not only passionate about this, for me it's personal.

Joel, what I'd say in terms of what I care about most in education with my own children going through the system, I want what every parent in this country wants. And it starts with something that actually doesn't necessarily cost money, and that is good discipline in our schools. You know, in a typical year now, you get 17,000 teachers being attacked by students. We've got a real problem here. There was a case in Manchester once where a child actually produced a knife in a school, got excluded and then the appeals panel put that child back into the school. Imagine what that does to the headteacher that's trying to keep order. So we say headteachers should be able to exclude difficult pupils and not be overruled by appeals panels. We say you've got to change the rules so teachers can keep order in class. Right now we seem to be treating the teachers like children and the children like the adults. We've got it topsy-turvy the wrong way round and we really need to change that so we have proper discipline and order, then people can learn.

Angela, please continue with what you were saying. She did listen to your point. I did listen to your point, that's right. So we will create those jobs and those skills so that we can get those people into construction. But the other difference as well is that we'll make brownfield sites available instead of allowing the planning law so that our green belt is taken, will create community housing again and council housing where people can live with parks, with recreational centres and with genuinely council housing. This idea of affordable housing, I'm not quite sure who's affording them but most people cannot afford to live at the moment and are in private rented accommodation that's way too high and they're spending too much of their wages on it and it's a rip-off.

No, I'm not saying detain them without trial, certainly not. But what I'm saying is, we've got to establish whether the evidence is there, and you can only do that properly if these people are apprehended under the existing provisions open to the police and the security services, and that's what should take place. But what I won't do...

OK, Richard Burgon, respond to that point. I'm very uncomfortable with the way the discussion from the Conservatives moves straight from a tragedy to reheating pre-packaged political lines, smearing the Labour Party. I think our democracy, regardless of our party, should be better than that. The reality is the Conservatives have been in power for a decade. They decided to cut the number of police by more than 20,000. We'll recruit 20,000 more police, including 2,000 more than the Conservatives on the front line. They've made a mess of probation. Most people recognise that. They've made a mess of our prison service so that people are coming out of prison more likely to commit crimes than when they went in. Our priority needs to be reducing the number of victims of crime, not writing rhetoric-filled articles like the Prime Minister did in the newspaper this morning.

I'm very clear that we will negotiate tariff-free trade access to European markets so that our manufacturing industry jobs are defended and supported and we have a growing economy as a result of that. We have made a number of very clear spending commitments in our manifesto. They're set out here in a spending plan. They're also set out in the manifesto itself. The government, the Conservative government, has made a lot of choices. We know what those choices are. Our schools are underfunded. Our hospitals are overcrowded. Our students are saddled with debt. There's a growing housing crisis. And at every single turn, their answer is to further reduce corporation tax and further reduce tax for the very wealthiest in our society.

Well, what I would do quite obviously, is look at the existing powers that are there and say, can such individuals, being such a major cause for concern, can they be brought in and questioned and can we go through it. What you can't do as a politician...

I welcome immigration, because I think that people come here and they're more likely to prop up our public services and work in our public services, rather than they are to put a drain on our public services. So I welcome immigration in this country, because they contribute to our public service. But the numbers are due to come down anyway. But I think one or part of the reason we have so many people coming here, and I welcome them when they do, is because we're not investing in our domestic workforce. and we can do both at the same time.

Mr. Bliski, the union is, of course, the most important thing, just to answer the question straight. The union, and it's a fantastic thing. I'm proud to be not only prime minister, but also minister for the union. OK. Now, let's be clear that Jeremy Corbyn has said that he's open to a second referendum on Scotland early in the lifetime of the next parliament if he's head of the government. And Nicola Sturgeon has made it absolutely clear that the price of her support will be a referendum on Scotland in 2020. So you're going to have a year of two referendums, one on Scotland and one... OK, let's allow Mr Corbyn to respond.

I mean, that could be 10 years or something. But my point is that there's a couple of big issues in this election campaign that really aren't seeing the light of day. And one of them is the disastrous impact of Brexit on the United Kingdom economy.

There's a pattern of behaviour here. He said he would get waiting lists down. They've gone up. He said he would stop the boats. We've got record numbers coming. The promises he make are not worth the paper they're written on. His plan is failing. We do have to deal with this problem, Stephen. We absolutely have to deal with it. We have to smash the gangs that are doing it. I will never accept that the only criminal gangs that can't be taken down are these vile gangs. I've taken down terrorist gangs in my past. I've seen how it's done. I know it can be done. We'll have a border security command, if I'm Prime Minister, and we will get that done and stop this vile trade and those boats coming home with very vulnerable people. Thank you very much.

Anyway, carry on. I think it's very interesting. It's never a great idea to attack the audience, Nigel, in my opinion. You cannot discuss. The real audience are sitting at home, actually. You cannot discuss. Hang on. Let him have his say. No, no, that's fine. Remarkable. We are talking about a market, folks. I know none of you have ever worked in business, but we're talking about a market. And markets are about demand and supply. And I just wonder, I'm curious, could I get any recognition from any of you that the demand side of this equation is that a rapidly rising population, due to open door immigration, started by Ed Miliband's Labour Party in the late 1990s, has directly contributed towards the housing crisis. Is there any flicker of recognition from any of you? Please, one of you, have a go, please.

Harry, you should just be careful. There are independent inquiries ongoing. Those haven't concluded. They're being done by very serious bodies. The Gambling Commission and the police. It's important that nobody says or does anything that compromises the integrity of those investigations. You said in the letter, I said things should be done properly. What's proper is to investigate things, to get to the bottom of it, to uncover the truth and then act on those findings. That's exactly what we are doing. So, in parallel, we have been conducting internal inquiries. These are very sensitive matters. They have to be done in a way that doesn't compromise the integrity of police and other investigations. But I've been crystal clear, if anyone has broken the rules, not upheld the standards that I would expect, then they will be held to account, they should face the full consequences of the law, and they will be booted out of the Conservative Party.

Boris Johnson? I see absolutely no reason to compromise people's human rights, such as the perpetrator of the atrocious killings at London Bridge, but I do think that it was extraordinary and wrong that he should be out on early release and automatic. early release. I said long before I became Prime Minister that we needed to campaign against short sentences. I said in August that we should get rid of automatic early release. I have been very sad to see this guy out on the streets able to do what he did. We have a bill waiting to go, stuck because of the blockade in Parliament, if we can get a working majority, we can get rid of automatic early release and make sure for serious sexual and violent offenders, and make sure that terrorists, people who are convicted of terrorist offences, serve their full sentence. I think that is what this country would expect.

Rebecca Lockhart. Caroline Lucas, briefly. Can you explain to her the fact that your government has been slashing support to renewable energies, that flogged off the Green Investment Bank, that has made it harder to have onshore wind in this country. Your track record on climate and the environment is abysmal.

What we're looking at in terms of the National Health Service and ensuring that we can provide the National Health Service in the future is, if we just look at how much money is now being spent on the NHS, in the five years to 2020 we will be spending half a trillion pounds on the National Health Service. Now we're putting more money in at the moment and we will continue to put more money in into the future, into the NHS. But it is important that we recognise, as you will know as a nurse, the demands on the NHS are increasing all the time. We recognise the work that the NHS staff do. But nurses get paid less and less. Nurses get an increase, and obviously there are some other payments as well.

Thank you. It's a really important question, because the first job of any government is to keep its citizens safe and secure. But I'm going to level with you. Since 2014, we have been at the threat level of severe, which means an attack is highly likely. As Home Secretary for the past year, I've seen the evidence, I've seen the warrants crossing my desk, I spend about two hours a day looking at them, seeing the real damage that some of these terrorists want to do us. The way we try to stop them is by supporting our security services and our counter-terrorism police. The best way to try and intervene in those plots is to make sure that they are well funded and we've increased their budget significantly since 2015 and we hope to further on.

Free school meals for infant school children was something the Liberal Democrats delivered in government. And we did it for a reason. We did it because all the evidence suggests it boosts attainment. It makes sure that children, particularly from poorer backgrounds, get at least one decent meal a day. And it means that you don't have the stigma of people registering for free school meals if they do not wish to do so. But to get rid of them after the schools have put so much money and effort to build kitchens, to bring in new ways of delivering those free school meals, is an absolute hideous assault. And just to add on the issue of class sizes, it's absolutely right that smaller class sizes do make a difference, but it's also about who is in the classroom. I say the average primary school will lose two teachers, they could instead lose three or four teaching assistants, which will make it that much harder to deliver good education in primary and secondary.

We want to maintain our economy, we want to make sure that... So you need more workers from outside Wales? Well, we're quite happy with the ones we've got. So why do you need a visa system to attract more workers to Wales then? Because Theresa May is going to end freedom of movement and that brings specific risks to the Welsh economy and the Welsh NHS.
...that was passed at, er, an earlier conference in the course of this parliament. But as you know...

The ERM was was the biggest mistake that government made and we were in government, although everybody else supported that decision, you're, you're, you're right to say, we were in government, we have to accept the responsibility for that and ...

Good evening, Mahesh. I'm the son of immigrants. My parents came here 60 years ago. And I'm a British Asian. I swore my oath as an MP on the Bhagavad Gita, which is the Hindu holy scripture. So I am living proof that this is a welcoming, meritocratic and tolerant country. But it's also right that we control our borders. And after we leave the EU, we can introduce an Australian points-based system, which will allow us to decide who comes here on the basis of what they have to offer, and as you said, not on the basis of what country they happen to come from. I don't think that's right, and that is why we must get Brexit done and introduce this new system. The alternative is unlimited, uncontrolled immigration that treats different people from different countries differently and that is simply not fair. LONG-BAILEY: We need to step away from the dog whistle rhetoric on this issue. LONG-BAILEY: There are a number of anomalies. LONG-BAILEY: One of the things that often strikes me in a general election campaign is how those who have got British citizenship who aren't from the EU can vote in an election, yet EU citizens who have been here for many, many years aren't allowed to take part in something that could determine their future, and we'd change that as a Labour government. LONG-BAILEY: We know that if we're still part of the EU, freedom of movement remains, but if not, LONG-BAILEY: We want every single EU citizen who's here in the UK to remain with full rights and protections, and we want that reciprocated for UK nationals in the EU. LONG-BAILEY: Now, on the wider system of immigration and what that could look like, obviously that would be part of negotiations, but it has to be sensible and it has to be fair and it has to work for businesses and the economy. LONG-BAILEY: We've got over 43,000 vacancies in the NHS for nurses, for example, and we need to fill them immediately while we train up LONG-BAILEY: our own nurses to be able to carry out those roles in the future. LONG-BAILEY: We've got to be sensible about this. Do you think, Rishi Sunak, have you got a question for her before I bring in Nicholas? I was going to make the observation that everyone on this stage who wants to stay in the European Union, that means accepting the free movement of people. So, to answer Mahesh's question, if you believe that we should treat everyone the same, then you have to extend free movement of people to everywhere in the world. And that's what everyone else on this stage who wants to stay in the European Union has to say to your question. Only if we can leave the EU can we introduce a system that controls and treats everybody the same. There was a deep sigh, First Minister.
Yes. We're going to cut taxes by four billion pounds in our first budget.

Where Nick and I are agreed is that to give an inheritance tax cut to the 3,000 richest estates in the country of £200,000 each, the biggest manifesto promise that the Conservative made, is totally unfair to the rest of the population of this country. And I say to him, we will use the national insurance to pay for healthcare, to pay for policing and to pay for schools. I'm going to stop you there. He will not be able to do that.

Harry, these are really serious matters. You promised... And they're subject... I'm just... They're really serious. They're subject to multiple independent investigations, including a police criminal investigation. It's right that nothing that I do or anyone does compromises the integrity of those investigations, and you understand that. But I want to reassure people, we are in parallel and have been conducting internal inquiries and won't hesitate to act if findings or information comes to light. And I've been crystal clear, if anyone has broken the rules, then they should face not just the full consequences of the law, but they will be booted out of the Conservative Party.

Schools are organised differently in Scotland than they are in England, so some of these points don't necessarily apply. We've got a challenge in Scotland. We've introduced a new curriculum. It's been the right thing to do. It's been praised by the OECD. But we know we've got work to do to focus on literacy and numeracy, really basic skills, and we're very, very focused on doing that. We also want to free up teachers much more. You know, teachers are best placed to know what works in our schools, but they do tell us they have had too much bureaucracy. So it's about taking that away and putting more resources into the hands of teachers. But it's not just about school. As I said earlier, it's about early years, but it's also then about making sure that there are choices for people leaving school. I want people to have equal opportunity to go to university, but if that's not for them, I want them to have other opportunities too.

Nick Clark. Nigel Farage seems to think that every single problem in the world can be solved by a referendum on Europe. Look, at the end of the day, Europe isn't perfect. Of course it's not perfect. But it is the world's largest marketplace of 500 million shoppers who buy our goods and buy our services. So if you do what Nigel Farage or significant parts of the Conservative Party want, which is to yank ourselves out of the family of nations that makes up the European Union, Unemployment will go up and I would never ever ever endorse an approach which would make our country poorer and would see more people out of work. I think it is deeply irresponsible.

Well, first of all, we absolutely are proud of saying that we are going to be honest with all of you that the NHS and social care across the United Kingdom is in crisis, and in England especially, I would observe. And that means that either you can have platitudes from people who will tell you that they can solve this problem without any extra money, or we can be brutally honest with you and say that for the price of a cup of coffee a week, we can have the best NHS and social care in the world. My view is this. that there isn't a single person who either themselves or their loved ones uses health and care in this country and doesn't know there is a crisis. In social care you have wonderful loving people, caring people, caring for our older people who could earn more money stacking shelves at the local supermarket. That is an absolute outrage and I'm determined to solve it and we'll solve it by being honest and saying that we'll put a penny on income tax for everybody to make sure we have the best health and social care in the world. Just spell that out a bit more, if you would.

I met Evo Morales in Mexico last year. I had a very long conversation with him about his country and about the economic development that's happened and the numbers of people that have been taken out of poverty. Obviously, I follow what's going on in that country as I do in many other countries in the world. He resigned from the presidency and now has moved out of Bolivia. I ask and I support the other Latin American governments that have asked for a process of peace in Bolivia so that the Constitution can be restored, so that democratic government can come back. That surely is the right thing to do.

Well, I think the first thing that we can do, which is so important, is actually about retrofitting homes to insulate them properly. And in terms of how we can encourage people to do that, well, that's actually going to be something which is good for people because it cuts energy bills, it helps people save money. And for low-income households, that should be something which we do, that we make sure that that is done by the government so that those homes, within the next five years, are insulated to help cut fuel poverty, cut energy bills. But the other thing we absolutely need to be doing and it's I mean it's madness and it's you know the Conservatives that have dropped this. We know how to build homes that are zero carbon homes. A couple of weeks ago I was in Sheffield eco construction site where they're building these homes. It was you know November. The homes weren't completed yet. I was in the home and even then it was warm because it was properly built in a way that retained heat. So we know how to do this. They've been doing it in Scandinavia for years. We need to make sure that now we have a target for all new build homes to be zero carbon and to have those high standards. And the Conservatives, who can't even be bothered to turn up tonight, scrapped those plans.

There's a crucial aspect of this debate, Julie, and a crucial aspect for people at home. David Cameron said earlier that work pays in our country. Rubbish. Work doesn't pay in our country. There are so many people around our country, millions of people, people watching at home tonight, who are going out, doing all the hours that God sends, and who can't feed their family, can't make ends meet at the end of the month. And let's take the issue of zero-hours contracts, because it's relevant to this debate. If work is insecure, if work doesn't pay properly, then you don't get the security that working people need. Now, I say we should deal with those exploitative zero-hours contracts. It is an absolutely crucial part of this immigration debate, because you've got to create security for the working families of Britain, and that's what I will do.

Five years ago, this country was on the brink. We had millions of people unemployed. And we had one of the biggest budget deficits anywhere in the world. For the last five years, we've been working with the British people through a long-term economic plan. And that plan is working. There are almost two million more people in work. It's a balanced plan, so we've invested in our NHS as well as reducing the deficit. And we've cut taxes for 30 million working people. And the plan's working because last year we had the fastest growing economy of any of the major Western countries. Now tonight you're going to hear a lot of people claiming a lot of things. But please remember these are the same people who claimed that if we followed our plan, unemployment would go up, that the deficit wouldn't come down, the economy wouldn't grow, that public services would be destroyed. They were wrong then and they're wrong now. The choice at this election is sticking with a plan that's working or going back to the debt, taxes, borrowing and spending that got us in this mess in the first place. I say let's not go back to square one. Britain can do so much better than that.

Well the Prime Minister Boris Johnson voted twice against Theresa May's deal, one time for it, then proposed his own deal which narrowly got through the House of Commons with the support of the DUP, which is actually even worse than Theresa May's deal. He cannot protect jobs in this country on that basis. And I think it is time that we actually recognise we have to maintain a firm and good trading relationship with Europe. Otherwise, more jobs will be lost all over the country as they've already been lost because of the uncertainty.

With the greatest respect, I was elected as First Minister in the Scottish Parliament election last year. I put forward a manifesto for a five-year term and at the heart of that manifesto was our priorities in education. Yes, I do think when we come to The next Scottish Parliament election, I should be judged and held to account on that. I think that is absolutely right. But on Thursday, we're electing MPs, hopefully to go to Westminster, to protect public spending and stop the Tories pushing more kids into poverty, which will make it more difficult for us to improve attainment in our schools.

There is absolutely no evidence that I know of... If that's the case, then why not release it? ..to show any interference in any British electoral event. And the reason I won't is I see no reason, or the reason I decided not to ages ago, is because I see no reason to interfere with a normal timetable The normal timetable... Hold on, that's not true. That is not true.

It is not an either or. You have to keep the public safe, you have to ensure that the police are there and properly funded, you have to ensure the prison system works and is effective, that the parole board works and is effective, that the probation service works and is effective. You have to give people that security. You don't get security on the cheap, you have to invest in it. Human rights are our defence against autocracy and our defence against abuse of power. There is no difference between wanting security and human rights. The two things are actually inextricably linked together.

Look, sometimes in politics, you have to do what you think is right. Don't get me wrong, all politicians make calculations and tactical calculations, but sometimes you have to be guided by principle. And my position on this is reasonably straightforward when you strip it all away. We face not just Brexit, but perhaps a very extreme form of Brexit. that could have implications and consequences for life in Scotland for generations to come. It could see jobs lost, it could see investment into our country fall, it could see the horizons of our young people in terms of freedom of movement seriously restricted. Now, my proposition is simply this. When we get to the end of that Brexit process, and we can see what the implications are for the future of our country, we should have a choice. Do we think that is acceptable and right for Scotland or do we want to choose a different future? Fundamentally, our future should be decided for us, not by us.

It does not say that. It says continue... The Resolution Foundation. said that the Tory manifesto could take levels of child poverty in the UK to record levels. How can you defend that? Already 4 million children living in poverty. It's rising despite what the Prime Minister said falsely this morning. Your policies will take more children into poverty. How can you stand here and defend a manifesto that would do that?
Isn't that terrible? Why aren't we training our own people? Why don't we train our own people? What's wrong with us?
I mean, come on. But we need people to come to study in our universe. Thank you.

I think Tarek actually touched on this. We feel that actually none of the other parties are facing up to what the real issue is. Yes, there is an issue of supply, but actually when you look at the 2011 census, we actually have 140 million rooms in this country for 65 million people. We have more bedrooms per head of population than we've had at any time in our history. Now that's kind of puzzling, isn't it? So what's going on here? What we have is a broken housing market, where housing has been turned into a speculative commodity. We know that buy-to-let investors and foreign investors are coming in, and they're driving up prices. And when you have something like Help to Buy, actually all it is doing is helping out developers who say, thank you very much. That gives us another chance to put up our prices further. What we have to do is get rid of the subsidies. We subsidise buy-to-let investment market by £6.5 billion a year. You take that money, you put it into deposits for council housing, you can lend against that, lift the cap on local authorities, they can borrow against that, and then the housing gets built and gets paid through housing benefit or private rental. You can also put not just rent controls, like rent caps, like Labour want to do, but that will only cap the increases in rents, but actually rent controls, which will allow prices to be stabilised and even in some cases brought down if you give those powers to local authorities. There is so much we can do. The problem here is the political will to go and take on those investors who are pushing up the price of housing and making housing integral commodities, not homes.

Thank you very much. I mean, the police force is devolved in Scotland, which means it's within the competence of the Scottish Government. And we have been maintaining our police numbers over the last few years. And ultimately, cuts are a political choice. And that's why in the aftermath of the atrocities and the hearts of everybody in Scotland is with Manchester and has been with Manchester over the last two weeks. There are questions to be asked about where we best deploy our resources. And we believe that law enforcement at every stage of the process needs to have the resources to do their job properly. We have legislation there, but they need resources. And in Scotland, we have made the decision over the last, the SNP hasn't been in government since 2007, to increase the number of police on on the beat, as it were, and to make sure that we have the resources we need. So in the aftermath of the atrocities, whilst we were very grateful for the offer of the armed forces to come in and patrol our streets, we did not need to call on that resource and we were able, we had enough capacity within the police force to do that.

We must let the future generation be relieved from this generation's mistakes. I just want to be very practical about this. What's one of the most important things for our young people? The quality of jobs. We've got 700,000 people in our country on zero-hours contracts. Probably some people watching tonight at home, waiting for that text message to say whether you have work tomorrow. Now, David Cameron says he couldn't live on a zero-hours contract. Well, nor could I, David. But the difference with me is I'm going to do something about it. I'm going to legislate. So if you do regular hours, you get a regular contract, not a zero-hours contract. And it goes to what kind of country we build. Do we build a country with security for working families and for our young people, or based on insecurity?

Well, Sean, you beautifully illustrate why there's division in the country, because the political parties, and most of Westminster, don't accept the result of the people. Losers' consent is the principle by which civilised democracy works. You fight elections, you lose them, you accept the result. That is how our system works. We have had three and a half years, and you're still doing it. You don't accept the result, and now you want a second referendum. just to give even more division and acrimony in the country. And I've no doubt that when we vote to leave again by an even bigger margin, you still wouldn't accept it. There's no perfect answer, given where we are. But we have to deliver Brexit. We have to become an independent country or our democracy in this country is, frankly, dead.

We would have parity within the NHS when it comes to esteem for mental health. But can I just take up this issue around mental health more broadly and that you raised, Sophie. In our schools, we are creating children who are effectively economic units to compete in a global marketplace. We are testing them to death. and we need a better education system where we allow our young people, our children from a very early age, not to be subject to that rigour. I took a SATS test the other day just to see what it was like, what my children were going through. How did you do? I think I got about 97%. But the stress was huge. But I think this issue of stress and mental health is something we have to address in the round. And I'm astonished to hear you talk about the Conservative government's record on this. Because when you look at social services and the Employment Support Allowance, the work-related activity group within Employment Support Allowance, these are people that the government accepts are too sick or disabled to work at the moment. Yet they've just put in a cut. of 30% for new claimants in the work-related activity group. This is for people that the government accepts are too sick or disabled to work. We know that there are people committing suicide because of sanctions to do with benefits, primarily who are mentally ill. This is absolutely despicable. And unless we get this out in the open, unless we have this conversation about it, it's going to go on and on. And I cannot accept the platitudes around mental health from this government.

We've got to make sure that the food that we are producing is produced in a way that is more environmentally friendly. There's a lot of work ongoing to do that right now. But for environmental reasons and for health reasons, yes, of course, we've got to be encouraging people to eat balanced diets and to think about getting their food much more locally sourced. That's good for the economy as well as being good for people's health and good for the environment.

All parties have had to take action. We've got to clean up the politics for every single party. And anybody who commits an offence is out. They're suspended. Out. They shouldn't be in politics at all. Public service is about serving the public. It's not about serving yourself.

Can we get a microphone there, sir? Can I just make this one point? Very quickly, if you would. What I stand for, what I think matters in politics, is ideas to change the country, principles, sticking to your principles when you've got powerful opponents like the energy companies or the banks or Rupert Murdoch, and frankly, decency. Reaching out to people who are the most vulnerable in our society.
If businesses leave, there's less tax, there's less jobs and more people will be unemployed. That's what you'll get if you vote Labour.

In a word, you are right, I agree with the sentiment. If we were to stop oil and gas production tomorrow, we would become more dependent on imports, which would actually increase the carbon footprint. So we've got to have a managed transition and a just transition. That transition is underway. Rightly so, we've got to accelerate it. We've got a net zero target in Scotland, in law already, of net zero emissions by 2045. That's five years ahead of the rest of the UK. So we've got to power on with these things and we're absolutely focused on doing it.

On June the 8th, you can lay the foundations for a new kind of politics. Your vote can change everything. Imagine this, a country that leads the world in clean energy, not fracks its way to climate chaos. A country that spends its money on hospitals and schools, not nuclear bombs. A country that welcomes child refugees, not blocks them at the border. A country that's open to its neighbours, not isolated from them. A country that celebrates diversity, not fears it. A country that's friends with the good guys, not the climate criminals and the dodgy dictators. A country that cares for those with dementia, not one that taxes them. We've shown time and again, you don't need the keys to number 10 to open the door to change. On June the 8th, let's unlock that door together. Fearless Green MPs in opposition to the Tories. Independent Green MPs holding Labour's feet to the fire. Pioneering Green MPs to make our economy fit for tomorrow. We decide the future by what we do now.

First of all, let me be clear that no one who is a threat to the public is ever going to get released early. Nick Thomas-Simmons has a cheek talking about this. The last Labour government released 80,000 people early. We are building 20,000 more prison places. 7,900 of those will be delivered by next December to make sure dangerous criminals get taken off the street. Last year we took, we had 35,000 more successful criminal justice outcomes than we had the previous year. That is to say more criminals were successfully prosecuted. We're going to make sure the prison places are available to take them where necessary off the streets so they can't offend and the public are kept THOMAS-SYMONDS: They have released people who are a threat to the public. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Look at the inspectorate, look at what the governor of Lewes Prison said. THOMAS-SYMONDS: And Rhiannon, in answer to your question, I make no apology at all. THOMAS-SYMONDS: I think of the terrorist street I grew up in in South Wales. THOMAS-SYMONDS: I think those people are entitled to be kept safe and that does mean the people who commit violent and sexual offences should be behind bars.

Well, Anna, this is one of the things that I, along with immigration, actually, the I probably hear about more than anything else as I travel around the country, a lack of affordable housing as I travel round the country. The lack of affordable housing. The people in your situation, but then there are, I think, 1.8 million families, that's five million people, who are still on the waiting list for an affordable home. What do you do about it? I would do three things: firstly, there are hundreds of thousands of empty properties in our communities boarded up, no doubt there are many in Birmingham, too, which I think for a relatively modest amount of money you could convert into homes which people could live in. It is just not right, and people either can't afford like you, Anna, or simply can't find places to live in, that we have all these empty properties. We have a plan, set out, costed in our manifesto to convert 250,000 empty homes into homes that people can live in. Secondly, I would give local councils more freedom to borrow against their own assets so they can invest in building new homes. And the third thing I would do, all these empty flats we see in our city centres, built for onepeople... person, I think they should be converted into the homes that people need for young families, like yours.

Well, the first thing I would say is that as I go around and talk to people, individuals, business representatives and others, I find that actually the greater majority of opinion here in the UK now is the decision was taken, the public were given their choice, they chose to leave the European Union, let's just have a government that gets on with it and delivers a good deal. And how I would respond for all of those who voted to remain, and I voted to remain, is to say that now we must make sure we get the negotiations right, we get that good free trade agreement, the continued cooperation, a deep and special partnership with the EU, but that also we take this opportunity of Brexit, new trade deals around the rest of the world, actually seeing how we can build a more prosperous, stronger and fairer Britain. I think we can do that and I think we can do that because I believe in Britain and I believe in the British people.

Yeah, we want to ban exploitative ones because we do not think that that is fair. We think that everybody deserves a proper living wage starting at £10 from the age of 18 because, to be quite honest, Prices of a loaf of bread or a bus fare are the same if you're 18 or 28. It's also taking it back a step, so we want to get apprenticeships, good quality apprenticeships, on a par with university degrees, whereas at the moment they seem to be drifting and there's a stigma associated with apprenticeships which is nonsense because particularly businesses are crying out for well-trained people. What we'll do, which is a really big thing, is we will scrap tuition fees because to come out of a degree with £40,000 debt is crazy. What we'll also be looking at doing is finally getting rid of the gender pay gap because I do not understand why it helps anyone and definitely our economy when a woman is earning on average 18% less than a man doing the same job. We've had Labour brought in legislation around equal pay in 1970 and we're still at an 18% pay gap. What we'll also look at doing is getting a national investment fund. We'll have banks regionally so that small businesses particularly can go to that and grow their business to bring in more employment in the area. But what we're looking for particularly is that sort of high earning jobs rather than just about doing jobs. So 3% of our GDP is actually going to go on research and development because that's what we're seeing is the fastest way to grow our economy.

If a private, if a private, if a private - no, many of them can't afford private insurance. I'm, I was with someone in Bolton a couple of days ago, who'd had to pay, borrow eight thousand pounds to get an operation which he couldn't get on the Health Service. He could, he was not rich by any stretch of the imagination. That's the sort of thing that is happening today, and for heavens sake, if the private sector can come up with education at the same level as, as the state sector, why shouldn't someone be able to choose that private, a private sector school.  BOTH TOGETHER

We're very clear about that and we are the only party that's produced a fully costed manifesto with a grey book that adds up to every piece of expenditure we want. The problem with this country is we've gone too far down the road. of free market economics. We've created too much inequality. And if every single bit of our manifesto was carried out tomorrow, we would just about reach the level of the public services of France or Germany. That is how far backwards we've gone. There has to be investment in our schools. Why are so many children in oversized classes? Why are so many children growing up in insecure housing because there isn't the sufficiency of council housing available? Why are there so many people waiting for social care? Why do so many people in middle age have to go into debt to pay for their kids to go through university and the social care that their older parents need? It's simply time to invest in our future.

80% of people in Australia at the beginning of this debate, thought yes and 20% were against. By the end of it, those positions had reversed. Why? People looked at the practicalities of identity cards. The costs to the individual involved. The curtailment of liberties and the sheer personal hassle involved in operating a scheme of identity cards. That's why.

David, I had to nationalise Northern Rock, and we had also to take over the Royal Bank of Scotland, and Halifax, Lloyd's TSB, and the reason we did so was to save the savings and deposits of families throughout the country, if we hadn't done that then the banks would have collapsed. But now we've got to restructure the banks in a way that is in the public interest. I have never been so angry as when I talk to the chairman of a bank, who told me the night before his bank collapsed that all he had was a cashflow problem when I knew it was a structural failing that was absolutely fundamental, and the banks needed to be recapitalised immediately. Now what we've got to do is recapitalise the banks so that they are safe for people. What we've then got to do is make sure, as we're doing, that remuneration is fair. We do need a worldwide agreement to get a global financial levy that is charged in every country so we're not undercut by other countries and there's a race to the bottom. I would say to those banks, we will never allow them to act in an irresponsible and unfair way again.

Yeah, always. Whenever there's a cycle race, there is. Well, let me tell you about our policy, Chris, and then maybe you come back and say how it might affect you. Our policy is this, which is that after 12 weeks, an employee will have a right to a regular contract, not a zero-hours contract, based on the average number of hours that they've done. And if the employee wants to carry on with the zero-hours contract, they can, but we think that should be in the hands of the employee having the right to the regular contract. Let me just explain why I say that. And I know this may be difficult for your business, but let me just explain the reasons for that. I don't think we can base the future of our economy on the idea, and I meet lots of people on zero-hours contracts, that people don't know from one day to the next how many hours they're doing or what wage they're going to be getting. Because I don't... Mr Cameron was honest enough to say the other day he couldn't live on a zero-hours contract. Well, nor could I. But if I can't live on it and he can't live on it, I don't think we should make the British people live on one. All right. One last question, if we can... That's the logic of my position.

Well, he says the plan is working. He says the plan is working. So the question for him is, why has he called the election now? Because if he thinks things are going to get better towards the second half of this year, why has he called it now? He's called it now because he knows, and I'll ask him this, he knows inflation is going to go back up. He knows energy prices are going to go back up in the autumn. That's what he's not telling you. So he says the plan is working, but I don't think he believes the plan is working, because if he thought the plan is working, he wouldn't have called this election right now. Rishi Sunak.
Yes, and that's always been our position, that any new licences have got to pass a climate compatibility assessment before they could be approved, and that's got to be a rigorous process.

Well, money's not going as far because inflation is rising. Inflation is rising partly because the value of the pound has plummeted. The value of the pound has plummeted because of Brexit. So these are the hard realities we've got to face. But we do have to seriously debate how we help people. Interestingly, when Theresa May published her manifesto today, she didn't give any commitment not to increase VAT or national insurance. And I think that is regrettable. So I've already spoken about the real living wage. As First Minister of Scotland, I know there's a real issue about the sustainability or unsustainability of the public sector pay cap. We must address wages in the public sector as well. But, you know, this issue about social security is a really important one. One of the things that I think is quite despicable about what the Tories and some others have done in this country is treat everybody who is reliant on social security as somehow being scroungers. actually so many of the people who rely to some extent on state benefits are working and working hard and Government policy is taking in some cases hundreds and hundreds of pounds of support away for them.

because it is important, because there's no way to get Janet and everyone else the care they need without a workforce. OK. But the British Medical Association... All right, thank you. ..has warned that Keir Starmer's pension tax... OK. ..risks an exodus of doctors. We're talking about doctors from the NHS. OK, we are going to... Gentlemen, please. Gentlemen, actually... It's all joined up.

Adam Price. There are two great opportunities for us, and let's be positive about this. So one is powering down, we were talking about that, so that's about energy efficiency in homes, the five billion that we in Wales would want to invest in that, and also transport, decarbonising our transport system. The other one then is powering up. powering up the great renewable revolution. And, you know, after Scotland, I think Wales has got incredible renewable energy resources. And the great thing is, you know, we were the cradle of the last industrial revolution. We could be there at the start of this new industrial revolution as well, if we tap into that potential. And the great thing is, as the entire world is looking at decarbonisation, if we get there earlier, if we get to 2030, if we set that kind of target, of course, there are advantages then. We can reap the benefits of that, because if we solve the world's problems first, then we actually have created a new global industry which is economically useful for us, as well as helping us meet our environmental responsibilities earlier than anyone else.

Oh, no, Nicola. Look, here's the situation. You've got a very odd approach, because you claim you want a Labour government, but you're saying anyone but Labour. In England, you're saying vote Green. In Wales, you're saying vote Plaid Cymru. And of course, in Scotland, you're saying vote SNP. And you know that for every one less Labour MP, the likelihood is that we'll end up with David Cameron and Nick Clegg not on the stage tonight, perhaps with a bit of Nigel thrown in. And the truth is, you want to gamble on getting rid of a Tory government. I can guarantee that we get rid of a Tory government if you vote Labour in this election.

The backlog issue is a complete mess entirely of the Conservatives making. So because of the legislation that they've introduced, there are now 118,000 people who can't claim asylum in this country. neither can they be deported to Rwanda to have their claims processed there. So there's 118,000 people stuck in no-man's land without any ability to do anything at all, and taxpayers are having to pay for that. So the first thing that we would do is scrap the Rwanda scheme, which has been incredibly expensive, unworkable, completely immoral, that has cost £291 million already, without a single person being sent to Rwanda who wasn't paid to go. And by scrapping that scheme, that money alone that's been spent already could have been used to pay for 6,000 Home Office caseworkers to process those claims, get the claims down. So what Liberal Democrats will do is scrap the Rwanda scheme, invest in Home Office processors, get the claims down to processors within three months for each person, and if anybody's claim can't be dealt with within three months, they then have the right to work, which means that taxpayers aren't subsidising people to work and pay tax.

We would study very carefully proposals that came forward. We voted against the Snoopers Charter because we thought that went too far in interfering with people's civil liberties and didn't practically enhance the police powers in the way that we thought was effective. But we would also always look very carefully and study the case for any proposal for additional powers.

Caroline Lucas. So, Alnus, the story that you've just painted is really... It strikes my heart, and I know many people who've come to my constituency surgeries telling me similar stories. I remember one woman in particular who was distraught because of the bedroom tax, and she just didn't have enough money, and all she wanted was enough money to buy some tights so she could go to a job interview. I mean, that is what we have come to in the fifth biggest economy in the world. I think that is shameful. We can afford to look after everybody in our society if we choose to do so. And do you know what? You know, I remember when I was growing up, we were all told that life was going to get a lot easier, things would be much, much less work we'd all have to do, we were all going to be enjoying ourselves more. Well, it hasn't worked out that way. Who would have predicted that today we would have four million children living in poverty in the UK? Who would have predicted 4,000 people who have to spend their lives on our streets because there's no home for them to go to? Who would have thought that nurses would be going to food banks? So we absolutely have choices here. Our choices would be a progressive taxation system where the rich is put in more, corporations pay more, and people like yourself would have a decent life worth living. Thank you. Nicola Sturgeon.

Well, I don't think we're going to solve this problem by banning people from doing things or wrecking the economy. What will solve this problem is going to be new technologies and innovation, whether it's more powerful offshore wind turbines, cheaper car batteries that drive faster, or cleaner, greener homes. But the only way you can bring those new technologies to life is through a strong economy, which is why that strong economy has to be at the foundation of our greener future as a country.

Of course we do, that's why Liberal Democrats are saying you invest it upstream because prevention is better than cure. If you have more GPs, you solve the dental crisis, you have access to mental health hubs in communities, people can access care when they need it and you don't end up firefighting all the problems in A&E, in ambulances and hospitals. That's the problem that we've had under the Conservatives. They've gutted primary care and everybody has ended up in hospital because they can't get the treatment they need upstream.
Let me come back on this because you said something very important which is that some other countries didn't suffer from that. Now that's because they were less exposed to financial services.

Croeso i Gymru, welcome to Wales. We're a country with great potential, rich in talent and resources, held back by poverty. And the greatest poverty of all, perhaps, is the poverty of our leadership. Labour have been in power in this building for 20 years, and for half of that time in Westminster too. We have the lowest wages across these islands, a third of our children living in poverty and the worst A&E waiting times on record. Labour and the Tories, of course, will blame each other, but the truth is neither of them are the solution to our problems. They are our problem source. On Brexit, like everything else, they lie to us or they let us down. We want Wales to remain in the European Union, but one thing we don't want to remain in is poverty. And we don't have to if we start to believe in our own future. The new start we need as a nation, the new investment, the new leadership, the new hope will never come from Westminster. It can only come from us, from all of us that live in Wales and want to see it prosper. Let's make this winter our Welsh spring and find our voice on the 12th of December.

Yes, it's actually £128 billion. The different sources are rescheduling the UK's finances and £10 billion, that's £118 billion and £10 billion in funds to be raised through taxation, largely through the £0.45 to £0.50 tax raise change. I'm just interested by Jeremy, following SNP policy and tuition fees, I'd like to congratulate you, Jeremy. It's a shame your party north of the border has opposed the SNP. Can I bring up a really serious issue, which I think has been the biggest so far in the campaign, and that relates to social care and to pensions. And I think when older people are facing the most likely prospect, which is a re-elected Tory government, sadly, and they are promising a dementia tax, an end to the winter fuel payment, and cuts to the pension, I think those people deserve to know by how much. And they haven't been told. Now the Prime Minister didn't have the guts to come along this evening to tell us, so I'd like to challenge... I would like to challenge Amber Rudd to tell us the answer to that question now because they must have costed it. They must know how much money that will bring in. Please tell the pensioners of this country how much they will have to pay to fund Tory austerity. How much?

Richard Bergen. The Labour Party created our National Health Service when they said there wasn't the money to do it. The Conservatives, by the way, voted against its creation time and time again, but they still can't be trusted with our National Health Service. I don't believe Boris Johnson when he says our NHS is not for sale on his watch. I know that there's a real risk that he's going to do a sweetheart deal with Donald Trump. We need to end the privatisation are the NHS, stealth and otherwise, and we need to properly fund it and resource it. We need to bring our NHS back from the brink.

Thank you very much indeed. Thank you. There has to be a test. Thank you very much. There has to be a test on the statements made and so when people paint slogans on the side of a bus that are totally unsustainable, perhaps it's time that we did have an independent monitoring of what goes on.

You can't have a balanced energy policy in the modern world as almost every country is now finding in the advanced world without using nuclear power. And I do say that the schemes that we've introduced, I met some young guys yesterday and girls who've been working on an energy project in Wales. They've been taken on as a result of our Future Jobs Fund, and they're in the business of helping insulation and giving people advice about the use of energy. So we're trying to do this at the moment, and we will do more in future years. Yes, we've got to insulate our houses. Yes, we've got to have carbon-free homes where possible. Yes, we've got to have the electric car. We're investing in that, the hybrid car. And yes, we've got to develop offshore wind power. But the energy balance has got to be fair. and any party that is now excluding nuclear power, which is already a substantial part of our country, is not really thinking about the needs of a future where we cannot be dependent on these high oil prices forever.

It's not shocking to me as a woman, it really isn't. What I would say is the first thing we have to do is work on prevention. And that's something that doesn't get talked about enough. So Liberal Democrats would require every social media company to publish regularly what they're doing to tackle online abuse and violence and hate towards women. We need to have powerful public safety campaigns. We know that they've worked in other parts of the world, particularly empowering men to root out misogyny and sexism amongst their male counterparts. then we have to make misogyny a hate crime, we have to, because it will then treat those hate crimes with the same seriousness as any hate that is driven by racial or religious hatred. We want to have a domestic abuse expert in every single police force and in every single 999 response room as well. And finally on the point about the Met, What I would say, and to Rory I think her name was, who had worked in the Met, I'm from Hertfordshire, Hertfordshire Police Force works very closely with the Met, and I have to say the Louise Casey review that happened into the standards of the Met was really shocking and it blew a lid off the institutional sexism, misogyny, homophobia and racism that existed in that force. And I know that there are many people in the Met who don't deserve to be working there, and there are many people who do, because many of them have said to me that they were the good guys. They're the ones that spoke up bravely to call out those people who were involved in the most horrific forms of conduct. So we have to support our police officers so that all of us can have trust in the police.

I strongly agree with you, Jean. I'm an MP from a city, Sheffield, also very proud of its industrial heritage. I think the first thing we need to do, I would say three things. The first thing is to get the banks lending. If you don't get them lending to manufacturing companies, it's like a body without blood circulating. Everything just sort of stops. I was at a small company in Warrington a few weeks ago, very, very good example, they manufacture new environmentally sustainable lighting fixtures. They've got lots of clients, lots of demand. They can't expand because the banks won't lend to them on reasonable rate. That is an outrage. The banks that we own lent less money last year than the year before. They should be lending more because, after all, it's your money that's gone to bail them out. The second thing we need to do is we need to invest in the kinds of things we need in the future anyway: affordable housing, green energy, renewable energy, public transport, the kind of things which create jobs for our young people, help manufacturing and create the green infrastructure that I think we need as a country, in any event.

Well, to be honest, where there is capacity in the private sector, we could be using some of it in order to bring down the waiting list. But let's not kid ourselves, there is very limited capacity in the private sector and we shouldn't be using it if it guts more capacity from the NHS. One of the problems we've had over the last few years, as you know, is that the Conservatives have absolutely gutted primary care. Some people go to see their GP. Your party promised 6,000 more GPs. We now have fewer per head. We have dentists fleeing the NHS and working privately because the dental contract is so broken and hasn't been fixed. Boris Johnson stood on the steps of Downing Street and promised to fix social care once and for good, and he hasn't. And it's now in crisis. So our plan is to fix the front door with more GPs and dentists and to fix the back door by fixing social care.

They're going to come from working with the private sector to help create those jobs. Apprenticeships for our young people, so that actually there are the skilled people that the private sector needs. A proper industrial policy, which will help the private sector. Cutting business rates, which will also help grow the economy and help grow jobs. Leading in the big industries of the future, like green industry. Take climate change for example. Look, some people see that as a burden. It isn't a burden, it's a necessity to tackle it, but it's a chance for us to lead as a country in one of the most important industries of the future.

Let me make this point. People know with me, if we fall short, I will do the right thing for the country. I did last time, I would again, but I would still plead with people with seven days to go, particularly when you're faced with the alternative of a Labour government backed by the SNP, people who don't even want our country to succeed or even exist. When that's the alternative, put your trust in 23 more seats for the Conservatives and we can have a very strong and very clear government. David Cameron, thank you very much indeed.

Unlike the other six, I don't need an autocue. I'm here because I believe in what I believe in. Our politics isn't working. You've heard these pathetic arguments tonight between the two big parties. Really, there isn't much difference. But electorally, Labour are going to win. The debate is, who forms the opposition in the next Parliament? Who fights for the rights of ordinary British people? Who fights to control our borders? Who fights for men and women running small businesses? Reform UK is about to become a political phenomenon, an historic one. So I urge you, join the revolt.
We've built more homes than the last Labour government.

I want to bring in Leanne Wood on this. Selection doesn't work. Selection doesn't work for the vast majority of people because then you have winners and you have losers. I believe in a public education system. In Wales, we're not going to have grammar schools, and as long as I draw breath, that will be the case. And the question I have here in England is, who is making the money out of all these free schools? Because somebody somewhere is making a big profit out of this.

When the minimum wage was first introduced by the Labour government after the 1997 election there were a lot of similar concerns and predictions which turned out not to be the case and in fact there was not any substantial number of jobless.

Anyone can go online when they get home. Go and see how much it will cost to convert your home. Go and see how much it will cost to install the heat pump. Go and see how much it will cost to change your car. Those are all the things that he's going to force you to do sooner than is necessary. You can go and see tonight the thousands of pounds that's going to cost you. I said it before, I'll say it again. He is going to put up your taxes, put up your bills as clear as night follows day.

Nigel Farage? We do know the areas in which knife crime is the most prevalent, and there is no question, you know, that we are scared... And those are the areas with the most inequality, which is why tackling inequality is an important part of the answer. Well, that may be what you have to do to solve the problem ultimately, by giving people equality of opportunity. Do we not want to solve the problem ultimately? Is that not the point? But you can't give people equality of outcome. We're scared of our own shadow here. We're not going to deal with the question unless we stop being scared of ourselves, we go out and do what needs to be done.
Well, I don't share any of the values and policies of the President Trump, nor do the Liberal Democrats. However, if he's elected by the people...

I'm already trying to do that. So in terms of how I keep my own home, my energy provider is one that gets all its energy from renewable sources, smart meter installed, trying to fly less myself. Sometimes it's inescapable in the job that I do, but trying to use the train much more when I'm coming to London, as I did today, for example, being much more conscious about what I eat and where I source the food that I eat from. So a whole host of things that I think it's really important that all of us lead by example. If we are asking other people to do these things, it is simply not right that we don't do these things ourselves. We have all got to stop putting more plastics into our oceans and that is why I think banning things like cotton buds, as we have already done, the deposit return scheme that we are introducing in Scotland, increasing the charge for single-use coffee cups, plastic bags, all of these things are important and we have got to keep doing all of them.

Leanne Wood. I hope that what you've heard here tonight doesn't fill you with too much despair. Despite what you've heard, there is an alternative to the Westminster consensus in favour of more cuts. Austerity is not inevitable. It's a choice. We can have a future where everyone has access to decent public services, where everyone can have a decent standard of living, but not if we keep doing things the way we always have done. For a stronger, more prosperous, greener Wales, for a Wales that counts, for a devolution and financial settlement that is no longer second rate, give your vote to Plaid Cymru, the Party of Wales. For Wales to be strong, like Scotland, Plaid Cymru must be strong. The more strength you give us, the greater influence we will have. Let us be the success we know we can be. Thank you. Diolch yn fawr. Diolch yn fawr. Leanne Wood. Natalie Bennett.

My grandparents emigrated here because this is an amazing country. We should be incredibly proud of the values that we stand for and the force for good that we are in the world more generally. And that will always be the case under the Conservatives. As it relates to America, I think the US is an important ally of ours, and it will always remain that case. First of all, security. We are kept safe. because of the intelligence that our American allies share with us, and millions of jobs in this country are supported by the investments that the US puts into this country. They're the single largest investor in our nation. So it's important that the Prime Minister of this country is able to build alliances with world leaders around the world, like the President of the United States, and indeed, like all the relationships that he's built in Europe, that not only keep us safe, but further our British interests.

They want our safety as much as anybody else. Caroline Lucas. Thank you to David for the question and it's an incredibly important question. Let me be very clear to begin with that people who commit the kind of atrocities like the one in Manchester are barbaric and what they stand for is evil. But the best form of defense against attacks like that is intelligence-led policing and community engagement. And the kind of response we've just heard from Paul, which seems to suggest that the violence in Manchester was somehow representative of Islam, I think is completely outrageous. There is no more representative of Islam than from the murderer of Joe Cox. was representative of the wider British public. So listen, I'm deeply concerned about the police cuts we've already talked about and it's interesting that the Police Federation warned that those police cuts could be very dangerous. But I also think it's right that we review our interventions overseas. I think it's a disservice to democracy to pretend there's no link and to close down the debate. The former head of MI5 herself has said that the invasion of Iraq exacerbated the terror threat to the UK and was, in her words, highly significant in terms of homegrown extremists. But what I want to say actually is something to Amber, because we can't solve all the world's problems, but we can at least stop adding to them. And my question is this really, that why is Britain the second biggest arms dealer in the world? Why are we selling to 22 of the 30 countries on the government's own human rights watch list? Why did we make 10 times more in arms sales to Saudi Arabia than we gave to Yemen in aid?

Well, I think Mr. Brown's going to agree with that, aren't you? I do. As a little reminder... It's an absolutely brilliant point. Two things I also want to say. First of all, the majority voted out. That is the result, that's the direction of the country. But all of us, especially our leaders, must not forget that three quarters of our young people voted for Remain and they will have to live with the consequences of this for longer than most of us. And the second point, freedom of movement is often raised, but what about freedom of movement for British people? our ability to live, work and study, love and explore overseas. These things matter too and that's why the content of the deal should be agreed by the people, if not the politicians.

All tuition fees do is simply preclude poorer students from going to university. They need to be taken away. We need to go back to a system of student grants. But the point is, and this is someone that nobody else on this platform will admit, that there are far too many people going to university at this present moment in time.

I can stand here as the leader of a government that's already introduced free personal care. It can be done. Free personal care has existed in Scotland now for more than a decade. My government's just extended it to those under 65. These other parties here, they need to stop talking about it, and they need to get on and doing it. And to be positive, I'm happy to answer them now.

Well, we have a three-point plan to try and tackle this problem. The first of it is to invest a billion pounds a year extra to deal with the immediate pressures in the system. The second thing is explicitly to work on a cross-party basis to try and find consensus the way we have with the NHS. And the third thing is to ensure that when it's reformed, no one will have to sell their home to pay for care.
Or, I mean, what's the detail of that? To put the process around how this is, you cannot have a landlord, or this is what we will outlaw.

In the last Parliament, Nicola, we found £21 billion of savings in welfare because everybody knows that welfare was overblown and needed to be properly dealt with. Now, what is the alternative to making reductions on welfare? It is putting up taxes and cutting people's pay. But let's explain... I don't want to see that happen, and that is what happened under the last Labour government. Let's explain what that means.

Yes, because I believe that we've been able to keep this country safe operating that approach as a policy for a very long time, and I believe that providing we maintain our institutions and our vigilance, and provide the public keep the sense of proportion that they have, through generations upon generations, we can continue in that fashion.

I believe in the principle of free education, and it's a real shame that there's no representative here from Labour tonight, because one of their proposals is to abolish tuition fees. Yet in Wales, where Labour runs the government, there is a charge for tuition fees. So again, we have a number of different Labour parties, and I'm not sure which policies you're going to be actually voting for, depending on whether you're in Wales, whether you're voting for Blairite Labour candidates, or whether you're voting for Jeremy Corbyn. I'd like to see the younger generation have as many job opportunities as possible. I'd also like to see the social security reforms that have been brought in, like those cuts to housing benefit, which disadvantage young people, be reversed. And I would like to see, as well, more effort put into further education and apprenticeships, because not everybody wants to start out in life with the huge debt that comes with going to university. Thank you. Tim Farron.
The global economic crisis caused by banks. Not the Labour Party, unless you're saying that you control the world.

And choices are what this government is making. It is choosing to spend money on extra free schools and extra grammar schools when it could be spending it to undo some of the £3 billion worth of cuts that are falling on our schools in the next three years. So the average secondary school will lose six teachers, the average primary school two teachers. That is the decision, the choice, Theresa May is making. And let's remember The argument for grammar schools is utterly and totally bogus. The average number of children at school in this country on free school meals is 16%. The average number of children on free school meals in grammar schools is 3%. It's 3%. There is no doubt that whilst many grammar schools do a good job, bringing them up again now is about undoing social mobility.
