It's about locking people into the 80% that get relegated into secondary models.

Well, Fiona, who asked the question, in England, under this government, we're building fewer homes than at any time since the 1920s. And that's got to change. Because I meet so many people around this country who say that they just can't afford to get on the housing ladder. They can't afford to rent, and it is a massive, massive problem. So we've got to have more social housing. We've got to have more private housing to buy. We've got to reform the way the private rental market works in housing as well. And that's what our plan does. A plan to build 200,000 homes a year by 2020, to make housing the top priority for new capital investment, and to do something that hasn't been done in this country for a very long time, to stand up to the large developers who are actually stopping housing being built. So we'll give councils use-it-or-lose-it powers to say either developers build on the land or councils can buy that land off them and actually make sure that housing is built. So it's a plan to build homes for people right across England. Leon Wood.

Well, actually, we talked about it, Mohamed, in Sue's question. I think it's right that we make savings from the welfare bill, because it's forecast to grow at an unsustainable rate. I don't think that's right. I don't think that's fair. Set out a comprehensive plan. You can go and have a look at it online. And those savings we will use to cut your taxes. And what I want to do is cut taxes for people at every stage of their life. So for everyone in work, we're going to continue cutting national insurance, 900 pounds this year. For pensioners, we have the Triple Lock Plus. We will raise the allowance delivering a tax cut for pensioners, making sure that the state pension is never subject to income tax. If you're a young couple wanting to buy your first home, we're effectively going to abolish stamp duty. If you have children, you're a family, we're going to expand the generosity of child benefit. These are the tax cuts that I want to deliver, and if you're a small business owner, an entrepreneur, we're going to abolish National Insurance for the self-employed. So those are the tax cuts, that's how we're going to pay for it, and that's the choice for you at this election. You can have tax cuts with the Conservatives, or you can have thousands of pounds of tax rises under the Labour Party, because he's simply not being straight with you about what is coming. Mark my words, your taxes are going up if he is in charge.

UKIP will always put the interests of British people first, whether that is prioritising our NHS and social care over a bloated and ineffective foreign aid programme, or protecting those who are most at risk from wage compression because of unskilled mass immigration. UKIP will always stand up for those who have been let down and left behind by the Westminster elites. A government's job, first and foremost, is to protect the public. Our police, our intelligence services, our armed forces and our border force must be given the tools and the resources they need to do their jobs effectively. But money isn't everything. Politicians must show leadership. They must, without fear or favour, ensure that our way of life, our values and our democracy is secure. I believe in our great country. I believe in British values. I believe in our way of life. And as you will see tonight, UKIP believes in Britain.

Well, Paula, I know how much of a strain the last few years have put on your family finances, the family finances of everyone in our country. And that's why my priority has always been to do what I can to support you. That started with bold action, like the furlough scheme during the pandemic, and more recently, my clear plan to bring inflation back to normal. And now, our economy is growing again, wages are rising, and crucially, we are able to now start cutting your taxes. But I know you're only just starting to see the benefits of that, as you described. So the choice at this election is with my clear plan and bold action to keep cutting your taxes to give you financial security, or put all that progress at risk with higher taxes, £2,000 worth of higher taxes under the Labour Party.

Thank you. Thank you, Nigel Farage. Just to make it absolutely clear, we want to lift aid to 1% of GDP, increase the aid, because we need a more secure, stable world. That means tackling hunger, tackling disease, supporting democracy and human rights. What age should be for?

My grandfather's journey through Alzheimer's, I remember it very well. He was treated well and at times not so well. I remember the stench of the first home he went into and we fought hard to get him out of there and it's a reminder that people who work in our social care service are brilliant people but most of them can earn more stacking shelves at their local supermarket. And if we care about our older people, then we care about the people who care for them. And that is why we're putting a penny on income tax for health, because that is about health and social care. It's about having a plan that William Beveridge, the great liberal, when he drew up the plans for the NHS in the 1940s, for sure, if we'd been living to the ages we are today, then he would have drawn a plan for social care too. Today, in 2017, it's time to finish.

In 1996, the average cost of a house was about three times the average salary. They were very, very affordable. But now it's about eight to ten times the cost of an average salary. The housing market has got completely out of balance. simply an issue of supply and demand. And we hear from all the parties, we're going to build way more houses than we have at the moment. It's about 150,000 a year is the amount that's been built over the last 20 years or so. It's not going to happen this time. It hasn't happened for the last 20 years. It's not going to happen again. But the reason is that it's got so out of balance is because of a huge population increase. And that's mostly due to rapid net immigration. There's been four million net immigration since 1997. All those people need to be housed somewhere. And that's driven up demand without bringing the supply in and the supply isn't going to come. What we have in UKIP is a policy of bringing immigration back into balance. It's going to take 20 years to get the housing market back into balance because it's taken 20 years to get it so out of balance. We've got to start somewhere. If we don't, the population of this country is going to be 80 million by 2050. That's simply unsustainable. There simply aren't enough homes and there never will be for 80 million by 2050. So we've got to be honest about this and UKIP are and we will deal with it.

We are taking the money back from the banks and we'll insist on doing so. We own the shares of most of the two big banks and the shares, as they rise in value, we will recoup that value for the country. So we've done a business for the country which is to nationalise, but then to get the profit when we sell on the shares at a future date. We will continue to fight for a global levy and it will happen this year, but it will happen in a way that banks don't leave our country but banks stay in our country. I come back to the question that I ask David again: he didn't answer the question on inheritance tax. Corporation tax he's going to cut by 3p for the banks. Why is he cutting corporation tax for the banks when he says that he wants to make sure that banks pay their share? To cut it by 3p is money taken from manufacturing industry. Again it's the same old Conservative Party.

I think there's two things that I would mention in addition to that. By the way, I'm proud of many of the things we did, but two things I would mention. First of all, I think we were too relaxed about inequality. I think that the gap got bigger and I think lots of people fell behind. Did you borrow too much? I think that the global financial crisis, I think the global financial crisis is what drove borrowing up.

No, Gordon Brown, what I'm saying is if you care so much about making sure that out of the rubble of this recession we create a new economy, why won't you, and indeed why won't David Cameron, take the radical steps needed to reform our banking system? Nothing has happened which will prevent a disaster in the banking system because of the greed in the banking system from occurring all over again. We're the only party saying split them up, make sure that they lend to viable British businesses here in Bristol and elsewhere. That's the way to get the recovery going.

But Angela, your party is committed to continuing with £18 billion worth of public sector... Don't shake your head. This isn't my words, this is the IFS. The IFS have outlined the conspiracy of silence between the Labour Party and the Conservative Party on £18 billion worth of public sector cuts.

Thank you. Thank you, Abigail. And can I first of all say good evening to everybody and thank you for you coming to be an audience at this programme tonight, which is an important part of the election campaign. Let me tell you about some of the things that I did as Home Secretary, Abigail. I said that I would ensure that we were dealing with extremist hate breaches and I excluded more than any Home Secretary before me. I said I would do something about stop and search because I don't think anybody should be stopped and searched on the streets of our country because of the colour of their skin. I said I would be tough on crime and I said I would ensure our police and our security services had the powers they need to be able to do their job and I gave them those in the legislation that I put through. And I made sure that we kept the records of criminals and terrorists on the DNA database, whereas Diane Abbott actually wants to wipe them clean. And I don't think that's a good idea because that helps us catch criminals.

I think few people would dispute that young people are having it tough at the moment. I would agree with the points that have been made about not wanting to divide society. Young people have a raw deal. But let's take a look at who's engaged in the political process. Older people, on the whole, vote much more in greater numbers than younger people do. If younger people voted, if they... I would advise everybody to look at all the political parties, look at our values, look at our vision, look at our principles, get involved and use your vote. Yes, there should be votes at 16, but that 18 to 25-year-old age group are the group that vote in fewest numbers. And if they increased their voting habits, then I think policy would change to follow that as well.

Well, I do appreciate how difficult it can be, particularly when you're starting out in your career at the bar, if you're doing legal aid work, if you're doing criminal work, I know it's very difficult. And there is actually, as a result of that, some people are not going into the profession. We do actually spend more money as a country on legal aid than almost any comparable country, more than France and Germany and other major countries. So there is a very significant budget of billions of pounds for legal aid, but we have said that we're going to be spending more on the criminal justice system, particularly on prisons, and those parts of the system I think do need now some investment. These were not easy choices, you know, they were choices born out of a very difficult circumstance that we came in in 2010 when Labour had crashed the economy, the debt...

I have, I have. And it was crass, and it was crude, and it was wrong. And men say dreadful things sometimes. But the point here... That's just all right then. No, they do. They do. But you know something? Some men in particular. If all of us were caught out for what we'd said on a night out after a drink...

Yes, it seems to me that there are, of course, individual things we need to do to help young people. We need to make sure they are able to access housing benefit, that we do build the homes that we need. But do you know what? It's more about building a future for our young people. What kind of country do we want to grow up in? I've talked about Brexit being a threat to our country and our young people. Do you know what? Climate change is a bigger threat. And if we don't tackle it by making a kind of joyous national endeavour to, for example, make ourselves energy self-sufficient in green energy, then we'll be letting our children down. We'll also be letting our children down if we turn into the kind of country that slams the door in the face of child refugees. I want to be the kind of country that welcomes those people. That's the kind of country I want my children to grow up in.

What we've said for the future, I can't guarantee to reverse what's already been done, but what we've said for the future is that we will protect education spending. So it will be protected at least against inflation. Now, the reason why we've made that choice, which we haven't made in other government departments, is because I think investing in education, investing in the future, is essential not just for our society but for our economy as well. Because I think it's a false economy to start cutting back on education spending. So I can't promise to immediately reverse the damage and I'm not going to make a false promise to you.

We've spelt out exactly where that money would come from. We would, for instance, stop this grotesque spectacle of this unfair tax system, which has been built up under succession of Labour and Conservative governments, where right now a greedy banker in the City of London pays a lower rate of tax on their capital gains than their cleaner does on their wages. Gordon Brown. We have a tax system which is grossly unfair.

OK, let's bring in Sean Barry, who wants to come in here. The NHS is staffed by people from around the world. Immigrants from around the world but also 60,000 people who come from other EU countries. You're far more likely to find a doctor treating you in the NHS than you are to find an immigrant ahead of you in the queue. This is one of the worst lists we've had to fight over the years.

Well, you say, what I'm doing, Faisal, is actually going out across the country asking people, yes, for me to gain their trust so that they will support me at this election. Because I think it is important to have a strong hand in the negotiations for Brexit, to have that strong mandate for those negotiations. But we also have a lot of challenges to face here in this country. And as I've said, there are Brexit is one of the challenges, getting that right, our ageing society is another, the divisions within our society is another and the issue of making sure we have a strong economy and facing the impact of changing technology and the impact that's having on our lives and our workplaces, everybody will know. These are big challenges ahead and the domestic policies that I've set out our policies to address those challenges and some of them will involve difficult choices but I think it's right if I'm saying to people I want a mandate to be Prime Minister and to have the government of this country that we're actually open with people that there will be some hard choices.

every urgent operational requirement that our armed forces have asked us for has been met. Now, I've got two big questions I've got to answer to the British people for. Why are we in Afghanistan? We're in Afghanistan because there is a terror threat and a chain of terror that comes from the Afghan-Pakistan border to our country and three quarters of the terrorist plots that we identify start not in Britain, start not in Europe, but start in that border area. The second question I've got to answer is how we can get our troops home, because that is what we all want to see at the end of the day. And we've got to build up the Afghan army, build up the police force in Afghanistan. And our brave troops are helping to train the Afghan army and police at the moment. And that's how we will gradually see the numbers of Afghan forces rise and our troops come down in number so that our troops can come home. As we do that, we've got to ensure that pay continues to rise for our troops, and those people who leave the forces have got to get proper protection, homes, health service, and, of course, the chance of jobs.
How is it good for our economy when we're in the longest recession per person?

Well, James, you work in manufacturing, and if there is a green jobs revolution, as they all call it, you will lose your job in manufacturing, and perhaps we'll come back to that later on in the programme. Look, I do agree with the point about the regions being left behind. Many parts of the UK feel as though they've been ignored for decades, taken for granted, and the argument for better infrastructure, the argument for access to broadband, even mobile phone connectivity in parts of this country is absolutely overwhelming. The question is, how do we pay for it? Well, Labour clearly are going to borrow vast amounts of money. The Conservatives are going to borrow some. We suggested cutting things like HS2, which cost £100 billion to benefit just a few thousand. You could help millions with £100 billion.

That's the opposite of what we're seeking to do. We have to recognise that Jewish communities and Muslim communities in this country and around the world are feeling very vulnerable at a time of a horrendous international conflict where tens of thousands of people have lost their lives, where we still have hostages that have not been released. And we've just got to talk about these issues in a really sensitive way that recognises the trauma that people are going through.

Well, we need to resolve the chaos of Brexit. We need to end the chaos of Brexit. We've seen for the last three and a half years it's costing more than people ever thought, it's taking longer, and it's not anywhere near done. There are so many problems in our country that do divide our country. the inequality that for too many people it feels like it's just too hard to get by to make ends meet and we need to resolve those things. But we know from the government's own analysis, from all of the economic analysis, that remaining in the EU will mean we have more money, we can invest more in our public services, we will be better able to improve people's lives and help to resolve that division.

Well, the last thing that I wanted to do was cause anyone, and particularly our veterans community, any hurt or upset. It was a mistake, and that's why I apologised unreservedly for that. And, you know, I'm deeply sad to have caused people hurt and upset by that. I was privileged to participate in all the commemoration events that I did, to speak to many veterans, to thank them in person for what they did, but also make sure that I can demonstrate to them how committed I am to making this the best country in the world to be a veteran for all the things that we are doing in government.

Let me tell Ed Miliband what we did. We took 20,000 bureaucrats out of the NHS and put 9,000 doctors and 7,000 nurses in. Now, he opposes those reforms. Presumably, he'd like to rehire the bureaucrats. I want doctors with stethoscopes, not bureaucrats with clipboards. But let me answer Terry very directly about the things we can really do to make a difference with our NHS. Dementia is a silent crisis in our country. We're raising the diagnosis rates. We need to keep going with that. This point about a seven-day operation is really important. Just a few miles down the road from where we are tonight is the Salford Royal Hospital. They carry out operations and scans and treatments on Saturday and Sunday as much as they do Monday to Friday and as a result they provide a fantastic service. So we can improve our NHS if we improve the way it works and go after the things like dementia, like public health, like some of the big public health challenges that put so much pressure onto our NHS.

Well, thanks for starters for sticking with us for a full 90 minutes. What I've tried to show you this evening is that there is an alternative to the two old parties. I know many of you think that all politicians are just the same. I hope I've tried to show you that that just isn't true. Whether it's on the questions from Alan on care, Jacqueline on crime, Helen on politics, Joel on schooling, Robert on the deficit, I believe we can answer all of those questions. I believe we can rise to all of those challenges if we say no to the old parties and yes to something new and something different. And that's what I offer, that's what the Liberal Democrats offer. So don't let them tell you that the only choice is between two old parties who've been playing pass the parcel with your government for 65 years now, making the same promises, breaking the same promises, making the same old mistakes over and over again. Because I think, despite all the challenges, all the problems we have, I think we can be really hopeful about the future. I genuinely believe we can have a better, fairer country if we do things differently. So give real change a chance. Trust your instincts. Support fairness. Choose something different. And that will give you and your family a better, fairer life. Thank you.

I am acutely aware that immigration is a really difficult topic for politicians because people do have concerns, and we can't ignore those concerns. But I also believe that politicians have a real duty to be honest with people as well. And the hard reality is our economy needs migration from the European Union. I mean, in Scotland alone, EU migrants contribute £7 billion every year. They are more likely to be in work, they are less likely to be claiming benefits, they are more likely to be university educated. than the population as a whole. One of our biggest challenges in Scotland is growing our working-age population. If we end EU migration, our working population, over the next 25 years, will fall, while our pensioner-age population will be going up by 50%. One last point, Julie. I need to go to Tim Farron. Public services are under pressure, but that's not the fault of migrants. That is the fault of the austerity agenda pursued by the Tories... Thank you. Thank you, Nicola Sturgeon, Tim Farron.

Well, I think that's a very good question, Hamza, and it's one that the UK government should be considering carefully. It's the reason why the Green Party would increase the government's international aid budget and take a more proactive role in working towards towards more peace especially in the Middle East at the moment and that's something the Green Party has been consistent on and also on tackling climate change because we know that with increasing climate change we are going to see increasing levels of conflict for example over water scarcity and it's going to cause increasing levels of migration and so yes UK policy on everything from climate to aid has a huge impact on migration. And if we are worried about numbers, and I have to say that I welcome migration, as you've heard from many of my other answers, but if we are worried about numbers, then the way to tackle that isn't to bring up the drawbridge, it's to reach out and to collaborate and to work internationally for the good of everybody.

Our funding has been disadvantaged in Wales since 1978, when the Barnett formula was first invented. When your party was in power and had an opportunity to sort that out, you didn't do it. Thank you. We deserve an additional £1.2 billion in Wales to take us up to parity with Scotland. If you get into government, will you give £1.2 billion to Wales?

Well, let me answer these questions. First of all, to Ed Miliband, we've created two million jobs because we've got a strong and growing economy. When he was sat in the Cabinet, about half a million people lost their jobs, so never mind zero hours. With Ed, there'd be zero jobs. But let me take on Nigel on this. Look, Nigel, you want to leave the EU. It's a very clear position. But the only way that can happen is by having a referendum. Now, I say, stay in, fight, get a better deal, but hold that referendum. The irony of Nigel's position is if people vote for him, they end up with Miliband... They get no referendum, no renegotiation, no immigration...

Well, we'll put it in place as soon as we possibly can. But I'll tell you that our priority would be to deal with the poverty on our streets at the moment. Sorry, when was that? As soon as we possibly can. We have to have legislation through and we do that. And we said that we would abolish the House of Lords and we're absolutely clear on that. But our priority has to be to fix the four million children in poverty and everything else. So we will do it, but we'll also make sure we end tuition fees and do all the other promises that we've said and we'll do it through our legislative programme. But one thing I would say...

Listen, people can make up their own mind about that. I'm determined to talk about Liberal Democrat policies. I didn't think you've got a lot of answers there. Take the cost of living, for example, what they're going to do about it. We're clear. Let's get free school meals to children from the poorest backgrounds. Let's help people with the energy bill through the Warm Home Discount. Let's make sure children who are living in poverty, the universal priority.

You know what the difference is, because we do not want a generation of young people growing up and not working. That's what happened in the 1980s under David's party. They left a generation, a wasted generation, and we've been dealing with the consequences. So we are saying, every young person should either be in training with an apprenticeship or at college or university, or preparing for that training. And everyone should be compelled to do one of these things. You can't have a something-for-nothing society any more, and under-25s will have to take up the opportunity of work or training if they're not employed. That's the best way to do it. But we've got to get young people into work. We cannot have a situation where they're on the street corners and we can't have a Conservative Party that keeps opposing all our jobs proposals, including our future jobs fund, that is designed to stop youth unemployment in the country. I just think the Conservative Party are still living in the age of the 1980s and 1990s. The same old Tories.

Why not sign a free trade deal with the biggest economy in the world? No, we're in it now. We're in the biggest economy in the world now. We can sign free trade deals with India, with China. Our economy can boom off the back of trade. That's the way we will get the economy moving. And the only reason we have these opportunities is because the people were sensible enough to vote to leave the European Union last year.

I have to say, I think a lot of working people like you feel quite resentful. The harder you work, the more taxes you pay, the less disposable income you have. And yet there are increasingly people opting just not to work. And that number has rocketed over the course of the last few years. So I understand how you feel. Your energy bills are too expensive. They have been for the last 20 years. Why? because we load money, we load tax onto your energy bill to give to wind farm companies. It's kind of the poorer in society giving to the richer in society. Rents, cost of housing, too high. That links back to the previous question. The population explosion has made housing, whether you buy them or rents, too expensive. Reducing net migration to zero would ease that. Getting rid of those subsidies on your energy bills would ease that. But the big one's taxes. Even during Tony Blair's time, the top rate of tax was 40p in this country, and it was paid by 1.5 million people. By the end of 2027, 8 million people will be paying 40p tax. So we're dragging more and more people doing middle-income jobs into higher and higher taxation. And that's why life is so tough. And to hear Penny Malden, whose government have put the tax burden up to the highest in this country since 1948, pretending they're a tax-cutting party, frankly, it is dishonesty on a breathtaking scale.

Here's the problem I have with you, Nigel, is you want to exploit people's fears rather than address them. And that is the problem with what you say on so many of these issues. You want to end all the racial equality laws, apparently. One of your MEPs made a disgusting slur on one of Nicola Sturgeon's ministers, Hamza Yusuf, and called him Abu Hamza. I mean, every time, Nigel, you cross the line. Now, look, I'm the son of immigrants. And I've recognized the Labour Party needed to change. And we had to do a lot of hard thinking about this. And I don't think we should just dismiss people's concerns as prejudice. But for the unity of our country, the diversity of our country, which is a strength, we've got to handle these issues in an appropriate, sensible, and balanced, not exploitative way. And I'm afraid, Nigel, I think you fail that test.

Nigel Farage. Well, like all markets, housing is about demand and supply. One of the big problems we've got is we have to build a new house every seven minutes in this country just to cope with current levels of migration. So a rapidly rising population has put massive, massive pressure on house building in Britain. Reducing immigration helps, but it doesn't solve the problem. There is a chronic lack of housing. What UKIP proposes is a brownfield building boom. Let's establish a brownfield register. Let's help people with decontamination grants to build on that land. Let's offer incentives such as no stamp duty on properties that are built on brownfield sites. By doing those things, by using empty government buildings, and there are plenty of them, we could produce 200,000 new affordable homes in this country every single year. But it's something we're going to have to start doing very quickly and we should make sure that all new social housing is for UK nationals only. Natalie Bennett.

Well, I've made clear to my candidates what the standards are, and they've seen by my actions what the consequences are if they fall short of those standards. But the Prime Minister hides behind a process. He could have taken decisive action last week, even the week before. But he eventually got round to it when he was bullied by other people into doing it. That's not leadership. And he talks about integrity. I'm sorry, when you get convicted for breaking the COVID rules, which you imposed on the rest of the country, you really shouldn't be talking about integrity. and politics.

What I want to do is make sure they get their treatment as quickly as possible, which is why we're putting more money in, hiring more doctors and nurses, and doing things differently so they can get the care they need in different ways, often outside of hospitals. Now, I can't promise that's going to be solved overnight. And to give you the context here, Ronald, here's the thing that we're grappling with. During the pandemic, six million referrals that otherwise would have happened didn't happen. Just think about that for a second. Six million. And that's what we're now grappling with. That's why the NHS is under the pressure that it's under. Six million missed referrals that we are now catching up on. So the NHS is doing more today than it's ever done in its history because of the investment we've done and all the improvements we've made. It's not treating more people. Yes, the NHS is today carrying out more checks, tests, scans, operations, treatments, appointments than it's done in its history. But when you're dealing with a six million missed referrals that we are now catching up on, it is going to take time to get them down.

Well, the most important priority was the first one. Because when I got this job, inflation was at 11%. And I think everyone knows the last few years have been difficult, the impact that was having on all your bills. Every week, you were seeing the impact in your shop. And that's why my priority was to bring down inflation, to ease that burden. And it wasn't a given. And it was 11%. Now it's back to normal, ahead of our competitors, which is the result of a lot of hard work. But also- So let's just go through the pledges.

Well, I believe that our limit for those with the skills that we need would include proper provision for doctors and nurses who we need to come into the country. But let me say something else about that. We have a problem at the moment. We have a problem because Vast numbers of nurses working in the health service at the moment are leaving because they're fed up with the system we have at the moment. And that's why we have to bring in nurses from abroad, often from countries which need those nurses, to stay there and nurse people in those countries. I'd far rather we kept nurses in our health service by having a system that helps them rather than hinders them, so they didn't leave the health service in the considerable numbers that they're doing today. All right. And the woman at the very back there.
Yes, well, we've already implemented charging reform. Pilots are in place to implement the reforms. They will be rolled out later this year.

I think it's completely consistent. What I'm saying is that the last election wasn't decisive. I believe I did the right thing by forming a coalition and having a decisive government to deal with the mess that we left. But I'm saying this time I think we can go one better. So I'm saying let's have a decisive outcome and these things don't have to be compromised on and we can have a really decisive government, more accountable government for you as a citizen.

I want to talk about a particular part of our NHS that hasn't been mentioned yet. One in four of the people in this room tonight will, at some point this year, face a mental health problem. And imagine if you went to hospital and you had a broken leg, and you were told you were going to have to wait six months to get it seen to, or you were going to have to travel hundreds of miles to get it sorted. You would be outraged, and rightly so. Now imagine being a teenager in mental health crisis, and when you need that help, you're told that you have to travel hundreds of miles, or it's going to have to wait weeks and weeks or months and months. It's not good enough. We've not given it the resource it needs. And that's why Liberal Democrats have identified £11 billion to invest in more mental health services over the next Parliament, to make sure that we can have those talking therapies when people need them, and make sure that we're properly dealing with mental health with the seriousness that it deserves.

This is what Brexit has done to our politics. It's about to do the same to our economy as well. Well, they respect the result and implement it then. I think there was a fatal flaw at the heart of the Brexit referendum and that is the responsibility of the Conservative Party. They put forward a proposition in a referendum and they had no plan. There was no plan behind it and that's why we're doing it. I think Brexit could have happened, and to go to the point that you were making earlier, if there was, if politicians were prepared to compromise, right? We're a passionate Remain party, right? But we actually voted in favor of a soft Brexit, right? But what happened was, and I think that would have reflected the narrowness of the results. So you find a compromise, okay, it's Brexit, but it should be a soft Brexit because it was a narrow result. Instead of, what happened is, the Conservative government took it ever further towards an extreme, Sorry, sorry, Adam, Adam, Adam, Adam.

They questioned some aspects of our programme, and we're happy to discuss that with them. But we are the party that actually produced the funding of it. The Conservative Party have produced nothing in the way of how they're going to pay for anything they suggest they're doing, other than tax cuts. It's very hard to see how you can expand services if you're cutting the income base of government in the first place. It's a fair question, isn't it? There has to be investment to deal with the inequalities within our society.

This is the choice you have. Boris Johnson, delivering Brexit in weeks and focusing on the NHS, schools and police. Or Jeremy Corbyn, ridiculously stuck in neutral on one of the biggest questions our country has ever faced. Only the Conservatives can break this deadlock and set Britain free.

Well, I think there were serious mistakes made by Paul O'Reynolds and the post office executives who were in charge, some of the lawyers, some of the people in Fujitsu. There's a public inquiry which I campaigned for because the people who've done this are appalling thing need to be held to account, and there's a criminal investigation which I totally support. I don't think I should prejudice that investigation or the outcome of that inquiry. I think the evidence that we've heard does speak for itself, but I'll let the judge in charge of the inquiry and the police make those final decisions. But I do hope, I really hope that people go to prison for this.

No I don't Leanne and let me tell you about the differences between me and David Cameron because you've raised this point and it is an important point. He refuses to have a mansion tax on the most expensive homes above £2 million to fund the NHS. Instead, he chooses to keep the bedroom tax. I'll have a mansion tax. I'll also have a banker's bonus tax, put our young people back to work. I will make fair choices, much fairer choices than him, and he wants to go much further on the deficit, he wants to go much further on spending cuts, and that will mean not just that we balance the books, but go further, and that will mean a crushing impact on public services. And that's the choice for working girls. Thank you, Nicola Sturgeon.

Well, Jacqueline, the system isn't working properly now. There's no doubt about it. We're not seeing enough police on the streets. We're not catching enough burglars. We're not convicting enough. And then when we do convict them, they're not getting long enough sentences. I went to Crosby the other day and I was talking to a woman there who had been burgled by someone who had just left prison. and he stole everything in her house, and as he left, he set fire to the sofa and her son died from the fumes. And that burglar, that murderer, could be out of prison in just four and a half years. Now, the system doesn't work, but that sort of sentence is, I think, just completely unacceptable in terms of what the public expect for proper punishment. What have we got to do? Well, we've got to get rid of the paperwork and the bureaucracy, and we've got to get the police out on the streets. We need very clear signals from our criminal justice system that if you cross someone's threshold and rob their home, you go to prison, and you go to prison for a long time. Nick Clegg.

Of course, Kamalina, yes, we do want you to stay. Of course we do. We want to make sure that our immigration system is reasonable in terms of numbers, which means, as we discussed previously, much, much lower numbers than we have at the moment. We want to make sure it's targeted at people with high skills that we need to come and work here so that it helps our economy to grow. And where there are people... I think it was... Was it Piers, I think, who was struggling to recruit in... I think it was the hospitality sector. We want to focus first on trying to get people who are living here already maybe on benefits or maybe just not working at all into those jobs. I think that's the sort of coherent strategy that I hope will encourage you to stay in this fantastic country, in this fantastic county of Essex.

Our thoughts are, of course, with all of those tragically affected today. And I pay tribute to the members of the public and the police for their incredible bravery. But turning to tonight's debate, everyone in this country is frustrated. Frustrated that Parliament's been stuck going round in circles on Brexit. We all want to move on. And with your support, we can do exactly that. We have a new Brexit deal ready to go. We'll bring it back to Parliament before Christmas and leave by the end of January. And then we can get back to focusing on your priorities, the NHS, schools, cutting crime. But the only way to make this happen is to elect a Conservative government. So let's end the delay and get Brexit done. Because there's so much else that we have to do to unleash the potential of a proud, prosperous and united Britain. Thank you.
Burqas and niqabs. Get it right.

These are, these are, these are things which will give people in this country a better deal. They involve fair play for people who've paid their taxes, who've paid for the cost of their NHS operations. Who've paid for the cost of their education. For heavens sake

If we make it quite simple, we need three things. Simplistically, we need more supply, but we need the right houses, and we need good jobs that pay well. We need more houses. And again, it comes back to looking at what parties' records are, not just what they're saying now in a campaign. And over the last parliamentary term in Scotland, the SNP exceeded their target of building 30,000 affordable homes. And by affordable, I mean houses that actually reflect what people are earning, what the average earnings are in the area. And that can be a variety of different types of home, that can be social rent, council houses, shared equity schemes, but to make sure that there is that increase of supply of affordable homes. But also looking more broadly, whilst housing is devolved, there are certainly policies that are made at Westminster that have a a really negative effect in Scotland. And since 2013, the Scottish government, the SNP government, have been spending about 400 million pounds trying to mitigate the effects of the bedroom tax. And we're also really disturbed to see the scrapping of housing benefit for 18 to 21-year-olds, because that will have a very negative effect on people in Scotland who are in need of housing support. So we're building more. We're building the right types of houses. But we need a bit of help from Westminster.

Because they're not in favour of that. You've correctly interpreted what I've said. So what happens to the government of the country? I'm saying, if I'm to form a government, and I hope to win the election in seven days' time, we're 23 seats short of an overall majority, and so I hope to govern the country as a majority, but if I don't, for whatever reason, I've been very clear, I would not lead a government that did not contain that pledge. Couldn't be clearer. Briefly you, sir.
Well, I think it can. I think it can. It's happened in our country before. We're going to have to skill up. We're going to have to find...

I think it's great we're having these debates and I hope they go some way to restore some of the faith and some of the trust into our politics because we badly need that once again in this country. The expenses saga brought great shame on Parliament and I'm extremely sorry for everything that happened. Your politicians, frankly all of us, let you down. There is a big choice at this election. We can go on as we are, or we can say no, Britain can do much better. We can deal with our debts. We can get our economy growing and avoid this jobs tax. And we can build a bigger society. But we can only do this if we recognise we need to join together. We need to come together. We need to recognise we're all in this together. Now, not everything Labour has done in the last 13 years has been wrong. They've done some good things, and I would keep those. But we need change, and it's that change that I want to help to lead.

No, I said 20,000 have been cut by the Conservative government. We would put 10,000 more on. What I would also want to add, and I think it's important, is the response of people in this country to what happened in Manchester. I was in Manchester straight after the event took place, and Tim was there as well, and Amber. The most amazing sense of community unity came together. They were not going to allow that individual who killed those people to divide our communities. They were going to be united and responsible.

Well, I think there is obviously it's been a very difficult time recovering from the appalling recession that we had. It has been tough. It has been difficult. But Britain has still got some great strengths. We've got a strong and growing economy. We're creating jobs. We're part of all these important networks in the world, whether it's the G8 or NATO or the European Union. We have clout in the world. We can get things done in the world. Think of what some of our people are doing right now. We've got nurses in West Africa helping to deal with Ebola. We've got some of the most brave and professional armed services anywhere in the world. And tonight's a good moment to say thank you to them for all they do. We're a nation of great inventors.

Yeah, I just wonder what everybody thinks about health tourism. Leanne made the point that it was christened the National Health Service, but as we now have a lot of people coming into Britain and using the National Health Service who are not British residents, that is estimated to cost about £2 billion a year. I just wonder, would the panel agree with me that actually it needs to be the National Health Service and wouldn't it be a sensible thing if, like every other sensible country in the world, we said to foreign workers who come to Britain, you must have health insurance when you come here?

Everybody's got to behave in a grown-up, responsible way, even if they don't during the election campaign. Well, we heard Ed Miliband here saying he wouldn't depend on the Scottish National Party. Shall I try and answer the question? Yes. David Cameron or Ed Miliband are going to walk into Number 10 as Prime Minister. I don't think that's much in doubt, and you have to decide which of those two. The real question is, who's going to go in there alongside them? Is it going to be Alex Salmond? Is it going to be Nigel Farage or is it going to be me and the Liberal Democrats? And my great fear, as I was saying earlier, is if you have David Cameron dancing to the tune of Nigel Farage, or the swivel-eyed brigade on the right wing of the Conservative Party, or you have Ed Miliband basically at the beck and call of Alex Salmond, you lurch off to the right and the left, which is not what we need as a country.
A national treasure. You want to privatise it. That is what you want to do. And you in Scotland, of all places, I mean, look, the NHS is very badly run. Let's change the funding model.

Well Clare, I have two young girls and this is what they talk to me about more than anything else. And of course we're going to tackle climate change and get to net zero. This election is about how we do it. And I don't think that British people should solve a global problem all by themselves, which is why I'm also prioritising our country's energy security, and your bills. We've heard a lot about the cost of living challenges today. So I have made some decisions, bold decisions, that will mean that we can save all of you thousands of pounds, still meet our targets, and we can ensure our country's energy security. The contrast is clear at this election, because Keir Starmer will reverse all of the changes I've made. That's going to cost everyone and you thousands of pounds and make our country less energy secure by banning North Sea energy. And that, I don't think, is the right course for our country.

Well, I think everybody feels just heartbroken at what we've witnessed today on London Bridge and huge, huge courage shown by those members of the public and the emergency services that should be commended. I think the other thing that I'm struck by when we face these types of challenges as a country, and we've seen these in London, we saw them a few years ago in Manchester, is the way in which the community pulls together is inspiring, and that gives me real hope about how we come together as a country. In terms of security we do need to have more police and we've set out plans to increase police numbers by 20,000. I think it's also important that we look at what the police do and that community policing and one of the threats that many people worry about in their everyday security is how safe they are on the streets and in particular how safe their children are on the streets. Far too many parents have had that horrendous call of a young person being stabbed, being another victim of knife crime, and something which in Scotland has worked really, really well. It started in Glasgow 15 years ago, the Violence Reduction Unit, the police working with public services, with the NHS, with social care, social work, to provide a holistic approach to tackling violence, a public health approach, It has seen the stabbing rate reduced by two thirds nearly in Glasgow and that is something that we should replicate elsewhere.
'and it's in the interests of the minorities themselves to maintain a strict control'. That's what I think. It's not what Mr Blair thinks. That's the difference in principle between us.
So you're opposed to trying to cut the energy bills of schools?

OK, Nicola Sturgeon and then Richard Bennett. Well, I'm still a bit taken aback hearing Nigel Farage accuse other people of lying about spending. Am I the only one who remembers the £350 million lie on the side of... The NHS will have £350 million a week more because of Brexit. I've just done it.
But you're on the final solution.

Well, I've never changed my stance, pretty much, on anything. I don't flip-flop. I've always been, for example, a Brexiteer. And look, you know, back when I joined UKIP, you don't join UKIP for a career. You join it because you've got principles. And our principle was simply this. We wanted to get our country out of the European Union. And we were very successful on June the 23rd. last year. Now, what we've got to do is we've got to ensure that we get the Brexit that people voted for. A Brexit whereby we control our own borders and cut immigration. We get full control of our waters back and our fisheries. We take full control of our laws and bring them back to Westminster. We leave the ECJ and indeed the ECHR. We're free to sign our own trade deals all over the globe and we're out of the single market. And, most importantly, we pay no divorce bill to the European Union. Because since we've been members, since 1973, we've paid £183 billion net in membership fee alone. I'd be strong enough going into these negotiations. I just hope the Prime Minister is as strong.

Well, we wouldn't pay the 35%. We've already said that to the doctors. We can't afford it because the government's already broken the economy, and there are many things that now can't be afforded. So we're not going to pay that. But what you can't do is what the Prime Minister has done, which is to say... Well, you just said you'd resolve it.

Well, look at what... Thank you. Let's get back to this central issue. Look at what I said I would do when I set out to be Mayor of London ten years ago. No, in fairness, Mr Johnson, that's not the question. The question is about the NHS. I'm having questions about whether I can deliver. We massively cut crime. I promised to cut crime and we did. We had a huge investment very substantially, a 50% cut in the murder rate. We massively invested in transport and cut delays on the tube by 30%. We outbuilt the Labour Party when it came to housing. I overdelivered on my promises. When you look at what I promised on the steps of Downing Street a few months ago, three or so months ago, I promised we would put 20,000 more police on the streets of our country. are coming there. I have talked to them. They are being recruited now. I said we would put the biggest ever cash boost into the NHS and it is happening. We are doing 20 hospital upgrades and 40 new hospitals. To get to all the points being raised, yes, of course, I understand that things have been tough in the NHS, and I understand the pressures that the NHS is under. But as I say, I've talked to hundreds of doctors and nurses in the last few months. I know the massive, massive demand that the NHS faces. But we can only meet that demand if we have a dynamic economy. And I'm afraid we won't get this economy really moving again We won't get the investment coming in, and you know what I'm going to say, until we get... It's true. It happens to be true. Until we get Brexit done. And at the moment, that is hanging over us. It's paralysing Parliament. And to get back to your trust point, that is, in my view, the single biggest corrosion of trust in politics. People voted three and a half years ago for their will to be respected. Every other party that you've had on tonight is basically trying to frustrate that outcome. We have a deal, and it's a good deal. It will allow us... It's a great deal. It's there. It's ready to go. It's, as I never tire of saying, oven ready. OK, we're out of time. I'm so sorry. And it will enable us to have 2020 not as a year of two pointless referendums, but 2020 as a year of prosperity and growth. That's what I want to see.
We propose, well let's put it beyond that, we're proposing an NHS Protection Act so that in law it would not be possible to put the NHS on the table and it would be protected from trade deals.

We will... First of all, about the flooding, I'm very, very determined to make sure that everybody whose lives have been affected by flooding gets the compensation and the help that they need. You've seen our announcement that every damaged home is going to get at least £5,000 to help them with repairs. in addition to what we're doing to help with council tax and other things. And we'll also help with insurance. But on your basic question about tackling poverty, of course we want to deal with not just the expression of poverty, but also with the causes of poverty. and that means dealing with educational standards. It means spreading ambition and hope around the country by investing in education, investing in social services, adult and child social services as we are now. Also, it's not just a question of the living wage, it's also a question of helping people with the cost of living. and that's why we're cutting national insurance contributions and trying to put more... doing things to help people with the cost of their heating, their fuel bills, and putting more money into people's pockets every year. We want to help people with the cost of living. How much is cutting those national insurance levels? How much is that going to save people every week?

Thank you. Sean Barry. I need to point out that... The Conservatives insisting that they've been managing the economy well is just not true. The credit reference agencies downgraded our credit scores based on the fact that the Conservatives were borrowing money but not saying how they were going to pay it back. They weren't making long-term investments. We saw the spending review delayed. Now the key thing about our plans is they're long-term and they pay for themselves. They bring money back in, in warmer homes, in cleaner air, in less burden on our health service, on healthier people. Now your plans don't do any of that. You're filling in the gaps that you left with 10 years of austerity now. You have to admit that was a mistake. Thank you. in response to that, Rishi Sunak.

There are other Prime Ministers who've said, look, this is my, you know this - I'll serve this term but I won't serve another term. I mean it's not, its - I think the public actually has a very clear and sensible view of this.

Yes, absolutely, and our party's had a very long-standing tradition in respect of that, because we believe that money can be better spent on conventional defence forces. And what we've seen in Europe over the course of recent years is how absolutely vital conventional defence forces are. But may I just return to the original question and thank Francis for his father's contribution. We need to be standing with our veterans. We need to make sure that our military is fully funded and that we have more people serving and that we look after them when they become veterans. And just finally on the Prime Minister. A Prime Minister who puts his own political career before public service is no Prime Minister at all. A Prime Minister who puts his own political career before Normandy war veterans is no Prime Minister at all. So it's incumbent upon all of us to do our national service and vote the Tories out of office.

That's what we all need. We're unequivocal. The NHS is not for sale, never has been, never will be. And this kind of desperate conspiracy theory that we keep hearing from the Labour Party is actually... I read today a former Labour health minister described it as delusional. So you really must stop making these baseless allegations. The real risk to the NHS are your reckless plans for the economy, Richard, which will mean there isn't money to invest, and silly plans like the four-day week. So again, how much is a four-day week going to cost the NHS?

The changes we've made actually are saving money in the NHS and because we got rid of 20,000 administrators and bureaucrats in the NHS, that's one of the ways we've been able to fund the extra doctors and the extra nurses. Again, I don't want to hark on about what I inherited, but when I became Prime Minister, the growth in the number of bureaucrats was going up four times as fast as the number of nurses. But I make the point because it's been a recovery job. That's the point. And the NHS is stronger today than it was five years ago.

Surely one of the problems here...Gordon Brown talks about a plan in the future, but has no details on it. David Cameron talks doing something now, but also has no details. Surely what we could do for once is get the Chancellors and Vice-Chancellors of all the parties together. I've suggested we call it the Council for Financial Stability, so that - you're right - the point of the question is that politicians are finally upfront with you and straight with you about how bad this problem is, and how long it'll take for us as a country, together, to deal with it.
Let's just be sensible about what we do. Net zero is a bad policy and it's bad for people.

Returning to the question, you don't have to be an economist to see that our economy is not working for the majority of people. Penny is talking about a cost of living crisis, and yes, of course, that's what it is, but you might more accurately describe it as an inequality crisis, because actually some people in this country have been doing very, very well. Some people got substantially wealthier during the pandemic while many of the rest of us struggled. It doesn't have to be like this. We could have well-paid jobs, secure jobs, and a welfare system that really acts as the safety net we need to catch people when they fall. A green economy would create jobs, well-paid jobs distributed all over the country, for example, on a nationwide home insulation program, which would bring down everyone's bills, help with the cost of living in the here and now, give us warmer, more comfortable homes, create hundreds of thousands of green jobs. and support local businesses. And the Greens would provide a real safety net, starting with a £15 minimum wage for all ages, removing the ageist cap on young working adults, an immediate uplift to universal credit, and, my final point, removing the cruel two-child benefit cap that both the Conservatives and Labour's party support, even though it holds 250,000 children down in poverty unnecessarily. Thank you, Paula Denyer.

We want to freeze lawful immigration with the exception of health care. But let's remember, we've got 5.2 million people, one in eight of the working age population, on out-of-work benefits. British citizens, we need to get those people, as many as possible, back into work. We've got to help businesses and motivate businesses. So those people into the NHS and into the care sector. We've got to skill up and train up our own people. Of course it does. These are urgent sectors that need jobs now. But in the 1980s and 1990s, we were training up our own people, our own young people, straight out of school into wards, into universities. Why don't we freeze the training caps on training doctors and nurses? This is madness. We've got to focus on our own people, skilling people up, motivating, training, developing young people.

OK, you made the point, let the Prime Minister answer it. There are two things I want to do, because I do care about education. As I've just said in response to Sally, I think it is so important for every youngster to get the best start in life. That's why we will be putting more money overall into the schools. We'll be ensuring the pupil premium is there for those children who are disadvantaged. But we will also ensure that there is a fairer distribution of school funding. Because at the moment, as you probably know, there are some schools that get twice the amount of money per pupil than other schools in other parts of the country. I want to see a fairer system of funding and in doing that we will make sure that no school sees a budget cut when that fairer funding is introduced. But getting a good quality education isn't just about the money going into schools. It's about ensuring that we're encouraging people to come into the teaching profession. So we're going to give student loan forgiveness to people who come into teaching and stay in teaching. It's about a diversity of types of school. So we see innovation and creativity in education. So we genuinely can say in this country that how far you go in life depends not on where you come from or who your parents are, but it depends on your talents and your abilities and your willingness to work hard.

I want to get rid of the Tories on May the 7th, and I think it's a disgrace that David Cameron's not here tonight to defend his record. But I want to see... I... I want to see the Tories replaced with something better. When Ed talks about cuts outside the protected areas, that's jargon. Let me tell you what that means. That means cuts to social care, to social security, to local government services, to defence. Ed's in the position that he's so thrilled to austerity, so scared to be bold, that he's not even doing the right thing by the NHS. He's not promising the money the National Health Service needs. I think it's time not for a pretend alternative to austerity, it's time for a real alternative to austerity. That's what I'm offering. And if Labour won't be bold enough on its own, I think people should vote for parties that will hold Labour to account and make them bolder.

Ed Miliband still thinks the last Labour government didn't borrow enough, didn't spend enough, didn't tax enough. I mean, that is the truth. And young people suffer the most. When you have an economy with out-of-control welfare, out-of-control debt, out-of-control spending, young people suffer the most because the deficit and the debt is on their heads.

I will make no apology for being a government that wants to defend this country. We will make sure that our defense budget is well funded. We will do that by having a strong economy. You were selling arms to Saudi Arabia? We have to make sure that we can do that by having a strong industry.

Well actually the situation is worse than Gordon Brown describes because actually according to the government's own figures we are potentially heading for power cuts in 2017 and actually nuclear power stations won't really come on stream by then. So we actually have a greater emergency and we do need to look at the level of gas storage we have in this country which is much lower than France or Germany. They have up to 100 days and we have a little over two weeks. And we also need to make sure that we get the renewables and maybe even extra gas capacity on stream faster otherwise we can see the lights go out. That's because we've had, over the last 13 years, so many different strategies and so little action.
Because we're prepared to look at different priorities, we can give the NHS an extra £9 billion a year. £2 billion extra for social care.

Oh, if you could redo one thing as your time as Prime Minister, what would it be? Well, there's definitely the thing, as I said, I haven't delivered. I promised better, less noise at Prime Minister's questions, more politeness. That certainly didn't work out. I fully accept that. Look, I think the most important thing I've had to try and do is turn the economy around, get people into jobs, get the deficit down, get the economy growing, because we were on the brink. And I wish that some of the things that we've done, like, for instance, the Help to Buy scheme that has got many more people able to buy their houses, I wish we'd done some of those things quicker. I wish we'd done some of those things sooner. Because in the end, you're going to have to make this huge choice in 42 days' time who runs the country. And all I'd say, what I've learned in the last five years, is nothing that you want to do will work without a strong and growing economy. The schools we want for our children, the hospitals we want when we're ill, those things need the strong economy.

to respond to something that Gordon Brown said, which is this issue of responsibility, saying he asked people to take responsibility. I think one of the problems in our country today is if you do the right thing, if you take responsibility, all too often actually you're punished rather than rewarded. If you work hard and save, actually you don't get the government behind you, you get You get punished if you do the right thing. Always in my constituency you're meeting couples who say, you know, we're trying to get everything together before we get married, before we have children, and they feel that people who don't do the right thing get pushed up the ladder ahead of them. And I think there's a sense of unfairness in our country today which goes to the heart of some of the issues we have in our politics.

I think National Service is transformational and positive for young people. I think cutting stamp duty and help to buy to get young people their first home is really positive for young people. I think creating more high-quality apprenticeships is really good for young people. And when young people getting into work, getting a tax cut so they can keep more of their hard-earned money is really positive for young people. And actually, I don't view society as an us versus them, because every young person also has a grandparent. And I think they want their grandparents to be looked after when they retire. And that's why I think it's right to do the Triple Lock Plus, too. And I think you agree with that as well.
Well, in fact, if you look at wind farm technology, as a means of electricity generation, you would have to cover a very large tract...

So, I mean, I think there's two things that need to happen. We all need to take personal responsibility, and that's everything from our recycling, to how we use packaging, to the economic decisions that we make of how we spend our own money, and indeed, what we think about in terms of where our food is coming from. Is it sustainable? Is it well-sourced? Is it local? But ultimately, this can't just be something that rests on each individual person. The government has a job to do to make sure that it is easier to make the decision that is the sensible decision. So if you want to go and buy an electric car and do your bit in that way at the moment, you'll find it is incredibly expensive. And the way to resolve that is to have government regulate so that car manufacturers have to bring forward the date at which they are not allowed to sell new diesel or petrol cars. And this is important for the climate in terms of global warming, but it's also important right now. And I want to tell you about Ella. She should have been studying for her GCSEs right now. But she's not. Because when she was seven, she was diagnosed with asthma. And when she was nine, she died. She died of polluted air. Because the air she was breathing was impacting her lungs because of the poison that we were emitting. And she's not the only person. 40,000 people a year die in our country because of dirty air. So if we get this right, it is not only that we are going to make sure we hand on to the next generation a planet that can thrive, we're going to improve people's health right now. This is urgent. This is now. It cannot wait. Thumbs up.

Leanne Wood. I'm speaking to everyone back home in Wales tonight. I'm from the Rhondda and I understand all too well the difficulties that have been faced by our communities in recent years. You tell me that jobs and services have been cut to the bone and that they can be cut no more. Plaid Cymru offers an alternative. We offer hope for a decent future for our young people, for thriving, successful communities. In a hung Parliament, Plaid Cymru can win for Wales, but we can only do that with your support. I'm asking you to support Plaid Cymru, the Party of Wales, to make our communities in Wales as strong as they can be. Please support Plaid Cymru to make Plaid Cymru Wales's voice in Westminster.

So 40% of people who do zero-hours contracts want to continue to do zero-hours contracts. But what we want to make sure is that that isn't used as a way of exploiting people. So if people are actually working regular hours, there should be a legal right for those people to move on to a fixed contract rather than zero-hours contracts. I'm afraid taking the minimum wage and just recalling it the living wage does not make it a living wage.

Well, thank you for your question, Alison. The answer is no. We put forward a very detailed costed manifesto, and it's got a big health and social care package at the centre, about £9 billion, and other things, for example, helping with free school meals, with costs of living. And we've shown how we'll pay for everything we've got in that. For example, we're thinking that some of the large corporates could pay more. For example, the oil and gas giants could pay more. They've made a huge fortune with oil and gas prices being so high. We've also said that the big banks, the tax cuts that the Conservatives have given the big banks, they should be reversed. That raises, for example, 4.3 billion. We've said to pay for our mental health programme to make sure that there's a mental health professional in every primary school and every secondary school. There should be an increase in the digital service tax on the social media giants, partly because some people think they're responsible for the mental health crisis for many young people.

I think Jean is just absolutely right. Here we are in Birmingham. This was known as the city of a thousand trades. And yet, in the last 13 years, we've lost 60,000 jobs in manufacturing. We've been losing manufacturing industry faster than the 1980s. It's been a complete tragedy. We've got to rebuild. How do we do that? Let's start with investing in our science base and making sure great universities like this are producing the scientists and entrepreneurs of the future. Let's make sure we invest in apprenticeships. So much of the government's training budget is wasted. We say, let's have an extra 200,000 apprenticeships. That would make a difference. But you can't ignore the basics of actually making it easier for businesses to employ people. That's why I come back to this point, that if we keep putting up the cost of one person saying to another person saying, "Come and work for me," we're never going to get more employment. It's science, it's technology, apprenticeships, raising the status of science teachers, making sure we reward entrepreneurship and, yes, having low taxes for businesses. That's part of getting them here and keeping them here.

That's not going to radically change things. I have to drop in the word that's not been mentioned, which is Brexit. Now, Brexit... Since Brexit happened, that's when you've seen the contraction of the labour market. That's when you've seen... For example, the UK took virtually every dentist out of Estonia previously, and hundreds of thousands of other people came across from Europe to work in our public services, and that stopped. And the other effect of Brexit, of course, is because of what's happened in terms of the loss, £69 billion last year to the UK economy, because that's happened, there's less money for public services. So what's rightly been said is, it's not just the people, you need to have the transport links, you need to have the investment in the health service and the care service, and people can talk about how much more is going into it. It's not enough. The population is growing, the needs of the population are growing. We have to grow the NHS. And the one way to do that, the biggest, most effective way to do it, certainly for Scotland, because we voted against Brexit, we never wanted it, we want to reverse it, and that's the biggest single thing you could do to reverse some of the problems that some people are talking about, especially in the NHS.
No, they suffer the most when you have a Conservative Prime Minister making unfair choices. Ed Miliband, thank you. Natalie Bennett. That's why they suffer, David.

Yeah, I mean, look, the problem that you've got with immigration at the moment is that there are simply too many people coming to the country. Last year alone, it was a city the size of Newcastle upon Tyne net, OK? We're adding a Birmingham. every three years. It's unsustainable. Even Theresa May said last year in the House of Commons that the immigration level that we have at the moment is economically neutral. Trade unions have said it's led to wage stagnation in working class communities. The Bank of England have admitted that that is the case too. The problem that we have is, quite simply, we're letting too many people come. The only way you solve it is by having an Australian points-based system whereby we have the right to say who comes and who doesn't come.

Howardism, if, if you want to use that word is a, a practical programme for dealing with the challenges facing this country. For changing the direction of the country and for putting in place things which really matter to ordinary people in their lives. That's why for example, we're talking today about crime, crimes gone up, crimes out of control. People need a government that's going to get a grip on the problems facing the country, and we're spelling out exactly how we're going to do that.

Right, we'll talk about Brexit being a huge threat or whatever you think. There's nothing bigger as a threat to our country, to our children's future, than the threat of climate change. You're worried about immigration? You see the mass movement of people as our land is desiccated and deluged by that climate change. So the reality is simply this. If it is simply for hair-shirt, muesli-eating Guardian readers to solve climate change, it ain't going to solve the problem. We're all stuffed. So let's do something that will actually make a difference. And that means a nationwide, indeed a planet-wide, concerted effort to build the future that we need. So for Britain, What can we do? We can make ourselves energy self-sufficient in renewable energy. 95% of the supply chain of energy in this country, of wind, of tidal and marine and coastal energy is British made. Why not rebuild our economy whilst guaranteeing our children's future?

Canada and Japan haven't signed the letter either. What did he say? He says he's taken the decision because he thinks it's in the best interests of America. I say that the Paris Agreement actually is important for us globally in terms of dealing with climate change. That's why the UK supported it and it's why the UK is continuing to support it.

It's not about softening our foreign policy. It is about absolutely condemning what happened in Manchester. Innocent lives were taken, young women and girls out enjoying themselves for the evening. That's the society we live in. That's the society I want to live in. And nobody should be allowed to take that away from anybody. The person who committed it did an appalling and abominable atrocious act, as did apparently those that were conspiring with him. The police investigation is ongoing. My point was that we have to have a foreign policy around the world that doesn't leave large areas without any effective government, such as in Libya at the present time, which can become a breeding ground of a place of enormous danger to all of us and indeed any Western country in Europe or in North America. My point was absolute condemnation. My point was that we need more police, not less. That's why we pledged to provide 10,000 more police on our streets and that we wanted a foreign policy that didn't leave large areas of the world ungoverned so that we had a more secure future for all of us. And do not allow this to become an attack on our multicultural society or on the wonderful faith of Islam. This is a perversion of Islam, what was done in Manchester, as indeed other attacks have been. What we need is a strategy that protects us, but a foreign policy that engages with the rest of the world as well, to bring about a safer world for all of us.

I think it was a big factor in it. I don't think it was the only factor, but it was a big factor. There is also an issue that communities and local government are often not sufficiently funded to deal with groups of people coming in, and so we would restore what Gordon Brown had in 2009, which was a migrant impact fund.

So to answer the question, I'm more than disappointed. I'm appalled that President Trump has walked away from a global approach to one of the biggest challenges that we face at home and abroad. And what he's done is what many of us fear he's going to do in a number of different ways. He's walking away from having a multilateral approach, working together with other countries to deal with serious problems. And that's why if the UK had a special relationship with the United States, and we remember of course the first trip that was made by Theresa May was to see a climate change denier, namely Donald Trump, what kind of influence was used by the Prime Minister then to make sure that he didn't go forward with this disastrous step? Absolutely nothing. This is a government that has got rid of the climate change responsibility at the cabinet table. It is absolutely appalling. So what we need to do is we need to do everything that we can at home. I'm delighted that more than 50% of all electricity generated in Scotland is now through renewables. We would have loved to have gone ahead with carbon capture and storage until Amber and her colleagues scrapped it. So there's much more that can be done. So the Prime Minister and her government need to take climate change seriously in a way that they haven't done in recent years, and we need to impress on all of our international colleagues that we need to work together and get this global challenge sorted.
