Yeah, and I'd add that Plaid Cymru is proposing £20 extra for every child as well, because again, like the SNP, we recognise that we have to get to grips with the issue of child poverty.

Ed Miliband. Here's what I believe. Britain succeeds when working people succeed. But that's not the way it's been over the last five years. For five years, wages haven't kept up with bills. For five years, the NHS has been going backwards. For five years, our young people have been fearing they'll have a worse life than their parents. It doesn't have to be this way. If I'm Prime Minister, I'll raise the minimum wage to £8 an hour and ban exploitative zero-hours contracts, so we reward hard work again in our country. If I'm Prime Minister, I'll rescue our NHS, hiring more doctors and nurses. If I'm Prime Minister, I'll build a future for all of our young people, saying to our young people, if you get the grades, you get an apprenticeship, and cutting the tuition fee from £9,000 to £6,000. and will cut the deficit every year and balance the books. Some people will tell you tonight this is as good as it gets for Britain. I say Britain can do so much better than it's done over the last five years.
I think all our sympathies should be, obviously, with the victims of Geoffrey Epstein, and the law must certainly take its course.

Well I don't believe that we can work as a Labour Party with the Conservative Party on virtually anything. The reason being they've meant that more people are using food banks, they've made more people homeless, they've made the majority of people in our country pay the price for the political choice of austerity and it was deliberate. I do believe we need people from different parties, to back the interests of the 99%, not the super privileged 1% at the top. But that requires all parties to make clear that they won't do anything to put the Conservatives in power. People were very disappointed in the past when the Liberal Democrats said vote Lib Dem to stop the Tories, then they put the Tories in power. Let's get the Tories out and let's get a Labour government in.

Well, to be fair, it's always all about Nigel, and we've had this sort of clown personality before with Boris Johnson, who you talked about, the pandemic penny, who was breaking lockdown rules and spending billions of pounds of money on crony contracts.

It is absolutely true that the other side of the coin is proper welfare reform. We have got too many people who could work, who are offered work, but who don't work, and that has actually drawn a lot of people into our country. So we do need to reform welfare. 13 years have gone by when welfare hasn't been properly reformed. Can I just ask one question about this regional approach. I don't quite understand how you can ask people to come to one part of the country and rely on them staying in that part of the country.

What I want to make sure is that we don't have any whiff of that whatsoever and that we work hard, as I answered Andrew's question, to win the trust and confidence of the public. One of the things that really worries me about politics today, and I accept that I might actually have played a part in creating this, is the polarisation of debate. And I think there's a need to bring people together. And when I became First Minister just six and a half weeks ago, I pledged that I would reach out beyond my party to other parties, to other opinions, to try to bring people together. Because I think what's wrong with our society today is that we're not together, we are polarised, and we need to bring people back together again.

No, but they could be a lot better. And that is the big argument of this election. I think this is a choice between those who think this is as good as it gets for Britain and those who think we can do a lot better than this. I don't think it's good enough that we've got 700,000 people on zero-hours contracts. I think we can do something about it. And the Prime Minister said earlier that he couldn't live on a zero-hours contract. Well, I couldn't either. But let's do something about it. Let's turn this country into a country where if you work month after month, year after year, you don't end up on a zero-hours contract, but you have a legal right to a regular contract. That's just one of the changes that I would make. We can do a lot better than this. We're a great country.
I'd have rather had my job than work for extremists like Abu Qatada and Hizb ut-Tahrir.

Give me a real opportunity, Leanne. Let me tell you what's going to be in Labour's first budget. We're going to have a mansion tax on properties above £2 million to fund our National Health Service, something David Cameron would never do. We're going to abolish the bedroom tax. We're going to have a bank bonus tax to put our young people right across every part of the United Kingdom back to work. And I do say to some of the people on stage tonight, Let's not pretend there's no difference between me and David Cameron. You know, Nicola, there's a huge difference between me and David Cameron. Just the three things that we've mentioned and so many other things besides. And look, the real danger in this country is of a re-elected David Cameron who doubles the spending cuts, falling living standards and a threat to our National Health Service. And that is the big choice on offer at this election. But Nicola is saying you should be bolder.

I mean, the Welsh negotiated a very bad deal back in 1978, and the canny Scots negotiated a very good deal. But this all has to be rebalanced, because, frankly, English taxpayers are a bit cheesed off with so much of their money going over Hadrian's Wall, giving people no prescription charges and no university tuition, whilst in England they're charged. So there needs to be a rebalancing, and in the future, Scotland should receive less money per capita than it currently does. That would be fair.

To do everything I can to improve recycling systems in local authorities and elsewhere. There are, I think, 40 different local recycling schemes across the country, which means that we don't have a great record as a country on the world stage about recycling. Also, do everything I can to reduce the levels of excessive packaging. The waste of plastic with bubble wrap and all that goes with that. What about your life?
You haven't dealt with any of it. You have lowered British politics. Your leader has tolerated extremism and malevolence.

Well, I don't think this is about putting an arbitrary number on the issue. I think it's about being welcoming and looking at the reasons why so many people are fleeing their homes. poverty around the world, a changing climate. I want to be in a situation where we tackle the root causes of why so many people are fleeing their homes, whether that's a changing climate, whether that's war, whether that's poverty. And so the Green Party has clear commitments in the manifesto that's coming out to not just restore the global aid budget to 0.7% but to increase it further, to put more money into climate finance that helps the poorest countries in the world to both reduce their emissions but adapt to the changes that are happening as well.

Well I think I made in my opening remarks reference to climate change and that of course is one of the critical issues that we have to deal with to provide an optimistic future. But much broader than that we have to stop trashing our planet. If we think about in Britain today, we're currently collectively using the resources of three planets when we've only got one. We have to rebuild and invest in our economy to deal with that. And just to think about what my generation, the kind of legacy we're leaving your generation. Thank you. World Wildlife Fund figures. Just let me... This is really important. The fact is that in the last 40 years, in my lifetime, the world has lost 50% of its vertebrate wildlife. Thank you very much. Half of the wild animals have gone. Nigel Farage.

Well, Liberal Democrats are being really clear about where the money's going to come from. So we're saying corporation tax will go up a bit and that there will be some changes to capital gains tax, and that will enable us to make sure we have free childcare for people from the point at which they're going back to work from the nine months onwards. We're also saying that by staying in the EU we know that the economy will do better, there will be more taxation revenue and as a result there'll be more money for schools and to put into universal credit to help the most vulnerable. But we're also saying that there isn't any easy shortcut to this. that we all have to pay more if we want better services. So we'd put a penny on income tax for the NHS and for those who are promising a wish list of policies without saying that we'll all actually have to pay a bit more for it. They're just not being credible or honest with you.

We have a Prime Minister who referred to gay men like myself, you know, as bum boys in tank tops, referred to Muslim women looking like letterboxes. You know, this is actually creating the climate, right, where abuse becomes acceptable, whether it's abuse of women or of minorities. And that's why neither Trump nor Boris Johnson are fit to be in public. OK.

I tend to agree with you, Nicola. I do want to see the issue as to whether EU migrants can stay in this country decided and sorted out pretty quickly, just as I want it sorted out for British people who live abroad. However, you're saying I'm the spokesman for Theresa May. I do not have I don't believe that she'll get the best deal possible for Britain. I think she'll begin to backslide. I think she'll backslide on fisheries. I think she'll sell out our fishermen once again like a former Tory prime minister once did in Ted Heath. I think there'll be some sort of dodgy deal over freedom of movement as well. And I think she'll capitulate and we'll pay a divorce bill. The only way you'll get the real Brexit that people want and the real Brexit that people voted for on June the 23rd is by electing UKIP MPs to the House of Commons on June the 8th.

Rebecca, you speak for so many young people right across our country, so many young people I meet who are asking why they're paying the price of hard times. And that's what we've got to turn around. And that's what I'm going to do if I'm your Prime Minister. Look, the first thing we've got to do is we've got to guarantee all young people access to a good education. So you get a high quality apprenticeship if you get the grades. That's a guarantee the next Labour government will make. And we'll also cut the tuition fee from £9,000 to £6,000 because I don't want our young people drowning in debt when they leave university. Next we've got to create good jobs for young people. That's why we'll ban the exploitation of zero hours contracts and we'll say if you do regular hours, you get a regular contract. And lastly, we'll build homes again in our country, 200,000 homes a year by 2020 and get a fair deal for young people in the private rented sector where many young people are being ripped off. I believe in what I call the promise of Britain, that the next generation does better than the last. I believe we can restore it.

Well, first of all, we introduced the National Living Wage. The Conservative Chancellor did that at £7.50. We've pledged to increase that to 60% of median wage and then continue for it to go up with inflation. I spent a year as the Prime Minister's Apprenticeship Advisor when David Cameron introduced the Apprenticeship Programme where we're basically putting a levy, half a percent of the wage bill of very large corporations. So any company that has a wage bill of three million pounds, half a percent of that is taken in apprenticeship levy. So that's basically going to deliver about three billion pounds of investment into apprenticeships. Now, when I looked at this, The Americans emailed, said, look, can we come and have a look at what you've done? Because we delivered about 2 million apprenticeships in the five years when David Cameron was prime minister. And then we set a target of 3 million for this parliament as well. We were doing something good, yes. It could always get better, I think. And degree apprenticeships, for example, are something that really is pioneering. So a teaching assistant can become a teacher on a degree apprenticeship. A nursing assistant can become a nurse on a degree apprenticeship. and be paid as well as getting that degree apprenticeship. But when you look at countries like Germany, one of the sort of insights that I took from that is that in Germany, businesses were embedded in the education system. So every child had exposure to both the opportunity of going to university or going on an apprenticeship. And the system was pretty stable. And I'm glad that actually Labour worked with us very much on the apprenticeship levy and support the apprenticeship levy on this. On zero-hours contracts, which I think you mentioned, so the problem with zero-hours contracts when we came into office is an employer can say, right, you're on zero-hours contracts and you're exclusive to me. You can't go anywhere else. You've got to sit at home, wait by the phone until I ring you. What we did was we changed the legislation to say, no, it should be equal. If you want me to give you my time, I should be able to go to three, four employers.

I don't think it is helping the planet that much for us all to be squabbling with each other here. This is something we should actually be trying to find unity and common purpose on. Now, I'm proud of the fact that Scotland is leading the way. The UN recognises us as a world leader. COP will be in Glasgow next year. And we should be agreeing that Scotland, the UK, should be leading the world. And then we should be putting pressure on other countries. Because right now, the world is not on track to limit temperature rises to 1.5 degrees. We've heard your ambitions. And let's make sure that we focus on the ambitions that we need to

And I'm answering your question, Jeremy. You're not. You have asked me about the just about managing and how they're being helped. And what I'm going to point out is that if you have a social care system which relies on taxing those just about managing at higher levels, that's not helping them. What we're doing is putting forward a proposal which means that people don't have to sell their house in their lifetime to pay for care, it means they can pass on savings to their children, and it means there will be that cap. But it's fair across the generations. And there are older people today who worry that their children and grandchildren won't have a better future.
You're flogging an energy company to these guys. You're expecting them to put their money in.

Well, let's look for a moment at why we're talking about that number. As members of NATO, we are rightly asked to contribute a fair amount towards the defence budget. That's totally fair enough for NATO to ask for that. But the UK is already contributing the 2% that NATO asks. That doesn't mean, however, that we couldn't be spending that money more effectively. And as Greens, we are clear that investing in tackling climate threats, the biggest threat facing humanity, as militaries all over the world are already doing because they recognise that if not tackled adequately, it will increase conflicts over water, for example, increase famine and create millions of refugees.

Well, I have a very simple view about this, which is we should do everything we can to help disabled people face as few disadvantages as possible and to get genuine equality in our country. The Disability Discrimination Act was brought forward by my good friend and colleague William Hague 20 years ago and I think it has led to some really good changes, but you're right, the job isn't done. One of the things I think we need to focus on is, as you say, getting more disabled people into work. This year has been a good year. We've got, I think, 140,000 more disabled people have got jobs. But the gap between the disabled unemployment rate and the unemployment rate for the whole country is still too big. I want to see that cut in half over the next five years. And I think we can do that, but we need a culture change from employers as much as from anybody. There are some employers who are absolutely brilliant, who really go out there and want to recruit disabled people because they know if they don't, they're missing out on some of the brightest, best talent in our country. But not all employers are that good, and that's why we've established something called Disability Confident to try and encourage all employers to take this approach. So I would say we've done some good work, we've got more to do, but in the end, It all comes back to the bigger picture of a strong and growing economy. If we keep the economy strong, if we keep creating jobs, we can keep solving some of these issues.

I am fighting this election to win it, to win it on our manifesto, to win it on our manifesto which I believe will bring real hope to those people that have been denied it during these years of austerity.

Nicola Sturgeon. I was just chuckling at Richard saying that he doesn't like manifestos. I think the Brexit Party prefer putting their spending commitments on the side of a bus. He used to say we've got £350 million a week extra for the NHS. It's people like him that the public don't trust.

Adam Price from Plaid Cymru. Let's go back to Lucy's question. The reality of it is the NHS is facing the deepest crisis probably in its existence. And that is also true here in Wales. I'm going to refer to the worst ever A&E waiting list that we're facing, worse than England or Scotland. Pharmacies, you know, there have been plans to privatise pharmacies in the health board in North Wales, that same health board that has been in special measures with the Labour government because it's been in crisis. There's been no improvement. They brought in somebody then, a consultant, paid £2,000 a day, and he came up with a recommendation. What was it? that nurses working in the NHS in North Wales should take unpaid breaks. So yes, absolutely, the Conservative Party has got some questions to answer, but the Labour Party that's running the NHS in Wales has some questions too. We need to value our NHS, and unfortunately, there's been a lack of focus on this from both of the Westminster parties over successive generations.

Well, first of all, I respectfully disagree that we have the exploding population in our schools. We have falling roles in our schools at the moment, actually, and that is a problem for many of our primary schools. But what we've said is that we would end the VAT tax break on private schools to put 6,500 qualified teachers in the classroom. We've got teachers teaching lessons that they're not qualified in at the moment. We think that every child should have a qualified teacher in the classroom. And we have the Tories who knew and were warned about RAC. and the problems with our schools' buildings at the moment. We've got more temporary classrooms because of that, because they did nothing, but they cut the capital budget for schools at the same time, knowing full well that there is dangers with racked buildings. OK. Nigel Farage and then Penny Mordaunt.

Firstly, I just want to share the thoughts that others have expressed about the horrific events in London today. But as we look forward, just imagine, with Brexit done properly, we have an amazing future. And that's why I am here. I want the least well-off to benefit the most, with cheaper fuel, food, clothing, shoes, with wages growing faster than for decades. Imagine thousands and thousands of new jobs created where they are most needed, with hundreds of new fishing boats filling our harbours, many built in Britain. Imagine £100 billion of new local road and rail projects being built in every region, including the North East, which has been so left behind. I want us to be world leaders in training doctors, nurses, carers, world leaders in planting new trees and recycling. But we have to restore trust in democracy with major political reforms. I'm excited by the future. It's time to change politics for good. It's time for the Brexit party.

And I've been to Grimsby a number of times now, and my strong impression is that you're absolutely right about the challenges that people are facing. And we've had discussions about that even this morning with various groups around Grimsby, the very real challenges, the cost of living crisis, the challenge that people have with schooling and with health. But what I've also been struck by is the pride and ambition that people have in their place and in Grimsby and the sense that they want a government that matches their own ambition. I think the way to do that is to ensure that the government works with local communities, local businesses. We need a growth plan for Grimsby, which your fingerprints are on, I don't want someone standing in Whitehall and Westminster looking at a map and deciding what they think is best for Grimsby, because I think with people with skin in the game are the people who make the best decisions. So I want a government that partners with you, drives through the change, creates the conditions for businesses to thrive. If that means changing the planning rules so we get our roads sorted out, the congestions and the potholes are an absolute nightmare, create the conditions for businesses to thrive. I was really struck, the time before last I was up here, I was looking at some of the offshore wind turbines and talking to apprentices here in Grimsby, brilliant young people, by the way, who all lived within about 20 or 30 minutes of where they were working. they were very skilled going out to maintain those wind turbines. We need more of that. The supply chains for that should be in and around here, so they're not other parts of the country or abroad. That's the sort of challenge that I relish. It's what I want to do on day one, which is to get to this place where I can roll up my sleeves, work with you, have the respect to listen to what you're saying here, not sit a long, long way away and make decisions about what should happen in Grimsby. And I'm convinced that if there's a government that has ambition that matches the people of Grimsby, we can move this forward very, very quickly. And I can see some of the green shoots there already, because some of the work that's going on... We had a roundtable this morning with some of the businesses, the community groups here in Grimsby, that were telling me not just the struggles and the challenges, but what they were doing about it. I want to roll up my sleeves and work with them and to say the government is on your side. That would be a massive difference to the last 14 years.

Thank you. Thank you very much for your question. We face a climate emergency across the whole world. It's very obvious. Unusual and extreme weather patterns have got worse and worse. The poorest and most vulnerable people in the poorest and most vulnerable places suffer first. Others suffer later. We signed up to the Paris Climate Change Accords in 2015. I was there, I thought it was great, but it didn't go far enough and it's got to go much further. So on May 1st this year, I proposed to Parliament that we declare a climate emergency in our country and that's now been replicated in many Parliaments around the world and I'm pleased about that. But it does mean we've got to play our part by reducing emissions to get to net zero emissions. It also means the possibilities of a huge number of jobs and development in our country in dealing with this emergency. It means jobs in retrofitting houses to make them environmentally sustainable, to make them better insulated, to use less energy. It means green energy jobs, wind, wave, solar. It means big projects like Swansea Bay Lagoon. It also means a lot has to be done in protecting our biodiversity, our seas and our rivers. And so our whole strategy is to develop a green industrial revolution which will create hundreds of thousands of jobs in the future, starting very, very quickly. And we will be therefore playing our part in getting to a more sustainable world and a Labour government on the international stage, we'll be working with others to get to net zero and make sure that by our trading policies we don't end up inadvertently exporting pollution by having strong controls here but not insisting on equally strong controls somewhere else. Thank you very much. I'm very excited by everything that's in our green industrial revolution strategy, just as much as I admire those in the USA with the Green New Deal that they're putting forward.

Our manifesto will be out soon, but the broad points I can cover now. Tough on crime is a rhetoric that we hear a lot from other parties, but the reality is that not all crime can be tackled by being tough, and that's something that the Green councillors in Bristol, the largest council in the country that's Green-led, are prioritising. Because the causes of knife crime are complex, but they include the fact that a generation of young people are growing up without access to the services they need. Youth centres are being closed. services that they need to support them.

He's talking about both of you. But there's a choice for everyone. There's a choice for everyone. about change. You don't get change unless you have a plan to deliver change. Just saying it isn't going to bring about any change. As you have heard tonight, there is no plan to cut your taxes, no plan to reform the welfare bill, no plan to deal with illegal migration. That is the reality of it. Yes, there will be change. It won't be change you like, it will be change when your taxes are going up, when we become the soft touch of Europe for illegal migrants, when pensioners are paying tax for the first time. That's the change that is coming. So don't surrender to that, because your families are going to pay the price.

We've got to put our own people first. Would you, Leanne, open it up to 17,000 people? Nigel... 27,000 people. The question is, how do we fund the NHS? And if up to £2 billion a year is being lost on health tourism, surely that is a very real problem.

So continuing exactly that theme, the Green Party absolutely would give young people that chance to look again at the deal that's under negotiation by Theresa May, because we feel that young people have been so betrayed by this government, by previous governments too. And that's the case when it comes to, for example, the loss of freedom of movement, that ability to be able to study and work in so many other countries. That is a precious thing that has just gone. And I feel that you should have had a say on that as young people. I think we would also want to do things like scrapping tuition fees. We want to cancel the burden of debt on young people. Education should be a public good, not a private commodity. We would want to restore student grants and nursing bursaries. We want to make housing more affordable. We would introduce rent controls. We want to see more affordable public transport. We want to see a safer environment. Because one of the things that really concerns me is the environment that we are leaving for you. We are trashing this environment. Last year, the hottest year on record. We need to have a safer environment for you. And finally, votes at 16. Votes at 16 would make sure that you would have your voice over what kind of future you have.

Boris Johnson? Actually, that is not true. The whole of the UK comes out England, Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland together. We do free trade deals together. Northern Ireland is part of the customs territory of the UK and we are united. I have to say, I think it is a great opportunity for our country. It says unfettered access, I think is what it says. It says unfettered access. Question the fettering that will happen. One at a time, please. That is what the document also says. is that Northern Ireland is part of the customs territory of the UK.

Nicola Sturgeon, will you? I think people's lifestyles do have to change, but I also think governments and industry has to take the lead, because there's a relatively small number of companies across the world that are responsible for a disproportionate amount of emissions. Now, again, this is one of the areas where I say, look at what my government is doing, not just on what we're promising. We've got the toughest targets anywhere in the world. 75% of Scotland's electricity already comes from renewable sources. Last year, we planted 84% of all of the trees planted in the UK. We're in Scotland, 22 million trees. We're about to invest half a billion pounds in new bus infrastructure.

And that is exactly what we are doing. We have grown the number of health care professionals. And they're leaving. It's one in one out. We have 70,000 more nurses. You're pouring into a leaky bucket. We have a dental recovery plan. It's not working. New providers coming in and also mobile services now. This is the only way. What about the 40 new hospitals? This is the only way. We are going to get waiting lists down. What won't get waiting lists down is cutting the budget and taxing healthcare professionals, which is what Labour will do.

What I have tried to do tonight is to show that if we do things differently, we can be a force for good in the world. We can lead. We can shape the world around us, not just complain about the world around us. We have talked about a number of things this evening. We talked about Europe, Afghanistan, climate change, the Pope. We talked about things closer to home as well. Immigration, MPs' expenses, pensions, I believe on all those things, all those issues, we can act differently. Now there's still some way to go before this election is decided, but I hope that whether you're going to vote in the next few days by post or you're going to make up your mind on the ballot box on May the 6th, you agree with me that something really exciting is beginning to happen. People are beginning to believe, beginning to hope that we can do something different this time. Of course there are people who will try to block change. Of course there are people who are spreading fear to stop the change that you want. I think they're wrong. I think if we do things differently, if we stand up for the values that have made our country great, then we can be proud again. Proud of greater fairness here at home and proud also of standing up for the things we believe in in the world. We don't simply need to choose from the old choices of the past. We don't need to repeat the mistakes of the past. Don't let anyone tell you that this time it can't be different. It can.

We need to make sure that... This is a huge problem because we don't have enough NHS dentists for adults or for children, and some areas are deserts now where you simply can't get an NHS dentist. So we've got to fix this. There are two things that we would do, three things, but the first is... to make the money available for 700,000 urgent appointments in NHS dentistry, including 100,000 for children, which is desperately needed. The second is to incentivise newly qualified dentists to set up as NHS dentists by paying them money, by paying them money, to put down their roots in the areas where they're most needed, so we can recreate those NHS dentist practices. We have to do this. I just... In addition to the people who are frustrated in the way that you rightly are, I was genuinely shocked the other day. I went to Alder Hey Children's Hospital. This is a brilliant hospital. Do you know what? For six- to ten-year-olds, the most common cause of admission to hospital is rotting teeth to have their teeth taken out because they haven't seen a dentist. It's a disgrace in 2024 England and Scotland and Wales. APPLAUSE That was quicker, at least. You were. Do you use a private dentist? Well, we're at an NHS dentist practice, but I'm private because I'm an adult and therefore don't get it on the NHS, but our kids get NHS dentistry, and I believe in it. OK.

There are 43,000 nurse vacancies at the present time. It was a Conservative government that cut the nurse bursary. It was a Conservative and Liberal Democrat coalition that increased student fees to £9,000 a year. That is a major contributory factor to the stress levels that exist within our NHS. It is a breaking point, it is a crisis point. Go to any A&E department and you'll see that straight away. So what we'd do... is end university fees, which will mean more people go into studying. What we will do is return the nurse bursary, probably on a more generous scale than it was in the past. And we will recruit as rapidly as we possibly can. But crucially, we will fund the NHS properly. £40 billion in total will go into the NHS in order to fund it properly. And that has to be done. The Prime Minister, the day after he was appointed, announced there was going to be 40 new hospitals. A week later, that became 20. A bit later on, it became six new hospitals. He seems to have a problem with the figures about hospital building in Britain.
Well, we can pass legislation to say you can't do it, because it is driving rents through the roof, and it's not fair on people. It's taking advantage of the market.

Hang on, it's a very important one, because there's nothing more important than education. And I think the answer to your question is almost certainly yes. What we've said we would do is to set up a committee under Chris Woodhead to look at the curriculum, to see if the national curriculum can be slimmed down, to look again at the kind of exams which people sit and the tests which they sit. I think we've got to keep the exams, but we've got to make the exams more useful than they are today, more useful to the people who sit the exams and more useful to the employers who need to be guided by them too often. They're a smokescreen. Let's just go back to where we were.

Mr Brown, I see you haven't denied. You're taking £6,000 million out of the economy in an emergency budget if you're elected. I would be very, very worried indeed. It seems a return to the old Conservative party. Now, Nick, on this question of banks, you don't solve the problem by making all your banks smaller. Northern Rock was a small bank and it went completely under. What you've got to do is to have proper capital controls. What you've got to do is have a global financial supervision system. Alistair Darling, our Chancellor, is now in Washington negotiating a global financial tax. That is the right thing to do.

Well, we do have benefits from immigration, as I said in my opening statement, but I think the choice really boils down to this. Nigel is saying there's nothing you can do inside the European Union, so just give up and leave. And Ed seems to be saying he doesn't want to renegotiate anything in Europe, so just give up altogether. I say get stuck in, renegotiate, get the changes we need, and then put those in an in-out referendum to the British people by the end of 2017. I've sat round that table in Europe and negotiated for Britain. You can get things done. And I've set out what I want to get done so that we can sort out this immigration issue once and for all.

Yeah, look, you know, again, I just think a whole approach to crime is wrong. Low-level crime is rapidly growing. Street-level crime is rapidly growing. And, you know, the old theories, the old theories about New York and how it was cleaned up, it was if you deal with the stuff at the bottom, i.e. people carrying knives, shoplifting, graffiti, broken windows, you might just deal with the more serious stuff. I think we also should add into all of this the blight of drugs and drug gangs in our cities. That whole culture, it has been absolutely disastrous and nobody, but nobody has yet found the answer to that.
Penny, that's rubbish and you've just said that we need a strong economy. You backed Liz Truss and crashed our economy. You made people like me redundant when we were in the home care service.

But by humiliating and separating children, do you think that that is an acceptable way forward? Diane put that forward in 2014. So, you know, David, I could possibly speak. Can you see that a mainstream party saying that is actually exacerbating and normalising hate crime, which is why young people feel terrified?

Well, I've explained before, I don't think there's much point to it again, because we are actually extending the winter fuel payment to people who are terminally ill and disabled who presently don't get it. We're doing that by bringing forward a decision that Gordon Brown's government has made. But can I just say again, before this completely collapses into a game of political ping-pong, there are big issues about how we look after the elderly, which are huge. They are financially huge, they're socially huge. It's one of the biggest issues we face on how we can provide social care. None of us... None of us have got, well none of us, well yes but it's also related to it because it's all about providing dignity to people in old age, which was Grace's question. And I don't think that we're going to serve the elderly today or those in the future very well if we hijack everything with cheap political point scoring when we could work together on a solution that works for many years into the future.

So we're talking about the snoopers charter or data surveillance, however you want to describe it. Now, here's the issue. When we're trying to deal with terrorists and suspected terrorists, we need to be able to focus on what they do and who they are talking to. What we have at the moment, if you like, is an ever-widening haystack and we're looking for a needle. And the answer is not to put more hay into the haystack, it is to put more magnets around the haystack so we can actually find what's in there in the first place. So just to be clear, you don't want those powers, you want them rolled up? There are two things here. I have got a practical concern and a principal concern. The practical concern is this. Our security services utterly need the ability to be able to catch people and to trace people. But the widening of powers is not something that the evidence is there to support. So it is a practical problem. There is a principal issue though... Lady in the middle, lady in the middle, Mr Farron. Okay, thank you.

and I find that very, very difficult to understand. And how can I possibly vote for you on that basis? Well, let me answer that very directly. In the last Parliament, we saved £20 billion on welfare, and we need to save something like half that in this Parliament. And as I say, if we don't save the money on welfare, and other parties don't seem to want to do that, then they're going to have to make deep cuts in things like the National Health Service, and I don't want to do that. I'm going to increase spending on the National Health Service every year in the next Parliament, just as I did in the last Parliament. But we can reduce welfare. If, for instance, we get another 2 million people back to work, that will cut the welfare bills. Here's another tough choice I think we do need to make. I think we should say to young people in our country The idea that you can leave school and immediately sign on to Jobseekers Allowance, immediately get a flat with housing benefit, I don't think that should be an option anymore. I think what we should do is say to young people, we want you to have a great future. You should be earning or learning. So we'll make available the apprenticeships, make available the university places, make available the training places, but say you can't start your life on benefits. That's not the way we should work in Britain today.
Thank you very much. He's been told not to repeat that lie and he's just done it.

We've set out in our manifesto what we want to do to go to renewable energy, and we've set out how much that will cost, exactly how much is going to be put in, and we've got a column saying how that's going to be funded. Yes, it is absolutely right that we want to get in investors to come alongside that government money, and we've been talking, you won't be surprised to learn this, to global investors for the best part of two years. to say that if we put down this money set out in our manifesto, will you come alongside it and put down many more billions of pounds so that in partnership with the government we can make the change that we need? Hang on, hang on. Let me just finish this. I'm sure we're going to come back to the same thing later on. This is your own shadow Chancellor. If you don't mind, I'm going to get to the next question. Just be straight with the people. How many hundreds is it?
We will rebuild new factories, Nigel, and we will redistribute dealing with the climate emergency.

Thank you. Let's just answer that. OK. So, ma'am, we are recruiting and training more doctors, nurses than we ever have in our history, and there are more today. And I know we need more, and that's why we've made those investments.
My brother was served in Iraq. I won't be lectured on whether or not I'm absolutely committed to the security of our country.

Well, some of these issues are the responsibility of my government in Scotland. So, on a cross-party basis in Scotland, we've already introduced and protected free personal care to help with the social care crisis. Just a few weeks ago, again, on a cross-party basis, we passed the most ambitious climate change targets anywhere in the world, and we're already making progress to meet those, although there's much, much more to be done, and it involves all of us working together. So, I will always try to work with others the political divide to make progress on these big issues that are so important, particularly for the next generation. But if we are to make progress on these issues, I think the priority in this election is to vote in a way that ensures that it is not Boris Johnson and Nigel Farage running the show as of the 13th of December, because they are the roadblock to the progress on so much of this. Thank you.

There does need to be some reviewing going on. We need to look at foreign policy. We need to look at the prevent strategy. It is a fact that policing has been cut by 20%, including when Theresa May was in the Home Office. And it's a question of priorities. We should be investing in police and other public services. Those are the people running into the dangerous situation when everyone else is running away.

Well, the first thing to say is that we would stop spending money on things that we simply don't think is a good use of money. And Trident Nuclear Weapons would come pretty close to the top of that list. So we would scrap nuclear weapons. That would give us around 130 billion minimum over the next 30 years. We wouldn't be building HS2. We wouldn't be going ahead giving massive subsidies to Hinkley Nuclear Power Station. So first of all, the thing you do is to stop wasting money. And then you have a principle that says that those people who have more money, who have the broadest shoulders, should be giving more into our system. So essentially what we need to be doing is to be levying things like a wealth tax, looking at issues around, as I said before, corporation tax. I think it's wrong that we're going for this bargain basement tax haven. I think we should be going for a country where corporations pay their tax. But the bottom line is that this country is not a poor country. The money is in the wrong hands. There is vast inequality in this country. And if we were to sort that problem out, then we would have a much better chance of ensuring that public services are properly paid for. Take the NHS, for example. We put far less into the NHS than most other countries of a similar GDP, the similar wealth. We don't put that money in our public services. And therefore, we don't have the world-class public services that we demand. The Greens are really clear that for us, public services should be in public hands. They should be not having the private sector in them. And they should be properly resourced.
Will you restore support for onshore wind if the Tories win this election?

How on earth does it help anyone in Bristol or anyone else in the country for that matter, David Cameron, to join together in the European Union with a bunch of nutters anti-Semites, people who deny climate change exists, homophobes. That doesn't help Britain. Of course we need to change the European Union, but you change clubs of which you are a member by getting stuck in, not standing on the sidelines and complaining about things.

Exactly. Which is why it's being structured over a period of time, so that we can help people, retrain and get other jobs, and the one thing we've done as a government, I think quite successfully, is where ever there are large scale job losses, but Rover is different, you've got six thousand jobs going immediately; jobs in the supply chain going immediately, you should go there and try and support them and and give some support to the infrastructure.

Dean. Dean, sorry. We were wrong on the regulation of the banks. We got it wrong. The banks were under-regulated, right? Now, other people were saying we should regulate them even less, but we were wrong, along with countries all round the world and regulators around the world. I'm sorry we got it wrong. OK. And we've got to learn from that, and I believe I have learnt from that in my five years as leader of the opposition. But has Ed Balls? He absolutely has.
Businesses will leave this country and if businesses leave the country... They're leaving already because of Brexit, Paul.

I would do everything I can to ensure that any threat is actually dealt with earlier on by negotiations and by talks, so that we do adhere to our obligations under the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty, that we do encourage China in its work in trying to bring about six-party talks in resolving the issue in North Korea. And we do follow the lead that President Obama took in doing a deal with Iran which would result in them not developing the nuclear capability. I think the idea of anyone ever using a nuclear weapon anywhere in the world is utterly appalling and terrible. It would result in the destruction of the lives and communities and environment for millions of people. And so I would be actively engaged to ensure that danger didn't come about. I would also be very aware of other dangers that we face. Cyber attacks hit our National Health Service a couple of weeks ago. The dreadful terrorist attack in Manchester indicates the danger of individual acts of terrorism that can take lives. We have to be secure here. We have to protect our society and our country. But we also have to engage in the rest of the world to make sure we don't have huge ungoverned spaces such as in Libya, which can become the basis of some terrible events that are going to take the lives of many other people. We have to deal with those issues here and globally. That is the function of a responsible government.

Thank you so much for this question. I mean, this is the climate election partly because of the actions of young people who've done such a good job of bringing this to other politicians' attention. They can't ignore you when you speak up as a school-age person. It's fantastic. But this is how Greens work. You know, we're very rarely in power. This is how I work on the London Assembly. I try and get Sadiq Khan to carry out things that are in my manifesto, not his. The Greens in Scotland, they hold Nicola's government to account and get good things like the new tax structures that you talked about. We're used to doing this and we've tried in this election too to bring together parties to try and put together some kind of agreement that we can actually try and stop Brexit together. I think that was a constructive and grown-up thing to do as well. And we do, we run councils with Labour, and we tried to get Labour to talk to us as well, and they didn't. But I believe that we're trying to show the way.

Please, let me finish answering the question. I'm not a defeatist like the Prime Minister who says to Julie, bad luck. That's as good as it gets. You'll have to go along with the problems you've got, however hard that is for you. I'm not in that. I'm not going to say to you, Julie, there's nothing I can do to help you or to other people in the same position. I'm going to say, I know we can get a better deal than the botched deal that we've got, and I'm going to go out and fight for it. I'm not going to have freedom of movement come back. I'm not going to go back into the EU, but I'm going to fight for a better deal so you can trade more easily across Europe, so you can succeed, and our economy can succeed, because I believe in our country.

Well, let me make a point about schools, because actually, under this government, we've got a million more children in good or outstanding schools. And one of the reasons for that is we've opened up the state education system and encouraged great education providers, teachers and charities to come in and set up schools. I was at one today, the Warrington Kings Academy. Three times more pupils want to go than they can take. And I think these free schools are a great institution but Ed Miliband's party says no more and is even contemplating shutting down some of the schools that are about to get going. I say we need more good school places and whether they're academies or free schools or university technical colleges, that's what we need. I want to come back to that point.

Gordon Brown. You know, at Reading Prison, we've been working at this young offenders institution with companies. And where people are in this institution, they're being trained for jobs that they can get if they don't re-offend and they go out and actually do a decent job. Now, there's been a 75% success in this project, so you can bring the re-offending rate down. But I do come back to this central problem that we face. I'm grateful, by the way, David, for you putting up all these posters about me and about crime and about everything else. There's no newspaper editor done as much for me in the last two years because my face is smiling on these posters. And I'm very grateful to you and Lord Ashcroft for funding that.

It's not hate crime. Everyone should be able to be free to criticise an ideology, to talk about writings in the Quran and the Hadith, to talk about Sharia law. We need to talk about these things. And internally Muslims, you are saying you're going to burn Muslims. We need to talk about the ideas there and how they affect people and how they encourage Islamism, which has led to violent mass murders. We need to talk about that. We need to be honest about it.
But let's vote, let's get that in Westminster and then we can get it across the funding that's in there to help.

It certainly is true that we're not building enough social housing, council houses for rent. I mean, the average of the last 20 years of the Labour government is 15 a year. I mean, it's absolutely appalling. So we've got to do something about that.

Well, we've suspended the right to buy. When Plaid Cymru was in government in the National Assembly for Wales, we passed legislation to enable local authorities to suspend the right-to-buy in areas of high housing pressure. Because of the point that Nicola just outlined, we need to protect the numbers of housing available for social rent. So, certainly if Plaid Cymru was in government in the National Assembly for Wales, we would not go ahead with this policy. It is one of the worst policies that you can think of, if you're thinking about reducing homelessness. This will increase levels of homelessness, and that's just not on.
He said, let's privatise it.

No, I don't. With the greatest respect, please don't be complacent about the state of the British economy. Our banking crisis was considerably worse than Greece's. Our deficit was just as bad. And so were our assets. If you want to see what happens where people don't step up to the plate, however controversial it is, to provide stable government. Look at the 50% youth unemployment in many other European countries where governments haven't got to grips with the economic crisis. I will never apologize, never apologize, whatever the short-term political effects, on the Liberal Democrats for having stepped up to the plate in a very plucky and brave way to put the country before party.

It's a very well-worded question, isn't it? I keep hearing that this is a problem, says Christine. And you know what? I think we need to heed and listen to people who have genuine concerns about the impact of the movement of population and pressures on public services, but we have to put that in the context of public spending cuts by the Conservatives. look at the numbers of immigrants that we need to bring in for certain sectors in health and care and parts of the economy. Listen, I've been on a whistle-stop tour of Wales in the past week. You know, I try to listen to people, but the problem we have is that Nigel Farage here has been on a dog-whistle tour of the UK for many, many years and exploiting the anxieties that people have, and we really need to change the way that we talk about this issue. It's making us poorer. That's the point. It's making us poorer. Thank you.

Which is why you've got to create economic growth in developing nations. That's the key thing. It's all about... Creating economic growth in itself doesn't tackle climate change. International trade is about creating economic growth, particularly in developing nations, to reduce that pressure on migration. But that doesn't tackle climate change. It absolutely does because what you've got economic growth and so that you can develop growth and systems within those developing nations in areas where there is greater climate change. You cannot expect mass immigration and migration to continue unparalleled.

Natalie Bennett first, then to Nigel Farage. I would partly agree with Nicola that we need to talk about the nature of the debate, but I disagree with her that this is a debate about economics. First of all, it's a debate about human lives. And if we look at one particular aspect of our immigration policy now, if you have a non-EU spouse or partner, you have to be earning more than £18,600 a year. your spouse or partner's salary cannot be counted. A judge described this as unfair and unreasonable. 19,000 Britons can't live in their own country with their family because of that rule. And a challenge to David Cameron, Syrian refugees. The UN has asked us very loudly and clearly to take our share of the most vulnerable Syrian refugees. You said no, we're not doing that, we're taking our own program. The last figure that I saw in that, we'd taken 143 Syrian refugees. I say to you, we should be taking our fair share of those most vulnerable Syrian refugees.
You don't have a workforce plan for social care.
I think the Committee on Climate Change would accept, or they do accept, that there will be a necessity to use fossil fuels, oil and gas for some time to come.

Well, what we would be doing is introducing 100,000 new council homes every year. We want to see half a million over the course of the next Parliament. And you'd do that quite simply by enabling a deposit to be put down. You'd put that £80,000 down for each house. The government would do that. you'd then allow councils to borrow, because right now, borrowing for councils hasn't been allowed, even though they've got that housing stock there to borrow against. And were you to do that, well, that would mean you would be able to build these houses cheaply, you'd get the money back in the rent that people would be paying. At the moment, that rent is going to private landlords. In other words, £8 billion and more is going to private landlords every year. Thank you. Just to finish on that, if you were to build those council houses, you'd find that that would actually pay for itself. in a number of years.

He was the first person to say we've got to make sure that the banks are properly regulated in the future. But let me say something else. The banks have got to work better for our businesses. That is a problem that goes back decades, these historic problems that we've got to solve.

Is anybody else on the platform concerned that there are 74 convicted terrorists out on our streets and 400 returning jihadis? Or do we just ignore it and pretend it's going to go away? Surely, surely we need... This is not just an ordinary criminal. These people want to destroy our civilisation. Surely they should be put in prison for life.

Well, I would add this. We've got a doctor's shortage in Wales. We've got fewer doctors per head of the population in Wales than almost every other country in the EU. This is due, in part, to a tightening of the immigration rules under the previous Labour Government, which saw fewer doctors coming to Wales from the Indian subcontinent. That caused particular problems in A&E and in paediatrics departments. As a result, the Labour Government in Wales are centralising hospital services, moving services away from people. So, this debate has caused real problems for our health service in Wales. Now, if you were a foreign doctor, would you want to come to the UK into the climate, the anti-immigration climate that we have here? Of course, we have to train more doctors, but it takes an awful long time to train a doctor, and we have problems in the Welsh NHS now. I want to come... David Cameron?

Theresa May called this election because she's taking you for granted. She won't turn up to these debates because her campaign of soundbites is falling apart. Unlike Theresa May, I'm not afraid to defend my policies, my principles, my values. Plaid Cymru exists to defend and build up our country. In Wales it is Plaid Cymru that stands for fairness and equality and everyone who chooses to call Wales home. For a hundred years Wales has voted Labour. It hasn't stopped Tory rule. Labour in Wales have their own manifesto and they're airbrushing their leader out of their campaign. When Plaid Cymru has tried to ban zero hours contracts and to stop the bedroom tax, Welsh Labour voted against us. It's time we stop doing what we've always done. Let's instead give Wales a voice. Give Plaid Cymru a mandate. Elect a strong team of Plaid Cymru MPs to fight for a successful future for Wales.

Penny Warden was referring to some areas having higher levels of crime than others. Let's be straight about it. It's areas which are suffering most poverty that will suffer the highest levels of crime. It is people who have less hope who are most likely to end up leading criminal lives. I absolutely agree that we need to look back through the education system. We have to make it very, very clear to our young people that there can be zero tolerance on knives. I was attacked as a teenager. Luckily, no knives. My son was attacked a couple of years ago. Again, luckily, no knives involved. But It makes me too very, very worried about the future for young people. But for us in Wales, what's absolutely key is that we bring the decision-making closer to communities such as yours, so that we can see the devolving of justice and policing, so that we can devise systems that work for Wales. Angela's Labour colleagues in Wales agree with me on that. Again, Labour at a UK level, hoping to become the next government, say no, no chance.

Well, what's wrong with that is, 12 hours or 15 hours after being appointed Prime Minister, he came to Parliament and grandly announced there was 40 new hospitals. It turned out there weren't. That's when he was asked where they were going to be. That was then reduced to 20, and then it became seed funding for six. It's not credible to make those kind of statements and not be able to back it up with any serious plan.

Government spending shouldn't just be thought about as expenditure. It should also be thought about as investment. Investment in the future. Now, austerity has delivered. We've paid a high price for austerity. 31% of children in Wales are in poverty. 29% in your constituency of children are in poverty. Thousands of people on food banks. 30% of people where I live, young people, are out of work. Now, my view is that Labour are letting the Tories off the hook by committing to the Tories' spending plans. So if you were Prime Minister, Ed, I wonder if you could tell us, would you be prepared to hold an emergency budget to reverse those Tory spending cuts that are causing so much pain to so many people in so many of our communities?

So it's completely head in the sand stuff. Keir Starmer's going to reverse the changes I made. To install a heat pump now, if you don't need to, costs about £7,000 or £10,000. To convert your home, again, if you're in a terraced house in Darlington, £10,000. Changing your car sooner than America, Canada, Sweden, France, Germany, as he wants you to do, thousands of pounds. OK, let Keir Starmer respond... There's a simple choice at this election. Thank you. If you think Labour are going to win, start saving.

If you vote Conservative, I've said I will serve every day of a full second term. What I was doing in that interview was just giving an honest answer to an honest question, because I think people need to know that sort of thing. Are you one of those leaders who, like Chairman Mao, thinks you can go on and on and on and on? Or actually, do you think, look, I'm really passionate about what I'm doing. I think we're turning this country around. I'm passionate about having another term, completing this vital work. But after that, 10 years, two terms, I think politicians do have a date by which they need to say, well, I think it's time for someone else to take over. I'm not some person who thinks that you just, you're indispensable. We're not indispensable. It's important to remember that.
And also what the effect it would be on innocent civilians around.
No, you had a deal. Parliament, it passed it. It passed that deal.
OK. It also says that children, basically non-white children, when they come back to school should be checked to see if they've had genital mutilation.
Again, we're back to the welfare debate. I'm happy to have that again. You don't think there's a penny to be made from welfare savings?

Well, look, it is just simply not the case. It's just simply not true that there's been some great push towards privatisation. It is simply not the case. What? Well, let me give you a statistic. You legislated to allow 49% of actual legislation. Thank you. I think it's outweighed. 14%. Thank you, Nicola Sturgeon. We legislated to outlaw the sweetheart deals which were entered into with the private sector by the Labour government, as it happened. I'm not defending them. When we took over five years ago, the total amount of NHS money devoted to the private sector was 4%. It is now 6%. I don't call that a great sweeping act of privatisation. But all I would say is it needs the money, £8 billion. We need to prioritise mental health and we need to bring social care and healthcare together because we have too many elderly folk in hospital beds who should be discharged from hospital but don't have a place to go. Thank you. Nigel Farage.

There's been a powerful opposition in Parliament in the last five years. Her name was Caroline Lucas. Caroline is leading the way in restoring the NHS and our railways and fighting against fracking. You can elect a strong group of Green MPs like Caroline with a vision of a humane, sustainable Britain. Don't believe the advocates of spiteful austerity. This is the world's sixth richest country. We can give our young a properly funded education. We can provide free health and social care for our elderly. We can, must, make sure that everyone has food on the table and a stable home to live in. The Green Party are the real challengers. Offering a million new jobs, combating climate change, restoring vital community services. That's why voters are turning to us. Challenge the establishment. Vote Green.

Dennis, I have heard different versions of your questions time and time again. I hear from people all over the country, they're working hard, they're striving, they're supporting their families, they're paying their taxes, and when they go to use their public services, whether it's the NHS, whether it's schools, whether it's the police or anything else, they're broken. They're completely broken and nothing works. Now, in this election, we Liberal Democrats have said that our manifesto is a manifesto to save the NHS and social care. We're pledging £9 billion to invest upstream. We see on the TV all these disastrous pictures of people dying in the back of ambulances, suffering the indignity of corridor care because they can't see a GP, they can't see a dentist, and that's what we want to fix. So, under Liberal Democrats, you'll have 8,000 more GPs, We'll fix the dental crisis and we'll invest free personal social care so people can get all the care they need when they need it.

Well Terry, I think it's really worth noting how the NHS wasn't a huge issue in the 2010 election, but it's a huge issue now. In 2010 we were well aware of the huge costs of the private finance initiative and how there was already a creeping privatisation of the NHS. But what we've seen since then, particularly with the Health and Social Care Act, has been a race towards setting up the structures for privatisation. Now what we say in the Green Party is very simple. The profit motive should have no place in healthcare. We're not happy with 5% of money that's supposed to be for healthcare going in profits. We want 0% going in profits. And to make our NHS more efficient, To deal with some of its problems, what we need to do is take the whole market mechanism out of the NHS, the whole purchaser-provider split that the Health Select Committee in 2010 said was costing us £10 billion a year. We need to put more money in, and in the Green Party, we want the free prescriptions they've got in Scotland, plus free eye care and free dental, but we need to look at the structures and say no private profit. Natalie Bennett, thank you very much indeed.

Because this election is about the future and making people's lives better. Now, I've got a clear plan to ease those anxieties that Paula and everyone is facing. Because things are tough, I want to make them easier by continuing to cut your taxes. The difference of this election is Keir Starmer is going to put your taxes up by £2,000. Thank you. So if people are worried about their electricity bill, their food bill, taking their kids on a holiday, £2,000 worth of tax rises are not going to help with any of those things. I'll ask again, why do you want to put everyone's taxes up?

Well we think that, we think two things about the Health Service. We, we think first, first of all we think that everybody should have more choice. That means that any NHS patient should have the choice of going to any NHS hospital. Secondly, we think that if the private sector can provide health care, at the same cost as the NHS, then it(?) should have the right to supply those services, and patients should have the right to go to the private sector for those services, and thirdly, we think that ur, people have paid their taxes, they've paid for the cost of their NHS treatment. If they choose to go private, the NHS should pay half of what it would have cost the NHS to pay for those operations, er, towards the cost of their treatment in the private sector.

And in this country, the Green Party has increased the number of elected Greens fivefold in the last five years, winning seats from Labour and from the Conservatives in all corners of the country. People are desperate for change. They're desperate for authentic voices in politics. And we're the only party that's being honest in this election and saying that if we're going to fund that transition to a green economy in a fair way and restoring our NHS, then we are going to need to ask the very richest in society, the multimillionaires, the billionaires, to pay modestly more in tax to bring about that transition.

I want to come back to this point about how one takes the right decisions to support the new technologies, the new manufacturing industries of the future. I think, for instance, it's fairly obvious, if you look at the huge number of offshore wind turbines which are now being installed off the coast of Britain in all sorts of places, that we should be a world leader in manufacturing this new green technology. And yet, the only manufacturer of onshore wind turbines has recently closed, and the London Array project, one of the biggest offshore wind projects, off the coast of Kent, 90% of the stuff installed there was built in Denmark and Germany. Why aren't we using small amounts of money to invest in old dockyards and shipyards so that we manufacture the new green technology of the future as well?

Well, just to explain, so the Gambling Commission who are investigating these, as well as the police, obviously are independent. of me, of government. I don't have access to the details of their investigation. They don't report to me. But what I can tell you is, in parallel, we have been conducting internal inquiries of our own into the situation and won't hesitate to act when information or findings come to light.

Yes, we have increased child tax credits actually, we increased them by £452 under this government because I was determined that while we had to take difficult decisions, and we have, we were left an absolute nightmare situation to clear up, I wanted to make sure that child poverty continued to fall and it has fallen under this government because of what we did
Of course not. We'll have the conversation when we're in government. We'll be set - we'll be asking parliament ¿

Mahesh, thank you. Look, I think the reality is what we want is a sensible, fair immigration policy that works for our country. And if we look back to the 1990s, as a nation, we've always welcomed immigration. And in the 1990s, we had immigration of about 1,000 people a week. Wages grew at a sensible pace. We had enough doctors and teachers and nurses for our public services. So, actually, if you have a sensible system, and we believe that an Australian-style points-based system, based on skills, is the right way forward, then that actually works for our economy and works for our own people.

Well, first of all, I want to say we all owe a huge debt to our veterans, those from D-Day and those from conflicts. since and it's a tragedy that so many veterans then often struggle in life, there's high rates of homelessness and that's something that's a high priority for myself and the Greens to tackle. In terms of defence more broadly, this election is about choosing what kind of country we want to be and of course part of that is security. We live in uncertain and dangerous times with war on our European borders and major threats from cyber attacks that don't care about geographical boundaries. The threats should not be underestimated. But the biggest threat facing the UK and the world is climate change. And militaries, interestingly, are taking this threat extremely seriously.

One of the biggest regrets of 14 years of mismanagement by the Conservatives is that they missed the opportunity to invest in economic growth in order to help our communities. During those years when interest rates were low, what they chose to do was have this ideological drive to cut public spending. that hit everybody, especially those who are poorest. And in this selection, you'll hear me talking a lot about ambition. Where is the ambition from either of the Westminster main parties to invest in allowing us in Wales to make the most of our natural resources through devolving the Crown Estate, allowing us to invest in transport and connectivity through giving us the billions we're owed as a result of HS2 rail in England? That's the ambition that we need. We're not hearing it from the Conservatives, and I regret we're not hearing it from them.

Well thank you Rebecca for your question. I think it's absolutely crucial. I think the most important thing is to make sure there are good jobs for people to do and obviously in the last Parliament we've created 2 million new jobs and in the next Parliament we want to create 2 million more good jobs for people. I think apprenticeships and universities are vital. I want our young people to have the choice of either. So we're going to have 3 million apprenticeships in the next Parliament, but also we've uncapped university places, so whoever wants a place can go to university. In terms of building homes, I want us to build homes that people can afford to buy, and that's what our starter homes are all about. Put at 80% of the market price, not available to foreign buyers or investment funds, but they're for British people to buy and to own. And with our Help to Buy, that has helped about 88,000 people onto the housing ladder, we can make that a reality for people. Now, I know it seems odd to answer a question from a young person about pensions, but actually it is important to be able to look forward to security and dignity in old age, and we've safeguarded the pension because people in our country should be able to look forward to dignity and security at the end of a hard-working life.

But Nick, just on a small point of fact, there was no parole or probation involved in this, he was released automatically, but your point, and there was nothing that any parole board or probation officer could do about that. That was the nature of the sentence. But there is a valid question about how now we make sure that we have a criminal justice system that protects everybody and delivers justice. Are we going to get a rare moment of agreement? You may be agreeing on this. We are putting a huge amount of cash into our criminal justice system. I do believe that it should run smoothly. And we're putting another £2.5 billion into our prison system. You can only do that if you have a strong economy.

Yes, I think teachers, of course, do deserve a pay rise. I think there is clear evidence that smaller class sizes do benefit not just the teacher, but also the pupil, because teacher-pupil time is premium. students or pupils who are in smaller class sizes do better academically. That is simply a fact and it is a scandal. It's a scandal that 500,000 primary school kids in this country are sitting now in classes with over 30 other people in them.

You will? Now what we've said is we will put two billion into the benefit system per year in order to... That's tax credits. In order to reduce the effects we will also end the indignity of the... Will you freeze benefits for three years then? Benefits will be paid, of course. Benefits will be uprated, and will be uprated, of course. And there will be a higher living wage, as I've outlined, which will of course mean that those benefits that are paid for people in work, and indeed the majority of benefits go to people in work, not out of work, will probably reduce.

Well, I think it's a really, really important question. We're already seeking to do many things for the younger generation, not charging them to go to university. For example, make sure their education can be based on the ability to learn. Housing is critical. Across the UK, we need to build more houses. We're working just now to a target of 50,000 affordable houses over the next few years. We need to make sure there are that we need to do to make sure young people have the best opportunities and the best start in life. And we shouldn't take away vital support like housing benefit support from under-20s and under-30s. We need to make sure that young people have the best opportunities and the best start in life. And we shouldn't take away vital support like housing benefit support from under-20s and under-30s. But the other point I would make here is this. I don't think we should be setting young people against older people. At a time when we see pensioner poverty rising again, we shouldn't be removing pension protection. We should end austerity and make sure we can provide these opportunities for people regardless of their age across the country.

One thing that hasn't been mentioned at all, which is absolutely crucial of course to dealing with a global problem, is acting globally. I remember seeing the television pictures of Gordon Brown sitting... to say slightly sort of on the sidelines in the summit in Copenhagen, while America and China basically cut everybody else out. And I think if you're going to lead on this, of course you have to lead at home, but you also have to lead in Europe. There's no point clubbing together, as David Cameron has done, with people who even deny the existence of climate change in Europe. You've got to create strengthened numbers in Europe so that we can really lead in the world, because this is a global problem which requires a global response.

Yeah, I mean, I highlighted at the beginning exactly where we would take the money. We would directly transfer that money from the foreign aid budget, which is costing us £30 million every single day.

The Governor of the Bank of England has quite clearly said that unless you split high-risk casino investment banking from low-risk high-street banking, the banks that you and I depend on, you're always going to get this risk that risks will be taken with your everyday savings. That's what you've got to stop. It's what they did, by the way, in the 1930s in the Depression in the United States. I think we need to do something similar. It's what President Obama is doing, something similar in the United States. And yet, neither of the old parties want to even contemplate the major reforms needed to our banks. I think we owe it to future generations to make sure that the implosion in our banking system never, ever, ever can happen again.

Huge damage has been done to our economy under this government. Huge damage. And if we are able to come into government and form a government, we're probably going to inherit a broken economy, a broken NHS, broken public services, in fact, I've broken everything. OK. And I'm not going to make promises that I can't keep. So I'm doing, actually, a difficult thing, which is saying to the electorate, saying to you now, I can't do something... You accept these are choices. ..which I know the benefits of it. We need to deal with child poverty.

No, not off the top of my head. You'd have to break off the, break down the number of households you're talking about. But the kind of couple that you're giving me, represent 20% of households, and I wouldn't imagine in Cardiff Central, it is that...

I understand that. And let me address, I didn't vote for Jeremy Corbyn to be leader of our party in 2015. I didn't vote for him to be leader in 2016. I supported other candidates. But our membership returned him as the leader. That was very difficult for me and for our party, because I thought that we were not going to be heading in the right direction. Everybody then took a decision about what they did, how we dealt with this. I felt that on issues such as Brexit, which I thought were going to define us for decades to come...
