Leaders are temporary, but political parties are permanent. that it was important to have a voice in the Shadow Cabinet, and it meant that I could challenge on anti-Semitism, and it meant that the Labour Party never veered from its position on things that are fundamentally important, like NATO. Now, I accept how the colleagues did different things, but I knew, everybody knew, there was always going to be a day after when we would have the opportunity for a new leader of our party and to make sure our party was there to face the future. And I'm proud of the fact that I was able then to win the leadership of the party and to put it back in front of you now as a fundamentally different party.
Crime's gone down. No, it hasn't. Crime has gone down. No, it hasn't.

I'm going to try to cover all three comments. I came to this country in 1978. I was about 11 years old. I couldn't speak English. My parents had to flee Iraq from a dictator, actually not too dissimilar to Abdi's parents. The difference is my parents embraced the country that rescued them, and we integrated. I learned English within six months. I went to university, I've built a great business, I'm now the member, well I used to be, I'm the candidate now, I was the member of parliament for Stratford-on-Avon, the heart of England. This country is a great country, it has incredible values, values of freedom, opportunity, democracy, rule of law, tolerance. I think one of the things that we have not done well is integration, but also we've for too long tolerated people who preached extremism and hatred amongst us, people who preached intolerance to our values, to our way of life, misogyny, all sorts of things. And I think we've got to call those people out in our communities. And I think the Muslim community, the mainstream Muslim community, is equally targeted by these extremists, by these criminals, because they target Ahmadiyya Muslims or other Muslims as well as being kuffar, which is people who are outside the religion, therefore easy game in many ways. But what we've got to do is come together as political parties, as a community, and call out intolerance and say, that's wrong. Our society is not like that. We're proud of what we are. What Manchester did at the concert was incredible, truly incredible. And that should be our role model in many ways of how we deal with this as a political party. I'd like us to come together. Let's not keep We're tearing chunks out of one another. Let's say, look, how can we work together to get the right legislation through Parliament so that we can do this? But it's not just legislation. It's also, how do we come together and call out these extremists?

Well, I want to stop Brexit. I'm not going to apologise for that, because remaining in the EU is better for our jobs, for future opportunities, for our public services, for our environment. How do you know? We've not Brexited yet. Well, I know because we'll be losing crucial environmental protections that the Tories will then want to rip up, because all of the economic analysis shows that when you pull up massive barriers with your closest trading partners, that will hit business hard. Answer the question. Is it anti-democratic? You're the only one here which says revoke liberal Democrats. In a democratic event, right? So it's giving people the choice in an election.

Well, we've been through the argument towards the end of this parliament and the government were putting up precisely arguments like that to favour things like the detention without trial, and as you well know, the House of Lords and a substantial view in the House of Commons, wasn't going to have it.

Well, if we are putting money into... When we put money into countries, we change the way that we spend the foreign aid budget. In the past, foreign aid budget was all too often just given to governments. And you know the stories in the past of the way the money was then spent. So we work through NGOs. We work through organizations that are helping people. So money that we put into countries is targeted on things like education, on health, on welfare of people, on support for the people who are the most vulnerable.

I could stand here on this stage tonight because an earlier generation gave me a chance. Born in a council house, educated in a comp, free school meals kept me fed in the minor strike, and education was my route out of poverty. There are children in Wales that will go to school tomorrow hungry, some of them without even a decent pair of shoes on their feet. It doesn't have to be this way. We could start to end child poverty this winter, not drop the target as Labour have done in Wales. We could give all our children, the best Christmas present ever, the gift of a better future. If we do what we've always done and put our faith in the Westminster parties, that cycle of poverty will continue. But if pride breaks through, we break that cycle and then everything for Wales becomes possible.
Processed? They're not... These claims are not being processed.

Well, of course, as I've already outlined, as I understand it to be the case, serious knife crime instances in Scotland are comparatively low in comparison to elsewhere, but our police officer numbers are of course still higher than when we came into government. Again, if I understand correctly but there's a wider picture to be had here and that means police officers working with local communities, working with schools, working with our young people to offer them hope and optimism for the future and I'm afraid that Westminster doesn't offer that hope and optimism right now.

What I would say is, for example, the Dome was not a good example of the way money is spent. Governments make mistakes. Governments make mistakes. There are always inefficiencies in government. Of course there are inefficiencies. Probably too many reorganisations of public services, which waste money. Of course that's right. But I'm talking about the overall picture, Jeremy. But the other thing I'd say to you is, look, let's talk about the future.

Terry, like so many people around the country, I'm deeply concerned about what I see happening in our National Health Service, because we see people waiting longer for their test results, longer in A&E, longer to see a GP, longer to have an operation. We've got to turn it round. And when you hear from us leaders tonight, I think at home you should ask, where specifically is the money coming from? I'll tell you. We're going to have a mansion tax on the most expensive homes above £2 million. That's a clear promise from us. We're going to get money from those hedge funds who are engaging in tax avoidance and we're going to get money from the tobacco companies for a £2.5 billion Time to Care Fund. That will hire 20,000 more nurses, 8,000 more doctors, 5,000 more care workers and 3,000 more midwives. And it's not just more staff. because it's also joining up services from home to hospital, taking on the biggest challenge our NHS faces, which is an ageing population. Because if people can't get in to see their GP or can't be looked after at home, they end up in A&E. It's a plan to turn the NHS round.

Yeah, I was incredibly sad to have caused people hurt and upset. That was actually the last thing that I wanted to do. And as I said, I hope people can find it in their hearts to forgive me. When I realised I'd made a mistake, I apologised. I've asked for forgiveness and also I have been speaking to veterans about the things that we are doing to make sure this is a good country for them after they've served because we owe them an enormous debt of gratitude and that's why I've made sure it's a priority to have a minister around the cabinet table to support them and focus on the things that matter to them.

There are lots of people who go for private healthcare at the moment who can't afford it. There was an item on the news this evening about someone who had to sell their home in order to get the private care they needed because they had to wait so long for their treatment under the NHS. These are all people who've paid their taxes. They've paid towards the cost of their NHS treatment.
